topics.title: Micro Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Micro Turning** is a non-traditional micro-machining variant of conventional turning that is used to produce micro-scale cylindrical components with diameters typically less than or equal to **1 mm** (and often down to **50 µm**). It is classified under the broader umbrella of *Mechanical Micro-Machining* processes and is performed on ultra-precision or modified conventional lathes equipped with high-resolution positioning systems, high-speed spindles, and single-crystal diamond (SCD) or micro-grain carbide cutting tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *Micro-turning is a material removal process in which a micro-scale cutting tool, guided along a defined tool path, removes material from a rotating micro-workpiece to generate axi-symmetric features with dimensional tolerances in the sub-100 µm regime and surface roughness (Ra) in the sub-micron range.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chalk-Tip" Model

Imagine a regular carpenter's pencil sharpener. When you rotate a large pencil against a large blade, large shavings come off smoothly. Now imagine shrinking the entire setup to the size of a **human hair (~70 µm)**. The blade becomes so tiny that its **cutting edge radius** is comparable to the *thickness of the chip it is trying to remove*. This is precisely the engineering challenge in micro turning.

In simple words:
- A normal lathe is like shaving a thick carrot with a chef's knife.
- A micro-lathe is like trying to shave a single strand of spaghetti with a tiny scalpel — every micrometre of tool deflection, vibration, or heat becomes critically important.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Intuition (The "Size Effect"):**
> As the depth of cut approaches the **cutting edge radius of the tool**, the tool no longer cuts — it **plows, rubs, and elastically deforms** the workpiece. This is called the **Size Effect**, and it is the single most important phenomenon in micro turning.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Boundaries

| Parameter | Conventional Turning | Micro Turning |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------|
| Workpiece Diameter | > 10 mm | **≤ 1 mm** (typically 0.05–1.0 mm) |
| Depth of Cut | 0.1 – 5 mm | **1 – 100 µm** |
| Feed Rate | 0.05 – 0.5 mm/rev | **1 – 50 µm/rev** |
| Spindle Speed | 500 – 3000 rpm | **10,000 – 100,000+ rpm** |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | 0.8 – 3.2 µm | **< 0.1 µm** |
| Tool Material | Carbide, HSS, Ceramics | **SCD, PCD, Ultra-fine grain Carbide** |
| Machine Type | Standard Lathe | **Ultra-precision Micro-lathe** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention Used Throughout KTU 2024 Notes:**
> - Length: **µm** (micrometre, 10⁻⁶ m) for chip thickness, feed, depth of cut
> - Rotational Speed: **rpm** (revolutions per minute)
> - Cutting Speed: **m/min**
> - Surface Roughness: **µm** (Ra)
> - Force: **mN to N**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The micro-turning process is governed by a strict sequence of physical events. Each step is critical because, at the micro-scale, a deviation of even 1 µm can cause catastrophic part rejection.

**Step 1 — Workpiece Preparation & Holding**
- The micro-workpiece (often a wire or pin) is mounted between a high-speed collet chuck and a tailstock centre.
- Runout (TIR — Total Indicator Reading) must be **< 1 µm** to ensure geometric accuracy.
- The holding force is delicate; excessive clamping causes **workpiece deflection** rather than cutting.

**Step 2 — Spindle Acceleration & Dynamic Balancing**
- Spindles operate at very high rpm (e.g., **100,000 rpm**) and must be dynamically balanced to avoid centrifugal vibration.
- The high rpm produces high cutting speeds, which in turn **reduce the chip thickness** and the heat-affected zone (HAZ).

**Step 3 — Tool Engagement & Size Effect Activation**
- As the single-crystal diamond (SCD) tool (nose radius typically **0.5–2.0 mm**) engages, the uncut chip thickness (h) may be **smaller than the tool edge radius (r_β)**.
- When **h ≤ r_β**, the material is not cut — it is **plowed and rubbed**. This is the **Size Effect** regime.

**Step 4 — Chip Formation & Elastic Recovery**
- Below the *minimum uncut chip thickness* (h_min), no chip is formed. Material is pushed aside and partially springs back (elastic recovery) after the tool passes.
- h_min is empirically given by:
$$h_{min} \approx \frac{r_\beta}{2} \quad \text{to} \quad h_{min} \approx 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$$

**Step 5 — Surface Generation & BUE Formation**
- The finished surface is generated by the **nose radius** of the tool (geometrically) and the **elastic recovery** of the material (physically).
- Built-Up Edge (BUE) is common at low cutting speeds because the tool-workpiece interface is dominated by adhesion rather than shearing.

**Step 6 — Tool Wear Progression**
- Three wear mechanisms dominate:
   1. **Adhesion** (tool material sticking to chip)
   2. **Diffusion** (atomic migration at high temperatures)
   3. **Abrasion** (mechanical scratching)

## 2.2 The "Size Effect" — The Heart of Micro Turning

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the Size Effect matter?**
> In macro turning, cutting force is **proportional** to the uncut chip cross-sectional area. In micro turning, as the depth of cut decreases, the specific cutting energy (energy per unit volume removed) **rises sharply** — sometimes 2× to 10× the macro value.

**The Ploughing Force Component:**
As chip thickness approaches the edge radius, the cutting force splits into two components:
1. **Shearing Force (F_s)** — actual material separation (dominant in macro).
2. **Ploughing Force (F_p)** — friction and elastic deformation (dominant in micro).

**Total cutting force:**
$$F_c = F_s + F_p = K_s \cdot A + F_p(r_\beta, h, E)$$

where:
- $K_s$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm² or MPa)
- $A$ = uncut chip cross-sectional area = feed × depth of cut
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of workpiece
- $r_\beta$ = tool cutting edge radius
- $h$ = uncut chip thickness

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Spindle Speed | rpm |
| 2 | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Cutting Speed | m/min |
| 3 | $h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa)$ | Uncut Chip Thickness | mm or µm |
| 4 | $A = f \cdot d$ | Uncut Chip Cross-Sectional Area | mm² or µm² |
| 5 | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material Removal Rate | mm³/min |
| 6 | $F_c = K_s \cdot A$ | Cutting Force (macro regime) | N |
| 7 | $r = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot f^2}{R}$ | Theoretical Surface Roughness (Ra) | mm or µm |
| 8 | $h_{min} \approx 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$ | Minimum Uncut Chip Thickness | µm |
| 9 | $F_p \propto \dfrac{HV}{\sqrt{r_\beta}}$ | Ploughing Force Approximation | N |
| 10 | $P_c = F_c \cdot V_c$ | Cutting Power | W or kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Legend for KTU 2024 Exam (must memorize):**
> - $N$ = Spindle speed (rpm)
> - $V_c$ = Cutting speed (m/min)
> - $D$ = Workpiece diameter (mm)
> - $f$ = Feed per revolution (mm/rev or µm/rev)
> - $d$ = Depth of cut (mm or µm)
> - $R$ = Tool nose radius (mm)
> - $\kappa$ = Tool lead angle (degree)
> - $HV$ = Vickers Hardness (kgf/mm²)
> - $r_\beta$ = Tool cutting edge radius (µm)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Micro turning is critical in:

1. **Biomedical Implants:** Manufacturing of micro-pins for bone fixation, micro-shafts for stents, and micro-needles for drug delivery.
2. **Watch & Instrumentation Industry:** Production of miniature shafts, pivots, and pinions in mechanical watches.
3. **Aerospace & Defence:** Micro fuel injector nozzles, micro-sensor housings.
4. **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems):** Micro-shafts, micro-gears, and micro-bearings.
5. **Optics:** Mounting components for fiber-optic alignment systems.
6. **Electronics:** Probe pins for testing integrated circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:**
> In the **watch industry (Switzerland)**, micro turning of hardened steel pivots (diameter ≈ 100 µm) with surface finish Ra < 0.05 µm is a daily production requirement. This is only achievable through precision micro turning with SCD tools.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Spindle Speed, Cutting Speed, and Diameter

**Starting Physical Principle:**
Cutting speed is defined as the *tangential velocity at the cutting interface*. For a rotating cylindrical workpiece, the tangential velocity at any radial distance is the product of angular velocity and radius.

**Step 1: Define the cutting speed in fundamental units.**

Cutting speed $V_c$ is the linear velocity of the workpiece surface relative to the cutting tool edge, measured in m/min.

$$V_c = \omega \cdot r$$

**Step 2: Express angular velocity in terms of spindle speed N.**

The spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. The angular velocity in radians per second is:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

**Step 3: Express the radius from the diameter.**

The cutting occurs at the outer surface of the workpiece, so the radius $r = D / 2$ where $D$ is the workpiece diameter in metres.

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

$$V_c = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot N \cdot D}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

**Step 5: Convert cutting speed to m/min (the KTU standard unit).**

Multiply by 60 to convert from per-second to per-minute basis:

$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot N \cdot D}{60} \cdot 60 = \pi \cdot N \cdot D \quad \text{[m/min if D is in metres]}$$

**Step 6: Re-scale D from metres to millimetres (1 m = 1000 mm).**

$$V_c = \pi \cdot N \cdot \dfrac{D_{mm}}{1000} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot N \cdot D}{1000}$$

**Final boxed formula (KTU 2024 exam-ready form):**

$$\boxed{V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

Inverting to find spindle speed:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Surface Roughness (Ra) in Turning

**Starting Physical Principle:**
The theoretical surface finish in turning is generated by the **nose radius of the tool** sweeping across the rotating workpiece. The feed per revolution leaves a periodic "feed mark" on the surface whose height depends on the feed and the nose radius.

**Step 1: Visualize the geometry.**

When the tool moves a distance equal to one feed (f) per revolution, the tool nose radius (R) leaves a small peak-valley pattern on the surface. The maximum peak-to-valley height (R_max) is given by the geometric sagitta formula.

**Step 2: Apply the sagitta equation for a circle.**

For a circle of radius $R$ subtended by a chord of length $f$:

$$R_{max} = R - \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3: Apply the binomial approximation.**

For $f \ll R$ (which is always true in micro turning), use the binomial series expansion:

$$\sqrt{R^2 - \left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^2} = R \cdot \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{f^2}{4R^2}} \approx R \left(1 - \dfrac{f^2}{8R^2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute the approximation.**

$$R_{max} \approx R - R + \dfrac{f^2}{8R} = \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$$

**Step 5: Convert R_max (peak-to-valley) to Ra (arithmetic mean roughness).**

For a sinusoidal surface profile, $Ra \approx 0.032 \cdot R_{max}$. For a feed-mark profile, the empirical conversion factor is approximately $1/4$:

$$R_a \approx 0.063 \cdot \dfrac{f^2}{R}$$

**Final boxed formula (KTU 2024 exam-ready form):**

$$\boxed{R_a = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot f^2}{R}}$$

where:
- $R_a$ is in mm if $f$ and $R$ are in mm
- For micro turning, use **µm**: $R_a[\mu m] = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot f[\mu m]^2}{R[\mu m]}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Uncut Chip Thickness (h_min) and the Ploughing Threshold

**Step 1: Define the geometric interaction.**

When a tool with cutting edge radius $r_\beta$ engages a workpiece with feed $f$, the effective uncut chip thickness $h$ is:

$$h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa)$$

For a sharp tool (zero edge radius), $h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa)$ and chip formation begins immediately.

**Step 2: Account for finite edge radius.**

For a real tool with edge radius $r_\beta$, the tool does not cut a clean chip when $h < r_\beta$. Instead, the material undergoes elastic-plastic deformation under the rounded edge.

**Step 3: Energy balance for chip formation.**

The energy required to form a chip = work needed for shear deformation + work needed for ploughing (friction at the rounded edge). When the energy required to form a chip exceeds the ploughing energy, the chip will not form — material is pushed aside and elastically recovers.

**Step 4: Empirical threshold.**

Experimental studies (from Ehmann, Bissacco, and others — classical micro-machining literature) show that the minimum uncut chip thickness is approximately:

$$\boxed{h_{min} \approx (0.4 \text{ to } 0.5) \cdot r_\beta}$$

**Step 5: Implication for process design.**

In micro turning, the designer must ensure that $f \cdot \sin(\kappa) > 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$, otherwise the surface will be characterized by **ploughing smearing** rather than clean cutting.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Exam Style)

**Problem:**
A micro turning operation is performed on a brass (C260) workpiece of diameter $D = 0.5$ mm using a single-crystal diamond tool with nose radius $R = 0.5$ mm, edge radius $r_\beta = 2$ µm, and lead angle $\kappa = 90°$. The spindle speed is $N = 50{,}000$ rpm and feed is $f = 10$ µm/rev. Compute:
(a) Cutting speed
(b) Uncut chip thickness
(c) Theoretical surface roughness
(d) Whether the tool is in the cutting regime or ploughing regime.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(a) Cutting Speed:**
$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 50{,}000}{1000} = \dfrac{25{,}000 \cdot \pi}{1000} = 25\pi \approx 78.54 \text{ m/min}$$

**(b) Uncut Chip Thickness:**
For $\kappa = 90°$, $\sin(\kappa) = 1$, so:
$$h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa) = 10 \cdot 1 = 10 \text{ µm}$$

**(c) Theoretical Surface Roughness:**
$$R_a = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot f^2}{R} = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot (10)^2}{0.5 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot 100}{500} = \dfrac{6.3}{500} = 0.0126 \text{ µm}$$

Wait — checking units. $f$ in µm = 10, $R$ in µm = 500.
$$R_a = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot 10^2}{500} = \dfrac{6.3}{500} = 0.0126 \text{ µm} = 12.6 \text{ nm}$$

**(d) Ploughing or Cutting?**
$$h_{min} = 0.5 \cdot r_\beta = 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1 \text{ µm}$$
Since $h = 10$ µm $\;>\; h_{min} = 1$ µm, the tool is in the **cutting regime** ✓.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** This is why micro turning uses such high spindle speeds — to maintain a high cutting speed despite the tiny diameter, which keeps the material removal rate and chip formation stable.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operating Regimes in Micro Turning

| Regime | Condition | Behaviour | Dominant Force |
|--------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Pure Ploughing** | $h \ll r_\beta$ | Material pushed aside, elastically recovers | Ploughing (F_p) |
| **Transitional** | $h \approx r_\beta$ | Mixed cutting + ploughing, surface tearing | F_s + F_p |
| **Pure Cutting** | $h \gg r_\beta$ | Clean chip formation | Shearing (F_s) |
| **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** | Low $V_c$, high adhesion | Workpiece material welds to tool tip | Adhesion-dominated |
| **Diffusion Wear** | Very high $V_c$, high temp | Atomic diffusion, tool dissolves into chip | Thermal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Micro Turning Process Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Micro Workpiece Mounted in High-Speed Collet Chuck] --> B[Spindle Drive System with Active Vibration Damping]
    B --> C[Dynamic Balancing and Runout Verification less than 1 micrometer]
    C --> D[Engagement of SCD Tool with Edge Radius r_beta]
    D --> E{Is Uncut Chip Thickness h greater than h_min}
    E -- Yes --> F[Pure Cutting Regime - Chip Forms Cleanly]
    E -- No --> G[Ploughing Regime - Elastic Recovery Dominates]
    F --> H[Surface Generation via Tool Nose Radius Geometry]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Workpiece with Ra less than 0.1 micrometer]
    I --> J[Tool Wear Monitoring via Acoustic Emission and Force Sensor]
    J --> K[Quality Inspection under Optical Profilometer]
    K --> L[Accept or Reject Part]
```

## 4.2 Size Effect and Force Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Cutting Force Fc] --> B[Shearing Component Fs]
    A --> C[Ploughing Component Fp]
    B --> D[Proportional to Uncut Chip Area A]
    C --> E[Function of Edge Radius r_beta and Elastic Modulus E]
    D --> F[Macro Dominant]
    E --> G[Micro Dominant due to Size Effect]
    F --> H[Linear Force versus Area Relation]
    G --> I[Non-linear Rise of Specific Cutting Energy]
    H --> J[Conventional Turning Behaviour]
    I --> K[Micro Turning Behaviour]
```

## 4.3 Tool Geometry Schematic (Textual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ToolGeometry [SCD Micro Turning Tool Geometry]
        A1[Tool Shank - Steel or Carbide Body] --> A2[Brazed Single Crystal Diamond Tip]
        A2 --> A3[Nose Radius R - typically 0.5 to 2.0 mm]
        A2 --> A4[Cutting Edge Radius r_beta - typically 1 to 5 micrometer]
        A2 --> A5[Rake Angle gamma - typically 0 to 5 degree for sharp edge]
        A2 --> A6[Clearance Angle alpha - typically 10 to 15 degree]
        A2 --> A7[Lead Angle kappa - typically 45 or 90 degree]
    end
```

## 4.4 Micro Turning Process Variables Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Spindle Speed N rpm] --> R1[Cutting Speed Vc m per min]
    P2[Workpiece Diameter D mm] --> R1
    R1 --> Q1[Surface Roughness Ra]
    P3[Feed f micrometer per rev] --> R2[Uncut Chip Thickness h micrometer]
    P4[Lead Angle kappa degree] --> R2
    R2 --> Q2[Cutting Regime - Cutting or Ploughing]
    P5[Tool Edge Radius r_beta] --> Q2
    P6[Depth of Cut d micrometer] --> R3[Material Removal Rate MRR]
    R1 --> R3
    P3 --> R3
    R3 --> Q3[Power Consumption Pc Watt]
    Q2 --> Q4[Tool Wear Rate]
    P7[Workpiece Hardness HV] --> Q4
    R1 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5[Tool Life and Surface Integrity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The block diagrams above represent the *functional interactions* between process variables. In the KTU 2024 examination, students may be asked to draw or describe such a process flow. The key learning point is that micro turning is **multi-variable coupled** — changing spindle speed affects cutting speed, which affects MRR, which affects power, which affects tool wear.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**Define micro turning. Mention any two distinct features that differentiate it from conventional turning. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Micro turning is a precision material removal process used to manufacture cylindrical micro-parts with diameters typically below 1 mm by rotating the workpiece against a sharp cutting tool.

**Two differentiating features:**
1. **Size Effect:** At depths of cut comparable to the tool edge radius, the cutting mechanism transitions from pure shearing to a mix of shearing and ploughing, causing non-linear rise in specific cutting energy.
2. **Ultra-High Spindle Speeds:** Micro turning uses spindles running at 10,000 to 100,000+ rpm (vs. 500–3,000 rpm in conventional turning) to maintain adequate cutting speed at sub-millimetre diameters.
3. **Tool Material:** Single-crystal diamond (SCD) or polycrystalline diamond (PCD) tools are preferred over HSS or carbide for sharp edge retention at the micro-scale.

*(Any two of the above — 1.5 marks each. Definition carries 1 mark.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is meant by the term "Size Effect" in micro machining? State its consequence on cutting force. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **Size Effect** refers to the phenomenon in which the specific cutting energy (cutting force per unit cross-sectional area of uncut chip) increases significantly as the uncut chip thickness becomes comparable to or smaller than the tool's cutting edge radius.

**Consequence on cutting force:**
- The cutting force no longer increases linearly with the uncut chip area.
- A substantial **ploughing force component** is added to the shearing force, leading to total cutting force values 2 to 10 times higher than predicted by macro-machining models.
- This causes **elastic recovery** of the workpiece, surface tearing, and poor dimensional accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable sketch, describe the **micro turning process**. Explain the role of **single-crystal diamond (SCD) tools** in micro turning and justify why they are preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **theoretical surface roughness (Ra)** in a turning operation. A brass micro-shaft of diameter **0.8 mm** is turned on a precision lathe at a spindle speed of **40,000 rpm** using a tool with nose radius **0.4 mm** and feed **5 µm/rev**. Calculate: (i) cutting speed, (ii) uncut chip thickness (assume $\kappa = 90°$), and (iii) theoretical surface roughness. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Sketch description (2 marks):**
The micro turning setup consists of:
- A micro-workpiece (e.g., brass pin) held in a collet chuck and tailstock centre.
- A high-speed spindle rotating the workpiece.
- A single-crystal diamond (SCD) tool mounted on a precision tool post with sub-micron positioning resolution.
- Sensors (force sensor, acoustic emission sensor) for in-process monitoring.
- A controller governing feed, depth, and spindle speed.

**Description of process (2 marks):**
- The workpiece is rotated at very high rpm.
- The SCD tool is fed radially (for facing) or axially (for cylindrical turning) at feed rates in the range of 1–50 µm/rev.
- Material is removed in the form of micro-chips, and the final surface is generated by the geometric profile of the tool nose.

**Why SCD tools are preferred (3 marks):**
1. **Extreme Hardness:** Diamond is the hardest known material, allowing cutting of non-ferrous metals (especially aluminium, brass, copper) without rapid wear.
2. **Sharp Edge Retention:** SCD tools can be honed to cutting edge radii as small as **0.5 µm**, which is critical in micro turning where the edge radius must be smaller than the uncut chip thickness.
3. **Low Friction Coefficient:** Reduces built-up edge (BUE) formation and improves surface finish.
4. **High Thermal Conductivity:** Dissipates cutting heat efficiently, reducing thermal damage to the micro-workpiece.
5. **Chemical Inertness with Non-Ferrous Metals:** No diffusion wear when machining aluminium, brass, and copper.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Derivation of Ra (3 marks — as shown in Section 3.2):**

$$R_a = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot f^2}{R}$$

**Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.8$ mm, $N = 40{,}000$ rpm, $R = 0.4$ mm, $f = 5$ µm/rev, $\kappa = 90°$.

**(i) Cutting Speed:**
$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.8 \cdot 40{,}000}{1000} = \dfrac{32{,}000 \pi}{1000} = 32\pi \approx 100.53 \text{ m/min}$$

**(ii) Uncut Chip Thickness:**
$$h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa) = 5 \cdot \sin(90°) = 5 \cdot 1 = 5 \text{ µm}$$

**(iii) Theoretical Surface Roughness:**
$$R_a = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot f^2}{R} = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot (5)^2}{0.4 \cdot 1000} \text{ µm} = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot 25}{400} = \dfrac{1.575}{400} = 0.00394 \text{ µm} \approx 3.94 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Such ultra-low Ra values are typical of high-quality micro turning operations on non-ferrous metals with SCD tools.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Size Effect** in micro turning with a neat diagram. Discuss its influence on **(i) cutting force, (ii) surface finish, and (iii) tool wear**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A micro turning operation is performed on an aluminium pin of diameter **1.0 mm** at a cutting speed of **100 m/min** and feed of **8 µm/rev** with a tool nose radius of **0.5 mm** and lead angle **75°**. The specific cutting energy is **0.8 N/m² × 10⁹** (i.e., 800 MPa) and the tool edge radius is **3 µm**. Determine: (i) spindle speed, (ii) uncut chip thickness, (iii) cutting force, (iv) material removal rate, and (v) verify whether the tool is in the cutting or ploughing regime. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Definition + Diagram (2 marks):**
The **Size Effect** is the phenomenon where, as the uncut chip thickness in a micro-machining process approaches the cutting edge radius of the tool, the material removal mechanism transitions from pure shearing to a combination of shearing, ploughing, and elastic recovery. (A diagram showing a tool with edge radius r_β engaging a workpiece with uncut chip thickness h, and three zones: cutting, transitional, and ploughing, should be drawn.)

**Influence on:**
**(i) Cutting Force (2 marks):**
- The cutting force no longer scales linearly with the uncut chip cross-sectional area.
- A **ploughing force component (F_p)** emerges due to friction and elastic deformation of the workpiece by the rounded tool edge.
- The total force becomes $F_c = F_s + F_p$, with $F_p$ dominating in the deep micro-scale.
- Specific cutting energy ($K_s = F_c / A$) rises sharply, often 2–10× the macro value.

**(ii) Surface Finish (1.5 marks):**
- Below $h_{min}$, the workpiece surface is **ploughed and smeared** rather than cleanly cut, leaving residual stress and micro-tearing.
- Elastic recovery of the material after the tool passes causes the surface profile to deviate from the ideal tool nose geometry.
- Ra values are typically **higher** than the theoretical $R_a = 0.063 f^2 / R$ prediction in the size effect regime.

**(iii) Tool Wear (1.5 marks):**
- The contact area between tool and workpiece is small, leading to **high local contact pressure**.
- This accelerates **adhesive wear** (especially with BUE) and **abrasive wear** at the rounded edge.
- Tool wear in micro turning is **non-uniform**, often concentrated at the nose radius region.
- The tool edge radius **grows with wear**, which further enlarges the size effect region — a positive feedback that degrades surface finish rapidly.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $D = 1.0$ mm, $V_c = 100$ m/min, $f = 8$ µm/rev, $R = 0.5$ mm, $\kappa = 75°$, $K_s = 0.8 \times 10^9$ N/m² = 800 MPa, $r_\beta = 3$ µm.

**(i) Spindle Speed:**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 100}{\pi \cdot 1.0} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{\pi} \approx 31{,}831 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Uncut Chip Thickness:**
$$h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa) = 8 \cdot \sin(75°) = 8 \cdot 0.9659 \approx 7.727 \text{ µm}$$

**(iii) Cutting Force:**
First, compute uncut chip area. Assume $d$ (depth of cut) is small; for thin micro turning, take $d \approx h$ or use given feed for radial turning. The problem does not specify depth of cut, so we assume the uncut chip area corresponds to the feed × unit depth. For simplicity, take $d = f$ for an orthogonal approximation:

$$A = f \cdot d = 8 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 8 \cdot 10^{-6} = 64 \cdot 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_c = K_s \cdot A = 0.8 \times 10^9 \cdot 64 \times 10^{-12} = 51.2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0512 \text{ N} \approx 51.2 \text{ mN}$$

*(Note to students: in exam settings, if depth of cut is not given, state the assumption clearly.)*

**(iv) Material Removal Rate:**
$$MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$$
Converting units: $V_c = 100$ m/min, $f = 8$ µm/rev = $8 \times 10^{-6}$ m/rev, $d = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m.
$$MRR = 100 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 60 = 100 \cdot 64 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 60 = 3.84 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 0.384 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**(v) Cutting or Ploughing Regime?**
$$h_{min} = 0.5 \cdot r_\beta = 0.5 \cdot 3 = 1.5 \text{ µm}$$
Since $h = 7.727$ µm $> h_{min} = 1.5$ µm, the tool is operating in the **cutting regime** ✓.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks typical):** Students often mix mm and µm in the Ra formula. Always convert to a common unit *before* substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Lead Angle (–1 to 2 marks):** In $h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa)$, students sometimes assume $\kappa = 90°$ by default. The problem may specify a different angle.
> 3. **Skipping the Regime Check (–1 mark):** Always explicitly compute $h_{min}$ and compare with $h$ to confirm cutting vs. ploughing. Examiners look for this discussion.
> 4. **Writing the formula but not the derivation (–2 marks):** For "derive" questions, the **full geometric reasoning** is required, not just the final formula.
> 5. **Ignoring the size effect discussion (–1 to 2 marks):** In any micro turning numerical, a brief comment on whether size effect is significant is rewarded.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Micro turning = sub-1 mm diameter cylindrical micro-machining on precision/ultra-precision lathes.
- [x] **Key Formula #1 (Spindle speed):** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$
- [x] **Key Formula #2 (Cutting speed):** $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$
- [x] **Key Formula #3 (Ra):** $R_a = \dfrac{0.063 \cdot f^2}{R}$
- [x] **Key Formula #4 (Chip thickness):** $h = f \cdot \sin(\kappa)$
- [x] **Key Formula #5 (Cutting force):** $F_c = K_s \cdot A = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$
- [x] **Key Formula #6 (MRR):** $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$
- [x] **Key Formula #7 (Min chip thickness):** $h_{min} \approx 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$
- [x] **Size Effect:** When $h \le r_\beta$, ploughing dominates; specific cutting energy rises sharply.
- [x] **Tool:** SCD (Single-Crystal Diamond) is the gold standard for non-ferrous micro turning; nose radius 0.5–2.0 mm; edge radius 1–5 µm.
- [x] **Spindle:** 10,000 to 100,000+ rpm; runout < 1 µm.
- [x] **Ploughing Force:** $F_p$ emerges due to friction and elastic deformation at the rounded edge; dominates in deep micro-scale.
- [x] **Shearing Force:** $F_s$ proportional to uncut chip area; dominates in macro and shallow micro-scale.
- [x] **Built-Up Edge (BUE):** Common at low cutting speeds in micro turning; degrades surface finish.
- [x] **Tool Wear Mechanisms:** Adhesion, diffusion, abrasion — diffusion dominates at high temperatures.
- [x] **Regime Check Rule:** Always verify $h > 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$ to ensure clean cutting.
- [x] **Applications:** Watch pivots, biomedical pins, MEMS shafts, fuel injector nozzles, fiber-optic mounts.
- [x] **Unit Vigilance:** $f$ in µm, $R$ in µm, $R_a$ in µm; or convert all to mm and stay consistent.
- [x] **Lead Angle:** Use $\sin(\kappa)$ in chip thickness; default 90° if unspecified.
- [x] **Cutting Power (bonus):** $P_c = F_c \cdot V_c$ (in SI units: W).
- [x] **Examiner Tip:** Always draw a labelled schematic in long-answer questions (2–3 marks reserved for figures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Module 1 Anchor:**
> Micro turning is the **bridge between conventional turning and true nano-machining**. Mastery of the size effect, the $h_{min}$ threshold, and the surface roughness formula is the *minimum* required competency for any KTU 2024 examination question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Drilling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Micro Drilling** is defined as a precision material removal process used to produce holes with diameters typically ranging from **5 μm to 1 mm** (most commonly between **50 μm and 500 μm**), with high positional accuracy, superior surface finish, and strict dimensional tolerances. The process is a scaled-down adaptation of conventional twist drilling, governed by the principle of relative motion between a rotating cutting tool (micro-drill bit) and a stationary workpiece, where material is removed primarily through shear deformation along the cutting lips.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET864)**, micro drilling is classified under *Mechanical Micro Machining Processes* and represents one of the most widely adopted non-conventional/precision conventional processes for fabricating micro-holes in components used in MEMS, biomedical devices, electronics, aerospace fuel injectors, and watch-making industries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** When the hole diameter is **less than 1 mm**, the term *micro-drilling* is used. The term *meso-drilling* refers to intermediate diameters (1–3 mm). Below **~100 μm**, the process enters the regime of *true micro-machining* where size effects dominate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to **drill a hole through a sheet of paper using a sewing needle** instead of a regular drill bit. The needle is much thinner, but you have to be extremely careful — too much force and the needle snaps; too little and it just rubs without cutting. The hole's accuracy depends entirely on the precision of your hand and the sharpness of the needle.

This is precisely what happens in **micro drilling**:
- The "sewing needle" is the **micro-drill bit** (often made of solid tungsten carbide or HSS, sometimes smaller than a human hair).
- The "paper" is your workpiece (which could be a metal, ceramic, or polymer).
- The "careful hand" is the **ultra-precision CNC machine** controlling spindle speed and feed rate down to **microns per revolution**.

The same physical principles apply — rotation + axial feed → cutting → chip formation — but the **tiny scale** introduces new challenges: tool deflection, chip evacuation (since the chips are smaller than a grain of sand!), heat accumulation, and **tool breakage** (the most common failure mode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Threshold Value:** The **critical drill diameter** below which conventional drilling becomes nearly impossible (due to tool fragility) is approximately **~5–10 μm**. Below this, processes like **micro-EDM**, **laser micro-drilling**, or **FIB** (Focused Ion Beam) are preferred.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in micro drilling specifications:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drill diameter | $D$ | 5 – 1000 | μm |
| Spindle speed | $N$ | 5,000 – 100,000 | rpm |
| Feed per revolution | $f_r$ | 0.1 – 5 | μm/rev |
| Cutting velocity | $v_c$ | 10 – 100 | m/min |
| Aspect ratio (L/D) | $\lambda$ | 1 – 30 | dimensionless |
| Surface roughness (Ra) | $R_a$ | 0.1 – 1.6 | μm |
| Hole positional accuracy | – | ± 2 – 10 | μm |
| Tool material HRC | – | 62 – 70 | Rockwell C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Constants to Memorize:** Drill diameter $D$ in **μm**, spindle speed $N$ in **rpm**, cutting speed $v_c$ in **m/min**, feed per revolution $f_r$ in **μm/rev**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of the Micro Drilling Process

The micro drilling process can be decomposed into **four sequential physical phases** that occur simultaneously at the cutting zone:

1. **Tool Workpiece Engagement:** The cutting lips of the micro-drill contact the workpiece surface. The point angle (typically **118°** for general use, **130°–140°** for harder materials) determines the geometry of initial engagement.

2. **Material Deformation and Shear:** A thin layer of material is plastically deformed ahead of each cutting lip. The shear plane angle ($\phi$) determines chip thickness.

3. **Chip Formation and Evacuation:** Chips flow up the **flute** (helical groove) of the drill. In micro drilling, flute volume is *critically small*, making **chip evacuation** a major bottleneck.

4. **Hole Generation and Exit:** As the drill advances, the chisel edge (the connecting edge between the two cutting lips) pushes material at the center. Hole quality is most compromised at **breakthrough** (drill exit), where delamination or burr can occur.

## 2.2 Process Variants of Micro Drilling

Micro drilling is broadly classified into **three families**, each governed by a different energy domain:

- **Mechanical (Conventional) Micro Drilling** — uses a solid micro-twist drill (HSS / Carbide).
- **Non-Conventional (Thermal / Electrochemical) Micro Drilling** — includes Micro-EDM, Laser micro-drilling, and Electron Beam drilling.
- **Hybrid Micro Drilling** — combines mechanical and non-conventional principles (e.g., micro-USM assisted drilling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus:** Be prepared to **compare conventional micro drilling with micro-EDM drilling** — the most frequently asked comparison in the KTU University ESE.

## 2.3 Micro Drill Bit Geometry

A micro-drill is a scaled-down version of a standard twist drill, but with extreme precision in its geometry:

- **Diameter (D):** **5 μm – 1 mm**.
- **Point Angle (2κ):** typically **118°** (general), **130°–140°** (hard materials), **90°** (PCB / soft materials).
- **Helix Angle (β):** **20°–30°** (standard), higher for softer materials (improved chip evacuation).
- **Flute Length ($L_f$):** typically $L_f \approx 5D$ to $12D$ for micro drills.
- **Chisel Edge:** The straight edge connecting the two cutting lips at the drill point. In micro drills, the chisel edge is *very short* and acts as a **ploughing tool**, generating significant thrust force.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains all **high-yield formulas** required for solving KTU numerical problems on micro drilling. All pipes use `\vert` to comply with markdown table safety.

| # | Formula | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Velocity | $$v_c = \pi D N$$ | $v_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm. **Most fundamental formula.** |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $$N = \dfrac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi D}$$ | Solve for $N$ given $D$ and $v_c$. |
| 3 | Linear Feed Rate | $$f = f_r \times N$$ | $f$ in mm/min, $f_r$ in mm/rev, $N$ in rpm. |
| 4 | Feed per Tooth (for micro-mills) | $$f_z = \dfrac{f}{Z \times N}$$ | For twist drills, $Z=2$ cutting lips. |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate | $$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f$$ | Volume of material removed per unit time (mm³/min). |
| 6 | Thrust Force (empirical) | $$F_t = K_s \times A_c$$ | $K_s$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm²), $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of cut. |
| 7 | Aspect Ratio | $$\lambda = \dfrac{L}{D}$$ | $L$ = hole depth, $D$ = drill diameter. Critical above $\lambda > 10$. |
| 8 | Chip Thinning Factor | $$r_c = \dfrac{h_m}{h_{nom}}$$ | $h_m$ = measured chip thickness, $h_{nom}$ = nominal chip thickness. |
| 9 | Specific Cutting Energy | $$K_s = \dfrac{F_t}{A_c}$$ | Depends on workpiece material; for aluminum $\approx 700$ N/mm², steel $\approx 2000$ N/mm². |
| 10 | Surface Roughness (theoretical) | $$R_a = \dfrac{f^2}{8 R}$$ | $R$ = tool nose radius. Approximate; in micro drilling, $R_a$ is dominated by runout. |
| 11 | Tool Life (Taylor's) | $$V T^n = C$$ | $V$ = cutting speed, $T$ = tool life, $C, n$ = constants. |
| 12 | Runout Effect | $$\Delta D = 2 e$$ | $e$ = radial runout (μm). Doubles the hole oversize. |
| 13 | Peck Depth (deep hole) | $$d_p = 0.5 D \text{ to } 2D$$ | Depth of each peck during retract cycle (chip evacuation). |
| 14 | Undeformed Chip Thickness | $$h = f_r \sin(\kappa)$$ | $\kappa$ = half point angle. |

### Real-World Utility

These equations are used daily in **CNC programming** for micro-drilling operations in:
- **Aerospace:** fuel injector nozzle holes (typically 100–300 μm diameter, 5–10 mm deep).
- **Medical:** microfluidic chip fabrication, surgical instrument cooling holes.
- **Electronics:** PCB via drilling, semiconductor packaging.
- **Automotive:** diesel injector spray holes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spindle Speed from Cutting Velocity

**Given:** Cutting velocity $v_c$ (m/min) and drill diameter $D$ (mm). **Find:** Spindle speed $N$ (rpm).

### Step 1: Define Cutting Velocity

The cutting velocity is the tangential speed at the outer diameter of the rotating drill. By definition of linear velocity of a rotating body:

$$v_c = \omega \times \frac{D}{2}$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in **radians per minute** and $D/2$ is the drill radius.

### Step 2: Convert Angular Velocity to RPM

Since one full revolution = $2\pi$ radians, and one minute = the time unit, the relationship between $\omega$ and $N$ is:

$$\omega = 2\pi N$$

### Step 3: Substitute

Plug the value of $\omega$ into the cutting velocity equation:

$$v_c = 2\pi N \times \frac{D}{2}$$

This simplifies to:

$$v_c = \pi D N$$

where $v_c$ is in **m/min**, $D$ in **meters**, and $N$ in **revolutions per minute**.

### Step 4: Unit Conversion to mm

In engineering practice, $D$ is given in **mm** and $v_c$ in **m/min**. To reconcile, multiply $D$ (mm) by $10^{-3}$ to convert to meters:

$$v_c = \pi \times (D \times 10^{-3}) \times N$$

Solving explicitly for $N$:

$$N = \frac{v_c}{\pi D \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000 \, v_c}{\pi D}$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{N = \frac{1000 \, v_c}{\pi D}}$$

with units: $N$ [rpm] $= \dfrac{\text{[m/min]}}{\text{[m]}} \times \dfrac{1}{\pi}$. Note the factor of 1000 arises from converting mm → m.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Micro Drilling

**Given:** Drill diameter $D$, feed per revolution $f_r$, and spindle speed $N$. **Find:** MRR in mm³/min.

### Step 1: Cross-Sectional Area of the Hole

The cross-section of material removed by the drill is a circle of diameter $D$:

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

with units mm² when $D$ is in mm.

### Step 2: Axial Feed Velocity

The drill advances axially at a rate $f$ (mm/min), given by:

$$f = f_r \times N$$

This is the **volumetric feed**, i.e., the axial length of hole produced per unit time.

### Step 3: Volume Removed per Unit Time

The volume of material removed per minute is the cross-sectional area multiplied by the axial feed:

$$\text{MRR} = A_c \times f = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r \times N$$

### Step 4: Final Form

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} f_r N}$$

**Units check:** mm² × mm/rev × rev/min = **mm³/min** ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thrust Force and Specific Cutting Energy

The thrust force $F_t$ in drilling opposes the axial feed of the drill. It is composed of contributions from:
1. The two cutting lips (shearing action).
2. The chisel edge (ploughing action).

### Step 1: Empirical Force Model

For a given workpiece material, the thrust force is empirically modeled as:

$$F_t = K_s \times A_c$$

where $K_s$ is the **specific cutting energy** (N/mm²) and $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of the uncut chip.

### Step 2: Expressing the Uncut Chip Area

For micro drilling, the uncut chip cross-section per lip is approximately:

$$A_c = f_r \times \frac{D}{2} \times \sin(\kappa)$$

For two cutting lips, total area is doubled. However, in the simplified model used in KTU problems, the total cross-sectional hole area is used:

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 3: Substituting into Force Equation

$$F_t = K_s \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 4: Solving for Specific Cutting Energy

$$\boxed{K_s = \frac{4 F_t}{\pi D^2}}$$

**Example Application (Sample KTU Problem):**  
If a micro drill of $D = 0.5$ mm experiences a thrust force of $F_t = 0.4$ N in aluminum, then:

$$K_s = \frac{4 \times 0.4}{\pi \times (0.5)^2} = \frac{1.6}{0.7854} \approx 2.04 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This value is then used in Taylor's tool life equation or to estimate power consumption.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameters on Micro Drilling Performance

| Parameter ↓ / Effect → | Hole Diameter Accuracy | Surface Finish ($R_a$) | Tool Life | Chip Evacuation | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Spindle Speed ($N$) | Decreases (runout effect amplified) | Slightly better | Decreases | Improves | – |
| ↑ Feed Rate ($f_r$) | Decreases (deflection) | Worsens (rough) | Decreases | Worsens (chip clog) | – |
| ↑ Point Angle (2κ) | Improves centering | Neutral | Improves thrust | Neutral | – |
| ↑ Helix Angle ($\beta$) | Neutral | Better | Improves | Improves (better chip flow) | – |
| ↓ Drill Diameter ($D$) | Worsens (runout dominates) | Better (smaller feed marks) | Worsens (fragile) | Worsens (flute too small) | Increases |
| ↑ Aspect Ratio ($\lambda$) | Worsens (deflection) | Worsens (vibration) | Worsens | Worsens (critical) | Increases |
| Coated tools (TiN, AlTiN) | Improves | Improves | **Significantly improves** | Neutral | Increases |
| Coolant / MQL | Improves (thermal) | Improves | Improves | Improves | Slight increase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** The **two most influential parameters** in micro drilling are (1) **drill diameter** (since tool stiffness $\propto D^4$) and (2) **feed per revolution** (since chip load = $f_r \times $ chip cross-section).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
