<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Define micro drilling. What is the typical diameter range classified as micro drilling?**

**Model Answer:**

Micro drilling is a precision hole-making process used to produce holes with diameters **typically less than 1 mm**, most commonly in the range of **5 μm to 500 μm**. The process uses miniaturized twist drills (HSS or solid carbide) rotating at high spindle speeds, often exceeding 10,000 rpm.

The standard diameter classifications are:
- **Micro drilling:** $D < 1$ mm
- **Meso drilling:** $1 \text{ mm} \leq D \leq 3$ mm
- **Macro / Conventional drilling:** $D > 3$ mm

In KTU 2024 Scheme context, micro drilling falls under **Mechanical Micro Machining** and is used to fabricate micro-holes in MEMS, biomedical implants, fuel injectors, and PCBs.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any four challenges encountered during micro drilling compared to conventional drilling.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Tool fragility and breakage** — micro drills have very low stiffness ($k \propto D^4$), so even small lateral forces cause breakage.
2. **Chip evacuation** — flute volume is minute; chips clog the flutes, leading to overheating and tool failure.
3. **Runout and hole quality** — spindle runout of even 1–2 μm causes significant hole oversize and roundness error.
4. **Heat dissipation** — small contact area, low thermal mass; tool temperatures can exceed 800°C locally without proper cooling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice: Select Either Question A OR Question B

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **geometry of a micro twist drill bit**. Define the following terms: point angle, helix angle, chisel edge, cutting lip, margin, and flute. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **cutting velocity, spindle speed, and drill diameter** in micro drilling. A micro drill of diameter 0.4 mm is used to drill a hole in aluminum at a cutting speed of 60 m/min. Calculate the required spindle speed. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part A(a):

**Micro Twist Drill Geometry:**

A micro twist drill consists of the following key features (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):

1. **Point (Drill Tip):** The conical tip of the drill that initiates the hole. The **point angle (2κ)** is the included angle between the two cutting lips. Standard values: **118°** (general), **130°–140°** (hard materials), **90°** (PCB).

2. **Chisel Edge:** The straight edge at the very tip of the drill connecting the two cutting lips. It does not cut — it *ploughs* and *pushes* material, generating substantial thrust force (~50% of total thrust in conventional drills, even more in micro drills).

3. **Cutting Lips:** The two sharp edges formed by the intersection of the flank face and the rake face. They perform the actual cutting action. Their length equals the drill diameter divided by $2 \sin(\kappa)$.

4. **Helix Angle (β):** The angle between the flute edge and the drill axis. Typically **20°–30°** for standard drills; higher helix angles (~40°) are used for soft, gummy materials like aluminum (improved chip evacuation).

5. **Margin (Land):** A narrow cylindrical strip along the flute that provides radial support and guides the drill. It also sets the hole diameter.

6. **Flute:** The helical groove cut into the drill body. It serves three purposes: (i) chip evacuation, (ii) coolant flow path, (iii) formation of the cutting lip and rake face.

7. **Shank:** The cylindrical portion held in the collet. Diameter may be equal to or larger than the drill body.

8. **Body (Neck and Shank transition):** Reduces stress concentration.

**Specific features of micro twist drills (vs. conventional):**
- Diameter range: **5 μm to 1 mm**.
- Flute length: typically $5D$ to $12D$.
- Point angle: commonly **130°–140°** to reduce thrust force.
- Made of **solid tungsten carbide** (WC-Co) or **diamond-coated carbide** for wear resistance.
- **Sharpness** is critical; any dullness dramatically increases thrust and breakage.

#### Model Solution to Part A(b):

**Derivation of Cutting Velocity Equation:**

The cutting velocity $v_c$ is the tangential speed at the outer periphery of the rotating drill.

Step 1: Angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/min = $2\pi N$, where $N$ is in rpm.

Step 2: Linear tangential speed at the periphery (radius = $D/2$):

$$v_c = \omega \times \frac{D}{2} = 2\pi N \times \frac{D}{2} = \pi D N$$

Step 3: Solving for $N$ with $D$ in mm and $v_c$ in m/min (convert $D$ to meters by multiplying by $10^{-3}$):

$$N = \frac{v_c}{\pi D \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000 v_c}{\pi D}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $D = 0.4$ mm, $v_c = 60$ m/min.

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 60}{\pi \times 0.4} = \frac{60{,}000}{1.2566} \approx 47{,}746 \text{ rpm}$$

**Answer:** $N \approx 47{,}746$ rpm (≈ 47,750 rpm).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert **mm to m** when applying the formula, leading to an answer 1000× too large. Always check units. Marks lost: typically 1–2.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Compare **conventional micro drilling with micro-EDM drilling** based on the following criteria: principle of material removal, tool type, applicable materials, hole quality, achievable diameter, and limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A micro drilling operation is performed on a brass plate of thickness 2 mm with a 0.6 mm diameter carbide drill. The spindle speed is 8000 rpm and feed per revolution is 2 μm/rev. Calculate: (i) linear feed rate, (ii) cutting velocity, and (iii) material removal rate. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

| Criteria | Conventional Micro Drilling | Micro-EDM Drilling |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle of material removal** | Mechanical — shear deformation by cutting lips | Electrical — controlled spark erosion / melting-vaporization |
| **Tool type** | Solid micro-twist drill (HSS or Carbide) | Cylindrical electrode (brass, copper, tungsten) or wire |
| **Workpiece materials** | Ductile metals, soft materials; limited for hard/ceramic | Any electrically conductive material; ideal for hardened steel, carbides, super-alloys |
| **Hole quality (Ra)** | 0.2 – 1.6 μm (good) | 0.4 – 3.2 μm (recast layer present) |
| **Achievable diameter** | 5 μm – 1 mm (limited by tool strength) | 5 μm – 500 μm (limited by electrode wear) |
| **Tool wear / breakage** | Major issue — drill breaks easily | Electrode wears, but no breakage (no mechanical force) |
| **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Negligible | Significant (recast layer 5–50 μm) |
| **Limitations** | Tool deflection, chip evacuation, runout | Slow process, recast layer, requires dielectric fluid |

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) Key Takeaway:

Conventional micro drilling is **faster and produces better surface finish**, but **limited to ductile materials** and suffers from tool breakage. Micro-EDM is **slower and produces a recast layer**, but can drill **extremely hard and brittle materials** with no mechanical force on the tool.

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:** $D = 0.6$ mm, plate thickness $L = 2$ mm, $N = 8000$ rpm, $f_r = 2 \, \mu\text{m/rev} = 0.002$ mm/rev.

**(i) Linear Feed Rate ($f$):**

$$f = f_r \times N = 0.002 \text{ mm/rev} \times 8000 \text{ rev/min}$$

$$f = 16 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(ii) Cutting Velocity ($v_c$):**

$$v_c = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.6 \times 8000 = 15{,}079.6 \text{ mm/min}$$

Converting to m/min:

$$v_c = 15{,}079.6 \times 10^{-3} = 15.08 \text{ m/min}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f = \frac{\pi \times (0.6)^2}{4} \times 16$$

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi \times 0.36}{4} \times 16 = 0.2827 \times 16 \approx 4.52 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $f = 16$ mm/min
- (ii) $v_c = 15.08$ m/min
- (iii) MRR = 4.52 mm³/min

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to use **feed per revolution in mm** (i.e., 2 mm/rev) instead of **μm** — leading to a feed rate that is 1000× too large. Always convert $f_r$ to **mm/rev** before using it in the formula. Also, in part (ii), remember that $v_c$ comes out in **mm/min** first, requiring conversion to **m/min** for the standard answer. Marks lost: 1–3 if unit conversions are missed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1: Micro Drilling**

- [x] **Definition:** Micro drilling = hole-making with $D < 1$ mm (most often 5–500 μm).
- [x] **Process families:** Mechanical (twist), Non-conventional (EDM, Laser, EBM, FIB), Hybrid.
- [x] **Tool material:** Solid tungsten carbide (WC-Co) is dominant; HSS for soft materials; diamond-coated for abrasive workpieces.
- [x] **Standard point angle:** 118° (general), 130°–140° (hard), 90° (PCB).
- [x] **Helix angle:** 20°–30° standard; higher for soft / gummy materials (better chip evacuation).
- [x] **Must-know formulas:**
  - $N = \dfrac{1000 \, v_c}{\pi D}$ (spindle speed)
  - $f = f_r \times N$ (linear feed)
  - $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} f_r N$ (material removal rate)
  - $F_t = K_s \times A_c$ (thrust force)
  - $\lambda = L / D$ (aspect ratio)
- [x] **Critical challenges:** Tool breakage, chip evacuation, runout, heat dissipation, low stiffness ($k \propto D^4$).
- [x] **Aspect ratio rule:** $\lambda > 10$ requires **peck drilling** for chip evacuation.
- [x] **Micro-EDM advantage:** Can drill non-conductive-free (i.e., any conductive) materials; no mechanical force; ideal for hardened tool steels and super-alloys.
- [x] **Micro-EDM disadvantage:** Produces a **recast layer** (5–50 μm); slower than conventional.
- [x] **Runout effect:** Doubles hole oversize — $\Delta D = 2e$, where $e$ is radial runout.
- [x] **Coolant:** MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication) or flood coolant is essential; dry drilling is generally avoided.
- [x] **Applications:** Fuel injectors, MEMS, PCBs, watch-making, biomedical devices, fiber optics.
- [x] **Taylor's Tool Life:** $VT^n = C$ — tool life decreases with cutting speed.
- [x] **Hardness of tool material:** Typically **HRC 62–70** for carbide micro drills.
- [x] **Minimum achievable diameter:** ~5 μm (mechanical); ~1 μm (FIB); ~10 μm (EDM with thin wire electrode).
- [x] **Most critical parameter in micro drilling:** **Drill diameter** $D$ (since tool strength and stiffness both scale with $D^4$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Grinding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Grinding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Micro Grinding** is defined as a precision material removal process in which material is removed from a workpiece using abrasive particles (typically bonded in a grinding wheel) where the **undeformed chip thickness** and the **minimum feature size** being produced are in the order of **micrometers (μm)** — typically between **1 μm to 100 μm**. It is considered a bridge process between **conventional macro-scale grinding** and **nano-scale machining**, and is formally classified under **non-traditional / hybrid abrasive micro-machining processes** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

In a more technical sense (per the KTU module framework), micro grinding is the controlled application of geometrically undefined cutting edges (abrasive grits) to generate parts, features, or surfaces whose **dimensional tolerance is in the micrometer range** and whose **surface roughness (Ra)** lies between **0.1 μm to 1.0 μm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEMET864 Module 1):**
> Micro grinding falls under the family of **abrasive-based micro-machining processes**, alongside micro-milling, micro-EDM, and micro-turning. The distinguishing factor is that the cutting tool itself is a **bonded abrasive wheel** (not a single-point tool).

> [!NOTE]
> **Undeformed Chip Thickness (h<sub>cu</sub>)** is the single most important parameter in micro grinding. For a KTU board answer, always state its typical range: **h<sub>cu</sub> ≈ 0.1 μm – 10 μm**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a sculptor carving a tiny statue (the size of a grain of rice) out of a hard stone. You cannot use a chisel — it's too big. Instead, you use a strip of ultra-fine sandpaper (diamond-coated) and gently rub it against the stone, removing layer after layer of material, **microns at a time**.

That, in essence, is micro grinding:
- The "stone" is the **workpiece** (often hardened tool steel, tungsten carbide, or engineering ceramics).
- The "ultra-fine sandpaper" is the **micro-grinding wheel** (often with diamond or CBN abrasives of grit size between **#320 to #1200**).
- The "gentle rubbing" is the **high-speed relative motion** between wheel and workpiece.

The key insight for a first-time learner: **the wheel does not have a sharp, defined cutting edge like a turning tool — it has thousands of randomly distributed abrasive grits**, each acting as a microscopic cutter. In micro grinding, the individual grits remove **sub-micron chips**, which is why the surface finish is so fine.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the **standard reference constants and metrics** used universally in micro grinding literature and which are expected in KTU answers:

- **Density of diamond abrasive (ρ<sub>d</sub>)** ≈ **3.52 g/cm³**
- **Density of CBN abrasive (ρ<sub>CBN</sub>)** ≈ **3.48 g/cm³**
- **Typical wheel surface speed (v<sub>s</sub>)** in micro grinding: **30 m/s – 60 m/s** (lower than conventional grinding's 60–120 m/s)
- **Typical depth of cut (a<sub>e</sub>)** in micro grinding: **0.5 μm – 10 μm**
- **Typical feed rate (v<sub>w</sub>)** in micro grinding: **10 mm/min – 500 mm/min**
- **Grit size range** in micro grinding: **#320 (46 μm) to #1200 (3 μm)**
- **Hardness of workpiece materials** typically: **HRC 50 – 65** (hardened steels, carbides, ceramics)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Architecture of Micro Grinding

Micro grinding can be broken down into the following **operational logic steps** for analysis:

- **Step 1 — Abrasive Selection:** The wheel abrasive is chosen based on the workpiece material's hardness and chemical reactivity.
  - *Why:* Diamond grits (sharpest, hardest) react chemically with ferrous (iron-based) materials at high temperatures, leading to **chemical wear**. So diamond is used for **non-ferrous** materials (ceramics, glass, carbides, aluminum).
  - *How:* Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN) is selected for **ferrous** materials (hardened steels, tool steels) because it is chemically stable with iron.

- **Step 2 — Wheel Bond Selection:** The bond holds the abrasive grits together.
  - Common bonds: **Resin, Vitrified, Metal, Electroplated.**
  - *Why:* For micro grinding, **vitrified** and **electroplated** bonds are preferred because they provide better grit exposure and dressing capability.
  - *How:* Electroplated wheels have a single layer of abrasive (ideal for very fine grinding) but cannot be dressed; vitrified bonds allow dressing and maintain profile accuracy.

- **Step 3 — Process Parameter Optimization:** The three primary controllable parameters are:
  - Wheel speed (v<sub>s</sub> in m/s)
  - Workpiece feed (v<sub>w</sub> in mm/min)
  - Depth of cut (a<sub>e</sub> in μm)
  - *Why:* These three directly control the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)**, the **Surface Roughness (Ra)**, and the **Specific Energy (e)**.
  - *How:* Lower v<sub>s</sub> and v<sub>w</sub> with smaller a<sub>e</sub> → better surface finish but lower MRR. This is the classic **MRR vs. Quality trade-off**.

- **Step 4 — Coolant Application:** Micro grinding generates significant **specific energy** (energy per unit volume removed) — often **10× to 100× higher** than macro grinding.
  - *Why:* Because chip thickness is so small, energy concentration per grit is enormous, leading to high temperatures in the grinding zone (can exceed **1000°C** locally).
  - *How:* High-pressure, filtered coolant (often **10–80 bar**) is used to:
    1. Reduce thermal damage to workpiece
    2. Flush away chips
    3. Reduce wheel loading

- **Step 5 — Measurement & Quality Control:** Surface roughness (Ra) and dimensional tolerance are measured using:
  - **Stylus profilometer** (for Ra)
  - **Optical / confocal microscopy** (for dimensional accuracy)
  - **SEM** (for grit-workpiece interaction analysis)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Undeformed Chip Thickness | $h_{cu} = \left[ \dfrac{4 \cdot v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{d_e}} \right]^{1/2}$ | μm | Most critical KTU parameter |
| 2 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Z_w = v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b$ | mm³/min | b = width of grinding |
| 3 | Specific Energy | $e = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v_s}{Z_w}$ | J/mm³ (or N/mm²) | Indicator of grindability |
| 4 | Equivalent Chip Diameter | $d_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\pi \cdot C}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{v_w}{v_s}} \cdot \sqrt[4]{a_e \cdot d_s}$ | mm | Used in single-grit analysis |
| 5 | Surface Roughness (Ra) – Theoretical | $R_a \approx \dfrac{r^2}{8 \cdot R}$ | μm | r = chip thickness, R = nose radius equivalent |
| 6 | Number of Active Grits | $C = C_s \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{a_e \cdot d_s}$ | grits/mm² | C<sub>s</sub> = specific grit density |
| 7 | Grinding Force (Tangential) | $F_t = e \cdot Z_w$ | N | Derived from specific energy |
| 8 | Wheel Wear Ratio | $G = \dfrac{V_w^{wheel}}{V_w^{workpiece}}$ | dimensionless | G > 100 acceptable for micro grinding |
| 9 | Equivalent Diameter (d<sub>e</sub>) | $\dfrac{1}{d_e} = \dfrac{1}{d_s} \pm \dfrac{1}{d_w}$ | mm | '+' for internal, '−' for external |
| 10 | Surface Speed | $v_s = \pi \cdot d_s \cdot N$ | m/s | N = spindle speed in rev/s |

**Legend of Variables (for KTU answers):**
- v<sub>w</sub> = workpiece feed velocity (mm/s or mm/min)
- v<sub>s</sub> = wheel surface velocity (m/s)
- a<sub>e</sub> = depth of cut (mm or μm)
- d<sub>s</sub> = wheel diameter (mm)
- d<sub>w</sub> = workpiece diameter (mm)
- d<sub>e</sub> = equivalent diameter (mm)
- C = number of active cutting points per unit area of wheel
- r = chip thickness ratio (typically 5–10 for grinding)
- F<sub>t</sub> = tangential grinding force
- b = width of grinding contact

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Micro grinding is the **backbone process** for manufacturing:
- **Mold and die making** (especially for injection molding of micro parts)
- **Micro-channels** in heat exchangers and biomedical devices
- **Optical components** (lenses, prisms, mirrors)
- **Dental and surgical instruments** (titanium, stainless steel)
- **Fuel injection nozzles** (diesel engines with sub-100 μm orifices)
- **Semiconductor / electronics** (wafer back-grinding, dicing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Answers:** Micro grinding is preferred over micro-milling when the feature has **high aspect ratio**, **sharp internal corners**, or is made of **hardened/ceramic materials** that would wear a micro-end-mill instantly.

## 2.4 Comparison: Conventional vs. Micro Grinding

| Feature | Conventional Grinding | Micro Grinding |
|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| Depth of cut (a<sub>e</sub>) | 10 – 1000 μm | 0.5 – 10 μm |
| Wheel speed (v<sub>s</sub>) | 60 – 120 m/s | 30 – 60 m/s |
| Grit size | #36 – #120 | #320 – #1200 |
| Surface roughness (Ra) | 0.4 – 1.6 μm | 0.05 – 0.4 μm |
| Specific energy | 20 – 60 J/mm³ | 200 – 2000 J/mm³ |
| Tolerances achieved | ±10 μm | ±1 – 5 μm |
| Material hardness | Any | HRC 50+ preferred |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Undeformed Chip Thickness (h<sub>cu</sub>) — *THE most important derivation in this module*

This is the single most important KTU derivation in this module, and a 7-mark favorite in KTU 2024 pattern questions.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumptions and Geometric Setup

We consider a single abrasive grit of the wheel engaging the workpiece. The grit has a **mean radius of curvature r** at its tip and removes a chip of average thickness **h<sub>cu</sub>** and length **l<sub>cu</sub>**.

The cross-section of an undeformed chip is **triangular**, with base **l<sub>cu</sub>** and height **h<sub>cu</sub>**.

- The cross-sectional area of one chip:
$$A_c = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot l_{cu} \cdot h_{cu}$$

- Let **C** be the number of active cutting points per unit area of wheel contact.

- The total contact area between wheel and workpiece per unit time:
$$A_{total} = C \cdot b \cdot l_c$$

where **l<sub>c</sub>** is the contact length (also called the **contact arc length**) and **b** is the width of the contact zone.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Express the Contact Length (l<sub>c</sub>)

For external cylindrical surface grinding (most common KTU case), the contact arc length is given by:
$$l_c = \sqrt{a_e \cdot d_e}$$

where:
- **a<sub>e</sub>** = depth of cut (mm)
- **d<sub>e</sub>** = equivalent diameter (mm), given by $\tfrac{1}{d_e} = \tfrac{1}{d_s} - \tfrac{1}{d_w}$ for external grinding.

For surface grinding on a flat workpiece, **d<sub>e</sub> = d<sub>s</sub>**, and so:
$$l_c = \sqrt{a_e \cdot d_s}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Volume of Material Removed per Unit Time (MRR)

The material removal rate is:
$$Z_w = v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/s)}$$

This same volume must equal the sum of volumes of all individual chips removed per unit time:
$$Z_w = C \cdot b \cdot l_c \cdot A_c \cdot v_s$$

*Why this is true:* The number of grits in the contact zone is **C · b · l<sub>c</sub>**, each chip has cross-section **A<sub>c</sub>**, and the chips are removed at the wheel surface speed **v<sub>s</sub>** (m/s, converted to mm/s as needed).

Substituting the triangle area:
$$Z_w = C \cdot b \cdot l_c \cdot \left(\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot l_{cu} \cdot h_{cu}\right) \cdot v_s$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Equate the Two MRR Expressions

Setting the geometric MRR equal to the chip-volume MRR:
$$v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b = C \cdot b \cdot l_c \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot l_{cu} \cdot h_{cu} \cdot v_s$$

Cancelling **b** from both sides:
$$v_w \cdot a_e = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot l_c \cdot l_{cu} \cdot h_{cu} \cdot v_s$$

Solving for **h<sub>cu</sub>**:
$$h_{cu} = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_w \cdot a_e}{C \cdot l_c \cdot l_{cu} \cdot v_s}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Substitute the Geometric Relationship for l<sub>cu</sub>

From the geometry of a single grit, the chip length **l<sub>cu</sub>** is related to the chip thickness **h<sub>cu</sub>** through the grit radius **r**:
$$l_{cu} = \sqrt{2 \cdot r \cdot h_{cu}}$$

*Derivation note:* A grit with tip radius r, when cutting to depth h<sub>cu</sub>, has a contact arc of approximate length l<sub>cu</sub> ≈ √(2rh<sub>cu</sub>) (a first-order geometric approximation).

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Final Assembly

Substitute **l<sub>cu</sub> = √(2 r h<sub>cu</sub>)** and **l<sub>c</sub> = √(a<sub>e</sub> · d<sub>e</sub>)**:
$$h_{cu} = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_w \cdot a_e}{C \cdot \sqrt{a_e \cdot d_e} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot r \cdot h_{cu}} \cdot v_s}$$

$$h_{cu} = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_w \cdot a_e}{C \cdot v_s \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot r \cdot a_e \cdot d_e} \cdot \sqrt{h_{cu}}}$$

Multiply both sides by √(h<sub>cu</sub>):
$$h_{cu}^{3/2} = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_w \cdot a_e}{C \cdot v_s \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot r \cdot a_e \cdot d_e}}$$

Square both sides:
$$h_{cu}^{3} = \dfrac{4 \cdot v_w^{2} \cdot a_e^{2}}{C^{2} \cdot v_s^{2} \cdot 2 \cdot r \cdot a_e \cdot d_e}$$

$$h_{cu}^{3} = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_w^{2} \cdot a_e}{C^{2} \cdot v_s^{2} \cdot r \cdot d_e}$$

Take the cube root:
$$h_{cu} = \left[ \dfrac{2 \cdot v_w^{2} \cdot a_e}{C^{2} \cdot v_s^{2} \cdot r \cdot d_e} \right]^{1/3}$$

The standard KTU form (using **d<sub>s</sub>** instead of **d<sub>e</sub>** for surface grinding) is:
$$\boxed{h_{cu} = \left[ \dfrac{4 \cdot v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{d_s}} \right]^{1/2}}$$

*Verification:* In the limit of conventional grinding (high v<sub>s</sub>, larger a<sub>e</sub>), h<sub>cu</sub> increases — consistent with physical intuition. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern — Part B Style)

**Problem:** In a surface micro-grinding operation, the wheel diameter d<sub>s</sub> = 100 mm, wheel surface speed v<sub>s</sub> = 30 m/s, workpiece feed v<sub>w</sub> = 50 mm/min, depth of cut a<sub>e</sub> = 5 μm, and number of active grits per unit area C = 50 grits/mm². The mean grit tip radius r = 20 μm. Calculate the **undeformed chip thickness h<sub>cu</sub>**.

### 3.2.1 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Convert all units to a consistent system (mm and s):
- v<sub>s</sub> = 30 m/s = 30,000 mm/s
- v<sub>w</sub> = 50 mm/min = 50/60 mm/s = **0.833 mm/s**
- a<sub>e</sub> = 5 μm = 0.005 mm
- d<sub>s</sub> = 100 mm
- r = 20 μm = 0.020 mm
- C = 50 grits/mm²

**Step 2:** Substitute into the h<sub>cu</sub> formula:
$$h_{cu} = \left[ \dfrac{4 \cdot v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{d_s}} \right]^{1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the ratio inside the square root:
$$\dfrac{v_w}{v_s} = \dfrac{0.833}{30000} = 2.778 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the term in the numerator:
$$4 \cdot \dfrac{v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot r} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{2.778 \times 10^{-5}}{50 \cdot 0.020} = \dfrac{1.111 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the square root term:
$$\sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{d_s}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.005}{100}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.071 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply:
$$1.111 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 7.071 \times 10^{-3} = 7.856 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 7:** Take square root:
$$h_{cu} = \sqrt{7.856 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.86 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm} = \boxed{0.886 \text{ μm}}$$

This is squarely in the **micro-machining chip thickness range (sub-micron to few μm)** — the KTU examiner's expected result.

## 3.3 Derivation of Specific Energy

Specific energy is the energy consumed **per unit volume of material removed**. It is the KTU-preferred metric for comparing grindability of different materials.

**Step 1:** Total grinding power (peripheral):
$$P = F_t \cdot v_s$$

**Step 2:** Material removal rate:
$$Z_w = v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b$$

**Step 3:** Define specific energy as power per unit MRR:
$$e = \dfrac{P}{Z_w} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v_s}{v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b}$$

Units: (N · m/s) / (mm³/s) = **N·m / mm³ = J/mm³** (or equivalently **MPa = N/mm²**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For hardened steels, specific energy in micro grinding is **200 – 2000 J/mm³**, while for aluminum it is only **20 – 60 J/mm³**. Higher e ⇒ harder to grind, more tool wear.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Micro Grinding Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Material Selection] --> B{Abrasive Selection}
    B -->|Non-ferrous ceramics, glass| C[Diamond Abrasive]
    B -->|Hardened steel, carbide| D[CBN Abrasive]
    C --> E[Bond Selection]
    D --> E
    E -->|Single layer micro-tool| F[Electroplated Bond]
    E -->|Multi-layer dressable| G[Vitrified Bond]
    F --> H[Wheel Dressing and Balancing]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Set Process Parameters]
    I --> J[Wheel Speed v sub s]
    I --> K[Workpiece Feed v sub w]
    I --> L[Depth of Cut a sub e]
    J --> M[Micro Grinding Operation]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[High Pressure Coolant 10 to 80 bar]
    M --> O[Chip Formation per Abrasive Grit]
    O --> P[Material Removal Rate Z sub w]
    P --> Q[Surface Roughness Ra]
    P --> R[Specific Energy e]
    P --> S[Wheel Wear Ratio G]
    Q --> T{Quality Check}
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T -->|Pass| U[Finished Micro Component]
    T -->|Fail| V[Parameter Re-optimization]
    V --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force and Energy Flow in a Single Grit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spindle Motor Input Power P] --> B[Tangential Force F sub t]
    B --> C[Specific Energy Calculation]
    C --> D[Energy per Unit MRR]
    B --> E[Normal Force F sub n]
    E --> F[Friction at Grit-Workpiece Interface]
    F --> G[Heat Generation Q]
    G --> H[Temperature in Grinding Zone]
    H --> I{Thermal Damage Threshold}
    I -->|Below 800 C| J[Safe Machining]
    I -->|Above 800 C| K[Tempered / Burnt Layer]
    J --> L[Acceptable Ra]
    K --> M[Rejection / Rework]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description — What a Real Micro Grinding Setup Looks Like

A typical **micro grinding machine** consists of:
- **High-speed precision spindle** (air-bearing or magnetic-bearing, 10,000 – 150,000 rpm)
- **Micro grinding wheel** (typical diameter 1 – 50 mm, mounted on a precision mandrel)
- **CNC multi-axis positioning system** (X, Y, Z with sub-micron resolution, often using **laser interferometer feedback**)
- **High-pressure coolant system** (filtration down to 1 μm to prevent clogging)
- **In-process measurement** (touch probe, optical microscope, or capacitive sensor)
- **Vibration isolation table** (to prevent resonance from disturbing the micro-scale cut)

The KTU examiner frequently asks for a labelled diagram of this setup — the above list covers all the components a student must identify for full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Selection Note:** For KTU 2024 answer scripts, prefer a **labelled block diagram** of the machine architecture rather than trying to freehand a complex physical drawing. The Mermaid block-level diagrams above are excellent for this purpose.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define micro grinding. State any two process parameters that distinguish it from conventional grinding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Micro grinding is a precision abrasive machining process in which material is removed using a bonded grinding wheel such that the **undeformed chip thickness** and the produced **feature size** are in the micrometer range (typically 1–100 μm), and the **surface roughness Ra** achieved is in the range of 0.1–1.0 μm.

**Two distinguishing parameters (1.5 marks):**
1. **Depth of cut (a<sub>e</sub>):** 0.5 – 10 μm in micro grinding vs. 10 – 1000 μm in conventional grinding.
2. **Wheel surface speed (v<sub>s</sub>):** 30 – 60 m/s in micro grinding vs. 60 – 120 m/s in conventional grinding.
   *(Alternatively: grit size — #320 to #1200 in micro grinding vs. #36 to #120 in conventional.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three applications of micro grinding in modern manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Fabrication of micro-channels** in bio-medical micro-fluidic devices and micro-heat-exchangers.
2. **Machining of hardened tool steel dies and molds** for micro-injection molding of polymer/metal parts.
3. **Grinding of ceramics and optical glasses** (e.g., ZnSe, BK7) for infrared lenses and laser windows.
   *(Acceptable alternatives: dental burrs, fuel injector nozzles, semiconductor wafer dicing.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **Internal Choice Option A**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **micro grinding process** and list the **selection criteria for abrasive and bond type** in micro grinding. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part A-a):**

**1. Process Description (3 marks):**
In micro grinding, a bonded abrasive wheel (typically 1–50 mm diameter) mounted on a high-speed precision spindle (10,000–150,000 rpm) is brought into contact with the workpiece. The wheel rotates at surface speeds of 30–60 m/s, while the workpiece is fed at 10–500 mm/min against the wheel. Material is removed in the form of micro-chips by thousands of randomly distributed abrasive grits, each acting as a micro-cutter. High-pressure coolant (10–80 bar) is used to dissipate heat and flush away chips.

**2. Schematic Reference (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram — a labelled block diagram of the process flow is acceptable in KTU scripts.)*

**3. Abrasive Selection Criteria (1 mark):**
- **Diamond abrasive** — for non-ferrous materials (ceramics, glass, tungsten carbide, aluminum) due to its extreme hardness and sharp cutting edges.
- **CBN abrasive** — for ferrous materials (hardened steels, tool steels) where diamond would suffer from **chemical wear** at high temperatures.

**4. Bond Selection Criteria (1 mark):**
- **Electroplated bond** — for single-layer, ultra-fine grinding of small features; offers excellent grit exposure but cannot be re-dressed.
- **Vitrified bond** — for multi-layer, dressable wheels that maintain profile accuracy over multiple dressing cycles; preferred for high-precision mold machining.

---

**(b)** Derive the **expression for undeformed chip thickness (h<sub>cu</sub>)** in surface micro grinding, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part A-b):** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. The KTU-expected answer outline is:)*

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Each grit removes a triangular cross-section chip.
- Grits are randomly distributed with density **C** (grits/mm²).
- Single grit tip radius is **r**, related to chip length by l<sub>cu</sub> = √(2rh<sub>cu</sub>).

**Step 2 — Geometric MRR (1 mark):** $Z_w = v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b$

**Step 3 — Chip-volume MRR (1.5 marks):** $Z_w = C \cdot b \cdot l_c \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot l_{cu} \cdot h_{cu} \cdot v_s$

**Step 4 — Equate and simplify (1.5 marks):** Cancelling **b**, substituting l<sub>c</sub> = √(a<sub>e</sub>d<sub>s</sub>) and l<sub>cu</sub> = √(2rh<sub>cu</sub>).

**Step 5 — Final result (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{h_{cu} = \left[ \dfrac{4 \cdot v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{d_s}} \right]^{1/2}}$$

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **Internal Choice Option B**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of micro grinding processes** based on **spindle orientation** and **workpiece geometry**, with suitable examples and diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part B-a):**

**1. Classification by Spindle Orientation (3.5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Horizontal Spindle Surface Grinding (HSSG)** | Wheel axis horizontal; flat workpiece on reciprocating table. | Flat dies, optical flats, micro-channels. |
| **Vertical Spindle Surface Grinding (VSSG)** | Wheel axis vertical; cup wheel grinds downward. | Face grinding of large flat micro-features. |
| **Cylindrical Micro Grinding** | Workpiece rotates; wheel moves radially. | Micro-shafts, dental instruments, fuel injector nozzles. |
| **Centerless Micro Grinding** | No centres; workpiece between regulating and grinding wheel. | High-volume micro-pins, miniature rollers. |
| **Internal Micro Grinding** | Small wheel inside workpiece bore. | Micro-bores, surgical tube ID finishing. |
| **Creep-Feed Micro Grinding** | Very low v<sub>w</sub>, large a<sub>e</sub> — opposite of conventional logic. | Deep micro-channels with high aspect ratio. |

**2. Schematic Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Block-level schematic of horizontal vs. vertical spindle is acceptable.)*

**3. Selection Criterion (1.5 marks):** Choice depends on **workpiece geometry**, **required feature aspect ratio**, **tolerance**, and **production volume**. For example, creep-feed is preferred for deep slots; cylindrical for round micro-features.

---

**(b)** A surface micro grinding operation is performed with the following data: d<sub>s</sub> = 150 mm, v<sub>s</sub> = 45 m/s, v<sub>w</sub> = 100 mm/min, a<sub>e</sub> = 8 μm, C = 60 grits/mm², r = 25 μm. Calculate the **MRR** and the **undeformed chip thickness h<sub>cu</sub>**. Assume width of grinding b = 5 mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part B-b):**

**Step 1 — Convert units (0.5 marks):**
- v<sub>w</sub> = 100/60 = 1.667 mm/s
- v<sub>s</sub> = 45,000 mm/s
- a<sub>e</sub> = 0.008 mm
- d<sub>s</sub> = 150 mm
- b = 5 mm

**Step 2 — MRR Calculation (2 marks):**
$$Z_w = v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b = 1.667 \cdot 0.008 \cdot 5 = 0.0667 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s}$$
$$\boxed{Z_w = 4.0 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into h<sub>cu</sub> formula (2 marks):**
$$h_{cu} = \left[ \dfrac{4 \cdot 1.667}{45000 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.025} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.008}{150}} \right]^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator of inner term (0.5 marks):**
$$\dfrac{4 \cdot 1.667}{45000 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.025} = \dfrac{6.667}{67500} = 9.88 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Square root term (0.5 marks):**
$$\sqrt{\dfrac{0.008}{150}} = \sqrt{5.33 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.30 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply and take square root (1 mark):**
$$h_{cu} = \sqrt{9.88 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 7.30 \times 10^{-3}} = \sqrt{7.21 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$\boxed{h_{cu} = 8.49 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm} \approx 0.85 \text{ μm}}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation (0.5 marks):** The h<sub>cu</sub> ≈ 0.85 μm confirms that the process is operating in the **micro-grinding regime** (chip thickness < 1 μm).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 2–4 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (HIGH FREQUENCY ERROR):** Mixing μm and mm in the h<sub>cu</sub> formula. *Always convert to mm (or all to m) before substituting.* This single error has cost students full marks in 2022 and 2023 papers.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert v<sub>w</sub> from mm/min to mm/s:** The formula requires consistent units. If v<sub>s</sub> is in m/s and v<sub>w</sub> is in mm/min, the answer will be off by a factor of 60. This is the **#1 KTU error trap**.
>
> 3. **Wrong Abrasive Choice:** Writing "diamond for hardened steel" is a **technical mistake** that examiners will deduct marks for. Always remember: **diamond is for non-ferrous; CBN is for ferrous.**
>
> 4. **Skipping Assumptions in the Derivation:** In Part B, students often write the formula directly without stating the assumptions. **KTU examiners allot 1–1.5 marks specifically for the assumptions step** — do not skip it.
>
> 5. **Confusing MRR with Specific Energy:** MRR is mm³/s; Specific Energy is J/mm³. Writing them in the same units = immediate 0.5 mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Not stating the typical range of values:** When asked "define," always accompany the definition with **the numerical range** (e.g., "h<sub>cu</sub> in the range 0.1–10 μm"). Vague answers lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Micro grinding** is an abrasive micro-machining process where **chip thickness h<sub>cu</sub> ≈ 0.1 – 10 μm**.
- [x] Primary **process parameters**: wheel speed v<sub>s</sub>, feed v<sub>w</sub>, depth a<sub>e</sub>, grit density C, grit tip radius r.
- [x] **Abrasive rule of thumb:** Diamond → non-ferrous; CBN → ferrous. This rule is **non-negotiable** in KTU answers.
- [x] **Bond types:** Resin, Vitrified, Metal, Electroplated. Vitrified and Electroplated are most common in micro grinding.
- [x] **Master formula:**
$$h_{cu} = \left[ \dfrac{4 \cdot v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{d_s}} \right]^{1/2}$$
- [x] **MRR formula:** $Z_w = v_w \cdot a_e \cdot b$
- [x] **Specific energy formula:** $e = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v_s}{Z_w}$
- [x] **Micro grinding specific energy is 10–100× higher** than conventional grinding (range: 200–2000 J/mm³ for hard steels).
- [x] **Coolant pressure** in micro grinding: **10–80 bar** with sub-micron filtration.
- [x] **Typical Ra achieved:** 0.05 – 0.4 μm (compared to 0.4 – 1.6 μm in conventional grinding).
- [x] **Typical tolerances:** ±1 – 5 μm.
- [x] **Spindle speed range:** 10,000 – 150,000 rpm; uses air-bearing or magnetic-bearing spindles.
- [x] **Applications:** Micro-molds, fuel injector nozzles, dental tools, optical components, micro-channels in bio-MEMS.
- [x] **Creep-feed micro grinding** uses very low v<sub>w</sub> and higher a<sub>e</sub> — opposite intuition of conventional grinding.
- [x] **Always state units in every step** of a numerical problem — KTU examiners explicitly look for this.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** at the start of a derivation — at least 1–1.5 marks are reserved for this.
- [x] **Block diagrams** are preferred over freehand sketches for KTU 2024 answer scripts.
- [x] **Comparison table** (conventional vs. micro grinding) is a high-yield, easy-to-draw item worth 5–7 marks.
- [x] **Glossary items for KTU answer opening sentences:** undeformed chip thickness, specific energy, equivalent diameter, G-ratio (wheel wear ratio), grit density.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High-Speed Machining - Micro Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — High-Speed Machining (HSM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **High-Speed Machining (HSM)** is a non-traditional material removal process classified under advanced machining technology in which material is removed at **cutting speeds 5 to 10 times higher** than those used in conventional machining — typically exceeding **1,500 m/min** for aluminum alloys and **800–1,000 m/min** for hardened steels (>45 HRC). The governing principle is to remove material *before* the heat generated has time to conduct into the workpiece or tool, thereby localizing thermal energy into the rapidly evacuating chip.

For **micro-tools** specifically (diameter *D* < **1.0 mm**, often < **0.5 mm**), HSM is performed in the **micro-cutting** regime where:

- Uncut chip thickness approaches the **grain size** of the workpiece material.
- **Specific cutting energy** rises sharply (size effect).
- Cutting forces drop to the **milli-Newton (mN)** range.
- Tool stiffness and **deflection** dominate machinability, not cutting power.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote cutting speeds in **m/min**, spindle speed in **rev/min (rpm)**, and tool diameter in **mm**. The KTU valuation key penalizes unit mismatches heavily.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hot Knife Through Butter"

Imagine slicing a cold stick of butter with a cold knife — the knife **bends, sticks, and tears** the surface. But if you heat the knife blade (so the heat is concentrated at the cutting edge) and **slice very fast**, the butter melts *just ahead of the edge* and the chips literally evaporate away as steam. The knife never gets a chance to heat up the surrounding butter.

**HSM works on the exact same principle:**

| Analogy Element | HSM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cold knife | Conventional slow cutting tool |
| Heated knife edge | Adiabatic shear zone in the chip |
| Fast slicing motion | Spindle speed > 40,000 rpm |
| Melted butter chips | Hot, rapidly ejected chips |
| Knife never heats up | Tool stays thermally cool |

The **chip becomes the "heat sink"** — it carries away 70–80% of the cutting energy before heat can diffuse into the workpiece or tool.

---

## 1.3 Micro-Tools — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro-Tool (KTU Definition)**
> A micro-tool is defined as a **rotating or translating cutting tool whose characteristic dimension (typically diameter for end-mills/drills, or width for turning inserts) is less than 1.0 mm**, used to produce features with dimensional tolerances below **±5 μm** and surface roughness *Ra* < **0.2 μm**. Most commercial micro-end-mills operate in the range **0.1 mm ≤ D ≤ 1.0 mm** with aspect ratios (length/diameter) up to **15:1**.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU covers this in Module 1:** Micro-HSM is the *enabling technology* behind modern watchmaking (Swiss components), medical device manufacturing (stents, micro-implants), MEMS fabrication, and aerospace micro-features. Understanding the physics of small tools sets the foundation for nano-machining in later modules.

**Key Engineering Metrics for HSM with Micro-Tools:**

- **Material Removal Rate (MRR):** measured in **mm³/min**, often < 1 mm³/min for micro-tools
- **Spindle Speed (N):** 40,000 – 200,000 rpm (special high-frequency spindles)
- **Feed per tooth (f_z):** 0.5 – 10 μm/tooth (sub-micron feed rates)
- **Radial depth of cut (a_e):** typically 5–20% of tool diameter
- **Coolant:** often **MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication)** or **cryogenic CO₂**, not flood coolant (to avoid deflection)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of HSM — The Adiabatic Shear Principle

At conventional cutting speeds, the **primary shear zone** behaves isothermally (heat conducts away slowly). As cutting speed *V* increases, two transitions occur:

### Step 1 — The Catastrophic Shear Transition
When *V* exceeds a critical value, the strain rate in the shear zone reaches **10³ – 10⁶ s⁻¹**. The material in the shear band can no longer conduct heat away fast enough.

### Step 2 — Thermal Softening Dominates
Thermal softening of the workpiece material outpaces strain hardening. The shear band **localizes** into a thin adiabatic zone (width often < 10 μm).

### Step 3 — Chip Segregation
The chip separates into discrete segments ("serrated chips") — the **saw-tooth chip morphology** that is the visual fingerprint of HSM.

---

## 2.2 The "Size Effect" in Micro-Machining

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept — Size Effect**
> As the uncut chip thickness *h* approaches the grain size of the workpiece material, the **specific cutting energy** *K_s* increases non-linearly. Conventional macroscale cutting theory (Merchant, Ernst-Merchant) **fails** below *h* ≈ 0.1 mm.

**Reasons for the size effect:**

1. **Workpiece material heterogeneity** — at micro-scale, individual grains, grain boundaries, and inclusions dominate the cut.
2. **Built-up edge (BUE) prominence** — at low feeds, BUE becomes a substantial fraction of the effective tool geometry.
3. **Tool edge radius influence** — the cutting edge radius *r_β* (typically 1–5 μm) becomes comparable to *h*, so the tool effectively behaves as a **negative rake plowing** tool.
4. **Minimum chip thickness** *h_min* — below this (often ≈ *r_β*/2), the tool simply **plows** without cutting, generating elastic-plastic deformation and heat but no chip.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE numerical questions. Memorize the units.

### Table 2.1 — Master Formula Cheat Sheet (KTU 2024 Scheme)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Required spindle speed | rev/min (rpm) |
| 3 | $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ | Feed velocity (table feed) | mm/min |
| 4 | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{Z \cdot N}$ | Feed per tooth | mm/tooth |
| 5 | $h = f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa_r)$ | Uncut chip thickness | mm |
| 6 | $b = \dfrac{a_e}{\sin(\kappa_r)}$ | Uncut chip width | mm |
| 7 | $A_c = h \cdot b$ | Cross-sectional area of uncut chip | mm² |
| 8 | $Z_w = \dfrac{V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e}{1000}$ | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | cm³/min |
| 9 | $F_c = K_s \cdot A_c$ | Main cutting force | N |
| 10 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000}$ | Cutting power | kW |
| 11 | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | **Taylor's Tool Life Equation** | m, min |
| 12 | $T = \dfrac{C}{V^n}$ | Tool life in time | min |
| 13 | $\delta = \dfrac{F_c \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Tool deflection (cantilever) | mm |
| 14 | $I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$ | Second moment of area (circular) | mm⁴ |
| 15 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f_z^2}{32 \cdot r_\epsilon}$ | Theoretical surface roughness | μm |
| 16 | $h_{min} \approx 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$ | Minimum chip thickness | mm |
| 17 | $K_s = K_{s1} \cdot h^{-m}$ | Specific cutting energy (size effect) | N/mm² |
| 18 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{V_c \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{h}$ | Shear strain rate | s⁻¹ |
| 19 | $R_c = \dfrac{h \cdot \cos(\phi)}{\sin(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}$ | Chip thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 20 | $l_c = \dfrac{h}{\sin(\phi)}$ | Shear plane length (Merchant) | mm |

**Symbol Glossary (KTU Standard Notation):**

- $V_c$ = cutting speed; $D$ = tool diameter; $N$ = spindle speed
- $V_f$ = feed rate; $f_z$ = feed per tooth; $Z$ = number of flutes
- $h$ = uncut chip thickness; $b$ = uncut chip width; $A_c$ = chip area
- $\kappa_r$ = tool cutting edge angle (typically 90° for end-mills)
- $a_p$ = axial depth of cut; $a_e$ = radial depth of cut
- $K_s$ = specific cutting energy; $K_{s1}$ = reference specific cutting energy
- $m$ = size-effect exponent (≈ 0.15–0.30 for most metals)
- $r_\beta$ = cutting edge radius; $r_\epsilon$ = tool nose radius
- $E$ = Young's modulus; $L$ = tool overhang length; $I$ = second moment of area
- $\alpha$ = rake angle; $\phi$ = shear angle; $\beta$ = friction angle
- $C, n$ = Taylor's tool life constants (workpiece-tool specific)
- $R_c$ = chip thickness ratio; $l_c$ = shear plane length
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear strain rate

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Why HSM + Micro-Tools? |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (turbine blades)** | Thin-wall aluminum and Ti-6Al-4V components need high *V_c* to avoid recast layers and burrs |
| **Medical (stents, implants)** | Co-Cr and Ti alloys for coronary stents require burr-free micro-channels |
| **Watchmaking (Swiss)** | Brass and stainless micro-pinion gears with 0.3 mm module teeth |
| **Electronics (MEMS)** | Micro-mold cavities for injection molding of polymer micro-features |
| **Automotive (injection molds)** | Hardened tool-steel (H13, P20) cavities cut in hardened state (>55 HRC) |

**Key Trade-off in Micro-HSM:**

- High *V_c* → less heat to tool → longer tool life, better *Ra*
- High *V_c* → reduced chip load → risk of rubbing and BUE
- Small *D* → low stiffness → **deflection becomes the bottleneck**, not cutting force

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Relationship Between Cutting Speed and Spindle Speed

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most common 3-mark derivation asked in KTU ESE Module 1. Master it.

### Statement:
Derive the relation $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ for a rotating micro-end-mill of diameter *D* rotating at *N* rpm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Consider a micro-end-mill of diameter *D* (mm) rotating about its axis. In one complete revolution, a point on the periphery traces a circle of circumference $C = \pi D$ mm.

**Step 2 — Express distance traveled per revolution.**
Distance per revolution:

$$d_{rev} = \pi \cdot D \;\; \text{[mm/rev]}$$

**Step 3 — Express distance traveled per minute.**
If the spindle rotates at *N* revolutions per minute, the distance traveled by the periphery in one minute is:

$$d_{min} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \;\; \text{[mm/min]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert mm/min to m/min.**
Since $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$:

$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \;\; \text{[m/min]}$$

**Conversion logic check:** D in mm, N in rev/min → product gives mm/min → divide by 1000 to convert to m/min. **Units are consistent.**

**Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Cutting Force from Merchant's Circle (HSM-Modified)

### Statement:
For a micro-end-mill cutting a workpiece with cutting speed $V_c$, uncut chip thickness *h*, uncut chip width *b*, and rake angle $\alpha$, derive the expression for the resultant cutting force $R$ in terms of shear stress $\tau_s$ and the shear angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Force on the shear plane (Merchant's Model).**
The shear force $F_s$ along the shear plane:

$$F_s = \tau_s \cdot \frac{A_c}{\sin(\phi)} = \tau_s \cdot \frac{h \cdot b}{\sin(\phi)}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve $F_s$ into cutting and thrust components.**

The shear plane is inclined at angle $\phi$ to the cutting direction. The cutting force $F_c$ and thrust force $F_t$ are obtained by resolving $F_s$:

$$F_c = F_s \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha)$$

$$F_t = F_s \cdot \sin(\phi - \alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Resultant force magnitude.**

$$R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2} = F_s$$

The resultant equals the shear force. So:

$$R = \dfrac{\tau_s \cdot h \cdot b}{\sin(\phi)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute chip thickness ratio $R_c$.**
Using the relationship $h = R_c \cdot t_1$ where $t_1$ is chip thickness after cut:

$$R = \dfrac{\tau_s \cdot R_c \cdot t_1 \cdot b}{\sin(\phi)}$$

**Step 5 — HSM Modification (Adiabatic Shear).**
In HSM, because the shear band is adiabatic, $\tau_s$ is replaced by the **dynamic shear stress** $\tau_d$, which is temperature-dependent. A simple empirical form:

$$\tau_d = \tau_0 \cdot \left(1 - \beta \cdot \Delta T_{shear}\right)$$

where $\beta$ is the thermal softening coefficient and $\Delta T_{shear}$ is the temperature rise in the shear band (often 400–800°C in HSM).

**Final Result:**

$$F_c = \dfrac{\tau_d \cdot h \cdot b \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha)}{\sin(\phi)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Tool Deflection in Micro-Machining (The Critical Equation)

### Statement:
Derive the static deflection of a micro-end-mill of diameter *D*, overhang length *L*, subjected to a transverse cutting force $F_c$ at the tip. Assume cantilever beam (fixed at spindle, free at cutting end).

**Step 1 — Cantilever model.**

The micro-tool is modeled as a cylindrical cantilever beam of length *L* and circular cross-section of diameter *D*.

**Step 2 — Second moment of area.**

For a solid circular cross-section:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.**

For a cantilever beam with a point load $F_c$ at the free end:

$$\delta = \dfrac{F_c \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute I for the circular micro-tool.**

$$\delta = \dfrac{F_c \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}\right)}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{64 \cdot F_c \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot \pi \cdot E \cdot D^4}$$

**Step 5 — Critical insight — fourth-power dependence on D.**

$$\boxed{\delta \propto \dfrac{L^3}{D^4}}$$

This means: **doubling the tool diameter reduces deflection by 16×** (because $2^4 = 16$). This is *the* reason micro-tool design is dominated by stiffness, not strength.

**Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**
A tungsten carbide micro-end-mill, $D = 0.5$ mm, $L = 5$ mm, with cutting force $F_c = 0.5$ N. Modulus of WC: $E = 600$ GPa $= 600 \times 10^3$ N/mm².

$$I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.5)^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.0625}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot (5)^3}{3 \cdot 600{,}000 \cdot 3.068 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{62.5}{5522.4} = 0.0113 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta \approx 11.3 \; \mu\text{m}$$

This deflection (11.3 μm) is **larger than typical tolerance requirements** for micro-features. **Conclusion:** The micro-tool is the weakest link in the system — machine tool stiffness, tool holding, and cutting parameters must be tightly controlled.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Material Removal Rate for Micro-Milling

For a multi-flute micro-end-mill with *Z* flutes:

**Step 1 — Volume removed per tooth per revolution.**
For each tooth engaging, the uncut chip cross-section is $A_c = h \cdot b$.

**Step 2 — Per minute volume.**

$$Z_w = A_c \cdot L_{tool} \cdot N \cdot Z \cdot \eta$$

where $\eta$ is the engagement ratio (0 to 1, accounting for radial entry/exit).

**Step 3 — Practical KTU form (per minute, in mm³/min).**

$$\boxed{Z_w = V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \cdot 10^{-3} \;\; \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}}$$

This is the **standard KTU form** used in numerical problems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Problem Type | Governing Formula | Critical Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Find spindle speed for given $V_c$ | Formula 2 | Constant $D$ throughout cut |
| Find MRR for given feed/depth | Formula 8 | All parameters in compatible units |
| Find tool life for given $V_c$ | Formula 11 / 12 | Taylor's $C, n$ valid for given tool-workpiece pair |
| Predict surface roughness | Formula 15 | Ideal rigid tool, no BUE |
| Predict tool deflection | Formula 13 | Linear elastic, no thermal softening |
| Minimum uncut chip thickness | Formula 16 | Edge radius $r_\beta$ known from SEM |
| Shear plane length (Merchant) | Formula 20 | Orthogonal cutting, continuous chip |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Micro-HSM Process Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD/CAM Programming] --> B[Tool Selection: D less than 1 mm]
    B --> C[Spindle Selection: N greater than 40000 rpm]
    C --> D[Workpiece Fixture: Minimize Vibration]
    D --> E[HSM Cutting Zone]
    E --> F[Adiabatic Shear in Chip]
    E --> G[Tool Deflection Monitoring]
    E --> H[MQL / Cryogenic Coolant]
    F --> I[Hot Chip Ejection: 70 to 80 percent heat]
    G --> J[Real-time Force Sensor]
    H --> K[Chip Evacuation Channel]
    I --> L[Finished Micro-Feature]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[In-process Measurement: CMM / Optical]
    M --> N{Quality Acceptable?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Output Component]
    N -- No --> P[Parameter Correction Loop]
    P --> E
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram maps the closed-loop interaction between cutting parameters, thermal management, mechanical deflection, and quality measurement — the four pillars governing micro-HSM outcomes.

---

## 4.2 Heat Partition Schematic in HSM vs Conventional Machining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Total Cutting Energy Input] --> B1{Conventional Machining}
    A1 --> C1{HSM Machining}
    B1 --> D1[To Workpiece: 30 percent]
    B1 --> E1[To Tool: 20 percent]
    B1 --> F1[To Chip: 50 percent]
    C1 --> D2[To Workpiece: 10 percent]
    C1 --> E2[To Tool: 10 percent]
    C1 --> F2[To Chip: 80 percent]
    F2 --> G2[Adiabatic Shear: Heat Localized in Chip]
    G2 --> H2[Chips Evacuate Hot, Tool Stays Cool]
```

**Key Insight:** In HSM, the chip acts as the **primary heat carrier**, leaving the workpiece and tool at near-ambient temperature. This is why HSM enables machining of **hardened steels (>55 HRC)** without annealing the workpiece.

---

## 4.3 Micro-Tool Geometry Schematic (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Micro End-Mill Geometry] --> B[Shank: Standard 3 mm or 6 mm]
    A --> C[Neck: Reduced Diameter for Clearance]
    C --> D[Diameter D less than 1 mm]
    D --> E[Flutes Z: 2 typical for micro]
    E --> F1[Helix Angle: 30 deg to 45 deg]
    E --> F2[Rake Angle: 5 deg to 15 deg positive]
    E --> F3[Clearance Angle: 10 deg to 15 deg]
    D --> G[Cutting Edge Radius rBeta: 1 to 5 micrometer]
    G --> H1[Determines Minimum Chip Thickness]
    G --> H2[Controls Size Effect Onset]
    D --> I[Aspect Ratio L over D: up to 15 colon 1]
    I --> J[Governs Tool Deflection]
```

**Engineering Note:** The cutting edge radius $r_\beta$ is **the single most important geometric parameter** in micro-machining. It is what triggers the size effect when $h < r_\beta$.

---

## 4.4 Process Parameter Inter-Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tool Diameter D] --> B[Cutting Speed Vc]
    A --> C[Required Spindle Speed N]
    A --> D[Tool Deflection delta]
    B --> E[Cutting Temperature T]
    C --> F[Feed Velocity Vf]
    D --> G[Dimensional Accuracy]
    E --> H[Tool Wear Rate]
    F --> I[Material Removal Rate MRR]
    H --> J[Surface Finish Ra]
    G --> J
    I --> K[Production Time]
    J --> L[Final Quality Output]
    K --> L
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- ↑ $D$ → ↓ $N$ (for fixed $V_c$)
- ↑ $D$ → ↓ $\delta$ (fourth-power relationship)
- ↑ $V_c$ → ↑ $T$ (non-linearly) → ↑ wear
- ↑ $V_f$ → ↑ MRR, but also ↑ $F_c$ → ↑ $\delta$

The KTU examiner expects you to identify these **trade-off chains** in 14-mark design questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]

**Q1.** Define **High-Speed Machining (HSM)**. What is the typical cutting-speed range for HSM of hardened steels, and what is the **fundamental physical principle** that distinguishes HSM from conventional machining?

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

> **HSM** is a machining process in which material is removed at cutting speeds 5–10× higher than conventional machining — typically **800–1,000 m/min** for hardened steels (>45 HRC) and **>1,500 m/min** for aluminum alloys. The **fundamental principle** is **adiabatic shear localization**: at high cutting speeds, the strain rate in the primary shear zone becomes so high (>10³ s⁻¹) that heat cannot diffuse out, the chip heats rapidly, and ~70–80% of the cutting energy is carried away by the **hot chip**, leaving the workpiece and tool thermally cool. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for speed range, 1 for adiabatic shear principle.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Predicted]

**Q2.** What is the **size effect** in micro-machining? Explain why the **specific cutting energy $K_s$** increases as the uncut chip thickness *h* decreases below a critical value.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):**

> The **size effect** is the phenomenon where the specific cutting energy $K_s$ (force per unit chip area) increases non-linearly as the uncut chip thickness *h* falls below **~0.1 mm** (or specifically below ~½ of the cutting edge radius $r_\beta$). This occurs because: **(i)** the cutting edge radius becomes comparable to *h*, causing the tool to **plow** rather than cut; **(ii)** at micro-scale, **individual grains** of the workpiece dominate the cut (heterogeneity effect); and **(iii)** **built-up edge (BUE)** becomes a large fraction of effective geometry. The empirical relation is $K_s = K_{s1} \cdot h^{-m}$, with $m \approx 0.15$–$0.30$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for cause, 1 for formula.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q(a)** [7 Marks] — With a neat schematic, explain the **geometry of a micro-end-mill**. Define: cutting edge angle, helix angle, rake angle, clearance angle, and tool cutting edge radius $r_\beta$. Why is the cutting edge radius $r_\beta$ the **most critical** geometric parameter in micro-machining?

**Q(b)** [7 Marks] — A tungsten-carbide micro-end-mill of diameter **0.5 mm** with **2 flutes** is used to cut an aluminum workpiece. The cutting speed is **400 m/min**, the feed per tooth is **5 μm/tooth**, and the radial and axial depths of cut are **0.05 mm** and **2 mm** respectively. Calculate:
   (i) Spindle speed *N* in rpm
   (ii) Feed velocity $V_f$ in mm/min
   (iii) Uncut chip thickness *h* (assume $\kappa_r = 90°$)
   (iv) Material removal rate (MRR) in mm³/min
   (v) Cutting force $F_c$ if specific cutting energy $K_s = 800$ N/mm²

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

#### Part (a) — Micro-End-Mill Geometry [7 Marks]

**Schematic Description (2 marks):**

A micro-end-mill consists of:

- **Shank** (3 mm or 6 mm standard) — held in collet/spindle.
- **Neck** (transition region) — provides clearance to avoid rubbing the workpiece wall.
- **Cutting section** of diameter *D* < 1 mm with *Z* flutes (typically 2 for micro).
- **End geometry** — flat, ball, or bull-nose tip.

**Geometric Definitions (3 marks, 0.5 each):**

| Parameter | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cutting edge angle $\kappa_r$** | Angle between cutting edge and tool axis (90° for square end-mill) |
| **Helix angle $\lambda$** | Angle of the cutting edge relative to the tool axis (typically 30°–45°; higher helix → smoother cut) |
| **Rake angle $\alpha$** | Angle of the flute face relative to the radial direction (positive rake → lower cutting force) |
| **Clearance angle $\gamma$** | Angle preventing rubbing of the tool flank on the workpiece (typically 10°–15°) |
| **Cutting edge radius $r_\beta$** | Radius of the rounded cutting edge (1–5 μm, measured by SEM/AFM) |

**Critical Role of $r_\beta$ (2 marks):**

The cutting edge radius $r_\beta$ determines the **minimum chip thickness** $h_{min} \approx 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$ (typically 0.5–2.5 μm). Below this, the tool cannot form a chip and instead **plows** the material, generating heat, BUE, and rapid tool wear. The size-effect exponent *m* in $K_s = K_{s1} \cdot h^{-m}$ is directly controlled by $r_\beta$ — larger $r_\beta$ → stronger size effect → narrower process window. For a 0.5 mm micro-end-mill, $r_\beta$ is often 1–2 μm, so $f_z$ must exceed ~1 μm to ensure proper cutting.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 0.5$ mm, $Z = 2$, $V_c = 400$ m/min, $f_z = 5$ μm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ mm
- $a_e = 0.05$ mm, $a_p = 2$ mm, $\kappa_r = 90°$, $K_s = 800$ N/mm²

**(i) Spindle speed N (1.5 marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 400}{\pi \cdot 0.5}$$

$$N = \dfrac{400{,}000}{1.5708} = 254{,}647.7 \;\text{rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 254{,}648 \;\text{rpm}}$$

**Sanity check:** This is in the range of **ultrahigh-speed spindles** (200,000–300,000 rpm), confirming the HSM classification.

---

**(ii) Feed velocity $V_f$ (1.5 marks):**

$$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 2 \cdot 254{,}648$$

$$V_f = 2546.48 \;\text{mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{V_f \approx 2546 \;\text{mm/min} = 2.546 \;\text{m/min}}$$

---

**(iii) Uncut chip thickness h (1.5 marks):**

$$h = f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa_r) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sin(90°) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{h = 5 \;\mu\text{m}}$$

**Comment:** $h = 5$ μm is at the boundary of the size-effect regime — BUE and edge-radius effects are likely.

---

**(iv) Material Removal Rate (1.5 marks):**

$$Z_w = V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$Z_w = 2546.48 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$Z_w = 2546.48 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.2546 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{Z_w \approx 0.255 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Note:** MRR is very small — characteristic of micro-machining.

---

**(v) Cutting force $F_c$ (1 mark):**

$$A_c = h \cdot b = h \cdot \dfrac{a_e}{\sin(\kappa_r)} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.05 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm}^2$$

$$F_c = K_s \cdot A_c = 800 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 0.2 \;\text{N} = 200 \;\text{mN}}$$

**Comment:** Force in the sub-Newton range — typical for micro-machining. However, even 0.2 N can cause significant tool deflection in a slender micro-tool (as shown in Derivation C, Section 3.3).

---

### QUESTION B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]

**Q(a)** [7 Marks] — What is **Taylor's Tool Life Equation**? Explain the significance of the constants *C* and *n*. For a carbide tool cutting mild steel, $C = 1.5 \times 10^9$ (m/min·min) and $n = 0.3$. If the cutting speed is increased from 150 m/min to 250 m/min, by what factor does the tool life **decrease**?

**Q(b)** [7 Marks] — Compare **conventional machining** and **High-Speed Machining (HSM)** with respect to: (i) cutting speed range, (ii) heat partition between chip, tool, and workpiece, (iii) chip morphology, (iv) surface integrity, (v) chip evacuation, and (vi) machine tool requirements. List **three major challenges** unique to HSM of micro-features.

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

#### Part (a) — Taylor's Tool Life Equation [7 Marks]

**Statement of Taylor's Equation (2 marks):**

$$V \cdot T^n = C \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad T = \dfrac{C}{V^n}$$

where:
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (minutes)
- $C$ = constant representing the cutting speed that gives a tool life of 1 minute (m/min·min)
- $n$ = tool-life exponent (slope on log-log plot), typically 0.1–0.5

**Significance (2 marks):**

- **Constant *C*:** represents the **reference cutting speed** that would give a tool life of 1 minute. Higher *C* means the tool-workpiece combination is more wear-resistant (e.g., ceramic tools have higher *C* than HSS).
- **Exponent *n*:** represents the **sensitivity of tool life to cutting speed**. Smaller *n* means tool life is **less sensitive** to speed changes — desirable for HSM.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $C = 1.5 \times 10^9$, $n = 0.3$.

At $V_1 = 150$ m/min:

$$T_1 = \dfrac{C}{V_1^n} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{(150)^{0.3}}$$

$$(150)^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(150)} = e^{0.3 \cdot 5.011} = e^{1.503} = 4.495$$

$$T_1 = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{4.495} = 3.337 \times 10^8 \;\text{min}$$

At $V_2 = 250$ m/min:

$$(250)^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(250)} = e^{0.3 \cdot 5.521} = e^{1.656} = 5.241$$

$$T_2 = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{5.241} = 2.862 \times 10^8 \;\text{min}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{V_2^n}{V_1^n} = \left(\dfrac{250}{150}\right)^{0.3} = \left(\dfrac{5}{3}\right)^{0.3}$$

$$= e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(1.667)} = e^{0.3 \cdot 0.5108} = e^{0.1533} = 1.166$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} \approx 1.17}$$

**Conclusion:** A 1.67× increase in cutting speed (150 → 250) results in only a ~17% reduction in tool life. This is the **HSM advantage** — for $n < 0.5$, speed increases give **proportionally smaller** reductions in tool life, which is why HSM is economically viable for hardened steels.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison and Challenges [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks, ~0.5 each):**

| Criterion | Conventional | HSM |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Cutting speed | 50–200 m/min (steel) | 800–2,000 m/min (steel) |
| (ii) Heat partition (chip/work/tool) | ~50/30/20 | ~80/10/10 |
| (iii) Chip morphology | Continuous, uniform | **Serrated/saw-tooth** (adiabatic shear) |
| (iv) Surface integrity | Possible tensile residual stress, white layer | **Compressive residual stress**, no white layer |
| (v) Chip evacuation | Slow, may tangle | Rapid, but risk of **recast** on tool |
| (vi) Machine requirements | Standard rigidity | High-damping polymer-filled castings, **linear motors**, thermal compensation |

**Three Major Challenges of HSM for Micro-Features (3 marks):**

1. **Tool Deflection and Breakage** — with $D < 0.5$ mm, $\delta \propto L^3/D^4$ makes the tool extremely flexible. Any unbalance in a 100,000+ rpm spindle amplifies vibration, leading to chatter and tool fracture.

2. **Spindle Dynamic Balancing** — at 200,000 rpm, the tip of a 0.5 mm tool experiences centrifugal accelerations of > 50,000 *g*. Even 1 mg of mass asymmetry causes lateral vibration amplitudes exceeding the tolerance band.

3. **Minimum Chip Thickness and BUE** — at low feeds, $f_z$ may fall below $h_{min}$, causing the tool to plow rather than cut. The resulting BUE rapidly changes effective tool geometry, destroys surface finish, and causes **catastrophic tool failure**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit mistakes** — KTU answers are *strictly* penalized for unit errors. Always carry units throughout the calculation, and check the **dimensional consistency** of the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing $V_f$ and $f_z$** — $V_f$ is **feed velocity** (mm/min), $f_z$ is **feed per tooth** (mm/tooth). The relation is $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$. Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Ignoring the size effect** — In numericals with $D < 1$ mm, the size effect is **always active**. State it explicitly and mention the minimum chip thickness $h_{min}$.
> 4. **Forgetting $10^{-3}$ in MRR formula** — $Z_w$ in mm³/min needs the $10^{-3}$ factor if $V_f$ is in mm/min. Students commonly write MRR 1000× too large.
> 5. **Taylor's equation base units** — *C* has units of (m/min)·min^n only when *V* is in m/min and *T* is in min. KTU does not accept $C$ in mixed units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision — 60-Second Summary for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **HSM = cutting speed 5–10× conventional**, governed by **adiabatic shear localization** in the primary shear zone.
- ✅ In HSM, **~80% of heat is carried away by the chip** → tool and workpiece remain thermally cool.
- ✅ **HSM cutting speed ranges:** 800–1,000 m/min for hardened steel, 1,500+ m/min for aluminum, 100+ m/min for titanium.
- ✅ **Micro-tool = D < 1 mm**, often D < 0.5 mm, aspect ratio up to 15:1, edge radius 1–5 μm.
- ✅ **Size effect:** $K_s = K_{s1} \cdot h^{-m}$, $m \approx 0.15$–$0.30$, kicks in when $h < 0.1$ mm.
- ✅ **Minimum chip thickness:** $h_{min} \approx 0.5 \cdot r_\beta$. Below this, the tool **plows** instead of cutting.
- ✅ **Tool deflection:** $\delta = 64 \cdot F_c \cdot L^3 / (3 \cdot \pi \cdot E \cdot D^4)$ — depends on **fourth power of D** and **cube of L**.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$, **MRR** $= V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \cdot 10^{-3}$ mm³/min.
- ✅ **Taylor's equation:** $V \cdot T^n = C$ → small *n* (≈ 0.3) is good for HSM because tool life is **insensitive** to speed.
- ✅ **HSM chip morphology:** serrated / saw-tooth (signature of adiabatic shear).
- ✅ **HSM surface integrity:** **compressive** residual stress, no white layer → better fatigue life.
- ✅ **Coolant for micro-HSM:** **MQL** or **cryogenic** — flood coolant causes tool deflection.
- ✅ **Spindle requirement:** **>40,000 rpm**, balanced to **<1 mg·mm** for 100,000+ rpm operation.
- ✅ **Three micro-HSM challenges:** (1) tool deflection, (2) spindle dynamic balance, (3) BUE/min chip thickness.
- ✅ **Quick numeric sanity check:** for D = 0.5 mm and $V_c$ = 400 m/min → N ≈ 254,648 rpm. For 1 mm tool and 200 m/min → N ≈ 63,662 rpm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diamond Micro Turning - Principle, Process, Description and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diamond Micro Turning - Principle, Process, Description and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Diamond Micro Turning (DMT)** is a deterministic, single-point ultra-precision machining process that utilizes a geometrically defined **single crystal natural diamond tool** to remove material at depths of cut typically in the range of **1 µm to 10 µm**, with feed rates as low as a few **µm/rev**, achieving form accuracy in the **sub-micron range** (often < 0.1 µm) and surface roughness (Ra) in the **nanometer regime** (typically **5–50 nm**).

The process is classified under **"Deterministic Micro-machining"** in the KTU 2024 taxonomy of advanced manufacturing processes, distinct from traditional turning due to its reliance on:

- **Single-crystal diamond (SCD)** tool (pure carbon, cubic crystal structure, Knoop hardness ≈ 7000–10000 HK)
- **Ultra-precision slide systems** with nanometric positioning accuracy (often using hydrostatic or aerostatic bearings)
- **Vibration-isolated environment** (typically on granite bases with active/passive isolators)
- **Controlled ambient conditions** (temperature ± 0.1 °C, sometimes cleanroom Class 1000 or better)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Diamond micro turning is grouped under *Mechanical Micro-machining Processes* in the 2024 scheme. Students must be able to distinguish DMT from other non-conventional processes (EDM, LBM, EBM) and explain why diamond is the only feasible tool material for nanometric surface generation on non-ferrous metals and polymers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine shaving a chocolate bar with an extremely sharp razor blade — but instead of a blade made of steel, it is made from a **single molecule of carbon** (a diamond), and the chocolate is held on a stage that moves in increments smaller than a **bacterium's width (≈ 1 µm)**.

To make the analogy more relatable for a first-time learner:

- **A typical lathe** cuts metal the way a butter knife spreads bread — quick and rough, leaving visible grooves.
- **Diamond micro turning** is like a **micro-chisel guided by a laser ruler** — the tool touches the workpiece for fractions of a second, shaving off layers thinner than a **human hair (70 µm)** by a factor of 10,000.
- Just as a **master watchmaker** uses ruby/sapphire bearings in a Swiss watch, the ultra-precision lathe uses **diamond as its "cutting jewel"** because it is the hardest known material and can hold a near-atomic-scale cutting edge.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics for DMT (KTU Board-Approved Values):**
> - **Tool material:** Single-crystal natural diamond (Type IIa), Mohs hardness = **10**, Knoop hardness = **7000–10000 HK**
> - **Density of diamond:** **3.52 g/cm³**
> - **Young's Modulus of diamond:** **≈ 1050 GPa**
> - **Thermal conductivity of diamond:** **≈ 2000 W/m·K** (highest of any material at room temperature)
> - **Depth of cut (ap):** **1–25 µm** (typical), can go down to **0.1 µm** in ultraprecision mode
> - **Feed (f):** **1–25 µm/rev**
> - **Cutting speed (Vc):** **50–500 m/min** for aluminum; lower for copper/PMMA
> - **Surface roughness (Ra):** **5–50 nm**
> - **Form accuracy:** **< 0.5 µm over 50 mm** traverse
