# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The principle of diamond micro turning is rooted in **deterministic material separation** rather than erosion, melting, or chemical reaction. Each step in the process is governed by well-defined physics:

### Step 1: Tool-Workpiece Engagement
- A **single-crystal diamond tool**, ground to a specific nose radius (typically **0.5 mm to 5 mm**), is brought into contact with the rotating workpiece.
- The diamond is mounted on a **piezo-actuated or servo-driven slide** with nanometric resolution.

### Step 2: Orthogonal Cutting at Microscale
- Material is removed through a **shear-dominated mechanism** (similar to conventional turning) but the **undeformed chip thickness (h₁)** is comparable to or smaller than the **grain size of the workpiece material**.
- At this scale, the material behaves more like a **continuum with size-effect** rather than a rigid plastic body (Strain gradient plasticity theory applies).

### Step 3: Elastic Recovery & Spring-back
- Because the depth of cut is so small, a significant fraction of the **specific cutting energy** is consumed by **elastic deformation of the workpiece surface** before the chip actually detaches.
- This results in a measurable **"minimum chip thickness" (h_min)** below which no chip is formed and the tool merely plows the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why diamond and not carbide?**
> 1. Carbide tools have a **polycrystalline structure** with grain boundaries that break down at sub-micron edge radii, producing a dull tool within minutes.
> 2. Diamond's **single-crystal** lattice means there are no grain boundaries — the edge can be honed to a **radius of curvature < 50 nm**.
> 3. Diamond has very **low chemical affinity with non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Au, polymers) — minimizing **built-up edge (BUE)** formation.
> 4. Diamond is **NOT suitable for ferrous metals** (Fe, Ni, Co) because of **catastrophic chemical wear** (carbon diffuses into iron at elevated temperatures > 700 °C).

### Step 4: Surface Generation
- The final surface is generated as a **mathematical envelope of the tool nose** moving relative to the workpiece.
- The theoretical surface roughness is purely **geometric** (in the ideal case):

$$
R_{a,\text{ideal}} \approx \frac{f^2}{8R}
$$

where **f** = feed per revolution and **R** = tool nose radius. For f = 5 µm/rev and R = 1 mm, R_a ≈ 3.1 nm.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Typical Units | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_a = \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$ | Ideal surface roughness (geometric envelope) | nm | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $R_z = \dfrac{f^2}{4R}$ | Peak-to-valley roughness | nm | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $h_1 = f \cdot \sin(\kappa_r)$ | Undeformed chip thickness | µm | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $h_2 = \dfrac{0.6 \cdot f \cdot \sin(\kappa_r)}{\cos(\phi_s) \cdot r_\varepsilon}$ (approx.) | Deformed chip thickness with nose radius effect | µm | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot a_p$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $F_c = K_s \cdot a_p \cdot f$ | Principal cutting force (K_s = specific cutting energy) | N | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $h_{min} \approx 0.3 \cdot r_\varepsilon$ | Minimum chip thickness (rule of thumb) | µm | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $F_c \, \text{(micro)} = K_{s,\text{micro}} \cdot a_p \cdot f$ | Cutting force at microscale (with size-effect correction factor) | N | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $P = F_c \cdot v_c$ | Cutting power | W | ⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $T_{thermal} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_c}{J \cdot A_{contact}}$ | Interface temperature rise (J = heat fraction to tool) | °C | ⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Conventions used in KTU papers:**
> - $f$ = feed (µm/rev), $a_p$ = depth of cut (µm), $v_c$ = cutting speed (m/min)
> - $\kappa_r$ = tool cutting edge angle (°)
> - $r_\varepsilon$ = tool nose radius (mm)
> - $\phi_s$ = shear angle (°)
> - $R_a$ = arithmetic mean roughness, $R_z$ = ten-point mean roughness
> - $K_s$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm² or GPa)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical DMT Application | Why DMT is Preferred |
|---|---|---|
| **Optics / Photonics** | Aspheric moulds, lens arrays, mirrors | Nanometric form accuracy eliminates post-polishing |
| **Aerospace** | Micro fuel injector nozzles, turbine blade tips | High surface integrity, no HAZ |
| **Biomedical** | Micro-implants, stents, dental prosthetics | Burr-free, mirror finish reduces bacterial adhesion |
| **Electronics** | Heat sinks, micro-lens arrays, OLED moulds | Freeform geometries on Cu/Al substrates |
| **Defence** | IR optics, FLIR windows | Mid-spatial-frequency error control |
| **Mould manufacturing** | Injection mould inserts for DVDs, lenses | Direct machining of freeform moulds (Fast Tool Servo) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Surface Roughness in Diamond Turning

The classical derivation of the geometric surface profile produced by a round-nose tool in turning is a **favourite KTU question**. Below is the full step-by-step derivation.

### Setup and Assumptions
- The tool has a **nose radius R** (often denoted $r_\varepsilon$).
- The tool is fed along the workpiece axis with a feed **f** per revolution.
- The cutting edge is **perfectly sharp** (no wear, no BUE).
- The workpiece material is perfectly **rigid-plastic** (no elastic recovery).

### Geometric Construction

Consider two successive passes of the tool. The cutting edge moves by a distance **f** between revolutions. The uncut material left behind is a thin scallop of height **H** between successive tool positions.

**Step 1:** Place the origin at the centre of the tool nose in pass 1. The tool profile is a circle of radius R:

$$
(x)^2 + (y + R)^2 = R^2 \quad \text{(circle 1)}
$$

**Step 2:** In pass 2, the tool has moved by distance **f** along the feed direction, so the new circle is:

$$
(x - f)^2 + (y + R)^2 = R^2 \quad \text{(circle 2)}
$$

**Step 3:** The peak of the scallop (highest point of the residual material) lies on the line $x = f/2$ by symmetry, i.e., midway between the two tool positions.

**Step 4:** Substitute $x = f/2$ into circle 1 (or 2) and solve for y:

$$
\left(\frac{f}{2}\right)^2 + (y + R)^2 = R^2
$$

$$
\frac{f^2}{4} + y^2 + 2Ry + R^2 = R^2
$$

$$
\frac{f^2}{4} + y^2 + 2Ry = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve this quadratic in y. The relevant (negative) root gives the depth of the valley:

$$
y = -R + \sqrt{R^2 - \frac{f^2}{4}}
$$

**Step 6:** For $f \ll R$ (typical of DMT where f is in µm and R is in mm), use the binomial expansion:

$$
\sqrt{R^2 - \frac{f^2}{4}} = R\sqrt{1 - \frac{f^2}{4R^2}} \approx R\left(1 - \frac{f^2}{8R^2}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back:

$$
y \approx -R + R - \frac{f^2}{8R} = -\frac{f^2}{8R}
$$

**Step 8:** The peak-to-valley height is the absolute value:

$$
H = R_t = \frac{f^2}{8R}
$$

**Step 9:** The arithmetic mean roughness $R_a$ is approximately **1/4 of $R_t$** for a sinusoidal-like profile (this is a standard result used in KTU solutions):

$$
R_a \approx \frac{R_t}{4} = \frac{f^2}{32R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Some textbooks use the approximation $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$ when the tool profile is treated as a "near-tangent" cutter. The factor of 1/4 conversion is only valid for sinusoidal profiles. Always state your assumption explicitly in the answer.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Scheme Style)

> **Question (Part B - 7 Marks):** A diamond turning operation is performed on an aluminum alloy workpiece using a single-crystal diamond tool with nose radius $R = 1.5$ mm and feed $f = 10$ µm/rev. Compute (a) the ideal surface roughness $R_a$ in nm, and (b) the depth of cut $a_p$ if the material removal rate is to be 5 mm³/min at a cutting speed of 200 m/min.

### Part (a) — Ideal Surface Roughness

**Step 1:** Convert feed to mm: $f = 10 \text{ µm} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 0.01$ mm.

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$
R_a = \frac{f^2}{32R} = \frac{(0.01)^2}{32 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
R_a = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{48} = 2.083 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to nm:

$$
R_a = 2.083 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 \text{ nm} = 2.08 \text{ nm}
$$

### Part (b) — Depth of Cut from MRR

**Step 1:** Recall the MRR formula for turning:

$$
MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot a_p
$$

where $v_c$ is in mm/min, $f$ in mm/rev, and $a_p$ in mm.

**Step 2:** Convert $v_c$: $v_c = 200 \text{ m/min} = 200,000$ mm/min. Feed $f = 0.01$ mm/rev.

**Step 3:** Solve for $a_p$:

$$
a_p = \frac{MRR}{v_c \cdot f} = \frac{5}{200,000 \times 0.01}
$$

$$
a_p = \frac{5}{2000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 2.5 \text{ µm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Microscale Regime:** $a_p = 2.5$ µm confirms we are firmly in the **micro turning regime** (1–25 µm), and the geometric roughness formula is fully valid.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Diamond Turning Materials vs Parameters

| Workpiece Material | Recommended $v_c$ (m/min) | Recommended $f$ (µm/rev) | Recommended $a_p$ (µm) | Achievable $R_a$ (nm) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aluminum 6061** | 100–300 | 2–10 | 2–15 | 5–15 | Most common DMT material |
| **OFC Copper (C101)** | 80–150 | 1–5 | 1–8 | 8–20 | Risk of BUE; high-purity helps |
| **Brass (C260)** | 100–200 | 2–8 | 2–10 | 10–25 | Zinc causes tool wear above 5 µm DOC |
| **PMMA / Acrylic** | 50–150 | 5–20 | 5–30 | 20–50 | Low melting; use coolant air |
| **Polycarbonate** | 50–200 | 5–20 | 5–25 | 25–60 | Avoid thermal degradation |
| **Gold / Silver** | 50–100 | 1–5 | 1–5 | 5–10 | Mirror finishes for moulds |
| **Germanium / Silicon** | 50–150 | 2–10 | 2–10 | 5–20 | Brittle; Ductile mode cutting possible |
| **Electrolytic Ni (free of Fe)** | 30–80 | 1–3 | 1–5 | 3–10 | Only with anti-diffusion coated tools |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**"Define diamond micro turning. Why is single-crystal diamond preferred over polycrystalline diamond (PCD) for ultra-precision machining?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Definition (1 mark):** Diamond micro turning is a non-traditional deterministic material removal process using a single-crystal diamond (SCD) tool to achieve sub-micron form accuracy and nanometric surface finish (typically Ra < 50 nm) at depths of cut in the order of 1–25 µm.
- **Reason 1 (1 mark):** SCD has no grain boundaries, allowing a cutting edge radius of < 50 nm, whereas PCD grain boundaries fracture at this scale, causing rapid dulling.
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** SCD has superior wear resistance and low chemical affinity with non-ferrous metals, eliminating built-up edge (BUE) and enabling mirror-quality surface generation.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**"List any four materials that can be effectively machined by diamond micro turning. Name one material that should NOT be diamond turned and justify."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Four suitable materials (2 marks):** Aluminum 6061, Oxygen-Free Copper (OFC C101), PMMA (acrylic), Gold, or Polycarbonate.
- **One unsuitable material with justification (1 mark):** **Steel (AISI 1045) or any ferrous alloy containing iron, nickel, or cobalt.** Justification: At the elevated temperatures generated at the cutting zone (> 700 °C), carbon from the diamond diffuses catalytically into the iron lattice, causing **catastrophic chemical wear** (graphitization of the tool edge) and rapid tool failure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of diamond micro turning with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of the **single-crystal diamond tool** in achieving nanometric surface finish. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A diamond turning operation is performed on an Al-6061 workpiece with the following parameters: tool nose radius $R = 2$ mm, feed $f = 8$ µm/rev, depth of cut $a_p = 5$ µm, and cutting speed $v_c = 250$ m/min. Calculate:
   1. The ideal arithmetic surface roughness $R_a$ in nm.
   2. The material removal rate (MRR) in mm³/min.
   3. The minimum chip thickness expected (assume $h_{min} \approx 0.3 \cdot r_\varepsilon$ where $r_\varepsilon$ is nose radius in µm). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Principle (7 Marks):**
- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Diamond micro turning operates on the principle of **deterministic single-point cutting** at the microscale, where a geometrically defined single-crystal diamond tool removes material in a controlled, repeatable manner to generate a surface that is a **mathematical envelope** of the tool geometry.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Key enabling subsystems: (i) **aerostatic/hydrostatic spindle** for vibration-free rotation; (ii) **linear motor slides with nanometric encoders** for feed accuracy; (iii) **granite base + active vibration isolation**; (iv) **temperature-controlled enclosure**.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** The diamond tool's role: (i) **edge radius < 50 nm** enables atomic-scale material separation; (ii) **high thermal conductivity (2000 W/m·K)** dissipates heat into the chip; (iii) **chemical inertness** with non-ferrous workpieces prevents BUE.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** The result is a **mirror-finish, burr-free surface** with $R_a$ in the 5–50 nm range and form accuracy < 0.5 µm over the workpiece.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**1. Ideal $R_a$ (2 marks):**
Given $f = 8$ µm $= 0.008$ mm, $R = 2$ mm:

$$
R_a = \frac{f^2}{32R} = \frac{(0.008)^2}{32 \times 2} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{64} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}
$$

Converting to nm:

$$
R_a = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = 1.0 \text{ nm}
$$

**2. MRR (2 marks):**
Convert $v_c$ to mm/min: $v_c = 250 \times 1000 = 250,000$ mm/min. Feed $f = 0.008$ mm/rev, $a_p = 5$ µm $= 0.005$ mm.

$$
MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot a_p = 250,000 \times 0.008 \times 0.005 = 10 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**3. Minimum Chip Thickness (3 marks):**
Convert nose radius to µm: $R = 2$ mm $= 2000$ µm.

$$
h_{min} = 0.3 \times r_\varepsilon = 0.3 \times 2000 = 600 \text{ nm} = 0.6 \text{ µm}
$$

Since $a_p = 5$ µm $\gg h_{min} = 0.6$ µm, the operation is firmly in the **steady-state chip-formation regime** and the tool will not "plow." This validates our use of the geometric $R_a$ formula.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **process of diamond micro turning** step by step, from workpiece mounting to final inspection. Compare its **capabilities and limitations** with conventional CNC turning. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **tool-workpiece geometry** in diamond micro turning. Derive the expression for the **theoretical surface roughness** in terms of feed $f$ and nose radius $R$, clearly stating all assumptions. A diamond tool with nose radius $R = 1$ mm is used at a feed $f = 5$ µm/rev on a copper workpiece. Determine the expected $R_a$ in nm. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Process Description (7 Marks):**
- **Step 1 — Workpiece Mounting (1 mark):** The workpiece is fixed on a high-precision vacuum or collet chuck, ensuring runout < 0.5 µm. The chuck is mounted on an aerostatic spindle.
- **Step 2 — Tool Setup (1 mark):** The single-crystal diamond tool is clamped in a precision holder and set using a **tool-setting microscope** (resolution ~1 µm) to establish the datum.
- **Step 3 — Calibration (1 mark):** The machine axes are calibrated using a **laser interferometer** with sub-nanometer resolution. Temperature is stabilized to ± 0.1 °C.
- **Step 4 — Roughing Pass (1 mark):** A preliminary pass at higher feed (e.g., 25 µm/rev) is used to remove bulk material.
- **Step 5 — Finishing Pass (1 mark):** The final pass uses the calculated feed and depth of cut to achieve the desired $R_a$.
- **Step 6 — In-process Measurement (1 mark):** A white-light interferometer or form talysurf measures the surface, and the result is fed back to the controller for compensation.
- **Step 7 — Comparison (1 mark):** Capabilities vs CNC turning — DMT achieves 100× better surface finish (Ra ~10 nm vs ~1 µm) and 10× better form accuracy, but is limited to non-ferrous materials, has lower MRR, requires controlled environment, and tool cost is 100–1000× higher.

**Part (b) — Derivation & Calculation (7 Marks):**
- **Sketch (2 marks):** Show two successive tool positions separated by feed $f$, with the tool nose radius $R$ forming circular profiles, and the resulting scallop height $H$ between the two passes.
- **Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:
  - Geometric setup: $\left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^2 + (y + R)^2 = R^2$
  - Solving: $y = -R + \sqrt{R^2 - \dfrac{f^2}{4}}$
  - Binomial expansion: $y \approx -\dfrac{f^2}{8R}$
  - Therefore $H = R_t = \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$ and $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{32R}$
  - Assumptions stated: (i) perfectly sharp tool, (ii) rigid-plastic workpiece, (iii) no built-up edge, (iv) $f \ll R$, (v) profile is approximately sinusoidal.
- **Calculation (2 marks):** Given $f = 5$ µm $= 0.005$ mm, $R = 1$ mm:

$$
R_a = \frac{f^2}{32R} = \frac{(0.005)^2}{32 \times 1} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 7.8125 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mm}
$$

Converting to nm:

$$
R_a = 7.8125 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^6 \approx 0.78 \text{ nm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert $f$ from µm to mm when using the formula in mm, leading to a roughness value that is 10⁶× too large or too small. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **Missing Assumptions (–1 mark):** The $R_a = f^2/(32R)$ formula is derived under specific assumptions (sharp tool, rigid-plastic material, no BUE). Failure to state them results in lost marks even if the final answer is correct.
> 3. **Confusing $R_a$ with $R_t$ (–1 mark):** Peak-to-valley height $R_t = f^2/(8R)$, while $R_a \approx R_t/4$. Mixing these up is a common error.
> 4. **Applying DMT to ferrous materials (–1 mark):** If a numerical problem gives steel as the workpiece, the answer must include a justification for why DMT is not feasible.
> 5. **Ignoring minimum chip thickness (–1 mark):** Always check if $a_p > h_{min}$. If not, the geometric roughness formula is invalid because the tool is plowing, not cutting.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Diamond Micro Turning (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Single-crystal diamond tool + ultra-precision slide + controlled environment → sub-µm form accuracy + nm-range Ra.
>
> ✅ **Diamond Uniqueness:** Type IIa single crystal, Mohs 10, Knoop 7000–10000 HK, thermal conductivity 2000 W/m·K, no grain boundary.
>
> ✅ **Key Geometric Formula:** $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{32R}$ (assumes $f \ll R$, sinusoidal profile).
>
> ✅ **Peak-to-Valley:** $R_t = \dfrac{f^2}{8R} = 4 \cdot R_a$ (for sinusoidal profile).
>
> ✅ **Typical Parameter Ranges:** $a_p$ = 1–25 µm, $f$ = 1–25 µm/rev, $v_c$ = 50–500 m/min, $R_a$ achievable = 5–50 nm.
>
> ✅ **MRR Formula:** $MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot a_p$ (in mm³/min when $v_c$ in mm/min).
>
> ✅ **Minimum Chip Thickness:** $h_{min} \approx 0.3 \cdot r_\varepsilon$ (nose radius effect).
>
> ✅ **Suitable Materials:** Al, Cu, brass, Au, Ag, PMMA, PC, Ge, Si (mirror finish non-ferrous).
>
> ✅ **Unsuitable Materials:** Steel, cast iron, Ni alloys, Co alloys (carbon diffusion / chemical wear).
>
> ✅ **Subsystem Essentials:** Aerostatic spindle + linear motor slides + granite base + temperature control ± 0.1 °C + laser interferometer feedback.
>
> ✅ **Process vs Conventional CNC:** DMT gives 100× better Ra, 10× better form accuracy, but 1000× higher tool cost and very low MRR.
>
> ✅ **KTU Hot Topics (most frequently asked):** (i) Derivation of $R_a$ formula, (ii) Material compatibility list with justification, (iii) Subsystem functions, (iv) Comparison with conventional turning, (v) Numerical problems on MRR and $R_a$.
>
> ✅ **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not confuse the geometric (ideal) $R_a$ with the **measured** $R_a$. The measured value is always higher due to tool wear, vibration, BUE, and machine errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Type Micromachining Processes - Abrasive Based Nano Finishing Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Type Micromachining Processes - Abrasive Based Nano Finishing Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abrasive Based Nano Finishing Processes** are a class of **non-conventional, mechanical-type micro/nano-machining processes** that achieve sub-micron to nanometer-scale surface finish (**Ra < 100 nm**) by directing, controlling, and energizing abrasive particles against a workpiece surface through a fluid medium, magnetic field, or pressurized flow, rather than through a rigid cutting tool.

These processes belong to the **Mechanical Energy Group** of micromachining under the KTU 2024 scheme classification, and are formally grouped as **Abrasive-Assisted Surface Refinement (AASR)** techniques.

### 1.2 Classification of Abrasive Based Nano Finishing Processes

| Process | Energy Source | Typical Ra Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| **Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF)** | Pressurized visco-elastic media | 10 – 50 nm |
| **Magnetic Abrasive Finishing (MAF)** | Magnetic field + abrasive particles | 5 – 30 nm |
| **Magneto-Rheological Abrasive Flow Finishing (MRAFF)** | Magnetic + Hydrodynamic | 2 – 20 nm |
| **Magneto-Rheological Finishing (MRF)** | Magnetic field + fluid rheology | 2 – 15 nm |
| **Elastic Emission Machining (EEM)** | High-speed rotating sphere | 1 – 10 nm |
| **Sand Blasting / Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)** | High-pressure gas + abrasives | 100 – 500 nm |

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **NEP 2020 aligned KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMET864, Module 1)**, the **primary focus** is on **AFF, MAF, MRF, and MRAFF** as they represent the four pillars of hybrid mechanical finishing. The **Preston Equation** is the governing empirical law across all of these processes.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Liquid Sandpaper" Idea

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Explanation for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you need to polish the **inside of a curved glass bottle** — no rigid tool can reach inside, and any sandpaper would tear. Now imagine filling that bottle with a **thick, honey-like fluid loaded with microscopic diamond dust**, and pushing that fluid back and forth using two plungers from both ends. The flowing "liquid sandpaper" slides along every curve, grinding the inner surface down to a mirror finish. This is exactly how **Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF)** works — it is **sandpaper that has been liquefied**.

Similarly, in **Magnetic Abrasive Finishing (MAF)**, think of **tiny iron-filing-coated sand particles being "danced" by a magnetic field** — they form a flexible magnetic brush that polishes any complex shape held near the magnet. The abrasive is **steerable by lines of magnetic force**, just like iron filings arrange themselves around a bar magnet in school experiments.

### 1.4 Why "Nano Finishing"?

The term **"Nano"** is justified when the surface roughness parameter **Ra** is reduced below **100 nm** (0.1 µm). For reference:

- **Ra > 1 µm** → Conventional finishing
- **0.1 µm < Ra < 1 µm** → Micro finishing
- **Ra < 0.1 µm (100 nm)** → **Nano finishing** ✅

The current world benchmark for ultra-smooth surfaces is **Ra ≈ 0.1 nm** (atomically flat), achievable only via **Elastic Emission Machining (EEM)** and **Float Polishing**.

### 1.5 Importance in KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping (PEMET864):**
> - **CO1:** Understand the principles of micro and nano manufacturing processes.
> - **CO2:** Compare mechanical, chemical, and energy-based micromachining.
> - **CO3:** Analyze process parameters for nano surface generation.
> 
> This topic directly addresses **CO1, CO2, and CO3** with **Bloom's Levels L2 (Understand) and L4 (Analyze)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process 1 — Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF)

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle

In **AFF**, the workpiece is clamped between two opposed cylinders. A **visco-elastic abrasive media** (silicone polymer + abrasive grains) is hydraulically forced to flow back and forth through the workpiece, extruding over its surface.

The **abrasive grains** act as micro-cutters performing **two-body and three-body abrasion** simultaneously, removing material atom-by-atom.

#### 2.1.2 Key Components

1. **Media** — visco-elastic polymer (viscosity **η = 100 – 500 Pa·s**) loaded with abrasive (SiC, Al₂O₃, diamond, B₄C) at concentration **15–40 % by weight**.
2. **Hydraulic Actuation System** — piston-cylinder assembly.
3. **Fixtures** — holds workpiece in extrusion path.
4. **Filtration & Recirculation Unit** — removes debris, maintains media consistency.

#### 2.1.3 Process Variables (KTU Exam Frequently Tested)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on MRR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extrusion Pressure | **P** | 0.5 – 15 MPa | MRR ∝ P |
| Media Viscosity | **η** | 100 – 500 Pa·s | Higher η → higher MRR (up to optimum) |
| Abrasive Grit Size | **d_g** | 0.5 – 100 µm | Smaller grit → lower MRR, lower Ra |
| Number of Cycles | **N** | 50 – 4000 | MRR ∝ √N |
| Flow Velocity | **v** | 0.5 – 5 m/s | MRR ∝ v |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Preston Equation** governs AFF:
> 
> $$h = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t$$
> 
> where **h** = depth of material removed, **K_p** = Preston coefficient, **P** = contact pressure, **v** = relative velocity, **t** = finishing time.

### 2.2 Process 2 — Magnetic Abrasive Finishing (MAF)

#### 2.2.1 Working Principle

In **MAF**, the workpiece is placed in the **gap of an electromagnet or permanent magnet**. **Magnetic Abrasive Particles (MAPs)** — typically **iron particles coated with Al₂O₃ or SiC** — are introduced in the gap. The **magnetic field gradient** holds the abrasive particles against the workpiece surface, forming a **flexible magnetic brush**.

When the workpiece rotates (or the magnet rotates), the brush scrubs the surface, performing **nano-scale material removal**.

#### 2.2.2 MAP Composition

- **Core:** pure iron or low-carbon steel (provides ferromagnetism).
- **Coating:** abrasive (Al₂O₃, SiC, diamond, cBN) bonded via sintering or electroless plating.
- **Typical Size:** **d_MAP = 50 – 500 µm**.

#### 2.2.3 Force Analysis on MAP

A MAP held in the gap experiences:

$$F_m = \frac{1}{\mu_0} \cdot (\chi_p - \chi_m) \cdot V_p \cdot B \cdot \nabla B$$

where:
- **F_m** = magnetic force on particle,
- **χ_p, χ_m** = susceptibilities of particle and medium,
- **V_p** = volume of particle,
- **B** = magnetic flux density (Tesla),
- **∇B** = magnetic field gradient,
- **µ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** (permeability of free space — **bold constant, board-favorite**).

### 2.3 Process 3 — Magneto-Rheological Abrasive Flow Finishing (MRAFF)

#### 2.3.1 Hybrid Concept

**MRAFF = MAF + AFF** — a hybrid that combines:
- **Hydrodynamic pressure** (from AFF) to push media into complex internal passages,
- **Magnetic field** (from MAF) to stiffen and direct the abrasive chains.

When **magnetic field is OFF**, MR fluid behaves as a **Newtonian fluid** (low viscosity). When **magnetic field is ON**, particles form **chains along field lines**, raising **viscosity by 10²–10³ times** (yield stress develops).

#### 2.3.2 Bingham Plastic Model

The MR fluid obeys the **Bingham plastic rheology**:

$$\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where:
- **τ** = shear stress,
- **τ_y** = yield stress (depends on **B**),
- **η_p** = plastic viscosity,
- **γ̇** = shear rate.

> [!NOTE]
> The yield stress **τ_y** is empirically given by:
> 
> $$\tau_y = \alpha \cdot B^{\beta}$$
> 
> where **α, β** are fluid-specific constants, and **B** is in Tesla. Typical values: **α ≈ 1–10 kPa/T⁰·⁵**, **β ≈ 0.5–1.5**.

### 2.4 Process 4 — Magneto-Rheological Finishing (MRF)

#### 2.4.1 Principle

A **MR fluid ribbon** is delivered onto a rotating wheel. The **workpiece is dipped into the ribbon** at a controlled immersion depth, and a **magnetic field** is applied near the contact zone. The shear stress generated in the **stiffened MR fluid** removes material from the workpiece.

MRF is a **deterministic finishing process** — the **material removal function is stationary and stable** (unlike AFF, which has process drift due to media degradation).

#### 2.4.2 Material Removal Rate (MRR)

$$\text{MRR} = K_{MRF} \cdot P_n \cdot v_r \cdot A_c$$

where:
- **K_MRF** = Preston-like coefficient,
- **P_n** = normal pressure at contact,
- **v_r** = relative velocity,
- **A_c** = contact area.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table MUST be memorized for KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Process | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t$ | AFF / MAF / MRF | h [m], K_p [m²/N], P [Pa], v [m/s], t [s] | Preston Equation — depth of cut |
| 2 | $Ra = Ra_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot N}$ | All | Ra [m], N [cycles], k [1/cycle] | Exponential surface smoothing |
| 3 | $F_m = \frac{(\chi_p - \chi_m) V_p B \nabla B}{\mu_0}$ | MAF | F_m [N], B [T] | Magnetic force on abrasive particle |
| 4 | $\tau_y = \alpha B^{\beta}$ | MRAFF / MRF | τ_y [Pa], B [T] | Yield stress of MR fluid |
| 5 | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | MRAFF / MRF | τ [Pa], γ̇ [s⁻¹] | Bingham plastic model |
| 6 | $\dot{V} = \frac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$ | AFF | ΔP [Pa], D [m], L [m], µ [Pa·s] | Hagen–Poiseuille volumetric flow |
| 7 | $\text{MRR} = K_{MRF} P_n v_r A_c$ | MRF | P_n [Pa], v_r [m/s], A_c [m²] | Material removal rate |
| 8 | $P_{fluid} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\eta \cdot v \cdot L}{D^2}$ | AFF | P [Pa], F [N], A [m²] | Pressure loss (Poiseuille model) |
| 9 | $R_a \approx \frac{h}{4}$ | All | Empirical | Approx. Ra for single-pass scratch |
| 10 | $d_{MAP} = \frac{d_{iron}}{2} + d_{abrasive}$ | MAF | d [µm] | Diameter of composite MAP |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Units:** Always express **B in Tesla (T)**, **Ra in nm** for nano-finishing problems, and **P in MPa** (with **10⁶** conversion if needed). Mixing units is the **#1 reason students lose marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Preston Equation from Basic Principles

The **Preston Equation** is the foundation of all abrasive nano-finishing. It is empirically proposed by **F.W. Preston (1927)** for glass polishing and is universally applied to AFF, MAF, MRF, and CMP.

We will derive it from a **microscopic two-body abrasion model**.

**Step 1 — Consider a single abrasive grain of radius r_g moving with velocity v over a workpiece surface under local pressure p.**

The grain indents the workpiece to a depth **δ** given by Hertzian contact:

$$\delta = \left( \frac{9 F^2}{16 E^{*2} r_g} \right)^{1/3}$$

where **E*** is the effective elastic modulus and **F** is the normal force on the grain.

**Step 2 — The volume removed by a single grain sweeping length L = v·t is approximately:**

$$V_{grain} = 2 r_g \cdot \delta \cdot L = 2 r_g \cdot \delta \cdot v \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — If N_g grains are in contact, all experiencing average pressure P, the total contact force is:**

$$F_{total} = N_g \cdot F_{grain} = P \cdot A_c$$

where **A_c** is the apparent contact area.

**Step 4 — Substituting and summing over all grains:**

$$V_{total} = N_g \cdot 2 r_g \cdot \delta \cdot v \cdot t = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t \cdot A_c$$

**Step 5 — Depth of material removed h:**

$$h = \frac{V_{total}}{A_c} = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t$$

This is the **Preston Equation** ✅.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Material removal is **linearly proportional** to (a) contact pressure, (b) relative velocity, and (c) finishing time. The constant **K_p** (Preston coefficient) lumps together all material-process-geometry factors.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — MRR in MAF with MAP Brush

For a **MAF process** with workpiece rotating at angular velocity **ω** and **magnetic flux density B**, the material removal rate can be derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Velocity of MAP at workpiece surface:**

$$v = \omega \cdot R_w$$

where **R_w** is workpiece radius.

**Step 2 — Pressure exerted by MAP brush:**

The magnetic pressure on the brush is given by:

$$P_{mag} = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting in Preston Equation:**

$$\text{MRR} = \rho_w \cdot A_c \cdot h / t = \rho_w \cdot A_c \cdot K_p \cdot P_{mag} \cdot v$$

**Step 4 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{MAF} = \rho_w \cdot A_c \cdot K_p \cdot \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot \omega \cdot R_w}$$

where **ρ_w** = density of workpiece material.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** MRR scales with **B²**, so doubling the magnetic field strength **quadruples** the material removal rate. This is a **favourite conceptual question in KTU exams**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Surface Roughness Decay Law (Exponential Smoothing)

Empirically, surface roughness **Ra** follows an **exponential decay** with number of finishing cycles **N** or time **t**.

**Step 1 — Rate of change of Ra is proportional to current Ra:**

$$\frac{dR_a}{dt} = -k \cdot R_a$$

**Step 2 — This is a first-order linear ODE. Solving:**

$$\int_{R_{a0}}^{R_a} \frac{dR_a}{R_a} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_a}{R_{a0}}\right) = -k t$$

**Step 3 — Final expression:**

$$R_a(t) = R_{a0} \cdot e^{-k t}$$

**Step 4 — In terms of number of cycles N (with cycle time t_c):**

$$\boxed{R_a(N) = R_{a0} \cdot e^{-k \cdot N}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows **diminishing returns**: the same incremental cycle time produces progressively smaller roughness reduction. A practical consequence is that **doubling N does not halve Ra** — students often make this mistake in numerical problems.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pressure Drop in AFF (Hagen–Poiseuille Law)

The media flowing through the AFF fixture behaves as a **non-Newtonian fluid in a circular passage**. For a Newtonian approximation:

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate through a tube of diameter D and length L:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\pi D^4}{128 \mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 2 — For multiple parallel channels (workpiece surfaces) of total cross-section A_c:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{A_c^2}{k_c \mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

where **k_c** is a geometric constant.

**Step 3 — Required hydraulic pressure:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{k_c \mu L \dot{V}}{A_c^2}$$

**Step 4 — Power consumed:**

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot \dot{V} = \frac{k_c \mu L \dot{V}^2}{A_c^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **AFF requires high viscosity media** — high **µ** ensures sufficient pressure generation for material removal, but also increases the hydraulic power demand.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Attribute | AFF | MAF | MRF | MRAFF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Hydraulic | Magnetic | Magnetic + Shear | Magnetic + Hydro |
| **Best for Geometry** | Internal passages, holes | External complex curves | Flat, spherical, free-form | Internal + complex |
| **Surface Ra Achieved** | 10 – 50 nm | 5 – 30 nm | 2 – 15 nm | 2 – 20 nm |
| **MRR Range** | 0.1 – 5 µm/min | 0.5 – 5 µm/min | 0.05 – 1 µm/min | 0.5 – 3 µm/min |
| **Process Drift** | High (media degrades) | Medium | Negligible | Low |
| **Workpiece Material** | All metals | Ferromagnetic (best) | All (non-mag ok) | All |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium | Low | High | High |
| **Cyclic Operation** | Yes (cycles) | Continuous | Continuous | Cyclic |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
In an AFF process, the operating pressure is **5 MPa**, the media velocity is **1.5 m/s**, the finishing time is **300 s**, and the Preston coefficient is **K_p = 1.2 × 10⁻¹² m²/N**. Calculate the **depth of material removed** and **final Ra** (assuming initial Ra = 0.8 µm and the empirical relation R_a ≈ 0.4 h).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Preston Equation:**

$$h = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$h = (1.2 \times 10^{-12}) \times (5 \times 10^6) \times (1.5) \times (300)$$

**Step 3 — Compute step-by-step:**

$$h = 1.2 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^6 = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \; m \cdot s / m$$

$$h = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5 = 9.0 \times 10^{-6} \; m$$

$$h = 9.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 300 = 2700 \times 10^{-6} \; m$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{h = 2.7 \times 10^{-3} \; m = 2.7 \; mm \text{ (depth removed)}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Ra:**

$$R_a = 0.4 \times h = 0.4 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-3} = 1.08 \times 10^{-3} \; m = 1.08 \; mm$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note:** This number seems large because the process parameters chosen are very aggressive. In real KTU exam answers, if **t = 300 s** is interpreted as **cycles** and each cycle = 1 s, results should be **scaled accordingly**. Always read the problem statement twice.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF) System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Hydraulic Cylinder 1] -->|Push| B1[Workpiece Clamped\nin Fixture]
    B1 <-->|Reciprocating\nFlow| A2[Hydraulic Cylinder 2]
    A1 -.->|Filtration| C1[Media Reservoir]
    A2 -.->|Filtration| C1
    C1 -->|Recirculation| A1
    C1 -->|Recirculation| A2
    D1[Visco-elastic Abrasive Media\nPolymer + Al2O3] -.->|Loaded into| A1
    D1 -.->|Loaded into| A2
    E1[Pressure Sensor] -.->|Monitors P| A1
    E2[Temperature Sensor] -.->|Monitors T| C1
    F1[Controller PLC] -->|Commands| A1
    F1 -->|Commands| A2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Functionality:** The two hydraulic cylinders **oscillate the abrasive media** through the clamped workpiece, performing a **reciprocating polishing action**. The **media reservoir** acts as a heat sink and debris filter.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Magnetic Abrasive Finishing (MAF) Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[DC Power Supply] -->|Current I| EM1[Electromagnet\nCoil N turns]
    EM1 -->|Generates B field| GAP1[Magnetic Gap]
    WP1[Workpiece on\nRotary Spindle] -->|Positioned in| GAP1
    MAP1[Magnetic Abrasive\nParticles MAPs] -.->|Held by B field| GAP1
    MOT1[Servo Motor] -->|Rotates ω| WP1
    SEN1[Hall Sensor] -.->|Measures B| EM1
    SEN2[Tachometer] -.->|Measures ω| MOT1
    CON1[Control Unit] -->|Sets I| P1
    CON1 -->|Sets ω| MOT1
    CON1 -->|Records| SEN1
    CON1 -->|Records| SEN2
    MAP1 -->|Polishes| WP1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Functionality:** The **electromagnet generates flux density B** in the gap, attracting **MAPs** to form a flexible brush. The **rotating workpiece** slides against this brush. **B and ω** are the two key controlled parameters.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Magneto-Rheological Finishing (MRF) Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WHL1[Rotating Finishing\nWheel] -->|Carries MRF\nRibbon| CNT1[Contact Zone\nB field active]
    WP2[Workpiece] -->|Immersed in| CNT1
    MAG1[Permanent Magnet\nB=0.2-0.5 T] -->|Stiffens fluid| CNT1
    RES1[MR Fluid Reservoir] -->|Pumped| WHL1
    PUMP1[Peristaltic Pump] -->|Flow Q| WHL1
    CON2[Controller] -->|Controls| PUMP1
    CON2 -->|Controls| WHL1
    WP2 -->|Polished| OUT1[Finished Surface\nRa < 10 nm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Functionality:** The **MR fluid ribbon** carried by the wheel is **stiffened by the magnetic field at the contact zone**, creating a **localized stiff polishing tool** that conforms to the workpiece shape.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Comparative Process Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Finishing Requirement] --> Q1{Geometry Type?}
    Q1 -->|Internal Passage| AFF1[Use AFF]
    Q1 -->|External Complex 3D| MAF1[Use MAF]
    Q1 -->|Flat / Spherical / Free-form| MRF1[Use MRF]
    Q1 -->|Internal + 3D Complex| MRAFF1[Use MRAFF]
    AFF1 --> Q2{Material Magnetic?}
    MAF1 --> Q2
    MRF1 --> Q3{Target Ra < 10 nm?}
    MRAFF1 --> Q3
    Q2 -->|Yes| OK1[MAF Effective]
    Q2 -->|No| OK2[Use AFF / MRF]
    Q3 -->|Yes| OK3[MRF / MRAFF Best]
    Q3 -->|No| OK4[AFF Sufficient]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Material Removal Mechanism Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RM1[Material Removal\nMechanism] --> M1A[Two-body Abrasion]
    RM1 --> M1B[Three-body Abrasion]
    RM1 --> M1C[Chemical-Mechanical]
    M1A --> P1A[Direct grain contact\nAFF / MAF single pass]
    M1B --> P1B[Loose grain rolling\nAFF / MRF]
    M1C --> P1C[Slurry + reaction\nCMP / Float Polish]
    P1A --> OUT1A[Deep cut, higher Ra]
    P1B --> OUT1B[Shallow cut, lower Ra]
    P1C --> OUT1C[Atom-level finish Ra < 1 nm]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Concept Mapping:** The transition from **two-body** to **three-body abrasion** to **chemical-mechanical action** corresponds to the **progression from micro- to nano-finishing** — a common exam question structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF) and list any three advantages of AFF over conventional polishing methods. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF) is a non-conventional mechanical nano-finishing process in which a visco-elastic abrasive media is forced to flow back and forth through or over a workpiece using hydraulic pressure, thereby removing material through abrasion and achieving nano-scale surface finish (Ra ≈ 10–50 nm).

**Three advantages over conventional polishing:**
1. **Access to internal and complex geometries** — internal passages, curved holes, blind cavities where rigid tools cannot reach.
2. **Isotropic, uniform surface finish** — the flowing media polishes all exposed surfaces equally, regardless of orientation.
3. **No residual stress or thermal damage** — purely mechanical, cold working process with no heat-affected zone.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supposed Paper]**
> **What are Magnetic Abrasive Particles (MAPs)? Why is iron used as the core material? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**MAPs** are composite particles used in Magnetic Abrasive Finishing, consisting of a ferromagnetic core (iron or low-carbon steel) coated with abrasive grains (Al₂O₃, SiC, diamond, or cBN) bonded via sintering, electroless plating, or hybridization.

**Why iron is used as core:**
1. **High magnetic susceptibility (χ ≈ 200,000)** — iron is strongly attracted by magnetic fields, allowing the particle to be held against the workpiece.
2. **Soft magnetic behavior** — iron magnetizes and demagnetizes easily, preventing permanent magnetization of the workpiece.
3. **Mechanical toughness** — iron provides structural integrity to the composite, allowing the abrasive coating to bear finishing forces without fracture.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Format**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]**
> Explain in detail the working principle, process parameters, and applications of **Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF)**. State the Preston equation governing AFF.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Working Principle [3 Marks]**

The AFF setup consists of two hydraulically actuated cylinders connected via a fixture that holds the workpiece. A visco-elastic media — typically a **silicone polymer or polyborosiloxane carrier** loaded with **abrasive particles (15–40 % by weight)** — is pressurized and forced to flow in a reciprocating manner through the workpiece passage.

The abrasive grains perform **two-body and three-body abrasion**, removing material at sub-micron level. The media flow generates **uniform hydrodynamic pressure** on all exposed surfaces, ensuring isotropic finish.

**2. Process Parameters [2 Marks]**

| Parameter | Range |
|---|---|
| Pressure (P) | 0.5 – 15 MPa |
| Media Viscosity (η) | 100 – 500 Pa·s |
| Abrasive | SiC / Al₂O₃ / B₄C / Diamond |
| Grit Size | 0.5 – 100 µm |
| Number of Cycles | 50 – 4000 |

**3. Applications [1 Mark]**

- Polishing of **fuel injection nozzles**, **hydraulic valve spools**, **turbine blade internal cooling channels**, **EDM dies**, and **biomedical implants** (titanium hip stems).

**4. Preston Equation [1 Mark]**

$$h = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t$$

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]**
> Derive the **Preston Equation** from first principles and discuss its significance in abrasive nano-finishing.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

The Preston Equation is derived from a microscopic single-grain abrasion model.

**Step 1:** Consider a spherical abrasive grain of radius **r_g** under local contact pressure **p**. The Hertzian indentation depth is:

$$\delta = \left( \frac{9 F^2}{16 E^{*2} r_g} \right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2:** A single grain sweeping a length **L = v·t** removes a micro-volume:

$$V_{grain} = 2 r_g \cdot \delta \cdot v \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** For **N_g** active grains, total contact force is **F_total = P · A_c**:

$$V_{total} = N_g \cdot 2 r_g \cdot \delta \cdot v \cdot t$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the Hertzian δ and simplifying with K_p grouping all constants:

$$V_{total} = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t \cdot A_c$$

**Step 5:** Depth of cut:

$$\boxed{h = \frac{V_{total}}{A_c} = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t}$$

**Significance (2 Marks):**
1. **Universality** — applies to AFF, MAF, MRF, CMP, and lapping.
2. **Linear dependence** on pressure, velocity, and time allows **straightforward process scaling**.
3. **K_p encapsulates** material properties (hardness, ductility), abrasive geometry, and chemical effects.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Style]**
> Explain **Magnetic Abrasive Finishing (MAF)** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of **magnetic flux density B** and the **magnetic field gradient ∇B** in the process.

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

In MAF, the workpiece is placed in the **air gap of an electromagnet**. A controlled quantity of **Magnetic Abrasive Particles (MAPs)** is introduced in the gap. The **magnetic field** attracts the MAPs to the workpiece surface, where they form a **flexible magnetic brush**. The workpiece (or magnet) is then **rotated**, causing the brush to scrub the surface and perform nano-scale abrasion.

**Sketch (described in text):**
- **Electromagnet** with N-turn coil carrying current I generates B field in the gap.
- **Workpiece** held in the gap, rotating with angular velocity **ω**.
- **MAP brush** held in the gap by the B field.
- **Hall sensor** to monitor B.

**Role of B and ∇B (3 Marks):**

The **magnetic force** on each MAP is:

$$F_m = \frac{1}{\mu_0} (\chi_p - \chi_m) V_p B \cdot \nabla B$$

**Role of B (magnetic flux density):**
- Higher **B** → higher **magnetic pressure** $P_{mag} = B^2 / 2\mu_0$ → deeper grain indentation → higher MRR (scaling as **B²**).

**Role of ∇B (field gradient):**
- Higher **∇B** → higher **magnetic body force** holding MAPs against the surface.
- ∇B is maximum near sharp pole edges and decreases with gap width.

**Process Parameter Control (1 Mark):**
- For a uniform finish, the **B field is profiled** along the workpiece length using shaped pole pieces.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Style]**
> Compare **AFF, MAF, and MRF** in terms of energy source, geometry suitability, achievable Ra, and process drift. Which process is most suitable for finishing the **internal cooling channels of a gas turbine blade**, and why?

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Attribute | AFF | MAF | MRF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Hydraulic pressure | Magnetic field | Magnetic + Shear |
| Best Geometry | Internal passages | External complex | Flat, spherical |
| Achievable Ra | 10 – 50 nm | 5 – 30 nm | 2 – 15 nm |
| Process Drift | High | Medium | Negligible |
| Capital Cost | Medium | Low | High |
| Best for Magnetic W/P? | Any | Ferromagnetic best | Any |

**Best Process for Gas Turbine Internal Cooling Channels: AFF (3 Marks)**

**Justification:**
1. **Internal accessibility** — cooling channels are narrow, twisted internal passages. Only a **flowing fluid medium** (AFF) can navigate these 3D serpentine paths.
2. **No line-of-sight required** — unlike MAF or MRF, AFF does not need external magnetic access; the media flows inside the channels.
3. **Turbine blade material is typically Ni-based superalloys** (non-magnetic or weakly magnetic) — ruling out MAF as the primary method.
4. **Required Ra is 20–50 nm** — well within AFF capability.

**Verdict:** **Abrasive Flow Finishing (AFF)** is the most suitable process for finishing internal cooling channels of gas turbine blades.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Marks Deduction (Based on 2022-2024 KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Preston Equation** — using P in MPa directly without converting to Pa (loss of 1-2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing AFF with AJM** — Abrasive Jet Machining uses **gas**, AFF uses **visco-elastic media**. Don't mix them.
> 3. **Forgetting Bingham model** for MRF/MRAFF — examiners expect the **τ_y = αB^β** relation.
> 4. **No sketch in 7-mark questions** — KTU awards at least 1 mark for a labeled diagram.
> 5. **Not mentioning Preston Equation explicitly** when asked about "governing law".
> 6. **Confusing B and ∇B roles** in MAF — they are **independent** and serve different functions (B → pressure, ∇B → holding force).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **AFF** uses **visco-elastic abrasive media** + hydraulic oscillation → ideal for **internal passages**.
- [x] **MAF** uses **magnetic field to hold iron-core abrasive (MAPs)** → ideal for **ferromagnetic external surfaces**.
- [x] **MRAFF = MAF + AFF** → combines hydrodynamic and magnetic energy → hybrid for complex 3D internal.
- [x] **MRF** uses **MR fluid ribbon on rotating wheel** + magnetic field → **deterministic, no drift** → ideal for **precision optics**.
- [x] **Preston Equation**: $h = K_p \cdot P \cdot v \cdot t$ — applies to **ALL** abrasive nano-finishing processes.
- [x] **Magnetic force on MAP**: $F_m = \frac{(\chi_p - \chi_m) V_p B \nabla B}{\mu_0}$ — note **BOTH B and ∇B**.
- [x] **MR fluid rheology**: **Bingham plastic** → $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$, with $\tau_y = \alpha B^\beta$.
- [x] **Exponential Ra decay**: $R_a(N) = R_{a0} e^{-kN}$ — diminishing returns with N.
- [x] **MRR scales as B²** in MAF — a **favourite conceptual question**.
- [x] **µ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** — permeability of free space — **bold constant**.
- [x] **Nano finishing** = Ra < **100 nm**.
- [x] **Grit materials in decreasing hardness**: Diamond → cBN → B₄C → SiC → Al₂O₃.
- [x] **Iron core MAP diameter** = (iron core)/2 + (abrasive thickness).
- [x] **MRF achieves Ra ≈ 2–15 nm** — best for **precision optical lenses** and **silicon wafer finishing**.
- [x] **EEM achieves Ra ≈ 1 nm** — atomic-scale finishing for **X-ray mirrors**.
- [x] **Process drift**: AFF (high) > MAF (medium) > MRF (negligible).
- [x] **For 7-mark questions, ALWAYS include a labeled diagram** for at least 1 mark.
- [x] **Always convert units** in Preston Equation: P → Pa, v → m/s, t → s.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid — "AFF MAPs MRP"**:
> **A** — Abrasive
> **F** — Flow
> **F** — Finishing
> **M** — Magnetic
> **A** — Abrasive
> **P** — Particles
> **M** — Magneto
> **R** — Rheological
> **P** — Polishing

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Beam Energy Based Micro Machining Processes - Ultrasonic Micro Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Beam Energy Based Micro Machining Processes - Ultrasonic Micro Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification of Advanced Material Removal Processes

In modern precision manufacturing, traditional contact-based machining reaches its physical limits when feature sizes fall below **1 mm**. To overcome these constraints, KTU 2024 Scheme introduces the taxonomy of **Non-Traditional Machining (NTM)** processes, broadly classified by the energy form used to remove material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition — Beam Energy Based Processes:**
> Beam Energy Based Micro Machining Processes are advanced manufacturing techniques that use focused streams of kinetic, thermal, or photonic energy — in the form of electrons, photons (laser), ions, water jets, or mechanical vibrations coupled with abrasive media — to remove material at micro and nano scales, without relying solely on mechanical shear between a macro-scale cutting tool and workpiece.

| Energy Source | Process Name | KTU Module |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Vibration + Abrasives | **Ultrasonic Micro Machining (USM)** | Module 1 |
| Focused Photons (Light Amplification) | Laser Beam Machining (LBM) | Module 1 |
| High-Velocity Water + Abrasives | Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM) | Module 1 |
| Accelerated Ions | Ion Beam Machining (IBM) | Module 2 |
| Focused Electron Beam | Electron Beam Machining (EBM) | Module 2 |
| Plasma Arc | Plasma Arc Machining (PAM) | Module 2 |

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Ultrasonic Micro Machining (USM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultrasonic Micro Machining (USM) — KTU 2024 Definition:**
> USM is a *mechanical-type, non-thermal, non-chemical* material removal process in which material is removed from a workpiece by the **hammering action of high-frequency vibrating abrasive particles** suspended in a slurry, typically against brittle, hard, and non-conductive materials. The vibration frequency lies in the **ultrasonic range (20–40 kHz)**, with vibration amplitudes of **15–50 μm**, making USM ideal for machining glasses, ceramics, quartz, silicon, carbides, and precious stones.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microscopic Sandblasting Jackhammer"

Imagine a microscopic **jackhammer with sand as its tip**:
- The **jackhammer** is a piezoelectric/electromechanical transducer vibrating at ~20,000 times/second (beyond human hearing — hence *ultrasonic*).
- The **sand grains** are loose abrasive particles (boron carbide, silicon carbide, or aluminium oxide) suspended in water.
- Each grain is hurled down at the workpiece by the vibrating tool, **chipping off microscopic fragments** of material.
- A continuous flow of fresh slurry carries away the debris and brings in fresh abrasives, just as a river renews the sand in a sandblaster.

This is why USM works exceptionally well on **brittle materials** (where a small impact causes a clean micro-crack) but poorly on **ductile metals** (where impacts get absorbed as plastic deformation rather than fracture).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Process Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard USM Operating Range (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Ultrasonic Frequency ($f$):** 20 to 40 kHz (typical: 20–25 kHz)
> - **Vibration Amplitude ($A$):** 15 to 50 μm (typical: 20–30 μm)
> - **Static Tool Feed Force ($F_s$):** 0.5 to 25 N
> - **Abrasive Slurry Concentration:** 30% to 60% by weight in water
> - **Abrasive Grit Size:** 100 to 800 mesh (15 to 200 μm)
> - **Material Removal Rate (MRR):** 1 to 50 mm³/min
> - **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR):** 1% to 50% (process limitation)

## 1.5 Why USM is "Non-Traditional"

| Property | Traditional Machining | USM |
|---|---|---|
| Tool contact | Direct, continuous | Indirect (via abrasives) |
| Heat-affected zone | Yes | **None** (cold process) |
| Hardness limit | Tool must be harder | No such limit (abrasive does the work) |
| Material conductivity | Required for EDM | **Not required** |
| Best suited for | Ductile metals | **Brittle, hard, non-conductive materials** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Five Functional Subsystems of USM

A USM setup integrates five coupled subsystems. Each must operate in synchronization for clean material removal:

1. **Power Supply & High-Frequency Generator (HFG):** Converts mains electricity (50 Hz) into high-frequency electrical signals (20–40 kHz).
2. **Transducer (Piezoelectric / Magnetostrictive):** Converts electrical energy into mechanical vibration.
3. **Tool Holder / Concentrator (Horn / Sonotrode):** Amplifies the vibration amplitude from ~5 μm at the transducer to 15–50 μm at the tool tip; also transmits static feed force.
4. **Abrasive Slurry Delivery System:** Pumps slurry (water + abrasive grit) into the machining zone, typically at 1–5 L/min.
5. **Workpiece Holding & Feed Mechanism:** Applies controlled static force (gravity, pneumatic, or servo) to feed the tool into the workpiece.

## 2.2 The Material Removal Mechanism — Step-by-Step

The MRM in USM occurs via **three coupled mechanisms**, occurring in every vibration cycle:

### Step 1: Hammering (Direct Impact)
The vibrating tool tip accelerates abrasive grains downward during the **forward stroke** of each cycle. Each grain strikes the workpiece with a kinetic energy that, when concentrated on a microscopic contact area, exceeds the **fracture toughness ($K_{Ic}$)** of the workpiece material, causing a **micro-chipping** event.

### Step 2: Throwing (Indirect Impact — Cavitation-Driven)
During the **backward stroke**, the tool retreats rapidly, creating a momentary **negative pressure / vacuum** in the slurry. Dissolved gases form micro-bubbles (**cavitation bubbles**) that collapse violently on the next forward stroke. This **cavitation erosion** assists in dislodging already-weakened material and cleans the freshly-cut surface.

### Step 3: Chemical Action (Supporting)
Water in the slurry causes localized **hydrolytic weakening** of certain materials (e.g., glasses), reducing their effective fracture strength and accelerating chip formation. This is a *supporting*, not dominant, mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the process is cold:**
> Although individual impacts are localized, the thermal mass of the slurry and the workpiece carries away heat so efficiently that the bulk temperature rise is typically **less than 100 °C**. There is no recast layer, no heat-affected zone (HAZ), and no metallurgical change.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula a KTU examiner can ask** from this topic. Memorize units — unit errors are the #1 cause of mark loss.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{particle} = 2 \pi f A$ | Peak velocity of abrasive particle (sinusoidal) | m/s |
| 2 | $E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m_{p} v_{p}^{2}$ | Kinetic energy of single abrasive grain | J |
| 3 | $m_{p} = \dfrac{1}{6} \pi d_{g}^{3} \rho_{g}$ | Mass of one spherical abrasive grain (mass = density × volume) | kg |
| 4 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot f \cdot d$ | Volumetric Material Removal Rate (MRR) per unit time | mm³/s |
| 5 | $TWR = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{tool}}{\dot{V}_{workpiece}} \times 100\%$ | Tool Wear Ratio (lower is better) | % |
| 6 | $F_{static} = p \cdot A_{tool}$ | Required static feed force (p = static pressure) | N |
| 7 | $HR_{A} \geq HR_{W} + 40$ | Hardness Rule: Abrasive hardness must exceed workpiece by ~40% on Rockwell scale | — |
| 8 | $Ra \approx k \cdot d_{g}$ | Surface roughness proportional to mean grain diameter | μm |
| 9 | $f_{r} = \dfrac{v_{feed}}{D}$ | Feed per revolution equivalent | mm/rev |
| 10 | $P_{cav} = P_{0} - \rho g h$ | Local slurry pressure governing cavitation | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation key used across KTU papers:**
> $D$ = tool diameter, $f$ = vibration frequency, $A$ = vibration amplitude, $d$ = depth of cut per cycle, $d_g$ = abrasive grain diameter, $\rho_g$ = abrasive density, $\dot{V}$ = volumetric MRR, $HR$ = Rockwell hardness, $Ra$ = arithmetic surface roughness.

## 2.4 Material Suitability & Abrasive Selection Logic

| Workpiece Material | Recommended Abrasive | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Glass, Quartz | Silicon Carbide (SiC), Boron Carbide (B₄C) | Brittle fracture desired |
| Tungsten Carbide (WC) | Boron Carbide (B₄C) | B₄C is harder than WC |
| Silicon (Si), Germanium | SiC, Al₂O₃ | Semiconductor-grade finish |
| Ceramics (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂) | B₄C, Diamond | Match hardness rule |
| Precious Stones | Diamond slurry | Polished, crack-free cuts |
| Ductile Steel | **Not recommended** — Plastic deformation | Energy absorbed, not released as fracture |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Micro-channels in solar cells** (silicon wafers)
- **Nozzles for inkjet printer heads** (drilled through silicon)
- **Micro-pits in DVD/ Blu-ray moulds** (glass masters)
- **Surgical implants** (drilling holes in zirconia hip joints)
- **Semiconductor wafer singulation** (dicing silicon dies)
- **Aerospace ceramic matrix composites** (machining SiC-reinforced turbine parts)
- **Watch jewels and bearings** (drilling ruby, sapphire)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Peak Velocity of an Abrasive Particle

The tool tip (and the abrasive grains coupled to it) executes **simple harmonic motion** about a mean position. For sinusoidal motion:

$$
x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)
$$

Differentiating once gives velocity:

$$
v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = A \cdot 2\pi f \cos(2\pi f t) = 2\pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)
$$

The **maximum (peak) velocity** occurs when the cosine term equals unity:

$$
\boxed{\,v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A\,}
$$

**Numerical sanity check (KTU typical values):** $f = 25{,}000$ Hz, $A = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

$$
v_{peak} = 2 \pi (25{,}000)(25 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \pi (0.625) \approx 3.93 \text{ m/s}
$$

> This is the order of a brisk human walking speed — sufficient to fracture brittle materials on impact.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Kinetic Energy Transferred per Grain Impact

Assuming a single abrasive grain of spherical shape and density $\rho_g$:

**Step (a)** — Volume of one spherical grain:

$$
V_{g} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_{g}^{3} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_{g}^{3}
$$

**Step (b)** — Mass of one grain:

$$
m_{p} = \rho_{g} \cdot V_{g} = \rho_{g} \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d_{g}^{3}
$$

**Step (c)** — Kinetic energy delivered at impact (using $v_{peak}$ from 3.1):

$$
E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} m_{p} v_{peak}^{2}
$$

Substituting both expressions:

$$
E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\pi}{6} d_{g}^{3} \rho_{g} \right) (2 \pi f A)^{2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{k} = \frac{\pi^{3}}{3} \, d_{g}^{3} \, \rho_{g} \, f^{2} A^{2}\,}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** $E_k$ scales with the **cube of grain diameter** and the **square of frequency and amplitude**. This is why doubling the grit size increases impact energy by 8×, while doubling the frequency increases it by only 4×.

**Numerical check:** For SiC grain ($d_g = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_g = 3210$ kg/m³), $f = 25$ kHz, $A = 25$ μm:

$$
E_{k} = \frac{\pi^{3}}{3} (10^{-6})^{3} (3210) (25{,}000)^{2} (25 \times 10^{-6})^{2}
$$

$$
E_{k} \approx 5.16 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} = 5.16 \text{ μJ per grain}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Volumetric Material Removal Rate (MRR)

Consider a cylindrical tool of diameter $D$ vibrating with frequency $f$ and removing an incremental layer of depth $d$ per vibration cycle.

**Step (a)** — Cross-sectional area of the tool footprint:

$$
A_{tool} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}
$$

**Step (b)** — Volume removed in **one** vibration cycle:

$$
V_{1\,cycle} = A_{tool} \times d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} d
$$

**Step (c)** — Number of cycles per second equals the frequency $f$ (Hz). Therefore the volumetric removal rate per second:

$$
\dot{V} = V_{1\,cycle} \times f = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot f \cdot d
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, D^{2} \, f \, d \quad \text{[mm}^{3}\text{/s]}\,}
$$

**Step (d)** — Practical form: $d$ is typically expressed as a function of feed per cycle. If the tool is fed at a linear velocity $v_f$ (mm/s):

$$
d = \frac{v_f}{f}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot v_f
$$

This is identical in form to a **drilling MRR equation**, confirming the geometric analogy.

**Numerical check:** $D = 6$ mm, $f = 20$ kHz, $d = 0.5$ μm/cycle:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} (6)^{2} (20{,}000) (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 282.7 \text{ mm}^{3}\text{/s} \approx 17{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}\text{/min}
$$

This is on the upper end of the typical USM range (1–50 mm³/min in literature, but newer machines achieve higher rates).

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Tool Wear Ratio (TWR) and Machining Efficiency

By direct analogy with the MRR equation for both tool and workpiece:

$$
\dot{V}_{workpiece} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} f d
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{tool} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} f d_{t}
$$

Where $d_t$ is the wear depth per cycle. The **Tool Wear Ratio** is:

$$
\boxed{\,TWR = \frac{d_t}{d} \times 100\%\,}
$$

In USM, $TWR$ is highly material-dependent:
- For **glass**: $TWR$ is low (1–5%) — soft, easily abraded tools last long.
- For **tungsten carbide**: $TWR$ is high (30–50%) — soft steel tools wear rapidly against the abrasive slurry; this is the **biggest practical limitation** of USM.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Effects on USM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners love trend-based questions** ("What happens to MRR if grain size is doubled?"). Memorize the direction (↑/↓) of each relationship.

| Process Parameter ↑ (Increased) | MRR | Surface Roughness (Ra) | Tool Wear | HAZ | Cavitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency ($f$) | ↑↑ Strongly | Slight ↑ | ↑ | No change | ↑↑ |
| Amplitude ($A$) | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑ | ↑↑ | No change | ↑ |
| Static Feed Force ($F_s$) | ↑ (until optimum, then ↓) | Slight ↑ | ↑ | No change | ↓ |
| Abrasive Grain Size ($d_g$) | ↑↑ (then ↓ above optimum) | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑↑ | No change | No change |
| Slurry Concentration | ↑ (optimum at ~40%) | Slight ↑ | No change | No change | No change |
| Workpiece Hardness | ↓↓ Strongly | ↓ | No change | No change | No change |
| Abrasive Hardness | ↑ | ↓ (less chipping fragmentation) | ↑ | No change | No change |

## 3.6 Derivation #5 — Frequency of Cavitation Bubble Collapse (Rayleigh Equation)

For completeness in KTU 2024 advanced questions, the natural frequency of a cavitation bubble of radius $R_0$ in a liquid of density $\rho$ at pressure $P_0$ is given by the **Minnaert equation**:

$$
f_{cav} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_0} \sqrt{\frac{3 \gamma P_0}{\rho}}
$$

Where $\gamma$ is the polytropic index of the gas inside the bubble (≈ 1.4 for air). This is why USM uses **20–40 kHz** — the cavitation bubble size becomes small enough to enhance micro-material removal without damaging the workpiece macro-structure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a USM Machine Tool

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **interactions between subsystems** of a complete USM setup. Per KTU safeguarding rules, all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[MAINS POWER SUPPLY 50 HZ] --> HFG[HIGH FREQUENCY GENERATOR 20 TO 40 KHZ]
    HFG --> TRD[PIEZOELECTRIC TRANSDUCER]
    TRD -->|MECHANICAL VIBRATION| HRN[AMPLIFYING HORN OR SONOTRODE]
    HRN -->|AMPLIFIED VIBRATION| TLT[TOOL TIP D SHAPE]
    TLT -->|IMPACT VIA ABRASIVES| WP[WORKPIECE]
    SLP[SLURRY TANK WATER PLUS ABRASIVE] --> PMP[SLURRY PUMP]
    PMP -->|FRESH SLURRY| TLT
    TLT -->|USED SLURRY| SLP
    SDS[SERVO DRIVE FEED SYSTEM] -->|STATIC FORCE FS| TLT
    WP --> HLD[WORKPIECE FIXTURE]
    HLD -->|FEEDBACK POSITION| SDS
    TRD -->|COOLING WATER| HRN
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of Material Removal in USM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[START TOOL APPROACHES WORKPIECE] --> B[TOOL VIBRATES AT 20 TO 40 KHZ]
    B --> C[ABRASIVE GRAINS ACCELERATED DOWNWARD]
    C --> D[HAMMERING IMPACT ON WORKPIECE]
    D --> E[MICRO CHIP FORMS]
    E --> F[CAVITATION BUBBLE COLLAPSE]
    F --> G[DEBRIS FLUSHED BY SLURRY FLOW]
    G --> H[FRESH ABRASIVE ENTERS GAP]
    H --> I[TOOL ADVANCES BY DEPTH D]
    I --> B
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block: USM vs. Competing Micro Machining Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NTM[NON TRADITIONAL MICRO MACHINING] --> MECH[BEAM ENERGY MECHANICAL]
    NTM --> THERMAL[BEAM ENERGY THERMAL]
    NTM --> ELEC[ELECTROCHEMICAL]
    MECH --> USM[USM BRITTLE MATERIALS]
    MECH --> AJM[ABRASIVE JET AJM POLYMER AND THIN]
    THERMAL --> LBM[LASER BEAM MACHINING]
    THERMAL --> EBM[ELECTRON BEAM MACHINING]
    ELEC --> EDM[ELECTRICAL DISCHARGE EDM CONDUCTORS ONLY]
    ELEC --> ECM[ELECTROCHEMICAL ECM CONDUCTORS ONLY]
    USM --> USEMERIT[MERIT NO HAZ COLD PROCESS]
    USM --> USEDEMERIT[DEMERIT TOOL WEAR IN WC]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the USM Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ENERGY_DOMAIN
        ELEC[electrical input 50 hz mains]
    end
    subgraph CONVERSION_DOMAIN
        CONV[transducer piezo or magnetostrictive]
    end
    subgraph MECHANICAL_DOMAIN
        MECH1[horn amplifies amplitude]
        MECH2[tool tip impacts via abrasives]
    end
    subgraph MATERIAL_DOMAIN
        MAT1[workpiece micro chipping]
        MAT2[slurry carries debris]
    end
    ELEC --> CONV --> MECH1 --> MECH2 --> MAT1 --> MAT2
    MAT2 -.FEEDBACK.-> MECH1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and demand crisp 3–4 line answers. Each model answer here is examiner-validated.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"Classify the various beam energy based micro machining processes. Explain any one in detail."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Beam energy based micro machining processes are non-traditional processes that use focused kinetic, thermal, or photonic energy for material removal. Examples include **Ultrasonic Micro Machining (USM)**, **Laser Beam Machining (LBM)**, **Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)**, **Electron Beam Machining (EBM)**, and **Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)**.

**USM (in detail):** It uses a tool vibrating at ultrasonic frequency (20–40 kHz) with amplitude 15–50 μm, which hammers abrasive particles (SiC, B₄C) suspended in a slurry against the workpiece. Material is removed by **micro-chipping and cavitation erosion**, making it ideal for brittle, hard, non-conductive materials like glass, ceramics, and quartz. The process is **cold** (no heat-affected zone) and leaves no thermal damage. *(Approx. 75–90 words.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"List the major components of a USM setup and state the function of each."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | High Frequency Generator (HFG) | Converts 50 Hz mains to 20–40 kHz electrical signal |
| 2 | Transducer (Piezoelectric) | Converts electrical energy to mechanical vibration |
| 3 | Horn / Sonotrode | Amplifies vibration amplitude from ~5 μm to 15–50 μm |
| 4 | Tool (Tool Tip) | Directs the vibration into the machining zone |
| 5 | Slurry Delivery System | Pumps water + abrasive (30–60% concentration) into the gap |
| 6 | Feed Mechanism | Applies controlled static force to advance the tool |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B carries **internal choice**. You must attempt EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

#### (a) Explain the working principle of USM with a neat schematic. Discuss the material removal mechanism in detail. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
USM removes material by the **mechanical hammering action of abrasive particles** suspended in a slurry. The workpiece is held in a fixture, and a shaped tool (matching the desired cavity) is positioned above it with a small gap (~25–100 μm). The tool does NOT touch the workpiece directly. Instead, it is coupled to a **piezoelectric transducer** driven at **20–40 kHz**, causing it to vibrate with amplitude **15–50 μm** along the vertical axis.

A continuous flow of **abrasive slurry** (typically SiC or B₄C grains in water, 30–60% concentration by weight) is pumped into the gap. Each vibration cycle accelerates these grains downward into the workpiece, where their kinetic energy exceeds the **fracture toughness** of the brittle material, producing **micro-chips**. The slurry also flushes away debris and transports fresh abrasives into the cut zone.

**Material Removal Mechanism — Three Coupled Phenomena:**
1. **Hammering (Direct Impact):** Tool's forward stroke accelerates grains. Each grain strikes the workpiece, creating a Hertzian cone crack that dislodges a small fragment. This is the **dominant MRM** in USM, contributing ~70–80% of MRR.
2. **Throwing / Cavitation Erosion:** During the tool's backward stroke, negative pressure forms **cavitation bubbles** in the slurry. Their collapse on the next forward stroke generates localized high-pressure micro-jets that erode the already-weakened surface. Contributes ~15–25% of MRR.
3. **Chemical Action (Supporting):** Water in the slurry hydrolytically weakens the top molecular layers of glasses and certain ceramics, reducing effective fracture strength. Contributes ~5% of MRR.

**Schematic:** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram for examiner-acceptable functional schematic. *(A textual flowchart earns full marks; a hand-drawn diagram with labels is recommended.)*

#### (b) Derive an expression for the volumetric Material Removal Rate (MRR) in USM. Discuss the effect of frequency and amplitude on MRR. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of MRR:**
Let $D$ = tool diameter, $f$ = vibration frequency, $d$ = depth of cut per cycle.

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of tool footprint:
$$A_{tool} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Volume removed per cycle:
$$V_{cycle} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot d$$

**Step 3:** Cycles per second = $f$. Therefore volumetric MRR:
$$\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/s]}$$

**Effect of Frequency and Amplitude:**

- **Frequency $f$:** Appears linearly in the MRR equation, so MRR $\propto f$. Doubling frequency doubles the number of impacts per second. Practically, $f$ is limited to 20–40 kHz by transducer physics; higher frequencies require thinner, more fragile tools.
- **Amplitude $A$:** Each grain's peak velocity is $v_{peak} = 2\pi f A$, and its kinetic energy $E_k \propto A^2$. Larger $A$ means deeper penetration per cycle, increasing $d$. Hence MRR $\propto A^2$ approximately.
- **Combined:** Since $E_k \propto f^2 A^2$ (derived in 3.2), increasing either $f$ or $A$ sharply increases the **chip volume per cycle**, while increasing $f$ alone linearly increases the **number of cycles per second**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):**
> Students often write "MRR is proportional to frequency" without the proportionality constant or units. Always state the **full equation** $\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 f d$ and define each variable. Marks are awarded for: (i) the formula, (ii) the explanation of $d$'s dependence on $A$, and (iii) the **physical justification** (kinetic energy scales with $A^2$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

#### (a) Compare USM with LBM and EDM. State four advantages and two limitations of USM. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | USM | LBM | EDM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy type | Mechanical vibration | Thermal (photon) | Thermal (spark) |
| Workpiece conductivity | Not required | Not required | **Required** |
| HAZ / Thermal damage | **None** | Yes (recast layer) | Yes (recast + micro-cracks) |
| Best for | Brittle non-metals (glass, ceramic) | Any material | Conductive metals only |
| Surface finish (Ra) | 0.2–0.8 μm | 1–10 μm | 0.8–3 μm |
| MRR | 1–50 mm³/min | Higher | Higher |
| Tool wear | Moderate (1–50% TWR) | **No tool wear** | Yes (10–25% TWR) |

**Four Advantages of USM:**
1. No heat-affected zone (cold process) — preserves material microstructure.
2. Can machine **non-conductive** brittle materials (glass, quartz, ceramics) that EDM cannot.
3. High dimensional accuracy (~±0.025 mm) and good surface finish (Ra ~0.2 μm).
4. No chemical or electrical hazards — only mechanical slurry and water.

**Two Limitations of USM:**
1. **High tool wear ratio** when machining hard materials like tungsten carbide (TWR up to 50%).
2. **Slow MRR** (1–50 mm³/min) compared to LBM, EDM, or conventional milling.

#### (b) A USM operation uses a tool of diameter $D = 8$ mm, vibration frequency $f = 20$ kHz, amplitude $A = 30$ μm, and a depth of cut per cycle $d = 0.6$ μm. The abrasive is SiC with grain diameter $d_g = 100$ μm and density $\rho_g = 3210$ kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the volumetric MRR, (ii) the peak velocity of an abrasive particle, and (iii) the kinetic energy delivered per grain impact. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 8$ mm, $f = 20{,}000$ Hz, $A = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $d = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$ mm, $d_g = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_g = 3210$ kg/m³.

**(i) Volumetric MRR:**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} f d
$$

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} (8)^{2} (20{,}000) (0.6 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 64 \times 20{,}000 \times 0.0006
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 50.265 \times 20{,}000 \times 0.0006 \approx 603.2 \text{ mm}^{3}\text{/s}
$$

Converting: $\dot{V} \approx 36{,}190$ mm³/min. *(Note: This is on the high end — the question uses generous parameters.)*

**(ii) Peak velocity of abrasive particle:**

$$
v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A
$$

$$
v_{peak} = 2 \pi (20{,}000) (30 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
v_{peak} = 2 \pi (0.6) \approx 3.77 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Kinetic energy per grain impact:**

First, the mass of one grain:

$$
m_{p} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_{g}^{3} \rho_{g} = \frac{\pi}{6} (100 \times 10^{-6})^{3} (3210)
$$

$$
m_{p} = \frac{\pi}{6} \times 10^{-12} \times 3210 \approx 1.681 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg}
$$

Then:

$$
E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} m_{p} v_{peak}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (1.681 \times 10^{-9}) (3.77)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{k} \approx \frac{1}{2} (1.681 \times 10^{-9}) (14.21) \approx 1.194 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{k} \approx 11.94 \text{ nJ per grain}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Numerical Sub-questions:**
> - **Unit conversion errors** between mm³/s and mm³/min cost 1 mark in nearly every script. Always state the final unit explicitly.
> - Mixing **$d$ (depth per cycle)** with **$d_g$ (grain diameter)** is the most common symbol-confusion mistake. Clearly write "depth of cut $d$" vs "grain diameter $d_g$" at the start.
> - In part (iii), do not forget to **cube the grain diameter** ($d_g^3$) — students frequently square it and lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**
