- ✅ **USM = mechanical, non-thermal, non-electrical** process using **abrasive + vibration** (20–40 kHz, 15–50 μm).
- ✅ **Five subsystems:** HFG, Transducer, Horn, Tool, Slurry delivery, Feed system.
- ✅ **MRM = Hammering (dominant) + Cavitation erosion (secondary) + Hydrolytic weakening (minor).**
- ✅ **MRR formula (must memorize):** $\dot{V} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 f d$.
- ✅ **Peak velocity:** $v_{peak} = 2\pi f A$. **Kinetic energy per grain:** $E_k = \frac{\pi^3}{3} d_g^3 \rho_g f^2 A^2$.
- ✅ **Hardness rule:** $HR_{abrasive} \geq HR_{workpiece} + 40$ (Rockwell scale).
- ✅ **Best for:** Glass, ceramics, quartz, Si, Ge, WC, precious stones (brittle, hard, non-conductive).
- ✅ **Not for:** Ductile metals (energy absorbed by plastic deformation).
- ✅ **Biggest limitation:** Tool wear ratio (TWR) — 1% in glass, up to 50% in tungsten carbide.
- ✅ **Abrasive order (hardness):** Diamond > B₄C > SiC > Al₂O₃.
- ✅ **Slurry concentration optimum:** ~40% by weight in water.
- ✅ **No HAZ** — USM is a cold process.
- ✅ **Frequency × Amplitude** are the two most powerful MRR levers; both are squared in the $E_k$ relation.
- ✅ **Cavitation** is governed by Minnaert's equation: $f_{cav} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_0} \sqrt{\frac{3 \gamma P_0}{\rho}}$.
- ✅ **Key competitors for KTU comparison questions:** LBM (thermal, all materials), EDM (thermal, conductive only), AJM (mechanical, polymer/thin), ECM (electrochemical, conductive only).
- ✅ **Examiner's trap:** MRR is *not* proportional to frequency alone — depth per cycle $d$ itself depends on $A$ and grain energy, so the overall dependency is non-linear.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Focused Ion Beam Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Focused Ion Beam Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Machining** is an advanced, maskless, non-traditional micro/nano-scale material removal process in which a finely focused beam of accelerated ions — most commonly **Gallium (Ga⁺)** ions — is directed onto a workpiece surface to sputter (eject) atoms via controlled kinetic energy transfer. The process operates inside a high-vacuum chamber (typically **<10⁻⁶ Torr**) and is governed by the principles of momentum transfer between the incident energetic ions and the target atoms, as described by Sigmund's linear cascade sputtering theory.

The process enables:
- **Sub-micrometer to nanometer-scale** feature fabrication
- **Maskless, direct-write** capability
- **3D nanomachining** with high precision
- **In-situ imaging** (often combined with SEM — called FIB-SEM dual-beam systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET864):**
> FIB Machining is classified under **Module 1** (Fundamentals of Micro/Nano Manufacturing) as a key **energetic beam-based material removal** technique. Students must master the ion–solid interaction physics, the LMIS source, the column optics, and the material removal rate equation for KTU University Exam scoring.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Nano-Sculptor with Atomic Sandblasting"

Imagine you have a **sandblasting machine** that uses grains of sand to etch patterns into a solid wall. Now shrink this idea to the atomic level:

- Replace the sand grains with **single ionized atoms** (gallium ions), each carrying **5–30 keV** of kinetic energy.
- Replace the wide spray nozzle with a **precision electromagnetic lens** that focuses these ions into a beam as thin as **5–10 nanometers** in diameter.
- The sandblasted wall becomes your silicon wafer, MEMS device, or TEM specimen.

When each fast-moving gallium ion strikes the surface, it behaves like a tiny billiard ball smashing into a cluster of bowling pins (target atoms). Through a chain of atomic collisions (a **collision cascade**), atoms on the surface are knocked loose and ejected — this is **physical sputtering**. By scanning the focused beam across the surface (raster scanning), you "sculpt" precise features: trenches, holes, or even complex 3D shapes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ions Instead of Electrons (as in SEM/EBL)?**
> Electrons are too light (~9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg) to displace atoms efficiently. A gallium ion (~1.16 × 10⁻²⁵ kg) is roughly **127,000× more massive**, so it carries enough momentum to physically knock target atoms off the surface. This is why FIB **removes material**, while electron beams only image it.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Beam spot diameter | **5 nm – 500 nm** |
| Accelerating voltage | **2 – 30 keV** |
| Beam current | **1 pA – 65 nA** |
| Material removal rate | **0.01 – 10 µm³/s** |
| Vacuum level | **< 10⁻⁶ Torr** |
| Most common ion | **Ga⁺** (Liquid Metal Ion Source) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Operational Logic of FIB Machining

The FIB process flows through a tightly controlled sequence:

**Step 1 — Ion Generation (Source Stage):**
A reservoir of liquid gallium (melting point **29.8 °C**) is held in contact with a tungsten needle. An **extraction voltage** of ~5–10 kV pulls gallium atoms from the tip's apex, ionizing them into Ga⁺ via **field evaporation** (Taylor cone formation).

**Step 2 — Acceleration (Beam Formation):**
The extracted Ga⁺ ions are accelerated by an adjustable **accelerating voltage (V_acc)** between **2–30 kV**. This determines the ion's kinetic energy:

$$E_{ion} = q \cdot V_{acc}$$

where $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ for singly charged Ga⁺.

**Step 3 — Focusing (Column Optics):**
A series of **electrostatic lenses** (condenser lens, octupole deflectors, objective lens) shape and steer the beam. Unlike magnetic lenses, electrostatic lenses keep the optical column short and vacuum-compatible.

**Step 4 — Raster Scanning & Material Removal:**
Deflector plates rapidly sweep the beam across the target in a raster pattern (similar to a TV scan). At each pixel, the beam "dwells" for a few microseconds, transferring momentum and sputtering target atoms.

**Step 5 — Vacuum Environment & Pumping:**
All of this occurs inside an ultra-high vacuum to prevent ion–gas collisions and surface contamination.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Physical Reason |
|---|---|
| Field evaporation of Ga | High surface tension of liquid metal + strong electric field (~10⁹ V/m) overcomes binding energy of Ga atoms |
| Acceleration stage | Imparts enough kinetic energy to exceed surface binding energy (~ few eV/atom) |
| Electrostatic focusing | Electric fields deflect charged ions efficiently without magnetic hysteresis |
| Raster scan + dwell time | Defines spatial resolution and volumetric removal rate |
| Vacuum | Mean free path of ions must exceed column length (~few m), which requires pressure < 10⁻⁶ Torr |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description of Terms | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ion} = q V_{acc}$ | Kinetic energy of an accelerated ion | **eV** or **Joules** |
| 2 | $v_{ion} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 q V_{acc}}{m_{ion}}}$ | Velocity of incident ion | **m/s** |
| 3 | $Y = \dfrac{N_{ejected}}{N_{incident}}$ | Sputtering yield (atoms removed per incident ion) | **dimensionless** |
| 4 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{S \cdot I \cdot M}{N_A \cdot \rho \cdot q}$ | Volumetric material removal rate | **m³/s** |
| 5 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{I \cdot Y \cdot m_{atom}}{q \cdot \rho}$ | Alternative MRR form | **m³/s** |
| 6 | $S(E) = K \cdot \dfrac{(E - E_{th})^{1/2}}{E_{th}^{1/2}}$ | Sputter yield energy dependence (approx. Bohdansky formula) | atoms/ion |
| 7 | $d = \dfrac{S \cdot I \cdot t}{A \cdot \rho \cdot N_A \cdot q}$ | Sputtered depth after dwell time *t* | **m** |
| 8 | $r_{spot} = \dfrac{r_0}{\sqrt{V_{acc}}}$ | Beam spot radius scaling with acceleration voltage | **m** |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, $I$ = beam current (A), $S$ or $Y$ = sputter yield (atoms/ion), $M$ = molar mass of target (kg/mol), $N_A$ = Avogadro's number, $\rho$ = target density (kg/m³), $q$ = ionic charge (C), $m_{atom}$ = atomic mass of target atom (kg), $E_{th}$ = sputtering threshold energy (eV), $K$ = material-dependent empirical constant.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

FIB Machining is the **workhorse of the semiconductor failure-analysis lab**. It is used for:

1. **TEM Lamella Preparation** — ultra-thin (<100 nm) samples cut from specific chip locations.
2. **Circuit Edit (CE)** — modifying ICs after fabrication to debug designs.
3. **Mask Repair** — repairing photolithography masks by etching opaque defects or depositing missing features using induced CVD.
4. **MEMS / NEMS Fabrication** — drilling micro-holes in inkjet printheads, fabricating micro-channels in lab-on-chip devices.
5. **Failure Analysis** — precision cross-sectioning of buried IC layers.
6. **Nanopatterning** — direct-write nanostructures without lithography.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Volumetric Material Removal Rate (MRR)

This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU examinations. Starting from first principles:

**Step 1 — Define the basic sputtering yield:**

$$Y = \frac{N_{ejected}}{N_{incident}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the number of incident ions per second:**

A beam current $I$ carries charge $I$ (Coulombs per second). Since each ion carries charge $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$N_{incident} = \frac{I}{q} \quad \text{(ions per second)}$$

**Step 3 — Number of atoms ejected per second:**

$$N_{ejected} = Y \cdot N_{incident} = \frac{Y \cdot I}{q}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of material removed per second:**

Each ejected atom has mass $m_{atom} = M/N_A$ (where $M$ = molar mass, $N_A$ = Avogadro's number). Hence:

$$\dot{m} = N_{ejected} \cdot m_{atom} = \frac{Y \cdot I}{q} \cdot \frac{M}{N_A}$$

**Step 5 — Convert mass-removal-rate to volume-removal-rate:**

Using density $\rho$:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$$

This is the **master MRR equation** that examiners expect.

Putting it together:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho} \\
&= \frac{(\text{atoms/ion}) \cdot (\text{C/s}) \cdot (\text{kg/mol})}{(\text{C/ion}) \cdot (\text{atoms/mol}) \cdot (\text{kg/m}^3)} \\
&= \frac{\text{kg/s}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \text{m}^3/\text{s} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:**
> A focused ion beam operating with Ga⁺ ions (atomic mass = 69.72 g/mol) at a beam current of **1 nA** is used to machine a silicon substrate (density = 2.33 g/cm³, sputtering yield $Y = 2.5$ atoms/ion). Compute:
> (a) The volumetric material removal rate.
> (b) The time required to drill a through-hole of diameter **5 µm** and depth **20 µm** through the silicon.

**Part (a) — MRR Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho} \\
&= \frac{(2.5) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.06972)}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \cdot (2330)}
\end{aligned}$$

Working the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 6.972 \times 10^{-2} = 1.743 \times 10^{-10}$$

Working the denominator:

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 2330 = 2.246 \times 10^{8}$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{1.743 \times 10^{-10}}{2.246 \times 10^{8}} = 7.76 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to µm³/s:

$$\dot{V} = 7.76 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{18} = 0.776 \text{ µm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Part (b) — Drilling Time:**

Volume to be removed:

$$V_{hole} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot d = \pi \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-6}) = 3.927 \times 10^{-16} \text{ m}^3$$

Time required:

$$t = \frac{V_{hole}}{\dot{V}} = \frac{3.927 \times 10^{-16}}{7.76 \times 10^{-19}} = 506 \text{ s} \approx 8.4 \text{ minutes}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Sputter Depth After Dwell Time

**Step 1 — Atoms removed per dwell cycle:**

If the beam dwells for time $t_d$ on a single pixel of area $A$:

$$N_{ejected} = Y \cdot \frac{I \cdot t_d}{q}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass removed:**

$$m_{removed} = N_{ejected} \cdot \frac{M}{N_A} = \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot t_d \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to depth removed:**

$$d_{removed} = \frac{m_{removed}}{A \cdot \rho} = \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot t_d \cdot M}{A \cdot q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$$

Final expression:

$$d = \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot t_d \cdot M}{A \cdot \rho \cdot q \cdot N_A}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FIB vs Competing Processes

| Parameter | FIB Machining | Electron Beam (EBM) | Laser Machining | EDM (Micro) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Beam particle | Ga⁺ ions | Electrons | Photons | Electrical sparks |
| Material removal mechanism | Momentum transfer (sputtering) | Thermal evaporation | Thermal ablation | Melting & vaporization |
| Minimum feature size | **5–10 nm** | ~100 nm (with EBL resist) | ~1 µm | ~5 µm |
| Suitable for non-conductors | Limited (charge buildup) | Limited | Yes | No |
| Vacuum needed | **Yes** (UHV) | Yes | No | No (dielectric fluid) |
| Typical MRR | Low (µm³/s range) | Low | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — FIB System Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Metal Reservoir Ga 69.72 g per mol] --> B[LMIS Source Taylor Cone Formation]
    B --> C[Suppressor Electrode Controls Emission Current]
    C --> D[Extractor Electrode 5 to 10 kV Pulls Ga Plus Ions]
    D --> E[Condenser Lens Controls Beam Current 1 pA to 65 nA]
    E --> F[Octupole Deflector Raster Scanning XY Steering]
    F --> G[Objective Lens Final Focusing Beam Spot 5 to 500 nm]
    G --> H[Faraday Cup Beam Current Measurement]
    G --> I[Workpiece Stage Inside Vacuum Chamber 10 to 6 Torr]
    I --> J[Secondary Electron Detector Optional SEM Imaging]
    I --> K[Sputtered Atoms Ejected From Target]
    K --> L[Vacuum Pump System Captures Ejected Material]
    I --> M[Process Result Nano Scale Trench Hole or Cross Section]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Mapping (Ion–Solid Interaction Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Ion Acceleration Stage 2 to 30 keV] --> stepB[Surface Impact of Ga Plus Ion]
    stepB --> stepC[Primary Knock On Atom PKA Generation]
    stepC --> stepD[Linear Collision Cascade Sigmund Theory]
    stepD --> stepE[Surface Binding Energy Threshold ~ few eV]
    stepE --> stepF[Sputtered Atom Ejection From Top 1 to 3 nm]
    stepF --> stepG[Net Material Removal Voxel by Voxel]
    stepG --> stepH[Secondary Electron Emission for Imaging]
    stepG --> stepI[Secondary Ion Emission for Analysis]
    stepG --> stepJ[Implanted Ga Plus Ions Side Effect]
```

## 4.3 Operational Parameter Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Accelerating Voltage V acc] --> R1[Higher Energy Deeper Penetration Higher Y]
    P2[Beam Current I] --> R2[Higher Current Higher MRR but Larger Spot Size]
    P3[Dwell Time t d] --> R3[Longer Dwell Deeper Sputter per Pixel]
    P4[Working Distance] --> R4[Shorter Distance Smaller Beam Spot]
    P5[Scan Step Size] --> R5[Smaller Step Smoother Surface]
    R1 --> OUT[Final Feature Geometry]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    R5 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:**
> The ion–solid interaction cascade forms the physical heart of FIB. Approximately **95% of the incident ion's energy** is converted into lattice phonons (heat), while only **~1–5%** results in sputtered atoms. The remainder is dissipated as implanted Ga⁺ ions in the near-surface region (typically within **20–30 nm** of the surface for 30 keV Ga⁺ on Si).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is Focused Ion Beam (FIB) machining? Mention any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Focused Ion Beam (FIB) machining is an advanced maskless micro/nano fabrication technique that uses a finely focused beam of accelerated ions — typically gallium (Ga⁺) ions generated from a Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS) — to sputter material from a workpiece via momentum transfer in a high-vacuum environment (10⁻⁶ Torr). The beam spot size can be as small as **5–10 nm**, enabling nanoscale material removal.

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **TEM sample (lamella) preparation** — site-specific ultra-thin sectioning for transmission electron microscopy.
2. **Circuit Edit (CE)** in integrated circuits — cutting or rerouting metal lines to debug chip functionality after fabrication.

*(Alternative applications accepted: Mask repair, MEMS fabrication, cross-sectional failure analysis.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Why is gallium commonly used as the ion source in FIB systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gallium is preferred in FIB systems because of the following reasons:
1. **Low melting point (29.8 °C)** — remains liquid at room temperature, ideal for Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS) operation without external heating.
2. **High mass (69.72 amu)** — provides sufficient momentum to sputter target atoms effectively, unlike lighter ions (He, Ne).
3. **Low vapor pressure** — enables stable emission in vacuum over long durations.
4. **Good focusing characteristics** — singly charged Ga⁺ ions are easily manipulated by electrostatic lenses.
5. **Reasonable surface diffusion** — allows stable Taylor cone formation at the emission tip.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) **Explain the working principle of Focused Ion Beam (FIB) machining with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of the Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS).** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
FIB machining uses a focused beam of energetic ions to remove material from a target surface by physical sputtering. The system consists of:

1. **Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS):** Generates Ga⁺ ions from a liquid gallium reservoir. A tungsten needle wetted with gallium is held at high negative potential. Under intense electric field (~10⁹ V/m), gallium atoms at the Taylor cone apex undergo **field evaporation**, releasing Ga⁺ ions.
2. **Extractor electrode:** Pulls Ga⁺ ions into the column with 5–10 kV extraction voltage.
3. **Suppressor electrode:** Limits emission current to ensure stable ion flux.
4. **Condenser lens:** Controls beam current (pA to nA range).
5. **Octupole deflectors:** Steer the beam in raster X–Y pattern.
6. **Objective lens:** Final focusing to a 5–500 nm spot.
7. **Vacuum chamber:** Maintains <10⁻⁶ Torr to maximize ion mean free path.
8. **Workpiece stage:** 5-axis stage for precise positioning.

The accelerated Ga⁺ ion strikes the target with KE in the range **2–30 keV**, transferring momentum in a **collision cascade**, ejecting surface atoms (sputtering yield typically 1–10 atoms/ion). Repeated raster scanning removes material voxel-by-voxel to form precise 3D features.

**Function of LMIS:**
- Provides a stable, high-brightness source of Ga⁺ ions.
- Operates at room temperature (Ga m.p. = 29.8 °C).
- Produces a virtual source size of **~50 nm**, enabling fine focusing.
- Emits ions with a small energy spread (~5 eV), minimizing chromatic aberration.

*(Diagram expected — block-level schematic showing LMIS → Extractor → Lenses → Sample.)*

---

#### (b) **Derive the volumetric material removal rate equation for FIB machining. A silicon wafer (ρ = 2.33 g/cm³) is machined using 30 keV Ga⁺ ions with a beam current of 5 nA and sputter yield Y = 3 atoms/ion. Calculate the MRR.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

Let $I$ = beam current (A), $Y$ = sputter yield, $q$ = ionic charge, $M$ = molar mass, $N_A$ = Avogadro's number, $\rho$ = density.

Incident ions per second:

$$\frac{I}{q}$$

Atoms ejected per second:

$$\frac{Y \cdot I}{q}$$

Mass removed per second:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A}$$

Volume removed per second:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{Y \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

Given:
- $Y = 3$ atoms/ion
- $I = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ A
- $M = 0.02809$ kg/mol (silicon)
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$
- $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{3 \times (5 \times 10^{-9}) \times 0.02809}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times 2330} \\
&= \frac{4.2135 \times 10^{-10}}{2.246 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= 1.876 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 1.88 \text{ µm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**MRR ≈ 1.88 µm³/s**

---

### **Question B — [Alternative Choice for Internal Selection 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) **Describe the ion–solid interaction mechanism in FIB machining. Explain the concept of sputtering yield and the factors affecting it.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Ion–Solid Interaction Mechanism:**
When a high-energy Ga⁺ ion strikes the target surface, it undergoes a series of elastic collisions with the target atoms, transferring kinetic energy. The principal steps are:

1. **Penetration:** The ion penetrates the top 1–3 atomic layers, losing energy through nuclear and electronic stopping.
2. **Primary Knock-on Atom (PKA) Generation:** A target atom receives sufficient energy to be displaced from its lattice site.
3. **Collision Cascade (Sigmund Theory):** The PKA collides with neighboring atoms, generating secondary, tertiary knock-ons — forming a **linear cascade** of displaced atoms.
4. **Energy Dissipation:** ~95% of the ion's energy is converted to lattice heat (phonons).
5. **Sputtering:** If an atom near the surface receives energy greater than the **surface binding energy** (~few eV), it overcomes the surface barrier and is ejected.

**Sputtering Yield (Y):**
$$Y = \frac{N_{ejected}}{N_{incident}}$$
Typical Y for 30 keV Ga⁺ on Si ≈ 2–5 atoms/ion.

**Factors Affecting Sputter Yield:**
| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| Ion energy | Increases with energy up to a saturation point |
| Ion mass | Heavier ions yield more sputtering |
| Target mass | Light target atoms are sputtered more easily |
| Angle of incidence | Maximum yield at ~60–80° off-normal |
| Crystal orientation | Channeling reduces yield along low-index directions |
| Surface binding energy | Higher binding energy reduces yield |

---

#### (b) **Compare Focused Ion Beam (FIB) machining with Electron Beam Machining (EBM) and Laser Beam Machining (LBM). List their relative advantages and limitations.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | FIB | EBM | LBM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Beam particle | Ga⁺ ions | Electrons | Photons |
| Removal mechanism | Physical sputtering | Thermal evaporation | Thermal ablation |
| Resolution | **5–10 nm** | ~100 nm (with resist) | ~1 µm |
| Vacuum required | **Yes (UHV)** | Yes | No |
| Non-conductive materials | Limited (charging) | Limited (charging) | Yes |
| MRR | Low | Low | High |
| Cost | Very high | High | Moderate |

**Advantages of FIB:**
- Highest resolution among the three.
- True 3D nanostructure fabrication.
- Compatible with in-situ SEM imaging (dual-beam).
- Excellent for TEM sample prep.

**Limitations of FIB:**
- Slow MRR (~ µm³/s).
- High capital cost (USD 1–3 million).
- Limited to conductive or thin-film samples.
- Ga⁺ implantation contaminates the surface.
- Requires UHV chamber.

**EBM advantages:** Maskless nanopatterning via resist exposure.
**LBM advantages:** Fast, atmospheric operation, works on most materials.

**Conclusion:** FIB is the preferred choice when sub-100 nm precision is critical; LBM is preferred when throughput is the priority.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in FIB Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion** in MRR problems — most students lose 1–2 marks by leaving answers in mixed SI/cgs units. Always express I in Amperes, M in kg/mol, ρ in kg/m³.
>
> 2. **Confusing "current" and "charge"** — a beam current of 1 nA means $10^{-9}$ C/s, NOT $10^{-9}$ ions/s. The number of ions per second is $I/q = I/(1.6 \times 10^{-19})$.
>
> 3. **Skipping the LMIS explanation** — in any 7-mark "working principle" question, mentioning **Taylor cone** and **field evaporation** is mandatory to score full marks.
>
> 4. **Missing the vacuum requirement** — many students ignore stating "**high vacuum (10⁻⁶ Torr)**" which examiners specifically check for (worth 1 mark).
>
> 5. **Not mentioning sputtering yield Y's dependence on ion energy and mass** — this is a KTU-favored conceptual question.
>
> 6. **Conflating "removal rate" with "drilling time"** — show both the rate and the integration over volume clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **FIB = Focused Ion Beam Machining** — maskless, vacuum-based, nano-scale material removal using energetic ions.
- ✅ **Most common ion:** Ga⁺ from a **Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS)** operating via Taylor cone field evaporation.
- ✅ **Ion energy range:** **2–30 keV** (acceleration voltage).
- ✅ **Beam spot size:** **5 nm – 500 nm** (smallest among all nano-machining processes).
- ✅ **Operating vacuum:** **< 10⁻⁶ Torr**.
- ✅ **Removal mechanism:** Physical sputtering via **linear collision cascade (Sigmund)**.
- ✅ **Sputtering yield Y** depends on ion energy, ion mass, target mass, and incidence angle.
- ✅ **Master MRR Equation:** $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Y \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$.
- ✅ **~95% of ion energy** → phonon (heat) loss; only ~1–5% → sputtering.
- ✅ **Side effect:** **Ga⁺ ion implantation** within ~20–30 nm of the surface.
- ✅ **Applications:** TEM lamella prep, circuit edit, mask repair, MEMS/NEMS fabrication, cross-sectional failure analysis, nanopatterning.
- ✅ **Comparison:** Highest resolution, lowest MRR, highest cost among competing micro-machining methods.
- ✅ **Dual-beam FIB-SEM** systems enable simultaneous milling and high-resolution imaging.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> For maximum marks, always (i) state the **physical constants** with units, (ii) draw a **labeled block diagram** of the FIB column, and (iii) show **full unit-balanced derivation** of the MRR equation. These three elements together routinely convert a 10-mark answer into a 14-mark answer under KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Beam Micro Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Micro Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Laser Beam Micro Machining (LBMM)** is a non-contact, thermal-based material removal process that utilizes a highly focused, monochromatic, and coherent beam of photons — generated by a laser source — to selectively vaporize, melt, or ablate material from a workpiece at micro and sub-micron scales. The process leverages the intense localized energy density (**10⁶ – 10¹² W/mm²**) of the laser beam to achieve feature sizes typically in the range of **1 µm to 500 µm** with positional accuracy of ±1 µm.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, LBMM falls under the umbrella of **Energy Beam Material Processing** and is classified alongside Electron Beam Machining (EBM), Ion Beam Machining (IBM), and Plasma Arc Machining. The acronym **LASER** stands for **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LBMM is a *thermal*-based material removal process, NOT a mechanical one. The fundamental mechanism is *photothermal* (heat-driven) for most industrial lasers (CO₂, Nd:YAG) and *photochemical* (cold ablation) for ultraviolet lasers (Excimer at 193 nm).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine using a magnifying glass on a sunny day to focus sunlight onto a single dry leaf. The concentrated light beam generates enough heat at the focal point to ignite the leaf, while the surrounding area remains unaffected. Similarly, a laser acts as an extremely precise "optical magnifying glass" that concentrates light energy into a micron-sized spot. Instead of burning a leaf, it vaporizes tiny amounts of metal, ceramic, or polymer.

Think of it this way:
- **Sunlight** = Broadband (many wavelengths), incoherent (waves out of phase)
- **Laser light** = Single wavelength (monochromatic), coherent (waves in phase), collimated (parallel rays)

Because laser waves march in perfect step (coherence) and travel as a tight, parallel column (collimation), they can be focused down to a spot smaller than a human red blood cell (~7 µm).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks for LBMM (Bolded for emphasis):**
> - **Wavelength (λ):** 193 nm (Excimer) to 10.6 µm (CO₂) — visible/IR spectrum dominant
> - **Spot Diameter:** **5 µm – 200 µm**
> - **Power Density:** **10⁶ W/mm² – 10¹² W/mm²**
> - **Pulse Duration:** **femtoseconds (10⁻¹⁵ s) to continuous wave (CW)**
> - **Speed of light (c):** **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Planck's constant (h):** **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - **Photon Energy:** E = hν, where ν = c/λ

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamentals of Laser Generation

The generation of laser light requires three essential ingredients:

1. **Active Medium (Gain Medium):** A material (gas, solid, or liquid) with atoms that can be excited to higher energy states. Examples: CO₂ gas, Nd:YAG crystal, Ti:Sapphire, Excimer gases.
2. **Pumping Source (Energy Source):** Provides external energy to populate the upper energy level. Types: Optical pumping (flash lamp), electrical discharge, diode pumping.
3. **Optical Resonator:** Two parallel mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially transmissive) that bounce photons back and forth through the active medium, causing stimulated emission.

**Three fundamental processes occur:**
- **Absorption:** Atom absorbs a photon and jumps to an excited state (E₁ → E₂)
- **Spontaneous Emission:** Excited atom randomly emits a photon and returns to ground state (E₂ → E₁)
- **Stimulated Emission:** An incoming photon of exact energy (E₂ − E₁) triggers the excited atom to emit a second, identical photon (same phase, direction, frequency). This is the amplification process.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Material Removal in LBMM

The laser-material interaction follows a sequential thermal process when the photon energy exceeds the material's binding threshold:

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption:** Free electrons or bound electrons in the lattice absorb photon energy. Energy transfer occurs on a timescale of **10⁻¹² s (picoseconds)**.

**Step 2 — Thermalization:** The absorbed energy redistributes among lattice vibrations (phonons), causing rapid local heating to vaporization temperature. This takes **10⁻¹¹ to 10⁻⁹ seconds**.

**Step 3 — Melting & Vaporization:** Once temperature exceeds the boiling point, material is ejected as vapor plume. Aided by recoil pressure and plasma formation at high intensities.

**Step 4 — Ablation:** For ultra-short pulses (femtosecond), material removal occurs via *cold ablation* — bonds break directly via photo-dissociation, with minimal heat diffusion.

### 2.3 Types of Lasers Used in Micro Machining

| Laser Type | Active Medium | Wavelength | Pulse Type | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ Laser** | CO₂/N₂/He gas | 10.6 µm | CW / pulsed | Polymers, wood, ceramics (non-metals) |
| **Nd:YAG Laser** | Neodymium-doped YAG | 1.064 µm | Q-switched pulsed | Metals (steel, aluminum, titanium) |
| **Fiber Laser** | Yb-doped fiber | 1.07 µm | CW / pulsed | Micro-drilling, micro-cutting of metals |
| **Excimer Laser** | ArF, KrF, XeCl | 193–351 nm | Pulsed (ns) | Photochemical ablation of polymers, ceramics |
| **Femtosecond Laser** | Ti:Sapphire | 800 nm | fs pulses (10⁻¹⁵ s) | Nanomachining, transparent materials |
| **Diode Laser** | Semiconductor | 635–1550 nm | CW | Direct micro-welding, polymer marking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why wavelength matters:** Metals reflect longer wavelengths (e.g., CO₂ 10.6 µm is ~95% reflected by steel). Shorter wavelengths like **Nd:YAG (1.064 µm)** or **Excimer (193 nm)** are better absorbed by metallic surfaces. Coupling efficiency η = 1 − R (where R is reflectivity).

### 2.4 Process Parameters Affecting LBMM

- **Pulse Energy (E):** Typically **0.1 mJ – 10 J** per pulse
- **Pulse Duration (τ):** **ns to fs** range
- **Pulse Repetition Rate (f):** **1 kHz – 100 MHz**
- **Average Power (P):** **1 W – 10 kW**
- **Scan Speed (v):** **mm/s to m/s**
- **Focal Spot Diameter (d):** Determines resolution
- **Assist Gas:** Oxygen (enhances exothermic reaction), Nitrogen (inert, prevents oxidation)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (J) |
| $I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2}$ | Power density / Intensity at focal spot | W/mm² |
| $MRR = \dfrac{\pi d^2 \cdot v \cdot \rho}{4}$ | Material removal rate (volume basis) | mm³/s |
| $Z = 2\sqrt{k \cdot \tau}$ | Heat Affected Zone depth (1D diffusion) | µm |
| $d_{min} = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{NA}$ | Minimum spot diameter (diffraction limit) | µm |
| $D = \dfrac{E_{th}}{\rho \cdot (c_p \Delta T + L_f + L_v)}$ | Ablation depth per pulse | µm |
| $P_{avg} = E \cdot f$ | Average power from pulse energy × repetition rate | W |
| $\eta = 1 - R$ | Absorptivity (coupling efficiency) | dimensionless |

Where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant, $c$ = speed of light, $\lambda$ = wavelength
- $P$ = laser power, $d$ = spot diameter
- $k$ = thermal diffusivity, $\tau$ = pulse duration
- $E_{th}$ = threshold energy density (J/cm²)
- $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion, $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization
- $R$ = surface reflectivity, $NA$ = numerical aperture

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

LBMM is indispensable in:
- **Microelectronics:** Drilling micro-vias in printed circuit boards (PCBs) and semiconductor packages
- **Medical Devices:** Stent cutting, fabrication of micro-channels in biochips
- **Aerospace:** Drilling cooling holes in turbine blades (super-alloys)
- **MEMS Fabrication:** Direct writing of micro-sensors and actuators
- **Automotive:** Fuel injector nozzle drilling

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Diffraction-Limited Spot Diameter

The minimum achievable focal spot diameter is governed by the **Airy disk** criterion from wave optics. For a Gaussian laser beam focused through a lens:

$$
d_{min} = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{NA}
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The angular divergence of a Gaussian beam (full angle) is given by:

$$
\theta = \frac{2\lambda}{\pi w_0}
$$

where $w_0$ is the beam waist radius at the focal plane. The numerical aperture of the focusing lens is defined as:

$$
NA = n \sin\alpha \approx \frac{w_0}{f}
$$

for small angles, where $f$ is the focal length and $n$ is the refractive index of the medium (typically air, n = 1).

The spot diameter at the focal plane (from Airy disk first minimum):

$$
d_{min} = f \cdot \theta = f \cdot \frac{2\lambda}{\pi w_0}
$$

Substituting $w_0/f = NA$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{min} &= f \cdot \frac{2\lambda}{\pi (f \cdot NA)} \\
&= \frac{2\lambda}{\pi \cdot NA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $2/\pi \approx 0.637$ and using the commonly quoted engineering approximation $1.22 \approx \pi/2.57$, the standard formula is:

$$
\boxed{d_{min} = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{NA}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For an Nd:YAG laser (λ = 1.064 µm) focused with NA = 0.25:

$$
d_{min} = \frac{1.22 \times 1.064 \times 10^{-6}}{0.25} = 5.19 \, \mu m
$$

This confirms why micro-machining spots of ~5 µm are achievable.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) Depth

For a single laser pulse of duration $\tau$, the heat diffuses into the material following the 1-D heat conduction equation. The thermal diffusion length in time $\tau$ is:

$$
Z = 2\sqrt{k \cdot \tau}
$$

where $k$ is the thermal diffusivity (m²/s). This represents the depth up to which the temperature rise is significant (>5% of peak).

**Derivation:**

From Fourier's law, the characteristic diffusion distance for a heat pulse satisfies:

$$
Z^2 \sim 4 k \tau
$$

Taking the square root for the half-depth:

$$
Z = 2\sqrt{k\tau}
$$

**Numerical Example for Stainless Steel (k = 4.2 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s):**
- Pulse duration τ = 10 ns:

$$
Z = 2\sqrt{4.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 2\sqrt{4.2 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.30 \times 10^{-7} \, m = 0.13 \, \mu m
$$

- Pulse duration τ = 1 ms:

$$
Z = 2\sqrt{4.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}} = 2\sqrt{4.2 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.30 \times 10^{-4} \, m = 130 \, \mu m
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **femtosecond lasers** are revolutionary for micro-machining — with τ = 100 fs, the HAZ becomes sub-nanometer, enabling truly *cold* machining with no collateral thermal damage.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Density (Intensity) at Focal Spot

For a laser of average power $P$ focused to a circular spot of diameter $d$, the average power density (irradiance) is:

$$
I = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{\pi d^2 / 4} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}
$$

For a **pulsed** laser, the peak intensity (during a single pulse) considers the pulse energy and pulse duration:

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{E}{\tau \cdot A} = \frac{4E}{\pi d^2 \tau}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Q-switched Nd:YAG, E = 1 mJ, τ = 10 ns, d = 50 µm:

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.09 \times 10^{11} \, W/m^2
$$

This equals **5.09 × 10⁵ W/mm²**, confirming the threshold regime for ablation.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Ablation Depth Per Pulse

Based on energy balance, the depth of material removed per pulse is:

$$
D = \frac{E_{th}}{\rho \cdot (c_p \Delta T + L_f + L_v)}
$$

where:
- $E_{th}$ = threshold fluence (J/cm²) = energy density per pulse
- $\rho$ = density of workpiece material
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise from ambient to boiling point
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization

This is derived from the assumption that all absorbed energy goes into:
1. Heating the solid from ambient $T_0$ to melting point $T_m$ (sensible heat: $\rho c_p \Delta T_1$)
2. Phase change solid → liquid ($L_f$)
3. Heating liquid to vaporization point ($c_p \Delta T_2$)
4. Phase change liquid → vapor ($L_v$)

Combining these into a single volumetric energy balance gives the above expression.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Process Regimes by Power Density

| Power Density (W/mm²) | Regime | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 10³ | Heating only | No removal | Annealing, surface modification |
| 10³ – 10⁵ | Melting | Liquid-phase removal | Welding, cladding |
| 10⁵ – 10⁷ | Vaporization | Vapor ejection (keyhole) | Cutting, drilling |
| 10⁷ – 10⁹ | Plasma formation | Plasma-mediated ablation | Deep penetration welding |
| > 10⁹ | Photochemical | Direct bond breaking (cold) | Femtosecond nano-machining |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Laser Beam Micro Machining System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodePS[Power Supply Unit] --> nodePU[Pumping Source]
    nodePU --> nodeAM[Active Gain Medium]
    nodeAM --> nodeOR[Optical Resonator MR1 MR2]
    nodeOR --> nodeBM[Beam Shutter]
    nodeBM --> nodeEX[Beam Expander]
    nodeEX --> nodeMO[Mirror Scanner or Galvo]
    nodeMO --> nodeFL[Focusing Lens]
    nodeFL --> nodeWS[Workpiece Stage XYZ]
    nodeWS --> nodeWS
    nodeCT[Cooling System] --> nodeAM
    nodeGC[Gas Controller] --> nodeNJ[Nozzle Assist Gas]
    nodeNJ --> nodeWS
    nodeCO[Controller Computer] --> nodeMO
    nodeCO --> nodeWS
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Description:**

1. **Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Provides regulated DC current/voltage to the pumping source (e.g., flash lamp driver, diode driver).
2. **Pumping Source:** Flash lamp, diode array, or RF exciter that injects energy into the gain medium.
3. **Active Gain Medium:** The lasing medium (CO₂ gas tube, Nd:YAG rod, fiber coil) where population inversion and stimulated emission occur.
4. **Optical Resonator:** Two mirrors (MR1 = 100% reflective rear mirror, MR2 = partially transmissive output coupler) that bounce photons to amplify the beam.
5. **Beam Shutter / Q-Switch:** Controls pulse timing. Q-switching produces nanosecond pulses with high peak power.
6. **Beam Expander:** Increases beam diameter to fill the focusing aperture, reducing divergence.
7. **Mirror Scanner / Galvo System:** Deflects the beam along X-Y axes for pattern generation.
8. **Focusing Lens (F-Theta Lens):** Concentrates the beam to a micron-sized spot on the workpiece. NA determines spot size.
9. **Workpiece Stage (X-Y-Z):** CNC-controlled stage for precise positioning and motion synchronization.
10. **Cooling System:** Maintains thermal stability of the gain medium (water or air cooled).
11. **Assist Gas Nozzle:** Delivers O₂, N₂, or Ar to the machining zone to aid material ejection or prevent oxidation.
12. **Controller Computer:** Synchronizes scanner, stage, and laser triggering for CAM-based pattern execution.

### 4.2 Process Flow: Laser-Material Interaction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIN[Laser Beam Incident] --> nodeAB[Photon Absorption]
    nodeAB --> nodeTH[Thermalization to Lattice]
    nodeTH --> nodeML[Local Melting]
    nodeML --> nodeVP[Vaporization]
    nodeVP --> nodeKP[Keyhole Formation]
    nodeKP --> nodePL[Plasma Plume]
    nodePL --> nodeEJ[Material Ejection]
    nodeEJ --> nodeHAZ[Heat Affected Zone Residual]
    nodeHAZ --> nodeRD[Recast and Debris Layer]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block: CW vs. Pulsed LBMM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CW[Continuous Wave Regime]
        nodeCW1[Constant Power Output] --> nodeCW2[Larger HAZ]
        nodeCW2 --> nodeCW3[Suitable for Cutting and Welding]
    end
    subgraph PLS[Pulsed Regime]
        nodePL1[Discrete Energy Bursts] --> nodePL2[Controllable HAZ]
        nodePL2 --> nodePL3[Suitable for Micro Drilling and Marking]
    end
    nodeCW --> nodeCMP[Comparison Outcome]
    nodePLS --> nodeCMP
    nodeCMP --> nodeFIN[Pulsed Preferred for Precision Micro Machining]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams depict **block-level functional architecture** rather than physical shapes, as per the engine's diagram adaptation rules for topics requiring complex physical geometries.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Laser Beam Micro Machining. List any two advantages and one limitation of the process.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Laser Beam Micro Machining (LBMM) is a non-contact, thermal energy-based manufacturing process in which a highly focused, coherent beam of monochromatic light is used to remove material through vaporization, melting, or ablation to produce micro-scale features.

**Advantages (Any Two):**
1. It is a **non-contact process**, so there is no tool wear and no mechanical stress on the workpiece.
2. It can machine **extremely hard and brittle materials** (ceramics, super-alloys, diamonds) that are difficult to process by conventional methods.
3. It achieves **very small feature sizes** (down to 1 µm) with high precision and minimal HAZ.
4. There is **no chemical or electrolyte required**, making it environmentally clean.

**Limitation (Any One):**
1. High initial capital cost of laser systems.
2. The Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) and recast layer can degrade surface quality in thermal regime.
3. Limited material removal rate for deep cavities compared to conventional machining.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between photo-thermal ablation and photo-chemical ablation in LBMM.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Photo-Thermal Ablation | Photo-Chemical Ablation |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Heat-driven vaporization/melting | Direct bond breaking by high-energy photons |
| **Photon Energy** | Lower (longer wavelength, e.g., 1.064 µm) | Higher (shorter UV wavelength, e.g., 193 nm) |
| **Typical Lasers** | CO₂, Nd:YAG, Fiber | Excimer lasers |
| **Pulse Duration** | ns – µs | ns – fs |
| **HAZ** | Significant | Negligible (cold ablation) |
| **Surface Quality** | Recast layer, micro-cracks possible | Clean edges, no thermal damage |
| **Materials** | Metals, ceramics | Polymers, biological tissues |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a laser with a neat schematic of an optical resonator. Discuss the three fundamental processes of stimulated emission. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the Laser Beam Micro Machining setup. Discuss the role of (i) assist gas, (ii) beam expander, and (iii) Q-switch. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Working Principle of Laser:**

A laser operates on the principle of **stimulated emission of radiation** combined with **optical amplification** within a resonant cavity.

**Schematic of Optical Resonator:**

```
        MR1 (100% Reflective)
            │
            ▼
    ┌───────────────┐
    │  Active Medium│  ◄── Pumping Source
    │  (Gain Medium)│
    └───────────────┘
            │
            ▼
       MR2 (Partial)
       (Output Coupler)
            │
            ▼
       OUTPUT BEAM
```

**Three Fundamental Processes:**

1. **Absorption:** When an atom in the lower energy state E₁ absorbs a photon of energy $E = h\nu = E_2 - E_1$, it is excited to the higher energy state E₂. This requires the external pumping source to supply energy.

2. **Spontaneous Emission:** An excited atom in E₂ randomly drops to E₁ after a characteristic lifetime (~10⁻⁸ s), emitting a photon of energy hν. The direction and phase of this photon are random.

3. **Stimulated Emission:** When an incoming photon of exactly the right energy (hν = E₂ − E₁) interacts with an excited atom, it triggers the atom to emit a second, identical photon. Both photons have:
   - Same frequency ν
   - Same phase (coherent)
   - Same direction (collimated)
   - Same polarization

**Population Inversion:** A non-equilibrium condition where N₂ > N₁ is required for net amplification. This is achieved by pumping (optical or electrical).

**Optical Resonator Function:** Two parallel mirrors trap photons, forcing them to traverse the gain medium repeatedly, exponentially amplifying the beam. One mirror is fully reflective (R = 1); the output coupler transmits a small fraction (~1–5%).

---

**(b) LBMM Setup Description:**

The major components are:
1. **Laser Source** (CO₂ / Nd:YAG / Excimer / Fiber)
2. **Beam Delivery System** (mirrors, optical fiber, or articulated arm)
3. **Beam Expander** (2–10× magnification)
4. **Scanning Head** (Galvanometer mirrors)
5. **F-Theta Focusing Lens**
6. **CNC X-Y-Z Workpiece Stage**
7. **Assist Gas Nozzle**
8. **Exhaust / Fume Extraction**
9. **Computer Controller** with CAM software

**Roles:**

**(i) Assist Gas:** Oxygen enhances exothermic oxidation reactions (improves cutting of carbon steel). Nitrogen acts as inert shielding gas (prevents oxide formation, used for stainless steel). Compressed air removes ejected debris and molten splash from the cut zone.

**(ii) Beam Expander:** Increases the beam diameter so that the focusing lens aperture is fully utilized, achieving a smaller spot size. It also reduces beam divergence, improving collimation over long working distances.

**(iii) Q-Switch:** A fast optical shutter (acousto-optic or electro-optic modulator) that "traps" the population inversion inside the cavity and then releases it in one giant pulse. This generates nano-second pulses with peak powers in the megawatt range from a modest average power, essential for clean micro-machining without excessive HAZ.

---

#### Alternative Choice:

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the mechanisms of material removal in Laser Beam Micro Machining. Explain the threshold fluence concept. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed Nd:YAG laser with pulse energy E = 5 mJ, pulse duration τ = 8 ns, and focal spot diameter d = 80 µm is used to machine a stainless steel sheet (k = 4.2 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s). Calculate:
  (i) Peak power density at the focal spot.
  (ii) Heat Affected Zone depth.
  (iii) Comment on the suitability of this process for micro-machining. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Material Removal Mechanisms & Threshold Fluence:**

There are **four primary mechanisms** of material removal in LBMM, depending on the power density and pulse duration:

1. **Melting and Vapourisation:** At moderate power densities (10⁵ – 10⁷ W/mm²), the workpiece surface melts and vaporizes. The vapor recoil pressure ejects molten material. This is the dominant mechanism in CO₂ and CW Nd:YAG cutting.

2. **Cold Ablation (Photochemical Ablation):** When photon energy exceeds the bond dissociation energy (e.g., Excimer at 193 nm on polymers), bonds are broken directly without heating. No HAZ is produced.

3. **Plasma-Mediated Ablation:** At very high intensities (>10⁸ W/mm²), the vaporized material ionizes into plasma. The plasma absorbs and re-emits energy, acting as a secondary energy source.

4. **Photothermal Ablation (Ultra-fast):** Femtosecond pulses deposit energy faster than electron-phonon coupling time, causing direct solid-to-plasma transition. HAZ is virtually eliminated.

**Threshold Fluence Concept:**

The **threshold fluence F_th** is the minimum energy density (J/cm²) required to initiate measurable material removal. Below this threshold, the absorbed energy is insufficient to overcome latent heats and reach vaporization.

Experimentally, the relationship follows Beer-Lambert law:

$$
D(F) = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\left(\frac{F}{F_{th}}\right)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient and $F$ is the applied fluence. For most metals with Nd:YAG (1.064 µm), $F_{th} \approx 1–10 \, J/cm^2$. Excimer lasers on polymers have $F_{th} \approx 0.05–0.5 \, J/cm^2$.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given:**
- Pulse energy, E = 5 mJ = 5 × 10⁻³ J
- Pulse duration, τ = 8 ns = 8 × 10⁻⁹ s
- Spot diameter, d = 80 µm = 80 × 10⁻⁶ m
- Thermal diffusivity of SS, k = 4.2 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s

**(i) Peak Power Density:**

Focal spot area:
$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (80 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-9} \, m^2
$$

Peak power:
$$
P_{peak} = \frac{E}{\tau} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.25 \times 10^{5} \, W = 625 \, kW
$$

Peak power density:
$$
I_{peak} = \frac{P_{peak}}{A} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{5}}{5.027 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.243 \times 10^{14} \, W/m^2
$$

Converting to W/mm²:
$$
I_{peak} = 1.243 \times 10^{8} \, W/mm^2
$$

**(ii) Heat Affected Zone Depth:**

$$
Z = 2\sqrt{k\tau} = 2\sqrt{4.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
Z = 2\sqrt{3.36 \times 10^{-14}} = 2 \times 1.833 \times 10^{-7} = 3.67 \times 10^{-7} \, m
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = 0.367 \, \mu m = 367 \, nm}
$$

**(iii) Suitability Comment:**

The peak power density of **1.24 × 10⁸ W/mm²** lies comfortably in the **ablation regime** (above 10⁵ W/mm²), confirming that vaporization/ablation will occur. The HAZ of **~0.37 µm** is exceptionally small — smaller than the spot diameter — indicating that thermal damage is minimal and the process is **highly suitable for precision micro-machining** of stainless steel. Features down to ~10 µm with sub-micron edge quality are achievable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students make the following mistakes and lose 2–3 marks each:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm² to m²** when calculating intensity — double-check units at every step.
> 2. **Using average power instead of peak power** in pulsed systems. Average power = E × f; peak power = E / τ. These differ by orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Writing HAZ formula without the factor of 2** — the standard form is $Z = 2\sqrt{k\tau}$, not $\sqrt{k\tau}$.
> 4. **Confusing fluence (J/cm²) with intensity (W/cm²)** — they are related by pulse duration: $F = I \cdot \tau$.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **LASER** = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
- LBMM is a **non-contact, thermal (or photochemical)** material removal process using focused coherent light.
- **Three essential laser components:** Active medium, pumping source, optical resonator (two mirrors).
- **Population inversion** (N₂ > N₁) is a prerequisite for lasing action.
- **Wavelength selection** is critical — shorter wavelengths (UV, green) couple better with metals; longer wavelengths (CO₂ 10.6 µm) suit non-metals.
- **Power density** at focal spot determines regime: < 10³ W/mm² (heating), 10⁵ – 10⁷ (cutting), > 10⁹ (ablation/plasma).
- **Q-switching** produces high-peak-power nanosecond pulses for clean micro-machining.
- **Femtosecond lasers** achieve "cold" ablation with sub-micron HAZ — ideal for nano-machining.
- **Minimum spot diameter:** $d_{min} = 1.22\lambda / NA$.
- **HAZ depth:** $Z = 2\sqrt{k\tau}$ — proportional to square root of pulse duration.
- **Threshold fluence** is material- and wavelength-dependent.
- **Assist gas roles:** Oxygen → exothermic cutting enhancement; Nitrogen → inert shielding; Compressed air → debris removal.
- **Applications:** Micro-via drilling in PCBs, stent cutting, fuel injector nozzles, turbine blade cooling holes, MEMS fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For numerical problems on LBMM, always begin by stating the given data, convert all units to SI (meters, seconds, watts) **first**, then substitute into the formula. The examiner expects clearly boxed final answers with correct units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermo-Electric Type Micromachining Processes - Chemical and Electro-Chemical Type Advanced Machining Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermo-Electric Type Micromachining Processes - Chemical and Electro-Chemical Type Advanced Machining Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermo-Electric Type Micromachining Processes

### Formal Academic Definition
**Thermo-Electric Type Micromachining Processes** are non-conventional material removal techniques that utilize **thermal energy** (generated through electrical means) to vaporize, melt, or ablate material at the micro/nano scale. Unlike conventional mechanical machining, there is **no direct physical contact** between the tool and the workpiece, making them ideal for machining materials with **high hardness, brittleness, or complex geometries**.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight
> The four major thermo-electric micromachining processes are:
> 1. **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)** – including micro-EDM and Wire-EDM (WEDM)
> 2. **Electron Beam Machining (EBM)**
> 3. **Laser Beam Machining (LBM)** – including micro-laser ablation
> 4. **Plasma Arc / Ion Beam Machining**

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of thermo-electric machining like a "**precision lightning sculptor**" 🌩️.
> Imagine you have a block of ultra-hard diamond (which a normal drill cannot touch). Instead of pushing a drill bit into it, you send a tiny, super-controlled **lightning bolt** (a spark, electron beam, or laser pulse) at one point. The temperature at that single spot jumps to **10,000 °C**, instantly vaporizing the material into a puff of gas. Move the lightning to the next spot, and so on, until you have carved out a microscopic hole or slot.
> - In **EDM**, the lightning is a spark between two electrodes.
> - In **EBM**, the lightning is a focused beam of electrons (like a tiny cathode-ray gun).
> - In **LBM**, the lightning is a beam of light (photons), all concentrated to one spot.

---

### Sub-Topic 1A: Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM) — Micro-EDM
**Definition:** EDM is a non-contact, thermo-electric process in which material is removed by **controlled, repetitive electrical discharges (sparks)** occurring between a tool electrode (cathode) and a workpiece (anode) submerged in a **dielectric fluid**.

**Key Process Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested):**
- **Spark Temperature:** 8,000 °C – 12,000 °C
- **Spark Duration:** 0.1 µs – 200 µs
- **Dielectric Fluid:** EDM oil, kerosene, or deionized water
- **Gap (Spark Gap):** 5 µm – 50 µm
- **Voltage:** 80 V – 200 V (open circuit)
- **Current:** 0.1 A – 50 A

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use EDM?** It can machine any electrically **conductive** material regardless of its hardness — tungsten carbide, titanium, hardened tool steel (HRC 60+), and even polycrystalline diamond (PCD).

---

### Sub-Topic 1B: Wire-EDM (WEDM)
**Definition:** WEDM uses a **continuously moving thin wire** (brass, copper, or tungsten, diameter 0.02 mm – 0.3 mm) as one electrode, while the workpiece serves as the other. The wire travels from a supply spool to a take-up spool, providing a "fresh" electrode constantly.

**Key Features:**
- **No Electrode Wear Issue** (constant wire feed)
- 2-D contour cutting capability (like a bandsaw, but with sparks)
- **Minimum Corner Radius:** 0.05 mm (limited by wire diameter)

---

### Sub-Topic 1C: Electron Beam Machining (EBM)
**Definition:** EBM removes material by **focusing a high-velocity beam of electrons** in a vacuum chamber (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr). When electrons strike the workpiece, their **kinetic energy is converted into heat**, melting and vaporizing the material.

**Working Principle — Why Vacuum?**
> Electrons travel in straight lines only in a vacuum. Air molecules would scatter the beam. Thus, the entire process happens inside a vacuum chamber.

**Key Metrics:**
- **Accelerating Voltage:** 50 kV – 200 kV
- **Beam Diameter:** 10 µm – 100 µm
- **Power Density:** 10⁶ – 10⁹ W/mm²
- **Vacuum Required:** 10⁻⁴ Pa to 10⁻² Pa

---

### Sub-Topic 1D: Laser Beam Machining (LBM)
**Definition:** LBM uses a **highly coherent, monochromatic, focused beam of photons** to cut, drill, or engrave materials by **melting, vaporization, or photo-ablation**.

**Types of Lasers Used in Micro-Machining:**
| Laser Type | Wavelength | Application |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 10.6 µm | Non-metals, polymers |
| Nd:YAG | 1.064 µm | Metals, micro-drilling |
| Fiber Laser | 1.07 µm | Precision micro-machining |
| Excimer (UV) | 193 nm, 248 nm | Nano-scale ablation |

---

## 1.2 Chemical & Electro-Chemical Type Advanced Machining Processes

### Formal Academic Definition
**Chemical Machining (CHM)** is a material removal process based on the **controlled chemical dissolution (etching)** of a workpiece using a reactive chemical solution (etchant) or plasma. **Electro-Chemical Machining (ECM)** accelerates this process by passing an **electric current** between the anode (workpiece) and cathode (tool) through an electrolyte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Difference:**
> - **CHM** → Pure chemical reaction (no electricity, no thermal effects, no mechanical force)
> - **ECM** → Electrolysis (controlled by Faraday's laws)
> - **ECDM** → Hybrid of ECM + EDM (uses electrochemical dissolution + spark assistance)

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of chemical/ECM machining like a **surgical acid bath** 🧪.
> Imagine dipping a steel key into a beaker of strong acid. The acid eats away the exposed metal but leaves the parts covered in wax untouched. By carefully designing **which areas are exposed** (using a photoresist mask), you can "etch" intricate shapes.
> - In **CHM**, the acid is the only agent.
> - In **ECM**, you also pass a **gentle electric current** through the acid, which makes the acid eat metal **10× faster** and in a much more controlled way — like using a battery to accelerate rusting.

---

### Sub-Topic 2A: Chemical Machining (CHM) — Wet Chemical Etching

**Steps in CHM:**
1. **Cleaning** the workpiece
2. **Masking** with chemically resistant photoresist
3. **Patterning** (photolithography to expose required areas)
4. **Etching** in etchant bath
5. **Demasking** and cleaning

**Etchants (KTU Frequently Tested):**
| Material | Etchant | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Silicon (Si) | KOH, TMAH, HF | Anisotropic / Isotropic |
| Copper (Cu) | FeCl₃ | Isotropic |
| Aluminum (Al) | HCl, NaOH | Isotropic |
| Glass | HF | Isotropic |
| Stainless Steel | FeCl₃ + HNO₃ | Isotropic |

**Anisotropic vs. Isotropic Etching:**
- **Isotropic** → Etches at the **same rate in all directions** (e.g., HF on SiO₂)
- **Anisotropic** → Etches **directionally** (e.g., KOH on silicon — etches Si(100) faster than Si(111))

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystal Plane Etch Ratio in KOH on Si:**
> Si(100) : Si(111) ≈ 100 : 1 — this allows fabrication of V-grooves and pyramidal pits.

---

### Sub-Topic 2B: Electro-Chemical Machining (ECM)

**Definition:** ECM removes metal by **anodic dissolution** in an electrolytic cell. The workpiece is the **anode (+)** and the tool is the **cathode (–)**. A high DC current (1,000–10,000 A) is passed through the flowing electrolyte (NaCl, NaNO₃).

**Faraday's Law (Key Formula for KTU):**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Where:**
- $m$ = mass of material dissolved (kg)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $M$ = atomic mass of workpiece (kg/mol)
- $n$ = valency of dissolution reaction
- $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol** (approx. 96,500 C/mol)

---

### Sub-Topic 2C: Electro-Chemical Discharge Machining (ECDM)
A hybrid of ECM + EDM, where sparks assist the electrochemical dissolution. Useful for **non-conductive materials** (glass, ceramics, quartz).

---

### Sub-Topic 2D: Dry / Plasma Etching (Advanced)
Used in semiconductor microfabrication:
- **RIE (Reactive Ion Etching)** — combines chemical etching with physical ion bombardment
- **DRIE (Deep Reactive Ion Etching — Bosch Process)** — alternates between SF₆ etching and C₄F₈ passivation to create high-aspect-ratio microstructures

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 EDM Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- EDM works because **dielectric fluid is an insulator** at rest, but **breaks down** at high voltage, becoming conductive.
- The spark occurs at the **closest point** (smallest gap), and material is removed from **both** the workpiece and the electrode.

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Setup:** The tool electrode (cathode) and workpiece (anode) are positioned with a small **spark gap** (5–50 µm) between them. The gap is filled with dielectric fluid.

2. **Charging:** A pulsed DC voltage (open circuit voltage ~200 V) is applied across the gap. No current flows initially because the dielectric is an insulator.

3. **Dielectric Breakdown:** When voltage exceeds the **breakdown voltage** of the dielectric, it ionizes, forming a **plasma channel** (a conductive path of ions and electrons).

4. **Spark Discharge:** Current flows through the plasma channel. Temperatures reach **8,000–12,000 °C**, vaporizing a small volume of both the workpiece and the electrode.

5. **Crater Formation:** A micro-crater (~5 µm deep) is formed on the workpiece due to melting and vaporization.

6. **Dielectric Flush:** The flow of dielectric carries away the debris (eroded particles) and cools the gap, restoring the insulating state.

7. **Pulse Off (Dwell Time):** Current is switched off for a short duration (dwell time) to allow **de-ionization** of the gap.

8. **Repeat:** Cycle repeats thousands of times per second. Each spark removes a tiny amount of material; the cumulative effect produces the desired shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Dielectric Fluid?**
> 1. Acts as an **insulator** until breakdown
> 2. **Cools** the workpiece and electrode
> 3. **Flushes** away debris
> 4. **Constrains** the spark to a small region (localizes energy)

---

### 2.1.2 Micro-EDM (µEDM) — Scaling Down EDM

In **micro-EDM**, the electrode diameter is reduced to **5–100 µm**, achieved by:
- **Wire-EDM grinding** (to make cylindrical micro-electrodes)
- **ED-dressing** (reverse EDM sharpening)

**Key Differences from Macro-EDM:**
- Spark energy: **nanojoules to microjoules** (vs. millijoules in macro-EDM)
- Capacitance-based RC pulse generator is used for **sub-micron precision**
- Material removal per spark: ~0.01 µm³

---

### 2.1.3 LBM Working Principle

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- A laser's photons carry energy. When concentrated, this energy **heats the material** until it melts or vaporizes.
- The **energy density (fluence)** determines the removal mechanism.

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Beam Generation:** A laser medium (gas, solid, or semiconductor) emits a coherent beam of light.
2. **Beam Focusing:** Lenses and mirrors focus the beam to a **spot size of 5–50 µm**.
3. **Energy Absorption:** The workpiece absorbs the photon energy (wavelength must match material absorption).
4. **Heating:** Surface temperature rises rapidly (10⁶–10⁸ K/s heating rate).
5. **Phase Change:** Material melts → vaporizes → plasma forms.
6. **Material Removal:** Vapor and plasma are ejected, leaving a clean cut.
7. **Recast Layer:** A thin (1–10 µm) layer of re-solidified material (HAZ) may remain.

**Three Removal Regimes in Laser Machining:**

| Fluence (J/cm²) | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| < 1 | Photothermal (melting) | Cutting, welding |
| 1 – 10 | Photophysical (vaporization) | Drilling |
| > 10 | Photochemical (ablation) | Nano-machining (Excimer) |

---

### 2.1.4 ECM Working Principle

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Setup:** Tool = cathode, workpiece = anode. Gap (~0.1–1 mm) is filled with electrolyte (NaCl, flowing at 5–50 m/s).
2. **Current Application:** DC current (5–25 V, 1,000–10,000 A) is applied.
3. **Anodic Dissolution:** Workpiece atoms lose electrons (oxidation) and dissolve as metal ions into the electrolyte.
4. **Tool Feed:** The cathode is advanced into the workpiece at a rate matching the dissolution rate to maintain a constant gap.
5. **Hydrogen Evolution:** At the cathode, H⁺ + e⁻ → ½H₂↑ (hydrogen gas evolves, requiring exhaust).

**Reaction Example (Iron in NaCl):**
- Anode: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻
- Cathode: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂↑
- Overall: Fe + 2HCl → FeCl₂ + H₂↑

---

### 2.1.5 Chemical Etching (Wet) — Step-by-Step

1. **Surface Preparation:** RCA cleaning of silicon wafer.
2. **Mask Deposition:** SiO₂ or Si₃N₄ layer (1 µm) grown/deposited.
3. **Photolithography:** Photoresist spun, soft-baked, exposed through a mask, developed.
4. **Mask Patterning:** Pattern transferred to hard mask by HF or RIE.
5. **Etching:** Wafer submerged in etchant (e.g., KOH at 80 °C).
6. **Rinsing & Drying:** DI water rinse, critical-point drying to prevent stiction.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Table 1: Thermo-Electric Process Formulas

| Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|
| $P = V \times I$ | Power input to EDM/EBM system | Watts (W) |
| $E = P \times t = V \times I \times t$ | Spark energy (energy per pulse) | Joules (J) |
| $MRR_{EDM} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times f$ | MRR for cylindrical electrode (volumetric) | mm³/min |
| $MRR_{EDM} = w \times d \times f$ | MRR for cavity sinking | mm³/min |
| $f = \frac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Pulse frequency (inverse of total cycle time) | Hz |
| $\theta = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Duty cycle (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| $E_{photon} = \frac{h \times c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy in laser | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| $H = \frac{E_{pulse}}{A_{spot}}$ | Laser fluence | J/cm² |
| $I_{intensity} = \frac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | Laser intensity | W/cm² |
| $R_{a} = k \times I_{p}^{1.1}$ | Surface roughness vs. peak current (EDM, empirical) | µm |
| $TWR = \frac{V_{electrode}}{V_{workpiece}} \times 100$ | Tool Wear Ratio (%) | % |

| Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|
| $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's 1st Law of Electrolysis** (mass dissolved) | kg; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol |
| $Z = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Electrochemical Equivalent (ECE)** | kg/(A·s) |
| $V_{MRR} = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Volumetric MRR in ECM | mm³/s |
| $v_{feed} = \frac{MRR}{A}$ | Tool feed velocity in ECM | mm/s |
| $S = \frac{R_{etch,target}}{R_{etch,mask}}$ | **Selectivity** of etching process | dimensionless |
| $A_R = \frac{D}{W}$ | **Aspect Ratio** (depth / width) | dimensionless |
| $E_{rate} = \frac{d}{t}$ | Etch rate (depth ÷ time) | µm/min |
| $A = k \times e^{-E_a / RT}$ | **Arrhenius equation** for etch rate | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| $\theta = \arctan \left( \frac{r_{(100)}}{r_{(111)}} \right) = 54.74°$ | Si(100) KOH etch profile angle (V-groove) | degrees |

| Parameter | EDM | LBM | EBM | ECM | CHM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Workpiece** | Conductive only | Any | Any (vacuum) | Conductive only | Any (with right etchant) |
| **Tool Wear** | Yes | None | None | None | None (tool is mask) |
| **Heat Affected Zone** | Yes | Yes (small) | Yes (small) | **No (cold)** | **No (cold)** |
| **MRR** | Medium | Low–Med | Low | High | Low |
| **Surface Finish** | Good (Ra 0.2–3 µm) | Excellent | Excellent | Good (Ra 0.1–1 µm) | Good |
| **Accuracy** | ± 2 µm | ± 1 µm | ± 0.5 µm | ± 10 µm | ± 1 µm |
| **Application** | Dies, molds, micro-holes | Micro-channels, stents | Nano-holes | Turbine blades | MEMS, IC fabrication |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **EDM**: Used to make **injection mold cavities** for plastic parts, **fuel injector nozzles**, and **gears in watches**.
> - **WEDM**: Cuts **stents** and **orthopedic implants** from titanium rods.
> - **LBM**: Cuts **stents** in nitinol (shape memory alloy) for angioplasty.
> - **EBM**: Drills **fuel injector spray holes** in diesel engine parts.
> - **ECM**: Shapes **turbine blades** and removes **burrs** from surgical instruments.
> - **CHM**: Fabricates **MEMS accelerometers**, **pressure sensors**, and **microfluidic channels** in silicon.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: EDM Material Removal Rate (MRR) — Cylindrical Electrode

**Problem Statement:** Derive the volumetric MRR for an EDM operation using a cylindrical electrode of diameter $D$ that plunges into a workpiece with feed rate $f$ (mm/min).

**Step 1 — Volume Removed per Unit Time (Setup):**
> The cylindrical electrode advances axially into the workpiece. In time $t$, the electrode moves a distance $f \cdot t$ into the workpiece.

**Step 2 — Volume Removed in Time $t$:**
The volume of material removed equals the volume of a cylinder of diameter $D$ and length $f \cdot t$:

$$V = A_{cs} \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times (f \cdot t)$$

**Step 3 — MRR (Volume per Unit Time):**

$$MRR = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times f$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> Given: $D = 10$ mm, $f = 0.5$ mm/min. Find MRR.

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (10)^2 \times 0.5 \\
&= \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 100 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 50 \\
&= 39.27 \; \text{mm}^3\text{/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**
> An MRR of ~40 mm³/min is typical for **rough EDM** (roughing). Finishing passes use lower current → lower MRR (1–5 mm³/min) but better surface finish.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric MRR in ECM (Faraday's Law Application)

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the MRR in ECM of an iron workpiece in NaCl electrolyte, given current $I = 1000$ A, density of iron $\rho_{Fe} = 7874$ kg/m³, atomic mass $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, valency $n = 2$.

**Step 1 — Write Faraday's 1st Law:**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{1000 \times t \times 55.85 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
&= \frac{55.85 \times t}{192{,}970} \\
&= 2.894 \times 10^{-4} \times t \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Volume Using Density $\rho$:**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{2.894 \times 10^{-4} \times t}{7874} = 3.675 \times 10^{-8} \times t \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — MRR (Volume per Second):**

$$MRR = \frac{V}{t} = 3.675 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm³/min (KTU Standard Units):**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= 3.675 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^9 \; \text{mm}^3\text{/s} \times 60 \; \text{s/min} \\
&= 36.75 \times 60 \\
&= 2{,}205 \; \text{mm}^3\text{/min}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** ECM achieves **~2,200 mm³/min**, which is **~50× faster** than EDM (40 mm³/min). This is why ECM is preferred for **heavy stock removal** in aerospace alloys.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Etch Depth in Anisotropic Silicon Etching (KOH)

**Problem Statement:** A (100) silicon wafer is etched in 30 wt% KOH at 80 °C. The etch rate of Si(100) is $r_{100} = 1.4$ µm/min and Si(111) is $r_{111} = 0.014$ µm/min. The mask opening is $W = 100$ µm. Find (a) the time to etch through 525 µm thick wafer, (b) the V-groove sidewall angle, (c) the lateral undercut.

**Step 1 — Time to Etch Through Thickness $d = 525$ µm:**

$$t = \frac{d}{r_{100}} = \frac{525}{1.4} = 375 \; \text{min} = 6.25 \; \text{hours}$$

**Step 2 — V-Groove Sidewall Angle $\theta$:**

The angle is governed by the relative etch rates:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan \theta &= \frac{r_{100}}{r_{111}} = \frac{1.4}{0.014} = 100 \\
\theta &= \arctan(100) \approx 89.43°
\end{aligned}$$

But relative to the (100) surface, the actual sidewall makes an angle of:

$$\theta_{sidewall} = 90° - 54.74° = 35.26° \; \text{(with respect to surface)}$$

This **54.74°** angle arises from the crystal geometry — it's a constant for KOH-etched (100) silicon V-grooves.

**Step 3 — Lateral Undercut $U$:**

Undercut occurs at the Si(111) rate:

$$U = r_{111} \times t = 0.014 \times 375 = 5.25 \; \text{µm}$$

**Step 4 — Verification — Mask Opening Required for Full Etch:**

For an anisotropic V-groove, the mask must be wide enough that the (111) planes meet at the bottom:

$$W_{min} = 2 \times d \times \tan(54.74°) = 2 \times 525 \times 1.376 = 1445 \; \text{µm}$$

If $W < W_{min}$, the (111) planes do not meet, and an **incomplete pyramid** forms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Frequently Tested Concept: **V-groove angle = 70.52°** (measured as the included angle at the bottom) — derived from $2 \times 35.26°$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: MRR in WEDM (Slit Cutting)

**Problem Statement:** A WEDM cuts a workpiece using a wire of diameter $d_w = 0.25$ mm, with kerf $k = 0.3$ mm, and feed $f = 30$ mm/min. Calculate the MRR for a cut of depth $D = 50$ mm.

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of Cut:**

$$A = k \times D = 0.3 \times 50 = 15 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Length of Cut per Minute (Feed):**

$$L = f = 30 \; \text{mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — MRR (Volumetric):**

$$MRR = A \times L = 15 \times 30 = 450 \; \text{mm}^3\text{/min}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Logic (KTU Vignette Type Question):**
> 
> | If the requirement is... | Choose... | Why? |
> |---|---|---|
> | Machining a 50 µm micro-hole in hardened tool steel | **Micro-EDM** | Non-contact, high hardness compatible |
> | Cutting a 50 µm wide channel in a polymer (PMMA) | **Excimer LBM** | Polymer absorbs UV, no thermal damage |
> | Drilling 0.5 mm deep, 20 µm diameter hole in a nickel super-alloy | **LBM or FIB** | Small diameter, no tool access |
> | Mass-producing 1000s of identical silicon micro-pillars | **DRIE** | High aspect ratio, batch fabrication |
> | Removing 10 mm of stock from a turbine blade made of Inconel | **ECM** | No tool wear, high MRR, no HAZ |
> | Cutting 2-D profile from a 0.5 mm thick titanium sheet | **WEDM** | 2-D contour, fine wire (0.05 mm) |
> | Fabricating 3-D V-grooves on a silicon wafer | **Anisotropic CHM (KOH)** | Crystal-dependent etch angles |

## 4.3 Process Flow — Chemical Machining (CHM) of Silicon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Silicon Wafer Cleaning<br>RCA Process] --> step2[Thermal Oxidation<br>SiO2 Layer 1 micrometer]
    step2 --> step3[Photoresist Spin Coating<br>HMDS Adhesion Promoter]
    step3 --> step4[Soft Bake<br>95 C for 1 min]
    step4 --> step5[UV Exposure<br>Through Photomask]
    step5 --> step6[Development<br>Positive Resist dissolves]
    step6 --> step7[Hard Bake<br>120 C for 2 min]
    step7 --> step8[SiO2 Etching<br>Buffered HF BHF]
    step8 --> step9[Photoresist Strip<br>Acetone and Piranha]
    step9 --> step10[Anisotropic Etching<br>KOH 30 percent at 80 C]
    step10 --> step11[Rinsing in DI Water<br>and Critical Point Drying]
    step11 --> step12[Final 3D Microstructure<br>V-groove or Pyramid]
```

flowchart TD

    ecm3[Electrolyte Tank<br>NaCl or NaNO3] --> ecm4[High Pressure Pump<br>5-50 m per sec]
    ecm4 --> ecm5[Nozzle and Gap Control<br>0.1-1 mm gap]

flowchart LR

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between EDM and ECM processes based on working principle, tool wear, and heat-affected zone (HAZ).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | EDM | ECM |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Material removed by **thermal vaporization** via electric spark | Material removed by **anodic dissolution** via electrolysis |
| **Tool Wear** | **Yes** — tool electrode erodes over time | **No** — cathode tool is unaffected |
| **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** | **Yes** — recast layer and thermal damage present | **No** — process is **cold** (no heat) |
| **Surface Finish** | Ra 0.2–3 µm | Ra 0.1–1 µm |
| **Power Source** | Pulsed DC, high voltage (80–200 V), low current | Low voltage (5–25 V), very high current (1000–10000 A) |
| **MRR** | Lower (~40 mm³/min) | Higher (~2000 mm³/min) |

(3 marks — award 1 mark each for the three parameters compared)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: What is anisotropic etching? Explain with reference to KOH etching of silicon. Why is the V-groove sidewall angle 54.74°?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Anisotropic etching** is a process in which the etch rate of a material depends on the **crystallographic orientation** of the surface being etched. Different crystal planes (e.g., Si(100), Si(110), Si(111)) etch at different rates in the same etchant.

**KOH Etching of Silicon (30 wt% KOH at 80 °C):**
- Etch rate of Si(100) ≈ 1.4 µm/min
- Etch rate of Si(111) ≈ 0.014 µm/min
- **Selectivity** = 1.4 / 0.014 = **100 : 1**

**Why 54.74°?**
- The Si(111) plane is inclined at **54.74°** to the Si(100) surface.
- Since Si(111) etches very slowly, etching **stops** when (111) planes are exposed.
- These planes form the **sidewalls of V-grooves** at exactly this angle.
- The geometry of a cubic crystal gives: $\cos(54.74°) = 1/\sqrt{3}$, which is a fundamental geometric constant.
