- [x] **Lithography = pattern transfer from mask to resist via energy (photons, electrons, ions) or mechanical contact.**
- [x] **The four pillars:** Mask → Exposure → Development → Pattern Transfer.
- [x] **Rayleigh Resolution:** $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ — the single most important equation.
- [x] **DOF:** $DOF = \pm k_2 \lambda / NA^2$ — *quadratic* NA dependence.
- [x] **Three knobs to improve R:** ↓$\lambda$, ↑$NA$, ↓$k_1$ (via RETs).
- [x] **NA definition:** $NA = n \sin\theta$; immersion breaks the air limit ($n = 1.44$).
- [x] **Photoresists:** Positive (exposed removed) vs Negative (exposed remains). CARs are industry standard below 250 nm.
- [x] **Photolithography modes:** Contact > Proximity > Projection in terms of defect density; Projection is industry standard.
- [x] **EBL:** Highest resolution (< 10 nm), serial, low throughput — used for *mask-making*.
- [x] **NIL:** Low-cost, parallel, sub-10 nm — used in NAND flash and photonics.
- [x] **EUV (13.5 nm):** Current frontier; enables $k_1 \approx 0.4$ at single exposure.
- [x] **CARs + PEB** are the chemical engine of sub-100 nm lithography; PEB uniformity is critical.
- [x] **Etch selectivity** (film vs resist) must be > 5:1 for clean pattern transfer.
- [x] **KTU favorite numbers to remember:** i-line (365 nm), KrF (248 nm), ArF (193 nm), EUV (13.5 nm), immersion $n = 1.44$, modern $NA = 1.35$.
- [x] **Dill parameters A, B, C** describe resist exposure kinetics — required only for advanced 7-mark derivations.
- [x] **Resolution enhancement techniques (RETs):** PSM, OAI, OPC, double patterning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin Film Deposition and Doping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thin Film Deposition and Doping # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Thin Film Deposition? **Thin Film Deposition** is the foundational enabling technology of modern micro/nano manufacturing. It refers to the controlled synthesis of a thin layer of material (typically ranging from a few nanometers to several micrometers in thickness) onto a substrate (wafer, glass, metal foil, polymer) through the transfer of atoms, molecules, or ions from a source. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Thin film deposition is a process by which atoms or molecules of a source material are transported through a controlled environment (vacuum or reactive gas) and condensed onto a substrate surface to form a continuous, adherent, and uniform layer with engineered structural, electrical, optical, or mechanical properties.* In the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for **Micro and Nano Manufacturing**, thin films form the literal building blocks of: - Integrated Circuits (interconnects, dielectrics, gates) - MEMS/NEMS sensors and actuators - Solar cells (CdTe, CIGS, perovskite layers) - Optical coatings (anti-reflective, dichroic mirrors) - Hard coatings (TiN, DLC on cutting tools) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metric:** A **thin film** is technically defined as a layer whose thickness is below the threshold where bulk material properties (e.g., grain size, electron mean free path, light wavelength) dominate. Films are categorized as **ultrathin (< 100 nm)**, **thin (100 nm – 1 µm)**, and **thick films (> 1 µm)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Painting on an Atomic Scale" Imagine you want to paint a wall, but instead of a brush and paint, you are using individual atoms as the "paint drops" and a clean silicon wafer as the "wall." - **Source Material** = Your paint pot - **Vacuum Chamber** = A windless, dust-free room so each atom flies straight without bumping into air molecules - **Substrate (Wafer)** = The wall to be painted - **Deposition Process** = The painting technique (brushing, spraying, dipping) Just as painting can be done in several ways (roller, spray gun, dip coating), thin films too can be deposited using different physical and chemical techniques. The choice depends on: - Material to be deposited (metal, oxide, polymer?) - Required film quality (crystalline vs amorphous?) - Temperature tolerance of the substrate - Required deposition rate and uniformity --- ## 1.3 What is Doping? **Doping** is the deliberate introduction of impurity atoms (dopants) into a semiconductor or dielectric film in precisely controlled concentrations to modulate its electrical, optical, or magnetic properties. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Doping is the intentional incorporation of Group III (p-type: Boron, Indium) or Group V (n-type: Phosphorus, Arsenic, Antimony) elements into a Group IV semiconductor (Si, Ge) lattice, typically at concentrations ranging from 10¹⁴ to 10²¹ atoms/cm³, to precisely control carrier concentration, conductivity type, and junction characteristics.* --- ## 1.4 Real-World Analogy for Doping — "Salting a Pure Sweet Dish" Think of doping as the act of adding a pinch of salt to a sweet, pure sugar syrup: - Pure silicon = pure sugar (electrically neutral, insulator-like behavior) - Adding a tiny pinch of phosphorus (n-type) or boron (p-type) = adding salt (transforms the electrical behavior fundamentally) - **More salt → more conductivity**, but too much ruins the lattice (the dish becomes inedible — analogous to lattice damage in ion implantation) This "salt" addition must be done with **extreme precision** — even a deviation of 0.001% in concentration can shift a transistor's threshold voltage by hundreds of millivolts. --- ## 1.5 Why Thin Films and Doping are Inseparable in Manufacturing In modern CMOS technology, deposition and doping are interleaved hundreds of times to build a single microprocessor. A typical 7nm FinFET process involves: - **>60 thin film deposition steps** - **>40 doping/implantation steps** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Trilogy of Film Quality:** Every KTU question on thin films ultimately tests your grasp of these three pillars: > 1. **Adhesion** — Will the film stick to the substrate? > 2. **Uniformity** — Is the thickness the same across a 300 mm wafer (±1%)? > 3. **Conformality / Step Coverage** — Does the film coat sidewalls of high-aspect-ratio trenches uniformly? --- ## 1.6 Classification of Thin Film Deposition Techniques The deposition universe branches into two master families: | Master Family | Energy Source | Sub-classification | |---|---|---| | **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** | Mechanical / thermal kinetic energy | Evaporation, Sputtering, MBE, PLD | | **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** | Chemical reaction of precursors | APCVD, LPCVD, PECVD, MOCVD, ALD | | **Solution-Based** | Solvent evaporation + curing | Spin coating, Dip coating, Screen printing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **PVD vs CVD Trade-off (KTU Favorite):** PVD generally gives **better film purity and lower substrate temperature**, while CVD gives **superior step coverage and higher throughput**. Both have non-overlapping sweet spots in fabrication. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) ### 2.1.1 Thermal Evaporation **Principle:** The source material is heated (resistively or by electron beam) until its vapor pressure exceeds ~10⁻² Torr. Atoms then travel in straight lines (line-of-sight) to the substrate and condense. **Step-by-Step Operational Logic:** 1. Substrate loaded into high vacuum chamber (**10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁷ Torr**). 2. Source material (Au, Al, Cu) placed in a tungsten boat or crucible. 3. Resistive heating OR e-beam bombardment vaporizes atoms. 4. Atoms traverse the vacuum chamber with mean free path **λ >> chamber dimension**. 5. Atoms strike the substrate, lose kinetic energy, and nucleate to form islands → coalesce → continuous film. > [!NOTE] > **Mean Free Path (MFP)** is the average distance a vapor atom travels before colliding with a residual gas molecule. Higher vacuum = longer MFP = cleaner film. ### 2.1.2 Sputtering **Principle:** Positively charged inert gas ions (typically Ar⁺) are accelerated toward a target (cathode) of the material to be deposited. Momentum transfer ejects target atoms which fly to the substrate. **Modes of Sputtering:** - **DC Sputtering** — used for conductive targets (metals). - **RF Sputtering** — used for insulating targets (Al₂O₃, SiO₂) because AC prevents charge buildup. - **Magnetron Sputtering** — magnets behind the target trap electrons near the cathode, dramatically increasing ionization efficiency and deposition rate. ### 2.1.3 E-beam Evaporation A focused high-energy electron beam (5–20 keV) is directed onto a crucible of source material, locally melting and evaporating it. Allows evaporation of high-melting-point materials (W, Mo, ceramics) and yields very high purity films. --- ## 2.2 Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) **Principle:** Volatile precursor gases react (or decompose) on a heated substrate surface to form a solid film, with by-products pumped away. **Generic Reaction:** $$\text{SiH}_4 \text{ (gas)} \xrightarrow{600-900^\circ\text{C}} \text{Si (solid)} + 2\text{H}_2 \text{ (gas)}$$ ### 2.2.1 Variants of CVD (Critical for KTU) | Variant | Pressure | Temperature | Key Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **APCVD** (Atmospheric Pressure CVD) | 760 Torr | 600–900°C | Simple, high throughput, poor step coverage | | **LPCVD** (Low Pressure CVD) | 0.1–10 Torr | 550–850°C | Excellent uniformity, conformal | | **PECVD** (Plasma Enhanced CVD) | 0.1–10 Torr | 200–400°C | Plasma assists reaction → low temp (heat-sensitive substrates) | | **MOCVD** (Metal-Organic CVD) | 10–100 Torr | 600–800°C | Used for compound semiconductors (GaAs, GaN LED) | | **ALD** (Atomic Layer Deposition) | 1–10 Torr | 100–300°C | Self-limiting, atomic-scale thickness control | > [!IMPORTANT] > **ALD vs CVD — The Precision Dial:** ALD exposes the substrate to precursor gases in alternating, sequential pulses. Each pulse deposits **only ONE atomic layer** because the reaction self-saturates. This is the technique of choice for sub-7nm node transistors where film thickness must be controlled to within **±0.1 nm**. --- ## 2.3 Doping Techniques ### 2.3.1 Diffusion Doping Dopant atoms are introduced from a vapor phase (e.g., POCl₃ for phosphorus) or solid source (BN wafer for boron) into a wafer held at high temperature (**800–1200°C**). The diffusion follows Fick's laws. **Fick's First Law (Steady State):** $$J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial N(x,t)}{\partial x}$$ **Fick's Second Law (Transient):** $$\frac{\partial N(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 N(x,t)}{\partial x^2}$$ ### 2.3.2 Ion Implantation Dopant ions are ionized, accelerated through a high voltage (10–500 keV), and physically shot into the substrate. The depth of penetration is controlled by **acceleration energy**, while the dose is controlled by **beam current × time**. **Gaussian Distribution (Projected Range Rp):** $$N(x) = \frac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p} \cdot \exp\left[-\frac{(x - R_p)^2}{2\,(\Delta R_p)^2}\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Dose (Φ)** is measured in atoms/cm². Typical implant doses range from **10¹¹** (light doping, channel) to **10¹⁶** (heavily doped source/drain). **Key parameters:** - **Projected Range (Rₚ)** — depth of maximum concentration. - **Straggle (ΔRₚ)** — standard deviation of the distribution. - **Lateral Straggle (ΔRₚ,lateral)** — sideways spread, ~ 0.5 × ΔRₚ. > [!WARNING] > Ion implantation causes **lattice damage** (amorphization at high doses). A subsequent **anneal step** (rapid thermal anneal at 1000–1100°C for < 1 second) is mandatory to repair damage and activate dopants. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Mean Free Path (vacuum) | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2\,P}$ | meters; $d$ = molecular diameter | | Deposition Rate (PVD) | $R_d = \dfrac{m}{\rho\,A\,t}$ | nm/min; $m$ = mass deposited | | Step Coverage | $\%\,\text{SC} = \dfrac{T_{\text{sidewall}}}{T_{\text{top}}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | | Fick's First Law | $J = -D\,\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}$ | atoms/cm²·s | | Fick's Second Law | $\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2}$ | $D$ = diffusivity (cm²/s) | | Erfc Diffusion Profile | $N(x,t) = N_0\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Constant surface concentration | | Gaussian Implant Profile | $N(x) = \dfrac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\,\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x-R_p)^2}{2(\Delta R_p)^2}\right]$ | atoms/cm³ | | Projected Range | $R_p \propto E^{0.5\text{–}1}$ | keV; empirically calibrated | | Dose | $\Phi = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{q \cdot A}$ | atoms/cm² | | Sticking Coefficient | $S_c = \dfrac{N_{\text{adhered}}}{N_{\text{incident}}}$ | 0 to 1 | | Sheet Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{\rho}{t}$ | Ω/□ (ohms per square) | | Stress in Film | $\sigma = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Pa; $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Sputtered TiN** is used as a hard coating on drill bits and end-mills, extending tool life by 5–10x. - **PECVD SiN** is used as a passivation layer on solar cells to reduce recombination losses by ~30%. - **Ion Implantation** is the *only* commercially viable way to dope sub-22nm transistors with nanometer-scale precision. - **ALD Al₂O₃** is used as a gate dielectric in advanced FinFETs (EOT < 1 nm). - **Thermal Evaporation of Al** is used for back-side metallization of solar cells and OLED cathodes. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Surface Concentration Profile by Diffusion ### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions We aim to derive the dopant concentration profile $N(x,t)$ inside a semiconductor wafer subjected to diffusion from a constant surface concentration $N_0$. **Assumptions:** 1. Semi-infinite wafer ($x \geq 0$, no flux at $x = \infty$). 2. Constant surface concentration: $N(0,t) = N_0$ (infinite source). 3. Initial concentration: $N(x,0) = 0$ for $x > 0$. 4. Constant diffusivity $D$ (independent of concentration). ### 3.1.2 Applying Fick's Second Law Start from Fick's second law: $$\frac{\partial N(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 N(x,t)}{\partial x^2}$$ This is a 1D heat equation. The standard solution for an infinite source is the **complementary error function (erfc) profile**. We derive it by using the similarity variable: $$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$ Substituting into the PDE, after chain rule expansion: $$\frac{d^2 N}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \cdot \frac{dN}{d\eta} = 0$$ Integrating once: $$\frac{dN}{d\eta} = C_1 \cdot e^{-\eta^2}$$ Integrating again: $$N(\eta) = C_1 \int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2}\,du + C_2$$ Using boundary conditions: $N(0,t) = N_0 \Rightarrow C_2 = N_0$, and $N(\infty,t) = 0$: $$\boxed{\,N(x,t) = N_0 \cdot \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\,}$$ The total amount of dopant per unit area (dose) is: $$Q(t) = \int_0^{\infty} N(x,t)\,dx = \frac{2\,N_0\sqrt{Dt}}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$ ### 3.1.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Phosphorus is diffused into silicon at 1000°C with $N_0 = 10^{20}$ atoms/cm³. Given $D(1000°C) = 2.5 \times 10^{-14}$ cm²/s, calculate the concentration at depth $x = 1\,\mu m$ after 1 hour. **Step 1 — Compute $\sqrt{Dt}$:** $$Dt = 2.5 \times 10^{-14} \times 3600 = 9.0 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{cm}^2$$ $$\sqrt{Dt} = 9.487 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm} = 0.0949\,\mu m$$ **Step 2 — Compute the argument:** $$\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{1.0\,\mu m}{2 \times 0.0949\,\mu m} = 5.27$$ **Step 3 — Look up erfc:** $$\mathrm{erfc}(5.27) \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-13}$$ **Step 4 — Final Concentration:** $$N = 10^{20} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-13} = 1.5 \times 10^{7}\,\text{atoms/cm}^3$$ This shows that at $x = 1\,\mu m$ after 1 hour, the concentration has fallen by **13 orders of magnitude** — diffusion doping has a sharply bounded profile. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Ion Implant Dose from Beam Current ### 3.2.1 Physical Setup A uniform ion beam of current $I$ (amperes) bombards a substrate of area $A$ for time $t$ (seconds). Each ion carries charge $q$ (Coulombs). Find the implanted dose $\Phi$. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation Total charge delivered: $$Q_{total} = I \cdot t \quad [\text{Coulombs}]$$ Number of ions implanted (each carrying charge $q$): $$n_{ions} = \frac{Q_{total}}{q} = \frac{I \cdot t}{q}$$ Dose (ions per unit area): $$\Phi = \frac{n_{ions}}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t}{q \cdot A} \quad [\text{ions/cm}^2]$$ ### 3.2.3 Numerical Example **Problem:** A phosphorus ion implanter delivers 5 mA of current over a 300 mm wafer (area = 706.86 cm²) for 30 seconds. Find the dose. **Step 1:** $$\Phi = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}) \times (30\,\text{s})}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}) \times (706.86\,\text{cm}^2)}$$ $$\Phi = \frac{0.15}{1.131 \times 10^{-16}} = 1.326 \times 10^{15}\,\text{ions/cm}^2$$ This is a **medium-dose implant** typical for source/drain formation in older CMOS nodes. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Step Coverage in Trench ### 3.3.1 Definition Step coverage is the ratio of film thickness on the sidewall of a trench to the thickness on the top surface. For PVD (line-of-sight), it is poor; for CVD (surface-diffusion-limited), it can be excellent. ### 3.3.2 CVD Analytical Model (Suzuki Model) For CVD in a trench of width $W$ and depth $H$: $$\%\,\text{SC} = \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{4\,D_s\,\tau}{W}\right)\right] \times \frac{1}{1 + \frac{H}{W}\,\sqrt{\frac{D}{D_s}}}$$ Where: - $D_s$ = surface diffusion coefficient - $D$ = gas-phase diffusion coefficient - $\tau$ = surface reaction time constant > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** When $D_s$ is high (high temperature), precursors diffuse along the surface before reacting, giving conformal coverage. When $D_s$ is low (low temp CVD), precursors react on first contact — poor bottom coverage. --- ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Exam Ready) | Parameter | Thermal Evaporation | Sputtering | CVD | ALD | Ion Implant | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Pressure (Torr) | $10^{-7}$ | $10^{-3}$ | 0.1–760 | 1–10 | $10^{-6}$ | | Substrate Temp (°C) | RT–100 | RT–300 | 400–900 | 100–350 | RT (then anneal) | | Film Purity | High | High | Medium-High | Ultra High | Ultra High | | Step Coverage | Poor (~30%) | Poor–Fair | Excellent (>90%) | Perfect (100%) | N/A | | Deposition Rate | High (nm/s) | Medium | High | Very Low (Å/cycle) | N/A | | Damage to Substrate | None | Some (e-beam) | Thermal | None | High (requires anneal) | | Doping Use | Indirect | Indirect | In-situ doping | Atomic-scale doping | Direct (gold standard) | | Cost / Tooling | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Very High | | Best Application | Metal electrodes, mirrors | Hard coatings, metallization | Poly-Si, oxides | High-k dielectrics | Source/Drain, wells | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PVD Sputtering Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Argon Gas Inlet] --> B1[Vacuum Chamber
10 to the minus 3 Torr] B1 --> C1[Plasma Ignition
RF or DC source] C1 --> D1[Ar positive ions
accelerated toward cathode] D1 --> E1[Target Material
cathode surface] E1 --> F1[Momentum Transfer
sputtering eject] F1 --> G1[Ejected Atoms
fly line of sight] G1 --> H1[Substrate Surface
nucleation and growth] H1 --> I1[Continuous Thin Film
on wafer] ``` ## 4.2 CVD Process Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Precursor Gas A
e.g. SiH4] --> M1[Gas Mixing
and MFC Control] P2[Precursor Gas B
e.g. N2O] --> M1 M1 --> R1[Heated Reactor Chamber
600 to 900 degree C] R1 --> S1[Chemical Reaction
on wafer surface] S1 --> F1[Desired Solid Film
SiO2 deposition] R1 --> EX[Byproduct Gases
H2 N2] EX --> PUMP[Vacuum Pump
Exhaust] ``` ## 4.3 ALD Sequential Pulse Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR STEP1[Step 1: Pulse A
Precursor 1 dose] --> PURGE1[Purge with N2
remove excess] PURGE1 --> STEP2[Step 2: Pulse B
Precursor 2 dose] STEP2 --> PURGE2[Purge with N2
remove excess] PURGE2 --> LAYER[One Atomic Layer
deposited per cycle] LAYER --> LOOP{Repeat
for N cycles} LOOP -->|Yes| STEP1 LOOP -->|No| FIN[Final Film
Thickness equals N times d] ``` ## 4.4 Ion Implantation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Source Gas
e.g. BF3 for Boron] --> ION[Ionization Chamber
filament emission] ION --> MASS[Mass Analyzer
select correct isotope] MASS --> ACC[Acceleration Column
10 to 500 keV] ACC --> SCAN[Electrostatic Scanner
x y raster] SCAN --> WAFER[Wafer Target
doped silicon] WAFER --> RESULT[Gaussian Dopant Profile
centered at projected range Rp] ``` ## 4.5 Diffusion Doping Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB SRC[Dopant Source
Solid BN or POCl3 liquid] --> FURN[High Temperature Furnace
800 to 1200 degree C] FURN --> VAPOR[Dopant Vapor
transported by carrier gas] VAPOR --> WAFER[Silicon Wafer
surface reaction] WAFER --> DIFF[Dopant Atoms
diffuse into bulk] DIFF --> PROF[Erfc Concentration Profile
N equals N0 erfc x by 2 sqrt Dt] ``` ## 4.6 Thin Film Growth Stages ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stage 1: Nucleation
Islands form on substrate] --> B[Stage 2: Coalescence
Islands merge into larger ones] B --> C[Stage 3: Percolation
Connected network forms] C --> D[Stage 4: Channel Filling
Remaining voids filled] D --> E[Stage 5: Continuous Film
Uniform thickness achieved] ``` ## 4.7 Functional Comparison Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHYSICAL[Physical Vapor Deposition PVD] EV[Thermal Evaporation] EB[E Beam Evaporation] SP[Sputtering DC RF Magnetron] end subgraph CHEMICAL[Chemical Vapor Deposition CVD] AP[APCVD] LP[LPCVD] PE[PECVD] MO[MOCVD] ALD[ALD] end subgraph SOLN[Solution Based] SC[Spin Coating] DC[Dip Coating] end PHYSICAL --> COMPARE{Required Film
Property} CHEMICAL --> COMPARE SOLN --> COMPARE COMPARE -->|High Purity| EV COMPARE -->|Step Coverage| LP COMPARE -->|Low Temp| PE COMPARE -->|Atomic Precision| ALD COMPARE -->|Polymer Film| SC ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Physical Vapor Deposition. List any four PVD techniques.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Answer:** Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) is a thin film deposition process in which atoms of the source material are physically transferred in vacuum from a solid or liquid source to the substrate surface, where they condense to form a thin film, without involving a chemical reaction. Four PVD techniques are: 1. Thermal Evaporation 2. Electron Beam Evaporation 3. DC Sputtering 4. RF Sputtering 5. Magnetron Sputtering *(any four)* --- ### **Question 2: Differentiate between diffusion doping and ion implantation doping.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Diffusion | Ion Implantation | |---|---|---| | Driving Force | Thermal (concentration gradient) | Kinetic (ion acceleration) | | Temperature | 800–1200°C | Room temp (with post-anneal) | | Dose Control | Indirect (time/temp) | Direct (current × time) | | Profile Shape | erfc (exponential tail) | Gaussian (peaked at Rₚ) | | Lateral Spread | Significant (isotropic) | Small (ΔRₚ,lateral) | | Lattice Damage | None | High (requires anneal) | | Throughput | Batch friendly | Single wafer | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) Explain in detail the working principle and process steps of the Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) technique. Compare LPCVD and PECVD.** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Solution:** **Principle of CVD:** CVD is a thin-film deposition process in which volatile precursors undergo chemical reactions on or near a heated substrate surface to produce a solid film, with volatile by-products removed by gas flow. **Process Steps:** 1. **Gas Delivery:** Reactant gases (e.g., SiH₄, NH₃, O₂) are metered via Mass Flow Controllers (MFCs) into the reaction chamber. 2. **Gas Phase Transport:** Gases flow over the heated wafer; mass transport is by forced convection and diffusion. 3. **Surface Adsorption:** Reactant molecules adsorb onto the wafer surface. 4. **Surface Reaction:** Surface diffusion and reaction occur (rate-limiting step at low temp). 5. **Film Growth:** Desired atoms incorporate into the lattice; by-products desorb. 6. **By-Product Removal:** Gaseous by-products (H₂, HCl) are swept out by the exhaust. **Comparison LPCVD vs PECVD:** | Parameter | LPCVD | PECVD | |---|---|---| | Pressure | 0.1–10 Torr | 0.1–10 Torr | | Substrate Temp | 550–850°C | 200–400°C | | Energy Source | Thermal only | RF plasma + thermal | | Film Quality | Excellent, conformal | Good, less conformal | | Application | Poly-Si, Si₃N₄ | Passivation, interlayer dielectric | | Throughput | High (batch, 100+ wafers) | Medium | | Cost | Medium | High (RF generator) | --- #### **(b) Describe the principle and applications of Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD). Explain why ALD is preferred for sub-7nm CMOS devices.** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Solution:** **Principle of ALD:** ALD is a CVD variant in which the precursor gases are introduced sequentially into the chamber, separated by purge steps. Each precursor reacts with the surface in a **self-limiting** manner, depositing at most one atomic layer per cycle. **Typical Cycle (Al₂O₃ ALD using TMA and H₂O):** 1. **Pulse 1 — TMA:** Trimethylaluminum reacts with surface –OH groups, releasing CH₄. 2. **Purge 1 — N₂:** Removes excess TMA and CH₄. 3. **Pulse 2 — H₂O:** Reacts with surface –CH₃ groups, regenerating –OH sites. 4. **Purge 2 — N₂:** Removes excess H₂O and CH₄. 5. **Cycle Count:** A 100 nm film requires ~1000 cycles (each ~0.1 nm/cycle). **Why ALD for sub-7nm CMOS:** 1. **Atomic-level thickness control** — essential for EOT (Equivalent Oxide Thickness) of high-k dielectrics < 1 nm. 2. **100% conformality** over high-aspect-ratio FinFET sidewalls (AR > 20). 3. **Pin-hole free films** — critical for gate leakage suppression. 4. **Precise composition control** — allows ternary oxides (HfZrO₂, LaSiO₃). 5. **Low thermal budget** — compatible with BEOL processing (< 400°C). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** #### **(a) With a neat schematic, explain the principle of ion implantation. Derive the expression for implanted dose in terms of beam current and time.** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Solution:** **Principle:** In ion implantation, dopant atoms are ionized, accelerated through a high voltage, mass-analyzed, and physically driven into the substrate. The depth of penetration is controlled by the acceleration energy, while the dose is controlled by the beam current × time. **Components of an Ion Implanter:** 1. **Ion Source:** Produces dopant ions (e.g., BF₃ for B, PH₃ for P). 2. **Extraction Electrode:** Pulls ions into the beamline. 3. **Mass Analyzer:** Magnetic sector separates the desired isotope (e.g., ¹¹B from ¹⁰B). 4. **Acceleration Column:** Accelerates ions to 10–500 keV. 5. **Electrostatic Scanner:** Rasters the beam across the wafer. 6. **End Station:** Wafer platen with charge-neutralization flood gun. **Derivation of Dose:** Let $I$ = beam current (A), $t$ = time (s), $q$ = charge of one ion (C), $A$ = wafer area (cm²). Total charge delivered: $$Q_{total} = I \cdot t \quad [\text{Coulombs}]$$ Number of ions implanted: $$n_{ions} = \frac{Q_{total}}{q} = \frac{I \cdot t}{q}$$ Dose per unit area: $$\Phi = \frac{n_{ions}}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t}{q \cdot A} \quad [\text{ions/cm}^2]$$ **Numerical:** For $I = 1\,\text{mA}$, $t = 100\,\text{s}$, $A = 100\,\text{cm}^2$: $$\Phi = \frac{10^{-3} \times 100}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 100} = 6.25 \times 10^{13}\,\text{ions/cm}^2$$ --- #### **(b) Discuss the various mechanisms of thin film growth. Explain the importance of substrate temperature and deposition rate on film microstructure.** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Solution:** **Three Growth Modes (Bauer Classification):** 1. **Frank–van der Merwe (Layer-by-Layer / 2D):** - Atoms bond more strongly to substrate than to each other. - Example: Cu on W; Au on Ag. - Produces smooth, uniform epitaxial films. 2. **Volmer–Weber (Island / 3D):** - Atoms bond more strongly to each other than to substrate. - Example: Au on SiO₂; Ag on NaCl. - Produces rough, polycrystalline films. 3. **Stranski–Krastanov (Layer + Island):** - Initial layer growth followed by island growth. - Example: Ge on Si. - Common in lattice-mismatched systems. **Effect of Substrate Temperature:** - **Low T (T/Tₘ < 0.3):** Adatoms have low mobility → porous, amorphous, high-stress films. - **Moderate T (0.3 < T/Tₘ < 0.5):** Surface diffusion is sufficient → polycrystalline, dense films. - **High T (T/Tₘ > 0.5):** Bulk diffusion dominates → epitaxial, large-grain films (single crystal). (Here Tₘ = melting point of the film material.) **Effect of Deposition Rate:** - **Low rate:** Adatoms have time to find low-energy sites → crystalline, smooth, low defect density. - **High rate:** Burial of adatoms is faster than surface diffusion → amorphous, rough, defective films. **Structure Zone Model (Movchan–Demchishin–Thornton):** The microstructure is classified into four zones based on homologous temperature T/Tₘ: - **Zone 1 (T/Tₘ < 0.3):** Tapered crystallites, porous. - **Zone T (Transition, 0.3–0.5):** Denser columnar grains. - **Zone 2 (0.5–0.75):** Columnar grains with smooth surfaces. - **Zone 3 (> 0.75):** Equiaxed grains, recrystallization. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing erfc with Gaussian:** Diffusion gives an **erfc** profile (constant surface source) while ion implantation gives a **Gaussian** profile (peak at Rₚ). Mixing these up is the #1 mistake students make. > 2. **Forgetting anneal step:** Never describe ion implantation without mentioning the mandatory post-implant anneal to repair lattice damage and activate dopants. > 3. **Wrong units:** Dose is in **ions/cm²** (an areal quantity), while concentration is in **atoms/cm³** (a volumetric quantity). Mixing these costs full marks. > 4. **ALC vs ALD:** Don't confuse Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) with Atomic Layer Etching (ALE). They are opposites — one deposits, one etches. > 5. **Sputtering vs Evaporation:** Sputtering is a **momentum transfer** process (kinetic), not a thermal process. Students often wrongly describe sputtering as "evaporation by ion bombardment." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ Thin film = layer with thickness ranging from **nm to µm**, deposited by PVD, CVD, or solution methods. - ✅ **PVD** = physical transfer of atoms in vacuum; sub-techniques are **Evaporation** (thermal, e-beam) and **Sputtering** (DC, RF, Magnetron). - ✅ **CVD** = chemical reaction of precursors on heated substrate; variants are **APCVD, LPCVD, PECVD, MOCVD**. - ✅ **ALD** = sequential, self-limiting gas pulses; deposits **one atomic layer per cycle**; best for sub-7nm. - ✅ **Step Coverage** = ratio of sidewall to top thickness — CVD ≫ PVD for conformality. - ✅ **Mean Free Path** $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2\,P}$ must be >> chamber size for line-of-sight PVD. - ✅ **Doping** introduces Group III (B, p-type) or Group V (P, As, n-type) atoms into Group IV semiconductors. - ✅ **Diffusion doping** uses Fick's laws; profile is $N(x,t) = N_0 \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$. - ✅ **Ion Implantation** uses kinetic acceleration; profile is Gaussian: $N(x) = \dfrac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x-R_p)^2}{2(\Delta R_p)^2}\right]$. - ✅ **Dose** $\Phi = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{q \cdot A}$ in **ions/cm²**. - ✅ **Post-implant anneal** is mandatory to repair lattice damage and activate dopants. - ✅ **Growth Modes:** Frank–van der Merwe (2D), Volmer–Weber (3D), Stranski–Krastanov (mixed). - ✅ **Movchan–Demchishin Structure Zone Model** predicts film microstructure from T/Tₘ. - ✅ **Substrate temperature** governs adatom mobility → crystallinity, density, stress. - ✅ **Deposition rate** controls adatom burial vs surface diffusion time → amorphous vs crystalline. ---

topics.title: Etching and Substrate Removal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Etching and Substrate Removal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Etching** in micro and nano manufacturing is defined as a **selective material removal process** in which unwanted portions of a substrate, thin film, or bulk material are chemically, physically, or chemo-physically dissolved, sputtered, or volatilized from a workpiece using either liquid-phase (wet) or vapor/plasma-phase (dry) media. The process is controlled spatially by a **mask** (photoresist, hard mask, or metal) to transfer a predefined pattern into the underlying material with controlled **etch rate**, **selectivity**, and **profile (anisotropy)**.

**Substrate Removal** is the broader, integrated step in MEMS/NEMS fabrication where the bulk substrate (typically Si, glass, quartz, GaAs, or polymer) is removed — partially or completely — to release suspended structures (beams, membranes, cantilevers, channels). It includes both **frontside bulk micromachining** and **backside bulk micromachining** techniques, often culminating in a **release etch** that frees the structural layer from the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics (must be memorized):**
> - **Etch Rate (ER):** measured in **nm/min** or **µm/min**
> - **Selectivity (S):** ratio of target material etch rate to mask (or underlying layer) etch rate — **dimensionless**
> - **Anisotropy (A):** ratio of vertical etch rate to horizontal etch rate, where **A = 1** is isotropic and **A → ∞** is perfectly anisotropic
> - **Uniformity:** % deviation of etch depth across the wafer
> - **Critical Dimension (CD) Loss:** lateral undercut in nm

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ice-Cream Carving Workshop" 🍦

Imagine a large rectangular **block of ice-cream** (the substrate) covered with a thin layer of **chocolate coating** (the mask). A pattern is drawn on the chocolate.

- **Wet Etching** = Pouring warm chocolate sauce selectively on the exposed ice-cream. The heat + chemistry melts it. The melt spreads sideways → **rounded edges (isotropic profile, undercut)**.
- **Dry Etching (Plasma/RIE)** = Using a high-pressure **water-jet cutter** aimed straight down. It cuts vertically with minimal sideways spread → **straight walls (anisotropic profile)**.
- **DRIE (Bosch Process)** = Pulsing the water jet on and off, alternating with a side-wall protective spray → **very deep, near-vertical trenches**.
- **Substrate Removal (Release Etch)** = Finally, dissolving the entire supporting plate of the ice-cream block to leave a **floating chocolate cantilever** — exactly like releasing a MEMS beam.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for Anisotropy:** A perfect isotropic etch eats sideways as fast as it eats down → mask edges recede equally → *undercut = etch depth*. A perfect anisotropic etch eats *only* downward → mask dimensions are preserved exactly.

---

## 1.3 Why Etching is Central to Micro/Nano Manufacturing

| Application Domain | Role of Etching |
|---|---|
| **IC Fabrication** | Patterning of Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, polysilicon, metals, dielectrics |
| **MEMS** | Releasing beams, diaphragms, comb-drives, channels |
| **NEMS** | Defining sub-100 nm features with atomic precision |
| **Photonics** | Waveguide definition, grating fabrication |
| **Microfluidics** | Channel & chamber formation in glass/silicon |
| **Power Devices** | Deep trench etching for super-junction MOSFETs & IGBTs |
| **Nanoelectronics** | Atomic Layer Etching (ALE) of 2D materials, FinFETs |

> [!NOTE]
> The **2024 Scheme** explicitly groups etching and substrate removal as a single Module 3 outcome, emphasizing the *process–microstructure–geometry* linkage expected in KTU ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Etching Processes

Etching processes are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: the *phase of the etchant* and the *mechanism of material removal*.

### 2.1.1 By Phase of Etchant

**A. Wet Chemical Etching**
- Liquid-phase chemical reaction
- **Isotropic** in most cases (single-crystal Si with KOH/TMAH is an exception — see orientation-dependent etching)
- High selectivity, low cost, simple equipment
- Examples: HF for SiO₂, KOH/TMAH/EDP for Si, H₃PO₄ for Al, Aqua Regia for Au

**B. Dry Etching (Plasma / Vapor-Phase)**
- Gas-phase chemistry assisted by plasma or ion bombardment
- Generally **anisotropic**
- High resolution, sub-100 nm capability
- Sub-categories: Plasma Etching, Reactive Ion Etching (RIE), Deep RIE (DRIE), Ion Beam Etching (IBE), Atomic Layer Etching (ALE)

### 2.1.2 By Mechanism

| Mechanism | Description | Example Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical (Pure)** | Reaction only, no physical component | Wet KOH etching, vapor HF |
| **Physical (Pure)** | Momentum transfer by ions | Sputter etching, IBE |
| **Chemo-Physical (Ion-Enhanced Chemical)** | Both — synergy | RIE, DRIE, ICP-RIE |

---

## 2.2 Wet Etching — Theory

Wet etching proceeds in **three sequential transport steps**:

1. **Diffusion** of etchant species to the wafer surface (through the boundary layer).
2. **Surface reaction** between etchant and material (governed by Arrhenius kinetics).
3. **Diffusion** of reaction products away from the surface.

The overall etch rate is determined by the **slowest of the three steps**. This is captured elegantly by a first-order kinetic model.

### Step-by-Step Wet Etch Model
- **Why three steps?** Because no single mechanism can dominate — a stagnant boundary layer, slow chemistry, or product poisoning each cap the rate.
- **How does temperature affect this?** Surface reaction rate follows Arrhenius: **doubling of rate for every ≈ 10 °C rise** (typical activation energy Eₐ ≈ 0.5 eV for Si-KOH).

> [!TIP]
> **Orientation-Dependent Etching (ODE) of Single-Crystal Si** is a *favorite KTU 14-mark topic*. Etch rates follow: **{110} > {100} > {111}**, with the {111} plane being ~100× slower due to higher atomic density. This produces the iconic **54.74° sloped sidewalls** on (100) wafers.

---

## 2.3 Dry Etching — Theory

### 2.3.1 Plasma Generation
- RF (13.56 MHz) electric field ionizes a low-pressure gas (e.g., CF₄, SF₆, Cl₂, BCl₃, O₂, Ar).
- Free electrons gain kinetic energy → collide with gas molecules → produce **ions, radicals, electrons, photons**.
- **Radicals** = neutral reactive species (e.g., F•) responsible for **chemical** etching.
- **Ions** (e.g., CF₃⁺, Ar⁺) accelerated toward the wafer for **physical** sputtering.

### 2.3.2 Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)
- Wafer sits on the **powered (cathode) electrode** → experiences high DC self-bias (–200 to –1000 V).
- Ions bombard vertically → directional etching.
- Radical chemistry supplies lateral removal → balances selectivity.

### 2.3.3 Deep RIE (DRIE) — Bosch Process
Alternating **two half-cycles** in a single chamber:
- **Step 1 (Etch):** SF₆ plasma → isotropic Si etching (sideways + down).
- **Step 2 (Passivation):** C₄F₈ plasma → deposits a Teflon-like fluorocarbon polymer on all exposed surfaces (sidewalls + floor).
- **Step 3 (Etch again):** SF₆ ions preferentially remove the *horizontal* polymer (ion bombardment is anisotropic), re-exposing the Si floor, but leaving the *sidewall* polymer intact.
- Repeating this cycle **n** times produces the characteristic **scalloped sidewall** profile.

### 2.3.4 Atomic Layer Etching (ALE)
- Two self-limiting half-cycles, like ALD but in reverse.
- **Cycle A:** Chemisorb reactive precursor (e.g., Cl for Si) → forms modified surface layer (~1 monolayer).
- **Cycle B:** Low-energy Ar⁺ ions or thermal activation desorb the modified layer.
- Sub-nm precision; **etch per cycle (EPC)** can be < 0.1 nm.

### 2.3.5 Ion Beam Etching (IBE) & Sputter Etching
- Inert gas ions (Ar⁺) from a Kaufman ion source bombard the target.
- Purely physical momentum transfer → low selectivity, no chemical specificity.
- Useful for noble metals, alloys, magnetic materials.

---

## 2.4 Substrate Removal & Bulk Micromachining

### 2.4.1 Frontside vs Backside
- **Frontside Bulk Micromachining:** Etch stops on a buried layer (e.g., p⁺ etch stop in KOH).
- **Backside Bulk Micromachining:** Etch from the wafer backside through the entire thickness, stopping on a membrane layer (e.g., SiO₂, Si₃N₄, p++ boron layer).

### 2.4.2 Common Release Methods
- **Isotropic Si release:** XeF₂ (vapor-phase, room temperature, highly selective to Si over photoresist, Al, SiO₂).
- **Anisotropic release:** TMAH + SiO₂ mask.
- **HF release of SiO₂:** Sacrificial oxide dissolved in HF; critical for surface-micromachined MEMS.
- **Dry release:** SF₆ / O₂ plasma for stiction-free release of high-aspect-ratio structures.

### 2.4.3 Stiction — The MEMS Nemesis
- **Release stiction:** Suspended structures adhere to the substrate after wet release due to capillary forces during drying.
- **Solution 1:** **Critical-point drying (CPD)** using liquid CO₂.
- **Solution 2:** **HF vapor-phase release** (no liquid).
- **Solution 3:** **Dimple structures** to reduce contact area.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for Etching & Substrate Removal. **Memorize the boxed relations.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Etch Rate (ER)** | $ER \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$ | nm/min or µm/min | $ \Delta h$ = etched depth; $ \Delta t$ = time |
| 2 | **Selectivity (S)** | $S \;=\; \dfrac{ER_{\text{target}}}{ER_{\text{mask}}}$ | dimensionless | $S \geq 10$ is typical; $S \geq 50$ for DRIE |
| 3 | **Anisotropy (A)** | $A \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{ER_{\text{lat}}}{ER_{\text{vert}}}$ | dimensionless | $A = 1 \Rightarrow$ perfectly vertical; $A = 0 \Rightarrow$ isotropic |
| 4 | **Undercut (U)** | $U \;=\; ER_{\text{lat}} \times t$ | nm or µm | Lateral mask recession |
| 5 | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $AR \;=\; \dfrac{D}{W}$ | dimensionless | $D$ = depth, $W$ = width |
| 6 | **Arrhenius Etch Rate** | $ER \;=\; A \, e^{-E_a / k_B T}$ | nm/min | $E_a$ = activation energy; $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K |
| 7 | **Loading Effect (Area)** | $ER_{\text{local}} \;=\; \dfrac{ER_{0}}{1 + k \cdot \theta_{A}}$ | nm/min | $\theta_{A}$ = fractional exposed area |
| 8 | **First-Order Transport-Controlled Rate** | $\dfrac{1}{ER} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{k_s} + \dfrac{1}{k_m}$ | min/nm | $k_s$ surface reaction; $k_m$ mass-transport |
| 9 | **Bosch Scallop Depth** | $d_{\text{scallop}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{R_{\text{etch}}}{f_{\text{cycle}}}$ | nm/cycle | $f$ = cycle frequency |
| 10 | **ALE Etch per Cycle** | $EPC \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta h}{N}$ | nm/cycle | $N$ = number of cycles |
| 11 | **Mask CD Loss** | $\Delta CD \;=\; 2 \, U$ | nm | Undercut counted on **both** sides |
| 12 | **Vertical Wall Angle (ODE)** | $\theta \;=\; 54.74^{\circ}$ | degrees | For (100) Si etched in KOH, bounded by {111} planes |
| 13 | **Boron Etch Stop Concentration** | $C_B \;\geq\; 5 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³ | atoms/cm³ | Heavily doped p⁺ Si stops KOH/TMAH/EDP |
| 14 | **Knudsen Number (Plasma Etching)** | $K_n \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{mfp}}}{L}$ | dimensionless | $K_n \ll 1$ continuum; $K_n \gg 1$ free-molecular |
| 15 | **Sputter Yield (Physical Etching)** | $Y_s \;=\; \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | atoms/ion | Function of ion energy, mass, angle, target mass |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Industry:** Plasma etching of high-NA EUV resist layers demands ALE-like control to avoid line-edge-roughness (LER) below 1 nm.
- **MEMS Accelerometers (e.g., ADXL, LIS3DH):** DRIE defines the proof-mass and comb fingers; **stiction-free dry release** is non-negotiable.
- **Microfluidics:** Glass etching using HF/HNO₃ yields optically transparent channels for lab-on-chip diagnostics.
- **Power Electronics:** Trench etching with AR > 30:1 enables super-junction MOSFETs and SiC/IGBT devices.
- **Quantum & Photonic Chips:** ALE of AlGaAs/GaAs heterostructures defines single-photon sources and photonic crystal cavities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Anisotropy from Etch Rate Components

**Problem Setup:** A vertical etch produces depth $d_v$ in time $t$. Simultaneously, the sidewall recedes laterally by $d_l$. Define the anisotropy $A$.

**Step 1 — Define Vertical Etch Rate**

$$ ER_v \;=\; \dfrac{d_v}{t} $$

**Step 2 — Define Lateral Etch Rate**

$$ ER_l \;=\; \dfrac{d_l}{t} $$

**Step 3 — Form the Anisotropy Ratio**

The standard textbook definition expresses anisotropy in terms of *only the vertical and lateral rates*:

$$ A \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{ER_l}{ER_v} $$

**Step 4 — Boundary Case Analysis**

- If $ER_l = 0$ (no lateral etch), then $A = 1$ → **perfectly anisotropic (vertical wall).**
- If $ER_l = ER_v$ (uniform in all directions), then $A = 0$ → **perfectly isotropic (spherical cavity).**

**Step 5 — Rewriting in terms of Geometry**

Since the undercut $U = ER_l \cdot t$ and the depth $D = ER_v \cdot t$:

$$ A \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{U}{D} $$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** When asked "what is the undercut after 30 min if the etch rate is 0.5 µm/min and the anisotropy is 0.9?", use $U = D (1 - A) = 0.5 \times 30 \times 0.1 = 1.5$ µm. Marks credited only when the *defining equation* is stated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selectivity for a Multi-Layer Stack

**Problem Setup:** A mask of thickness $t_m$ must survive while a target layer of thickness $t_t$ is etched through. Given $ER_t$ and $ER_m$, find the minimum selectivity.

**Step 1 — Time to Etch Through Target**

$$ t_{\text{etch}} \;=\; \dfrac{t_t}{ER_t} $$

**Step 2 — Mask Consumed in That Time**

$$ \Delta t_m \;=\; ER_m \cdot t_{\text{etch}} \;=\; \dfrac{ER_m \cdot t_t}{ER_t} $$

**Step 3 — Survival Condition**

We require $\Delta t_m \leq t_m$:

$$ \dfrac{ER_m \cdot t_t}{ER_t} \;\leq\; t_m \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{ER_t}{ER_m} \;\geq\; \dfrac{t_t}{t_m} $$

**Step 4 — Hence, Required Selectivity**

$$ \boxed{\, S_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{t_t}{t_m} \,} $$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**

Etch 2 µm polysilicon with a 0.5 µm SiO₂ hard mask:

$$ S_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0}{0.5} \;=\; 4 $$

If the process recipe delivers $S = 30$ (CF₄/H₂ RIE), the mask survives with a safety factor of $30 / 4 = 7.5\times$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Wet Etch Rate

**Problem Setup:** The etch rate of (100) Si in 25 wt% TMAH at 70 °C is 0.6 µm/min, and at 90 °C is 1.8 µm/min. Find the activation energy $E_a$.

**Step 1 — Arrhenius Equation**

$$ ER(T) \;=\; A_0 \, e^{-E_a / k_B T} $$

**Step 2 — Take the Ratio of Two Temperatures**

$$ \dfrac{ER_2}{ER_1} \;=\; e^{-(E_a / k_B) \left( \tfrac{1}{T_2} - \tfrac{1}{T_1} \right)} $$

**Step 3 — Take Natural Logarithm**

$$ \ln\!\left( \dfrac{ER_2}{ER_1} \right) \;=\; -\dfrac{E_a}{k_B} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right) $$

**Step 4 — Solve for Eₐ**

$$ E_a \;=\; k_B \, \dfrac{\ln(ER_2 / ER_1)}{\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}} $$

**Step 5 — Numerical Substitution**

- $T_1 = 343.15$ K; $T_2 = 363.15$ K
- $ER_2 / ER_1 = 1.8 / 0.6 = 3.0$
- $1/T_1 - 1/T_2 = 1/343.15 - 1/363.15 = 1.6085 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹
- $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K

$$ E_a \;=\; 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{\ln(3.0)}{1.6085 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ E_a \;=\; 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{1.0986}{1.6085 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ \boxed{\, E_a \;\approx\; 0.588 \; \text{eV} \;\approx\; 56.7 \; \text{kJ/mol} \,} $$

This is consistent with the *diffusion-limited* regime in TMAH etching of Si (literature range: 0.5–0.7 eV).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bosch Scallop Amplitude vs Cycle Parameters

**Problem Setup:** In a DRIE Bosch process, the etch step lasts $t_e$ and produces an instantaneous isotropic etch depth $d_e = ER_{\text{iso}} \cdot t_e$. The passivation step of duration $t_p$ deposits a polymer thickness $p$. The scallop amplitude $S$ at the sidewall is half the lateral etch during one cycle.

**Step 1 — Lateral Etch Per Cycle**

The lateral etch during the SF₆ step, in the absence of ion shielding, equals $d_e$ (isotropic).

**Step 2 — Net Lateral Etch (Sidewall Polymer Effective Suppression)**

With strong vertical ion bombardment, the sidewall retains most of the polymer. The effective lateral etch is reduced by a *retention factor* $r$ (typically 0.05–0.15):

$$ S \;=\; r \cdot d_e \;=\; r \cdot ER_{\text{iso}} \cdot t_e $$

**Step 3 — Total Etch Depth After N Cycles**

$$ D_{\text{total}} \;=\; N \cdot d_e \cdot \cos\theta_v $$

where $\theta_v$ is the deviation of the sidewall from vertical (≈ 88–90°).

**Step 4 — Aspect Ratio of Final Trench**

$$ AR \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{total}}}{W} $$

**Step 5 — Worked Example**

Suppose $t_e = 5$ s, $ER_{\text{iso}} = 3$ µm/min, $r = 0.1$, $W = 5$ µm, $N = 200$ cycles:

- $d_e = 3 \times (5/60) = 0.25$ µm
- $S = 0.1 \times 0.25 = 0.025$ µm = 25 nm (sidewall roughness)
- $D_{\text{total}} = 200 \times 0.25 = 50$ µm
- $AR = 50 / 5 = 10$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection

| Material | Recommended Etch | Etchant / Gas | Selectivity | Profile | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Si (amorphous) | RIE | SF₆ / O₂ | High vs SiO₂ | Anisotropic | MEMS release |
| Si (single-crystal) | Wet (KOH/TMAH) | KOH 30%, TMAH 25% | Infinite vs SiO₂ | Anisotropic (54.74° walls) | Bulk micromachining |
| Si (deep, vertical) | DRIE (Bosch) | SF₆ / C₄F₈ | > 50:1 vs SiO₂ | Anisotropic, scalloped | High-AR trenches |
| SiO₂ | RIE / wet | CHF₃ / Ar, BOE, HF | High vs Si | Anisotropic (RIE) / Iso (wet) | Dielectric patterning |
| Si₃N₄ | RIE / wet-hot | CF₄ / O₂, H₃PO₄ 180 °C | High vs SiO₂ | Anisotropic (RIE) | Mask, membrane |
| Al | Wet / RIE | H₃PO₄/HNO₃, Cl₂/BCl₃ | Modest | Isotropic (wet) / Anis (RIE) | IC metallization |
| Cu | Wet | HCl/H₂O₂ or FE-3.0 | Good | Isotropic | Damascene clean |
| Au | Wet (Aqua Regia) / IBE | HCl:HNO₃, Ar⁺ | High | Isotropic / Anisotropic | Bonding pads |
| GaAs | RIE | SiCl₄ / Ar, BCl₃ | High vs oxide | Anisotropic | Optoelectronics |
| Polymers (SU-8, PMMA) | RIE / O₂ plasma | O₂ / CF₄ | High vs Si | Anisotropic / isotropic | Microfluidic molds |
| 2D Materials (MoS₂, hBN) | ALE | Cl₂ + Ar⁺ low-energy | Excellent | Atomic-layer precise | Nanoelectronics |

---

## 3.6 KTU Numerical Problem — Standard Pattern

> **Sample 14-mark KTU numerical** (paraphrased from typical ESE pattern):

A 525 µm thick (100) Si wafer is etched in 30 wt% KOH at 80 °C through a 1 µm SiO₂ mask patterned with 100 µm × 100 µm square openings. The (100) etch rate is 1.4 µm/min, and the SiO₂ etch rate is 28 nm/min. (a) Will the SiO₂ mask survive the full etch? (b) What is the etch depth after 6 hours? (c) If the wafer is backside-etched to produce a 20 µm thick membrane, what is the remaining time needed after reaching the membrane?

**Solution outline for students:**

1. Time for SiO₂ to be fully consumed: $t_{m} = 1\,\mu m / 0.028\,\mu m\cdot min^{-1} = 35.7$ min.
2. Etch depth after 6 h = $1.4 \times 360 = 504$ µm — mask is long gone.
3. To reach 20 µm membrane: $t = 505/1.4 = 360.7$ min ≈ 6.01 h.

**Hence the design must use a thicker hard mask, an etch stop, or DRIE.**

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`**

### Q1. Define **etch selectivity** and **anisotropy**. Why are both critical in MEMS fabrication? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

- **Etch Selectivity (S):** Ratio of the etch rate of the target material to the etch rate of the mask (or underlying layer) being preserved:
$$ S \;=\; \dfrac{ER_{\text{target}}}{ER_{\text{mask}}} $$
- **Anisotropy (A):** Quantifies directionality of etching:
$$ A \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}} $$
- **Importance:** Selectivity prevents the mask from being consumed prematurely, enabling deep or long-duration etches. Anisotropy controls sidewall verticality and critical-dimension fidelity. Both together determine whether a fabricated structure meets design tolerances (e.g., comb-finger gaps, beam widths).

---

### Q2. Differentiate between **wet etching** and **dry (plasma) etching** with two key points. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wet Etching | Dry (Plasma) Etching |
|---|---|---|
| **Profile** | Mostly isotropic (except KOH/TMAH on Si) | Anisotropic, directional |
| **Selectivity** | Very high (chemical specificity) | Moderate, depends on chemistry |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low (beaker + heater) | High (vacuum, RF source, gas handling) |
| **CD Control** | Poor due to undercut | Excellent, sub-100 nm possible |
| **Waste Handling** | Acid/base disposal needed | Gases scrubbed; cleaner |
| **Particle Sensitivity** | Less critical | Very critical — particles cause micro-masking |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic and process description, explain the **Bosch process** for Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) of silicon. Why does it produce high aspect-ratio features with vertical sidewalls? Mention the role of scalloping.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon wafer is patterned with 2 µm wide trenches and etched using DRIE at a net vertical etch rate of 2 µm/min. The scallop amplitude is 50 nm. The mask is 1 µm SiO₂. After 60 minutes, (i) what is the trench depth, (ii) what is the aspect ratio, (iii) will the SiO₂ mask survive if the selectivity is 75:1?

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Bosch Process Definition (1 mark):** An alternating, two-step plasma process carried out in a single ICP/RIE chamber to etch silicon anisotropically with high aspect ratio.
2. **Step 1 — Etching (2 marks):** SF₆ gas ionized → F• radicals isotropically etch exposed Si. Simultaneously, ion bombardment enhances vertical etch on the trench floor.
3. **Step 2 — Passivation (2 marks):** C₄F₈ gas → dissociates into CF₂ radicals → deposit a fluorocarbon polymer (~tens of nm) on all exposed surfaces (floor + sidewalls).
4. **Step 3 — Selective Removal (1 mark):** Next SF₆ step: ions bombard the *horizontal* floor and sputter off polymer; sidewall polymer is preserved because ions are directional.
5. **Result (1 mark):** Net effect — floor advances downward, sidewalls protected, producing vertical walls. Repeated N times yields N scallops on the sidewall.

**Model Solution (b):**

(i) Trench depth:

$$ D \;=\; ER_{\text{vert}} \times t \;=\; 2 \; \mu\text{m/min} \times 60 \; \text{min} \;=\; 120 \; \mu\text{m} $$

(ii) Aspect ratio:

$$ AR \;=\; \dfrac{D}{W} \;=\; \dfrac{120}{2} \;=\; 60 $$

(iii) Mask survival check:

- Silicon etched in 60 min = 120 µm.
- Selectivity = 75:1 → SiO₂ etched = $120/75 = 1.6$ µm.
- Mask thickness = 1 µm.
- Since 1.6 µm > 1 µm, **the mask will be fully consumed** (and in fact, the underlying layer will start etching 0.6 µm deep).
- **Recommendation:** Either use a thicker mask, a higher selectivity chemistry, or a hard mask (e.g., Al, Cr, or Si₃N₄).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **wet anisotropic etching of (100) silicon** in KOH. Why is the etch rate of (111) plane negligible compared to (100)? How is the **54.74° sidewall slope** obtained? Mention the role of **boron etch stop** in bulk micromachining.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For (100) Si etched in 25 wt% TMAH at 70 °C, the etch rates are $ER_{100} = 0.6$ µm/min and $ER_{111} = 0.006$ µm/min. A 50 µm × 50 µm square opening aligned to the <110> direction is patterned on a 525 µm thick wafer. (i) Determine the sidewall angle, (ii) after 8 hours, will the etch reach the wafer backside, and (iii) if the wafer is replaced by a p⁺ wafer (B = 7 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³), what happens?

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **KOH chemistry (2 marks):** KOH + H₂O → OH⁻ + K⁺; OH⁻ attacks Si:
$$ \text{Si} + 2\,\text{OH}^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SiO}_2(\text{OH})_{2}^{2-} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \uparrow $$
2. **Anisotropy reason (2 marks):** (111) planes have the highest atomic packing density and 1 dangling bond per atom (vs 2 for (100)). The (100) surface has 2 back-bonds per surface atom → easier chemical attack → faster etch.
3. **Sidewall angle (1 mark):** $\tan\theta = ER_{100}/ER_{111} \Rightarrow$ intersection of {111} planes exposed by (100) etching → $\theta = 54.74°$ from horizontal.
4. **Boron etch stop (2 marks):** Heavily boron-doped Si (> 5 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³) becomes a *p⁺* degenerate semiconductor with lattice strain that passivates the surface. KOH, TMAH, and EDP all stop on it. Used to form thin membranes and cantilevers.

**Model Solution (b):**

(i) Sidewall angle:

$$ \theta \;=\; \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{ER_{100}}{ER_{111}} \right) \;=\; \arctan(100) \;\approx\; 89.43^{\circ} $$

In a perfectly aligned opening on a (100) wafer, the sidewall meets the surface at exactly 54.74° from the wafer plane (this is the angle between (100) and (111) planes; the ratio of rates is technically infinite for perfect crystals, but in practice the 54.74° arises from crystallography, not rate ratio).

(ii) Etch depth after 8 h:

$$ D \;=\; 0.6 \; \mu\text{m/min} \times 480 \; \text{min} \;=\; 288 \; \mu\text{m} $$

Since 288 µm < 525 µm, **the backside is NOT reached** — a 237 µm-thick unetched Si layer remains.

(iii) p⁺ wafer (B = 7 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³ > 5 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³): **etch stop activates.** The etch self-terminates after penetrating the lightly-doped region, leaving a thin p⁺ membrane.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Etching & Substrate Removal:**
> 1. **Forgetting the defining equation for selectivity or anisotropy** — always write $S = ER_t/ER_m$ and $A = 1 - ER_l/ER_v$ before plugging in numbers. Marks are awarded for the *formulation*, not just the final number.
> 2. **Confusing RIE with DRIE:** RIE = continuous process; DRIE = pulsed Bosch cycles. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Mixing KOH and TMAH properties:** KOH is faster but CMOS-incompatible (K⁺ contamination); TMAH is CMOS-compatible but slower. Many students lose 1 mark for not specifying the application context.
> 4. **Forgetting scalloping in DRIE** — quoting vertical walls as "perfectly smooth" is wrong; mention the periodic scallop amplitude formula.
> 5. **Stiction in release etch** — must be addressed in any "MEMS release" question for full marks.
> 6. **ALE vs ALD confusion:** ALE removes material in self-limiting half-cycles; ALD deposits. Examiners trick students by mixing them up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

- [x] **Etching** = selective material removal via chemical, physical, or chemo-physical means.
- [x] **Substrate removal** = bulk micromachining (frontside or backside) + release etch.
- [x] **Wet etching** is mostly isotropic, cheap, high selectivity, and can be orientation-dependent on single-crystal Si.
- [x] **Dry etching** (RIE, DRIE, ICP, ALE) is anisotropic, high resolution, and plasma-driven.
- [x] **Bosch DRIE** alternates SF₆ (etch) and C₄F₈ (passivation) → scalloped, high-AR sidewalls.
- [x] **ALE** is the *most precise* etch technique — atomic-layer control.
- [x] **Selectivity** = $ER_t / ER_m$; required $S \geq t_t/t_m$.
- [x] **Anisotropy** = $1 - ER_l/ER_v$; $A=1$ vertical, $A=0$ isotropic.
- [x] **(100) Si in KOH/TMAH** → 54.74° sidewalls due to {111} etch stop.
- [x] **Boron etch stop** at $C_B \geq 5 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³.
- [x] **XeF₂** = vapor-phase isotropic Si release etch — stiction-free, room temperature.
- [x] **Stiction mitigation:** critical-point drying, dimples, vapor-phase release, freeze-drying.
- [x] **Mask materials:** photoresist (low AR), SiO₂/Si₃N₄ (hard mask, mid AR), Al/Cr (high AR).
- [x] **Plasma parameters:** pressure, RF power, gas flow ratio, DC bias, temperature.
- [x] **Loading effect:** higher exposed area → lower local etch rate (consumption of etchant).
- [x] **ALE cycles:** chemisorption + activation = self-limiting → sub-nm precision.
- [x] **DRIE scallop amplitude** ≈ $r \cdot ER_{\text{iso}} \cdot t_e$ (typically 10–100 nm).
- [x] **KEEP THE FOLLOWING READY FOR THEORY:** Bosch Process steps, ALE cycle, RIE vs DRIE vs ALE, etch-stop mechanisms, stiction causes & cures, KOH anisotropy origin, selectivity formula derivation, Arrhenius etch rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always link the *etch mechanism* to the *application outcome* in long answers. For example, "RIE is preferred over wet etching in sub-micron IC gate patterning *because* its anisotropy preserves CD whereas wet etching's isotropic profile causes unacceptable undercut." This is exactly how a **7-marker** answer becomes a **full-marker** answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 3 — MEMS FABRICATION TECHNIQUES
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMS Fabrication Techniques** constitute the specialized set of micro-manufacturing processes used to design, pattern, and assemble mechanical and electromechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronic components on a common silicon substrate at the micrometer and sub-micrometer scale. These techniques are a synergistic integration of **lithography, thin-film deposition, etching (wet and dry), doping, oxidation, and wafer bonding** technologies that have been adapted from the semiconductor (CMOS) industry to manufacture three-dimensional microstructures with moving parts.

The acronym **MEMS** stands for **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems** — systems that combine electrical and mechanical functionality at micron-level dimensions, typically ranging from **1 µm to 1000 µm (1 mm)** in feature size.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine building a complete, fully functional city (with skyscrapers, bridges, tunnels, and moving cars) on a tiny LEGO plate no bigger than your fingernail. Now imagine doing this using nothing but light (to "draw" the city plan), chemical baths (to "wash away" unwanted bricks), and thin-layer spraying (to "paint" new buildings). That is essentially what MEMS fabrication does — but instead of LEGO, it uses silicon wafers; instead of light, it uses ultraviolet rays or electron beams; and instead of chemical baths, it uses highly controlled acid solutions or plasma gases to sculpt microscopic machines that can sense, move, and compute.

### 1.3 Why MEMS Fabrication is a Distinct Engineering Discipline

MEMS manufacturing borrows heavily from **integrated circuit (IC) fabrication**, but adds critical 3D shaping capabilities:

1. **Depth control** — ICs are essentially 2D (planar); MEMS need true 3D geometries (beams, membranes, channels, gears).
2. **Material diversity** — ICs use primarily silicon, polysilicon, and metals; MEMS adds glass, polymers (SU-8, PDMS), ceramics, and piezoelectrics.
3. **Mechanical integrity** — MEMS structures must withstand residual stress, stiction, fatigue, and packaging-induced loads.

### 1.4 Core Categories of MEMS Fabrication

| S. No. | Process Family | Principal Function | Typical Feature Size |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | **Lithography** | Pattern transfer onto substrate | **0.1 µm – 100 µm** |
| 2 | **Thin-Film Deposition** | Material layering | **10 nm – 100 µm** |
| 3 | **Etching (Wet/Dry)** | Material removal | **0.1 µm – 500 µm** |
| 4 | **Doping / Ion Implantation** | Modify electrical/mechanical properties | **Surface to 1 µm** |
| 5 | **Oxidation** | Insulating SiO₂ layer formation | **10 nm – 2 µm** |
| 6 | **Wafer Bonding** | Substrate assembly | **Full wafer** |
| 7 | **LIGA / X-Ray** | High-aspect-ratio 3D structures | **Aspect ratio > 100** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** A "4-inch" or "6-inch" silicon wafer is the universal MEMS substrate. Modern fabs use **200 mm (8-inch)** or **300 mm (12-inch)** wafers, with **wafer thickness ≈ 525 µm (for 200 mm)** and **775 µm (for 300 mm)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of MEMS Fabrication Routes

MEMS fabrication is broadly classified into three main routes, each with sub-categories:

### 2.1.1 Bulk Micromachining

This is the **oldest and most widely used** MEMS technique. It involves the **selective removal of significant portions of the silicon substrate (bulk)** to create structures such as membranes, beams, cavities, and channels.

**Process Flow (Generic):**
1. Start with a single-crystal silicon wafer (typically 100 or 111 oriented).
2. Grow or deposit a masking layer (SiO₂ or Si₃N₄).
3. Pattern the mask using photolithography.
4. Etch the exposed silicon using **wet chemical etchants** (KOH, TMAH, EDP) or **dry plasma** (DRIE).
5. The remaining silicon forms the mechanical structure.

**Wet Etchants Used in Bulk Micromachining:**

| Etchant | Chemical Formula | Etch Rate (Si) | Selectivity (Si : SiO₂) | Anisotropy | CMOS Compatible? |
|---------|------------------|----------------|--------------------------|------------|-----------------|
| **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** | KOH + H₂O | **~1 µm/min @ 80°C** | **400 : 1** | High (54.74° for 100-Si) | **No** (K⁺ contamination) |
| **TMAH (Tetramethyl Ammonium Hydroxide)** | (CH₃)₄NOH | **~0.5 µm/min @ 85°C** | **1000 : 1** | High | **Yes** |
| **EDP (Ethylenediamine Pyrocatechol)** | C₂H₈N₂ + C₆H₄(OH)₂ | **~0.7 µm/min @ 110°C** | **5000 : 1** | Very high | **No** |
| **CsOH (Cesium Hydroxide)** | CsOH + H₂O | **~1 µm/min** | **200 : 1** | High | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Crystallographic Principle:** Wet anisotropic etching of (100) silicon produces **54.74° sidewalls** because these are the slowest-etching {111} planes. The undercut is therefore geometrically predictable and forms the basis of V-grooves, pyramids, and suspended membranes.

### 2.1.2 Surface Micromachining

Unlike bulk machining, surface micromachining **builds structures on top of the substrate** by sequentially depositing and selectively etching thin films. The structural layer is **sacrificially released** to leave a free-standing micromechanical element.

**The "Sacrificial Layer" Concept:**
- A **structural layer** (e.g., polysilicon) is deposited over a **sacrificial layer** (e.g., SiO₂).
- The sacrificial layer is later dissolved (typically in **HF — hydrofluoric acid**), leaving the structural layer free to move.

**Process Sequence:**
1. Deposit sacrificial layer (e.g., PSG — Phosphosilicate Glass, 1–2 µm thick).
2. Pattern sacrificial layer.
3. Deposit structural layer (e.g., LPCVD polysilicon, 1–5 µm thick).
4. Pattern structural layer.
5. **Release etch** — dissolve sacrificial layer in HF to free the structure.
6. Critical-point dry to prevent **stiction** (sticking).

> [!WARNING]
> **Stiction Problem:** When rinsing a released microstructure, surface tension of the rinse liquid can pull the structure into permanent contact with the substrate. The industry standard remedy is **Critical Point Drying (CPD)** using liquid CO₂, where the liquid is transitioned past its critical point (T_c = 31 °C, P_c = 7.4 MPa) so that there is no liquid–gas interface to exert capillary forces.

### 2.1.3 LIGA (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung)

LIGA is a German acronym for **Lithography + Electroplating + Molding** — a high-aspect-ratio MEMS process capable of producing structures with **heights of several hundred micrometers and aspect ratios > 100**.

**Steps:**
1. Coat a substrate with a thick **PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate)** resist (up to several mm).
2. Expose with **synchrotron X-rays** through a patterned mask.
3. Develop the exposed PMMA to create a high-aspect-ratio mold.
4. Electroplate the mold with metal (Ni, Cu, Au).
5. Remove the PMMA to leave a free-standing metal structure (or use it as a mold for plastic replication).

**LIGA Variants:**
- **UV-LIGA** — uses SU-8 photoresist and standard UV exposure (cheaper, but lower aspect ratio ~ 20).
- **X-ray LIGA** — true LIGA using synchrotron (expensive, but aspect ratio > 100).

## 2.2 Lithography (Pattern Definition)

Lithography is the **first step** in nearly every MEMS process — it defines the geometry of the final device.

| Lithography Type | Wavelength | Resolution | Application |
|------------------|------------|------------|-------------|
| **Optical (UV) Photolithography** | 365 – 436 nm | **~0.5 µm** | Standard MEMS/CMOS |
| **Deep UV (DUV)** | 193 – 248 nm | **~0.13 µm** | Advanced CMOS |
| **Electron Beam (E-beam)** | 0.005 – 0.05 nm (de Broglie) | **< 10 nm** | Masks, R&D |
| **X-ray (Synchrotron)** | 0.1 – 1 nm | **< 100 nm** | LIGA |
| **Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL)** | Mechanical | **< 10 nm** | High-throughput nano |

**Resolution Limit (Rayleigh Criterion):**

$$R = \frac{k_1 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

Where:
- $R$ = minimum resolvable feature size
- $k_1$ = process-dependent factor (typically 0.4 – 0.8)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of exposure light
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the optical system

## 2.3 Etching — Wet vs. Dry

### Wet Chemical Etching
- Uses liquid chemicals (acids, bases).
- **Isotropic** (etches equally in all directions) for most etchants, e.g., **HNA** (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH) for silicon.
- **Anisotropic** for alkaline etchants on single-crystal Si (KOH, TMAH, EDP).

### Dry (Plasma) Etching
- Uses ionized gas (plasma) to remove material.
- Includes **RIE (Reactive Ion Etching)**, **DRIE (Deep RIE)**, and **Ion Beam Etching (IBE)**.
- **DRIE** uses the **Bosch process** — alternating cycles of SF₆ (etch) and C₄F₈ (passivation) to achieve **aspect ratios > 50:1** with near-vertical sidewalls.

## 2.4 Deposition Techniques

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Materials | Conformality |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Thermal Oxidation** | High-T Si + O₂ → SiO₂ | SiO₂ | Excellent |
| **LPCVD (Low-Pressure CVD)** | Gas-phase reaction at low P | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄, SiO₂ | Excellent |
| **PECVD (Plasma-Enhanced CVD)** | Plasma-assisted at low T | SiO₂, Si₃N₄, a-Si | Good |
| **Sputtering (PVD)** | Ar⁺ bombardment of target | Al, Ti, Au, Mo | Moderate |
| **Evaporation (PVD)** | Thermal/e-beam heating of source | Al, Au, Cr | Poor (line-of-sight) |
| **Electroplating** | Electrochemical reduction | Cu, Ni, Au | Excellent (mold-fill) |

## 2.5 Wafer Bonding

Used to **join two or more wafers** to form complex 3D structures or hermetically sealed packages.

| Bonding Type | Temperature | Materials Joined | Strength |
|--------------|-------------|------------------|----------|
| **Anodic Bonding** | **300 – 450 °C** | Glass + Si | Very High |
| **Fusion Bonding** | **800 – 1100 °C** | Si + Si, Si + SiO₂ | Very High |
| **Eutectic Bonding** | **363 °C (Au-Si)** | Si + Metal | High |
| **Adhesive Bonding** | **< 200 °C** | Any + Any (with polymer) | Moderate |
| **Direct (Plasma-Activated) Bonding** | **Room T – 400 °C** | Si + Si, glass + glass | High |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains ALL high-yield formulas for MEMS fabrication numerical problems.**

| Parameter | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|-----------|----------|----------------|-------|
| **Etch Rate** | $$R_{etch} = \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta t}$$ | $\Delta z$ = depth removed, $\Delta t$ = time | µm/min |
| **Selectivity** | $$S = \frac{R_{material}}{R_{mask}}$$ | Ratio of etch rates | dimensionless |
| **Anisotropy Factor** | $$A_f = 1 - \frac{R_{lateral}}{R_{vertical}}$$ | $R_{lat}$: lateral, $R_{vert}$: vertical | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Etch Depth (V-groove on 100-Si)** | $$d = \frac{w_0}{\sqrt{2}}$$ | $w_0$ = mask opening width | µm |
| **Rayleigh Resolution** | $$R = \frac{k_1 \lambda}{NA}$$ | $k_1$ = factor, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $NA$ = numerical aperture | nm |
| **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | $$DOF = \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$ | Optical lithography parameter | µm |
| **Mean Free Path (vacuum)** | $$\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$$ | $k_B$ = Boltzmann const., $d$ = molecular diameter, $P$ = pressure | m |
| **Knudsen Number** | $$Kn = \frac{\lambda_{mfp}}{L}$$ | $L$ = characteristic dimension | dimensionless |
| **Deposition Rate (evaporation)** | $$R_{dep} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$$ | $m$ = mass deposited, $\rho$ = density, $A$ = area, $t$ = time | µm/min |
| **Residual Stress (biaxial)** | $$\sigma = \frac{E}{1-\nu} \cdot \epsilon$$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $\epsilon$ = strain | Pa |
| **Stiction Force (capillary)** | $$F_{cap} = \frac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{d}$$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension, $A$ = contact area, $\theta$ = contact angle, $d$ = gap | N |
| **Oxide Growth (Deal-Grove)** | $$x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$$ | $x_0$ = oxide thickness, $A, B$ = rate constants, $\tau$ = initial time | µm², µm·h |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in:
> - **Industry fabs** — to design process recipes (etch time, deposition thickness).
> - **Failure analysis** — to predict stiction, residual stress, and yield losses.
> - **MEMS design** — to convert a desired 3D geometry into the required lithographic mask dimensions.
> - **Foundry services** — to quote turnaround time and cost to customers.
