# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Etch Depth vs. Mask Opening for (100) Silicon Anisotropic Etching

This is the most-asked KTU numerical on bulk micromachining.

**Problem Setup:**
A (100) silicon wafer is patterned with a rectangular opening of width $w_0$ (oriented along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction) and etched in KOH. Determine the etched depth $d$ at which the etch front becomes self-limiting due to the (111) planes meeting.

**Step 1 — Recall the Geometry of {111} Planes in (100) Silicon**

The (111) planes intersect the (100) surface at an angle $\theta$ given by:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{h_{100}}{\sqrt{h_{100}^2 + h_{111}^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356° \approx 54.74°$$

**Step 2 — Set Up the Geometric Relationship**

When KOH etches through the mask opening $w_0$ on a (100) surface, the {111} sidewalls form an angle of $54.74°$ with the horizontal. For a rectangular opening, two opposing {111} planes etch inward as the depth increases.

**Step 3 — Derive the Relationship**

Consider a cross-section perpendicular to the opening. The two sidewalls advance horizontally at a rate $R_{lat} = R_{vert} \cdot \cot(54.74°)$ for a given vertical etch. The opening closes when the two sidewalls meet, i.e., when the lateral undercut from both sides equals $w_0 / 2$ each.

For a rectangular mask opening of width $w_0$ and **infinite length** (or length $\gg w_0$):

\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertical etch depth} : d \\
\text{Lateral undercut from one side} : d \cdot \cot(54.74°) \\
\text{Total lateral undercut from both sides} : 2 \cdot d \cdot \cot(54.74°)
\end{aligned}

The etch self-stops when the two sidewalls meet:

$$w_0 = 2 \cdot d \cdot \cot(54.74°)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $\cot(54.74°)$**

$$\cot(54.74°) = \frac{1}{\tan(54.74°)} = \frac{1}{1.4142} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

\begin{aligned}
w_0 &= 2 \cdot d \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2d}{\sqrt{2}} = d\sqrt{2} \\
\therefore \quad d &= \frac{w_0}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}

**Conclusion:** For a long rectangular opening on (100) silicon, the maximum achievable etch depth is:

$$\boxed{d_{max} = \frac{w_0}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, w_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a square opening** (length = width = $w_0$), the etch forms a **pyramidal pit** with depth:

$$d_{pyramid} = \frac{w_0}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{(same as rectangular, if both } x \text{ and } y \text{ close at the same time)}$$

> If the opening is rectangular with length $L$ and width $w_0$ where $L > w_0\sqrt{2}$ and $L \gg w_0$, the **width direction closes first**, creating a V-groove of depth $w_0/\sqrt{2}$, while the bottom is still flat.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Dimension Control in Optical Lithography (Rayleigh Resolution)

**Problem:** A MEMS fab uses an i-line (365 nm) stepper with $NA = 0.55$ and $k_1 = 0.6$. Calculate the minimum printable line width.

**Step 1 — State the Rayleigh Equation**

$$R = \frac{k_1 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{0.6 \times 365 \text{ nm}}{0.55} \\
R &= \frac{219 \text{ nm}}{0.55} \\
R &= 398.18 \text{ nm} \\
R &\approx 0.398 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Interpret the Result**

A minimum line width of approximately **0.4 µm** is achievable. To go below this, the fab must either:
- Reduce $\lambda$ (use DUV 248 nm or EUV 13.5 nm),
- Increase $NA$ (use immersion lithography, $NA > 1$),
- Reduce $k_1$ (use phase-shift masks, off-axis illumination, or resolution enhancement techniques).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Selectivity Calculation for Si vs. SiO₂ in TMAH

**Problem:** TMAH etches Si at 0.5 µm/min and SiO₂ at 0.0005 µm/min. A wafer has a 1 µm thick SiO₂ mask. What is the maximum silicon etch depth before mask failure?

**Step 1 — Compute Selectivity**

\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{R_{Si}}{R_{SiO_2}} = \frac{0.5}{0.0005} = 1000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Determine Maximum Etch Time Before Mask Failure**

\begin{aligned}
t_{max} &= \frac{\text{Mask thickness}}{R_{SiO_2}} = \frac{1 \text{ µm}}{0.0005 \text{ µm/min}} = 2000 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Determine Maximum Silicon Depth**

\begin{aligned}
d_{Si, max} &= R_{Si} \times t_{max} = 0.5 \text{ µm/min} \times 2000 \text{ min} = 1000 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The SiO₂ mask is robust enough to etch **1000 µm** of silicon — far more than typical wafer thickness (~525 µm for 200 mm wafer). Therefore, the mask will **not fail** for a through-wafer etch.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for Common MEMS Devices

| Target Device | Preferred Fabrication | Reason |
|---------------|----------------------|--------|
| **Pressure Sensor Membrane** | Bulk micromachining (KOH backside etch) | Thick (10–50 µm) robust membrane |
| **Accelerometer Proof Mass** | Surface micromachining (polysilicon) | Movable structure with comb fingers |
| **Micro-Gear / Micro-Motor** | LIGA / UV-LIGA | High-aspect-ratio 3D shapes |
| **Microfluidic Channel** | Bulk + anodic bonding | Sealed glass channel |
| **RF MEMS Switch** | Surface micromachining (Au on Si) | Low-mass metal beam |
| **Gyroscope** | DRIE on SOI wafer | Thick, precise, vertical sidewalls |
| **Optical MEMS Mirror** | Surface micromachining | Flat reflective surface |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Aspect Ratio of a DRIE-Etched Via

**Problem:** A via of diameter 20 µm is etched through a 400 µm thick silicon wafer using the Bosch process. Compute the aspect ratio.

**Step 1 — Apply the Definition**

$$AR = \frac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Diameter}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$AR = \frac{400 \text{ µm}}{20 \text{ µm}} = 20$$

**Step 3 — Assessment**

An aspect ratio of **20:1** is well within the capability of standard DRIE processes (typical max = 50:1 to 100:1 with optimized Bosch recipes).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Stiction Force on a Released Cantilever

**Problem:** A 100 µm × 100 µm polysilicon beam is released with a 1 µm air gap. After rinsing, capillary force pulls it down. The surface tension of water is 72 mN/m and contact angle is 0°. Compute the stiction force.

**Step 1 — Apply the Capillary Force Equation**

$$F_{cap} = \frac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

\begin{aligned}
F_{cap} &= \frac{2 \times 0.072 \text{ N/m} \times (100 \times 10^{-6})^2 \text{ m}^2 \times 1}{1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}} \\
F_{cap} &= \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-6}} \\
F_{cap} &= \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-6}} \\
F_{cap} &= 1.44 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N} = 1.44 \text{ mN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Restoring Force of a 1 µm Cantilever**

The elastic restoring force of a 1 µm thick, 100 µm long polysilicon cantilever is typically on the order of **microNewtons (µN)** — orders of magnitude smaller than the **milliNewton** stiction force. Hence, **stiction is the dominant failure mechanism** if the structure is not dried using **critical point drying** or **freeze-drying (sublimation)**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General MEMS Fabrication Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Bare Si Wafer]) --> stepA1[Wafer Cleaning - RCA Process]
    stepA1 --> stepA2[Thermal Oxidation - Grow SiO2 Mask Layer]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Photoresist Coating - Spin Coater]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Soft Bake - Remove Solvent]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[UV Exposure - Through Photomask]
    stepA5 --> stepA6{Exposed or Unexposed Area Develops}
    stepA6 --> stepA7[Pattern Transfer - Mask Now Has Opening]
    stepA7 --> stepA8[Etching Step - Wet KOH or Dry DRIE]
    stepA8 --> stepA9[Photoresist Strip - Acetone or O2 Plasma]
    stepA9 --> stepA10[Final MEMS Structure - Membrane, Beam, or Cavity]
    stepA10 --> endA([End: Released MEMS Device])
```

## 4.2 Surface Micromachining — Sacrificial Layer Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: Si Wafer with SiO2 Isolation]) --> stepB1[Deposit Sacrificial Layer - PSG 2 um]
    stepB1 --> stepB2[Pattern Sacrificial Layer - Photolithography]
    stepB2 --> stepB3[Deposit Structural Layer - LPCVD Polysilicon 2 um]
    stepB3 --> stepB4[Pattern Structural Layer - Photolithography]
    stepB4 --> stepB5[Release Etch - HF Dip to Dissolve PSG]
    stepB5 --> stepB6[Critical Point Drying - Liquid CO2 to Prevent Stiction]
    stepB6 --> stepB7[Released Cantilever or Beam]
    stepB7 --> endB([End: Free-Standing MEMS Structure])
```

## 4.3 LIGA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Start: Conductive Substrate]) --> stepC1[Cast Thick PMMA Resist - Up to 1 mm]
    stepC1 --> stepC2[Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure - Through Au Mask]
    stepC2 --> stepC3[Develop PMMA - High Aspect Ratio Mold Forms]
    stepC3 --> stepC4[Electroplating - Ni or Cu Fills Mold]
    stepC4 --> stepC5[Remove PMMA - Solvent or Plasma]
    stepC5 --> stepC6[High Aspect Ratio Metal Microstructure]
    stepC6 --> stepC7[Optional Plastic Molding - For Mass Production]
    stepC7 --> endC([End: Final LIGA Part])
```

## 4.4 Bulk vs. Surface Micromachining Comparison — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph bulkproc[Bulk Micromachining]
        dirA1[Substrate Etched] --> dirA2[Structures from Wafer Itself]
        dirA2 --> dirA3[Thick - 10 to 500 um Features]
        dirA3 --> dirA4[Example: Pressure Sensor Diaphragm]
    end
    subgraph surfproc[Surface Micromachining]
        dirB1[Sacrificial Layer Deposited] --> dirB2[Structural Layer Deposited On Top]
        dirB2 --> dirB3[Sacrificial Layer Dissolved]
        dirB3 --> dirB4[Thin - 1 to 10 um Features]
        dirB4 --> dirB5[Example: Comb-Drive Accelerometer]
    end
    bulkproc -. competes with .-> surfproc
```

## 4.5 Bosch DRIE Process — Alternating Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startD([Start: Exposed Si Surface]) --> stepD1{Cycle Phase}
    stepD1 -->|Etch Phase 5 to 10 sec| dirD1[SF6 Plasma Strikes]
    dirD1 --> dirD2[Isotropic Si Etching Down and Laterally]
    dirD2 --> stepD2[Sidewall Becomes Scalloped]
    stepD2 --> stepD1
    stepD1 -->|Passivation Phase 3 to 5 sec| dirD3[C4F8 Plasma Deposits Fluorocarbon]
    dirD3 --> dirD4[Sidewall Protected with Teflon-Like Film]
    dirD4 --> stepD3[Bottom of Etch Cleared by Ion Bombardment]
    stepD3 --> stepD1
    stepD1 -->|After 100 to 300 Cycles| endD([End: High Aspect Ratio Vertical Sidewall])
```

## 4.6 Wafer Bonding — Anodic Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startE([Start: Si Wafer and Pyrex Glass]) --> stepE1[Deposit Electrode on Glass - Optional]
    stepE1 --> stepE2[Align and Contact Si and Glass]
    stepE2 --> stepE3[Heat to 350 to 450 C]
    stepE3 --> stepE4[Apply 500 to 1500 V DC Bias]
    stepE4 --> dirE1[Na Plus Ions in Glass Migrate to Cathode]
    dirE1 --> dirE2[Depletion Layer Forms at Si-Glass Interface]
    dirE2 --> dirE3[Strong Electrostatic Attraction]
    dirE3 --> dirE4[Permanent Chemical Bond - Si-O-Si]
    dirE4 --> endE([End: Hermetically Sealed Wafer Stack])
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define MEMS. List any four important MEMS fabrication techniques.**

**Model Answer:**

> **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** are integrated systems that combine mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronic circuits on a common silicon substrate, fabricated using microfabrication technology, with feature sizes typically in the range of **1 µm to 1000 µm**.

Four important MEMS fabrication techniques are:
1. **Bulk Micromachining** — selective etching of the silicon wafer itself.
2. **Surface Micromachining** — building structures layer-by-layer with a sacrificial release step.
3. **LIGA (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung)** — high-aspect-ratio micromolding using X-ray lithography and electroplating.
4. **Wafer Bonding** — joining of two or more processed wafers (e.g., anodic bonding of Si-to-glass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptable alternative 4th technique:** Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE), or specific lithography methods.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the principle of anisotropic wet etching of silicon. Why is (100)-oriented silicon preferred for V-groove formation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Anisotropic Wet Etching:**
Anisotropic wet etching exploits the **direction-dependent etch rate** of single-crystal silicon when exposed to alkaline solutions such as KOH, TMAH, or EDP. The etch rate is **highest in the $\langle 100 \rangle$ direction** and **lowest in the $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction** (etch rate ratio can exceed **400:1** for KOH).

This is because the (111) plane has the **highest atomic density** (atoms more tightly bonded), making it chemically more resistant to attack by hydroxyl ions (OH⁻).

**Why (100) Silicon is Preferred for V-Grooves:**
- On a (100) surface, the four {111} planes intersect at an angle of **54.74°** with the surface.
- A rectangular opening aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction produces two opposing {111} planes that etch inward and meet to form a **V-shaped groove** with self-limiting depth:

$$d = \frac{w_0}{\sqrt{2}}$$

- The V-groove walls are atomically smooth, the depth is geometrically precise, and the process is self-terminating (no time-control needed). Hence (100) silicon is the **industry standard** for V-grooves, fiber alignment, and pyramidal cavities.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model]

#### (a) Compare Bulk Micromachining and Surface Micromachining in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | Bulk Micromachining | Surface Micromachining |
|----------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Material Removed** | The bulk silicon wafer itself | Only thin sacrificial layers |
| **Structural Material** | The wafer (single-crystal Si) | Deposited thin films (poly-Si, SiN, metal) |
| **Typical Thickness** | **10 – 500 µm** | **1 – 10 µm** |
| **Geometry** | Thick membranes, beams, channels | Thin cantilevers, comb drives, membranes |
| **Etching Direction** | Anisotropic (wet) or vertical (DRIE) | Selective lateral etching through release holes |
| **Masking** | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ (on backside or frontside) | Photoresist + etch-selective chemistry |
| **Process Steps** | Fewer (3 – 5 steps) | More (deposition, patterning, release) |
| **Stiction Risk** | Low (no thin gap structures) | **High** (small gaps, capillary force) |
| **CMOS Compatibility** | Limited (KOH not CMOS-clean) | Better with TMAH or vapor HF |
| **Typical Device** | Pressure sensor diaphragm | Accelerometer, gyroscope |
| **Through-Wafer Etch** | **Yes** (front-to-back alignment needed) | No |
| **Critical Point Drying** | Not required | **Required** to prevent stiction |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
Bulk micromachining is preferred when **structural thickness and robustness** are critical (pressure sensors, inkjet channels). Surface micromachining is preferred when **low mass, low stiffness, and in-plane motion** are needed (inertial sensors, RF switches). Modern devices often **combine both** (e.g., DRIE-etched inertial mass with surface-micromachined sense electrodes).

---

#### (b) Describe the LIGA process with a neat process flow diagram. List two of its main applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**LIGA Process Steps:**

1. **Substrate Preparation (1 mark):** A conductive substrate (e.g., silicon with a seed metal layer) is cleaned.

2. **Resist Casting (1 mark):** A thick layer of **PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate)** — up to several hundred micrometers to millimeters — is cast or laminated onto the substrate.

3. **X-Ray Exposure (1 mark):** The PMMA is exposed through a patterned **X-ray mask** using a **synchrotron radiation source** (high-energy, parallel X-rays ~ 0.1 – 1 nm wavelength). The exposed PMMA undergoes chain scission and becomes soluble in a developer.

4. **Development (1 mark):** The exposed PMMA is dissolved in a chemical developer (e.g., GG developer), leaving a high-aspect-ratio **mold cavity** in the unexposed regions.

5. **Electroplating (1 mark):** The mold is filled with metal (typically **Ni, Cu, or Au**) via **electrochemical deposition** using the seed layer as the cathode.

6. **PMMA Removal (1 mark):** The remaining PMMA is stripped using solvents or plasma, leaving a free-standing metal microstructure with **aspect ratios exceeding 100:1** and sidewalls nearly vertical.

7. **Optional Molding Step (0.5 mark):** The metal structure can itself be used as an **injection mold insert** for mass production of plastic replicas.

**LIGA Process Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    staL1([Si + Seed Layer]) --> staL2[Cast PMMA Thick]
    staL2 --> staL3[Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure]
    staL3 --> staL4[Develop PMMA]
    staL4 --> staL5[Electroplating Ni or Cu]
    staL5 --> staL6[Strip PMMA]
    staL6 --> staL7([High AR Metal Microstructure])
```

**Applications of LIGA (1 mark):**
1. **Micro-Gears and Micro-Turbines** — for micro-motors and micro-fluidic pumps.
2. **Optical MEMS** — micro-lens arrays, X-ray zone plates, and spectrometer slits.
3. (Optional) **Micro-connectors** and **RF components** in telecommunications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the **German meaning** of LIGA but **forget the process steps**. Marks are heavily deducted if the **synchrotron X-ray exposure** step is missing or misidentified. Always state the source and the role of the conductive seed layer in electroplating.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]

#### (a) Explain in detail the Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) using the Bosch process. Why is it preferred over KOH wet etching for high-aspect-ratio structures? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**DRIE Bosch Process (5 marks):**

DRIE is a dry, plasma-based etching process that achieves **vertical sidewalls** with **aspect ratios > 50:1** in silicon. The Bosch process, developed by **Robert Bosch GmbH**, achieves this through **alternating cycles of two plasma phases**:

1. **Etch Phase (SF₆ Plasma):**
   - Sulfur hexafluoride (SF₆) gas is ionized to produce **F⁻ radicals**.
   - These radicals **isotropically** etch silicon: $Si + 4F \rightarrow SiF_4 \uparrow$.
   - Duration: **5 – 12 seconds** per cycle.
   - Etch depth per cycle: ~ 1 – 2 µm.

2. **Passivation Phase (C₄F₈ Plasma):**
   - Octafluorocyclobutane (C₄F₈) gas deposits a **fluorocarbon polymer film** ($\sim$ 10 – 100 nm thick) on **all exposed surfaces**, including sidewalls and the etch front.
   - Duration: **3 – 7 seconds** per cycle.
   - This film **passivates** the silicon and prevents lateral etching.

3. **Cycle Repetition (Ion Bombardment Step):**
   - At the start of the next etch phase, **ions accelerated by the bias voltage** strike the **bottom of the trench** (vertically) and **clear the polymer from the bottom**, while the sidewall polymer remains largely intact.
   - This **anisotropic** removal proceeds cycle-by-cycle.

**Result:** A characteristic **"scalloping"** pattern on the sidewalls (typical amplitude < 100 nm) is produced due to the cyclical process, but the overall sidewall is **near-vertical (89 – 90°)**.

**Why DRIE is Preferred over KOH Wet Etching (2 marks):**

| Property | KOH Wet Etching | DRIE (Bosch) |
|----------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Sidewall Angle** | Fixed at **54.74°** (V-groove) | **Vertical (90°)** |
| **Aspect Ratio** | Limited (~ 30:1) | **> 50:1, up to 100:1** |
| **Mask Material** | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ | Photoresist, SiO₂, metal |
| **CMOS Compatibility** | **Poor (K⁺ contamination)** | **Excellent** |
| **Etch Selectivity (Si:Mask)** | High (400:1) | Moderate (50:1 to 100:1) |
| **Feature Geometry** | Convex corners are **undercut**; convex compensation needed | **Arbitrary shapes** preserved |

> **Conclusion:** DRIE is preferred whenever **high aspect ratio, vertical sidewalls, and CMOS compatibility** are required — for example, **gyroscopes, inertial sensors, through-silicon-vias (TSVs), and microfluidic channels**.

---

#### (b) Discuss the major challenges in MEMS fabrication. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Major Challenges (1 mark each):**

1. **Stiction:**
   - When free-standing microstructures are released and dried, **capillary forces** can pull them into permanent contact with the substrate.
   - Mitigated by **critical point drying (CPD)** with liquid CO₂, **freeze-drying**, or **self-assembled monolayer (SAM) anti-stiction coatings** (e.g., FDTS — perfluorodecyltrichlorosilane).

2. **Residual Stress and Stress Gradients:**
   - Thin-film deposition processes (LPCVD polysilicon, sputtered metals) introduce **intrinsic stress** that can bow, buckle, or curl released structures.
   - Mitigated by **process optimization** (annealing, low-pressure deposition), **stress-engineered layers**, and **design compensation**.

3. **Packaging:**
   - MEMS devices often contain **moving parts** and require **hermetic, low-pressure, or controlled-atmosphere** packages.
   - Standard IC packaging is **insufficient** — specialized wafer-level packaging, cavity packages, or **anodic/glass-frit bonding** are needed.
   - Packaging can cost **> 50%** of total device cost.

4. **Wafer-Level Testing and Calibration:**
   - MEMS must be tested **before dicing** because they cannot be calibrated post-package easily.
   - Requires **built-in self-test (BIST)** structures, on-wafer probe stations with **optical and electrical** actuation, and statistical process control.

5. **Material Compatibility:**
   - Combining structural silicon with metals, polymers, piezoelectrics (PZT, AlN), and glass often creates **CTE mismatch** and **delamination** at interfaces.

6. **Yield and Reliability:**
   - MEMS failures include **fatigue fracture, wear, creep, and contamination** — issues rarely considered in IC design.
   - Requires **accelerated life testing** and **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**.

7. **Cost and Scalability:**
   - Each MEMS process step (lithography, DRIE, bonding) is **expensive**. Wafer throughput is limited compared to standard CMOS.
   - Move to **200/300 mm wafers** and **CMOS-compatible processes** for cost reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Loss of 2–3 Marks:** Students commonly list "stiction" but **forget to mention the engineering solution** (critical point drying, SAM coating). Marks are awarded for **both problem and solution**. Similarly, simply listing "packaging" without explaining **why** it is challenging costs marks. Always state: *moving parts + contamination sensitivity = special hermetic package needed*.

---

## KTU TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick Revision — Read This the Night Before the Exam!**

- **MEMS** = Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems, feature sizes **1 – 1000 µm**.
- **Bulk micromachining** removes the silicon wafer itself; **surface micromachining** builds on top using a **sacrificial layer**.
- **Key bulk etchant:** **KOH** (most common), **TMAH** (CMOS-compatible), **EDP** (high selectivity, toxic).
- **V-groove depth on (100) Si:** $d = w_0 / \sqrt{2}$ — geometric, **self-limiting**, no time control needed.
- **Sidewall angle on (100) Si with KOH:** **54.74°** (between (100) and {111} planes).
- **Selectivity** = $R_{target} / R_{mask}$; **Anisotropy factor** = $1 - R_{lat}/R_{vert}$.
- **Surface micromachining** needs **critical point drying (CPD)** to prevent **stiction**.
- **Stiction force:** $F_{cap} = 2 \gamma A \cos\theta / d$ — capillary force from rinse liquid.
- **LIGA** = Lithography + Electroplating + Molding; uses **synchrotron X-rays** and **PMMA**; aspect ratio **> 100:1**.
- **DRIE (Bosch)** alternates **SF₆ etch** and **C₄F₈ passivation** cycles for **vertical sidewalls**, aspect ratio **> 50:1**.
- **Rayleigh resolution:** $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ — to print smaller features, reduce $\lambda$ or increase $NA$.
- **Anodic bonding:** joins **Pyrex glass + Si** at **300 – 450 °C** with **high DC voltage (500 – 1500 V)** — used for hermetic sealing.
- **Wafer bonding types:** Anodic, Fusion, Eutectic, Adhesive, Direct (Plasma-activated).
- **Standard wafer sizes:** 100 mm (4"), 150 mm (6"), 200 mm (8"), 300 mm (12"); typical thickness **525 – 775 µm**.
- **Deposition methods:** Thermal Oxidation, LPCVD, PECVD, Sputtering, Evaporation, Electroplating.
- **Common MEMS materials:** Single-crystal Si, polysilicon, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, Al, Au, Cu, Ni, SU-8, PDMS, PZT, AlN.
- **Lithography hierarchy (resolution):** UV (0.5 µm) → DUV (0.13 µm) → E-beam (< 10 nm) → EUV (13.5 nm).
- **MEMS major challenges:** Stiction, residual stress, packaging cost, testing, material compatibility, yield.
- **Critical Point Drying (CPD):** Uses **liquid CO₂** at **T_c = 31 °C, P_c = 7.4 MPa** to avoid liquid–gas interface.
- **LIGA advantage over DRIE:** Taller structures (> 500 µm), smoother sidewalls; **disadvantage:** requires synchrotron (expensive).
- **Bosch process** is **time-multiplexed**; sidewall has characteristic **scallops** (~ 50 – 200 nm amplitude).
- **Anisotropy factor of KOH on (100) Si:** ≈ 1.0 (perfect anisotropy for {111} planes).
- **Surface micromachining standard layer stack:** Substrate → Isolation (SiO₂/SiN) → Sacrificial (PSG) → Structural (poly-Si) → Release.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro-Fabrication Using Deposition Techniques: Epitaxial, Sputtering, Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro-Fabrication Using Deposition Techniques: Epitaxial, Sputtering, Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Deposition Techniques in Micro-Fabrication

**Deposition** is the controlled addition of material onto a substrate to build thin films that constitute functional, structural, or protective layers in a Micro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS) or integrated circuit (IC). In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the three principal vapour-phase and beam-based deposition routes used to fabricate micron and sub-micron features are **Epitaxy**, **Sputtering**, and **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET863 Module 3):** The focus is on **physical vapour-phase mechanisms** (Epitaxy, Sputtering) and **chemical vapour-phase mechanisms** (CVD, LPCVD, PECVD). Each technique differs in the **source of depositing atoms**, the **energy of arrival**, and the **substrate temperature regime**.

## 1.2 Epitaxial Growth

### Formal Definition
**Epitaxy** (from Greek *epi* = "upon" and *taxis* = "arrangement") is the ordered, single-crystalline growth of a film on a crystalline substrate such that the deposited film inherits the crystallographic orientation of the substrate.

$$d_{\text{film}} \parallel d_{\text{sub}} \quad \text{and} \quad [uvw]_{\text{film}} \parallel [uvw]_{\text{sub}}$$

When the film and substrate are of the **same material**, it is called **homoepitaxy** (e.g., Si on Si). When they are of **different materials** but share a similar lattice structure, it is called **heteroepitaxy** (e.g., GaAs on Si, AlGaAs on GaAs).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine laying a perfectly cut **second deck of cards on top of an existing deck**. If every card you place aligns exactly with the card directly beneath it (same colour, same suit pattern, same direction), that is epitaxy. The bottom deck is the *substrate* (template), and the upper deck is the *epitaxial layer*. If the upper deck were a different brand with slightly different card dimensions, it would still align but with some strain — that is **heteroepitaxy** with **lattice mismatch strain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two principal epitaxial variants in industry:**
> 1. **Vapour-Phase Epitaxy (VPE)** — Chemical transport using chloride or hydride precursors.
> 2. **Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)** — Ultra-high-vacuum physical beam deposition.

## 1.3 Sputtering

### Formal Definition
**Sputtering** is a physical vapour deposition (PVD) process in which energetic ions (typically Ar⁺) bombard a target material, ejecting (sputtering) atoms from its surface. The ejected atoms traverse a low-pressure chamber and condense on the substrate to form a thin film.

The **sputter yield** $Y$ is defined as:

$$Y = \frac{\text{Number of target atoms ejected}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture an **archery range**, but reversed. Instead of arrows hitting a target and sticking, imagine hundreds of tiny invisible marbles (ions) being fired at a solid wall (target). Each marble knocks loose a few atoms from the wall. These knocked-out atoms drift across the room and stick to a cold windowpane (substrate). The windowpane slowly develops a thin, even layer of "wall material." That, in essence, is sputtering — momentum transfer, not chemical reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sputtering is the workhorse of metallic and refractory thin films** in MEMS — Al, Au, Ti, W, Mo, Cr, TiW, and dielectric compounds like SiO₂ and Si₃N₄ are routinely deposited this way.

## 1.4 Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)

### Formal Definition
**Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)** is a process in which volatile precursors react (or decompose) on or near a heated substrate to form a non-volatile solid film, with by-products being pumped away as gases.

The generalised reaction scheme is:

$$\text{Reactant gases} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat/Plasma/Catalyst}} \text{Solid film (on substrate)} + \text{Volatile by-products}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **kitchen stovetop covered in frost on a cold winter morning**. As you boil water (precursor vapour) in a pot, the steam rises. Where the steam meets the cold window (cold substrate), it condenses into a thin layer of water droplets (solid film). CVD is similar, but the "steam" chemically reacts on the substrate to deposit a solid material — for instance, silane (SiH₄) decomposing on a silicon wafer to leave pure silicon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CVD is critical:** CVD produces films of **excellent conformality** (uniform coverage over steps and trenches), **high purity**, and **good adhesion**, which makes it indispensable for deep-submicron ICs and 3D MEMS structures where sputtering would fail.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Epitaxial Growth — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

Epitaxial growth proceeds through a sequence of **surface-kinetic steps** that determine film quality:

1. **Transport of adatoms** to the substrate surface via the gas phase (VPE) or molecular beam (MBE).
2. **Adsorption** of precursor species onto the heated substrate surface.
3. **Surface diffusion** of adatoms to energetically favourable lattice sites (kink sites, steps).
4. **Chemical reaction / decomposition** at the surface (in VPE) or incorporation (in MBE).
5. **Nucleation and layer-by-layer (Frank–van der Merwe) or 3D island (Volmer–Weber) growth.**
6. **Desorption** of by-products and unincorporated species.

### Growth Modes Determined by Lattice Mismatch

| Growth Mode | Behaviour | Lattice Mismatch Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Frank–van der Merwe (FM)** | Layer-by-layer (2D) | $f < 0$ or small positive |
| **Volmer–Weber (VW)** | 3D island | $f > 0$ large, surface energy dominates |
| **Stranski–Krastanov (SK)** | Layer + island | Intermediate $f$ (most common in practice) |

where the **lattice mismatch** $f$ is:

$$f = \frac{a_s - a_f}{a_f}$$

with $a_s$ = substrate lattice constant, $a_f$ = film lattice constant.

## 2.2 Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)

### Operating Principle
- Substrate held at **elevated temperature (~500–700 °C)** in **UHV (~10⁻¹⁰ Torr)**.
- Effusion cells (Knudsen cells) produce **directed molecular beams** of source materials.
- Beam fluxes precisely controlled by **cell temperatures** ($\pm 0.1$ °C) and **shutters**.
- **In-situ RHEED** (Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction) monitors layer-by-layer growth in real time.

### Strengths and Limitations

| Aspect | MBE Characteristic |
|---|---|
| Growth rate | Very slow (**0.1–1 µm/h**) |
| Thickness control | Mono-layer precision |
| Material purity | Extremely high (**UHV environment**) |
| Equipment cost | Very high |
| Typical use | Quantum wells, HEMTs, superlattices |

## 2.3 Vapour-Phase Epitaxy (VPE)

In **Silicon VPE**, the reaction is:

$$\text{SiCl}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{1200\,°\text{C}} \text{Si} + 4\,\text{HCl}$$

For **GaAs VPE**:

$$\text{GaCl} + \text{AsH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{GaAs} + \text{HCl} + \text{H}_2$$

VPE operates at **atmospheric or low pressure** and uses **hydrogen as carrier gas**.

## 2.4 Sputtering — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.4.1 Physics of Momentum Transfer

When an incident ion of mass $m_i$ and energy $E_i$ strikes a target atom of mass $M$, momentum transfer is governed by the **elastic collision kinematics**. The maximum energy transferred in a head-on collision is:

$$E_{\max} = E_i \cdot \frac{4\,M\,m_i}{(M + m_i)^2}$$

This equation has two important physical consequences:

- The transfer is **maximised when $M \approx m_i$** — that is why **argon (Ar, m = 40 u)** is the preferred sputtering gas for most metals (Cu, Ti, Al — all in the 27–207 u range).
- The angular distribution of sputtered atoms follows approximately a **cosine law** from the target surface (Knudsen cosine emission).

### 2.4.2 Plasma Generation in DC/RF Sputtering

The plasma is sustained by electrons accelerated in an electric field, ionising neutral Ar atoms:

$$e^- + \text{Ar} \longrightarrow \text{Ar}^+ + 2\,e^-$$

The standard **DC sputtering** configuration requires a **conductive target** (e.g., Al). For **insulating targets** (SiO₂, Al₂O₃), **RF sputtering (typically 13.56 MHz)** is mandatory because charge accumulation on the target surface would otherwise quench the plasma.

### 2.4.3 Magnetron Sputtering

A magnetic field confines secondary electrons near the target, increasing ionisation efficiency and allowing operation at **lower pressures (~1–10 mTorr)** and **higher deposition rates**. This is the industrial standard.

### 2.4.4 Sputtering Variants Comparison

| Variant | Pressure (Torr) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Diode** | 10⁻² – 10⁻¹ | Conductive metal films |
| **RF Diode** | 10⁻² – 10⁻¹ | Insulator films |
| **Magnetron (DC/RF)** | 10⁻³ – 10⁻² | High-rate industrial deposition |
| **Reactive Sputtering** | 10⁻³ – 10⁻² | Oxides/nitrides (TiO₂, Si₃N₄) via O₂/N₂ reactive gas |
| **Ion-Beam Sputtering (IBS)** | 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁴ | Ultra-clean, controlled films |

## 2.5 Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.5.1 Step-Sequence of CVD Film Growth

1. **Gas transport** — Reactant gases flow into the reactor via carrier gas (H₂, N₂, Ar).
2. **Gas-phase reactions** — Homogeneous reactions may occur in the gas phase (often undesirable).
3. **Convective/diffusive transport** — Species diffuse through the **boundary layer** to the substrate.
4. **Adsorption** — Species adsorb on the heated substrate surface.
5. **Surface diffusion** — Adatoms migrate to nucleation sites.
6. **Surface reaction** — Chemical reaction forms the solid film + by-products.
7. **Desorption & removal** — By-products desorb and are swept away by the gas flow.

### 2.5.2 Reactor Types and Pressure Regimes

| CVD Variant | Pressure Regime | Temperature | Plasma? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **APCVD** (Atmospheric) | 760 Torr | 600–1200 °C | No |
| **LPCVD** (Low Pressure) | 0.1–10 Torr | 550–900 °C | No |
| **PECVD** (Plasma-Enhanced) | 0.1–10 Torr | **200–400 °C** | Yes (RF) |
| **HDPCVD** (High-Density Plasma) | mTorr | 200–400 °C | Yes (ICP/ECR) |
| **MOCVD** (Metal–Organic) | 10–760 Torr | 600–800 °C | No |
| **ALD** (Atomic Layer Deposition) | 0.1–10 Torr | 100–350 °C | Optional |

### 2.5.3 Common CVD Reactions (High-Yield)

**Polysilicon (LPCVD):**
$$\text{SiH}_4 \xrightarrow{580–650\,°\text{C}} \text{Si} + 2\,\text{H}_2$$

**Silicon Dioxide (LPCVD):**
$$\text{SiH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{400–450\,°\text{C}} \text{SiO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2$$

**Silicon Nitride (LPCVD):**
$$3\,\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 4\,\text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{700–800\,°\text{C}} \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4 + 6\,\text{HCl} + 6\,\text{H}_2$$

**Tungsten (CVD, for vias):**
$$\text{WF}_6 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{400\,°\text{C}} \text{W} + 6\,\text{HF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Must-Know Distinction:**
> - **Sputtering** = **physical** momentum transfer (cold process, no chemistry).
> - **CVD** = **chemical** reaction on substrate (hot process, conformal).
> - **Epitaxy** = **crystalline** growth (template-driven, mono-layer precision).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Use in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y = \dfrac{N_{\text{ejected}}}{N_{\text{incident}}}$ | Sputter yield definition | Sputter rate calculation |
| 2 | $E_{\max} = E_i \cdot \dfrac{4\,M\,m_i}{(M + m_i)^2}$ | Max energy transfer (head-on collision) | Choice of inert gas |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{a_s - a_f}{a_f}$ | Lattice mismatch | Epitaxial strain analysis |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{R \cdot h}{\rho}$ | Mass-deposition relation: $R$ = deposition rate, $h$ = thickness, $\rho$ = density | Deposition time calculation |
| 5 | $\Lambda_{\text{mfp}} = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2\,P}$ | Mean free path of gas molecules | CVD/sputter chamber design |
| 6 | $v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{flux}}}{\rho \cdot N_A} \cdot \tilde{M}$ | Deposition rate from molecular flux | MBE growth rate |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{step coverage}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{sidewall}}}{t_{\text{top}}}$ | Step coverage metric | CVD vs sputtering quality |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{k \cdot C_{\text{surf}}}{1 + k \cdot C_{\text{surf}} / k_s}$ | Langmuir-Hinshelwood CVD kinetics | Rate-limiting step |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants to Remember:**
> - **k_B** = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - **N_A** = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
> - **13.56 MHz** = Standard RF plasma frequency (ITU-allocated ISM band)
> - **760 Torr** = 1 atm
> - **RHEED** = Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction (in-situ MBE monitor)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lattice Mismatch and Critical Thickness in Heteroepitaxy

### Problem Setup
Consider a thin film of lattice constant $a_f$ grown epitaxially on a substrate of lattice constant $a_s$. Determine the **mismatch strain** $\epsilon$ and the **critical thickness** $h_c$ beyond which misfit dislocations form.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define lattice mismatch**

The fractional lattice mismatch $f$ is defined as the relative difference between substrate and film lattice constants:

$$f = \frac{a_s - a_f}{a_f}$$

**Step 2 — Initial strain in the film (before relaxation)**

When the film grows coherently (i.e., adopts the substrate lattice in-plane), the in-plane strain is:

$$\epsilon_{\parallel} = \frac{a_s - a_f}{a_f} = f$$

**Step 3 — Out-of-plane (perpendicular) strain via Poisson's ratio**

If the film is free to relax vertically, the perpendicular strain is given by Poisson's effect:

$$\epsilon_{\perp} = -\frac{2\nu}{1-\nu}\,\epsilon_{\parallel} = -\frac{2\nu}{1-\nu}\,f$$

where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio of the film material.

**Step 4 — Elastic strain energy per unit area**

The strain energy density (per unit volume) of a biaxially strained film is:

$$u = \frac{E}{1-\nu}\left(\epsilon_{\parallel}\right)^2 = \frac{E\,f^2}{1-\nu}$$

The total elastic energy per unit area for a film of thickness $h$ is:

$$U_{\text{elastic}} = u \cdot h = \frac{E\,f^2\,h}{1-\nu}$$

**Step 5 — Energy of a misfit dislocation**

A single misfit dislocation of Burgers vector $b$ (with edge component $b_\perp$) introduced at the interface contributes energy per unit length:

$$U_{\text{disl}} \approx \frac{G\,b^2}{4\pi}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{h}{b}\right)\left(\frac{1-\nu\cos^2\theta}{1-\nu}\right)$$

where $G$ is the shear modulus. Per unit length of dislocation line, simplified:

$$U_{\text{disl}} \approx \frac{G\,b}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\,\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{h}{b}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Equating energies to obtain critical thickness**

A dislocation becomes energetically favourable when the strain-energy release equals the dislocation self-energy. For a simple balance:

$$U_{\text{elastic}}(h_c) \approx U_{\text{disl}}(h_c)$$

Solving this balance (after detailed algebraic manipulation) gives the **Matthews–Blakeslee equation**:

$$\boxed{\;h_c \approx \frac{b}{8\pi f}\,\frac{(1 - \nu\cos\lambda)}{(1+\nu)\cos\phi}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{\alpha\,h_c}{b}\right)\;}$$

where:
- $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude,
- $\lambda$ = angle between dislocation line and Burgers vector,
- $\phi$ = angle between slip direction and film normal,
- $\alpha$ = core-cutoff parameter (~4).

### Simplified Working Expression

For a quick estimation, the **inverse proportionality** is often quoted:

$$h_c \approx \frac{b}{2\,f} \quad (\text{order-of-magnitude estimate})$$

**Example:** For Si on a Si–Ge substrate with $f \approx 0.04$ and $b \approx 0.4$ nm:

$$h_c \approx \frac{0.4~\text{nm}}{2 \times 0.04} = 5~\text{nm}$$

This shows that even modest mismatch caps the coherent film thickness at only a few nanometres.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sputter Yield Using the Sigmund Model

### Problem Setup
Estimate the sputter yield $Y$ for a target bombarded by ions at normal incidence, using Sigmund's cascade-sputtering theory.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the energy-deposition regime**

Sigmund assumes the incident ion creates a **collision cascade** in the target. The relevant quantity is the **nuclear energy deposited per unit depth** at the surface, $F_D(0)$.

**Step 2 — Sigmund's formula**

For low-to-moderate ion energies (linear cascade regime):

$$Y(E) = \frac{0.042\,\alpha\,S_n(E)}{U_0}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = dimensionless material-dependent parameter (≈ 0.2–0.4 for many metals),
- $S_n(E)$ = **nuclear stopping power** (energy lost to nuclear collisions per unit path length),
- $U_0$ = **surface binding energy** (≈ sublimation enthalpy per atom).

**Step 3 — Express $S_n$ using the Lindhard partition**

In the energy regime $E \sim 100$ eV – 1 keV typical of sputtering:

$$S_n(E) = 4\pi\,a\,Z_1\,Z_2\,e^2\,\frac{M_1}{M_1 + M_2}\,s_n(\epsilon)$$

where $a$ is the screening radius, $Z_1, Z_2$ are atomic numbers, $M_1, M_2$ are atomic masses, and $s_n(\epsilon)$ is the reduced stopping cross-section (tabulated in Lindhard's tables).

**Step 4 — Thomas–Fermi screening radius**

$$a = \frac{0.8853\,a_0}{Z_1^{2/3} + Z_2^{2/3}}$$

where $a_0 = 0.529$ Å is the Bohr radius.

**Step 5 — Combine and solve**

Combining all factors and substituting into $Y(E)$ yields the famous Sigmund sputter-yield curve. A useful **empirical approximation** that captures the threshold and saturation behaviour is the **Yamamura–Matsunami fit**:

$$Y(E) = 0.42\,\frac{\alpha^*\,Q\,K\,s_n(\epsilon)}{U_0}\,\left[1 - \left(\frac{E_{\text{th}}}{E}\right)^{1/2}\right]^{2.5}$$

where $E_{\text{th}}$ is the **sputtering threshold energy** (typically 20–40 eV for most metals under Ar bombardment).

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check**

For **Ar⁺ (500 eV) → Cu**:
- $U_0 \approx 3.5$ eV (Cu sublimation enthalpy per atom)
- $E_{\text{th}} \approx 17$ eV
- $\alpha^* Q K s_n \approx$ tabulated → yields $Y \approx 2.3$ atoms/ion

This is in excellent agreement with experimental TRIM/SRIM simulations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sputtering vs CVD vs Epitaxy

| Evaluation Criterion | Sputtering (PVD) | CVD | Epitaxy (MBE/VPE) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Momentum transfer (physical) | Surface chemical reaction | Surface diffusion + templated incorporation |
| **Substrate Temperature** | **RT – 200 °C** (cold) | 400 – 1200 °C | 500 – 700 °C (MBE), 700 – 1200 °C (VPE) |
| **Deposition Rate** | **High** (1–10 nm/s for metals) | Moderate (0.1–1 nm/s) | Very slow (0.01–0.3 nm/s) |
| **Film Crystallinity** | Polycrystalline / amorphous | Poly / amorphous / epi | **Single crystalline** |
| **Step Coverage** | Poor (~30% on deep trenches) | **Excellent (>90%)** | Excellent (atomic-scale) |
| **Conformality** | Poor (line-of-sight) | Good to excellent | Excellent |
| **Purity** | Moderate (residual gases) | High | **Ultra-high** (UHV) |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Very high |
| **Throughput** | High | High | Low |
| **Typical Materials** | Metals, alloys, ceramics | Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, W, poly-Si | Si, GaAs, InP, GaN, AlGaAs |
| **Industrial Application** | IC metallization, MEMS electrodes | Inter-layer dielectric (ILD), STI fill, poly-Si gates | HBT, HEMT, quantum wells, LEDs |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Overall Deposition Process Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Micro-Fabrication Deposition Techniques] --> B[Physical Vapour Deposition PVD]
    A --> C[Chemical Vapour Deposition CVD]
    A --> D[Epitaxial Growth]

    B --> B1[Evaporation]
    B --> B2[Sputtering]
    B2 --> B2a[DC Sputtering]
    B2 --> B2b[RF Sputtering]
    B2 --> B2c[Magnetron Sputtering]
    B2 --> B2d[Reactive Sputtering]
    B2 --> B2e[Ion Beam Sputtering]

    C --> C1[APCVD]
    C --> C2[LPCVD]
    C --> C3[PECVD]
    C --> C4[HDPCVD]
    C --> C5[MOCVD]
    C --> C6[ALD]

    D --> D1[Homoepitaxy]
    D --> D2[Heteroepitaxy]
    D --> D3[MBE]
    D --> D4[VPE]

    B2a --> B2aNote[Conductive targets only]
    B2b --> B2bNote[Insulators via 13.56 MHz RF]
    B2c --> B2cNote[Magnetic field for plasma confinement]
    B3Note[Heated substrate low pressure] --> C
    D1 --> D1Note[Same material no mismatch]
    D2 --> D2Note[Different material lattice mismatch]
    D3 --> D3Note[UHV molecular beams]
    D4 --> D4Note[Carrier gas reaction]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Sputtering Chamber Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sputter_Chamber
        T[Target Material Cathode]
        S[Substrate Anode]
        M[Magnet Array Behind Target]
        G[Gas Inlet Ar]
        V[Vacuum Pump]
    end

    G -->|Ar gas| Sputter_Chamber
    V -->|exhaust| Sputter_Chamber

    P1[High Voltage DC or 13.56 MHz RF] --> T
    P1 --> S

    T -->|Ar ions bombard| T
    T -->|ejected atoms| S

    M -.->|confines electrons near target| T

    Ar[Ar Atom] -->|ionisation by e-| ArIon[Ar Positive Ion]
    ArIon -->|accelerated to target| T

    S -->|thin film grows| Film[Deposited Film]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: CVD Reactor Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Gas_Delivery
        P1[Precursor 1 e.g. SiH4]
        P2[Precursor 2 e.g. NH3]
        CG[Carrier Gas H2 or N2]

        MFC2[MFC2 Mass Flow Controller]
        MFC3[MFC3 Carrier Controller]
    end

    subgraph Reactor_Zone
        INLET[Gas Inlet Showerhead]
        SUB[Heated Substrate 400 to 900 C]
        B[Boundary Layer]
        DEPO[Film Growth Surface]
        EXH[Exhaust Out]
    end

    subgraph Byproduct_Management
        SCRUB[Scrubber]
        PUMP[Vacuum Pump]
    end

    P1 --> MFC1 --> INLET
    P2 --> MFC2 --> INLET
    CG --> MFC3 --> INLET

    INLET --> B
    B --> DEPO
    SUB --> DEPO
    DEPO --> Film[Thin Solid Film]
    DEPO -->|byproducts HCl H2| EXH

    EXH --> PUMP --> SCRUB
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow: Epitaxial Growth Mechanism Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Source Atoms or Precursor Molecules] --> A2[Transport to Substrate Surface]
    A2 --> A3[Adsorption on Heated Substrate]
    A3 --> A4[Surface Diffusion to Kink Sites]
    A4 --> A5{Nucleation Mode}

    A5 -->|low mismatch| A6[Frank van der Merwe 2D layer by layer]
    A5 -->|high mismatch| A7[Volmer Weber 3D islands]
    A5 -->|intermediate| A8[Stranski Krastanov layer plus islands]

    A6 --> A9[Crystalline Epitaxial Film]
    A7 --> A10[Polycrystalline or Defective Film]
    A8 --> A11[Strained Film with Misfit Dislocations]

    A9 --> A12[RHEED or In-situ Monitor Verification]
    A10 --> A12
    A11 --> A12
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Comparison Matrix: Process Energy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Thermal Evaporation 0.1 to 0.5 eV per atom]
    E2[Sputtering 1 to 10 eV per atom]
    E3[Ion Beam Deposition 50 to 500 eV per atom]
    E4[CVD 0.1 to 1 eV per atom thermal]
    E5[MBE 0.05 to 0.3 eV per atom thermal]

    E1 --> Compare[Energy of Arriving Atoms]
    E2 --> Compare
    E3 --> Compare
    E4 --> Compare
    E5 --> Compare

    Compare --> Result[Adatom Mobility and Film Quality]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the schematic:** Higher adatom energy (sputtering, IBD) increases **film adhesion** and **density** but can cause **substrate damage** and **interfacial mixing**. Lower energy (MBE, thermal evap.) yields **sharper interfaces** but may give **poor step coverage**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) and Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD). Mention two examples of each.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PVD (e.g., Sputtering) | CVD (e.g., LPCVD) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Physical – momentum transfer / evaporation | Chemical – surface reaction of gases |
| Temperature | Low (RT – 300 °C) | High (400 – 1200 °C) |
| Step coverage | Poor (line-of-sight) | Excellent (conformal) |
| Films | Metallic, refractory | Dielectric, poly-Si, W |

**Examples:**
- **PVD**: Sputtering (Al, TiW), Thermal evaporation (Au).
- **CVD**: LPCVD poly-Si, PECVD SiO₂, Tungsten CVD.

**Marks Allocation (3):** Mechanism (1), Differences (1), Examples (1).

---

### **Q2. What is meant by "homoepitaxy" and "heteroepitaxy"? Explain the role of lattice mismatch.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
- **Homoepitaxy:** Growth of a film of the *same* material on the substrate (e.g., Si on Si). No lattice mismatch; high crystalline perfection.
- **Heteroepitaxy:** Growth of a *different* material on the substrate (e.g., GaAs on Si). Lattice mismatch $f = (a_s - a_f)/a_f$ induces strain.
- **Role of mismatch:** When $f$ is small, layer-by-layer growth occurs. Beyond a **critical thickness** $h_c$, **misfit dislocations** form to relieve strain. This determines the usable thickness of strained heterostructures.

**Marks Allocation (3):** Homo/Hetero definitions (1), Lattice mismatch formula (1), Critical thickness implication (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the working principle of a DC magnetron sputtering system with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the magnetic field. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

A DC magnetron sputtering system consists of:
- A **vacuum chamber** evacuated to ~10⁻⁶ Torr base pressure.
- A **target** (cathode) — the material to be deposited (e.g., Al).
- A **substrate holder** (anode) placed a few cm away.
- **Ar gas** inlet and a **throttle valve** maintaining ~1–10 mTorr.
- **Permanent magnets** arranged behind the target in a ring or torus pattern.
- A **DC power supply** (typically 300–1000 V) driving the discharge.

**Operating sequence:**
1. Ar gas is introduced at low pressure.
2. A high DC voltage ionises Ar atoms, producing Ar⁺ ions and free electrons.
3. Ar⁺ ions are accelerated towards the negatively biased target, bombarding its surface.
4. Momentum transfer ejects target atoms (sputtering).
5. Ejected atoms traverse the chamber and condense on the substrate, forming a thin film.
6. Secondary electrons emitted from the target are trapped near the target surface by the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ perpendicular to $\vec{E}$.

**Role of the Magnetic Field:**

The magnetic field confines secondary electrons in **cycloidal orbits** close to the target. This increases the **electron path length**, raising the **probability of ionising collisions** with Ar atoms. Consequently:
- Plasma density rises by **10×–100×**.
- Lower operating pressure (**1–10 mTorr** vs 50–100 mTorr in diode sputtering).
- **Higher deposition rates** (1–10 nm/s).
- **Lower substrate heating** (because electrons do not reach the substrate).

This is why magnetron sputtering is the **industry standard** for metallisation.

**Marks Allocation (7):** Setup (2), Sequence (2), Role of magnetic field (3).

---

#### (b) Describe the LPCVD process for polysilicon deposition. State the chemical reaction and discuss the advantages of LPCVD over APCVD. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**LPCVD Polysilicon Process:**

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$\text{SiH}_4 \xrightarrow{580\,°\text{C – 650\,°\text{C}}} \text{Si} + 2\,\text{H}_2$$

(Alternative: $\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\,\text{HCl}$ at ~700 °C for higher-quality films.)

**Process Parameters:**
- **Pressure:** 0.1 – 10 Torr (low).
- **Temperature:** 580 – 650 °C.
- **Reactor:** Hot-wall, vertical or horizontal furnace.
- **Substrate:** Si wafer with SiO₂ or Si₃N₄ underlayer.

**Sequence:**
1. Wafers loaded into a quartz furnace tube.
2. Tube pumped to vacuum, then silane introduced with N₂ carrier.
3. Pyrolysis of SiH₄ on hot wafer surface produces Si atoms.
4. Si atoms diffuse along the surface, nucleating a continuous polysilicon film.
5. H₂ by-product is pumped away.

**Advantages of LPCVD over APCVD:**

| Parameter | APCVD | LPCVD |
|---|---|---|
| Step coverage | Poor (~50–70%) | **Excellent (>90%)** |
| Film uniformity | Inherits gas-flow gradients | Excellent (diffusion-dominated transport) |
| Pinhole density | High | Low |
| Throughput | High | Slightly lower (but higher per-wafer quality) |
| Particle contamination | More | Less |

LPCVD's lower operating pressure extends the gas mean free path, making gas-phase diffusion uniform and reproducible. The trade-off is the need for higher operating temperatures (typically **above 600 °C**), which constrains integration with metals already deposited.

**Marks Allocation (7):** Reaction (2), Process description (2), Comparison table (3).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) with its components and working. List four advantages over VPE. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE):**

MBE is an ultra-high-vacuum (UHV) thin-film deposition technique for growing single-crystalline epitaxial layers with monolayer precision.

**Major Components:**
1. **UHV Chamber** (~10⁻¹¹ Torr) — minimises contamination.
2. **Effusion Cells (Knudsen Cells)** — contain source materials (e.g., Ga, As, Al) and produce thermal molecular beams.
3. **Beam Flux Monitors** — ion gauges and quartz crystal microbalances for rate control.
4. **Mechanical Shutters** — fast (<0.1 s) beam interruption for layer switching.
5. **Substrate Heater** — maintains substrate at 500–700 °C.
6. **RHEED** (Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction) — in-situ growth monitor providing monolayer-resolved surface information.

**Working:**
1. Source materials are heated in effusion cells until they produce a steady flux of atoms/molecules.
2. Beams travel in straight lines (mean free path >> chamber dimensions due to UHV) toward the heated substrate.
3. Atoms adsorb, diffuse, and incorporate into the crystal lattice of the substrate.
4. Shutter operation allows switching between sources for heterostructures (e.g., GaAs/AlGaAs superlattices).
5. RHEED oscillations provide real-time feedback on layer-by-layer growth.

**Four Advantages over VPE:**

| # | Advantage |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Lower growth temperature** (500–700 °C vs 800–1200 °C in VPE) — reduces interdiffusion and doping redistribution. |
| 2 | **Atomic-layer thickness control** (~0.1 nm) — monolayer precision via shutter timing. |
| 3 | **In-situ RHEED monitoring** — real-time surface diagnostic, not available in VPE. |
| 4 | **Sharper heterointerfaces** due to UHV and abrupt shutter switching — ideal for quantum wells and superlattices. |

A fifth advantage is **flexibility** — MBE can deposit materials that are difficult to obtain via chemical precursors.

**Marks Allocation (7):** Components (3), Working (2), Advantages (2).

---

#### (b) With a suitable diagram, explain a CVD reactor setup. Discuss the role of plasma in PECVD and its advantages over conventional LPCVD. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**CVD Reactor Setup:**

A typical CVD reactor consists of:

1. **Gas Delivery System:** Precursor and carrier gas cylinders, mass-flow controllers (MFCs), and gas manifolds.
2. **Reaction Chamber:** Quartz tube (for hot-wall LPCVD) or stainless-steel chamber (for PECVD), containing the substrate heater.
3. **Heating System:** Resistance heaters (hot-wall) or lamp heaters (cold-wall).
4. **Substrate Holder:** Susceptor (often graphite-coated) holding wafers.
5. **Exhaust System:** Vacuum pump + scrubber for safe disposal of corrosive by-products (HCl, HF).
6. **Plasma Source (PECVD only):** RF electrode (13.56 MHz) or microwave source.

The reactants (e.g., SiH₄ + NH₃ + N₂) flow into the chamber, react on the heated wafer, and the solid film (Si₃N₄) deposits while by-products (H₂, HCl) exit through the exhaust.

**Role of Plasma in PECVD:**

In PECVD, an RF electric field ionises the reactant gases, creating a plasma that contains ions, electrons, and free radicals:

$$\text{SiH}_4 + \text{e}^- \longrightarrow \text{SiH}_3^* + \text{H}^* + \text{e}^-$$

The **free radicals** $\text{SiH}_3^*$ and $\text{H}^*$ are highly reactive, allowing film deposition at **much lower temperatures (200–400 °C)** than thermal CVD, because the plasma provides the activation energy instead of heat.

**Advantages of PECVD over LPCVD:**

| # | Advantage |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Low deposition temperature** (200–400 °C) — compatible with Al metallisation already on the wafer. |
| 2 | **Higher deposition rates** at low temperature. |
| 3 | **Tunable film stress** (tensile to compressive) by adjusting RF power, pressure, and frequency. |
| 4 | **Better gap-fill for high-aspect-ratio structures** in some configurations (HDPCVD). |
| 5 | **Lower thermal budget** — critical for back-end-of-line (BEOL) processing. |

The trade-off is that PECVD films have **higher hydrogen content** and **lower density** than LPCVD films.

**Marks Allocation (7):** Reactor description (3), Plasma role (2), Comparison (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "step coverage" with "conformality"** in your answers. Step coverage specifically refers to the sidewall/top thickness ratio; conformality includes curvature behaviour.
> 2. **Magnetron sputtering ≠ DC sputtering**: Magnetron adds magnetic confinement; omitting this loses **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 3. **For PECVD, always mention the RF frequency** (13.56 MHz) — students frequently omit this and lose a mark.
> 4. **In epitaxy, "substrate temperature" is the single most important parameter** — MBE operates at ~500–700 °C, *not* room temperature.
> 5. **Lattice mismatch sign matters**: If $a_s > a_f$, the film is under **tensile** strain; if $a_s < a_f$, the film is under **compressive** strain.
> 6. **Always cite at least one numerical value** (e.g., growth rate, temperature, pressure) in long answers — KTU examiners award marks for quantitative precision.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3**

### Epitaxy
- ✅ Epitaxy = single-crystalline film grown on a crystalline substrate.
- ✅ Homoepitaxy (same material) vs Heteroepitaxy (different material, possible lattice mismatch).
- ✅ Lattice mismatch $f = (a_s - a_f)/a_f$ — drives strain.
- ✅ **MBE:** UHV, effusion cells, RHEED monitoring, monolayer precision, very slow (~µm/h).
- ✅ **VPE:** Carrier gas (H₂), high T (700–1200 °C), chemical transport.
- ✅ **Critical thickness** $h_c$ — beyond it, misfit dislocations form (Matthews–Blakeslee).
- ✅ Growth modes: Frank–van der Merwe (2D), Volmer–Weber (3D), Stranski–Krastanov (layer + island).

### Sputtering
- ✅ Sputtering = momentum-transfer PVD using Ar⁺ ion bombardment.
- ✅ **Sputter yield** $Y$ = ejected atoms per incident ion (Sigmund model, Yamamura fit).
- ✅ Max energy transfer formula: $E_{\max} = E_i \cdot 4 M m_i / (M + m_i)^2$.
- ✅ **DC sputtering** = conductive targets; **RF sputtering (13.56 MHz)** = insulators.
- ✅ **Magnetron sputtering** = magnetic confinement → higher ionisation → 10× deposition rate.
- ✅ **Reactive sputtering** = O₂/N₂ added during metal sputtering to form oxides/nitrides.
- ✅ Sputtering gives **poor step coverage** (line-of-sight).

### Chemical Vapour Deposition
- ✅ CVD = chemical reaction of gas precursors on heated substrate.
- ✅ **APCVD** (760 Torr, 600–1200 °C); **LPCVD** (0.1–10 Torr, 550–900 °C); **PECVD** (RF plasma, 200–400 °C).
- ✅ Polysilicon CVD: SiH₄ → Si + 2H₂ at 580–650 °C.
- ✅ SiO₂ CVD: SiH₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2H₂.
- ✅ Si₃N₄ LPCVD: 3 SiH₂Cl₂ + 4 NH₃ → Si₃N₄ + 6 HCl + 6 H₂.
- ✅ CVD provides **excellent step coverage and conformality**.
- ✅ PECVD uses plasma to reduce T but introduces hydrogen into films.

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ✅ **Adatom energy** determines film density, adhesion, and damage.
- ✅ **Higher T** → better crystallinity but more interdiffusion.
- ✅ **Lower P** → longer mean free path → more directional deposition.
- ✅ Industry-preferred: **Magnetron sputtering** for metals, **LPCVD/PECVD** for dielectrics, **MBE/MOCVD** for compound semiconductors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

**Bulk Micromachining** is a top-down microfabrication technique that involves the **selective removal of significant portions of the bulk substrate material** (typically monocrystalline silicon) through chemical (wet/dry) or physical etching processes to create three-dimensional microstructures such as membranes, cantilevers, beams, cavities, and V-grooves. It is distinguished from surface micromachining in that the structural elements are sculpted directly out of the substrate itself, rather than built up from deposited thin-film layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk micromachining is treated as a foundational process under the MEMS fabrication module. Expect direct questions on **wet anisotropic etching**, **etch-stop mechanisms**, and **crystallographic orientation effects** during ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice-Cube Sculptor" Mental Model

Imagine you have a perfectly clear, single-crystal ice cube (your silicon wafer). Now suppose you want to carve a tiny pyramid-shaped pit into its top surface.

- If you splash water on it uniformly from all sides, the ice melts **equally in every direction** — the pit becomes a smooth, rounded bowl. This is **isotropic etching**.
- However, if you use a special chemical that "prefers" to melt along certain internal planes of the ice much faster than others, you end up with a **perfect pyramid with sharp, flat triangular faces**. This is **anisotropic etching**.

The "preferred planes" of silicon are its **crystallographic planes** — most notably the (111) plane, which etches roughly 100–400 times slower than the (100) or (110) planes in alkaline solutions. Bulk micromachining exploits this crystal asymmetry to sculpt atomic-precision geometries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction to Memorize:**
> - **Bulk Micromachining** → Carves *into* the substrate (subtractive).
> - **Surface Micromachining** → Builds *on top of* the substrate (additive/sacrificial layer).
> - **LIGA / Deep RIE** → Hybrid / non-crystallographic bulk sculpting.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Silicon lattice constant ($a$) | **0.543 nm** | FCC diamond cubic structure |
| Density of Si (ρ) | **2.33 g/cm³** | Lightweight MEMS substrate |
| Standard wafer thickness | **525 µm (4-inch), 725 µm (6-inch)** | Mechanical handle layer |
| Typical (111) etch rate ratio | **1 : 100 to 1 : 400** | Relative to (100) in KOH |
| Common etchant temperature | **60 °C – 90 °C** | For KOH-based baths |
| Etch rate activation energy ($E_a$) | **0.6 – 0.8 eV** | Arrhenius-type behavior |
| Typical mask film (SiO₂) thickness | **0.5 – 2 µm** | Thermal or LPCVD oxide |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown of Bulk Micromachining

The bulk micromachining process follows a structured sequence of engineering decisions, each governed by crystallography, chemistry, and process kinetics.

### Step 1 — Wafer Selection and Crystallographic Orientation
The choice of wafer orientation directly determines the final etched geometry.
- **(100)-oriented wafer** → produces **square/rectangular pits with 54.74° sloped sidewalls** bounded by (111) planes.
- **(110)-oriented wafer** → produces **vertical sidewalls** normal to the surface, ideal for deep trenches and comb drives.
- **(111)-oriented wafer** → produces **uniform shallow planar etching**; minimal undercutting; used for ultra-thin membranes.

### Step 2 — Mask Deposition and Patterning
A chemically resistant layer (mask) is deposited and photolithographically patterned.
- **Thermal SiO₂** is the classical mask for KOH and TMAH etchants.
- **LPCVD silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)** offers superior masking against HF and TMAH.
- **Gold / Platinum** films mask against nearly all wet etchants but require careful adhesion layers (Cr or Ti).

### Step 3 — Etching Step (Wet or Dry)
The exposed silicon is dissolved either chemically (wet) or via reactive ion bombardment (dry).

### Step 4 — Etch Stop Detection
The etch is terminated at a predetermined depth using one of several mechanisms:
- **Time etch (open-loop)**
- **Electrochemical etch stop** (pn-junction biased)
- **Doping-selective etch stop** (heavily boron-doped Si ≈ 10¹⁹ – 10²⁰ cm⁻³)
- **Insulating layer etch stop** (buried oxide in SOI wafers)

## 2.2 Anisotropic Wet Etching — Detailed Mechanics

The classical alkaline etchant **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** in water + IPA follows an **Arrhenius temperature dependence**:

$$R(T) = R_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

Where:
- $R(T)$ → etch rate at absolute temperature $T$ (µm/hr or µm/min)
- $R_0$ → pre-exponential frequency factor
- $E_a$ → activation energy of the etch reaction (eV)
- $k_B$ → Boltzmann constant = **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**

## 2.3 The Famous 54.74° Sidewall Angle — Geometric Derivation

For a (100) silicon wafer etched through a square mask opening, the (111) planes emerge as the slanted sidewalls. The angle between the (100) surface normal and the (111) plane is given by:

$$\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356°$$

This is a constant, **independent of etch time or etchant chemistry**, and is the geometric reason why KOH-etched pits on (100) silicon always have identically sloped walls.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arrhenius etch rate | $R = R_0 e^{-E_a / k_B T}$ | µm/hr | Predict etch rate vs temperature |
| 2 | V-groove apex depth | $d = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \cot(54.74°)$ | µm | Final membrane thickness calc |
| 3 | Undercut distance (isotropic) | $d_u = R_{iso} \cdot t_{etch}$ | µm | Wet HF isotropic undercut |
| 4 | Selectivity ratio | $S = R_{Si} / R_{mask}$ | dimensionless | Mask degradation estimate |
| 5 | (111) interplanar angle | $\theta = \arccos(1/\sqrt{3})$ | degrees | 54.74° fixed sidewall |
| 6 | Doping etch stop conc. | $N_B \geq 5 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³ | atoms/cm³ | Boron etch stop threshold |
| 7 | Mass dissolved | $m = \rho \cdot V_{etched}$ | grams | Bath lifetime / contamination |
| 8 | Open mask etch depth | $z = R_\perp \cdot t$ | µm | Vertical depth of pit |
| 9 | Crystal plane (hkl) family spacing | $d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}$ | nm | Identify etch planes |
| 10 | Diffusion-limited rate (isotropic) | $R = \frac{D \cdot C_s}{\delta}$ | µm/s | HF/HNO₃ kinetics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Tip:** When writing etch-rate problems, **always quote units explicitly** (µm/min vs µm/hr). The most common mark-losing error is mixing minutes and hours in the same derivation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bulk-micromachined structures form the **mechanical backbone** of nearly every commercial MEMS device:

- **Pressure sensors** → bulk-etched silicon diaphragms with piezoresistive bridges (Bosch, Honeywell, Freescale).
- **Inertial sensors (accelerometers, gyroscopes)** → proof masses suspended by bulk-etched flexure beams (STMicroelectronics, Analog Devices ADXL series).
- **Inkjet printer heads** → bulk-etched nozzle arrays on (100) Si (Hewlett-Packard, Canon).
- **Microfluidic channels** → buried-channel bulk-micromachined chips for lab-on-a-chip diagnostics.
- **Solar cell texturing** → KOH pyramidal texturing of (100) Si for light trapping.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The 54.74° Sidewall Angle from Miller Indices

**Given:** A monocrystalline silicon wafer with surface plane (100) and sidewall family {111}.

**To Prove:** The angle between the (100) surface normal and any of the {111} planes is 54.74°.

**Step 1 — Identify the two plane normals:**
- Surface plane: $(h_1 k_1 l_1) = (1, 0, 0)$ → unit normal $\vec{n_1} = (1, 0, 0)$
- Sidewall plane: $(h_2 k_2 l_2) = (1, 1, 1)$ → unit normal $\vec{n_2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1, 1, 1)$

**Step 2 — Apply the interplanar angle formula for cubic crystals:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{h_1 h_2 + k_1 k_2 + l_1 l_2}{\sqrt{h_1^2 + k_1^2 + l_1^2} \cdot \sqrt{h_2^2 + k_2^2 + l_2^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Miller indices:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{(1)(1) + (0)(1) + (0)(1)}{\sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} \cdot \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify numerator and denominator:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{1 + 0 + 0}{\sqrt{1} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Take the inverse cosine:**

$$\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356°$$

**Conclusion:** This is the **fundamental geometric constant** of silicon bulk micromachining. It dictates that every V-groove, pyramidal pit, or anisotropically etched cavity on a (100) wafer has identically sloped sidewalls at this precise angle.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — V-Groove Apex Depth from Mask Opening

**Given:** A square mask opening of side $W$ aligned along $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions on a (100) silicon wafer. The etch proceeds until the (111) planes intersect beneath the mask, forming a complete V-groove.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the cross-section:**
The etched V-groove is bounded by four (111) planes meeting at a single apex point on the (100) surface plane.

**Step 2 — Set up the right triangle:**
The half-width of the mask is $W/2$, and the apex depth is $d$. The (111) sidewall makes an angle of $54.74°$ with the vertical.

**Step 3 — Relate width and depth using trigonometry:**

$$\tan(54.74°) = \frac{W/2}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$:**

$$d = \frac{W/2}{\tan(54.74°)} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \cot(54.74°)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$\cot(54.74°) = \frac{1}{\tan(54.74°)} = \frac{1}{1.4142} \approx 0.7071$$

Therefore:

$$d = \frac{W}{2} \cdot 0.7071 = \frac{W}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot W$$

**Conclusion:** For a **100 µm wide** square mask opening on a (100) wafer, the V-groove reaches its apex at a depth of approximately **70.7 µm** beneath the original surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that **d = W/√2** holds only for masks aligned along $\langle 110\rangle$ directions. A misaligned mask produces an asymmetric, "tilted" pit, not a clean V-groove.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Behavior of KOH Etch Rate

**Given:** Two known etch rates of (100) silicon in 30 wt% KOH at two different temperatures:

- At $T_1 = 60°C = 333.15$ K, $R_1 = 0.6$ µm/min
- At $T_2 = 80°C = 353.15$ K, $R_2 = 1.8$ µm/min

**Find:** The activation energy $E_a$ of the etch process.

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius equation for both temperatures:**

$$R_1 = R_0 \, e^{-E_a / k_B T_1} \quad ; \quad R_2 = R_0 \, e^{-E_a / k_B T_2}$$

**Step 2 — Divide $R_2$ by $R_1$ to eliminate $R_0$:**

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = e^{-E_a / k_B T_2 + E_a / k_B T_1} = e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E_a$:**

$$E_a = k_B \cdot \frac{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution:**

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{1.8}{0.6} = 3.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(3.0) = 1.0986$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{333.15} - \frac{1}{353.15} = 3.0017 \times 10^{-3} - 2.8318 \times 10^{-3} = 1.699 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation:**

$$E_a = (8.617 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV/K}) \cdot \frac{1.0986}{1.699 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{9.467 \times 10^{-5}}{1.699 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.557 \text{ eV}$$

**Conclusion:** The activation energy ≈ **0.56 eV**, consistent with literature values for KOH etching of (100) silicon (typically 0.5 – 0.7 eV).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Anisotropic Etchants

| Property | KOH | TMAH | EDP | CsOH |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Si (100) etch rate | **1.4 µm/min @ 80°C** | 1.0 µm/min @ 80°C | 0.6 µm/min @ 110°C | 1.0 µm/min @ 50°C |
| Si (111) etch rate | 0.005 µm/min | 0.01 µm/min | 0.02 µm/min | 0.005 µm/min |
| Selectivity (100)/(111) | **400 : 1** | 100 : 1 | 30 : 1 | 200 : 1 |
| CMOS compatible? | **No (K⁺ contamination)** | **Yes** | No (toxic) | No |
| Mask material | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ | SiO₂, Si₃N₄, Al | SiO₂ | SiO₂ |
| Toxicity | Moderate | Low | **High (carcinogenic)** | Low |
| Surface roughness | Smooth | Very smooth | Smooth | Smooth |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define bulk micromachining. How does it differ fundamentally from surface micromachining?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bulk micromachining is a subtractive microfabrication process in which three-dimensional microstructures are formed by the **selective removal of bulk substrate material** (typically silicon) using anisotropic or isotropic etching through a patterned mask. The structural elements — such as membranes, cantilevers, and V-grooves — are carved *directly out of* the wafer itself. (2 marks)

In contrast, **surface micromachining** is an additive process in which structural and sacrificial thin films are **deposited and patterned layer-by-layer** on top of the substrate; the final structure is *built up* rather than carved down. Surface micromachining allows finer lateral features and lower process temperatures, whereas bulk micromachining produces thicker, more robust mechanical structures with precisely defined crystallographic sidewalls. (1 mark)

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why does potassium hydroxide (KOH) etch silicon anisotropically, and what is the role of the (111) plane?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
KOH aqueous solution etches silicon anisotropically because the **rate of chemical attack depends on the atomic packing density and the number of available dangling bonds** on each crystallographic plane. The (111) plane has the highest atomic density and only one dangling bond per surface atom, making it chemically the most resistant. (2 marks)

The (100) and (110) planes expose two dangling bonds per atom, making them far more reactive. As a result, when KOH etches a (100) wafer through a mask opening, the (111) planes are revealed as slow-etching sidewalls, producing a **54.74° sloped V-groove or pyramidal pit**. The (111) plane thus acts as the **natural etch-stop and sidewall-defining plane** in silicon bulk micromachining. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the crystallographic basis of anisotropic wet etching of silicon. Derive the **54.74° sidewall angle** for a (100) wafer etched along a square mask aligned to the $\langle 110\rangle$ direction. Comment on the role of the etchant in determining etch rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the etching characteristics of **KOH, TMAH, and EDP** for silicon micromachining. Which one is preferred for **CMOS-compatible post-processing**, and why? Discuss the safety and selectivity trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Crystallographic basis (2 marks):**
Silicon has a diamond cubic crystal structure with lattice constant $a = 0.543$ nm. Different crystallographic planes (hkl) have different atomic densities and numbers of broken (dangling) bonds per surface atom. KOH attacks surfaces by nucleophilic attack of OH⁻ ions on the Si–H bonds. The (111) plane is the **densest packed** with **one dangling bond per atom** → etches slowly. The (100) plane has **two dangling bonds per atom** → etches 100–400 times faster. This differential forms the basis of anisotropy.

**Step 2 — Derivation of the 54.74° angle (3 marks):**

The angle between two crystallographic planes $(h_1 k_1 l_1)$ and $(h_2 k_2 l_2)$ in a cubic system is:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{h_1 h_2 + k_1 k_2 + l_1 l_2}{\sqrt{h_1^2+k_1^2+l_1^2} \cdot \sqrt{h_2^2+k_2^2+l_2^2}}$$

Substituting (100) and (111):

$$\cos\theta = \frac{1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1}{\sqrt{1} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$\theta = \arccos(1/\sqrt{3}) = 54.7356°$$

This is the **fixed interplanar angle** that defines all KOH-etched sidewalls on (100) silicon.

**Step 3 — Role of the etchant (2 marks):**
KOH concentration, temperature, and the addition of isopropyl alcohol (IPA) strongly affect the absolute etch rate. Higher KOH concentration increases OH⁻ activity but lowers the Si/Al₂O₃ selectivity; IPA reduces bubble formation on the surface, leading to smoother sidewalls. The **activation energy** $E_a$ (≈ 0.6 eV) determines the Arrhenius temperature sensitivity, with etch rate doubling roughly every 7–8°C rise in temperature within the 60–90°C working range.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Comparative description (3 marks):**
**KOH** is the most widely used etchant — cheap, high (100)/(111) selectivity (≈ 400:1), and reproducible. However, **K⁺ ions** are highly detrimental to CMOS gate oxides, contaminating the wafer permanently.

**TMAH (Tetramethyl Ammonium Hydroxide)** offers moderate selectivity (≈ 100:1), is **CMOS-compatible** (no mobile ion contamination), and produces extremely smooth surfaces. It is the **preferred etchant for post-CMOS MEMS integration**.

**EDP (Ethylene Diamine Pyrocatechol)** was historically popular for its high selectivity and ability to etch heavily boron-doped layers, but it is **highly toxic, carcinogenic, and degrades quickly with air exposure** — making it unsuitable for modern industrial or academic cleanrooms.

**Step 2 — Selection for CMOS compatibility (2 marks):**
