TMAH is preferred because it does not introduce alkali metal contamination, can etch through **Al bond pads** (with proper formulation), and is compatible with standard photoresist masks. The slightly lower selectivity vs KOH is acceptable for most MEMS post-processing applications, and modern TMAH formulations with added surfactant (e.g., Triton X-100) recover much of the lost selectivity.

**Step 3 — Safety and selectivity trade-off (2 marks):**
KOH offers the best selectivity but the worst contamination profile. EDP offers good selectivity but extreme toxicity. TMAH offers the best balance — low toxicity, high purity, and sufficient selectivity. The trade-off is that TMAH-etched surfaces are sometimes slightly rougher than KOH-etched surfaces, requiring post-etch polishing for optical MEMS applications.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **four major etch-stop techniques** used in silicon bulk micromachining, with a neat sketch of the **electrochemical etch-stop setup**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square mask opening of side **W = 200 µm** aligned along $\langle 110\rangle$ is used to etch a (100) silicon wafer in KOH until the V-groove apex is reached. Calculate **(i)** the depth of the V-groove, **(ii)** the slant length of each (111) sidewall, and **(iii)** the volume of silicon removed per V-groove. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Time etch stop (1 mark):**
The simplest method. The etch is timed based on a pre-calibrated etch rate. Drawback: etch rate variations (temperature, concentration) cause **depth uncertainty of 5–10%** in practical use.

**Step 2 — Doping-selective etch stop (2 marks):**
Heavily boron-doped silicon (≥ 5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³, ~1 atomic %) etches in KOH at < 1/1000th the rate of lightly doped Si. The boron atoms **passivate** the dangling bonds and act as an etch inhibitor. Used to form precision membranes, beams, and cantilever structures with sub-µm thickness control.

**Step 3 — Buried-oxide (SOI) etch stop (1 mark):**
Silicon-On-Insulator wafers have a buried SiO₂ layer. The wet etch (e.g., KOH or TMAH) stops naturally at the SiO₂ because the etch rate ratio Si:SiO₂ > 1000:1. Provides **atomically precise thickness control** but adds wafer cost.

**Step 4 — Electrochemical etch stop (3 marks):**
The wafer forms the **anode** of an electrochemical cell in an HF-based or KOH-based electrolyte. A four-point probe applies a voltage between the n-type epilayer (to be etched) and the p-type substrate. When the **pn-junction is reverse-biased**, the anodic dissolution of silicon is passivated, and etching ceases. The setup consists of:
- Working electrode: silicon wafer (anode)
- Counter electrode: Pt or carbon
- Reference electrode: Ag/AgCl or SCE
- Potentiostat controlling V between 0.2 – 0.5 V vs reference

This technique gives the **most precise etch-depth control** (better than ± 0.5 µm) and is standard in industrial pressure sensor fabrication.

**Sketch (textual description):**
A Pt counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode are immersed in the etchant; the silicon wafer is clamped on the backside with a metal contact; a potentiostat applies a controlled anodic potential. Etching proceeds in the n-region but is self-terminating at the pn-junction.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Square mask opening $W = 200$ µm on (100) Si, V-groove geometry, $\theta = 54.74°$.

**Part (i) — Depth of V-groove (2 marks):**

$$d = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \cot(54.74°) = \frac{200}{2} \cdot 0.7071 = 100 \times 0.7071 = 70.71 \text{ µm}$$

**Part (ii) — Slant length of each (111) sidewall (2 marks):**
The slant length $L$ is the hypotenuse of the right triangle:

$$L = \frac{W/2}{\sin(54.74°)} = \frac{100}{0.8165} = 122.47 \text{ µm}$$

**Part (iii) — Volume of silicon removed per V-groove (3 marks):**
The V-groove forms a **square pyramidal pit** with base side $W$ and height $d$:

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \cdot W^2 \cdot d$$

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \cdot (200)^2 \cdot 70.71 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 40000 \cdot 70.71$$

$$V = \frac{2{,}828{,}400}{3} = 942{,}800 \text{ µm}^3$$

Convert to mm³ (since 1 mm³ = 10⁹ µm³):

$$V = 9.428 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^3 \approx 0.000943 \text{ mm}^3$$

Equivalently, the mass of silicon dissolved:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 2.33 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 9.428 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}^3 = 2.20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g} = 2.20 \text{ µg}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** µm to cm before multiplying by ρ → order-of-magnitude error in mass.
> 2. **Using θ = 45°** instead of 54.74° → causes a 22% error in depth.
> 3. **Mixing KOH isotropic vs anisotropic regimes** — KOH at very low concentration or very high temperature becomes more isotropic; always quote the chemistry.
> 4. **Ignoring mask undercut during long etches** — even with KOH, finite (111) etch rate causes slow lateral undercut; long etches (>4 hours) may need compensation.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the final numerical answer — KTU strictly deducts for missing µm or µm³ units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Bulk Micromachining**

- **Bulk micromachining = subtractive**, removes substrate to form 3D MEMS structures.
- **Anisotropic etchants** for Si: KOH, TMAH, EDP, CsOH, hydrazine.
- **Isotropic etchants**: HNA (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH), xenon difluoride (XeF₂, dry).
- The **54.74°** angle is the fixed interplanar angle between (100) and (111) planes.
- **(100) wafer + square mask → V-groove**; **(110) wafer → vertical sidewalls**; **(111) wafer → near-planar etching**.
- **Etch rate follows Arrhenius** with $E_a \approx 0.6$ eV for KOH.
- **Mask materials:** thermal SiO₂ (KOH, TMAH), LPCVD Si₃N₄ (HF, TMAH), Au/Pt (nearly universal).
- **Etch stops:** time, doping (B ≥ 5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³), buried oxide (SOI), electrochemical (pn-junction).
- **TMAH = preferred CMOS-compatible etchant**; KOH = highest selectivity.
- **Convex corner compensation** structures (e.g., (111)-aligned square tabs) are required at mask corners to prevent undercutting into a curved profile.
- **Wet etching is chemical/diffusion-controlled**; temperature is the dominant process variable.
- **DRIE (Deep Reactive Ion Etching)** is the dry-etch alternative — produces vertical sidewalls with aspect ratios > 50:1, used when crystallographic constraints are not acceptable.
- The **apex depth of a V-groove** is $d = W / \sqrt{2}$ when mask is aligned along $\langle 110\rangle$.
- **Applications:** pressure sensors, accelerometers, inkjet nozzles, solar cell texturing, microfluidic channels.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Micromachining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface Micromachining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Surface Micromachining** is a planar batch-fabrication process used in Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) where three-dimensional micromechanical structures are built up, layer by layer, on the *surface* of a single crystal silicon (or any compatible) substrate by alternating **deposition** of thin films and **selective etching** of an underlying *sacrificial* layer. The released structural layer remains suspended above the substrate by means of mechanical **anchor points** etched into the sacrificial layer prior to deposition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus**: The Board expects the student to clearly distinguish **Surface Micromachining** from **Bulk Micromachining** (Module 2). Surface micromachining NEVER etches the substrate; it only manipulates thin films added on top of it. The substrate is used purely as a passive carrier and mechanical ground.

The standard nomenclature accepted in KTU valuation is:

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Structural Layer** | The thin film that becomes the moving/suspended MEMS element (e.g., polysilicon, Si₃N₄). |
| **Sacrificial Layer** | A temporary film (e.g., SiO₂, PSG, photoresist) placed *underneath* the structural layer and later dissolved. |
| **Anchor** | A window opened in the sacrificial layer so the structural layer bonds directly to the substrate. |
| **Release** | The final wet/dry etch step that removes the sacrificial layer, freeing the structure. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Cake" Model

Imagine baking a multi-tier cake where:
- The **bottom plate (platter)** = the silicon wafer (substrate).
- A thin layer of **chocolate frosting** = the *sacrificial* oxide.
- A solid layer of **vanilla sponge** = the *structural* polysilicon film.
- **Windows cut into the chocolate frosting** before pouring the sponge = *anchor points* that pin the sponge to the platter.

Now, if you dissolve the chocolate frosting with warm syrup, the vanilla sponge stays in place only where it touches the platter (anchors), and everywhere else it is **free to flex, rotate, or translate** — forming cantilever beams, bridges, plates, and gears. That is precisely what happens in surface micromachining when the wafer is immersed in **HF (hydrofluoric acid)** to release the structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in Kerala's electronics industry?** Surface micromachining is the dominant technology behind accelerometers used in smartphones (ST Microelectronics, Bosch fabs), DLP mirror arrays (Texas Instruments), and gyroscopes. Every automobile airbag sensor and drone IMU contains a surface-micromachined proof mass suspended on a sacrificial oxide.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are *examinable* in numerical problems:

- Surface tension of water at 25 °C : **γ = 72.8 mN/m**
- Young's modulus of polysilicon (poly-Si) : **E ≈ 160 GPa**
- Young's modulus of LPCVD SiO₂ : **E ≈ 70 GPa**
- Poisson's ratio of poly-Si : **ν ≈ 0.22**
- Hamaker constant for SiO₂–SiO₂ across water : **A ≈ 6.5 × 10⁻²⁰ J**
- HF etch rate of thermal SiO₂ : **≈ 100 nm/min** (49 % HF, room temperature)
- HF etch rate of polysilicon : **≈ 1–2 nm/min** (very high selectivity)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Step Canonical Process Flow

Surface micromachining is always described in KTU answers as a closed loop of six operations. The examiner looks for the order, not memorised jargon.

1. **Substrate Preparation** — A clean, oxidized single-crystal silicon wafer (typically a thermally grown SiO₂ underlayer acts as the *first* isolation/sacrificial layer).
2. **Sacrificial Layer Deposition & Patterning** — Conformal deposition (LPCVD, PECVD, or spin-coated photoresist) followed by photolithography and etching to open **anchor windows**.
3. **Structural Layer Deposition** — Conformal deposition of the mechanical film (poly-Si, Si₃N₄, SiC, Au, Ni) directly over the patterned sacrificial film. Inside the anchor windows the structural film bonds permanently to the substrate.
4. **Structural Layer Patterning** — Photolithography + Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) to shape beams, plates, gears, comb fingers.
5. **Release Etch** — Immersion in HF (or BOE, or vapour HF) to dissolve the sacrificial oxide **without attacking the structural layer**. **Selectivity** is the key metric (etch-rate ratio = etch rate of sacrificial / etch rate of structural).
6. **Drying & Anti-Stiction Treatment** — Critical step. Supercritical CO₂ drying or sublimation drying or HMDS vapour treatment is used to prevent the released structures from collapsing onto the substrate.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** If the question asks "why is the structural layer deposited *after* anchor patterning," the answer is: to enable mechanical interlocking and avoid undercut of the anchor. A structure deposited before opening anchors would have no contact region.

## 2.2 Why Sacrificial Layers?

The sacrificial layer must satisfy three simultaneous engineering constraints:

- **High etch selectivity** over the structural layer (≥ 100 : 1 is preferred).
- **Compatible deposition temperature** — must not melt or stress the previously deposited layers.
- **Compatible thermal expansion** — to prevent stress-induced cracking or buckling during deposition.

Common sacrificial / structural pairs used in industry:

| Sacrificial Material | Compatible Structural Material | Release Etchant |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal / LPCVD SiO₂ | Polysilicon | HF, BOE, vapour HF |
| Phosphosilicate glass (PSG) | Polysilicon | HF (high etch rate due to P content) |
| Undoped poly-Si | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ | KOH, TMAH, EDP |
| Photoresist (AZ series) | Polyimide, Au, Cu | Acetone, NMP |
| Copper | Nickel, Poly-Si | Nitric acid (selective) |
| Polysilicon | SiC, Si₃N₄ | KOH / TMAH |

## 2.3 Stiction — The Arch-Enemy of Surface Micromachining

Stiction (static friction-induced adhesion) is the *single biggest yield-killer* in released MEMS structures. The KTU Board allocates **3–4 marks** to this topic in any module-3 question. It has **four** physical origins:

| Mechanism | Trigger Condition | Force Equation | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capillary (meniscus) stiction** | Liquid trapped during wet release | $F_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Dominant |
| **van der Waals adhesion** | Atomic contact after drying | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H A}{12 \pi d_0^2}$ | Moderate |
| **Electrostatic stiction** | Residual charge on dielectric | $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{d^2}$ | Severe at low d |
| **Casimir / quantum stiction** | Sub-100 nm gaps | $F_c = \dfrac{\pi^2 \hbar c A}{240 d^4}$ | Negligible > 200 nm |

Where:
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the rinse liquid.
- $A$ = contact area between structure and substrate.
- $d$ = gap (sacrificial-layer thickness).
- $\theta$ = contact angle of liquid on structural surface.
- $A_H$ = Hamaker constant.
- $d_0$ ≈ 0.3–0.5 nm = atomic equilibrium separation.
- $\hbar$ = reduced Planck constant, $c$ = speed of light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-stiction strategies tested in KTU:** (i) **Supercritical CO₂ drying** (no liquid–vapour interface, hence no meniscus), (ii) **Vapour-phase HF release** (no rinse liquid at all), (iii) **Self-Assembled Monolayers (SAMs)** like octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) or perfluorodecyltrichlorosilane, (iv) **Geometric dimples** etched into the underside of the structure so only a tiny area contacts the substrate, (v) **Stiction bumps / dimples**, (vi) **Hydrophobic HMDS priming**.

## 2.4 Residual Stress in Structural Films

Two stresses develop during thin-film deposition:

- **Intrinsic stress** $\sigma_i$ : due to grain boundary relaxation, void formation, and atomic peening during sputtering/LPCVD.
- **Thermal stress** $\sigma_T$ : due to mismatch in coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) between film and substrate upon cooling from deposition temperature $T_{dep}$ to operating temperature $T_{op}$:

$$\sigma_T = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)(T_{op} - T_{dep})$$

Total stress: $\sigma_{tot} = \sigma_i + \sigma_T$.

- **Tensile stress** (positive) keeps beams straight and taut.
- **Compressive stress** (negative) causes buckling when it exceeds the **Euler critical stress**:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(L/t)^2}$$

For a fixed-free cantilever beam of length $L$, thickness $t$ and width $w$ :

$$\sigma_{cr}^{cantilever} = \dfrac{(1.38)\, E\, t^2}{L^2} \approx \dfrac{\pi^2 E t^2}{4 L^2}$$

A typical design rule to avoid buckling in KTU problems: $\dfrac{L}{t} \le 10$ for unreleased poly-Si cantilevers.

## 2.5 Step Coverage & Conformality

A conformal film must deposit uniformly on **sidewalls, corners and the bottom of deep trenches** with aspect ratio $AR = \text{depth}/\text{width}$. Two metrics are used:

$$\text{Step coverage} = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_{top}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Bottom coverage} = \dfrac{t_{bottom}}{t_{top}} \times 100\%$$

LPCVD gives near-100 % conformality; PECVD gives ≈ 60–80 %; evaporated films give ≈ 5–10 % (very poor sidewall coverage).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the *single most important rapid-revision sheet* for Module 3. Memorise the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Validity / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capillary stiction force | $F_c = \dfrac{2\gamma A\cos\theta}{d^2}$ | N | $d \le 1\,\mu m$ wet release |
| 2 | Laplace pressure across meniscus | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{d}$ | Pa | Parallel-plate approximation |
| 3 | van der Waals adhesion force | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H A}{12\pi d_0^2}$ | N | Contact separation $d_0 \approx 0.3$ nm |
| 4 | Hamaker constant (Lifshitz approx.) | $A_H \approx \dfrac{3}{4}k_B T\Big(\dfrac{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_3}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_3}\Big)^2$ | J | For two identical materials 1 across medium 3 |
| 5 | Thermal film stress | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$ | Pa | $\Delta T = T_{op} - T_{dep}$ |
| 6 | Euler buckling stress (cantilever) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{4}\Big(\dfrac{t}{L}\Big)^2$ | Pa | Long, slender beam |
| 7 | Tip deflection of stress-gradient cantilever | $\delta = \dfrac{3L^2}{t}\dfrac{\Delta\alpha\,\Delta T}{2}$ | m | Bilayer bimetallic approximation |
| 8 | Mass of released proof mass | $m = \rho_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot t$ | kg | Rectangular plate |
| 9 | Natural frequency of cantilever | $f_1 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\dfrac{t}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$ | Hz | First flexural mode |
| 10 | Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{R_{sacr}}{R_{struct}}$ | – | Should be $> 100$ for viable release |
| 11 | Etch time for sacrificial layer | $t_{etch} = \dfrac{d_{sacr}}{R_{sacr}} \cdot \text{SF}$ | min | SF ≈ 1.5 safety factor |
| 12 | Release undercut (isotropic etch) | $u = R_{sacr} \cdot t_{etch}$ | m | Lateral etch = vertical etch for wet release |
| 13 | Gas-flow conductance through channel | $Q = \dfrac{w\, h^3}{12 \mu L}\Delta P$ | m³/s | Slip flow regime, $h$ = channel height |
| 14 | Comb-finger electrostatic force | $F = \dfrac{n\, \varepsilon_0\, L\, t}{d}V^2$ | N | $n$ = finger pairs, $d$ = gap |
| 15 | Damping coefficient (squeeze film) | $b = \dfrac{\mu\, L\, w^3}{d^3}$ | N·s/m | $w \gg d$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Surface-Micromachined Element | Industry User |
|---|---|---|
| Accelerometer (airbag, smartphone) | Suspended proof mass + interdigitated comb fingers | ST Microelectronics (Italy), Bosch, Analog Devices |
| Gyroscope (drone, IMU) | Vibrating ring/frame driven by comb drive | InvenSense (TDK), Murata |
| DLP® projector | 17 µm tilting micromirror array (13.7 million mirrors) | Texas Instruments |
| Inkjet printhead | Cantilever / heater plate firing ink bubble | HP, Canon, Epson |
| RF MEMS switch | Suspended gold membrane over CPW line | Radant MEMS, DelfMEMS |
| Bio-MEMS cantilever | Functionalised micro-cantilever for biosensing | Universitat Barcelona, CEA-Leti |
| Pressure sensor (high range) | Bossed diaphragm (poly-Si on Si) | Honeywell |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Note**: For every derivation below, **state the starting physical principle, write the mathematical form, simplify step-by-step, and finally substitute numerical values**. Skipping any one of these four steps costs **at least 2 marks** in the KTU ESE.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capillary Stiction Force Between a Released Beam and the Substrate

**Starting principle:** When a thin liquid film is trapped between the underside of a suspended microbeam (length $L$, width $w$, gap to substrate $d$) and the silicon substrate, a concave meniscus forms at the perimeter. The meniscus exerts a Laplace pressure on the beam, pulling it toward the substrate.

### Step 1 — Laplace Pressure Across a Curved Interface

For a meniscus with two principal radii of curvature $R_1$ and $R_2$, the pressure difference between the liquid and the vapour is given by the **Young–Laplace equation**:

$$\Delta P = \gamma \Big(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\Big)$$

For a parallel-plate geometry, the meniscus is cylindrical in the transverse direction ($R_1 \to \infty$) and curved in the normal direction ($R_2 = R$, equal to the gap for zero contact angle):

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{\gamma}{R} = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{d}$$

The factor of 2 arises because the liquid wets both the top of the substrate and the bottom of the beam, doubling the curvature contribution.

### Step 2 — Force on the Beam

The pressure acts uniformly over the contact (or near-contact) area of the beam, $A = L \cdot w$. The total downward capillary force is:

$$F_c = \Delta P \cdot A = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{d}\, L\, w$$

### Step 3 — Equivalently via Surface Energy Minimisation

By minimising the surface energy of the liquid bridge, an alternative expression treating the beam as a flexible plate of bending stiffness $D$ gives the effective stiffness of the meniscus $k_m$:

$$k_m = \dfrac{2\gamma L w \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

Hence the maximum attractive force, when the spring restoring force of the beam $k_{beam}\,\delta$ equals $F_c$:

$$F_{c,max} = k_m \cdot \delta = \dfrac{2\gamma L w \cos\theta}{d^2}\cdot \delta$$

For full deflection down to the substrate ($\delta = d$):

$$\boxed{F_c = \dfrac{2\gamma\, L\, w\, \cos\theta}{d}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Sanity Check (KTU Standard)

Take a 100 µm long, 2 µm wide poly-Si beam, gap $d = 2$ µm, $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting), in water ($\gamma = 72.8$ mN/m):

$$F_c = \dfrac{2 \times 72.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_c = \dfrac{2 \times 72.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_c = 14.56 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N} = 14.56\,\mu\text{N}$$

Compare this with the elastic restoring force of the same beam (tip spring constant $k \approx 0.05$ N/m × 2 µm deflection = $10^{-7}$ N = 0.1 µN). The capillary force is **two orders of magnitude larger**, which is precisely why the beam collapses irreversibly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Euler Critical Buckling Stress of a Surface-Micromachined Cantilever

**Starting principle:** A long, slender cantilever beam under axial compressive load $P$ deflects laterally. When $P$ exceeds the Euler critical load $P_{cr}$, the beam buckles and the deflection grows without bound.

### Step 1 — Beam Equation with Axial Load

For a beam of bending stiffness $EI$ with an axial compressive load $P$, the deflected shape $y(x)$ satisfies:

$$EI\,\dfrac{d^4 y}{dx^4} + P\,\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Boundary Conditions for a Cantilever

Fixed at $x = 0$ (anchor): $y(0) = 0$, $y'(0) = 0$.
Free at $x = L$: $y''(L) = 0$, $y'''(L) = 0$.

### Step 3 — Trial Solution

Assume $y(x) = A\cos(kx) + B\sin(kx) + Cx + D$ with $k^2 = P/EI$. Applying all four boundary conditions yields the transcendental equation:

$$\cos(kL) + 1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kL = \pi,\, 3\pi,\, 5\pi,\dots$$

The lowest (fundamental) buckling mode gives $kL = \pi$, hence:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Critical Stress

Dividing by the cross-sectional area $A_c = w\,t$ and noting that the second moment of area for a rectangular cross-section is $I = \dfrac{wt^3}{12}$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A_c} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E t^2}{12 L^2}$$

Accounting for the fact that a *built-in* cantilever is effectively a propped structure, the corrected expression widely accepted in MEMS texts is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{4}\Big(\dfrac{t}{L}\Big)^2}$$

### Step 5 — Design Implication

For poly-Si ($E = 160$ GPa), if the cantilever length is $L = 100$ µm and thickness $t = 2$ µm:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 160 \times 10^9}{4}\Big(\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}}\Big)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{1.579 \times 10^{12}}{4} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 1.58 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Pa} = 158\,\text{MPa}$$

Any residual compressive stress above this value will buckle the beam — a frequent KTU numerical.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Release Time of a Surface-Micromachined Cantilever from a Sacrificial SiO₂ Layer

**Starting principle:** The lateral etch of the sacrificial layer proceeds at a constant etch rate $R$ in all directions. The etch must travel a distance $L$ from the nearest anchor (or release hole) to fully free the beam.

### Step 1 — Geometry

Consider a cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, supported only at one end. Etchant enters from the free end and side, dissolving the SiO₂ isotropically at rate $R_{ox}$.

### Step 2 — Time Required

The longest distance the etchant must travel is the full cantilever length $L$:

$$t_{release} = \dfrac{L}{R_{ox}}$$

### Step 3 — Applying the Safety Factor

To ensure full release even with non-uniformities, etch rate variation with temperature, and gas-bubble masking, a safety factor $SF \in [1.3,\, 2.0]$ is multiplied:

$$\boxed{t_{release} = SF \cdot \dfrac{L}{R_{ox}}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

For $L = 200$ µm, $R_{ox} = 1$ µm/min in 49 % HF (typical), $SF = 1.5$:

$$t_{release} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{200}{1} = 300\,\text{min} = 5\,\text{h}$$

This shows why sacrificial wet releases of large membranes can take many hours — a KTU favourite calculation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Anti-Stiction Design: Critical Dimple Area

**Starting principle:** If the structural layer has periodic **dimples** of diameter $d_{dimple}$ and pitch $p$ etched into its underside, only the dimples touch the substrate. The capillary force is reduced by the ratio of dimple contact area to total area.

### Step 1 — Total Capillary Force Without Dimples

$$F_{c,0} = \dfrac{2\gamma A \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

### Step 2 — Contact Area With Dimples

If $N$ dimples of area $a_d = \pi d_{dimple}^2/4$ are present in total area $A$:

$$A_{contact} = N \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_{dimple}^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Reduced Force

$$F_{c,dimple} = \dfrac{2\gamma A_{contact} \cos\theta}{d^2} = F_{c,0} \cdot \dfrac{N \pi d_{dimple}^2}{4A}$$

### Step 4 — Design Rule of Thumb

To keep $F_{c,dimple} < 0.05 F_{c,0}$:

$$\dfrac{N \pi d_{dimple}^2}{4A} < 0.05 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{area coverage} < 5\%$$

Hence dimples typically cover only **1–3 %** of the underside area.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Surface Micromachining Problems

```
Is the material to be released sensitive to HF?
   ├── YES → Use PSG (etch rate in HF ≈ 10× thermal oxide)
   └── NO  → Use thermal SiO₂
Is the gap > 5 µm?
   ├── YES → Sacrificial photoresist (SU-8, polyimide) + O2 plasma release
   └── NO  → Conformal LPCVD SiO₂ + HF release
Are sub-100 nm gaps required?
   ├── YES → SACM (Silicon-Aluminium-CMOS-MEMS) or vapour HF
   └── NO  → Standard wet HF + supercritical drying
Does the structure have large released area (> 500 × 500 µm²)?
   ├── YES → Add etch holes every 50 µm + dimples
   └── NO  → Single release hole at free end
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process-Flow Diagram — Generic Surface Micromachining Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Clean silicon wafer with thermal oxide base layer] --> B[Step 2: Deposit sacrificial layer SiO2 by LPCVD]
    B --> C[Step 3: Photolithography and etch anchor windows]
    C --> D[Step 4: Deposit structural layer polysilicon by LPCVD]
    D --> E[Step 5: Photolithography and RIE etch structural layer into beams and plates]
    E --> F[Step 6: Wet etch in HF removes sacrificial SiO2 releasing the structure]
    F --> G[Step 7: Supercritical CO2 drying or HMDS anti-stiction treatment]
    G --> H[Released MEMS device suspended above substrate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Surface-Micromachined Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph substrate[Silicon Substrate Ground Plane]
        gnd[Anchor Contact and Electrical Ground]
    end
    subgraph suspension[Suspension Beams]
        b1[Tether Beam 1 with Folded Flexure]
        b2[Tether Beam 2 with Folded Flexure]
    end
    subgraph inertial[Inertial Proof Mass]
        pm[Central Proof Mass with Anti Stiction Dimples]
    end
    subgraph capacitive[Capacitive Sense Electrodes]
        cf[Fixed Comb Fingers Anchored to Substrate]
        cm[Moving Comb Fingers Attached to Proof Mass]
    end
    gnd --> b1
    gnd --> b2
    b1 --> pm
    b2 --> pm
    pm --> cm
    cf -. capacitive gap .-> cm
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree for Choosing Sacrificial-Release Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose Release Strategy] --> q1{Is HF compatible with all layers?}
    q1 -- YES --> q2{Is gap larger than 2 micrometre?}
    q1 -- NO --> q3[Use photoresist sacrificial and oxygen plasma release]
    q2 -- YES --> q4[Wet HF with isopropanol rinse]
    q2 -- NO --> q5[Vapour phase HF with no rinse liquid]
    q4 --> q6[Supercritical CO2 drying]
    q5 --> q7[No stiction treatment needed]
    q6 --> done[Released and dried structure]
    q7 --> done
    q3 --> q8[Critical point dryer required]
    q8 --> done
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Stiction-Mechanism Taxonomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stiction[Stiction Failure of Released MEMS] --> cap[Capillary Stiction]
    stiction --> vdw[van der Waals Stiction]
    stiction --> elec[Electrostatic Stiction]
    stiction --> cas[Casimir Quantum Stiction]
    cap --> capfix[Supercritical drying and vapour HF release]
    vdw --> vdwfix[Self Assembled Monolayer SAM coating]
    elec --> elecfix[Discharge annealing and grounded shielding]
    cas --> casfix[Increase gap beyond 200 nm]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Cross-Section Schematic of a Released Cantilever Beam

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph crosssec[Cross Section of Released Cantilever]
        L[Released polysilicon beam at top]
        G[Air gap equals sacrificial layer thickness]
        S[Silicon substrate at bottom]
        A[Anchor region where beam joins substrate on left side]
        D[Anti stiction dimples protruding downward from beam underside]
    end
    L --> G
    G --> S
    L --> A
    L --> D
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Q: Differentiate between bulk micromachining and surface micromachining with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full marks requires clear table format):**

| Aspect | Bulk Micromachining | Surface Micromachining |
|---|---|---|
| Substrate role | Substrate is the functional material (etched away to form the structure) | Substrate is a passive mechanical ground; structures are built on top using thin films |
| Direction of etching | Anisotropic (typically into the depth of the wafer) | Lateral (undercut of a sacrificial layer) |
| Mask material | LPCVD Si₃N₄ or thermally grown SiO₂ | Photoresist (sacrificial + structural patterned independently) |
| Aspect ratio | High (deep cavities, membranes) | Low (typical gap ≤ 5 µm) |
| Typical release | KOH, TMAH, EDP, DRIE (Bosch process) | HF, BOE, vapour HF |
| Example 1 | Silicon pressure-sensor diaphragm | Polysilicon accelerometer proof mass |
| Example 2 | Bulk PZT inkjet chamber | DLP micromirror torsional hinge |

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q: What is sacrificial layer? Why must it have high etch selectivity with respect to the structural layer? Give two examples of sacrificial–structural pairs.**

**Model Answer:**

A **sacrificial layer** is a temporary thin film deposited under the structural layer that is selectively dissolved during the *release etch* to free the moving MEMS element.

It must have **high etch selectivity** ($S = R_{sacr}/R_{struct} \ge 100$ typically) because:
1. During release the structural layer is exposed to the etchant for the *entire* release time (often hours). Even a small non-zero etch rate will damage, thin, or completely destroy the structural layer.
2. Selectivity directly determines the **maximum process window** — a low-selectivity pair forces a thin structural layer and short release distance, limiting design freedom.

Two standard sacrificial–structural pairs:
- **SiO₂ (sacrificial) + polysilicon (structural)** — released in HF; selectivity > 200.
- **PSG (sacrificial) + Si₃N₄ (structural)** — released in HF; PSG etches ~10× faster than thermal oxide.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe in detail the step-by-step process flow for fabricating a polysilicon surface-micromachined cantilever beam with silicon dioxide as the sacrificial layer. Include a labelled cross-section sketch.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wafer preparation (1 mark):**
Start with a 100 mm, <100>-oriented, p-type silicon wafer. Grow a 500 nm thermal SiO₂ isolation layer by wet oxidation at 1050 °C. This base oxide isolates the MEMS structures electrically from the substrate.

**Step 2 — Sacrificial layer deposition (1 mark):**
Deposit a 2 µm thick LPCVD SiO₂ film at 425 °C using TEOS or silane chemistry. This is the *sacrificial layer* that will eventually be removed.

**Step 3 — Anchor window definition (1 mark):**
Spin-coat photoresist (e.g., AZ 5214), soft-bake, expose through mask #1 using a contact aligner, develop in AZ 300 MIF. The mask pattern opens a single rectangular window 10 µm × 10 µm at one end of the future cantilever. Etch the exposed SiO₂ in a buffered HF (BOE 7:1) for 25 minutes to expose the underlying silicon. Strip resist in acetone + piranha.

**Step 4 — Structural layer deposition (1 mark):**
Deposit a 2 µm thick *in-situ* phosphorus-doped polysilicon film by LPCVD at 620 °C using SiH₄ + PH₃. The polysilicon fills the anchor window and bonds to the silicon substrate there, while conformally covering the sacrificial oxide everywhere else.

**Step 5 — Structural layer patterning (1 mark):**
Spin photoresist, expose through mask #2 (cantilever shape, e.g., 200 µm × 20 µm), develop. Etch polysilicon in an RIE using SF₆/O₂ or Cl₂/HBr chemistry until the sacrificial SiO₂ is exposed. Strip resist.

**Step 6 — Release etch (1 mark):**
Immerse the wafer in 49 % HF for 20 minutes at room temperature. The HF attacks the SiO₂ isotropically and etches laterally under the cantilever, releasing it. The HF selectivity over polysilicon is > 200, so the beam is unaffected.

**Step 7 — Critical-point drying / anti-stiction (1 mark):**
Rinse in deionised water followed by isopropanol, then transfer to a critical-point dryer where CO₂ is taken around its critical point (31.1 °C, 7.4 MPa) — eliminating surface tension. Alternatively, expose the wafer to HMDS vapour to form a hydrophobic monolayer, preventing capillary collapse.

**Cross-section (described verbally for KTU answer sheet):**
- Bottom: silicon substrate.
- Above substrate at the anchor: thermal oxide (500 nm), then a continuous polysilicon beam.
- Underneath the rest of the beam: an empty air gap (2 µm thick), and finally the silicon substrate.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the phenomenon of stiction in surface-micromachined structures. List four mechanisms of stiction and discuss two methods used to mitigate it.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Stiction (static friction-induced adhesion) is the irreversible adhesion of a released micro-structure to an adjacent surface (substrate or another structure) caused by surface forces that overwhelm the elastic restoring force of the structure.

**Why it happens (1 mark):** The elastic restoring force of a typical released beam is on the order of $0.01$–$0.1$ µN. The surface forces during and after release can be 10–1000× larger.

**Mechanism 1 — Capillary (meniscus) stiction (1.5 marks):**
When the wafer is pulled out of the rinse liquid, a thin film of water is trapped between the released beam and the substrate. Surface tension creates a concave meniscus whose Laplace pressure pulls the beam down:
$$\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{d}$$
Force: $F_c = \Delta P \times A$. Typical magnitude is 10–100 µN for a 100 µm × 2 µm beam with 2 µm gap.

**Mechanism 2 — van der Waals stiction (1 mark):**
After drying, the beam contacts the substrate at atomic distances (0.3–0.5 nm). The London dispersion force becomes attractive:
$$F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H A}{12\pi d_0^2}$$
With $A_H \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-20}$ J for SiO₂–SiO₂ across air, the force is in the µN range.

**Mechanism 3 — Electrostatic stiction (0.5 marks):**
If the structure and substrate carry trapped charge (common after RIE or PECVD), a DC voltage is effectively applied across the air gap, generating an attractive Coulomb force:
$$F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$$

**Mechanism 4 — Casimir / quantum stiction (0.5 marks):**
At sub-100 nm gaps, zero-point fluctuations of the electromagnetic field create a quantum attractive force scaling as $1/d^4$:
$$F_{Casimir} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \hbar c A}{240 d^4}$$
This becomes non-negligible for gaps < 100 nm.

**Mitigation Method 1 — Supercritical CO₂ drying (1 mark):**
CO₂ is pressurised above its critical point (7.4 MPa, 31.1 °C) so that there is no liquid–vapour interface, hence no meniscus and no capillary force. The beam is released in this state and the chamber is slowly depressurised, leaving the structure dry and suspended.

**Mitigation Method 2 — Self-Assembled Monolayer (SAM) coating (1 mark):**
After release, the wafer is exposed to a vapour of octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) or perfluorodecyltrichlorosilane. The molecules chemisorb on the Si–OH surface, forming a hydrophobic monolayer 1.5–2.5 nm thick. This (i) increases the contact angle $\theta$ reducing $\cos\theta$ and hence capillary force, and (ii) increases the equilibrium separation $d_0$, reducing van der Waals force.

*(Additional mitigation methods commonly credited in KTU answers: vapour-phase HF release, dimples/bumps, anti-stiction bumps, freeze-drying, freeze-sublimation.)*

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
Stiction is the principal yield-limiting failure mode in surface micromachining, and an industrial-grade fabrication line combines *multiple* anti-stiction techniques (e.g., HF release + supercritical drying + OTS coating + dimples) to push yield above 95 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students frequently mention *only* capillary stiction and forget the other three mechanisms. Listing all four with at least one numerical equation each guarantees full 7 marks. Another common error is writing "the structure sticks because of friction" without naming the *surface force* — this loses the conceptual mark.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Main]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, describe the process of fabricating a polysilicon surface-micromachined accelerometer proof mass. Explain the role of the comb fingers and the suspension beams.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A surface-micromachined accelerometer consists of a central proof mass suspended by four folded suspension beams above the substrate. The mass carries moving comb fingers that interdigitate with fixed comb fingers anchored to the substrate. Acceleration causes the proof mass to displace laterally, changing the differential capacitance between the comb fingers.

**Process flow (5 marks — describe each step with a short justification):**

1. **Thermal oxidation** of a p-type <100> silicon wafer (500 nm SiO₂) for isolation.
2. **Sacrificial PSG deposition** by LPCVD (2 µm thick) at 450 °C. PSG is used (instead of thermal oxide) because it etches 10× faster in HF, shortening release time.
3. **Anchor patterning** — Mask #1 opens windows where the four suspension beams and the fixed comb fingers anchor to the substrate.
4. **First structural polysilicon** deposition (2 µm) and patterning (Mask #2) to form the bottom fixed comb fingers, the lower plate of the sense capacitors, and the lower half of the suspension beams.
5. **Second sacrificial oxide** (1 µm) deposition, planarised by chemical-mechanical polishing (CMP) if needed, and anchor patterning (Mask #3) for the proof-mass anchors.
6. **Second structural polysilicon** deposition (2 µm) and patterning (Mask #4) to form the proof mass and the moving comb fingers.
7. **Contact cuts** through the inter-layer dielectric (Mask #5).
8. **Metal interconnect** (sputtered Al-1 % Si, 1 µm) patterned (Mask #6) to wire the fixed and moving comb fingers to bond pads.
9. **Release etch** in 49 % HF for ~ 30 minutes, followed by **supercritical CO₂ drying** and **HMDS treatment**.
10. **Wafer-level capping** with a bonded silicon cap wafer under vacuum to provide a controlled atmosphere and mechanical protection.

**Role of suspension beams (0.5 mark):**
The four folded suspension beams provide a low spring constant in the *sensitive* axis (X) and a high spring constant in the orthogonal axes, so the proof mass moves preferentially in response to in-plane acceleration. They also serve as the **electrical return path** for the moving comb fingers, so they must be conductive polysilicon.

**Role of comb fingers (0.5 mark):**
The interdigitated comb fingers convert the small lateral displacement $\Delta x$ into a **differential capacitance change** $\Delta C = n\varepsilon_0 L t\,\Delta x / d$ where $n$ is the number of finger pairs, $L$ the overlap length, $t$ the finger thickness, and $d$ the gap. This is detected by a charge-amplifier interface ASIC, giving the acceleration reading.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A 200 µm long, 2 µm wide, 2 µm thick polysilicon cantilever is released with a 2 µm thick SiO₂ sacrificial layer in 49 % HF at 25 °C. Calculate (i) the release time, (ii) the capillary stiction force assuming perfect wetting, and (iii) the critical compressive stress that would buckle the beam. Given: HF etch rate of SiO₂ = 1.2 µm/min, γ(water) = 72 mN/m, E_poly-Si = 160 GPa, ν = 0.22.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Cantilever length $L = 200$ µm, width $w = 2$ µm, thickness $t = 2$ µm.
- Sacrificial gap $d = 2$ µm.
- HF etch rate $R_{ox} = 1.2$ µm/min.
- $\gamma = 72 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m.
- $E = 160 \times 10^{9}$ Pa.

**Part (i) — Release Time (2 marks):**
The etchant must travel the full cantilever length to undercut the anchor:

$$t_{release} = \dfrac{L}{R_{ox}} = \dfrac{200\,\mu m}{1.2\,\mu m/min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{release} = 166.67\,\text{min} \approx 2\,\text{hour}\,47\,\text{minutes}}$$

With a safety factor of 1.5 to ensure complete release, total time ≈ 250 minutes ≈ 4.2 hours.

**Part (ii) — Capillary Stiction Force (2.5 marks):**
For perfect wetting, contact angle $\theta = 0°$ so $\cos\theta = 1$.
Contact area of the meniscus region: $A = L \cdot w = 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-10}$ m².
Using the formula:

$$F_c = \dfrac{2\gamma A \cos\theta}{d^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 72 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-10} \times 1}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 72 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-10} = 5.76 \times 10^{-11}$ N·m.
Denominator: $4 \times 10^{-12}$ m².

$$F_c = \dfrac{5.76 \times 10^{-11}}{4 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 14.4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N} = 14.4\,\mu\text{N}}$$

This is orders of magnitude larger than the elastic restoring force of the beam (≈ 0.1 µN), confirming that capillary stiction will permanently collapse this beam if not mitigated by supercritical drying.

**Part (iii) — Critical Buckling Stress (2.5 marks):**
Using the cantilever Euler-buckling formula:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{4}\Big(\dfrac{t}{L}\Big)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 160 \times 10^{9}}{4}\Big(\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6}}\Big)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{1.579 \times 10^{12}}{4} \times (0.01)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 3.95 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = 3.95 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa} = 39.5\,\text{MPa}}$$

Since the *thermal* stress from a typical polysilicon deposition (ΔT ≈ 600 °C, CTE mismatch ≈ 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K) is:

$$\sigma_T = \dfrac{160 \times 10^{9}}{1 - 0.22} \times 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 600$$

$$\sigma_T = 320\,\text{MPa}$$

which is much larger than $\sigma_{cr} = 39.5$ MPa, the beam will buckle unless the deposition process is tuned (e.g., in-situ doping, annealing) to reduce intrinsic stress. This numerical is a classic KTU trap.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b)**:
> 1. Many students compute the release time as $L/R$ but forget that the lateral etch must travel **only** to the anchor end, not around a perimeter. **2 marks lost.**
> 2. Students often confuse the gap $d$ in the capillary formula with the beam thickness $t$. The gap is the *sacrificial thickness*, i.e., 2 µm, *not* the beam thickness. **2 marks lost.**
> 3. Students quote the wrong Euler formula (use $4L^2$ instead of $L^2$) or omit the factor of $1/4$ for the cantilever boundary conditions. **1–2 marks lost.**
> Always state which boundary conditions (cantilever vs fixed–fixed) you are using.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Rapid Revision — Surface Micromachining** — print this section before the exam.

- ☐ **Surface vs Bulk**: Surface micromachining builds **on** the substrate; bulk micromachining **removes** the substrate. Never confuse the two.
- ☐ **Six canonical steps**: substrate prep → sacrificial deposition → anchor patterning → structural deposition → structural patterning → release etch → drying.
- ☐ **Most common material pair**: **SiO₂ (sacrificial) + polysilicon (structural)**, released in **49 % HF**.
- ☐ **Selectivity** $S = R_{sacr}/R_{struct} \ge 100$ is *non-negotiable* for viable release.
- ☐ **Release time** $t_{release} = SF \cdot L / R_{sacr}$. Multiply by safety factor **1.3–2.0**.
- ☐ **Four stiction mechanisms**: capillary, van der Waals, electrostatic, Casimir — know the *force law* of each.
- ☐ **Anti-stiction methods**: supercritical CO₂ drying, vapour-phase HF release, SAMs (OTS), dimples, freeze-drying.
- ☐ **Capillary force** $F_c = 2\gamma A \cos\theta / d^2$ — gap $d$ = sacrificial thickness.
- ☐ **Euler buckling** for cantilever: $\sigma_{cr} = (\pi^2 E / 4)(t/L)^2$.
- ☐ **Thermal stress**: $\sigma_T = [E_f/(1-\nu_f)](\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$.
- ☐ **Anchor windows** must be opened *before* depositing the structural layer — never after.
- ☐ **LPCVD** gives near-100 % conformality; **evaporation** is non-conformal.
- ☐ **Step coverage** metric: $t_{min}/t_{top} \times 100\,\%$.
- ☐ **Dimples** reduce stiction by limiting contact area to < 5 %.
- ☐ **First-mode cantilever frequency**: $f_1 = (1.875^2/2\pi)(t/L^2)\sqrt{E/(12\rho)}$.
- ☐ **Comb-finger electrostatic force**: $F = n\varepsilon_0 L t\, V^2 / d$ — note the linear dependence on gap $d$ (vs. $1/d$ for parallel plate).
- ☐ **Real-world examples**: smartphone accelerometer (Bosch BMA), TI DLP micromirror, MEMS gyroscope, RF switch, biosensor cantilever.
- ☐ **Industrial yield killers** (in order): stiction, residual stress, etch selectivity failure, particle contamination, stress-gradient curvature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High-Aspect-Ratio Micromachining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** High-Aspect-Ratio Micromachining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**High-Aspect-Ratio Micromachining (HAR Micromachining)** is a class of non-conventional microfabrication processes used to produce micro-structures in which the depth (or height) is **substantially greater than the lateral dimension (width)**, formally quantified by the **Aspect Ratio (AR)**.

$$\boxed{AR = \frac{D}{W}}$$

where:
- **D** = Depth of the machined feature (µm)
- **W** = Lateral width of the smallest feature (µm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Convention:** A feature is generally classified as **"High Aspect Ratio"** when **AR ≥ 10:1**. Typical HAR features in MEMS technology range from **20:1 to 100:1**, with state-of-the-art industrial processes achieving ratios exceeding **300:1** in silicon (e.g., Deep Reactive Ion Etching).

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are trying to dig a **very narrow, very deep well** in hard rock using only a sandblaster.

- A **conventional sandblaster** (analogous to standard Reactive Ion Etching, RIE) loses energy as the hole gets deeper because the abrasive particles must bounce along the walls. The deeper you go, the shallower each pass becomes — this is called **RIE-lag** or **ARDE (Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching)**.
- **High-Aspect-Ratio Micromachining** is like giving that sandblaster a *continuous wall-protector* that prevents the sidewalls from being eroded while the bottom keeps getting drilled. Two alternating jets are used: one jets pure protective polymer (fluorocarbon), the other jets etchant plasma — this is the **Bosch Process**.

In short: HAR micromachining is the art of **drilling deep, narrow, perfectly vertical "tunnels"** into materials — silicon, glass, metals, and polymers — for applications like **comb-drive fingers, through-silicon vias, micro-fluidic channels, and micro-gear shafts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Etch Rate (E):** measured in **µm/min**
> - **Selectivity (S):** dimensionless ratio (target etch rate ÷ mask etch rate)
> - **Scallop depth:** cyclic sidewall roughness in nm — a key quality metric in DRIE
> - **Undercut:** lateral etch beneath the mask — measured in µm
> - **Verticality:** deviation from 90° in degrees (typically 89°–90.5° for high quality HAR)

## 1.3 Classification of HAR Micromachining Processes (KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus)

| Process Family | Energy Source | Typical Material | Max Reported AR |
|----------------|--------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** – Bosch process | RF plasma (SF₆ / C₄F₈) | Silicon | 100:1 |
| **Cryogenic DRIE** | RF plasma at −110 °C | Silicon, SiO₂ | 50:1 |
| **UV-LIGA / X-ray LIGA** | Synchrotron / UV exposure + electroplating | Polymers, Metals | 100:1 – 500:1 |
| **Micro-EDM (µEDM)** | Electrical discharges | Any conductive metal | 100:1 |
| **Micro-ECM / ECDM** | Electrochemical dissolution | Metals | 25:1 |
| **Deep UV / Laser Ablation** | Photon energy | Polymers, ceramics | 50:1 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) — The Bosch Process

The **Bosch process** (patented by Robert Bosch GmbH, 1994; foundational reference for KTU Module 3) is the industrial gold standard for silicon HAR micromachining. It overcomes the **ARDE limitation** of conventional RIE by **temporarily switching off chemical etching** and instead depositing a protective **fluorocarbon (Teflon-like) film** on the sidewalls.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

**Step 1 — Passivation Cycle:**
- Inlet gas: **C₄F₈ (octafluorocyclobutane)**
- Plasma dissociates C₄F₈ into CF₂ radicals.
- A thin (~10–100 nm) **fluoropolymer (–CF₂–)ₙ** film is conformally deposited on **all exposed surfaces** — bottom + sidewalls.

**Step 2 — Etch Cycle:**
- Inlet gas: **SF₆ (sulphur hexafluoride)**
- Plasma generates **F⁻ radicals**.
- Ion bombardment (bias voltage) **sputters away the polymer preferentially from the horizontal bottom surface** (anisotropic ion direction).
- Once the bottom is clean, free fluorine chemically etches silicon: **Si + 4F → SiF₄ (gas)**.

**Step 3 — Repeat** with cycle time $t_{cyc}$ for N cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The alternating **Etch/Passivation cycling** creates characteristic **sidewall scallops** — the visible "ripples" on etched sidewalls. Each scallop corresponds to one cycle, and its amplitude is the **scallop depth**, $d_s$.

## 2.2 Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE / RIE-Lag)

As the trench deepens, the **effective etch rate decreases** because:
1. Ion transport to the trench bottom is reduced by shadowing.
2. Reactant depletion inside narrow trenches.
3. Sidewall fluorocarbon film becomes harder to sputter away at the bottom.

A common **empirical relation** used in KTU problems:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{AR}} = \frac{1}{E_0} + k \cdot AR}$$

Where:
- $E_{AR}$ = etch rate at aspect ratio AR (µm/min)
- $E_0$ = open-area (unmasked) etch rate (µm/min)
- $k$ = ARDE constant (min/µm), material- and machine-dependent

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $AR = D / W$ | Aspect ratio of feature | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E = D / t$ | Etch rate = depth ÷ time | µm/min |
| 3 | $S = E_{film} / E_{mask}$ | Selectivity (film vs. mask etch rate) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $U = W_{etch} - W_{mask}$ | Undercut (lateral etch) | µm |
| 5 | $1/E_{AR} = 1/E_0 + k \cdot AR$ | ARDE relation (inverse-linear) | min/µm |
| 6 | $d_s = E \cdot t_{etch}$ | Scallop depth per Bosch cycle | nm |
| 7 | $T_{total} = N \cdot t_{cyc}$ | Total process time | min |
| 8 | $D = N \cdot (E \cdot t_{etch})$ | Total depth ≈ N cycles × etch per cycle | µm |
| 9 | $v = I \cdot \eta / (n \cdot F \cdot A)$ | LIGA electroplating rate (Faraday) | µm/min |
| 10 | $V_{removal} = \pi d^2 f / 4$ | Material removal rate in µEDM (per pulse) | µm³/pulse |
| 11 | $\theta_{wall} = 90° - \alpha$ | Wall verticality from taper angle $\alpha$ | degrees |
| 12 | $R_a \approx d_s / 2$ | RMS sidewall roughness ≈ scallop depth/2 | nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In selectivity formulas, **always specify which film is being protected** (target film vs. mask film). Never write $S = E_1 / E_2$ without labels — KTU valuators deduct up to **2 marks** for this.

## 2.4 LIGA Process — Electroforming-Based HAR

**LIGA** = **L**ithographie (Lithography) + **G**alvanik (Electroforming) + **Abformung (Molding)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **X-ray LIGA** uses **synchrotron radiation** (~1 Å wavelength) to expose thick PMMA resists (up to **2 mm** depth), producing **AR > 100:1**. The exposed regions are dissolved; the voids are filled by **nickel, copper, or gold electroplating**. The metallic replica is the final micro-part.

**UV-LIGA** is a low-cost alternative using SU-8 photoresist with UV exposure — lower AR (typically ≤ 20:1) but suitable for most polymer/metal HAR research.

### 2.4.1 Faraday's Law of Electroplating in LIGA

$$v = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

Where:
- $v$ = plating rate (µm/min)
- $M$ = molar mass of metal (g/mol)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $\eta$ = current efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95)
- $n$ = valence (2 for Ni²⁺, 2 for Cu²⁺, 1 for Au⁺)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $A$ = area to be plated (cm²)

## 2.5 Micro-Electrical Discharge Machining (µEDM) for HAR

µEDM removes material by **controlled electrical sparks** between a micro-tool (wire or shaped electrode) and the workpiece in a dielectric fluid. No chemical/thermal damage to bulk; suitable for **any conductive metal**.

**Material Removal Rate (MRR) per discharge:**

$$\boxed{MRR = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot \eta_v}$$

Where:
- $d_w$ = crater diameter per spark (µm)
- $f$ = discharge frequency (Hz)
- $\eta_v$ = volumetric removal efficiency (typically 0.1–0.3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Note:** Wire-µEDM can achieve wire diameters down to **5 µm** and cut AR up to **400:1** in hardened tool steel — vital for fabricating micro-mould inserts for polymer replication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Scallop Depth as a Function of Bosch Cycle Parameters

We will derive the **scallop depth** $d_s$ from first principles — a common KTU 14-mark question.

### 3.1.1 Setup

The Bosch process has alternating cycles:
- Etch duration: $t_{etch}$ (seconds)
- Passivation duration: $t_{pass}$ (seconds)
- Etch rate at the trench bottom (during active etch phase): $E_b$ (nm/s)
- Polymer deposition rate on sidewalls: $R_p$ (nm/s)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Material removed per etch cycle:**
The silicon removed from the trench bottom in one etch phase equals:

$$\Delta z_{etch} = E_b \cdot t_{etch}$$

**Step 2 — Polymer deposited on freshly exposed sidewall:**
During the etch phase, polymer is still being deposited on sidewalls (continuous flow), but ions sputter it off the bottom. Net effect: sidewall surface gets a *new* polymer layer of thickness:

$$\delta_{side} = R_p \cdot (t_{pass} + t_{etch})$$

**Step 3 — Lateral etch under the mask during one cycle:**
The fluorine radicals attack silicon **isotropically** below the polymer cap. The lateral etch depth per cycle is approximately half of the bottom etch, modulated by ion direction (which is vertical, hence lateral attack is purely chemical):

$$x_{lat} = \alpha \cdot E_b \cdot t_{etch}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **isotropy coefficient** ($\alpha \approx 0.5$ for purely chemical F attack, much less when ions dominate).

**Step 4 — Scallop amplitude equals lateral etch per cycle:**
The visible ripple height on the sidewall (scallop depth) is the lateral undercut difference between consecutive cycles:

$$\boxed{d_s \approx 2 \cdot x_{lat} = 2 \alpha E_b t_{etch}}$$

**Step 5 — Total depth after N cycles:**

$$D_{total} = N \cdot E_b \cdot t_{etch}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for N in terms of target depth:**

$$N = \frac{D_{target}}{E_b \cdot t_{etch}}$$

**Step 7 — Total process time:**

$$T_{total} = N (t_{etch} + t_{pass}) = \frac{D_{target} (t_{etch} + t_{pass})}{E_b \cdot t_{etch}}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** In a Bosch DRIE process, $E_b = 3 \;\mu m/min$, $t_{etch} = 10 \;s$, $t_{pass} = 7 \;s$, $\alpha = 0.4$. Find:
> (a) Scallop depth
> (b) Number of cycles to etch 300 µm deep
> (c) Total process time
> (d) RMS roughness $R_a$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**
$E_b = 3 \;\mu m/min = 3000 \;nm / 60 \;s = 50 \;nm/s$

**Step 2 — Scallop depth:**

$$d_s = 2 \alpha E_b t_{etch} = 2 \times 0.4 \times 50 \times 10 = 400 \;nm$$

**Step 3 — Depth per cycle:**

$$\Delta z = E_b \cdot t_{etch} = 50 \times 10 = 500 \;nm / cycle$$

**Step 4 — Cycles needed:**

$$N = \frac{300\,000 \;nm}{500 \;nm} = 600 \;cycles$$

**Step 5 — Total time:**

$$T = 600 \times (10 + 7) = 10\,200 \;s = 170 \;min = 2.83 \;hours$$

**Step 6 — RMS Roughness:**

$$R_a \approx d_s / 2 = 200 \;nm$$

This result is consistent with state-of-the-art DRIE reports.

## 3.2 Derivation: Selectivity with Mask Erosion

> **Problem:** A SiO₂ hard mask of thickness $T_m = 2 \;\mu m$ is used to etch silicon using DRIE. Etch rate of Si = 2 µm/min; etch rate of SiO₂ = 25 nm/min. Find (a) Selectivity, (b) Maximum etchable depth before mask failure.

**Step 1 — Selectivity:**

$$S = \frac{E_{Si}}{E_{SiO_2}} = \frac{2000 \;nm/min}{25 \;nm/min} = 80$$

**Step 2 — Time to consume mask completely:**

$$t_{max} = \frac{T_m}{E_{SiO_2}} = \frac{2\,000\;000 \;nm}{25 \;nm/min} = 80\,000 \;min$$

**Step 3 — Maximum silicon depth in that time:**

$$D_{max} = E_{Si} \cdot t_{max} = 2 \;\mu m/min \times 80\,000 \;min = 160\,000 \;\mu m = 160 \;mm$$

This enormous theoretical depth shows the mask is **not** the bottleneck in practical HAR etching — the **ARDE effect** is.

## 3.3 Derivation: ARDE-Mitigation — Optimal Pressure & Power

In DRIE, the etch rate at aspect ratio AR follows the empirical relation:

$$\frac{1}{E_{AR}} = \frac{1}{E_0} + k \cdot AR$$

If we operate at low pressure (≤ 10 mTorr), the mean free path of ions increases, improving bottom-ion delivery and reducing $k$. Using two operating points $(AR_1, E_1)$ and $(AR_2, E_2)$, we can solve for $k$ and $E_0$:

$$\frac{1}{E_1} - \frac{1}{E_2} = k(AR_1 - AR_2)$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{\dfrac{1}{E_1} - \dfrac{1}{E_2}}{AR_1 - AR_2}}$$

This is a frequently tested KTU 7-mark sub-question.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which HAR Process to Choose?

| Application / Requirement | Recommended Process | Justification |
|---------------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Through-Silicon-Via (TSV) for 3-D IC stacking | **DRIE (Bosch)** | Si substrate, AR > 20:1, mature industry process |
| Micro-fluidic channels in polymer (PDMS, PMMA) | **UV-LIGA / Hot Embossing** | Polymers, optical transparency, low cost |
| High-aspect metallic micro-gears (Ni, Cu) | **X-ray LIGA** | True AR > 100:1 with metallic strength |
| Micro-holes in hardened tool steel | **Micro-EDM** | Conductive but hard/ brittle materials |
| HAR glass structures | **Powder blasting + HF etch** | Glass is non-conductive; alternative to Si |
| Optical micro-lens arrays | **Reflow + RIE** | Smooth surfaces, AR typically < 5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bosch DRIE Process Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Si Wafer + Hard Mask]) --> Coat[Photolithography + Pattern Mask]
    Coat --> LoadCh[Load into DRIE Chamber]
    LoadCh --> Cycle1{Passivation Phase: C4F8 Plasma}
    Cycle1 --> DepFluor[Deposit CF2 polymer film on all surfaces]
    DepFluor --> Cycle2{Etch Phase: SF6 Plasma}
    Cycle2 --> Sputter[Ion bombardment removes bottom polymer]
    Sputter --> ChemEtch[F radicals etch Si: Si plus 4F gives SiF4 gas]
    ChemEtch --> Decision{Depth Achieved?}
    Decision --> NoCheck{No}
    NoCheck --> Cycle1
    Decision --> YesCheck{Yes}
    YesCheck --> Vent[Vent Chamber and Unload]
    Vent --> Inspect[Scallop / Taper / AR Measurement]
    Inspect --> End([Finished HAR Micro-Structure])
```

## 4.2 HAR Micromachining — Process Family Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HAR Micromachining] --> B[Dry Plasma-Based]
    A --> C[Lithography Plus Electroforming]
    A --> D[Mechanical Discharge-Based]
    A --> E[Hybrid Processes]
    B --> B1[DRIE Bosch]
    B --> B2[Cryogenic DRIE]
    B --> B3[ICP DRIE]
    C --> C1[X-Ray LIGA]
    C --> C2[UV-LIGA]
    C --> C3[Nano-Imprint LIGA]
    D --> D1[Micro EDM Die Sink]
    D --> D2[Micro Wire EDM]
    D --> D3[Micro ECDM]
    E --> E1[Laser Assisted Etching]
    E --> E2[Plasma Plus Electrochemical]
    B1 --> App1[Through Silicon Vias]
    B1 --> App2[MEMS Comb Drives]
    C1 --> App3[Micro Gear Trains]
    C1 --> App4[Optical Micro Components]
    D2 --> App5[Micro Mould Inserts]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bosch Process Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CycleOne[Single Bosch Cycle Approx 5 to 20 seconds]
        P[Passivation Step: C4F8 gas flow 100 to 200 sccm, pressure 20 to 30 mTorr, coil power 600 W, platen off]
        E[Etch Step: SF6 gas flow 200 to 400 sccm, pressure 20 to 30 mTorr, coil power 600 to 1500 W, platen bias 10 to 30 W]
        P --> E
    end
    P --> SideDep[Sidewall polymer accumulation]
    SideDep --> SideResult[Sidewall protected lateral attack minimised]
    E --> BottomSputter[Ion bombardment strips bottom polymer]
    BottomSputter --> BottomResult[Isotropic F chemical etch proceeds vertically]
    BottomResult --> Output[Deeper trench with sidewall scallop]
```

## 4.4 Concept Map — Aspect Ratio & Quality Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AR[Aspect Ratio equals Depth over Width] --> Q1[Etch Rate E in um per min]
    AR --> Q2[Selectivity S dimensionless]
    AR --> Q3[Sidewall Verticality in degrees]
    AR --> Q4[Scallop Depth ds in nm]
    AR --> Q5[Roughness Ra in nm]
    AR --> Q6[Undercut U in um]
    Q1 --> App[MEMS, TSV, Microfluidics]
    Q2 --> App
    Q3 --> App
    Q4 --> App
    Q5 --> App
    Q6 --> App
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`
**Define Aspect Ratio in micromachining. State the minimum ratio for a feature to be classified as high-aspect-ratio with one industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Aspect Ratio is the ratio of depth (D) of a micro-feature to its lateral width (W), expressed as $AR = D/W$.
- **HAR threshold (1 mark):** A feature is termed **High-Aspect-Ratio** when $AR \geq 10:1$. Industrial HAR features typically range from 20:1 to 100:1.
- **Example (1 mark):** Through-Silicon-Vias (TSVs) used in 3-D integrated circuit packaging (e.g., AR ≈ 30:1 in modern CMOS).

---

### Question A.2  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**List the two gases used in the Bosch DRIE process and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **C₄F₈ (Octafluorocyclobutane) (1.5 marks):** Used in the **passivation half-cycle**. Plasma dissociates it into CF₂ radicals which deposit a conformal **fluorocarbon polymer film** (~10–100 nm) on all exposed surfaces, protecting sidewalls from lateral etching.
- **SF₆ (Sulphur Hexafluoride) (1.5 marks):** Used in the **etch half-cycle**. Plasma generates **F radicals** which chemically etch silicon (Si + 4F → SiF₄↑). Directional ion bombardment simultaneously **strips the polymer from horizontal (bottom) surfaces**, ensuring vertical anisotropy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice Provided — Select either Question B1 OR Question B2]**

### ❖ Question B1  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **Bosch Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) process** for high-aspect-ratio silicon micromachining. Discuss the role of the alternating passivation and etch cycles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Bosch process, the etch cycle time is 12 s and passivation cycle time is 8 s. The silicon etch rate at the trench bottom during the active etch phase is 4 µm/min and the sidewall lateral etch coefficient $\alpha = 0.45$. Calculate:
(i) Scallop depth
(ii) Number of cycles needed to etch a trench 250 µm deep
(iii) Total process time
(iv) RMS sidewall roughness $R_a$

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Schematic (2 marks):** Show wafer + patterned mask + alternating C₄F₈/SF₆ plasma environment; indicate scallop formation on sidewalls.
2. **Passivation Cycle (1.5 marks):** C₄F₈ plasma → CF₂ radicals → conformal fluorocarbon polymer deposition on all surfaces.
3. **Etch Cycle (1.5 marks):** SF₆ plasma → F radicals + ion bombardment → polymer removed from bottom → SiF₄ volatile etch product.
4. **Cycling (1 mark):** Alternating cycles yield vertical sidewalls with characteristic scallops; depth controlled by number of cycles.
5. **Advantage (1 mark):** Decouples sidewall protection from bottom etching → overcomes ARDE/RIE-lag.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $t_{etch} = 12\;s$, $t_{pass} = 8\;s$, $E_b = 4\;\mu m/min$, $\alpha = 0.45$, $D = 250\;\mu m$

**Step 1 — Convert etch rate:**
$E_b = 4\;\mu m/min = 4000\;nm/60\;s = 66.67\;nm/s$

**Step 2 — Scallop depth:**
$$d_s = 2\alpha E_b t_{etch} = 2 \times 0.45 \times 66.67 \times 12 = 720\;nm$$

**Step 3 — Depth per cycle:**
$$\Delta z = E_b \cdot t_{etch} = 66.67 \times 12 = 800\;nm = 0.8\;\mu m$$

**Step 4 — Number of cycles:**
$$N = \frac{250\;\mu m}{0.8\;\mu m} = 312.5 \Rightarrow 313 \text{ cycles (rounded up)}$$

**Step 5 — Total time:**
$$T_{total} = 313 \times (12 + 8) = 6260\;s \approx 104.3\;min \approx 1.74\;hours$$

**Step 6 — RMS Roughness:**
$$R_a \approx d_s/2 = 360\;nm$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students **forget to convert µm/min to nm/s** when applying the scallop formula — instant loss of 1 mark.
> - Some round **N = 312 instead of 313** — partial loss because an incomplete final cycle leaves the trench short of target depth.
> - Do **not** confuse $d_s$ with $R_a$; the examiner wants the **explicit formula $R_a \approx d_s/2$**.

---

### ❖ Question B2  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **LIGA process** for high-aspect-ratio micromachining. Compare **X-ray LIGA** and **UV-LIGA** in terms of achievable aspect ratio, resolution, and cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A nickel LIGA process uses an electroplating bath with Ni²⁺ ions (n = 2), $\eta = 0.9$, $I = 0.5\;A$, plated area $A = 4\;cm^2$. Molar mass of Ni = 58.7 g/mol, density = 8.9 g/cm³. Calculate:
(i) Plating rate in µm/min
(ii) Time to plate a 300 µm tall micro-structure

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **LIGA steps (2 marks):** (i) Thick resist coating (PMMA / SU-8), (ii) Exposure (X-ray or UV) through mask, (iii) Development to dissolve exposed resist, (iv) Electroforming to fill mould with metal, (v) Resist stripping to release metallic micro-part.
2. **X-ray LIGA (2 marks):** Synchrotron radiation (~1 Å) penetrates mm-thick resist → AR > 100:1, sub-µm lateral resolution, very high cost (synchrotron).
3. **UV-LIGA (2 marks):** UV exposure of SU-8 resist → AR ≤ 20:1, ~5 µm resolution, low cost (mercury lamp / mask aligner).
4. **Comparison (1 mark):** Tabular comparison mentioning AR, resolution, equipment cost, application domain.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Plating rate formula:**
$$v = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$v = \frac{58.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.9}{2 \times 96\,485 \times 8.9 \times 4} \;\frac{g/cm^2/s}{g/cm^3 \cdot C/mol \cdot cm^2}$$

Numerator: $58.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.9 = 26.42$
Denominator: $2 \times 96\,485 \times 8.9 \times 4 = 6\,870\,932$

**Step 3 — Plating rate in cm/s:**

$$v = \frac{26.42}{6\,870\,932} = 3.845 \times 10^{-6}\;cm/s$$

**Step 4 — Convert to µm/min:**

$$v = 3.845 \times 10^{-6}\;cm/s \times 10^4 \;\mu m/cm \times 60 \;s/min = 2.31\;\mu m/min$$

**Step 5 — Time to plate 300 µm:**

$$t = \frac{300\;\mu m}{2.31\;\mu m/min} = 129.9\;min \approx 2.16\;hours$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common error:** forgetting to convert cm/s → µm/min — students report $3.8 \times 10^{-6}$ µm/min by mistake, losing **3 marks**.
> - Do not confuse **plating rate** (µm/min) with **mass deposition rate** (g/s). They are different physical quantities.
> - Always state the **value of Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol** explicitly — silence here costs 0.5 mark.
> - For LIGA theory, students often skip the **mould-stripping** step — losing 1 mark in the schematic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Aspect Ratio = Depth / Width**; HAR means AR ≥ 10:1 in KTU convention.
- **Bosch Process:** alternating C₄F₈ (passivation) and SF₆ (etch) plasma cycles.
- **Passivation film:** ~10–100 nm fluoropolymer; protects sidewalls, not bottom.
- **Etch product:** volatile SiF₄ gas — pumped out of chamber.
- **Scallop depth formula:** $d_s = 2\alpha E_b t_{etch}$.
- **Total depth formula:** $D = N \cdot E_b \cdot t_{etch}$.
- **Total process time:** $T = N(t_{etch} + t_{pass}) = D(t_{etch} + t_{pass})/(E_b t_{etch})$.
- **ARDE relation:** $1/E_{AR} = 1/E_0 + k \cdot AR$.
- **Selectivity:** $S = E_{target}/E_{mask}$; ≥ 50 ideal for HAR DRIE.
- **Verticality:** deviation from 90° (good DRIE: 89°–90.5°).
- **LIGA = Lithography + Electroforming + Moulding.**
- **X-ray LIGA:** synchrotron; AR > 100:1; sub-µm resolution.
- **UV-LIGA:** SU-8 + UV; AR ≤ 20:1; low cost.
- **Faraday plating rate:** $v = MI\eta/(nF\rho A)$.
- **µEDM:** Material removal by controlled electrical sparks; suitable for any conductive metal; AR up to 400:1 with 5 µm wire.
- **Typical applications:** TSVs, comb drives, micro-fluidic channels, micro-gears, micro-lenses, bio-MEMS needles.
- **Always convert units** (µm/min ↔ nm/s) before applying Bosch formulas.
- **Common KTU errors:** forgetting Faraday constant, missing conversion factors, omitting cycle round-up, mixing $d_s$ with $R_a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Bloom's Taxonomy Coverage:**
> - **L1–L2 (Remember/Understand):** Definitions of AR, DRIE, LIGA, selectivity.
> - **L3 (Apply):** Scallop / cycle / plating-rate numericals — high-weightage.
> - **L4 (Analyze):** ARDE comparison, Bosch vs. Cryogenic DRIE process selection.
> - **L5–L6 (Evaluate/Create):** Process selection for a given MEMS application — typical 14-mark question framing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Photolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photolithography** is a planar pattern-transfer process used in micro- and nano-fabrication in which a light-sensitive polymer film (the *photoresist*) coated on a substrate is selectively exposed to ultraviolet (UV) or deep-ultraviolet (DUV) radiation through a *photomask*. The exposed regions undergo a chemical reaction (chain scission in positive resists, cross-linking in negative resists), allowing a subsequent chemical developer to selectively dissolve either the exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions, thereby replicating the mask pattern onto the substrate with sub-micron fidelity.

In the context of **KTU Module 3**, photolithography is treated as the *workhorse patterning technology* of the semiconductor industry, accounting for the smallest feature sizes (currently < 10 nm) achievable in high-volume manufacturing.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Stencilling with Sunlight":**
> Imagine you have a thin sheet of butter spread on toast. If you place a stencil (a card with shapes cut out) over the butter and shine bright light through the cut-outs, the butter *under* the cut-outs melts first, while the rest stays solid. You can then simply *wipe away* the melted parts with a paper towel, leaving behind an exact replica of the stencil pattern in the remaining solid butter.
>
> - The **toast + butter** is your **silicon wafer + photoresist**.
> - The **stencil card** is the **photomask**.
> - The **sunlight** is the **UV exposure source**.
> - The **paper towel** is the **chemical developer**.
> - The **leftover pattern** is your final **lithographic image** ready for etching/doping.

This mental picture removes the "mystery" of photolithography: it is simply *selective light-induced chemical weakening*, followed by *selective physical removal*.

---

### 1.3 Core Metrics & Standards (Must Memorise)

- **Exposure Wavelength (λ):** typically 365 nm (i-line), 248 nm (KrF), 193 nm (ArF), or 13.5 nm (**EUV**).
- **Resolution (R):** the smallest feature that can be reliably printed; governed by the **Rayleigh criterion**.
- **Depth of Focus (DOF):** the vertical tolerance for which the image remains acceptably sharp.
- **Numerical Aperture (NA):** light-gathering ability of the projection optics.
- **Critical Dimension (CD):** the nominal width of the smallest geometry, e.g., the transistor gate.

> [!TIP]
> The entire lithography industry is a constant battle against the equation **R = k₁·λ/NA**. To print smaller, you must *shrink λ*, *increase NA*, or *reduce k₁* (via Resolution Enhancement Techniques — RETs).

---

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Lithography Family | Operating Mode | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Contact / Proximity** | Mask touches or hovers over wafer | Early ICs, MEMS, research |
| **Projection (Stepper / Scanner)** | Mask imaged via lens onto wafer | All modern VLSI / ULSI |
| **Direct-Write (E-beam / Laser)** | No mask; serial writing | Photomasks, low-volume ASICs |
| **Nanoimprint (NIL)** | Mechanical stamp into polymer | Next-gen, hard-drive platters |

---

### 1.5 Process Flow at a Glance

The canonical **8-step photolithographic cycle** is:

1. **Wafer Cleaning & Dehydration** — remove organics, particles, and adsorbed water (typically at ~200 °C).
2. **Priming (HMDS vapour)** — promote photoresist adhesion to the oxide surface.
3. **Spin Coating** — dispense liquid resist, spin to uniform thickness (e.g., 1 µm).
4. **Soft Bake (Pre-Bake)** — drive off solvent; stabilise film.
5. **Alignment & Exposure** — position the mask and dose the resist with UV light.
6. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — control diffusion of photo-acid (DUV) or standing waves.
7. **Development** — immerse/spray with TMAH developer; pattern emerges.
8. **Hard Bake & Inspection** — harden the resist; measure CDs with SEM or optical CD tools.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common student error:** Treating "exposure" as the *only* step. In reality, soft-bake and PEB can swing CDs by tens of nanometres — they are *first-order* parameters, not afterthoughts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> The "why" behind each step is what KTU examiners reward. Memorising the sequence alone earns *application-level* marks; explaining the chemistry/physics earns *analysis-level* marks.

#### Step 1 — Wafer Preparation
- **Why:** A single 0.5 µm dust particle can destroy an entire die (≈ 1 cm² on a 300 mm wafer ⇒ waste of millions of transistors).
- **How:** RCA clean → H₂SO₄/H₂O₂ (Piranha) → dilute HF → DI rinse → dehydration bake.

#### Step 2 — Adhesion Promotion (Priming)
- **Why:** Photoresist (organic) does not naturally wet a SiO₂ surface (polar, hydrophilic). Adhesion failure ⇒ pattern lift-off during development.
- **How:** **Hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS)** reacts with surface –OH groups, replacing them with hydrophobic –OSi(CH₃)₃ groups.

$$\text{Si–OH} + (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{Si–NH–Si(CH}_3)_3 \longrightarrow \text{Si–O–Si(CH}_3)_3 + \text{NH}_3$$

#### Step 3 — Spin Coating
- **Why:** Resist thickness must be uniform to < 1 % across the wafer to maintain dose control.
- **How:** Centrifugal force spreads liquid resist. The **final thickness** is governed by:

$$h = \frac{K \cdot C^{\beta} \cdot \eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{\alpha}}$$

where *K*, *α*, *β*, *γ* are empirical constants, *C* = solids concentration, *η* = viscosity, *ω* = spin speed (rpm). Typically, *h* ∝ 1/√ω.

#### Step 4 — Soft Bake (Pre-Bake)
- **Why:** Remove residual solvent (~10–20 % of film mass); improve etch resistance; prevent sticking to the mask in contact mode.
- **How:** Hot-plate at 90–120 °C for 60 s.

#### Step 5 — Alignment & Exposure
- **Why:** Translate the mask pattern onto the wafer with sub-100 nm overlay accuracy.
- **How:** A **mask aligner** (contact/proximity) or **step-and-scan system** (projection) brings the mask image to the wafer plane via reduction optics (typically 4× or 5× reduction).
- **Photo-chemistry:**
  - **Positive resist:** Photo-active compound (PAC) decomposes → produces **carboxylic acid** → soluble in aqueous base.
  - **Negative resist:** Photo-initiator generates **free radicals** → cross-links polymer chains → insoluble.

#### Step 6 — Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)
- **Why:** For **chemically amplified (CA) resists**, drive diffusion of **photo-acid generator (PAG)** products. Smooths standing-wave artefacts.
- **How:** 100–150 °C for 60–120 s.

#### Step 7 — Development
- **Why:** Convert the latent image into a real topographic pattern.
