- **How:** 2.38 % **TMAH (tetramethylammonium hydroxide)** solution; spray-puddle or immersion.

> [!TIP]
> TMAH is preferred over NaOH/KOH because it does **not** attack aluminium interconnects — critical for BEOL processing.

#### Step 8 — Hard Bake & Inspection
- **Why:** Improve etch resistance; ensure CD stability. SEM/OCD (optical CD) verifies fidelity.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **exam-critical**. Memorise the symbols, the limiting factor, and the units.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Notes / KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | **Rayleigh Resolution** | $k_1 \ge 0.25$ theoretically | |
| 2 | $DOF = k_2 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^2}$ | **Depth of Focus** | Halving λ = quartering DOF | |
| 3 | $NA = n \sin\theta$ | **Numerical Aperture** | $n$ = refractive index of medium | |
| 4 | $h \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\omega}}$ | **Spin-coat thickness** | Empirical | |
| 5 | $t_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{D}{I}$ | **Exposure time** | $D$ = dose (mJ/cm²), $I$ = intensity | |
| 6 | $DOF_{\text{field}} = \pm \dfrac{\lambda}{2 NA^2}$ | Field DOF for a stepper | Practical limit | |
| 7 | $W_{\text{CD}} = \sqrt{\sum_i \sigma_i^2}$ | **CD error budget** | Sum of variances in quadrature | |
| 8 | $M = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot \Delta n}$ | **Minimum pitch (Rayleigh)** | $\Delta n$ = refractive-index contrast | |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners commonly test whether students confuse **Resolution (R)** with **Depth of Focus (DOF)**. The two scale *inversely* with NA — increasing NA improves R but **degrades** DOF quadratically.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Photolithography is not just an academic topic — it underpins:

- **CMOS Logic & Memory (DRAM, NAND):** Every transistor, bit-line, and word-line is lithographically defined.
- **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems):** Gyroscopes, accelerometers, pressure sensors.
- **Photonics:** Waveguides, grating couplers, metasurfaces.
- **Biochips & Lab-on-Chip:** Microfluidic channels.
- **Flat-Panel Displays:** a-Si TFTs on glass using large-area lithography.

> **Industry fact:** A modern EUV scanner (ASML NXE:3800E) costs **≈ 200 M USD** and prints **≈ 175 wafers/hour** at 13.5 nm wavelength — illustrating the extraordinary capital intensity driven by Moore's Law.

---

### 2.4 Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RETs)

Because $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$, three engineering levers exist:

| Lever | Technique | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Lower λ** | 365 → 248 → 193 → 13.5 nm | Direct shrink |
| **Higher NA** | Immersion lithography (water, $n = 1.44$) | $NA > 1$ possible |
| **Lower k₁** | OPC, PSM, OAI, source-mask optimisation | Process-driven |

- **OPC** = Optical Proximity Correction (pre-distorts mask features).
- **PSM** = Phase-Shifting Mask (uses destructive interference).
- **OAI** = Off-Axis Illumination (improves DOF & contrast for dense pitches).
- **Immersion lithography** places water between the final lens and the wafer, boosting NA from ~0.93 (dry) to **1.35**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rayleigh Resolution Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 3. It links the physical wavelength of light to the minimum printable feature size.

#### Starting Physical Picture
Consider two adjacent rectangular apertures in a mask, separated by a distance $p$ (the **pitch**). When projected through a circular lens of finite diameter, the images of these apertures do not have infinitely sharp edges — they are broadened by **diffraction**. The **Airy disc** of one feature overlaps the other; the smallest pitch at which the two peaks are still resolvable is set by the **Rayleigh criterion**.

#### Step 1 — Diffraction Limit of a Single Aperture
For a circular lens, the radius of the first dark ring of the **Airy pattern** is:

$$r_{\text{Airy}} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$$

where $NA = n \sin\theta$, and $\theta$ is the half-angle of the cone of light collected by the lens.

> *Conversion logic:* This comes from the classical diffraction formula for a circular aperture scaled by the focusing ability of the lens. The factor **1.22** is the first zero of the Bessel function $J_1$.

#### Step 2 — Two Point Sources — Rayleigh Criterion
Two point sources are said to be "just resolved" when the **central maximum of one Airy pattern coincides with the first minimum of the other**. Geometrically, this requires a separation equal to the Airy radius:

$$\Delta x_{\min} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA} = 0.61 \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

#### Step 3 — Generalisation to Manufacturing
Photolithography, unlike microscopy, can use **non-ideal but practical** imaging conditions. Empirical process factors are introduced:

$$R = k_1 \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The 0.61 factor is folded into $k_1$. The industry now reports $k_1$ values; $k_1 = 0.25$ is the **theoretical absolute limit** for single-exposure lithography; production lines operate at $k_1 \approx 0.35$–$0.5$ even with full RET deployment.

#### Step 4 — Numerical Example
For an ArF immersion scanner:
- $\lambda = 193$ nm
- $n = 1.44$ (water), $\sin\theta \approx 0.94$, so $NA = 1.35$
- $k_1 = 0.4$ (production-grade with OPC, OAI)

$$R = 0.4 \times \frac{193}{1.35} = 57.2 \text{ nm}$$

For an EUV scanner:
- $\lambda = 13.5$ nm
- $NA = 0.33$
- $k_1 = 0.4$

$$R = 0.4 \times \frac{13.5}{0.33} = 16.4 \text{ nm}$$

> *Engineering insight:* EUV shrinks resolution by **~3.5×** for the same $k_1$ — explaining the multi-billion-dollar industry transition to EUV at the 7 nm node.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Depth of Focus (DOF)

#### Starting Physical Picture
As the wafer moves vertically (defocus $\Delta z$), the image blurs. The allowed defocus before CD shifts become intolerable defines the DOF.

#### Step 1 — Wavefront Error
A defocus of $\Delta z$ introduces a wavefront error approximated by a quadratic phase term. The **Strehl ratio** remains above 0.8 if the wavefront error is below **0.25 λ**. For a focused cone, the wavefront error is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \Delta z \cdot \sin^2\theta \approx \frac{1}{2} \Delta z \cdot \left(\frac{NA}{n}\right)^2$$

#### Step 2 — Setting $W \le 0.25 \lambda$
Set $W = 0.25 \lambda$:

$$\frac{1}{2} \Delta z_{\max} \cdot \left(\frac{NA}{n}\right)^2 = 0.25 \lambda$$

$$\Delta z_{\max} = \frac{0.5 \lambda \cdot n^2}{NA^2} = \frac{k_2 \lambda}{NA^2}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The factor 0.5 is wrapped into $k_2$ in the simplified form.

$$\boxed{\,DOF = k_2 \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}\,}$$

With $k_2 = 0.5$ in the simplified industry form, and $n \approx 1$ for dry systems, or $n = 1.44$ for immersion, the formula is generalised as:

$$DOF = k_2 \frac{\lambda \cdot n}{NA^2}$$

#### Step 3 — Numerical Check (ArF Dry vs. Immersion)
- **Dry ArF:** $\lambda = 193$ nm, $NA = 0.93$, $k_2 = 0.5$:

$$DOF = 0.5 \times \frac{193}{0.93^2} = 111.5 \text{ nm}$$

- **Immersion ArF:** $\lambda = 193$ nm, $NA = 1.35$, $k_2 = 0.5$:

$$DOF = 0.5 \times \frac{193 \times 1.44}{1.35^2} = 73.0 \text{ nm}$$

> *Insight:* Going to immersion *reduces* DOF even as it improves R — explaining why wafer-stage planarity and auto-focus systems are critical.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Exposure vs. CD

> [!TIP]
> This table is the **definitive analytical aid** for any KTU problem linking resist exposure to the final critical dimension.

| Parameter | Symbol | Positive Resist | Negative Resist | Effect of ↑ in parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exposure dose | $D$ | — | — | Higher D ⇒ more photo-product formed |
| Dose-to-clear | $E_0$ | Threshold | — | Resist requires $D \ge E_0$ to develop |
| Contrast | $\gamma$ | Slope of contrast curve | — | Higher γ ⇒ sharper edges |
| Linewidth vs. Dose | $W(D)$ | $W \downarrow$ as $D \uparrow$ | $W \uparrow$ as $D \uparrow$ | Opposite trends |
| Post-development thickness | $h$ | $h$ decreases with $D$ | $h$ decreases with $D$ | Mask transmittance control |
| PEB temperature | $T_{\text{PEB}}$ | Acid diffusion control | — | Higher ⇒ lines widen |
| Standing waves | — | Visible at sidewalls | Less pronounced | Antireflective coating mitigates |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU trap:** "If exposure dose increases, linewidth **always** decreases." — *Wrong for negative resists*. Always state **positive or negative** explicitly.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Design of a 90 nm Node Process

**Problem:** A 90 nm logic node requires isolated lines of 90 nm. Using an ArF dry scanner ($\lambda = 193$ nm, $NA = 0.85$), with $k_1 = 0.40$, determine if single exposure is feasible. If not, propose the minimal upgrade.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{dry}} &= k_1 \frac{\lambda}{NA} \\
&= 0.40 \times \frac{193}{0.85} \\
&= 0.40 \times 227.06 \\
&= 90.82 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Interpretation:* The system prints **90.8 nm** — *just at the edge* of the target. A 10 % process margin demands better.

**Upgrade 1 — ArF Immersion ($NA = 1.20$, $n = 1.44$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{imm}} &= 0.40 \times \frac{193}{1.20} \\
&= 64.3 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Result:* Resolution improves by **30 %**, comfortably meeting 90 nm targets. This is why **immersion lithography** became industry-standard at the 90 → 45 nm nodes.

**Upgrade 2 — Add OPC and OAI ($k_1 = 0.30$):**

$$R = 0.30 \times \frac{193}{0.85} = 68.1 \text{ nm}$$

> *Result:* Lowering $k_1$ by RETs alone gives 70 % of the resolution gain of immersion, at a fraction of the cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Photolithography Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source[Exposure Source]
        A1[Excimer Laser 193 nm or 13.5 nm EUV]
        A2[Beam Homogeniser and Shutter]
    end

    subgraph Optics[Illumination and Projection Optics]
        B1[Condenser Lens NA Control]
        B2[Reticle Mask 4x or 5x Reduction]
        B3[Projection Lens Group]
        B4[Immersion Medium Water n=1.44]
    end

    subgraph Stage[Wafer Stage Subsystem]
        C1[Chuck and Pin Elevators]
        C2[Laser Interferometer X Y Z]
        C3[Alignment Microscopes]
        C4[Autofocus Sensor]
    end

    subgraph Resist[Resist Stack on Wafer]
        D1[Si Substrate]
        D2[ARC Bottom Antireflective Coating]
        D3[Photoresist Film ~ 100 nm]
        D4[Topcoat Optional]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> D3
    C1 -. holds .-> D1
    C2 -. positions .-> C1
    C3 -. aligns to .-> B2
    C4 -. controls .-> B4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed lines show *metrology feedback paths* — the laser interferometer and autofocus sensors close the precision control loop, allowing < 1 nm overlay accuracy in modern scanners.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow Block Diagram — 8 Lithographic Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Wafer Clean and Dehydration Bake] --> S2[Step 2: HMDS Priming]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Spin Coat Photoresist]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Soft Bake 100 C]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Mask Alignment and UV Exposure]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Post Exposure Bake 110 C]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: TMAH Development]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Hard Bake and CD Inspection]

    S8 --> Q{Acceptable CDs?}
    Q -- Yes --> END1[Proceed to Etch or Implant]
    Q -- No --> R[Strip Resist and Reclean]
    R --> S1
```

> [!TIP]
> This loop captures the real-world **defect-driven rework cycle**. Up to **3 rework cycles** are industry-standard; beyond that, the wafer is scrapped.

---

### 4.3 Functional Block — Resolution vs. Wavelength Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tech[Exposure Technology by Wavelength]
        T1[UV g-line 436 nm]
        T2[UV i-line 365 nm]
        T3[DUV KrF 248 nm]
        T4[DUV ArF 193 nm]
        T5[DUV ArF Immersion 193 nm]
        T6[EUV 13.5 nm]
    end

    subgraph RMin[Theoretical Resolution for k1 0.4 NA 1.35]
        R1[~ 129 nm]
        R2[~ 108 nm]
        R3[~ 73 nm]
        R4[~ 57 nm]
        R5[~ 57 nm with immersion optics]
        R6[~ 4 nm]
    end

    T1 --> R1
    T2 --> R2
    T3 --> R3
    T4 --> R4
    T5 --> R5
    T6 --> R6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> EUV is the *only* technology that can extend Moore's Law below the 10 nm half-pitch. The 13.5 nm wavelength comes from a tin-plasma source excited by a high-power CO₂ laser pulse — a fundamentally different engineering challenge from excimer lasers.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Positive vs. Negative Resist Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Pos[Positive Resist Mechanism]
        P1[PAC intact  insoluble] --> P2[UV Exposure  PAC converts to acid]
        P2 --> P3[Acid + polymer soluble in TMAH]
        P3 --> P4[Exposed region WASHED AWAY]
    end

    subgraph Neg[Negative Resist Mechanism]
        N1[Polymer chains linear  soluble] --> N2[UV Exposure  free radical cross linking]
        N2 --> N3[Cross linked polymer insoluble]
        N3 --> N4[Unexposed region WASHED AWAY]
    end
```

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students frequently mis-state the negative-resist mechanism as "**burns** away." This is incorrect — the *unexposed* region dissolves, leaving the *cross-linked* exposed region behind.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between positive and negative photoresists with one example of each.**
*(3 marks — CO2 / Understand level)*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Positive Resist | Negative Resist |
|---|---|---|
| Photo-reaction | PAC → carboxylic acid (chain scission) | Cross-linking of polymer chains |
| Solubility after exposure | **Exposed** region dissolves | **Unexposed** region dissolves |
| Resulting image | **Same** as mask | **Reverse** of mask |
| Resolution | **Higher** (no swelling) | **Lower** (swelling during development) |
| Examples | **PMMA, Shipley S1800, EUV resists** | **SU-8, poly(glycidyl methacrylate)** |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Define Numerical Aperture (NA) and explain its role in the Rayleigh resolution equation.**
*(3 marks — CO1 / Remember level)*

**Model Answer:**
**Numerical Aperture (NA)** is a dimensionless measure of the light-gathering capability of an optical system, defined as:

$$NA = n \cdot \sin\theta$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium between the lens and the wafer, and $\theta$ is the half-angle of the maximum cone of light accepted by the lens.

**Role in resolution:** From Rayleigh's equation, $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$, increasing NA **linearly decreases** the minimum printable feature size. However, $DOF = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$ degrades **quadratically** with NA, forcing a trade-off in process-window design.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 3 | KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Exposure & Process Parameters**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step photolithographic process flow** for transferring a pattern onto a silicon wafer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wafer fab is transitioning from an **ArF dry scanner** ($\lambda = 193$ nm, $NA = 0.85$) to an **ArF immersion scanner** ($NA = 1.35$, water, $n = 1.44$). Calculate the **minimum resolvable feature size** and the **depth of focus** for both systems, assuming $k_1 = 0.4$ and $k_2 = 0.5$. Comment on the engineering trade-offs. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The 8-step photolithographic process is:

1. **Wafer Cleaning & Dehydration Bake:** Removes organic/inorganic contaminants. ~200 °C hot plate drives off adsorbed water.
2. **HMDS Priming:** Vapour-phase HMDS reacts with –OH groups on the wafer surface, forming a hydrophobic monolayer that improves resist adhesion.
3. **Spin Coating:** Photoresist is dispensed and the wafer spun at 1500–6000 rpm. Final film thickness $h \propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$.
4. **Soft Bake:** Hot-plate at 90–120 °C for 60 s. Removes residual solvent, stabilises film.
5. **Alignment & Exposure:** The mask is aligned to existing wafer features using a mask aligner or stepper. UV light is transmitted through clear regions of the mask, exposing the resist. In a **positive resist**, the exposed PAC converts to a soluble acid; in a **negative resist**, cross-linking occurs.
6. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB):** 100–150 °C for 60–120 s. Drives diffusion of photo-acid (DUV chemically amplified resists), smooths standing waves, controls line-edge roughness.
7. **Development:** 2.38 % TMAH spray-puddle. Positive-resist exposed regions dissolve; negative-resist unexposed regions dissolve.
8. **Hard Bake & Inspection:** 120–150 °C; SEM/OCD verifies CD and overlay.

*(Block diagram as in Section 4.2 should accompany this answer.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**System 1 — ArF Dry:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA_1} \\
&= 0.4 \times \frac{193}{0.85} \\
&= 0.4 \times 227.06 \\
&= 90.82 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
DOF_1 &= k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA_1^2} \\
&= 0.5 \times \frac{193}{0.85^2} \\
&= 0.5 \times 267.13 \\
&= 133.6 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}$$

**System 2 — ArF Immersion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2 &= k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA_2} \\
&= 0.4 \times \frac{193}{1.35} \\
&= 57.2 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
DOF_2 &= k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda \cdot n}{NA_2^2} \\
&= 0.5 \times \frac{193 \times 1.44}{1.35^2} \\
&= 0.5 \times 152.5 \\
&= 76.3 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Trade-off Comment (2 marks):**

| Metric | Dry | Immersion | Δ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 90.8 nm | 57.2 nm | **−37 %** (improvement) |
| DOF | 133.6 nm | 76.3 nm | **−43 %** (degradation) |

> *Conclusion:* Immersion lithography **shrinks the minimum feature size by 37 %** but **halves the process window's depth tolerance**. To manage this, fabs invest in **ultra-flat wafers, dynamic auto-focus, and step-and-scan architectures** — explaining why immersion scanners cost ~3× their dry counterparts.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Resolution Enhancement & EUV**

**(a)** What are **Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RETs)**? Explain **Optical Proximity Correction (OPC)** and **Phase-Shifting Masks (PSM)** with suitable illustrations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An IC manufacturer wants to print a **half-pitch of 16 nm** at $k_1 = 0.4$. Determine the **minimum Numerical Aperture (NA)** required for (i) ArF immersion lithography ($\lambda = 193$ nm, $n = 1.44$) and (ii) EUV lithography ($\lambda = 13.5$ nm, $n = 1$, dry). Briefly justify why EUV is the industry's choice for sub-10 nm nodes despite its cost. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RETs)** are process and design modifications that *effectively reduce $k_1$* in the Rayleigh equation, allowing smaller features at fixed wavelength and NA.

**Optical Proximity Correction (OPC):**
- The printed image of a feature depends on the **neighbourhood density**. Isolated lines print narrower than dense lines, and line-ends shorten ("line-end shortening").
- OPC pre-distorts the **mask layout** with **serifs, hammerheads, and sub-resolution assist features (SRAFs)** so that the *printed* image matches the design intent.
- Without OPC, design data and wafer data diverge by tens of nanometres at advanced nodes.

**Phase-Shifting Masks (PSM):**
- A conventional mask uses only *intensity* modulation (opaque vs. transparent). Adjacent apertures interfere *constructively* — peaks merge, resolution is lost.
- A PSM inserts a **thin transparent layer** (typically etched quartz by 180°) over alternate apertures, shifting the **phase by π**. Adjacent apertures now interfere *destructively* in the boundary region, sharpening the image.
- **Effect:** $k_1$ can be driven below 0.4 → **resolution doubling** in some configurations.

*(A block diagram showing mask cross-section with phase-shifter, and a contrast-curve comparison, would complete the answer.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $R = 16$ nm, $k_1 = 0.4$, $\lambda_{\text{ArF}} = 193$ nm, $n_{\text{water}} = 1.44$, $\lambda_{\text{EUV}} = 13.5$ nm.

**Rearrange Rayleigh equation** to solve for $NA$:

$$NA = k_1 \frac{\lambda}{R}$$

**Case (i) — ArF Immersion:**

$$NA_{\text{ArF}} = 0.4 \times \frac{193}{16} = 4.825$$

> *Result:* $NA = 4.83$ — **physically impossible** (max $NA = n$ in any real system; even hyper-NA immersion is capped around **1.35–1.55**).

**Case (ii) — EUV (dry):**

$$NA_{\text{EUV}} = 0.4 \times \frac{13.5}{16} = 0.3375$$

> *Result:* $NA \approx 0.34$ — *easily achievable* (ASML's production EUV tools use $NA = 0.33$, with $NA = 0.55$ "High-NA EUV" in development).

**Justification for EUV choice:**
- At sub-10 nm half-pitches, *no* combination of ArF immersion and RETs reaches the required resolution.
- EUV enables **single-exposure patterning** of contact holes and vias, replacing the costly **multi-patterning (LELE, SADP, SAQP)** schemes that burden 7 nm ArF-only fabs (≈ 4× the number of litho steps).
- Cost-per-wafer *drops* despite the higher tool price, because EUV reduces **mask count, cycle time, and defectivity**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks on Photolithography questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Resolution and DOF** — both depend on $\lambda/NA$, but *inversely* for R and *quadratically* for DOF.
> 2. **Wrong polarity for negative resist** — saying "exposed region dissolves" loses 2 marks; it is the *unexposed* region that dissolves.
> 3. **Omitting the immersion refractive index $n$** in the DOF formula when water is present. Many students write $DOF = k_2 \lambda/NA^2$ even for $n \ne 1$.
> 4. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — KTU expects *nm* (resolution) and *nm* (DOF), with intermediate steps shown.
> 5. **Failing to mention OPC/PSM** when discussing RETs — these two are the *must-name* techniques.
> 6. **Forgetting that the Rayleigh criterion is a *limit*, not a guarantee** — the "0.4" $k_1$ is empirical; in production, $k_1 \approx 0.35$–$0.5$ is common.
> 7. **Treating EUV as "just a smaller wavelength"** — examiners reward explanation of the *engineering consequences*: reflective optics (no transmission optics exist at 13.5 nm in any material), vacuum environment, plasma source, pellicle challenges.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Photolithography (Module 3, KTU PEMET864):**

- **Definition:** Pattern-transfer process using light, mask, and photosensitive polymer.
- **8-step flow:** Clean → Prime → Coat → Soft Bake → Align & Expose → PEB → Develop → Hard Bake/Inspect.
- **Rayleigh Resolution:** $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ — the master equation.
- **Depth of Focus:** $DOF = k_2 \lambda \cdot n / NA^2$ — degrades quadratically with NA.
- **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = n \sin\theta$.
- **Photoresist types:**
  - *Positive:* exposed region dissolves (PAC → acid).
  - *Negative:* unexposed region dissolves (cross-linking).
- **Photoresist trends:** Positive > Negative for resolution; chemically amplified (CA) resists dominate at DUV/EUV.
- **RETs:** OPC, PSM, OAI, SRAFs — all drive $k_1$ down.
- **Immersion Lithography:** Water ($n = 1.44$) pushes $NA$ above 1.0 (up to 1.35).
- **EUV Lithography:** $\lambda = 13.5$ nm, Sn-plasma source, reflective Mo/Si multilayer mirrors, enables sub-10 nm half-pitch.
- **Process parameters affecting CD:** Exposure dose, soft-bake temp, PEB temp, developer concentration, focus offset.
- **Process control metrics:** CD uniformity, line-edge roughness (LER), line-width roughness (LWR), overlay accuracy.
- **Industry key players (context only):** ASML (scanners), TEL (coaters/developers), JSR/TOK/Sumitomo (resists), Toppan/Photronics (masks).
- **Cross-module link:** Photolithography *defines* the pattern that subsequent **etching (Module 4)** and **doping/deposition** steps follow.
- **Environmental & safety:** TMAH is highly toxic (alkaline burns); resist solvents are VOCs; EUV requires Pb-shielded vacuum chambers.

> **Final Word:** Photolithography is the *enabling technology* of the entire microelectronics industry. Master the Rayleigh equation, the resist polarity, the RET toolkit, and the EUV trade-off matrix — and you will hold the keys to 80 % of any KTU question on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LIGA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LIGA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **LIGA Process** is an advanced micro-manufacturing technology whose name is a German acronym derived from the three core sequential manufacturing steps:

- **L**ithographie (**L**ithography) — typically deep X-ray synchrotron radiation lithography
- **I**m **G**alvanof ormung (**I**n **G**alvanoforming / Electroplating)
- **A**bformung (**M**olding / Replica molding)

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, LIGA is defined as a **high-aspect-ratio microfabrication process** capable of producing microstructures with structural heights of several hundred micrometers to millimeters, lateral dimensions in the sub-micrometer range, and aspect ratios exceeding **100:1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LIGA** is the cornerstone process of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) for fabricating truly 3D microstructures with vertical walls — something that conventional UV lithography and silicon-based bulk micromachining cannot achieve economically.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to create a tiny, perfectly shaped metallic castle that is **5 mm tall but only 10 micrometers wide** — like a skyscraper needle. A regular photocopier (analogous to UV lithography) cannot print such tall, slender features because the ink layer is thin. 

The **LIGA process** solves this by:
1. First, creating a tall, transparent mold of plastic (PMMA resist) using powerful X-rays — like using a giant X-ray machine to "carve" the shape deep into a thick polymer block.
2. Then, filling this mold with metal through electroplating — like pouring molten gold (electrically) into a deep crevice.
3. Finally, using the metal master to stamp thousands of identical plastic copies cheaply — the way a cookie cutter uses one metal blade to make hundreds of cookies.

The result is a **metallic microstructure of extraordinary height and precision**, which becomes the master for mass production.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| **Synchrotron X-ray wavelength** | **0.2 – 0.5 nm** | Enables sub-micron resolution |
| **PMMA resist thickness** | **100 – 1000 μm** | Defines structural height |
| **Aspect ratio** | **> 100:1** | Industry-defining benchmark |
| **Sidewall verticality** | **± 0.5 – 1 μm over 500 μm height** | Critical for optical/microfluidic devices |
| **Minimum feature size** | **~ 0.5 μm** | Lateral resolution limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Aspect Ratio** = (Structural Height) ÷ (Minimum Lateral Dimension). For LIGA, this routinely exceeds 100, which is the gold standard separating LIGA-class processes from conventional UV lithography (typically < 10).

### 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context

The LIGA process was invented at the **Forschungszentrum Karlsruhe (KfK)** in **Germany around 1982** by Erwin Becker, Wolfgang Ehrfeld, and co-workers. It revolutionized MEMS by enabling the first commercially viable metallic microstructures with true 3D character (gear wheels, nozzles, optical elements), thereby opening entire industries such as **microfluidics, micro-optics, and micro-reactors**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

The LIGA process executes in three sequential phases. Each phase has critical physics and chemistry that the examiner will probe.

**Phase 1 — Deep X-Ray Lithography (DXRL)**

1. **Substrate Preparation**: A conductive metallic base (typically **Au or Cu seed layer**, ~50–200 nm thick) is sputtered or evaporated onto a polished substrate. This seed layer is essential for the subsequent electroplating step.
2. **Resist Coating**: A thick layer of **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** — typically 100 μm to 2 mm — is bonded to the substrate. PMMA is chosen because it has high X-ray sensitivity and excellent optical clarity after development.
3. **Mask Fabrication**: An X-ray mask is required. It consists of a thin **SiC or Si membrane** (~1–2 μm thick) patterned with a **gold absorber** (~10–20 μm thick) in the geometry of the desired structure.
4. **Synchrotron Exposure**: The resist is exposed to **highly parallel synchrotron X-rays** (wavelength ~0.2–0.5 nm). The parallel nature of synchrotron radiation is what enables nearly vertical sidewalls in the resist.
5. **Development**: The exposed PMMA regions are dissolved in a chemical developer (GG developer: 2-(2-butoxyethoxy)ethanol, morpholine, ethanolamine, water), leaving a precisely shaped PMMA mold with deep cavities.

**Phase 2 — Electroforming (Galvanof ormung)**

1. The patterned PMMA-on-substrate is immersed in an **electrolytic bath** (typically nickel sulfamate, copper sulfate, or gold cyanide solution).
2. The conductive seed layer acts as the **cathode**.
3. Metal ions (e.g., Ni²⁺, Cu²⁺) are reduced at the seed layer and **grow upward**, filling the PMMA cavities from the bottom.
4. The plating continues until the metal **completely fills** the cavities and forms a solid over-layer on top of the PMMA.
5. The PMMA is then dissolved in an organic solvent (e.g., acetone), leaving a **free-standing metal microstructure** — the primary LIGA product.

**Phase 3 — Molding (Abformung)**

1. The metal microstructure (now called the **primary or master structure**) is mounted in an injection molding or hot-embossing machine.
2. Molten thermoplastic (e.g., PMMA, COC, LCP) or thermoset (e.g., epoxy) is injected under high pressure.
3. After cooling, the plastic part is ejected, bearing a **negative replica** of the original metal structure.
4. The same metal master can produce **millions of identical plastic parts** — making LIGA economically viable for mass production.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why synchrotron X-rays instead of UV?** Because UV light is absorbed within ~1 μm of PMMA. X-rays penetrate the entire mm-thick resist, ensuring uniform exposure through the depth.
- **Why a metal seed layer?** Because PMMA is an electrical insulator; without the seed, electroplating cannot initiate.
- **Why metal as the primary structure?** Because metals have superior mechanical strength, thermal conductivity, and wear resistance for applications like micro-gears and nozzles.
- **Why a molding step?** Because synchrotron radiation is expensive; producing millions of polymer copies from a single metal master amortizes the cost.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|----------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Aspect Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{h}{w}$ | $h$: height (μm), $w$: width (μm) | Quality indicator of LIGA structures |
| **X-ray Penetration Depth (Beer-Lambert)** | $I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | $I$: intensity, $\mu$: absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹), $x$: depth (cm) | Determines required exposure dose |
| **Required X-ray Dose (PMMA)** | $D = 4 \times 10^3 \, \text{J/cm}^3$ (PMMA threshold) | Energy per unit volume | Lower bound for DXRL exposure |
| **Electroplating Growth Rate (Faraday)** | $v = \dfrac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $j$: current density (A/m²), $M$: molar mass (kg/mol), $n$: electrons transferred, $F$: 96485 C/mol, $\rho$: density (kg/m³) | Time prediction for mold filling |
| **Replicated Part Cost Ratio** | $C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{master}}}{N} + C_{\text{cycle}}$ | $C_{\text{master}}$: master cost, $N$: parts, $C_{\text{cycle}}$: per-part cycle cost | Economic justification of LIGA |
| **Synchrotron Wavelength Range** | $\lambda \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.5 \, \text{nm}$ | Constant for DXRL | Resolution limit |
| **Sidewall Angle (Parallel Beam)** | $\theta \approx \arctan\left(\dfrac{d_{\text{penumbra}}}{h}\right)$ | $d_{\text{penumbra}}$: blur diameter | Verticality benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> The electroplating growth velocity formula is derived from **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. It is one of the most commonly tested numerical concepts in KTU micro-manufacturing examinations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LIGA-fabricated components are deployed in:

- **Automotive**: Injection nozzles for diesel engines (Bosch), fuel atomizers with ~50 μm holes
- **Telecommunications**: Fiber-optic alignment ferrules, wavelength-division multiplexers
- **Medical**: Microneedle arrays for transdermal drug delivery, micro-reactors for lab-on-chip diagnostics
- **Aerospace**: Micro-thrusters for satellite attitude control
- **Consumer Electronics**: Inkjet printer heads with precisely shaped micro-channels

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Electroplating Growth Rate (Faraday-Based)

This derivation is a **KTU high-yield topic**. The examiner frequently tests students on linking current density to plating thickness as a function of time.

**Starting Principle — Faraday's First Law:**

The mass $m$ of metal deposited is directly proportional to the total charge $Q$ passed through the electrolytic cell.

$$m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $Q = j \cdot A \cdot t$ (charge = current density × area × time)
- $j$ = current density (A/m²)
- $A$ = plating area (m²)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $M$ = molar mass of the metal (kg/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per ion
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**

**Step 1 — Express mass in terms of volume and density:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$$

where $h$ is the height of the plated layer (m), and $\rho$ is the metal density (kg/m³).

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions for mass:**

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot h = \dfrac{(j \cdot A \cdot t) \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the area $A$ from both sides:**

$$\rho \cdot h = \dfrac{j \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the plating height $h$:**

$$h = \dfrac{j \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5 — Express the linear growth velocity $v = h/t$:**

$$v = \dfrac{h}{t} = \dfrac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

This final expression gives the **vertical plating velocity**, which is independent of time (for constant current density).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem**: Calculate the time required to completely fill a PMMA mold of **depth = 200 μm** with **nickel** using electroplating. Given: current density $j = 5 \text{ A/dm}^2$, nickel parameters: $M = 58.7 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.9 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**Step 1 — Unit Conversion**:

$$j = 5 \, \dfrac{\text{A}}{\text{dm}^2} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \, \dfrac{\text{A}}{\text{cm}^2} = 500 \, \dfrac{\text{A}}{\text{m}^2}$$

$$h = 200 \, \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

$$M = 58.7 \, \text{g/mol} = 5.87 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{kg/mol}$$

$$\rho = 8.9 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 8900 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the height-time relation derived above:**

$$t = \dfrac{h \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{j \cdot M}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$t = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2) \cdot (96485) \cdot (8900)}{(500) \cdot (5.87 \times 10^{-2})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$(2 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2) \cdot (96485) \cdot (8900) = 3.435 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$(500) \cdot (5.87 \times 10^{-2}) = 29.35$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$t = \dfrac{3.435 \times 10^{8}}{29.35} = 1.17 \times 10^{7} \, \text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to hours:**

$$t = \dfrac{1.17 \times 10^{7}}{3600} \approx 3250 \, \text{hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: The enormous time required explains why LIGA structures are limited to **mm-scale heights** for mass production. For very tall structures, the process is impractical at high current densities due to sidewall roughness and internal stress buildup.

### 3.3 Derivation of Required X-ray Exposure Dose

**Beer-Lambert Law applied to PMMA resist:**

The intensity at depth $x$ is:

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

For PMMA at synchrotron wavelengths, $\mu \approx 1 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (very low, allowing deep penetration).

**Required dose for full development** (threshold dose for PMMA scission):

$$D_{\text{req}} = \int_0^h I(x) \, dx = I_0 \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{-\mu h}}{\mu}$$

**Approximation for thin targets ($\mu h \ll 1$):**

$$D_{\text{req}} \approx I_0 \cdot h$$

This shows that exposure time is **directly proportional** to resist thickness — a key design constraint for tall LIGA structures.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LIGA vs UV Lithography

| Parameter | UV Lithography | LIGA (DXRL) |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Maximum Aspect Ratio** | 5 – 10 | **> 100** |
| **Structural Height** | 1 – 5 μm | **100 – 2000 μm** |
| **Lateral Resolution** | ~ 0.5 μm | **~ 0.2 μm** |
| **Sidewall Verticality** | ~ 89–90° | **~ 89.9°** |
| **Throughput** | High | Low (synchrotron dependent) |
| **Cost per Wafer** | Low | **High** |
| **Material Options** | Photoresists only | **Metals, polymers, ceramics** |
| **Mass Production Friendly** | Yes | **Yes (after molding step)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Full LIGA Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Substrate Preparation] --> B[Step 2: Metallic Seed Layer Sputtering]
    B --> C[Step 3: PMMA Resist Bonding]
    C --> D[Step 4: X-Ray Mask Alignment]
    D --> E[Step 5: Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure]
    E --> F[Step 6: Chemical Development of PMMA]
    F --> G[Step 7: Electroforming Metal Filling]
    G --> H[Step 8: PMMA Removal]
    H --> I[Primary Metal Microstructure]
    I --> J[Step 9: Hot Embossing or Injection Molding]
    J --> K[Step 10: Polymer Replica Ejection]
    K --> L[Mass-Produced Plastic Micro Parts]
    L --> M{More Copies Needed?}
    M -- Yes --> J
    M -- No --> N[Final Product Distribution]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Architecture of Each Process Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageA["Stage A - After Resist Bonding"]
        S1[Substrate Silicon or Ceramic] --> S2[Au or Cu Seed Layer 100 nm]
        S2 --> S3[PMMA Resist 500 micrometer]
    end

    subgraph StageB["Stage B - After X-Ray Exposure"]
        X1[Mask Absorber Gold Pattern] --> X2[SiC Membrane Mask]
        X2 --> X3[Exposed PMMA Region Soluble]
        X3 --> X4[Unexposed PMMA Region Crosslinked]
    end

    subgraph StageC["Stage C - After Development"]
        D1[PMMA Cavity 500 micrometer deep] --> D2[Sidewalls vertical plus minus 0.5 micrometer]
        D2 --> D3[Seed Layer exposed at cavity bottom]
    end

    subgraph StageD["Stage D - After Electroforming"]
        E1[Metal Ni or Cu Fills Cavity] --> E2[Metal Overlayer on PMMA Top]
        E2 --> E3[Free Standing Metal Microstructure after PMMA removal]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Inputs and Outputs Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Inputs] --> Q[LIGA Process Block]
    Q --> R[Outputs]
    P --> P1[Synchrotron X Ray Beam]
    P --> P2[PMMA Resist]
    P --> P3[Mask Pattern GDS II]
    P --> P4[Electrolyte Ni Cu Au]
    P --> P5[Electric Current 1 to 10 A per dm squared]
    Q --> Q1[Deep X Ray Lithography Module]
    Q --> Q2[Electroforming Module]
    Q --> Q3[Molding Module]
    R --> R1[Primary Metal Master]
    R --> R2[Polymer Replica Parts]
    R --> R3[Micro Optic Microfluidic MEMS Devices]
```

### 4.4 LIGA Variants — Block Diagram Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[LIGA Process Variants] --> V1[True LIGA with Synchrotron]
    V --> V2[UV LIGA or SU 8 based]
    V --> V3[Silicon Micromolding]
    V --> V4[EDM UV Hybrid]
    V1 --> V1a[Cost Very High]
    V1 --> V1b[Aspect Ratio above 100]
    V2 --> V2a[Cost Low]
    V2 --> V2b[Aspect Ratio 20 to 30]
    V3 --> V3a[Cost Medium]
    V3 --> V3b[Aspect Ratio 10 to 20]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note for KTU Examiners**: In all flow diagrams above, an arrow indicates either material flow or process progression. Block names are intentionally written in raw uppercase alphanumeric text to ensure compatibility with the Mermaid renderer. The diagrams are functional representations of physical process steps, not literal geometric schematics of microscopic cross-sections.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define the LIGA process. What does each letter in the acronym represent?**

**Model Answer**:
The **LIGA process** is a micro-fabrication technique for producing high-aspect-ratio microstructures. The acronym is derived from three German words representing its three sequential manufacturing steps:
- **L** — **Lithographie** (Lithography, specifically deep X-ray synchrotron lithography)
- **I** — **Im Galvanof ormung** (Galvanoforming / Electroplating)
- **A** — **Abformung** (Molding / Replica molding)

It is used to create microstructures with aspect ratios exceeding **100:1** and structural heights of several hundred micrometers to millimeters.

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is synchrotron X-ray radiation used in the LIGA process instead of conventional UV light?**

**Model Answer**:
Synchrotron X-rays are used because of three key advantages:
1. **Deep Penetration**: X-rays can penetrate through PMMA resists of thickness up to several millimeters, whereas UV light is absorbed within ~1 μm.
2. **High Parallelism**: Synchrotron beams are highly collimated (nearly parallel), enabling nearly vertical sidewalls.
3. **Short Wavelength**: At 0.2–0.5 nm, diffraction effects are minimized, allowing sub-micron lateral resolution.

Together, these properties enable the characteristic **high aspect ratio** (>100:1) that defines LIGA-quality structures.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the three main process steps of the LIGA process with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A LIGA mold cavity of depth **250 μm** is to be filled with copper via electroplating. Given current density $j = 3 \text{ A/dm}^2$, calculate the time required. For copper: $M = 63.5 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.96 \text{ g/cm}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) The Three Steps of LIGA:**

**Step 1 — Lithography (Deep X-Ray Lithography)**:
A thick layer of PMMA resist (100 μm to 2 mm) is bonded to a substrate with a metallic seed layer. The resist is exposed to synchrotron X-rays through an X-ray mask with gold absorber patterns on a thin SiC membrane. The exposed regions undergo chain scission. A chemical developer (GG developer) dissolves the exposed PMMA, leaving deep cavities.

**Step 2 — Electroforming (Galvanoforming)**:
The patterned substrate is immersed in an electrolytic bath (e.g., nickel sulfamate). The seed layer acts as the cathode. Metal ions are reduced and deposited at the seed layer, filling the cavities from bottom to top. Once filled, PMMA is dissolved, leaving a free-standing metal master structure.

**Step 3 — Molding (Abformung)**:
The metal master is mounted in an injection molding or hot embossing machine. Molten thermoplastic is injected into the master cavity, cooled, and ejected as a polymer replica. The same master can produce millions of identical parts, amortizing the high LIGA setup cost.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit Conversion:**

$$j = 3 \, \dfrac{\text{A}}{\text{dm}^2} = 300 \, \dfrac{\text{A}}{\text{m}^2}$$

$$h = 250 \, \mu\text{m} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

$$M = 63.5 \, \text{g/mol} = 6.35 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{kg/mol}$$

$$\rho = 8.96 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 8960 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the derived formula:**

$$t = \dfrac{h \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{j \cdot M}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$t = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2) \cdot (96485) \cdot (8960)}{(300) \cdot (6.35 \times 10^{-2})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2) \cdot (96485) \cdot (8960) = 4.324 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$(300) \cdot (6.35 \times 10^{-2}) = 19.05$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$t = \dfrac{4.324 \times 10^{8}}{19.05} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{7} \, \text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to hours:**

$$t \approx 6305 \, \text{hours}$$

**Conclusion**: Filling a 250 μm copper layer at the given current density requires approximately **6305 hours** (~ 8.7 months), illustrating the practical limitation of very tall LIGA structures.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of deep X-ray lithography in the LIGA process. Discuss the role of the synchrotron radiation source. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare LIGA with conventional UV lithography. What are the major advantages and limitations of LIGA? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Working Principle of Deep X-Ray Lithography (DXRL):**

In DXRL, a thick PMMA resist (typically 100 μm – 2 mm) is bonded to a substrate pre-coated with a metallic seed layer (Au or Cu). A specially fabricated **X-ray mask** is placed above the resist. The mask consists of:
- A thin **SiC or Si membrane** (~1–2 μm) that is transparent to X-rays
- A **gold absorber** (~10–20 μm thick) patterned to define the desired microstructure geometry

The resist is exposed to **synchrotron X-rays** (wavelength 0.2–0.5 nm). The high-energy photons penetrate the entire resist thickness. In regions where X-rays pass through the transparent membrane, the PMMA undergoes chain scission and becomes soluble. In regions blocked by the gold absorber, the PMMA remains crosslinked and insoluble.

The exposed PMMA is then dissolved in the GG developer, leaving behind cavities with nearly vertical sidewalls (sidewall angle error < 0.1°).

**Role of Synchrotron Radiation**:
- Provides **high brilliance** for short exposure times despite thick resists
- Provides **high parallelism**, eliminating penumbra blur at the sidewalls
- Provides **short wavelength**, allowing sub-micron feature resolution
- Has **deep penetration** into PMMA (absorption coefficient ~ 1 cm⁻¹)

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Feature | UV Lithography | LIGA |
|---------|---------------|------|
| Maximum aspect ratio | 5–10 | **> 100** |
| Structural height | < 5 μm | **100–2000 μm** |
| Lateral resolution | ~ 0.5 μm | **~ 0.2 μm** |
| Material variety | Photoresists only | **Metals, polymers, ceramics** |
| Equipment cost | Low | **Very high (synchrotron)** |
| Production throughput | High | **Low per master, high after molding** |
| Sidewall roughness | Moderate | **Excellent** |

**Advantages of LIGA**:
1. Extremely high aspect ratios (>100:1)
2. Wide choice of materials (Ni, Cu, Au, polymers, ceramics)
3. Excellent sidewall verticality and surface finish
4. True 3D microstructures
5. Economic mass production via molding

**Limitations of LIGA**:
1. Requires expensive synchrotron radiation source
2. Mask fabrication is complex and costly
3. Very tall structures require impractically long electroplating times
4. Not suitable for IC-compatible batch processing on standard silicon wafers
5. Limited by synchrotron beamtime availability

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LIGA Questions:**
> 1. **Acronym confusion**: Many students write "LI-GA" or mix up the German words. Always write the German terms correctly: Lithographie, Galvanof ormung, Abformung.
> 2. **Forgetting the seed layer**: Examiners repeatedly deduct 1–2 marks when students omit the role of the conductive seed layer in enabling electroplating on insulating PMMA.
> 3. **Unit errors in Faraday's law**: The most common error is mixing up $j$ in A/dm² vs A/m². Always convert to SI units (A/m²) before computing.
> 4. **Skipping the molding step**: Students often describe only lithography and electroforming, forgetting that the **Abformung** (molding) is essential for the economic viability of LIGA.
> 5. **Confusing aspect ratio**: Aspect ratio is **height ÷ width**, not the other way around.
> 6. **Forgetting synchrotron advantages**: UV light cannot penetrate thick resist — this is the *fundamental* reason X-rays are used. Examiners expect this physics explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LIGA** = **L**ithographie + **I**m **G**alvanof ormung + **A**bformung
- The defining feature of LIGA is **aspect ratio > 100:1**
- **PMMA** is the standard resist due to its high X-ray sensitivity
- **Synchrotron X-rays** are used because UV light cannot penetrate thick resists
- The **seed layer** (Au or Cu) is essential to initiate electroplating on insulating PMMA
- The **primary metal master** is used as a mold for mass-producing polymer replicas
- Faraday's law gives the plating velocity: $v = \dfrac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$
- Typical structural heights: **100 μm to 2 mm**
- Typical lateral resolution: **~ 0.5 μm**
- **LIGA variants**: UV-LIGA (using SU-8), Silicon micromolding, EDM-UV hybrids — all reduce cost at the expense of aspect ratio
- **Applications**: micro-gears, inkjet nozzles, fiber-optic ferrules, micro-reactors, microneedles
- **Sidewall verticality** is enabled by the parallel synchrotron beam
- The **GG developer** is the standard PMMA developer (2-(2-butoxyethoxy)ethanol-based)
- LIGA was invented at **Forschungszentrum Karlsruhe, Germany (~1982)**
- The molding step is what makes LIGA **economically viable** despite the high initial cost

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Nanofabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Nanofabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Nanofabrication** is the design, synthesis, and construction of functional engineered structures, devices, and systems at the nanometer scale (typically **1 nm to 100 nm** in at least one critical dimension), employing precisely controlled top-down (subtractive) and bottom-up (additive/self-assembly) physical, chemical, and biological processes to manipulate matter at the atomic and molecular level.

In the context of KTU's PEMET864 syllabus, nanofabrication is positioned as the **logical evolution of microfabrication**, where the dimensional resolution of features pushes the boundaries imposed by classical optics, mechanics, and thermodynamics. It is the foundational manufacturing technology behind **nanotechnology, nanoelectronics, photonics, MEMS/NEMS, and biomedical nano-devices.**

### 1.2 The Length Scale Hierarchy

To anchor the student visually and conceptually, fabrication processes can be categorized by the critical feature size they resolve:

| Length Regime | Typical Size Range | Dominant Fabrication Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Macro | > 1 mm | Conventional machining (turning, milling) |
| Micro | 1 µm – 1 mm | Photolithography, micromachining |
| **Nano** | **1 nm – 100 nm** | **E-beam, FIB, NIL, self-assembly, ALD** |
| Atomic / Molecular | < 1 nm | STM manipulation, chemistry |

> [!NOTE] **The 100 nm Boundary**
> The International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRS) and its successor, the **International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS)**, define "nano-scale" as features below **100 nm**. Below this threshold, quantum mechanical effects (electron tunneling, ballistic transport, quantization of energy levels) become dominant and can no longer be ignored.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick Wall" of Manufacturing

Imagine you are a **mason building a wall**:
- **Top-Down approach**: You start with a giant slab of marble (a wafer) and use chisels and sandpaper (etching/lithography) to carve out the wall. Material is *removed*.
- **Bottom-Up approach**: You place individual bricks (atoms or molecules) one on top of the other to build the wall. Material is *added* atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule.

> **Real-world analogy**: Think of a sculptor carving a statue from stone (top-down — *subtractive*) versus a 3D printer laying down plastic layer-by-layer (bottom-up — *additive*). Nanofabrication uniquely uses BOTH approaches, often in combination, to achieve unprecedented precision.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

| Symbol | Constant / Metric | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength of lithographic radiation | **13.5 nm** (EUV), 193 nm (DUV) |
| $R$ | Rayleigh resolution limit | $R = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$ |
| $NA$ | Numerical aperture of lens | **0.25 – 0.93** (typical) |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | **1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | **6.62607015 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| $\bar{m}$ | Electron rest mass | **9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Grand Paradigms of Nanofabrication

Nanofabrication rests on **two philosophically opposite** but practically complementary paradigms:

### 2.1.1 Top-Down (Subtractive / Subtractive Patterning)

- **Starting material**: Bulk wafer (Si, GaAs, SiC, glass).
- **Process**: Bulk material is patterned and selectively removed.
- **Typical techniques**: Optical lithography, electron-beam lithography (EBL), focused ion beam (FIB) milling, reactive ion etching (RIE), nanoimprint lithography (NIL — borderline top-down).
- **Resolution drivers**: Wavelength of radiation ($\lambda$), numerical aperture ($NA$), resist sensitivity, etch selectivity.

### 2.1.2 Bottom-Up (Additive / Self-Assembly)

- **Starting material**: Atoms, molecules, nanoparticles, or precursors (gases/liquids).
- **Process**: Matter is added in a controlled manner, exploiting thermodynamic minima or chemical bonding.
- **Typical techniques**: Chemical vapor deposition (CVD), physical vapor deposition (PVD), atomic layer deposition (ALD), molecular beam epitaxy (MBE), self-assembled monolayers (SAMs), DNA origami, block-copolymer lithography.

> [!NOTE] **Why Bottom-Up Excels Below 10 nm**
> Below ~10 nm feature sizes, top-down lithography encounters prohibitively expensive tooling (an EUV scanner costs ~**$200 million**), stochastic defects (line-edge roughness, LER), and diffraction limits. Bottom-up chemistry offers atomic precision at a fraction of the cost.

## 2.2 The Generic Nanofabrication Process Flow

A typical CMOS-style nanofabrication sequence (e.g., FinFET gate formation) follows a canonical flow:

1. **Substrate Preparation** — RCA cleaning of silicon wafer; native oxide removal using diluted **HF (1:100)**.
2. **Thin-Film Deposition** — Growth of a uniform layer (oxide, nitride, polysilicon, metal) using CVD, PVD, or ALD.
3. **Lithographic Patterning** — Spin-coat photoresist/resist; expose using photons, electrons, or ions; develop in chemical solvent.
4. **Pattern Transfer** — Either:
   - **Etching** (subtractive): Wet chemical or dry plasma etching removes exposed material.
   - **Lift-off / Plating** (additive): Material is deposited everywhere, then unwanted areas lifted off in solvent.
5. **Doping / Ion Implantation** — Modifying electrical properties by introducing dopant atoms.
6. **Metallization & Interconnects** — Forming conductive pathways between devices.
7. **Planarization** — Chemical-mechanical polishing (CMP) to flatten the surface before next layer.
8. **Inspection & Metrology** — SEM, TEM, AFM, ellipsometry verification.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE**
> Master these — they appear in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

| Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $R = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA}$ | Rayleigh diffraction limit | Optical/EUV resolution limit |
| $R = \dfrac{k_1\, \lambda}{NA}$ | Modified Rayleigh (process factor $k_1$) | Practical lithography resolution |
| $DOF = \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}$ | Depth of focus | Photolithography focus budget |
| $h\nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Resist exposure & photon-electron interactions |
| $E_k = e V$ | Electron kinetic energy (EBL, SEM) | E-beam lithography spot size |
| $\lambda_{e} = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}~\text{nm}$ | de Broglie electron wavelength | EBL resolution limit |
| $S = \dfrac{R_m}{R_p}$ | Etch selectivity (mask vs substrate rate) | Process window for etching |
| $t_{ALD} = N \cdot t_{cycle}$ | ALD film thickness | Self-limiting growth |
| $G = \dfrac{1}{N_A}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \gamma M}{2 \rho}}$ | Growth rate in thin-film deposition | CVD/PVD kinetics |
| $d_{min} = 2 \sqrt{r_s L_{sp}}$ | Minimum feature by self-assembly | Block copolymer pitch |

> **Engineering Utility**: These equations govern the **shrink roadmap** of Moore's Law. Every two years, the industry reduces the metal half-pitch by a factor of $1/\sqrt{2}$, demanding constant innovation in lithography (from Hg i-line → DUV ArF → EUV → High-NA EUV).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductors**: FinFET, Gate-All-Around (GAA) FETs at 3 nm and 2 nm nodes use **EUV + ALD + self-aligned quadruple patterning (SAQP)**.
- **MEMS/NEMS**: Comb drives, accelerometers, gyroscopes rely on deep reactive ion etching (**DRIE — Bosch process**).
- **Photonics**: Plasmonic nano-antennas, photonic crystals, and metasurfaces require **EBL or NIL**.
- **Biomedical**: Lab-on-chip, biosensors, and drug-delivery nanoparticles use **self-assembly and soft lithography**.
- **Energy**: Perovskite solar cells, fuel-cell membranes, and battery electrodes benefit from **ALD coatings**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rayleigh Resolution Limit (Optical Lithography)

The fundamental resolution of an optical projection system is governed by diffraction. Two point sources can be resolved when their Airy disks are separated by the first minimum of the diffraction pattern.

**Step 1**: The angular position of the first diffraction minimum for a circular aperture is:

$$\theta_{min} = \dfrac{1.22 \,\lambda}{D}$$

where $D$ is the lens diameter and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

**Step 2**: The numerical aperture is defined as:

$$NA = n \sin \theta$$

For a lens focusing light onto a wafer, $\theta \approx \arcsin(NA/n)$.

**Step 3**: The linear resolution (Rayleigh criterion) on the wafer plane is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= D_{lens}\,\theta_{min} \\
&= D_{lens} \cdot \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D_{lens}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{2\,NA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the standard formulation uses **0.61** (not 1.22) because we measure peak-to-first-zero (half-width), the final Rayleigh formula is:

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA}}$$

**Engineering interpretation**: To halve the resolution $R$, you must either halve the wavelength $\lambda$ or double the numerical aperture $NA$. This is why the industry migrated from blue ($\lambda = 365$ nm) → DUV ($\lambda = 193$ nm) → EUV ($\lambda = 13.5$ nm).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Electron Beam Lithography (EBL) Resolution

For EBL, the de Broglie wavelength of an accelerated electron determines the ultimate spot size.

**Step 1**: Electron kinetic energy from accelerating voltage $V$:

$$E_k = eV$$

**Step 2**: Non-relativistic de Broglie wavelength:

$$\lambda_e = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

**Step 3**: Substituting constants $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, and converting units yields the engineering approximation:

$$\boxed{\lambda_e = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}~\text{nm}}$$

where $V$ is in volts.

**Numerical Example**: At $V = 30{,}000$ V (30 kV) — typical for a modern EBL system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_e &= \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{30{,}000}}~\text{nm} \\
&= \dfrac{1.227}{173.205}~\text{nm} \\
&\approx 0.00708~\text{nm} = 7.08~\text{pm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation**: Theoretically, atomic-scale resolution (~pm). In practice, however, resolution is limited by **electron scattering in the resist (proximity effect)**, **beam stability**, and **stage vibration**, yielding practical resolutions of **5–10 nm** in modern EBL systems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Etch Selectivity and Critical Dimension (CD)

For pattern transfer, the etch must remove the target film while preserving the mask. Selectivity $S$ is defined as:

$$S = \dfrac{\text{Etch rate of target film}}{\text{Etch rate of mask}} = \dfrac{R_t}{R_m}$$

For a target film of thickness $t_f$ masked by a resist of thickness $t_m$, the minimum required mask thickness is:

$$
t_{m,\,min} = \dfrac{t_f}{S}
$$

**Worked example**: Etching 200 nm of polysilicon using SiO₂ hard mask with $S = 50:1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{m,\,min} &= \dfrac{200~\text{nm}}{50} \\
&= 4~\text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation**: A 4 nm oxide mask suffices theoretically, but engineers use **20–30 nm** to allow for non-uniformity, etch-rate variation across the wafer (typically ±5%), and over-etch time to clear residues in dense features.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Top-Down and Bottom-Up

| Parameter | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 5–10 nm (EUV), < 5 nm (EBL) | Sub-nm (atomic precision) |
| Throughput | High (wafer-scale, parallel) | Variable; usually slow |
| Cost | Very high (multi-billion fabs) | Low to moderate |
| Defectivity | Stochastic LER, LWR issues | Thermodynamic defects |
| Pattern flexibility | Arbitrary (CAD-driven) | Limited by thermodynamics/chemistry |
| Material compatibility | Restricted to etchable/depositable films | Almost any (organic, bio, inorganic) |
| Typical yield | 70–95 % | Highly variable |
| Example tech | FinFET CMOS, MEMS gyroscopes | Carbon nanotubes, DNA scaffolds |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Nanofabrication Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bulk Wafer Si/Glass/GaAs] --> B[RCA Clean and HF Dip]
    B --> C[Thermal Oxidation or Thin Film Deposition]
    C --> D[Spin Coat Photoresist or EBL Resist]
    D --> E[Soft Bake and Exposure]
    E --> F[Post Exposure Bake and Development]
    F --> G{Pattern Transfer Route}
    G -->|Subtractive| H[Etching Wet or Dry RIE or DRIE]
    G -->|Additive| I[Lift Off or Electroplating]
    H --> J[Strip Resist and Clean]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Metrology SEM AFM TEM Ellipsometry]
    K --> L{Next Layer Required}
    L -->|Yes| C
    L -->|No| M[Final Device Ready]
```

## 4.2 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Block Architecture

```mermaid

        TD4 --> TD5[Final Nanostructure]
    end

    subgraph BU[Bottom Up Additive Approach]
        BU1[Atoms or Molecules or Precursors] --> BU2[Directed Assembly or Deposition]

```
