[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]** **Define Electron-Beam Nanolithography. List any three advantages of EBL over photolithography.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Electron-Beam Nanolithography (EBL) is a direct-write, maskless lithographic technique that uses a focused and accelerated beam of electrons (1 – 100 kV) to expose a resist-coated substrate, enabling the fabrication of sub-10 nm features. > > **Advantages over photolithography (3 × ½ = 1.5 marks, plus 0.5 for structure):** > 1. **Higher resolution** — Electron wavelength (≈ 0.005 nm at 50 kV) is far smaller than photon wavelength (193 nm for ArF), enabling sub-10 nm features. > 2. **Maskless operation** — Direct pattern writing from a CAD database eliminates the need for expensive photomasks. > 3. **Versatility** — Pattern can be modified easily by changing the CAD file, ideal for R&D and prototyping. > 4. (Bonus) **Direct patterning on non-planar surfaces** with appropriate depth of focus. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]** **What is the proximity effect in EBL? How does the parameter $\eta$ influence pattern fidelity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Proximity Effect (1.5 marks):** The proximity effect is the unwanted exposure of resist in regions adjacent to the intended exposure area, caused by **forward-scattered** electrons (inside the resist) and **back-scattered** electrons (from the substrate). It results in **pattern distortion, CD errors, and bridging** between adjacent features. > > **Role of $\eta$ (1.5 marks):** $\eta$ is the ratio of backscattered to forward-scattered energy: > $$\eta = \frac{E_{backscattered}}{E_{forward}}$$ > - **Higher $\eta$** (typical for high-Z substrates) → stronger proximity effect → more severe pattern distortion. > - **Lower $\eta$** (low-Z substrates, thin membranes) → proximity effect minimized. > - $\eta$ governs the **long-range exposure tail** in the double-Gaussian model and must be corrected via software dose modulation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every Module-4 question provides an **internal choice** between two alternative questions. Both options are presented below with full model solutions. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice Option]** **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the construction and working of an Electron-Beam Nanolithography system. Explain the role of the electron source, electromagnetic lenses, deflection coils, and beam blanker. **[(7 marks)]** **(b)** Derive an expression for the total beam diameter in EBL considering spherical aberration, diffraction, and chromatic aberration. Explain why there exists an **optimum convergence half-angle** $\alpha$ for minimum spot size. **[(7 marks)]** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** **Introduction (1 mark):** EBL is a maskless direct-write lithography technique using a focused electron beam to expose a resist. The system consists of an **electron column** (under high vacuum, $\sim 10^{-7}$ Torr) mounted vertically above a movable substrate stage. **Functional Blocks:** 1. **Electron Source (1.5 marks):** Either a **thermal field emitter (TFE)** or **Schottky emitter** (ZrO/W). High brightness ($> 10^8$ A/cm²·sr), small virtual source size (~ 10 nm). Emits electrons by thermionic + field emission. 2. **Acceleration Anode (0.5 marks):** Accelerates electrons to kinetic energy $E = eV$ (1 – 100 kV). Determines de Broglie wavelength. 3. **Electromagnetic Lenses (1.5 marks):** - **Condenser lenses** demagnify the source and control beam current. - **Objective lens** focuses the final demagnified spot onto the resist. - **Stigmator** corrects astigmatism — a common aberration. 4. **Deflection Coils (1 mark):** Electro-magnetic coils that steer the beam in X and Y to write patterns. Operate in **vector-scan** or **raster-scan** mode. 5. **Beam Blanker (0.5 marks):** An electrostatic deflector that rapidly switches the beam **on/off** between pixels, preventing exposure of non-pattern areas. 6. **Stage & Alignment (1 mark):** Laser interferometer-controlled stage with < 1 nm resolution; mark-detection cameras for multi-layer alignment. **Conclusion:** All blocks work synchronously to deliver a precisely controlled electron dose at each pixel, achieving nanometer-scale pattern writing. --- **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** **Step 1: List the three dominant resolution limiters.** - **Spherical aberration** disk: $d_s = 0.5 \, C_s \, \alpha^3$ - **Diffraction** (Airy) disk: $d_d = 0.61 \, \lambda / \alpha$ - **Chromatic aberration** disk: $d_c = C_c \, \alpha \, (\Delta V / V)$ **Step 2: Quadrature sum (assuming statistical independence).** $$ d_{total} = \sqrt{d_s^2 + d_d^2 + d_c^2} = \sqrt{(0.5 C_s \alpha^3)^2 + (0.61 \lambda / \alpha)^2 + (C_c \alpha \Delta V / V)^2} $$ **Step 3: Optimal α (considering $d_s$ and $d_d$ only — chromatic is small at high V).** Minimize $d^2 = 0.25 C_s^2 \alpha^6 + (0.61 \lambda)^2 \alpha^{-2}$ Set $\partial d^2 / \partial \alpha = 0$: $$ 1.5 \, C_s^2 \, \alpha^5 - 2 (0.61 \lambda)^2 \alpha^{-3} = 0 $$ Solving: $$ \alpha_{opt} = \left[ \frac{(0.61 \, \lambda)^2}{0.75 \, C_s^2} \right]^{1/8} = \left( \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{0.866 \, C_s} \right)^{1/4} $$ **Step 4: Why an optimum exists (2 marks):** - As $\alpha \to 0$: Diffraction $d_d \to \infty$ (electron beam diverges too much). - As $\alpha \to \infty$: Spherical aberration $d_s \to \infty$ (lens cannot focus off-axis rays). - The optimum is where $d_s = d_d$, giving the **minimum total spot size**. **Step 5: Practical implication (1 mark):** At the optimum, $d_{min} = \sqrt{2} \times 0.5 C_s \alpha_{opt}^3$. For modern systems with $C_s \approx 50$ mm, this yields **sub-nanometer beam diameters** in theory. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice Option]** **(a)** What is the **proximity effect** in EBL? Derive the **double-Gaussian point exposure function** and explain the roles of the parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\eta$. **[(7 marks)]** **(b)** A 50 kV EBL system writes a 2 µm × 2 µm square in PMMA resist (sensitivity = 250 µC/cm²) with a beam current of 500 pA. If the pixel size is 20 nm × 20 nm, calculate the **total exposure time** and **dwell time per pixel**. **[(7 marks)]** --- **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** **Step 1: Define proximity effect (1 mark).** Unwanted exposure of resist near intended features due to electron scattering, leading to **CD errors, line bridging, and pattern distortion**, especially in dense layouts. **Step 2: Electron scattering components (1 mark).** - **Forward scattering** in the resist: Gaussian with characteristic width $\alpha$ (range 5 – 50 nm). - **Backscattering** from the substrate: Gaussian with characteristic width $\beta$ (range 1 – 30 µm). **Step 3: Derive the point exposure function (3 marks).** Let the incident point deliver total energy $E_0$ to the resist. The forward-scattered energy per unit area is: $$f_f(r) = \frac{E_0}{\pi \alpha^2 (1+\eta)} \, e^{-r^2/\alpha^2}$$ The backscattered energy per unit area is: $$f_b(r) = \frac{\eta E_0}{\pi \beta^2 (1+\eta)} \, e^{-r^2/\beta^2}$$ Total point exposure function: $$f(r) = f_f(r) + f_b(r) = \frac{1}{\pi(1+\eta)} \left[ \frac{1}{\alpha^2} \, e^{-r^2/\alpha^2} + \frac{\eta}{\beta^2} \, e^{-r^2/\beta^2} \right]$$ **Step 4: Role of parameters (2 marks).** - $\alpha$ — **Forward scatter range.** Smaller $\alpha$ means tighter forward-scattered distribution → sharper short-range exposure. - $\beta$ — **Backscatter range.** Sets the long-range "halo" of exposure. $\beta$ depends on substrate Z and beam energy. - $\eta$ — **Backscatter-to-forward ratio.** Higher $\eta$ means stronger proximity effect. --- **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** **Given:** - Area $A = 2\,\mu\text{m} \times 2\,\mu\text{m} = 4 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{cm}^2$ - Sensitivity $D = 250\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{C/cm}^2$ - Beam current $I = 500\,\text{pA} = 5 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{A}$ - Pixel size $= 20 \times 20\,\text{nm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{cm}^2$ **Step 1: Number of pixels.** $$ N = \frac{A}{A_{pixel}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{cm}^2}{4 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{cm}^2} = 10^6 \text{ pixels} $$ **Step 2: Total charge required.** $$ Q_{total} = D \times A = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{C/cm}^2 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{cm}^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{C} $$ **Step 3: Total exposure time.** $$ T_{total} = \frac{Q_{total}}{I} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-11}}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ms} $$ **Step 4: Dwell time per pixel.** $$ T_d = \frac{T_{total}}{N} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-2}}{10^6} = 2.0 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ns} $$ **Final Answer:** Total exposure time = **20 ms**; Dwell time per pixel = **20 ns**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Unit errors** — Many students write µC instead of C. Always convert µC/cm² → C/cm² (multiply by $10^{-6}$). > 2. **Confusing beam current** — 500 pA = $5 \times 10^{-10}$ A, not $5 \times 10^{-7}$ A. > 3. **Forgetting to square** the pixel size when computing N. > 4. **Missing $10^6$ factor** in step 1 — the most common mistake. > 5. **Omitting final units** in the answer. Always state "in seconds" or "in nanoseconds." --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision before walking into the KTU exam hall — read this section twice.** - ✅ **EBL = Electron-Beam Nanolithography** — a maskless, direct-write, charged-particle lithography technique. - ✅ **Resolution** is limited by **spherical aberration, diffraction, chromatic aberration, source size, and stigmation** — combined in **quadrature**. - ✅ **de Broglie wavelength of electron** at 50 kV is **≈ 0.005 nm** — orders of magnitude smaller than optical wavelengths. - ✅ **Dose** $D = I \cdot t / A$ has units of **µC/cm²**; typical PMMA sensitivity is **100 – 500 µC/cm²**. - ✅ **Proximity effect** is the single biggest resolution limiter in EBL; modeled by the **double-Gaussian function** with parameters $\alpha$ (forward), $\beta$ (backward), and $\eta$ (ratio). - ✅ **Gaussian beam** = highest resolution, lowest throughput. **Shaped beam** = moderate resolution, high throughput. - ✅ **Vector scan** visits only pattern pixels; **Raster scan** visits all pixels in a field. - ✅ **PMMA** is the most common positive resist; **HSQ** is used for ultra-high resolution; **ZEP** and **CSAR** offer higher sensitivity. - ✅ **High voltage (≥ 100 kV)** gives better penetration and lower proximity effect but increases column cost. - ✅ **Lift-off** is used for metal patterning; **dry etching** is used for pattern transfer into substrates. - ✅ **Major manufacturers** of EBL systems include **Raith, JEOL, Vistec, Elionix, and NuFlare** (knowledge of tool ecosystem is a bonus mark). - ✅ **Beam blanker** is a key component for pixel-by-pixel exposure control in vector-scan systems. - ✅ **Stage interferometry** provides overlay accuracy of < 10 nm for multi-layer alignment. - ✅ **Charging effect** on insulating substrates can be mitigated by conductive coatings (e.g., Espacer) or charge-dissipation layers. - ✅ **Resist heating** is negligible in EBL due to low average power, but can affect sensitive polymeric resists in high-current exposure. - ✅ For **sub-10 nm features**, **100 kV EBL with HSQ resist** is the standard approach in research laboratories worldwide. > [!TIP] > **Memorize these three equations first** if you have only 5 minutes to prepare: > $$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}} \quad ; \quad d^2 = d_s^2 + d_d^2 + d_c^2 \quad ; \quad D = \frac{I \cdot t}{A}$$ > Combined with the proximity double-Gaussian, these cover > 80% of KTU ESE Module 4 questions.

topics.title: Introduction to Nanotechnology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Nanotechnology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Nanotechnology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Nanotechnology is the science, engineering, and technology conducted at the nanoscale (typically $\mathbf{1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ nm}}$ in at least one dimension), involving the understanding, manipulation, and control of matter at the atomic, molecular, and supramolecular levels to produce novel materials, devices, and systems with fundamentally new properties and functions due to their small structure.*

The term **"Nanotechnology"** was first introduced by Japanese scientist **Norio Taniguchi** in **1974** at the International Conference on Production Engineering in Tokyo. However, the conceptual foundation was laid by the legendary physicist **Richard P. Feynman** in his famous 1959 lecture titled *"There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom"*, where he envisioned the possibility of manipulating matter atom by atom.

$$1 \text{ nanometer} = 10^{-9} \text{ meters} = 1 \text{ billionth of a meter}$$

For perspective, a single human hair is approximately **80,000–100,000 nm** in diameter, and a strand of DNA is about **2.5 nm** wide.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Shrinking Workshop":**
> Imagine you have a wooden log. If you carve it into a chair, the wood behaves like ordinary wood. But if you keep cutting it smaller and smaller — first into toothpicks, then into splinters, then into individual wood fibers — eventually you reach the scale of the *cellulose molecules themselves*. At that microscopic scale, the wood suddenly becomes transparent, ultra-strong, and chemically reactive in ways that a log never was.
> **Nanotechnology is exactly this principle** — when you shrink materials to the nanoscale, they don't just become "smaller versions" of the bulk; they gain **entirely new physical, chemical, and biological properties** that are absent in their larger counterparts.

## 1.3 Why "Nano" is Special — The Nanoscale Realm

| Size Regime | Characteristic Behavior | Dominant Phenomena |
|---|---|---|
| $\gt 1 \text{ \mu m}$ (Micro/Macro) | Classical physics rules | Gravity, classical mechanics |
| $100 \text{ nm} - 1 \text{ \mu m}$ | Transition zone | Surface effects begin |
| $\mathbf{1 - 100 \text{ nm}}$ (**Nanoscale**) | **New properties emerge** | **Quantum effects, surface dominance** |
| $\lt 1 \text{ nm}$ (Atomic) | Quantum chemistry | Electron orbital behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Threshold:** The **100 nm** boundary is critical because below this size, two quantum phenomena become dominant:
> 1. **Quantum Confinement** — electrons are restricted in motion, altering electrical and optical behavior.
> 2. **Surface-to-Volume Ratio (SVR)** skyrockets, making surface atoms (with unsaturated bonds) dominate the material's chemistry.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Nanotechnology

1. **Nanoscience** — Study of phenomena and manipulation of materials at atomic/molecular scales.
2. **Nanoengineering** — Design and fabrication of functional nanostructures, devices, and systems.
3. **Nanomanufacturing** — Scalable, reproducible production of nanomaterials and nanodevices (the focus of this course, PEMET864).

## 1.5 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Bohr Radius ($a_0$):** $\mathbf{0.529 \text{ Å} = 0.0529 \text{ nm}}$ — natural atomic length scale.
- **C-C Bond Length:** $\mathbf{\approx 0.154 \text{ nm}}$ — reference for molecular dimensions.
- **Avogadro's Number ($N_A$):** $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}}$ — for nanoparticle surface atom calculations.
- **Thermal de Broglie Wavelength at 300 K:** $\mathbf{\lambda_{th} \approx 0.7 \text{ nm}}$ — sets the scale where quantum statistics matter.
- **Surface-to-Volume Ratio (SVR):** Governs catalytic, chemical, and mechanical surface-dependent properties.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Materials Behave Differently at the Nanoscale

When a material's dimensions shrink below a critical size, four fundamental shifts occur:

### (a) Quantum Confinement
When particle size approaches or falls below the **Bohr exciton radius**, electrons and holes are spatially confined. This discretizes the energy levels (similar to a "particle in a box") and shifts optical/electronic properties.

$$\text{Bohr Exciton Radius } (a_B) = \frac{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \hbar^2}{\mu e^2}$$

where $\mu$ is the reduced effective mass of the exciton, $\varepsilon_r$ is relative permittivity, and $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant.

### (b) Surface-to-Volume Ratio (SVR) Dominance
A high SVR means a large fraction of atoms reside on the surface, where they have **dangling (unsaturated) bonds**. These surface atoms are highly reactive and dramatically alter mechanical, chemical, and catalytic properties.

### (c) Increased Surface Energy
Surface atoms possess higher energy than bulk atoms, increasing chemical reactivity, melting point depression, and solubility.

### (d) Quantum Tunneling and Ballistic Transport
Electrons can traverse nanoscale barriers (tunneling) or travel without scattering (ballistic), enabling novel electronic devices like resonant tunneling diodes.

## 2.2 Classification of Nanomaterials by Dimensionality

| Dimensionality | Structural Class | Examples | Key Features |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0D** (all 3 dimensions $\le 100$ nm) | Nanoparticles, Quantum dots, Fullerenes | C60 (Buckminsterfullerene, 0.7 nm) | All electrons confined; size-tunable bandgap |
| **1D** (2 dimensions $\le 100$ nm) | Nanowires, Nanotubes, Nanorods, Nanofibers | Carbon nanotubes (CNTs, 1–50 nm diameter) | Electron transport along 1 axis |
| **2D** (1 dimension $\le 100$ nm) | Thin films, Nanosheets, Nanoplates, Graphene | Graphene monolayer (0.345 nm thick) | Confinement in thickness direction |
| **3D** (Bulk with nanoscale features) | Nanocomposites, Nanostructured bulk materials, Aerogels | Carbon nanotube-reinforced polymers | No confinement; nanoscale internal structure |

## 2.3 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Approaches

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **two fundamental strategies** for nanostructure fabrication.

### Top-Down Approach
- Starts with a bulk material and progressively removes/shrinks it to nanoscale.
- **Methods:** Photolithography, electron-beam lithography, focused ion beam (FIB) milling, nanoimprint lithography, mechanical milling.
- **Analogy:** Sculpting a statue from a marble block.
- **Advantages:** Mature, repeatable, compatible with existing semiconductor processes.
- **Limitations:** Tool wear, surface defects, lower throughput below 10 nm.

### Bottom-Up Approach
- Builds nanostructures atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule via self-assembly.
- **Methods:** Chemical vapor deposition (CVD), self-assembled monolayers (SAMs), sol-gel synthesis, molecular beam epitaxy (MBE), DNA origami.
- **Analogy:** Building a brick wall brick by brick.
- **Advantages:** Atomic precision, lower cost at scale, biomimetic.
- **Limitations:** Defect control, scalability challenges, contamination sensitivity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nanometer definition | $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ | Unit conversion |
| 2 | Bohr exciton radius (generic) | $a_B = \dfrac{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \hbar^2}{\mu e^2}$ | Predicts onset of quantum confinement |
| 3 | Particle-in-a-box energy (1D) | $E_n = \dfrac{n^2 h^2}{8 m L^2}$ | Energy quantization in confined systems |
| 4 | Surface-to-volume ratio (cube) | $\text{SVR} = \dfrac{6}{a}$ where $a$ is edge length | Quantifies surface atom fraction |
| 5 | Surface atom fraction (cube) | $f_{\text{surface}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{a-2r_{\text{atom}}}{a}\right)^3$ | Predicts reactivity onset at small sizes |
| 6 | Melting point depression (size-dependent) | $T_m(d) = T_{m,\infty}\left(1 - \dfrac{4\sigma_{sl}}{\rho_s L d}\right)$ | Pawley's equation — important for nanosintering |
| 7 | de Broglie wavelength (thermal) | $\lambda_{th} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$ | Determines quantum regime entry |
| 8 | Quantum dot bandgap shift | $\Delta E_g \approx \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu R^2}$ | Brus equation for nanoparticle bandgap |
| 9 | Carbon nanotube chirality vector | $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$ | Determines metallic/semiconducting CNT |
| 10 | Surface energy (sphere) | $E_{\text{surface}} = 4\pi r^2 \gamma$ | Drives sintering, Ostwald ripening |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the Bohr radius (atomic scale, $\approx 0.05$ nm) with the **Bohr exciton radius** (typically 1–10 nm in semiconductors). Remember: exciton radius is much larger and is what governs quantum confinement onset in semiconductor nanoparticles.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Nanotechnology Application | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Electronics** | FinFET transistors (Intel 22 nm, 2011) | Continued Moore's Law scaling |
| **Medicine** | Liposomal drug delivery, gold nanoshells for photothermal therapy | Targeted cancer treatment |
| **Energy** | Quantum-dot solar cells, Si nanowire anodes in Li-ion batteries | Higher efficiency, faster charging |
| **Materials** | Carbon nanotube-reinforced composites, graphene coatings | Ultra-strong, lightweight structures |
| **Environment** | TiO₂ nanoparticle photocatalysts for water purification | Pollutant degradation |
| **Aerospace** | Nanocoatings for anti-icing, nanostructured Al alloys | Improved performance and durability |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Surface-to-Volume Ratio for a Cube of Edge Length $a$

We begin with a cubic nanoparticle of edge length $a$ and want to determine how the surface-to-volume ratio varies with size.

**Step 1: Define the geometric quantities.**

$$\text{Surface Area} = S = 6a^2$$

$$\text{Volume} = V = a^3$$

**Step 2: Form the ratio.**

$$\text{SVR} = \frac{S}{V} = \frac{6a^2}{a^3} = \frac{6}{a}$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

As $a$ decreases toward the nanometer regime, the SVR grows linearly. For a $1 \text{ nm}$ cube:

$$\text{SVR} = \frac{6}{1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} = 6 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

This is approximately **6 orders of magnitude** greater than for a $1 \text{ mm}$ cube, demonstrating the dramatic increase in surface dominance at the nanoscale.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fraction of Surface Atoms in a Cube

**Step 1: Count total atoms in the cube.**

Assume a simple cubic lattice with atomic radius $r_{\text{atom}} \approx 0.15$ nm (approximate for many metals). The number of unit cells along one edge is:

$$N_{\text{edge}} = \frac{a}{2r_{\text{atom}}}$$

**Step 2: Total number of atoms (simple cubic).**

$$N_{\text{total}} = \left(\frac{a}{2r_{\text{atom}}}\right)^3$$

**Step 3: Interior atoms (after removing a 1-atom-thick surface layer).**

$$N_{\text{interior}} = \left(\frac{a - 2r_{\text{atom}}}{2r_{\text{atom}}}\right)^3$$

**Step 4: Surface atom fraction.**

$$f_{\text{surface}} = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{interior}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \left(\frac{a - 2r_{\text{atom}}}{a}\right)^3$$

**Step 5: Numerical example — Gold nanoparticle of 5 nm edge.**

Using $r_{\text{Au}} = 0.144$ nm:

$$f_{\text{surface}} = 1 - \left(\frac{5 - 0.288}{5}\right)^3 = 1 - (0.9424)^3 \approx 1 - 0.837 = 0.163$$

So about **16% of atoms are on the surface** for a 5 nm gold cube, compared to $\lt 1\%$ for a 100 nm cube. This huge surface fraction is why gold nanoparticles are excellent catalysts.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Size-Dependent Melting Point (Pawley / Hanszen Equation)

Classical thermodynamics predicts that smaller particles melt at lower temperatures due to excess surface energy.

**Step 1: Start with the Clausius-Clapeyron relation applied to a small particle.**

The chemical potential of a solid sphere of radius $r$ exceeds that of the bulk by an amount governed by the surface energy $\gamma_{sl}$ at the solid-liquid interface:

$$\mu_{\text{solid}}(r) - \mu_{\text{liquid}}(r) = \frac{2\gamma_{sl} V_m}{r}$$

**Step 2: Set the condition for melting (chemical potentials equal) at the reduced melting temperature $T_m(r)$.**

Using a linearization of the enthalpy difference between solid and liquid near $T_{m,\infty}$:

$$\Delta H_m = \rho_s L \cdot V_m$$

where $L$ is the latent heat of fusion per unit mass, $\rho_s$ is the solid density.

**Step 3: Solve for the depressed melting point.**

$$T_m(r) = T_{m,\infty}\left(1 - \frac{2\gamma_{sl}}{\rho_s L r}\right)$$

For an approximate cube of edge $d$ (replacing $2r$ with $d$):

$$T_m(d) = T_{m,\infty}\left(1 - \frac{4\gamma_{sl}}{\rho_s L d}\right)$$

**Step 4: Numerical validation — Gold nanoparticle ($d = 5$ nm).**

Using $\gamma_{sl} \approx 0.27 \text{ J/m}^2$, $\rho_s = 19300 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $L = 64.5 \times 10^3 \text{ J/kg}$, $T_{m,\infty} = 1337 \text{ K}$:

$$\frac{4\gamma_{sl}}{\rho_s L d} = \frac{4 \times 0.27}{19300 \times 64500 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= \frac{1.08}{6.224} \approx 0.173$$

$$T_m(5 \text{ nm}) \approx 1337 \times (1 - 0.173) \approx 1106 \text{ K}$$

This is a **~230 K** depression — a substantial effect that is critical in **nanomanufacturing processes** such as nanosintering and nanowelding.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Top-Down vs Bottom-Up

| Evaluation Parameter | Top-Down Approach | Bottom-Up Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting point** | Bulk material | Atoms / molecules |
| **Minimum feature size** | $\sim 5$ nm (E-beam) | Atomic scale ($\sim 0.1$ nm) |
| **Defect density** | Higher (lattice damage, dislocations) | Lower (self-correcting assembly) |
| **Scalability** | High (wafer-level) | Moderate (often batch-limited) |
| **Cost at high volume** | Lower (established fabs) | Often lower (chemistry-based) |
| **Material waste** | High (subtractive) | Low (additive) |
| **Surface finish** | Imperfect (staircase, roughness) | Atomically smooth possible |
| **Best suited for** | CMOS, MEMS, microfluidics | Quantum dots, CNTs, molecular machines |
| **Example technique** | Electron beam lithography | CVD, sol-gel, self-assembly |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Historical Timeline of Nanotechnology (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["A1959 Feynman Lecture Plenty of Room at the Bottom"] --> B["A1974 Taniguchi Coins Nanotechnology"]
    B --> C["A1981 Binnig and Rohrer Invent STM"]
    C --> D["A1985 C60 Buckminsterfullerene Discovered"]
    D --> E["A1991 Carbon Nanotubes Iijima"]
    E --> F["A2004 Graphene Isolated Geim and Novoselov"]
    F --> G["A2010s Commercial Nanoproducts Boom"]
    G --> H["A2020s Molecular Nanotechnology and AI Driven Nano Design"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Reading:** The above diagram traces the evolutionary path of nanotechnology from a visionary idea (Feynman) → nomenclature (Taniguchi) → enabling tools (STM) → key discoveries (fullerenes, CNTs, graphene) → commercial maturity and AI-driven molecular design.

## 4.2 Classification of Nanomaterials by Dimensionality (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Nanomaterials"] --> B["0D All 3 Dims less than 100 nm"]
    A --> C["1D 2 Dims less than 100 nm"]
    A --> D["2D 1 Dim less than 100 nm"]
    A --> E["3D Bulk with Nanoscale Features"]
    B --> B1["Quantum Dots"]
    B --> B2["Fullerenes C60"]
    B --> B3["Gold Nanoparticles"]
    C --> C1["Carbon Nanotubes"]
    C --> C2["Silicon Nanowires"]
    C --> C3["Nanofibers"]
    D --> D1["Graphene Monolayer"]
    D --> D2["MoS2 Nanosheets"]
    D --> D3["Thin Films less than 100 nm"]
    E --> E1["Nanocomposites"]
    E --> E2["Aerogels"]
    E --> E3["Nanostructured Alloys"]
```

## 4.3 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TD["Top Down Approach"]
        TD1["Bulk Material"] --> TD2["Patterning Lithography"]
        TD2 --> TD3["Etching or Milling"]
        TD3 --> TD4["Nanoscale Feature"]
    end
    subgraph BU["Bottom Up Approach"]
        BU1["Atoms or Molecules"] --> BU2["Self Assembly or CVD"]
        BU2 --> BU3["Nucleation and Growth"]
        BU3 --> BU4["Nanoscale Structure"]
    end
    TD4 -. Convergence .- BU4
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of Nanotechnology Applications (Mermaid Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Nanotechnology Core"] --> B["Electronics"]
    A --> C["Medicine"]
    A --> D["Energy"]
    A --> E["Materials"]
    A --> F["Environment"]
    B --> B1["FinFETs and Quantum Dots"]
    C --> C1["Drug Delivery and Diagnostics"]
    D --> D1["Solar Cells and Batteries"]
    E --> E1["CNT Composites and Coatings"]
    F --> F1["Water Purification and Catalysis"]
```

## 4.5 Size Scale Comparison (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1 m Human"] --> B["1 mm Grain of Sand"]
    B --> C["100 um Human Hair Width"]
    C --> D["10 um Red Blood Cell"]
    D --> E["1 um Bacterium"]
    E --> F["100 nm Virus"]
    F --> G["10 nm Quantum Dot"]
    G --> H["1 nm DNA Width"]
    H --> I["0.1 nm Atom Diameter"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define nanotechnology. Discuss briefly the historical milestones that led to its emergence as a distinct scientific field. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Nanotechnology is the manipulation, control, and engineering of matter at the nanoscale (1–100 nm) to develop materials, devices, and systems with novel properties arising from quantum effects and dominant surface phenomena.
- **1959** – Richard Feynman's lecture *"There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom"* laid the conceptual foundation.
- **1974** – Norio Taniguchi first coined the term "Nanotechnology."
- **1981** – Invention of the Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) by Binnig and Rohrer enabled visualization and manipulation of individual atoms.
- **1985** – Discovery of C60 (Buckminsterfullerene); **1991** – Carbon nanotubes (Iijima); **2004** – Graphene isolation (Geim and Novoselov). *(Any 2 historical milestones suffice for full marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Explain with examples the difference between 0D, 1D, and 2D nanomaterials. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **0D Nanomaterials:** All three dimensions are confined to the nanoscale. Example: Quantum dots, C60 fullerenes, gold nanoparticles.
- **1D Nanomaterials:** Two dimensions are nanoscale; one is macroscopic. Example: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), silicon nanowires.
- **2D Nanomaterials:** Only one dimension is nanoscale (the thickness). Example: Graphene monolayer, MoS₂ nanosheets, ultrathin films.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **top-down and bottom-up approaches** used in nanofabrication. Compare their merits and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the **melting point of a nanoparticle and its size**, starting from thermodynamic principles. Use a gold nanoparticle of diameter **5 nm** as a numerical example (Given: $T_{m,\infty} = 1337$ K, $\gamma_{sl} = 0.27$ J/m², $\rho_s = 19300$ kg/m³, $L = 6.45 \times 10^4$ J/kg). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
*Top-down* starts from bulk material and progressively reduces dimensions via methods such as photolithography, electron-beam lithography, focused ion beam milling, and mechanical milling. *Bottom-up* builds structures atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule using chemical vapor deposition (CVD), molecular beam epitaxy, sol-gel synthesis, or self-assembly. Top-down is mature and reproducible but suffers from tool wear and surface defects; bottom-up offers atomic precision and lower cost but is challenging to scale.

**Model Solution (b):**
Using the derived Pawley relation:

$$T_m(d) = T_{m,\infty}\left(1 - \frac{4\gamma_{sl}}{\rho_s L d}\right)$$

Substituting $d = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ m:

$$\frac{4 \times 0.27}{19300 \times 6.45 \times 10^4 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.08}{6.224} \approx 0.173$$

$$T_m(5 \text{ nm}) = 1337 \times (1 - 0.173) = 1337 \times 0.827 \approx 1105.7 \text{ K}$$

Hence, a 5 nm gold nanoparticle melts at approximately **1106 K** — a **~230 K** reduction compared to bulk gold.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define **Surface-to-Volume Ratio (SVR)**. For a cubic nanoparticle, derive the SVR as a function of edge length and compute the fraction of surface atoms for a 3 nm cube. Take atomic radius as 0.15 nm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **quantum dots**? Explain the phenomenon of **quantum confinement** and derive the size-dependent bandgap shift using a simplified particle-in-a-box model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
SVR quantifies the surface area exposed per unit volume. For a cube of edge $a$:

$$\text{SVR} = \frac{6a^2}{a^3} = \frac{6}{a}$$

For $a = 3$ nm:

$$\text{SVR} = \frac{6}{3 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Surface atom fraction:

$$f_{\text{surface}} = 1 - \left(\frac{a - 2r}{a}\right)^3 = 1 - \left(\frac{3 - 0.30}{3}\right)^3 = 1 - (0.9)^3 = 1 - 0.729 = 0.271$$

So about **27% of atoms** are on the surface for a 3 nm cube.

**Model Solution (b):**
Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanocrystals (typically 2–10 nm) in which all three dimensions are smaller than the Bohr exciton radius, leading to discrete, size-tunable electronic states. The phenomenon of **quantum confinement** occurs when electrons are spatially restricted to a region smaller than their natural delocalization length. In a 1D infinite potential well of width $L$:

$$E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8 m L^2}$$

The ground-state energy scales as $1/L^2$, so the effective bandgap increases as size decreases:

$$\Delta E_g \approx \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$$

where $\mu$ is the reduced exciton mass and $R$ is the nanoparticle radius. This explains why smaller QDs emit shorter-wavelength (bluer) light.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in 'Introduction to Nanotechnology' questions:**
> 1. **Confusing atomic Bohr radius ($\approx 0.05$ nm) with Bohr exciton radius (1–10 nm)** — examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for this in 3-mark questions.
> 2. **Forgetting units in SVR calculations** — always state SVR in m⁻¹ or nm⁻¹; missing units cost partial marks.
> 3. **Mixing up 0D, 1D, 2D, 3D nanomaterial definitions** — state clearly *how many dimensions* are below 100 nm.
> 4. **Not showing all intermediate steps** in derivations like melting-point depression — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes step-wise evaluation, and skipping the $\mu$, $\gamma_{sl}$, or $L$ substitution loses up to **2 marks**.
> 5. **Failing to attribute credit to Feynman (1959) and Taniguchi (1974)** — examiners often allocate **½ to 1 mark** just for the historical chronology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Nanotechnology = engineering matter at **1–100 nm** in at least one dimension.
- ✅ **Origin:** Term coined by **Norio Taniguchi (1974)**; vision articulated by **Richard Feynman (1959)**.
- ✅ **Critical size threshold:** **100 nm** — below this, quantum effects and SVR dominate.
- ✅ **Surface-to-Volume Ratio (cube):** $\text{SVR} = 6/a$ — increases as $a$ decreases.
- ✅ **Bohr exciton radius** (not Bohr radius) governs quantum confinement onset in semiconductors.
- ✅ **Particle-in-a-box energy** scales as $1/L^2$ — explains size-tunable optical properties of QDs.
- ✅ **Pawley equation:** $T_m(d) = T_{m,\infty}\left(1 - \dfrac{4\gamma_{sl}}{\rho_s L d}\right)$ — predicts melting point depression.
- ✅ **Nanomaterial classes:** 0D (QDs, fullerenes), 1D (CNTs, nanowires), 2D (graphene, MoS₂), 3D (nanocomposites, aerogels).
- ✅ **Top-down** = subtractive (lithography, milling); **Bottom-up** = additive (CVD, self-assembly).
- ✅ **Key inventions:** STM (1981), C60 (1985), CNTs (1991), Graphene (2004).
- ✅ **Applications span:** Electronics (FinFETs), Medicine (drug delivery), Energy (QD solar cells), Materials (CNT composites), Environment (photocatalysis).
- ✅ **Killer conversion:** $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9}$ m; $1 \text{ Å} = 0.1$ nm — always double-check units in derivations.
- ✅ **Always state SVR units** (m⁻¹) and **always show stepwise substitution** in numerical problems for full KTU marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Soft Lithography and Applications
Principle of Micro Contact Printing and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Principle of Micro Contact Printing and Applications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Micro Contact Printing (μCP)** is a non-photolithographic, soft-lithographic pattern-transfer technique in which an elastomeric stamp (typically fabricated from **Polydimethylsiloxane, PDMS**) is wetted ("inked") with molecules that form a **Self-Assembled Monolayer (SAM)** and is then brought into conformal contact with a substrate. The molecules transfer only from the raised regions of the stamp to the substrate, producing patterned SAMs at the micro- and nano-scale that subsequently act as a **molecular resist** for selective etching, deposition, or surface functionalisation. The technique was pioneered by **Kumar and Whitesides (1993–1994)** at Harvard University and is classified by KTU under **Module 4: Non-Traditional Micro/Nano Patterning Techniques** alongside NIL, LIGA, and electrochemical fabrication. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Rubber Stamp" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world analogy:** Think of μCP exactly like a child's **rubber stamp toy**, but operating at the scale of molecules. | Child's Stamp | Micro Contact Printing | |---|---| | Hard rubber die carved with a pattern | Soft PDMS stamp moulded from a photolithographic master | | Ink pad soaked in coloured ink | Stamp wetted in alkanethiol / silane solution | | Pressing the stamp onto paper | Conformal contact with metal/silicon substrate | | Ink transfers only on raised letters | SAM molecules transfer only from raised features | | Patterned picture | Patterned monolayer (resist) | The crucial difference is **scale**: μCP routinely produces features in the range of **1 µm – 100 nm**, and under ideal conditions resolutions down to **~50 nm** have been demonstrated. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Young's modulus of PDMS (Sylgard 184, 10:1):** **≈ 1.8 MPa** - **Surface energy of cured PDMS:** **≈ 19–21 mJ/m²** - **Bond dissociation energy of Au–S bond:** **≈ 44 kcal/mol (~184 kJ/mol)** - **Typical SAM packing density (alkanethiol on Au(111)):** **≈ 7.7 × 10¹⁴ molecules/cm²** - **Typical SAM thickness (C18 alkanethiol):** **≈ 2.3 nm** - **Standard contact time:** **5 s – 60 s** - **Inking concentration (thiols in ethanol):** **1–10 mM** - **Demonstrated feature resolution:** **~50 nm (laboratory); ~1–2 µm (production)** > [!TIP] > KTU board examiners frequently test students on the **Au–S bond energy** and the **role of chain length (n)** in SAM stability — memorise these numbers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind μCP — Why Soft Lithography? Conventional photolithography is bottlenecked by **diffraction-limited optics** and the cost of stepper/scanner equipment. μCP circumvents these problems because: 1. **Conformal contact** between a soft elastomer and a non-planar substrate is mechanically forgiving. 2. The "ink" is a **molecular monolayer**, not a polymer — so feature size is governed by the **stamp geometry**, not by the chemistry. 3. It is **parallel, fast, and inexpensive** — a single stamp can be reused **>100 times**. ## 2.2 The "How" — Six Logical Steps 1. **Master Fabrication** — A photolithographic (or e-beam, or NIL) master with the inverse relief is fabricated on a silicon wafer. 2. **PDMS Moulding** — Liquid PDMS prepolymer + curing agent (10:1) is poured over the master, degassed, and cured at **~70 °C for 1–2 hours**. 3. **Stamp Release** — The cross-linked PDMS is peeled off; its relief is the **negative** of the master. 4. **Inking** — The stamp surface is exposed to a solution of SAM-forming molecules (e.g., **hexadecanethiol, HDT, in ethanol**). 5. **Conformal Contact** — The inked stamp is placed on the substrate (Au, Ag, Cu, SiO₂) for a controlled dwell time. 6. **Pattern Transfer** — SAMs form in contacted regions; un-contacted areas are subsequently etched, functionalised, or used as templates. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains every equation and metric a KTU 2024 student must memorise for this topic. | # | Equation / Concept | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Young's equation for wettability | $\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | $\theta$ in degrees | Predicts ink wetting on stamp | | 2 | PDMS Young's modulus | $E_{PDMS}$ | **1.8 MPa** | Controls stamp deformation | | 3 | Feature aspect ratio limit | $\frac{h}{w} \leq 5$ (approx.) | dimensionless | Beyond this, stamp collapses | | 4 | SAM thickness vs. chain length | $d \;\approx\; (n \cdot 1.27 \;+\; 0.3)\;\text{Å}$ | n = number of CH₂ units | Each CH₂ adds ~1.27 Å | | 5 | Au–S bond energy | $E_{Au-S}$ | **~44 kcal/mol** | Strength of chemisorption | | 6 | SAM surface coverage | $\Gamma$ | **7.7 × 10¹⁴ /cm²** | Molecules per unit area | | 7 | Diffusion-limited resolution | $\Delta x \;\propto\; \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | D = diffusivity, t = time | Limits minimum feature | | 8 | Critical stamping pressure | $P_{crit} \;\sim\; \frac{E_{PDMS} \cdot h^{2}}{R^{2}}$ | Pa | Buckling threshold for posts | | 9 | Contact angle hysteresis | $\Delta \theta \;=\; \theta_{adv} - \theta_{rec}$ | degrees | Predicts ink transfer fidelity | | 10 | Reusability of stamp | $N_{reuse}$ | **> 100 cycles** | Cost-effectiveness metric | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown rule observed:** All absolute values are written using `\vert` / `\mid` instead of `|` so they do not break table syntax. Watch out for this when copy-pasting into your own notes. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Microelectronics:** Defining etch masks for sub-100 nm gold interconnects. - **Biomedical Microdevices:** Patterning cell-adhesion regions for tissue engineering scaffolds. - **Lab-on-Chip:** Sealing PDMS microchannels against glass using SAM-mediated bonding. - **Optoelectronics:** Pixelation of OLEDs and organic photovoltaic (OPV) active layers. - **Sensor Manufacturing:** Anti-fouling or protein-binding zones in Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) and QCM biosensors. - **Catalyst Patterning:** Selective growth of carbon nanotubes, ZnO nanowires, or metal-organic frameworks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SAM Thickness as a Function of Alkyl Chain Length The thickness of an alkanethiol SAM on a gold (Au(111)) surface can be derived from the projection of each CH₂ unit along the chain axis. **Step 1:** Each CH₂ unit adds a length of approximately **1.27 Å** along the chain axis when the molecule adopts an all-trans zigzag conformation. The terminal CH₃ group adds an extra **~0.3 Å**. $$ \Delta l \;=\; 1.27 \text{ Å per CH}_2 $$ **Step 2:** For an alkanethiol with $n$ methylene units (HS-(CH₂)$_n$-CH₃), the total SAM thickness (tilt-corrected) is therefore: $$ d \;\approx\; (n \cdot 1.27 \;+\; 0.3)\;\text{Å} $$ **Step 3 (numerical example):** A **C18 alkanethiol (n = 17)** gives: $$ \begin{aligned} d &\;\approx\; (17 \times 1.27 \;+\; 0.3)\;\text{Å} \\ &\;\approx\; (21.59 \;+\; 0.3)\;\text{Å} \\ &\;\approx\; 21.89\;\text{Å} \\ &\;\approx\; 2.19\;\text{nm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 (verification):** This matches experimental ellipsometry measurements for octadecanethiol (ODT) SAMs, which yield **2.2–2.3 nm** — confirming the derivation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion logic:** $1 \text{ Å} = 0.1 \text{ nm}$. Multiplying $n$ by 1.27 converts methylene units into Ångströms; division by 10 converts to nanometres for KTU numerical problems. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Stamp Collapse Pressure A raised PDMS post of height $h$ and lateral dimension $w$ buckles when subjected to an axial compressive stress during stamping. The critical stress is given by Euler's elastic-buckling formula adapted for PDMS: **Step 1:** Define the slenderness ratio $\lambda$: $$ \lambda \;=\; \frac{h}{w} $$ **Step 2:** The critical buckling stress for a clamped-free PDMS column is: $$ \sigma_{crit} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E_{PDMS}}{(K \lambda)^{2}} $$ where $K \approx 2$ is the effective-length factor for clamped-free conditions. **Step 3:** Substituting $E_{PDMS} = 1.8 \text{ MPa}$ and $K = 2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{crit} &\;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} \times 1.8 \times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa}}{(2 \lambda)^{2}} \\ &\;=\; \frac{9.8696 \times 1.8 \times 10^{6}}{4 \lambda^{2}}\;\text{Pa} \\ &\;\approx\; \frac{4.44 \times 10^{6}}{\lambda^{2}}\;\text{Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 (numerical example):** For a square post with $h = 10\;\mu\text{m}$ and $w = 5\;\mu\text{m}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda &\;=\; \frac{10}{5} \;=\; 2 \\ \sigma_{crit} &\;\approx\; \frac{4.44 \times 10^{6}}{(2)^{2}}\;\text{Pa} \\ &\;\approx\; 1.11 \times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa} \\ &\;\approx\; 1.11\;\text{MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 (interpretation):** If the stamping pressure exceeds **1.11 MPa**, the PDMS post will collapse, leading to **roof collapse** (sagging) and **pairing/sagging defects** in the printed pattern. This is a classic KTU Part-B essay question. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SAM Composition vs. Substrate Compatibility | Substrate | SAM "Ink" Molecule | Tail Group (-X) | Functional Outcome | |---|---|---|---| | Gold (Au(111)) | Alkanethiol HS-(CH₂)$_n$-X | -CH₃ / -OH / -COOH / -NH₂ | Hydrophobic / hydrophilic / bioactive | | Silver (Ag) | Alkanethiol | as above | Stronger S–Ag bond; faster assembly | | Copper (Cu) | Alkanethiol | as above | Requires inert atmosphere | | Silicon / SiO₂ | Alkylsilane (e.g., OTS) | -CH₃ / -NH₂ | Covalently bonded via Si-O-Si | | Glass | Alkylsilane | epoxy / aldehyde | Surface for protein immobilisation | | PDMS / polymer | Silane or fluorosilane | -CF₃ | Anti-sticking release layers | > [!TIP] > **Memorise** "**thiols for coinage metals, silanes for oxides**" — this is the single most-tested KTU distinction for SAM chemistry. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Block Architecture of μCP The following block diagram captures the end-to-end functional architecture of a typical μCP fabrication line, from master creation to downstream SAM exploitation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Master Fabrication
Si wafer, photolithography or e-beam] --> B[Step 2: PDMS Pouring and Curing
10 to 1 ratio, 70 deg C, 1 hour] B --> C[Step 3: Stamp Demoulding
Peel PDMS from master] C --> D[Step 4: Inking
SAM forming molecules in ethanol] D --> E[Step 5: Conformal Contact
Press stamp on substrate 5 to 60 s] E --> F[Step 6: Patterned SAM on Substrate
Au or SiO2 with monolayer] F --> G{Downstream Application} G --> G1[Etching
Wet or dry using SAM as mask] G --> G2[Selective Deposition
Lift off or electroless plating] G --> G3[Biomolecule Immobilisation
Antibodies DNA proteins] G --> G4[Cell Patterning
Tissue engineering scaffolds] ``` ## 4.2 Mechanism Block — Conformal Contact and SAM Transfer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PDMS Stamp
Raised micro features] --> B[Ink Layer
Alkanethiol monolayer] B --> C[Contact Zone
Molecular diffusion] C --> D[Au or SiO2 Substrate
SAM forms here] D --> E[Non Contact Regions
Bare substrate remains] E --> F[Etchant or Reactant
Selective removal or growth] ``` ## 4.3 Defect and Resolution-Limit Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[μCP Defect Map] --> B[Roof Collapse
High aspect ratio posts] A --> C[Sagging
Low modulus stamp] A --> D[Ink Diffusion
Long contact time] A --> E[Pairing Defects
Adjacent posts stick together] A --> F[Incomplete Transfer
Low contact pressure] B --> G[Remedy: Reduce H over W] C --> H[Remedy: Use harder PDMS 5 to 1] D --> I[Remedy: Shorter dwell time] E --> J[Remedy: Increase spacing] F --> K[Remedy: Higher load or longer time] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > These flowcharts are designed to be **rendered directly by Mermaid** — every node ID follows the **alphanumeric-with-prefix rule** (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepG1`) and every label is plain uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting tags. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** > *"What is micro contact printing? Mention any two advantages of this process over conventional photolithography."* **Model Answer:** - **Definition (2 marks):** Micro Contact Printing (μCP) is a soft-lithographic technique in which an elastomeric PDMS stamp inked with self-assembled monolayer (SAM)-forming molecules (e.g., alkanethiols) is brought into conformal contact with a substrate, transferring a patterned molecular monolayer only at raised regions of the stamp. - **Advantages (1 mark, any two):** 1. **Low cost** — no stepper/scanner required; a single PDMS stamp can be reused >100 times. 2. **Conformal contact on non-planar surfaces** — soft elastomer adapts to curved substrates. 3. **Sub-100 nm resolution** without diffraction-limited optics. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *"Why is PDMS preferred as a stamp material in micro contact printing? List two key properties."* **Model Answer:** 1. **Low Young's modulus (~1.8 MPa)** — enables conformal contact with the substrate without requiring high pressure. 2. **Low surface energy (~21 mJ/m²)** — permits easy demoulding from the master and prevents the stamp from sticking to the printed pattern. 3. (Optional) **Chemically inert and optically transparent**, allowing easy alignment and reuse. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Choice-1]** > *(a) Explain the step-by-step procedure of micro contact printing with a neat schematic. (7 marks)* > *(b) Discuss the role of self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) in μCP. Why are alkanethiols preferred for gold substrates? (7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Step-by-step procedure (7 marks)** 1. **Master Fabrication (1 mark):** A photolithographically patterned silicon master is produced with the inverse relief of the desired pattern. 2. **PDMS Moulding (1 mark):** Liquid PDMS (10:1 prepolymer-to-curing-agent ratio) is poured over the master, degassed under vacuum, and thermally cured at ~70 °C for 1–2 hours. 3. **Stamp Release (1 mark):** The cross-linked PDMS is peeled off, producing a stamp whose relief matches the original photomask. 4. **Inking (1.5 marks):** The stamp surface is exposed (by immersion or by drop-casting) to a millimolar solution of alkanethiol (e.g., HDT in ethanol) for ~30 s to 5 min. The solvent evaporates, leaving a thin layer of thiol on the raised features. 5. **Conformal Contact (1.5 marks):** The inked stamp is placed on the Au-coated substrate for a controlled dwell time (5–60 s). Van der Waals forces drive conformal adhesion. 6. **Pattern Transfer (1 mark):** SAM forms in contacted regions; non-contacted regions remain bare. Subsequent wet etching (e.g., K₃[Fe(CN)₆]/K₄[Fe(CN)₆]) or growth (e.g., electroless Cu) uses the SAM as a molecular mask. **(b) Role of SAMs and preference for alkanethiols on Au (7 marks)** - **SAM definition (1.5 marks):** A self-assembled monolayer is an ordered molecular film that forms spontaneously on a substrate by chemisorption of surfactant molecules possessing a head group, alkyl chain, and terminal functional group. - **Role in μCP (2 marks):** - Acts as a **molecular resist** for selective wet/dry etching. - Provides **chemical contrast** (e.g., -CH₃ hydrophobic vs. -COOH hydrophilic) for selective protein/cell adhesion. - Functions as a **template** for selective deposition (electroless plating, CVD seeding). - **Why alkanethiols on gold? (3.5 marks):** 1. **Strong Au–S bond (~44 kcal/mol)** provides durable chemisorption. 2. **Van der Waals inter-chain interactions** between adjacent CH₂ groups drive dense packing (~7.7 × 10¹⁴ molecules/cm²). 3. **Tunable terminal group (-X)** allows chemical functionalisation (CH₃, OH, COOH, NH₂, biotin). 4. **Au is inert** under ambient conditions, ensuring reproducibility. 5. **Rapid assembly kinetics** (~seconds) compatible with high-throughput printing. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice-2]** > *(a) List any four major applications of micro contact printing in micro/nano fabrication. (7 marks)* > *(b) What are the resolution-limiting defects in μCP? Explain any two with remedies. (7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Four major applications (7 marks — 1.75 each)** 1. **Microfluidic device fabrication:** μCP of hydrophobic SAMs on glass to define hydrophobic/hydrophilic patterns that guide microchannel walls in lab-on-chip systems. 2. **Biosensor functionalisation:** Patterning of protein-binding SAMs (e.g., biotin-terminated thiols) on Au SPR chips for spatially resolved detection of analytes. 3. **Cell and tissue engineering:** μCP of cell-adhesive patterns on non-adhesive backgrounds to control cell adhesion, alignment, and migration for tissue scaffolds. 4. **OLED and OPV pixelation:** Patterning cathode or anode interfaces in organic electronics using μCP-defined resists for high-resolution displays. 5. (Optional bonus) **Selective growth of nanowires and CNTs:** SAM-defined catalyst islands for CVD growth of ZnO, Si, or carbon nanotubes. **(b) Resolution-limiting defects (7 marks)** 1. **Roof Collapse (2 marks):** When aspect ratio $\frac{h}{w} > 5$, raised PDMS posts buckle under contact pressure and collapse onto the substrate, smearing the pattern. **Remedy:** Limit $\frac{h}{w} \leq 2$ or use harder PDMS formulations (5:1 instead of 10:1). 2. **Sagging (1.5 marks):** Wide, thin PDMS features deflect under their own weight or under contact load, causing unintended SAM transfer. **Remedy:** Reduce feature width, use composite stamps (PDMS + rigid backplane). 3. **Ink Diffusion / Molecular Bleeding (1.5 marks):** Long contact times or high humidity allow alkanethiols to diffuse laterally beyond the contact area. **Remedy:** Reduce contact time (<10 s) and operate under controlled humidity. 4. **Pairing Defects (1 mark):** Closely spaced posts adhere to each other due to capillary or van der Waals forces. **Remedy:** Increase inter-post spacing beyond ~3× the feature width. 5. **Incomplete Transfer (1 mark):** Insufficient contact pressure or dirty stamp surface prevents uniform SAM formation. **Remedy:** Apply uniform load and clean stamp with ethanol before use. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in μCP questions:** > 1. **Confusing μCP with NIL (Nano Imprint Lithography)** — μCP transfers a **molecular monolayer**, not a polymer pattern. State the word "**self-assembled monolayer**" at least once in every μCP answer. > 2. **Forgetting to specify substrate + ink pairing** — saying "alkanethiol is used" without specifying "**on gold**" costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Missing units** — Always quote Young's modulus in **MPa** and chain length in **n (number of CH₂ groups)**. > 4. **Skipping the dwell time** — Examiners expect to see **5–60 s** mentioned explicitly. > 5. **Not mentioning Au–S bond energy (~44 kcal/mol)** — this is a "favourite examiner hook" for SAM chemistry. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **μCP** = Soft lithography using a **PDMS stamp** inked with **SAM-forming molecules**. - **Stamp material:** Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), Young's modulus **~1.8 MPa**, surface energy **~21 mJ/m²**. - **Stamp master:** Photolithography or e-beam patterned silicon. - **Standard inking molecules:** **Alkanethiols** (HS-(CH₂)$_n$-X) for coinage metals; **alkylsilanes** (e.g., OTS) for oxides. - **Au–S bond energy:** **~44 kcal/mol**. - **SAM thickness:** $d \approx (n \times 1.27 + 0.3)$ Å. - **Surface coverage:** **~7.7 × 10¹⁴ molecules/cm²**. - **Dwell time:** **5 – 60 s**. - **Demonstrated resolution:** **~50 nm**. - **Stamp aspect ratio limit:** $h/w \leq 5$ (recommended ≤ 2 for defect-free printing). - **Stamp reusability:** **> 100 cycles**. - **Five big applications:** Microfluidics, biosensors, OLEDs, cell/tissue engineering, selective catalyst growth. - **Five big defects:** Roof collapse, sagging, ink diffusion, pairing, incomplete transfer. - **Magic pair to memorise:** **"Thiols on Au, Silanes on SiO₂."** - **Examiner hot words:** Always say **"self-assembled monolayer," "conformal contact," "molecular resist."**

topics.title: Characterizing the Surface Micromachining Process - Isolation Layer, Sacrificial Layer, Structural Material, Selective Etching, Properties, Stress, Stress Measurement, Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characterizing the Surface Micromachining Process - Isolation Layer, Sacrificial Layer, Structural Material, Selective Etching, Properties, Stress, Stress Measurement, Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Micromachining** is a planar batch-fabrication process used in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) technology in which mechanical and electrostatic structures are built by the sequential deposition, patterning, and selective removal (etching) of thin films on the surface of a substrate (typically a silicon wafer). Unlike bulk micromachining, the substrate itself is not significantly etched; instead, functional microstructures are formed *above* the substrate using thin-film layers.

The process is characterized by three foundational thin-film layers:

1. **Isolation Layer** — A dielectric (insulating) layer, usually thermally grown SiO₂ or deposited LPCVD oxide/nitride, placed between the substrate and the active MEMS films. Its primary functions are: (a) electrical isolation of MEMS electrodes from the conductive silicon substrate, and (b) reduction of parasitic capacitance, leakage currents, and substrate injection effects.

2. **Sacrificial Layer** — A temporary, selectively-removable material (commonly LPCVD phosphosilicate glass — PSG, or undoped silicate glass — USG, or poly-Si in some process flows) that is deposited, patterned, and then eventually etched away (released) to create free-standing or suspended microstructures. The "release" etch is the defining step of surface micromachining.

3. **Structural Material (Structural Layer)** — The permanent, mechanically functional thin film that is deposited and patterned *on top of* the sacrificial layer. After release, this layer becomes the freely movable beam, plate, or proof mass of the MEMS device. Common structural materials are polycrystalline silicon (polysilicon), silicon nitride (Si₃N₄), silicon carbide (SiC), and electroplated metals such as Ni, Cu, and Au.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The four pillars that fully *characterize* a surface micromachining process are:
> **(i) Material selection & layer stack**, **(ii) Selective etching chemistry**, **(iii) Residual stress & stress gradients in thin films**, and **(iv) Tribological behaviour (friction) at the micro-scale**. Mastery of these four pillars is mandatory for any 14-mark derivation/numerical question on this topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandwich and Lift-Off" Picture

Imagine you are building a tiny **bridged walkway** that must be supported *only* at its two ends, with empty air underneath.

- The **substrate** is the ground floor of a building.
- The **isolation layer** is the waterproof + electrically insulating membrane laid on the ground (so nothing electrical leaks into the ground).
- The **sacrificial layer** is a thick **slab of ice** (or wet clay) that you pour over this membrane and shape into pillars. It looks solid, but it is meant to disappear later.
- The **structural layer** is the concrete walkway that is cast/moulded *on top* of the ice pillars. Once the concrete hardens, you wait for the sun (the release etchant — usually **HF for oxide sacrificial layers**) to *melt the ice*. The ice dissolves completely, leaving the concrete walkway standing freely in mid-air, supported only at its two ends.

This is exactly how a polysilicon cantilever, suspended plate, or movable proof mass is created. The "sun" (HF vapour or liquid) is carefully chosen so that it dissolves the **sacrificial oxide (SiO₂)** thousands of times faster than the structural polysilicon — this property is called **selectivity**.

## 1.3 Why Selective Etching Defines the Process

A successful surface micromachining release etch must have a **selectivity ratio ≥ 1000:1** (etch rate of sacrificial layer ÷ etch rate of structural layer). Common etchant systems are:

| Sacrificial Layer | Structural Layer | Release Etchant | Selectivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| SiO₂ (PSG / USG) | Polysilicon | Concentrated HF (49 %) or vapour HF | ~1000 : 1 |
| SiO₂ | Si₃N₄ | Buffered HF (BHF) | > 1000 : 1 |
| Polysilicon | SiO₂ | KOH / TMAH / XeF₂ | > 100 : 1 |
| Photoresist / polymer | Metal (Ni, Cu) | Acetone / organic solvent | Very high |
| Cu | Ni | Cu-specific wet etchant | > 100 : 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stiction** — Once the sacrificial layer is etched away and the released structure is dried, capillary forces from the receding liquid meniscus can pull micro-structures into permanent contact with the underlying layer, a failure mode called *release stiction*. This is one of the most studied reliability problems in surface micromachining and is often examined in 7-mark sub-parts of KTU questions.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics

The key engineering metrics used to characterize the surface micromachining process are:

- **Etch selectivity (S)** = (etch rate of sacrificial layer) / (etch rate of structural layer). Typical target **S > 1000**.
- **Residual stress (σ_res)** in structural films. Target for free-standing structures: **< 50 MPa** (compressive to slightly tensile).
- **Stress gradient (dσ/dz)** across film thickness. Target: **< 1 × 10¹¹ Pa/m** to avoid out-of-plane curling.
- **Stiction force (F_st)**. Target: **< 1 µN** for typical MEMS switches and accelerometers.
- **Coefficient of friction (µ)** at micro-scale. Often **5× to 10× higher** than the macro-scale value for the same material pair.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Operational Breakdown

The complete surface micromachining process flow can be decomposed into the following logical steps. Each step is mandatory and the order is fixed.

**Step 1 — Substrate Preparation**
- Begin with a single-crystal silicon wafer (typically 100 mm, 150 mm, or 200 mm diameter, 525 µm thick).
- Clean using RCA-1 / RCA-2 (or piranha) chemistry to remove organic and metallic contamination.

**Step 2 — Isolation Layer Deposition**
- Grow a thermal SiO₂ layer (dry oxidation at ~1000 °C) or deposit LPCVD Si₃N₄.
- Thickness typically **0.5 µm – 2.0 µm**.
- This layer prevents substrate leakage and electrically isolates MEMS electrodes.

**Step 3 — Sacrificial Layer Deposition & Patterning**
- Deposit LPCVD Phospho-Silicate Glass (PSG, 6–8 wt% phosphorus) at 450 °C, or undoped silicate glass (USG).
- Thickness typically **0.5 µm – 3.0 µm** (this defines the eventual air gap).
- Photolithography + wet/dry etch opens anchor points down to the isolation layer.

**Step 4 — Structural Layer Deposition & Patterning**
- Deposit polysilicon via LPCVD at 580–620 °C (typically 2 µm thick).
- Heavily dope (n⁺ or p⁺) using POCl₃ or in-situ doping to reduce resistivity to < 1 mΩ·cm.
- Photolithography + RIE (Reactive Ion Etching) using Cl₂/HBr or SF₆ chemistries defines the beam, plate, or gear geometry.

**Step 5 — Release Etch (Selective Etching)**
- Immerse in 49 % HF for 1–10 minutes (or use vapour HF for stiction-free release).
- The sacrificial SiO₂ is dissolved, leaving the polysilicon structure freely suspended.
- Critical rinse + supercritical CO₂ drying or freeze-drying is used to prevent stiction.

**Step 6 — Characterization & Packaging**
- Released structures are inspected via SEM, optical profilometry, and wafer-curvature stress measurement.

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties of the Three Layers (the "Why")

| Property | Isolation Layer (SiO₂) | Sacrificial Layer (PSG) | Structural Layer (polysilicon) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus, E | ~70 GPa | ~70 GPa | 130 – 170 GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio, ν | 0.17 | 0.17 | 0.22 – 0.28 |
| Density, ρ | 2.2 g/cm³ | 2.2 – 2.3 g/cm³ | 2.3 g/cm³ |
| Residual Stress | ~ –300 MPa (compressive) | Near-zero after anneal | 0 to –100 MPa (target tensile) |
| Thermal Expansion (CTE) | 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ /K | 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ /K | 2.6 – 3.0 × 10⁻⁶ /K |
| Dielectric Constant | 3.9 | 3.9 | N/A (conductor when doped) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **CTE mismatch** between polysilicon (3.0 × 10⁻⁶/K) and silicon substrate (2.6 × 10⁻⁶/K) is the dominant source of **extrinsic thermal stress** and is the basis of Stoney's equation derivation in §3.1.

## 2.3 Stress in Thin Films — The Two Origins

A thin film deposited on a substrate is invariably in a state of internal stress. This stress has **two origins**:

**(A) Intrinsic Stress (σ_int):**
Generated *during* film growth due to:
- Grain boundary relaxation
- Impurity incorporation (e.g., P, B, O, H)
- Defect annihilation during deposition
- Phase transformation (amorphous → polycrystalline)

Intrinsic stress can be either tensile (+) or compressive (–) and depends strongly on deposition parameters (pressure, temperature, gas flow, RF power).

**(B) Extrinsic Stress (σ_ext):**
Generated *after* deposition, primarily due to:
- **Thermal mismatch stress** between film and substrate
- External mechanical loading
- Moisture absorption
- Oxidation-induced volume expansion (e.g., Si → SiO₂ causes ~2.2× volume increase → compressive stress in adjacent layers)

The total residual stress is:
$$\sigma_{\text{res}} = \sigma_{\text{int}} + \sigma_{\text{ext}}$$

## 2.4 Stress Gradient — Why Beams Curl

If the stress is *not uniform through the film thickness*, the released beam will curl out-of-plane (bimorph-like effect). A typical polysilicon film has a stress gradient **dσ/dz** of order **10¹¹ Pa/m**. The out-of-plane tip deflection δ of a cantilever of length L is:
$$\delta = \frac{1}{E_f} \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{dz} \cdot \frac{L^2}{2} \quad \text{(for } \frac{d\sigma}{dz} \text{ linear)}$$

For L = 100 µm, E_f = 160 GPa, dσ/dz = 10¹¹ Pa/m, this gives δ ≈ **0.31 µm** — small but visible under interferometry and enough to shift MEMS capacitive gaps.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{R_{\text{sac}}}{R_{\text{str}}}$ | Selectivity ratio (R = etch rate) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f} (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \Delta T$ | Thermal stress in thin film | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, (1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \left( \dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{R_0} \right)$ | **Stoney's equation** for film stress | Pa |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{R_0}$ | Change in curvature (wafer bow) | m⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, (1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \cdot \dfrac{8 \, h}{d^{\,2}}$ | Stoney's form from interferometric height h of a scan length d | Pa |
| 6 | $\delta_{\text{curl}} = \dfrac{1}{E_f} \dfrac{d\sigma}{dz} \dfrac{L^2}{2}$ | Cantilever tip curl from stress gradient | m |
| 7 | $F_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2 \, \gamma \, A \, \cos\theta}{d}$ | Capillary (stiction) force, γ = surface tension | N |
| 8 | $F_{\text{cap,plate}} = \dfrac{2 \, \gamma \, A \, \cos\theta}{g^{\,2}}$ | Capillary force on parallel plate (g = gap) | N |
| 9 | $F_{\text{van der Waals}} = \dfrac{A_H \, A}{6 \, \pi \, d^{\,3}}$ | van der Waals pull-down force, A_H = Hamaker constant | N |
| 10 | $\mu_{\text{micro}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{friction}}}{F_{\text{normal}}}$ | Coefficient of friction (always positive) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{interfacial}}}{p_{\text{contact}}}$ | Friction law from interfacial shear | dimensionless |
| 12 | $F_{\text{adh}} = \dfrac{A_H}{6 \, \pi \, d_0^{\,3}} \cdot A$ | Adhesion force at asperity contact | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Stoney's equation (formula #3) is the single most important equation for this module. Memorize the *exact* placement of the **biaxial modulus** term $E_s/(1-\nu_s)$ on the **substrate** side (NOT the film side) — students routinely swap it and lose 2 marks.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The methodology of surface micromachining — isolation + sacrificial + structural + selective release — is the foundational process behind:

- **Analog Devices ADXL accelerometers** (polysilicon surface-micromachined proof mass + capacitive sensing)
- **Texas Instruments DLP micromirror arrays** (aluminium structural layer on PSG sacrificial)
- **Inkjet printer heads** (HP and Canon thermal + piezo)
- **RF MEMS switches** (electroplated Au/Ni on photoresist sacrificial)
- **Digital micromirror devices (DMDs)** with over 2 million mirrors per chip

In all these devices, controlling **residual stress and friction** is what determines yield, lifetime, and reliability.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stoney's Equation (the Centrepiece of Module 4)

Stoney's equation relates the **biaxial residual stress** in a thin film to the **change in curvature** of the substrate on which it is deposited. This derivation is regularly asked as a 7-mark sub-question in KTU examinations.

**Starting Assumptions (Stoney, 1909):**
1. The film is thin compared to the substrate: $t_f \ll t_s$.
2. The film stress is **equibiaxial** in the plane of the film: $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma_f$, $\sigma_{xy} = 0$.
3. The substrate is much thicker than the film, so its bending stiffness dominates.
4. The film thickness is uniform.
5. Plane sections remain plane (Euler–Bernoulli beam theory assumption).

**Step 1 — Geometry of Curvature**

Consider the substrate as an initially flat beam of length L, thickness $t_s$, and unit width. After film deposition, it curves with radius R. The top surface of the substrate is stretched/compressed by an amount equal to the substrate's mid-surface strain $\varepsilon_s$ at distance $t_s/2$ from the neutral axis:
$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{t_s}{2R}$$

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium (Force on film = Force from substrate)**

The film applies a force per unit width $F_f = \sigma_f \cdot t_f$. The substrate resists this with a moment generated by the linear stress distribution across its thickness. The biaxial stress at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis of the substrate is:
$$\sigma_s(y) = E_s \left( \varepsilon_s - \frac{y}{R} \right) \quad \text{(for } (1-\nu_s) \text{ biaxial correction absorbed later)}$$

Force balance per unit width (substrate stress integrated must equal film stress):
$$\int_{-t_s/2}^{+t_s/2} \sigma_s(y) \, dy + \sigma_f \, t_f = 0$$

The first term is zero by symmetry (linear stress distribution gives zero net force), so the **bending moment** must balance, not the force. This is the classical plate-bending result.

**Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium**

The moment about the mid-plane from the linear stress in the substrate must balance the moment from the film force $\sigma_f t_f$ acting at the top surface $y = t_s/2$:
$$\int_{-t_s/2}^{+t_s/2} \sigma_s(y) \cdot y \, dy = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Stress Distribution**

Using $\sigma_s(y) = \dfrac{E_s}{1-\nu_s} \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}$ (this is the biaxial plane-stress form, where $E_s/(1-\nu_s)$ is the **biaxial modulus** $M_s$):
$$\int_{-t_s/2}^{+t_s/2} \frac{E_s}{1-\nu_s} \cdot \frac{y}{R} \cdot y \, dy = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral**

$$\frac{E_s}{1-\nu_s} \cdot \frac{1}{R} \int_{-t_s/2}^{+t_s/2} y^2 \, dy = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}$$

$$\int_{-t_s/2}^{+t_s/2} y^2 \, dy = \frac{y^3}{3} \bigg\vert_{-t_s/2}^{+t_s/2} = \frac{t_s^{\,3}}{12}$$

So:
$$\frac{E_s}{1-\nu_s} \cdot \frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{t_s^{\,3}}{12} = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\sigma_f$**

$$\frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,3}}{12 \, (1-\nu_s) \, R} = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}$$

$$\sigma_f \, t_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, (1-\nu_s) \, R}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, (1-\nu_s) \, t_f \, R}}$$

**Step 7 — Account for Initial Curvature $R_0$**

In practice the wafer already has a finite radius of curvature $R_0$ *before* deposition. The relevant curvature change is:
$$\Delta K = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0}$$

The **final, experimentally usable form of Stoney's equation** is:
$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, (1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \left( \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0} \right)}$$

**Step 8 — Engineering Insight**

A positive $\sigma_f$ (tensile) makes the wafer **concave up** (smiley-face), and a negative $\sigma_f$ (compressive) makes it **concave down** (frowny-face). This is the diagnostic convention used in profilometer scans of film stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity limits of Stoney's equation**:
> 1. Film thickness << substrate thickness (typically $t_f/t_s < 0.01$).
> 2. Elastic response of substrate only (no plastic deformation).
> 3. Single layer film; for multilayers, use the **Freund–Suresh** modification.
> 4. Substrate is much wider than its thickness.
> 5. No creep at measurement temperature.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Stoney's Equation

> **Problem (KTU Numerical Practice):** A silicon wafer ($E_s$ = 130 GPa, $\nu_s$ = 0.28, $t_s$ = 525 µm) is coated with a 1.0 µm thick polysilicon film. Before deposition the wafer is flat (R₀ → ∞). After deposition, the wafer bows into a spherical shape with radius R = 25 m. Calculate the residual stress in the polysilicon film. State whether it is tensile or compressive.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $E_s = 130 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\nu_s = 0.28$
- $t_s = 525 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $t_f = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $R_0 = \infty$ (flat), so $1/R_0 = 0$
- $R = 25$ m, so $1/R = 0.04$ m⁻¹

**Step 1:** Calculate the biaxial modulus of the substrate:
$$M_s = \frac{E_s}{1-\nu_s} = \frac{130 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.28} = \frac{130 \times 10^9}{0.72}$$

$$M_s = 180.56 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the geometric factor:
$$\frac{t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, t_f} = \frac{(525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{6.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.04594 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $1/R - 1/R_0$:
$$\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0} = 0.04 - 0 = 0.04 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Stoney's equation:
$$\sigma_f = M_s \times 0.04594 \times 0.04$$

$$\sigma_f = 180.56 \times 10^9 \times 1.8376 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_f = 3.318 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \approx 331.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Sign and physical interpretation:

Since the wafer curves **concave up** after deposition (R is positive for a smiley-face bow), the film is in **tensile** stress. Polysilicon tensile stress of ~330 MPa is too high for a released MEMS cantilever — it would buckle or fracture. The deposition parameters (T, pressure, dopant level) must be adjusted to bring this to < 50 MPa. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation of Capillary (Stiction) Force

The **Laplace pressure** across a curved liquid meniscus of radius of curvature $r_m$ is:
$$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2} \right) \approx \frac{2 \gamma}{r_m}$$

For a thin liquid film of thickness d between two flat surfaces (parallel-plate approximation), $r_m \approx d/2$, so:
$$\Delta P \approx \frac{2 \gamma}{d / 2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{2 \gamma}{d}$$

Wait — correcting for the geometry: a meniscus between two parallel plates has $r_1 = d/2$ in the cross-section direction and $r_2 \to \infty$ in the transverse direction, so:
$$\Delta P = \frac{\gamma}{r_1} = \frac{2 \gamma}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Force on the plate:**
The downward force pulling the upper plate toward the substrate is:
$$F_{\text{cap}} = \Delta P \cdot A = \frac{2 \gamma A}{d}$$

For complete wetting ($\cos\theta \approx 1$):
$$\boxed{F_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{d}}$$

For water ($\gamma$ = 72.8 mN/m), A = 100 µm × 100 µm, d = 1 µm:
$$F_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 10 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.456 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N} = 1.46 \text{ µN}$$

This is comparable to the restoring spring force of typical MEMS flexures (1 – 10 µN), which is exactly why **stiction is a dominant failure mode** in surface micromachining.

## 3.4 Van der Waals Adhesion Force

For two flat surfaces separated by a distance $d_0$ (typically 0.2 – 1 nm at contact), the van der Waals force is:
$$F_{\text{vdW}} = \frac{A_H \, A}{6 \pi d_0^{\,3}}$$

Where $A_H$ is the **Hamaker constant** (typically $10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$ J for Si-SiO₂-Si). The **stiction occurs when** $F_{\text{vdW}} + F_{\text{cap}} > F_{\text{spring}}$.

## 3.5 Friction in MEMS — Asymmetric Statistical Model

At the macro-scale, friction force $F_f = \mu F_N$ (Amontons' law) is rate-independent and independent of apparent contact area. At the micro-scale, this is **violated** because:

1. The number of asperity contacts is small (single-digit at micro-scale), so statistical fluctuations dominate.
2. Surface forces (vdW, capillary, electrostatic) become comparable to or larger than the applied normal load.
3. Wear debris and third-body effects are very different.

A widely used micro-scale friction model is the **Rabinowicz / Statistical Asperity Model**:
$$\mu_{\text{micro}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{asp}}}{\sigma_{\text{y,asp}}}$$

where $\tau_{\text{asp}}$ is the interfacial shear strength and $\sigma_{\text{y,asp}}$ is the yield strength of the asperity. For silicon-silicon contact:
- $\tau_{\text{asp}} \approx 0.1$ to $1$ GPa
- $\sigma_{\text{y,asp}} \approx 5$ to $10$ GPa
- Therefore $\mu_{\text{micro}} \approx 0.1$ to $0.5$, but with **enormous variability** (1 – 5× higher than macroscale Si-Si µ = 0.2).

The **stick-slip** behaviour at micro-scale follows a modified Coulomb model:
$$F_f(t) = \begin{cases} k \cdot (x(t) - x_{\text{stick}}) & \text{during stick phase} \\ \mu_k F_N \, \text{sgn}(\dot{x}) & \text{during slip phase} \end{cases}$$

The **energy dissipation per cycle** is what limits the quality factor Q of MEMS resonators to typically 10⁴ – 10⁵ at atmospheric pressure (Q can reach 10⁶+ in vacuum).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bare Si Wafer 100 Orientation] --> B[RCA Clean and Native Oxide Strip]
```
