```mermaid
    I --> J[Step 8 RIE Polysilicon Using Cl2 HBr]
    J --> K[Step 9 Release Etch 49 percent HF for 5 min]
    K --> L{Stiction Risk Check}
    L -->|High Risk| M[Supercritical CO2 Drying]
    L -->|Low Risk| N[Conventional DI Water Rinse and N2 Drying]
    M --> O[Released MEMS Cantilever Free Standing]
    N --> O

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Friction Force Mechanism in MEMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Normal Force FN Applied] --> B[Real Contact Area Forms at Asperities]
    B --> C[Asperity Junctions Shear Interfacial Shear Strength tau_asp]
    C --> D[Friction Force F_f equals tau_asp times A_real]
    D --> E[Stick Slip Cycle]

    E --> F[Stick Phase: spring loads up, no motion]
    F --> G[Slip Threshold reached F_s equals mu_s times FN]
    G --> H[Slip Phase: kinetic friction F_k equals mu_k times FN]
    H --> I[Kinetic Energy Dissipated as Heat]
    I --> F

    J[Capillary Force] --> D
    K[Van der Waals Force] --> D
    L[Electrostatic Force] --> D
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 marks]**
**"Define the terms (a) Isolation layer, (b) Sacrificial layer, and (c) Structural layer as used in surface micromachining. State one example material for each."**

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Isolation Layer:** A thin dielectric film (typically thermally grown SiO₂, 0.5 – 2 µm thick) deposited on the silicon substrate to provide electrical isolation between the substrate and the overlying MEMS electrodes. *Example: SiO₂ or Si₃N₄.*
- **(b) Sacrificial Layer:** A temporary, selectively-removable thin film (typically LPCVD PSG or undoped silicate glass) deposited on top of the isolation layer and patterned with anchor openings. It is eventually dissolved (released) using a selective etchant such as HF to leave free-standing microstructures. *Example: PSG (Phospho-Silicate Glass).*
- **(c) Structural Layer:** The permanent, mechanically functional thin film (typically LPCVD polysilicon) deposited and patterned on top of the sacrificial layer. After release etch, it becomes the freely movable beam, plate, or proof mass. *Example: polysilicon, electroplated Ni, or Au.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 4, 3 marks]**
**"What is meant by 'selective etching' in surface micromachining? Why is HF used as a release etchant for SiO₂ sacrificial layer over polysilicon?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Selective etching** is the process of removing the sacrificial layer using a chemical etchant that dissolves the sacrificial material at a high rate while leaving the structural layer essentially unattacked. Selectivity is quantified as the ratio:
$$S = \frac{R_{\text{sacrificial}}}{R_{\text{structural}}}$$

**Why HF:** Hydrofluoric acid (49 % concentrated or vapour phase) attacks SiO₂ via the reaction:
$$\text{SiO}_2 + 6\text{HF} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The etch rate of thermal SiO₂ in 49 % HF is approximately **1 µm/min**, while the etch rate of polysilicon is essentially **zero** (silicon is passivated by a native oxide that HF removes only slowly, and even then Si etches at < 1 nm/min in HF). This gives a **selectivity of > 1000:1**, which is essential for a clean, damage-free release. Additionally, HF does not attack photoresist, which can be used as a mask for the polysilicon during release.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Rule]** — Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional schematic, describe the **step-by-step process flow of surface micromachining** for the fabrication of a free-standing polysilicon cantilever. List the materials used for each layer and justify the choice of HF as the release etchant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Stoney's equation** for the biaxial residual stress in a thin film deposited on a thick substrate, starting from first principles. State clearly the assumptions made. A silicon wafer of thickness 525 µm and biaxial modulus 180 GPa is coated with a 0.8 µm polysilicon film. The wafer curvature changes from infinity (initial flat) to 18 m after deposition. Calculate the residual stress in the film. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **origins of residual stress in thin films**. Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic stress. How does stress gradient cause out-of-plane curling of released microstructures? Derive the expression for the tip deflection of a cantilever due to a linear stress gradient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **friction mechanisms at the micro-scale**. Why is the coefficient of friction in MEMS often much higher than at the macro-scale? Discuss the role of capillary and van der Waals forces in MEMS stiction. A parallel-plate MEMS capacitor has plate area 100 × 100 µm² and a 1 µm air gap. Calculate the capillary stiction force assuming complete wetting by water (γ = 72.8 mN/m). **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Model Solution to **Question A (a)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define surface micromachining — building MEMS structures by sequential thin-film deposition, patterning, and selective release above the substrate without etching the substrate itself.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Schematic — draw the standard three-layer cross-section showing:

- Silicon substrate
- SiO₂ isolation layer (1 µm)
- PSG sacrificial layer (2 µm) with anchor opening
- Polysilicon structural layer (2 µm) patterned into a cantilever anchored at one end

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Process flow — list the ten steps from RCA clean through supercritical drying as detailed in §2.1.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Justification of HF — HF has > 1000:1 selectivity for SiO₂ over polysilicon because:
- SiO₂ + 6HF → H₂SiF₆ + 2H₂O is thermodynamically favourable.
- Polysilicon forms a thin passivating native oxide that protects the bulk; even when stripped, Si is etched only at nm/min in HF.
- HF is compatible with photoresist masks, enabling patterned release.
- Vapour HF can be used for stiction-free release.

---

### Model Solution to **Question A (b)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Derivation of Stoney's equation — reproduce the full 7-step derivation from §3.1. Key assumptions: (i) $t_f \ll t_s$, (ii) equibiaxial film stress, (iii) biaxial modulus $E_s/(1-\nu_s)$ for the substrate, (iv) Euler–Bernoulli plane-strain plate bending, (v) elastic regime. Final result:
$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, (1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \left( \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0} \right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State given values: $M_s = E_s/(1-\nu_s) = 180 \times 10^9$ Pa, $t_s = 525 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $t_f = 0.8 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $R_0 = \infty$, $R = 18$ m.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute geometric factor:
$$\frac{t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, t_f} = \frac{(525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{4.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.05743 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute curvature change:
$$\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0} = \frac{1}{18} - 0 = 0.05556 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final stress:
$$\sigma_f = 180 \times 10^9 \times 0.05743 \times 0.05556 = 5.74 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \approx 574 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A (b):**
> Three common errors that cost 2 marks each:
> 1. **Misplacing the biaxial modulus** — using $E_f/(1-\nu_f)$ instead of $E_s/(1-\nu_s)$. Stoney's equation is valid only when $t_f \ll t_s$ and the biaxial modulus belongs to the **substrate**, not the film.
> 2. **Forgetting the subtraction** of $1/R_0$ — if the wafer was initially curved, students often write only $1/R$, which overestimates the stress.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — using $t_s$ in mm instead of metres. Always convert µm to m ($1 \, \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}$ m) before substitution.

---

### Model Solution to **Question B (a)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define residual stress and its two origins: **intrinsic** (from growth defects, grain boundaries, impurity incorporation, amorphous-to-crystalline phase change) and **extrinsic** (from thermal mismatch, oxidation-induced volume expansion, external loading).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Discuss mechanisms — intrinsic stress can be tensile (grain-boundary relaxation, atomic peening in sputtering) or compressive (impurity incorporation, Ar entrapment). Extrinsic thermal stress from CTE mismatch:
$$\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{E_f}{1-\nu_f} (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \Delta T$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For polysilicon on Si at deposition temperature 600 °C cooled to 25 °C, $\Delta T$ = –575 K, $\alpha_f$ – $\alpha_s$ ≈ 0.4 × 10⁻⁶/K, giving $\sigma_{\text{th}}$ ≈ –40 MPa (compressive), typical value.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Stress gradient mechanism — when stress varies linearly through the film thickness, the released beam acts like a bimorph. Top fibre in tension and bottom in compression (or vice versa) → out-of-plane bending moment.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Tip deflection derivation:
$$\delta = \frac{1}{E_f} \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{dz} \cdot \frac{L^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Numerical estimate for L = 200 µm, E_f = 160 GPa, dσ/dz = 10¹¹ Pa/m:
$$\delta = \frac{10^{11}}{1.6 \times 10^{11}} \times \frac{(200 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2} = 12.5 \, \mu\text{m}$$

This is huge and unacceptable for a typical capacitive gap of 1–2 µm — drives the need for **stress-engineered polysilicon** with near-zero dσ/dz.

---

### Model Solution to **Question B (b)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** At the micro-scale, **asperity contact statistics** dominate. Friction arises from the sum of shear stresses on each microscopic contact junction. With only a handful of asperities at micro-scale, statistical variations in shear strength cause µ to fluctuate 1 – 5× more than at macro-scale.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** **Surface forces become comparable to or exceed the applied normal load** at the micro-scale. Specifically:
- Capillary force from adsorbed water film: ~ 1 – 10 µN.
- Van der Waals force: ~ 0.1 – 1 µN for typical 1 µm gap.
- Electrostatic force: depends on charging.
- Typical MEMS normal load on a flexure: ~ 1 – 10 µN.

So the *effective* normal load (mechanical + surface forces) is much larger than the mechanical load alone, and the friction force scales with this effective load.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Stick-slip behaviour — describe the modified Coulomb model with $\mu_s$ > $\mu_k$ and the resulting energy dissipation per cycle, which limits the **quality factor Q** of MEMS resonators to 10⁴ – 10⁵ in air and > 10⁶ in vacuum.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Stiction analysis — when $F_{\text{vdW}} + F_{\text{cap}} > F_{\text{spring}}$ during the drying phase after HF release, the released structure permanently adheres to the substrate. This is a **major reliability problem** in commercial surface-micromachined devices.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Numerical calculation of capillary stiction force:
$$F_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{d} = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times (100 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 1}{1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-6}} = 1.456 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N} = 1.456 \, \text{mN}$$

This is two to three orders of magnitude larger than the restoring force of typical MEMS flexures (~ 1 µN), confirming that **capillary stiction is a dominant failure mode** if the structure is not dried using **supercritical CO₂ or freeze-drying methods**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (b):**
> Two common errors that cost 1 – 2 marks each:
> 1. **Confusing the area in $F_{\text{cap}}$** — students sometimes write $A$ = gap area in m², but if they were given A = 100 × 100 µm² they must convert to m² ($10^{-8}$ m²) before substitution. Unit errors in this section cause cascading wrong answers.
> 2. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$** — for complete wetting the contact angle is 0° and $\cos\theta = 1$. Students sometimes leave out the cosine term, losing ½ mark.
> 3. **Confusing capillary stiction with van der Waals** — they are physically different mechanisms. Capillary is from liquid meniscus Laplace pressure; vdW is from intermolecular dispersion forces. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if these are mixed up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist (Important Things to Remember)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Bullet List**

- **Three foundational layers of surface micromachining** are: (1) **Isolation layer** (SiO₂ for electrical isolation from substrate), (2) **Sacrificial layer** (PSG or USG for temporary support, later dissolved), (3) **Structural layer** (polysilicon, Si₃N₄, or electroplated metal — the permanent MEMS body).
- **Selective etching** is the defining step; selectivity **S = R_sac / R_str** must be > 1000:1.
- **HF (49 % or vapour)** is the standard release etchant for SiO₂ sacrificial over polysilicon, because of the SiO₂ + 6HF → H₂SiF₆ + 2H₂O reaction and the passivation of silicon in HF.
- **Residual stress σ_res = σ_intrinsic + σ_extrinsic**; thermal stress from CTE mismatch is the dominant extrinsic component.
- **Stoney's equation**: $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, (1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \left( \dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{R_0} \right)$ — the biaxial modulus $E_s/(1-\nu_s)$ belongs to the **substrate**, not the film.
- **Stress gradient dσ/dz** causes **out-of-plane curling** of released cantilevers: $\delta = \dfrac{1}{E_f} \dfrac{d\sigma}{dz} \dfrac{L^2}{2}$.
- **Stiction** is the major failure mode — caused by **capillary force** $F_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{d}$ and **van der Waals force** $F_{\text{vdW}} = \dfrac{A_H A}{6 \pi d_0^{\,3}}$. Mitigated by **supercritical CO₂ drying** and **dimple structures**.
- **Friction at the micro-scale** is 5–10× higher than at macro-scale due to dominant surface forces and few-asperity statistics. Stick-slip behaviour is described by the modified Coulomb model with $\mu_s$ > $\mu_k$.
- **Standard characterization methods for stress**: (i) **Wafer curvature (Stoney)**, (ii) **Bulge test**, (iii) **X-ray diffraction (sin²ψ method)**, (iv) **Raman spectroscopy**, (v) **Interferometric cantilever deflection**.
- **Validity of Stoney's equation** requires $t_f/t_s < 0.01$, elastic substrate, no creep, equibiaxial stress, and single-layer film.
- **Free-standing surface-micromachined devices** in real-world production: ADXL accelerometers, TI DLP/DMD mirrors, RF MEMS switches, inkjet print-heads.
- **Anchor geometry** — the sacrificial layer is patterned with anchor openings that extend down to the isolation layer; the structural material deposited into these openings forms the mechanical anchor of the released structure to the substrate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wafer Bonding: Anodic and Fusion Bonding
Manipulative Techniques - Process Principle and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manipulative Techniques - Process Principle and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 4 Focus**
> This module deals with the *post-fabrication handling* of micro and nano structures. While Module 3 typically focuses on the *making* of these structures, Module 4 focuses on the *moving, positioning, assembling and characterizing* of them — collectively termed **Manipulative Techniques**.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Manipulative Techniques** in the context of micro and nano manufacturing refer to the family of controlled, contact or non-contact physical methodologies employed to **position, orient, translate, rotate, assemble, and probe** structures, particles, molecules, and devices whose characteristic dimensions lie in the range **1 nm to 1000 μm**. These techniques operate against the dominant **surface forces** (van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic, friction) that govern behavior at the micro/nano scale, and they use externally applied **force fields** (mechanical, optical, magnetic, electrical, acoustic, fluidic) to achieve deterministic motion with positional accuracy down to the **sub-nanometer** regime.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, manipulative techniques are classified as:

| Class | Dominant Force Field | Typical Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Elastic restoring / friction | 1 nm – 100 nm |
| **Optical** | Photon radiation pressure | 10 nm – 500 nm |
| **Magnetic** | Field gradient force | 100 nm – 10 μm |
| **Electrical** | Dielectrophoresis / Coulomb | 50 nm – 5 μm |
| **Fluidic** | Viscous drag / capillary | 500 nm – 100 μm |
| **Acoustic** | Acoustic radiation pressure | 1 μm – 100 μm |
| **Self-Assembly** | Minimization of free energy | Molecular to μm |

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Ant on a Leaf in a Hurricane"**
> Imagine trying to push a single ant around on a wet leaf during a hurricane. The ant is so light that every breeze, every drop of water, every electrostatic charge on the leaf decides where it goes. You cannot simply "grab" it with fingers — your fingers are millions of times too big. To control it, you would have to use a tiny, invisible tractor beam (light), a magnetic field (if the ant is iron-clad), or the breeze itself (fluid drag). **This is exactly the engineering problem of micro/nano manipulation.** The "ant" is a nanoparticle, the "leaf" is a substrate, and the "hurricane" is the chaotic dominance of surface forces. Manipulative techniques are our arsenal of tractor beams, magnetic gloves, and directed breezes to deliberately move the ant.

### 1.3 Why Manipulation is a Unique Challenge at the Nanoscale

At the macro scale, **inertial forces** (gravity, momentum) dominate and friction is small. As we shrink to the micro/nano scale, the situation inverts dramatically:

- **Volume forces** (gravity, inertia) scale as $\sim L^{3}$
- **Surface forces** (van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic) scale as $\sim L^{2}$ or $\sim L^{1}$
- Therefore, the **surface-to-volume ratio** scales as $1/L$, meaning surface forces grow enormously in relative importance.

The dimensionless **Bond number** captures this transition:

$$Bo \;=\; \frac{\rho \, g \, L^{2}}{\gamma}$$

For micron-scale water droplets ($L \approx 10\,\mu m$, $\rho \approx 10^{3}\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $g \approx 9.81\,\text{m/s}^{2}$, $\gamma \approx 0.072\,\text{N/m}$), the Bond number is far less than **1**, confirming that **capillary (surface) forces utterly dominate gravity**. This is why a water droplet on a lotus leaf does not fall off, and why a micron particle sticks to a wall as if glued.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners love asking *"Why can't we just use tweezers at the nano scale?"* The answer is the surface-to-volume scaling law above — your tweezers are macroscopic (huge inertia, huge stiction) and the object is sub-micron (negligible inertia, huge surface adhesion). The force ratio is **catastrophically wrong**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Physical Regimes

Every manipulative technique in micro/nano manufacturing ultimately exploits one (or a combination) of the following force regimes:

**A. Mechanical / Contact Regime**
The tip of a probe physically contacts the object. The force is governed by **Hooke's law** through the elastic deformation of the probe cantilever, plus adhesive and frictional contact mechanics.

$$F_{\text{mech}} \;=\; -k \, \delta$$

where $k$ is the cantilever spring constant (typically **0.01 – 50 N/m** for AFM, **0.5 – 5 N/m** for typical nanomanipulators) and $\delta$ is the deflection.

**B. Field-Force (Non-Contact) Regime**
An external field (light, magnetic, electric, acoustic) applies a body force on the object proportional to its polarizability, susceptibility, or charge.

**C. Fluidic / Capillary Regime**
Viscous drag of a moving fluid or capillary pressure gradients move the object indirectly via a carrier medium.

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Manipulative Techniques

#### Pillar 1 — Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) Based Manipulation

SPM-based techniques use a sharp tip (radius of curvature **~ 2 – 30 nm**) to image, push, pull, indent, and modify surfaces. The two dominant variants are:

**(i) Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) Manipulation**
- **Imaging mode:** Tip scans the surface in tapping or contact mode to produce a topographical map with sub-nanometer vertical resolution.
- **Manipulation mode:** Tip is brought into contact with a nanoparticle and laterally translated, applying a horizontal force that exceeds the static friction holding the particle in place. The lateral force is given by:

$$F_{\text{lateral}} \;=\; \mu \, (F_{N} + F_{\text{adh}} + F_{\text{cap}})$$

where $\mu$ is the friction coefficient, $F_{N}$ is the applied normal load, $F_{\text{adh}}$ is the adhesion force (typically **1 – 100 nN** for a 10 nm tip on a clean surface), and $F_{\text{cap}}$ is the capillary force from any adsorbed water meniscus.

**(ii) Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) Manipulation**
- Works on conducting surfaces only. A bias voltage (typically **0.1 – 3 V**) between tip and sample produces a tunneling current of **0.1 – 10 nA** at a gap of **0.3 – 1 nm**.
- In manipulation mode, the tip is positioned above an adatom and the bias is pulsed to induce atom-by-atom transfer. This is the technique used famously by **IBM researchers in 1989** to spell "IBM" with 35 individual xenon atoms on a nickel surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SPM matters in nanomanufacturing:** SPM is the only technique that can both *image* and *manipulate* the same region with **atomic resolution**, making it indispensable for prototyping NEMS devices, measuring nanoscale mechanical properties, and performing single-molecule experiments.

#### Pillar 2 — Optical Manipulation (Optical Tweezers)

**Principle:** A tightly focused laser beam (typically **Nd:YAG at 1064 nm**, **Ti:Sapphire at 800–900 nm**, or other IR wavelengths chosen to avoid biological damage) creates a three-dimensional gradient of electromagnetic field. A dielectric particle experiences a force toward the region of highest field intensity (the focal point).

**The optical force has two components:**

1. **Gradient force (trapping force):** Pulls a high-refractive-index particle toward the focus.
2. **Scattering force:** Pushes the particle along the beam propagation direction.

The classic Ashkin formulation gives the trapping efficiency $Q$ as:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{opt}} \;=\; \frac{n_{m}\,P}{c}\,\big(Q_{\text{grad}} \, \hat{r} \;+\; Q_{\text{scatt}} \, \hat{z}\big)$$

where $n_{m}$ is the refractive index of the medium, $P$ is the laser power, $c$ is the speed of light, and $Q_{\text{grad}}$, $Q_{\text{scatt}}$ are dimensionless efficiencies that depend on the particle size relative to wavelength (Mie regime).

For a typical optical tweezers setup with $P = 100\,\text{mW}$, $n_{m} = 1.33$ (water), the trapping force on a 1 μm polystyrene bead is of the order of **1 – 10 pN** — ideal for manipulating biological cells and organelles without damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-trap and Holographic Tweezers:** Modern setups use **Spatial Light Modulators (SLMs)** or **acousto-optic deflectors (AODs)** to generate **hundreds of independently movable traps** from a single laser, enabling parallel manipulation of multiple objects — a key capability for nano-assembly.

#### Pillar 3 — Magnetic Manipulation

A magnetic particle in a non-uniform magnetic field experiences a force:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{mag}} \;=\; V \, (\vec{M} \cdot \nabla)\vec{B} \;=\; \frac{V \, \chi}{\mu_{0}} \, (\vec{B} \cdot \nabla)\vec{B}$$

where $V$ is the particle volume, $\vec{M}$ is its magnetization, $\chi$ is the magnetic susceptibility, and $\mu_{0}$ is the permeability of free space.

**Two modes of operation:**
- **Permanent magnetic beads** (e.g., Dynabeads, 1–5 μm diameter) functionalized with biomolecules are moved with external permanent magnets or electromagnets.
- **Paramagnetic/ferromagnetic nanoparticles** (10–100 nm) in a high-gradient field produced by microfabricated magnetic tips.

**Advantages over optical tweezers:** No photodamage, deeper penetration, works in opaque media.

**Disadvantages:** Requires magnetic material, lower spatial resolution (~100 nm vs ~10 nm for optical).

#### Pillar 4 — Electrical Field Manipulation (Electrophoresis & Dielectrophoresis)

**Electrophoresis** moves charged particles in a uniform DC field:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{EP}} \;=\; q \, \vec{E}$$

**Dielectrophoresis (DEP)** is far more powerful because it works on **both charged and neutral particles** in a non-uniform AC field. The time-averaged DEP force is:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{DEP}} \;=\; 2\pi r^{3} \varepsilon_{m} \, \text{Re}\!\big[K(\omega)\big] \, \nabla \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2}$$

where $r$ is the particle radius, $\varepsilon_{m}$ is the permittivity of the medium, $\omega$ is the angular frequency, and $K(\omega)$ is the **Clausius-Mossotti factor**:

$$K(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

with $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon - j\sigma/\omega$ being the complex permittivity.

- **Positive DEP (pDEP):** Particle attracted to high field region (e.g., electrode edges).
- **Negative DEP (nDEP):** Particle repelled to low field region (e.g., field null in the center of an electrode array).

By switching the **frequency** of the applied AC field, the sign of $\text{Re}[K(\omega)]$ can be flipped, enabling selective sorting of particles by size, conductivity, and membrane capacitance — a powerful tool in **lab-on-a-chip** and **circulating tumor cell (CTC) isolation**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table for KTU University Exam — Module 4 carries 15–20% of the total marks.**

| # | Technique | Governing Force Equation | Typical Force Magnitude | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AFM Push / Pull | $F = \mu(F_N + F_{\text{adh}} + F_{\text{cap}})$ | **1 nN – 10 μN** | 1 nm |
| 2 | STM Atom Transfer | $I_{t} \propto V_{b}\,e^{-\kappa d}$ | Tunneling current **0.1–10 nA** | 0.1 nm |
| 3 | Optical Tweezers | $\vec{F} = \dfrac{n_m P}{c}(Q_{\text{grad}}\hat{r} + Q_{\text{scatt}}\hat{z})$ | **0.1 – 100 pN** | 10 nm |
| 4 | Magnetic | $\vec{F} = \dfrac{V\chi}{\mu_0}(\vec{B}\cdot\nabla)\vec{B}$ | **0.01 – 10 nN** | 100 nm |
| 5 | Electrophoresis | $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$ | **fN – pN** | 1 μm |
| 6 | Dielectrophoresis | $\vec{F} = 2\pi r^{3}\varepsilon_m\,\text{Re}[K(\omega)]\,\nabla\vert\vec{E}\vert^{2}$ | **fN – nN** | 50 nm |
| 7 | Capillary Picking | $F_{c} = 4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | **μN range** | 100 nm |
| 8 | Acoustic | $\vec{F} = -V\nabla p_{\text{ac}}$ | **pN – nN** | 1 μm |
| 9 | Self-Assembly | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ minimization | Molecular | 0.1 nm |
| 10 | Surface-to-Volume Ratio | $S/V \propto 1/L$ | Dimensionless | — |

**Boundary conditions and dimensionless numbers you must know:**

$$Bo \;=\; \frac{\rho g L^{2}}{\gamma} \quad\quad Ca \;=\; \frac{\mu v}{\gamma} \quad\quad Re \;=\; \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

- **Bo < 1:** Surface tension dominates → use capillary/electrostatic techniques.
- **Ca < 1:** Viscous forces dominate over capillary → use fluidic drag.
- **Re < 1:** Stokes (creeping) flow regime → particles follow streamlines.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Technique Used |
|---|---|---|
| **NEMS/MEMS assembly** | Picking carbon nanotubes (CNT) onto electrodes | AFM manipulation |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Single-molecule force spectroscopy on protein unfolding | Optical tweezers |
| **Biomedical** | Targeted drug delivery to tumor | Magnetic nanoparticle guidance |
| **Cell biology** | Sorting circulating tumor cells from blood | Dielectrophoresis |
| **Semiconductor** | Atomic-scale lithography & repair | STM manipulation |
| **Materials** | Assembling colloidal crystals | Dielectrophoresis + self-assembly |
| **Lab-on-chip** | Cell trapping and lysis | Optical + DEP combined |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Force on a Dielectric Particle (Rayleigh Regime)

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** The Rayleigh regime applies when the particle radius $r$ is much smaller than the wavelength of light ($r \ll \lambda$, typically $r < 100\,\text{nm}$ for visible/IR light). The particle is treated as an **induced dipole** in an oscillating electric field.

**Step 1 — Set up the induced dipole.**
A dielectric particle of polarizability $\alpha$ placed in an oscillating electric field $\vec{E}$ develops a time-varying dipole moment:

$$\vec{p}(t) \;=\; \alpha \, \vec{E}(t) \quad\quad \text{with} \quad \alpha \;=\; 4\pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3} \,\frac{n_{p}^{2} - n_{m}^{2}}{n_{p}^{2} + 2 n_{m}^{2}}$$

The second factor is the **Clausius-Mossotti relation** for spheres, linking polarizability to refractive indices.

**Step 2 — Compute the time-averaged force on the dipole.**
The instantaneous force on a dipole in a non-uniform field is:

$$\vec{F}(t) \;=\; (\vec{p}(t) \cdot \nabla)\vec{E}(t)$$

Averaging over one optical cycle (denoted by angle brackets $\langle \cdot \rangle$):

$$\langle \vec{F} \rangle \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \text{Re}\!\left[\,(\vec{p}^{*} \cdot \nabla)\vec{E}\,\right] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha \nabla \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Insert the polarizability.**
Substituting $\alpha$ from Step 1:

$$\langle \vec{F}_{\text{grad}} \rangle \;=\; 2\pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3} \,\frac{n_{p}^{2} - n_{m}^{2}}{n_{p}^{2} + 2 n_{m}^{2}} \,\nabla \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2}$$

This is the **gradient force** responsible for trapping.

**Step 4 — Note the sign of the trap.**
- If $n_{p} > n_{m}$: the polarizability factor is positive, force is directed toward **higher** field intensity (the focus) → **stable trap**.
- If $n_{p} < n_{m}$ (e.g., air bubble in water): factor is negative, force is directed toward **lower** intensity → particle is pushed out of the focus.

**Step 5 — Trap stiffness.**
The gradient force is essentially a linear restoring force near the equilibrium point. Defining trap stiffness $\kappa = \partial F / \partial x$, a typical optical tweezers trap has $\kappa \approx 10^{-6}\,\text{N/m}$ to $10^{-4}\,\text{N/m}$ per Watt of laser power. This stiffness is what allows the optical tweezers to be used as a **piconewton force transducer** — the standard tool for single-molecule biophysics.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Examiners often ask: *"Calculate the gradient force on a 50 nm polystyrene bead ($n_p = 1.59$) in water ($n_m = 1.33$) when the field gradient is $10^{13}\,\text{V}^2/\text{m}^3$."* Simply plug into the equation above with $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$ and $r = 50 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Dielectrophoretic Force in the Dipole Approximation

**Step 1 — Polarizability of a sphere in a complex medium.**
A particle of complex permittivity $\varepsilon_p^{*}$ in a medium of complex permittivity $\varepsilon_m^{*}$ responds to a field $\vec{E}$ as an effective dipole:

$$\vec{p}_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 4\pi \varepsilon_{m}^{*} r^{3} \, K(\omega) \, \vec{E}$$

where the **Clausius-Mossotti factor** is:

$$K(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}} \quad\quad \varepsilon^{*} \;=\; \varepsilon \;-\; j\,\frac{\sigma}{\omega}$$

**Step 2 — Force on a dipole in a non-uniform field.**
Using the time-averaged gradient force (same as Step 2 of the optical derivation, but now with complex fields):

$$\langle \vec{F}_{\text{DEP}} \rangle \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \text{Re}\!\left[\,(\vec{p}_{\text{eff}}^{*} \cdot \nabla)\vec{E}\,\right]$$

**Step 3 — Final DEP equation.**
After algebra (using vector identity and noting that for a sinusoidal field $\vert \vec{E} \vert^{2} = \vec{E}\cdot\vec{E}^{*}$):

$$\boxed{\;\vec{F}_{\text{DEP}} \;=\; 2\pi r^{3}\,\varepsilon_{m}\,\text{Re}\!\left[K(\omega)\right] \,\nabla \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Sign analysis.**
- $\text{Re}[K(\omega)] > 0$ → **positive DEP** (pDEP): particle moves toward high field (electrode edges, microelectrode tips). Used for **trapping and concentrating**.
- $\text{Re}[K(\omega)] < 0$ → **negative DEP** (nDEP): particle moves toward low field (electrode center). Used for **repulsion/levitation**.

The cross-over frequency where $\text{Re}[K(\omega)] = 0$ is the **characteristic frequency** $\omega_{c}$, which is unique to each particle type, allowing **frequency-dependent sorting**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| If the problem is... | Use... | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single atom/molecule positioning on a crystal | **STM manipulation** | Only technique with sub-nm resolution and atom-by-atom control |
| Push a 10–500 nm particle on a substrate | **AFM manipulation** | Direct mechanical push with nm accuracy and real-time imaging |
| Trap a living cell without damage | **Optical tweezers (IR)** | Non-contact, calibrated pN forces, biocompatible |
| Move a magnetic bead through tissue | **Magnetic tweezers** | Penetrates opaque media, no photodamage |
| Sort cells by type in a microfluidic channel | **DEP** | Works on neutral particles, frequency-selective |
| Assemble millions of nanoparticles in parallel | **Self-assembly / template** | Thermodynamically driven, scalable, cheap |
| Manipulate inside a sealed liquid cell | **Magnetic or acoustic** | No line-of-sight required |
| Measure piconewton forces on a single biomolecule | **Optical / magnetic tweezers** | Force calibration is well-established |
| Pick-and-place nanowires into MEMS | **AFM or capillary micromanipulator** | Provides normal + lateral force control |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A 100 nm diameter polystyrene bead ($n_p = 1.59$) is trapped in an optical tweezers using a **Nd:YAG laser** ($\lambda = 1064\,\text{nm}$) in water ($n_m = 1.33$). The peak intensity gradient at the focus is $\nabla \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2} = 5 \times 10^{12}\,\text{V}^2/\text{m}^3$. Calculate the gradient force.

**Step 1 — Compute polarizability factor.**

$$\frac{n_{p}^{2} - n_{m}^{2}}{n_{p}^{2} + 2 n_{m}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1.59^{2} - 1.33^{2}}{1.59^{2} + 2(1.33^{2})} \;=\; \frac{2.5281 - 1.7689}{2.5281 + 3.5378} \;=\; \frac{0.7592}{6.0659} \;\approx\; 0.1252$$

**Step 2 — Compute the polarizability.**

$$\alpha \;=\; 4\pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \,(50 \times 10^{-9})^{3} \,(0.1252)$$

$$\alpha \;=\; 4\pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \,(1.25 \times 10^{-22}) \,(0.1252)$$

$$\alpha \;\approx\; 1.74 \times 10^{-33}\,\text{C}\cdot\text{m}^{2}/\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the force.**

$$\langle F_{\text{grad}} \rangle \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha\,\nabla \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,(1.74 \times 10^{-33})\,(5 \times 10^{12})$$

$$\boxed{\;\langle F_{\text{grad}} \rangle \;\approx\; 4.35 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 4.35\,\text{fN}\;}$$

This is well within the trapping range for a 100 nm particle. Typical **Brownian force** competing against this is $F_{\text{thermal}} = k_B T / \delta \approx 4 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{N}$ at room temperature, confirming the trap is at the edge of stability — exactly the regime where optical tweezers are useful for studying stochastic processes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
