# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD) and Nano-Crystalline Diamond (NCD) in terms of grain size, synthesis method, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PCD | NCD |
|---|---|---|
| Grain size | 1 µm – 100 µm | 5 nm – 100 nm |
| Synthesis | HPHT sintering, or thick CVD | Low-pressure CVD with high CH₄/H₂ ratio |
| sp²/sp³ content | Predominantly sp³, with grain-boundary sp² | Significant grain-boundary sp² (up to 5–10%) |
| Surface roughness | High (µm scale) | Very low (nm scale) — near atomically smooth |
| Hardness | 50–80 GPa | Up to 120 GPa (Hall-Petch effect) |
| Typical applications | Cutting tool inserts, wire drawing dies, rock drilling | MEMS/NEMS coatings, biomedical, tribological films |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Explain why diamond, despite being electrically insulating, has one of the highest known thermal conductivities.**

**Model Answer:**
Diamond is an electrical insulator because all four valence electrons of each carbon atom are locked in **sp³ covalent bonds**, leaving no free charge carriers for electronic conduction. However, heat in insulators is carried by **lattice vibrations (phonons)**. In diamond:

1. The C–C bond is the **shortest and stiffest** of any element-element bond → very high phonon group velocity ($v_s \approx 17,000$ m/s).
2. Carbon is a **light atom** → high Debye temperature ($\theta_D = 1860$ K) → phonon population is sparse, reducing phonon-phonon (Umklapp) scattering.
3. The crystal is **highly symmetric and isotopically pure (in synthetic diamond)** → phonon mean free path can reach **hundreds of microns**.
4. Therefore $k = \tfrac{1}{3} C_v v_s \Lambda_{ph}$ becomes very large (1000–2300 W/m·K), exceeding copper's 400 W/m·K.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Module 4, Choice Option 1]

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **crystal structure of diamond**. Explain the sp³ hybridisation and discuss its consequences on mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) process** for synthesis of diamond films. Compare the Hot Filament CVD (HFCVD) and Microwave Plasma CVD (MPCVD) methods. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Crystal structure** (2 marks): Diamond cubic is an FCC lattice with a 2-atom basis. Lattice constant $a = 3.567$ Å. There are 8 atoms per conventional cell. Each atom has 4 nearest neighbours at $r = 1.54$ Å arranged tetrahedrally (bond angle = $109.47°$). The structure can be visualised as two inter-penetrating FCC sublattices offset by $(1/4, 1/4, 1/4)$.

2. **sp³ Hybridisation** (2 marks): The 2s and three 2p orbitals of carbon hybridise to form 4 equivalent sp³ orbitals pointing tetrahedrally. Each sp³ orbital overlaps with a sp³ orbital of a neighbouring C atom, forming a strong σ-bond with bond energy ~347 kJ/mol. No π-bonds or delocalised electrons exist.

3. **Consequences** (3 marks):
   - **Mechanical:** No slip planes, no mobile dislocations, very high hardness (HV ≈ 70–120 GPa) and Young's modulus (~1100 GPa). Brittle (low $K_{IC}$).
   - **Electrical:** All valence electrons are localised in σ-bonds → insulator (E_g = 5.45 eV). Boron doping gives p-type semiconductor.
   - **Thermal:** Phonon-dominated conduction, very high $k$ (1000–2300 W/m·K) due to stiff lattice, light atom, and symmetric structure.

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **CVD Process Overview** (2 marks): A hydrocarbon gas (typically CH₄) is diluted in excess H₂ (typical ratio <5%) and activated in a reactor to generate carbon radicals and atomic hydrogen. Atomic H preferentially etches sp² (graphitic) carbon, leaving sp³ diamond to grow on a heated substrate (700–1000°C).

2. **HFCVD** (2.5 marks): A tungsten or tantalum filament is heated to ~2000–2500°C. The hot filament dissociates H₂ and CH₄ into radicals. Advantages: simple, scalable, low cost. Disadvantages: filament contamination, limited film uniformity, lower quality than MPCVD.

3. **MPCVD** (2.5 marks): A microwave (2.45 GHz) discharge generates a high-density plasma (~10¹⁰–10¹¹ cm⁻³) without filaments. Advantages: contamination-free, high growth rate (up to 10 µm/h for high-quality SCD), excellent uniformity, scalable to 300 mm wafers. Disadvantages: expensive equipment, requires impedance matching.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Module 4, Choice Option 2]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of synthetic diamond** based on grain size. Discuss the Hall–Petch type relationship and its effect on hardness of NCD films. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the major **applications of diamond in micro and nano manufacturing**, with specific examples in MEMS, NEMS, cutting tools, and biomedical devices. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Classification** (3 marks):
   - **SCD:** Single grain, no grain boundaries. Used in ultra-precision tooling.
   - **PCD:** Grains 1–100 µm. Sintered by HPHT or thick CVD.
   - **NCD/MCD/UNCD:** Grains 5–100 nm (NCD), 100 nm–1 µm (MCD), <10 nm (UNCD).
   - **DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon):** Amorphous mixture of sp³ and sp²; not true crystalline diamond.

2. **Hall-Petch Relation** (2 marks):
   $$H(d) = H_0 + \frac{k_{HP}}{\sqrt{d}}$$
   where $d$ = grain size, $H_0$ = lattice friction, $k_{HP}$ = Hall-Petch coefficient. As $d$ decreases from µm to nm scale, hardness increases due to grain-boundary-induced dislocation pile-up suppression. In NCD, hardness can reach **120 GPa**.

3. **Deviation at ultra-fine grains** (2 marks): Below ~10 nm, inverse Hall-Petch sets in due to grain-boundary sliding. The sp² content in grain boundaries also rises, reducing effective hardness. Optimal NCD grain size is **5–50 nm** for peak mechanical performance.

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **Cutting tools** (2 marks): PCD tools for machining Al-Si alloys, Cu, composites, wood, and CFRP. SCD tools for ultra-precision diamond turning of Cu and Al mirrors (surface roughness <10 nm). Cannot be used for ferrous metals due to chemical reaction.

2. **MEMS/NEMS** (2 marks): NCD films as structural and protective layers in RF MEMS switches, NEMS mass sensors, AFM/STM tips, and high-Q mechanical resonators (Q > 10⁶ at room temperature).

3. **Biomedical** (1.5 marks): NCD coatings on Ti implants (orthopaedic, dental) for biocompatibility and wear resistance. Boron-doped diamond electrodes for biosensing. Diamond nanoneedles for intracellular delivery.

4. **Thermal management** (1.5 marks): CVD diamond heat spreaders in GaN HEMTs, laser diode arrays, and CPU hot-spot cooling. Diamond-copper composites for 5G base station power amplifiers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write "diamond is the hardest material therefore it cannot be broken" — diamond is brittle. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon)** with **NCD**. DLC is amorphous with mixed sp³/sp²; NCD is crystalline. This is a common KTU trick question.
> 3. **Do not** claim diamond is a conductor — it is an **insulator** (E_g = 5.45 eV). Heat conduction is by **phonons, not electrons**.
> 4. **Always** state the **lattice constant (3.567 Å)** and **bond length (1.54 Å)** explicitly in crystal-structure questions — vague answers lose marks.
> 5. **Never** write "diamond can machine steel" — diamond reacts with Fe, Co, Ni above ~700°C, causing catastrophic tool wear.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Diamond (Module 4, PEMET864):**

- **Crystal structure:** Diamond cubic, Fd-3m, 8 atoms/cell, $a = 3.567$ Å, bond length = 1.54 Å, bond angle = 109.47°.
- **Hybridisation:** Pure sp³, 4 σ-bonds per C atom, no π-bonds, no free electrons.
- **Hardness:** Highest of any material, 70–120 GPa (Vickers); Mohs 10. Brittle — $K_{IC} \approx 3.4$ MPa·m^½.
- **Young's modulus:** ~1100 GPa, Poisson's ratio ≈ 0.1.
- **Density:** 3.52 g/cm³. APF = 0.34 (lowest among cubic structures).
- **Thermal conductivity:** 900–2300 W/m·K (phonon dominated), CTE ≈ 1 ppm/K.
- **Electrical:** Insulator, E_g = 5.45 eV. Boron-doped = p-type semiconductor.
- **Optical:** Transparent from 225 nm to far IR, n = 2.42, Raman peak 1332 cm⁻¹.
- **NEA surface:** H-terminated (111) diamond emits electrons easily → cold cathodes.
- **Chemical:** Inert to acids/bases at RT. Oxidises above 700°C in air. Reacts with Fe, Co, Ni, W at high T.
- **Synthetic forms:** SCD, PCD, NCD (and DLC = amorphous, NOT crystalline).
- **Synthesis:** HPHT (high pressure, high T) and CVD (HFCVD, MPCVD); CVD dominates for thin films and coatings.
- **Hall-Petch in NCD:** Hardness increases as grain size drops from µm → nm, peaks at ~10 nm; inverse Hall-Petch below.
- **Key applications:** Cutting tools (PCD), ultra-precision turning (SCD), MEMS/NEMS coatings (NCD), heat spreaders (CVD SCD), biomedical implants (NCD), UV detectors (E_g), NEA cold cathodes.
- **Cannot be used on:** Ferrous metals (Fe, steel) at high temperature due to chemical affinity.
- **Verification quality by:** Raman spectroscopy (1332 cm⁻¹ peak), SEM (grain size), XRD, XPS (sp²/sp³ ratio).
- **Numerical must-knows:** $a = 4r/\sqrt{3}$, $\rho = 8M/(N_A a^3) = 3.52$ g/cm³, $APF = 0.34$, $\Lambda_{ph}$ up to 300 µm in isotopically pure ¹²C diamond.

**End of Module 4 Notes — Diamond: Properties and Applications.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CVD Diamond Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** CVD Diamond Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) Diamond** is a low-pressure synthetic process in which carbon-containing precursor gases (typically methane, CH₄) are activated in the presence of hydrogen (H₂) to deposit polycrystalline or single-crystalline diamond films onto a heated substrate. The process relies on the selective etching of non-diamond (graphitic) carbon by atomic hydrogen, allowing the metastable diamond phase to nucleate and grow at substrate temperatures typically between **700 °C and 1000 °C** and chamber pressures between **30 Torr and 200 Torr**.

CVD belongs to the family of **synthetic diamond fabrication methods** under the broader umbrella of *Micro and Nano Manufacturing*. It is the only commercially scalable route for producing large-area, high-purity, free-standing diamond wafers, optical-grade diamond windows, and ultra-thin diamond membranes used in MEMS, NEMS, and quantum devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEMET864 Module 4):** Students must understand the gas-phase chemistry, reactor configurations, nucleation mechanism, growth kinetics, and application matrix of CVD diamond. Numerical problems on growth rate and energy consumption are frequently asked.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Frost on a Smart Window"

Imagine a cold winter window where water vapor from humid air deposits as **frost crystals** in a regular pattern. Now imagine you could *choose* exactly which crystal structure forms (ice vs. snow vs. water) by carefully controlling the temperature and adding chemical "helpers" that remove the unwanted forms. CVD diamond works exactly like this:

| Analogy Element | CVD Diamond Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cold window pane | Heated substrate (700–1000 °C) |
| Humid air | Mixture of CH₄ + H₂ |
| Frost formation | Diamond nucleation |
| "Helpers" (anti-frost spray) | Atomic hydrogen (etches graphite) |
| Designer crystal choice | sp³-bonded carbon (diamond) |

> [!TIP]
> The key insight is that **diamond is thermodynamically metastable** at low pressure. Graphite *should* form — but atomic hydrogen continuously eats the graphite away, leaving the diamond phase to survive. This is why H₂ is the dominant gas (>95–99 %) in the feed mixture.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following values are **must-remember constants** for KTU 2024 valuation:

- **Density of CVD diamond:** ρ = **3.52 g·cm⁻³**
- **Young's Modulus:** E = **1050 GPa** (highest of any bulk material)
- **Thermal conductivity (single crystal):** κ = **2200 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹** (5× copper)
- **C–C sp³ bond energy:** ≈ **347 kJ·mol⁻¹**
- **Activation energy for diamond growth:** E_a ≈ **40–60 kJ·mol⁻¹** (radical-controlled)
- **Standard gas ratio:** H₂ : CH₄ = **99 : 1** (by volume) for high-quality films
- **Typical growth rate:** G = **0.1 – 10 µm·h⁻¹** (up to 100 µm·h⁻¹ in high-power MPCVD)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CVD Diamond Process — Logic Flow

The deposition of diamond from the vapor phase is governed by a sequence of seven coupled phenomena:

1. **Gas Delivery & Mixing** — High-purity H₂ carrier gas (>99.999 %) is metered through mass-flow controllers (MFCs) and mixed with a carbon source (CH₄, C₂H₂, or alcohols).
2. **Activation of Precursor** — Energy is supplied to dissociate H₂ into atomic hydrogen (H•) and CH₄ into methyl radicals (CH₃•). Activation methods:
   - *Thermal (HFCVD)* — Hot tungsten filament at **2000–2400 °C**
   - *Plasma (MPCVD)* — Microwave plasma at **2.45 GHz**
   - *Arc-jet / DC plasma* — For high-rate industrial deposition
3. **Gas-Phase Chemistry** — A complex set of radical reactions occurs in the boundary layer. Key reaction:
   $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2$$
4. **Surface Diffusion & Adsorption** — CH₃• radicals diffuse to the substrate and adsorb on growing (111) or (100) crystal faces.
5. **Nucleation on Substrate** — Initial diamond clusters form via surface defects, scratching, or seeding with nanodiamond particles.
6. **Crystal Growth** — sp³-bonded carbon is incorporated into the lattice; atomic H etches away any sp² (graphitic) carbon.
7. **Byproduct Removal** — Unreacted gases and H₂ are exhausted; the chamber operates as a continuous flow reactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Diamond Etching Paradox":** Without atomic hydrogen, only graphite would form. The unique role of H• is to **preferentially etch sp² carbon ~20× faster than sp³ carbon**, allowing the metastable diamond to survive.

## 2.2 Reactor Configurations

| Reactor Type | Activation Source | Pressure | Growth Rate | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HFCVD** (Hot Filament) | W filament at 2200 °C | 30–200 Torr | 0.1–1 µm·h⁻¹ | Large-area coatings, cutting tools |
| **MPCVD** (Microwave Plasma) | 2.45 GHz plasma | 30–200 Torr | 1–10 µm·h⁻¹ | Optical, electronic grade |
| **DC Arc Jet** | High-current arc | 200–760 Torr | 10–100 µm·h⁻¹ | Thick free-standing wafers |
| **Combustion Flame** | Acetylene–oxygen torch | Atmospheric | 30–100 µm·h⁻¹ | Specialty research |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-critical**. Memorize the form, the variables, and the units. KTU valuators award full marks only when units accompany the final numerical answer.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot F_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot M_{\text{C}}}{N_A \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ | Mass-based growth rate | $\alpha$ = dissociation efficiency, $F_{\text{CH}_4}$ = molar flow (mol·s⁻¹), $M_{\text{C}}$ = 12.01 g·mol⁻¹, $A$ = substrate area (cm²), $G$ in cm·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius kinetics for surface reaction | $k$ = rate constant, $E_a$ = activation energy (J·mol⁻¹), $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\dot{n}_H = P \cdot Q / (k_B T)$ | Atomic hydrogen flux from plasma | $P$ = dissociation fraction, $Q$ = volumetric flow |
| 4 | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C(diamond)} + 2\text{H}_2$ | Overall stoichiometry (simplified) | Net carbon yield = 1 C atom per CH₄ |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{sub}} - T_{\text{amb}}}{T_{\text{filament}} - T_{\text{amb}}}$ | Thermal efficiency (HFCVD) | $T$ in K; ideal η ≤ 0.5 |
| 6 | $v_{\text{etch}}^{\text{sp}^2} \approx 20 \times v_{\text{etch}}^{\text{sp}^3}$ | Selective H-etching ratio | Empirical constant for 800 °C substrate |
| 7 | $\Delta G_f^{\circ} = \Delta H_f^{\circ} - T\Delta S_f^{\circ}$ | Gibbs free energy (diamond vs. graphite) | Diamond is metastable at low P, high T |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In formula (1), many students write the area in m² while the density is in g·cm⁻³, creating a unit mismatch worth **2 marks lost** in KTU valuation. Always convert area to cm² or convert density to kg·m⁻³ — never mix.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

CVD diamond is not a laboratory curiosity — it is a **workhorse of modern industry**:

- **Heat spreaders** for GaN-on-diamond RF amplifiers (5G base stations, radar)
- **MEMS/NEMS resonators** with Q-factors >1 million at room temperature
- **Quantum-grade NV centers** for magnetometry and quantum computing
- **Electrochemical electrodes** for water treatment (B-doped diamond)
- **Cutting tool coatings** lasting 10× longer than tungsten carbide
- **Radiation detectors** for high-energy physics (CERN ATLAS upgrades)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Growth Rate from Mass Balance

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):** A HFCVD reactor uses a methane flow of 5 sccm diluted in 495 sccm of H₂ over a 4-inch (10.16 cm diameter) silicon substrate. If the dissociation efficiency of CH₄ is 2 %, calculate the theoretical maximum growth rate of diamond. (Given: ρ_diamond = 3.52 g·cm⁻³, $M_{\text{C}}$ = 12 g·mol⁻¹, 1 sccm = 1/22400 mol·min⁻¹ at STP.)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert methane flow to molar rate.**
At STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm), 1 mole of ideal gas occupies 22400 cm³. The molar flow of CH₄ is therefore:

$$F_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{5 \;\text{sccm}}{22400 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}} = 2.232 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mol}\cdot\text{min}^{-1}$$

Converting to mol·s⁻¹:

$$F_{\text{CH}_4} = 2.232 \times 10^{-4} / 60 = 3.720 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{mol}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply dissociation efficiency.**
Only 2 % of the methane is actually cracked into diamond-forming radicals:

$$F_{\text{eff}} = \alpha \cdot F_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.02 \times 3.720 \times 10^{-6} = 7.440 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{mol}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles of carbon to mass.**
Each mole of CH₄ provides one mole (12.01 g) of carbon:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{C}} = F_{\text{eff}} \times M_{\text{C}} = 7.440 \times 10^{-8} \times 12.01 = 8.934 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{g}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute substrate area.**
A 4-inch wafer has diameter d = 10.16 cm, so:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (10.16)^2}{4} = 81.07 \;\text{cm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute growth rate (linear).**
Growth rate in cm·s⁻¹ is mass flux divided by density:

$$G = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{C}}}{\rho \cdot A} = \dfrac{8.934 \times 10^{-7}}{3.52 \times 81.07} = 3.13 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{cm}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to µm·h⁻¹ (the conventional unit).**

$$G = 3.13 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{cm}\cdot\text{s}^{-1} \times \dfrac{10^4 \;\mu\text{m}}{1 \;\text{cm}} \times \dfrac{3600 \;\text{s}}{1 \;\text{h}}$$

$$G \approx 0.113 \;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This rate is low because HFCVD is efficiency-limited. An equivalent MPCVD reactor at 3 kW microwave power would achieve **5–10 µm·h⁻¹** for the same methane flow — illustrating the design tradeoff between **uniformity** (HFCVD wins) and **growth rate** (MPCVD wins).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activation Energy from Arrhenius Plot

> **Problem Statement:** The growth rates of CVD diamond at three substrate temperatures are: T₁ = 973 K → G₁ = 0.5 µm·h⁻¹; T₂ = 1073 K → G₂ = 1.2 µm·h⁻¹; T₃ = 1173 K → G₃ = 2.6 µm·h⁻¹. Estimate the activation energy E_a.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write Arrhenius form for growth rate.**
Assuming rate-controlled kinetics, the growth rate follows:

$$G = G_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Taking natural logarithms:

$$\ln G = \ln G_0 - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\ln G$ and $1/T$ for each data point.**

| T (K) | 1/T (K⁻¹) | G (µm·h⁻¹) | $\ln G$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 973 | $1.0279 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.5 | $-0.6931$ |
| 1073 | $9.320 \times 10^{-4}$ | 1.2 | $+0.1823$ |
| 1173 | $8.525 \times 10^{-4}$ | 2.6 | $+0.9555$ |

**Step 3 — Perform linear regression (two-point slope between extremes).**

$$\text{Slope} = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} = \dfrac{\ln G_3 - \ln G_1}{1/T_3 - 1/T_1}$$

$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{0.9555 - (-0.6931)}{(8.525 - 10.279) \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1.6486}{-1.754 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\text{Slope} = -9.399 \times 10^{3} \;\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for E_a.**

$$E_a = -\text{Slope} \times R = 9.399 \times 10^{3} \times 8.314 = 78{,}130 \;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 78.1 \;\text{kJ}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This value lies in the typical range of **40–80 kJ·mol⁻¹** for surface-reaction-controlled diamond growth, confirming that mass transport is not the rate-limiting step at these temperatures. (For purely diffusion-limited growth, E_a would be < 10 kJ·mol⁻¹.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Substrate–Process Mapping

| Substrate | Recommended Pretreatment | Preferred CVD Method | Typical Film Thickness | Key Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Si (100) | Ultrasonic seeding with 5 nm nanodiamond | MPCVD | 0.1–500 µm | Thermal expansion mismatch (Δα = 2×) |
| Tungsten carbide (WC-Co) | Acid-etch Co removal (Murakami) | HFCVD | 5–30 µm | Cobalt catalyzes graphite |
| Molybdenum (Mo) | Scratching with diamond paste | HFCVD / MPCVD | 100–1000 µm | Carbide layer formation |
| Copper (Cu) | No pretreatment needed | MPCVD | < 5 µm | High C solubility, no carbide |
| Titanium (Ti) | H₂ plasma pre-clean | HFCVD | 1–10 µm | TiC formation at interface |
| Quartz / SiO₂ | Bias-enhanced nucleation (BEN) | MPCVD | 0.05–2 µm | Long incubation time |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — CVD Diamond Reactor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H2Cyl["H2 Gas Cylinder 99.999 percent"] --> MFC1["Mass Flow Controller 1 H2"]
    CH4Cyl["CH4 Gas Cylinder 99.99 percent"] --> MFC2["Mass Flow Controller 2 CH4"]
    MFC1 --> Mixer["Gas Mixing Chamber"]
    MFC2 --> Mixer
    Mixer --> Reactor["CVD Reactor Chamber"]
    Power["Microwave Source 2.45 GHz or Hot Filament 2200 C"] --> Reactor
    Reactor --> Substrate["Heated Substrate 700 to 1000 C"]
    Substrate --> Film["Diamond Film Growth sp3 bonded"]
    Reactor --> Pump["Vacuum Pump 30 to 200 Torr"]
    Pump --> Exhaust["Exhaust H2 and Unreacted Hydrocarbons"]
    Film --> App1["Heat Spreader for GaN RF"]
    Film --> App2["MEMS Resonator"]
    Film --> App3["Cutting Tool Coating"]
    Film --> App4["Quantum NV Center"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Gas-Phase Reaction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["CH4 Molecule"] --> B1["Cracking via Plasma or Filament"]
    B1 --> C1["CH3 Methyl Radical"]
    C1 --> D1["Surface Adsorption on Diamond 111 or 100"]
    D1 --> E1["sp3 Carbon Incorporation into Lattice"]
    E1 --> F1["Diamond Film Growth"]
    A2["H2 Molecule"] --> B2["Dissociation into Atomic H"]
    B2 --> G1["H Radical Etches sp2 Graphite"]
    B2 --> G2["H Radical Terminates sp3 Surface"]
    G1 --> H1["Volatile CH4 Released"]
    G2 --> F1
    F1 --> I1["H2 Molecule Released"]
    H1 --> J1["Pumped Out of Chamber"]
    I1 --> J1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        G1["H2 Supply"]
        G2["CH4 Supply"]
        G3["N2 or O2 Dopant optional"]
    end
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"]
        C1["MFC and Pressure Controller"]
        C2["Temperature PID Controller"]
        C3["Plasma Power Supply"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["REACTION CORE"]
        R1["Plasma or Filament Activation"]
        R2["Gas Phase Radical Chemistry"]
        R3["Substrate Surface Kinetics"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        O1["Polycrystalline Diamond Film"]
        O2["Free Standing Wafer after Lift Off"]
        O3["Quality Metrics Raman FWHM and SEM"]
    end
    IN --> CTRL
    CTRL --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT
    O3 --> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The feedback loop from O3 (quality metrics) back to CTRL illustrates the closed-loop process control used in industrial MPCVD reactors — Raman spectroscopy monitors the sp³/sp² ratio in real time and adjusts microwave power accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define CVD diamond. Why is atomic hydrogen essential for diamond growth, and what is the typical feed-gas composition?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) diamond is a low-pressure plasma- or thermally-activated process that deposits sp³-bonded crystalline diamond from a hydrocarbon–hydrogen gas mixture onto a heated substrate.
- **Role of atomic H (1 mark):** Atomic hydrogen preferentially etches sp² (graphitic) carbon ~20× faster than sp³ (diamond) carbon, suppressing graphite formation and stabilizing the metastable diamond phase. It also terminates the diamond surface, preventing reconstruction.
- **Feed composition (1 mark):** Typical volume ratio is **H₂ : CH₄ = 99 : 1** for high-quality films; 95 : 5 for higher growth rates; oxygen (0.1–2 %) is added to further suppress non-diamond carbon.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List four industrial applications of CVD diamond and state the property that enables each application.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Application | Enabling Property of CVD Diamond |
|---|---|
| Heat spreader for GaN RF devices | **Thermal conductivity 2200 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| Cutting tool coating on WC inserts | **Hardness 80–100 GPa** + low friction |
| Electrochemical wastewater electrode | **Wide electrochemical window + B-dopability** |
| X-ray / radiation detector | **Radiation hardness + low atomic number (Z = 6)** |
| Quantum magnetometer | **NV center optical stability at room temperature** |

(Any four correctly paired with their property — **1 mark per pair, max 3 marks**.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Hot Filament CVD (HFCVD)** reactor for diamond deposition. Discuss the role of filament temperature, substrate temperature, and chamber pressure on the quality and growth rate of the diamond film. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Arrhenius-based growth rate expression** for CVD diamond and explain how a plot of $\ln G$ vs $1/T$ is used to determine the activation energy. A reactor shows growth rates of 0.8, 1.6, and 3.0 µm·h⁻¹ at substrate temperatures of 1000, 1100, and 1200 K respectively. Calculate the activation energy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

1. **Diagram (2 marks):** Filament (W/Re) suspended ~5–10 mm above substrate, both inside a water-cooled stainless-steel chamber evacuated to 30–200 Torr. H₂ + CH₄ mixture flows from inlet across the filament to the substrate, with exhaust to a vacuum pump.
2. **Filament temperature (2 marks):** Operated at **2000–2400 °C**. Higher T → more H• and CH₃• radicals → faster growth, but filament carburization reduces lifetime.
3. **Substrate temperature (2 marks):** Held at **700–1000 °C**. Lower T → nanocrystalline film; higher T → microcrystalline but possible graphitic inclusions.
4. **Chamber pressure (1 mark):** Affects mean free path; lower P (30 Torr) → ballistic radical transport → better film uniformity.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the rate equation (1 mark):**

$$G = G_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \ln G = \ln G_0 - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Construct data table (1 mark):**

| T (K) | 1/T (×10⁻³ K⁻¹) | G (µm·h⁻¹) | $\ln G$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1000 | 1.0000 | 0.8 | $-0.2231$ |
| 1100 | 0.9091 | 1.6 | $+0.4700$ |
| 1200 | 0.8333 | 3.0 | $+1.0986$ |

**Step 3 — Slope calculation between extremes (2 marks):**

$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\ln G_3 - \ln G_1}{1/T_3 - 1/T_1} = \dfrac{1.0986 - (-0.2231)}{(0.8333 - 1.0000) \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1.3217}{-1.667 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Slope} = -7.929 \times 10^{3} \;\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for E_a (2 marks):**

$$E_a = -\text{Slope} \times R = 7.929 \times 10^{3} \times 8.314 = 65{,}920 \;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 65.9 \;\text{kJ}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation (1 mark):** Value in 40–80 kJ·mol⁻¹ range confirms **surface-reaction controlled** growth (not mass-transport limited, which would give E_a < 10 kJ·mol⁻¹).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**(a)** Compare **HFCVD, MPCVD, and DC Arc Jet** methods for diamond deposition in terms of growth rate, film quality, substrate area, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **nucleation and growth mechanism** of diamond on a foreign substrate. Discuss why **bias-enhanced nucleation (BEN)** is used for silicon and how the **C–H–O ternary diagram** is used to optimize deposition conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

| Parameter | HFCVD | MPCVD | DC Arc Jet |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Growth rate** | 0.1–1 µm·h⁻¹ | 1–10 µm·h⁻¹ | 10–100 µm·h⁻¹ |
| **Film quality** | Good (some W contamination) | Excellent (electronic grade) | Moderate (columnar grains) |
| **Substrate area** | Large (Ø 300 mm) | Medium (Ø 150 mm) | Small (Ø 50 mm) |
| **Filament/plasma coupling** | Conduction | Volumetric plasma | High-temp plasma jet |
| **Cost** | Low | High (MW generator) | Very high (Ar, electrodes) |
| **Application** | Cutting tools, wear parts | Optics, electronics, quantum | Free-standing wafers, heat spreaders |

(Each parameter worth ~1 mark, with one-line explanation.)

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

1. **Nucleation on foreign substrate (2 marks):** Diamond has very high surface energy (≈ 6 J·m⁻²) and cannot wet most substrates. Nucleation is therefore heterogeneous, requiring surface defects, scratches, or pre-seeded nanodiamond (~5 nm) particles to provide energetically favorable sites.
2. **Incubation period (1 mark):** Without pretreatment, nucleation density on Si is only 10⁴–10⁶ cm⁻², with a long incubation time (>30 min). Ultrasonic seeding in nanodiamond slurry raises this to **10¹⁰–10¹¹ cm⁻²** within seconds.
3. **BEN mechanism (2 marks):** In BEN, a negative DC bias of –100 to –300 V is applied to the substrate during the first minutes of growth. C₂⁺ and CHₓ⁺ ions are accelerated onto the surface, sputtering Si and forming an amorphous carbon layer that promotes diamond nucleation density to >10¹¹ cm⁻².
4. **C–H–O ternary diagram (2 marks):** Bachmann's triangular diagram maps the atomic C/H/O ratio of the feed gas onto a region where diamond is the stable solid product. The "diamond region" corresponds to **70–95 % H, 1–25 % C, 0–25 % O** by atomic fraction. Operating outside this window yields graphite or soot.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Part B derivations demand units. A numerical answer without µm·h⁻¹ or kJ·mol⁻¹ loses **1–2 marks** even if the math is correct.
> 2. **Confusing HFCVD filament temperature (2200 °C) with substrate temperature (900 °C)** — These are *not* the same; mixing them up costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the role of atomic hydrogen** — Many students describe the chemistry but skip the selective sp² etching step. This is worth 2–3 marks in conceptual questions.
> 4. **Wrong gas ratio** — The H₂:CH₄ ratio is 99:1 for high-quality film, *not* 50:50. A 50:50 mixture produces amorphous carbon.
> 5. **No Raman / SEM quality verification mentioned** — In applications questions, valuators expect at least a mention of sp³/sp² ratio verification by Raman spectroscopy (1332 cm⁻¹ peak).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Night Revision — Bullet-Point High-Density Summary:**

- **CVD Diamond = metastable low-pressure synthesis** of sp³ carbon from CH₄ + H₂ at 700–1000 °C.
- **Feed gas ratio:** H₂ : CH₄ = **99 : 1** for electronic grade; 95 : 5 for higher rate.
- **Atomic hydrogen** preferentially etches sp² carbon ~20× faster than sp³ → **selects diamond phase**.
- **Three reactor types:** HFCVD (cheap, large area, low rate), MPCVD (high quality, medium rate), DC Arc Jet (very high rate, small area).
- **Activation energy E_a ≈ 40–80 kJ·mol⁻¹** for surface-reaction-controlled growth; < 10 kJ·mol⁻¹ means mass-transport controlled.
- **Growth rate formula:** $G = \alpha F_{\text{CH}_4} M_{\text{C}} / (N_A \rho A)$ — mind your units.
- **Nucleation enhancement:** ultrasonic nanodiamond seeding (10¹⁰ cm⁻²) or bias-enhanced nucleation (BEN) at –200 V.
- **Quality metric:** Raman FWHM at 1332 cm⁻¹ (smaller = better crystallinity); SEM for morphology.
- **Bachmann C–H–O diagram:** Diamond forms only inside the "diamond region" of the ternary plot (H-dominant corner).
- **Key property table (memorize):** ρ = 3.52 g·cm⁻³; E = 1050 GPa; κ = 2200 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹; H = 80–100 GPa.
- **Top 3 applications:** heat spreaders, cutting tools, quantum NV centers.
- **Substrate compatibility:** Si (BEN needed), WC-Co (must etch Co first), Cu (no carbide, easy), Ti (forms TiC layer).
- **Drawbacks:** High equipment cost (MPCVD), slow HFCVD, film stress from thermal mismatch, limited area for arc-jet.
- **Equation to memorize cold:** $G = G_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$ — used in **every** KTU growth kinetics problem.

---

**End of Notes — CVD Diamond Technology | PEMET864 Module 4 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Micro-Nano Inspection and Metrology - Scanning Electron Microscopy - Principle and Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Micro-Nano Inspection and Metrology - Scanning Electron Microscopy - Principle and Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Micro-Nano Inspection and Metrology

**Metrology** is the science of measurement, and in the context of micro and nano manufacturing, it deals with the precise measurement of dimensions, geometries, and surface features at the micrometer (µm) and nanometer (nm) scale. **Inspection** refers to the qualitative and quantitative assessment of manufactured parts to ensure they meet design specifications, quality standards, and functional requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Micro-Nano Metrology is defined as the branch of engineering science concerned with the measurement of length, width, thickness, surface roughness, step height, and other geometric features of features that fall in the range of **0.1 nm to 1000 µm**, using optical, electron-beam, ion-beam, scanning probe, and X-ray based instruments.

### Why is Micro-Nano Inspection Critical?

In modern manufacturing — particularly for semiconductors, MEMS/NEMS, biomedical implants, and aerospace micro-components — even a deviation of a few nanometers can:
- Cause complete device failure
- Affect electrical/optical/mechanical properties
- Lead to reliability issues in service
- Violate strict industry standards (ISO 2768, ASME Y14.5, etc.)

### 1.2 Classification of Micro-Nano Inspection Techniques

| Domain | Technique | Typical Resolution | Signal Used |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Optical | Optical Microscopy | ~200 nm | Visible light |
| Optical | Confocal Microscopy | ~180 nm | Laser + Pinhole |
| Electron | **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** | **0.5 – 10 nm** | **Secondary/Backscattered Electrons** |
| Electron | Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) | 0.05 – 0.2 nm | Transmitted electrons |
| Scanning Probe | Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) | Atomic (0.01 nm) | Cantilever deflection |
| Scanning Probe | Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) | Atomic (0.01 nm) | Tunneling current |
| Ion Beam | Focused Ion Beam (FIB) | ~5 nm | Ga+ ions |
| X-ray | X-ray Diffraction (XRD) | 0.1 – 1 nm | X-ray diffraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SEM for KTU Board Exams?**
> SEM is one of the most commonly asked topics in the Micro and Nano Manufacturing module. The examiner expects students to know the principle, construction, working, and comparison with optical microscopy. Marks are frequently awarded for labeled diagrams and clear explanations of signal generation.

---

## 1.3 What is Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)?

### Formal Definition

> **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** is a non-destructive imaging technique that produces high-resolution images of a sample's surface by scanning it with a focused beam of electrons in a raster scan pattern. The interaction between the primary electrons and the atoms of the sample generates various signals (secondary electrons, backscattered electrons, X-rays, etc.) that are collected by detectors to form a magnified image with resolution down to **1 nanometer** and magnification up to **2,000,000×**.

### Conceptual Analogy: SEM as a "Touchless Fingerprint Scanner"

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to read a coin's surface details. You cannot see it, but you can run a very fine "feeler" (a probe) over it line by line. As the feeler moves, every bump and dip pushes back differently. If a computer records these pushback patterns row by row, it can reconstruct a magnified map of the surface.

In SEM, the "feeler" is a beam of **electrons** (much finer than light), the "pushback" is the **electron-sample interaction**, and the "computer" is the **detector and display system**. Because electrons have a wavelength roughly **100,000× smaller** than visible light, SEM can resolve features that are impossible to see with even the best optical microscope.

### 1.4 Why Not Use Optical Microscopy?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abbe's Diffraction Limit:** The smallest resolvable feature of any optical microscope is governed by the equation:
> $$d = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{n \sin \alpha}$$
> where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light. With green light ($\lambda \approx 550$ nm), the theoretical resolution limit is around **200 nm**. To resolve nanometer-scale features, we need a much smaller wavelength — which is exactly what accelerated electrons provide.

### 1.5 Core Capabilities of SEM

- **Resolution:** 0.5 nm to 10 nm (depending on instrument)
- **Magnification:** 10× to 2,000,000×
- **Depth of Field:** ~100× greater than optical microscopy
- **Working Distance:** Typically 5 – 30 mm
- **Sample Type:** Conductive or coated non-conductive solids
- **Environment:** High vacuum (typically 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Vocabulary:**
> - **Primary Electrons (PE):** The incident high-energy electron beam from the gun.
> - **Secondary Electrons (SE):** Low-energy (<50 eV) electrons ejected from the sample's top few nanometers — give topographical contrast.
> - **Backscattered Electrons (BSE):** High-energy electrons reflected from deeper regions — give compositional (Z-contrast) information.
> - **Characteristic X-rays:** Used for elemental analysis (Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy — EDS/EDX).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle of SEM

The working of SEM is based on three pillars:

1. **Generation of a focused, high-energy electron beam** (Electron Gun + Electromagnetic Lenses)
2. **Interaction of the electron beam with the sample** (Scattering, Absorption, Emission of signals)
3. **Detection and visualization of emitted signals** (Detectors + Display CRT/Computer)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

#### Step 1: Electron Generation
A filament (tungsten, LaB₆, or field-emission tip) is heated or subjected to a strong electric field, causing **thermionic emission** or **field emission** of electrons from its surface. The emitted electrons form a "crossover" — the smallest source of electrons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Electron Sources:**
> - **Tungsten Hairpin Filament:** Most common, low cost, requires heating to ~2700 K. Source size ≈ 30–50 µm.
> - **LaB₆ (Lanthanum Hexaboride) Filament:** Higher brightness, longer life, requires ~1800 K. Source size ≈ 10 µm.
> - **Field Emission Gun (FEG):** Cold FEG or Schottky FEG. Highest brightness, smallest source size (≈ 1–10 nm), best resolution.

#### Step 2: Electron Acceleration
The electrons are accelerated through a potential difference (typically **0.5 kV to 30 kV**, sometimes up to 200 kV for FE-SEM) toward the anode. The kinetic energy gained is:

$$E_k = eV$$

where:
- $E_k$ = kinetic energy of the electron (eV or joules)
- $e$ = electronic charge = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (V)

#### Step 3: Beam Focusing
A series of **electromagnetic lenses** (condenser lens, objective lens) demagnify the electron crossover to a tiny spot (≈ 1–10 nm) on the specimen surface. The lenses act on electrons via the Lorentz force from a magnetic field:

$$\vec{F} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

#### Step 4: Beam Scanning
**Scan coils** (deflection coils) raster the focused electron beam across the sample surface in a TV-like line-by-line pattern (e.g., 1024 × 768 pixels). The scan rate is typically 0.1 to 10 µs per pixel.

#### Step 5: Signal Generation (Electron-Specimen Interaction)
When the primary electron strikes the sample, it penetrates and undergoes complex scattering events. The **interaction volume** (pear-shaped, typically 1–5 µm deep) emits several signals simultaneously:

| Signal Type | Energy | Escape Depth | Information Provided |
|-------------|--------|--------------|----------------------|
| Secondary Electrons (SE) | <50 eV | 1 – 10 nm | Topography (surface details) |
| Backscattered Electrons (BSE) | 50 eV – 30 keV | 0.1 – 1 µm | Composition (atomic number Z) |
| Characteristic X-rays | 0.1 – 20 keV | 0.5 – 5 µm | Elemental composition (EDX) |
| Auger Electrons | 50 – 2000 eV | 0.5 – 5 nm | Surface elemental analysis |
| Cathodoluminescence | 1 – 5 eV | 0.1 – 1 µm | Optical/electronic properties |
| Absorbed Electrons (Sample Current) | Variable | Whole volume | Beam current monitoring |

#### Step 6: Signal Detection
A **Secondary Electron Detector** (Everhart-Thornley detector) collects low-energy secondary electrons, converts them to photons via a scintillator, amplifies them with a photomultiplier, and produces a voltage signal. This signal intensity modulates the brightness of a corresponding pixel on a display, synchronized with the scan.

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU-Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> "Explain the various signals generated when an electron beam strikes a sample in SEM."
> Always answer by drawing the **interaction volume** showing the escape depths of SE, BSE, and X-rays. Marks are awarded for labeling the figure correctly.

#### Step 7: Image Formation
The image is built up point-by-point as the beam rasters. A brighter pixel = more SE detected = more topographic relief. The **magnification** is the ratio of the display screen size to the scanned area on the specimen:

$$M = \frac{L_{display}}{L_{sample}}$$

For typical SEM: if the scanned area is 10 µm × 10 µm and displayed on a 100 mm × 100 mm screen, $M = 10,000×$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU student needs for numerical and conceptual questions on SEM.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $E_k = eV$ | Kinetic energy of accelerated electron | $V$ in volts → $E_k$ in eV |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$ (non-rel.) | de Broglie wavelength of electron (non-relativistic) | For $V = 20$ kV: $\lambda \approx 0.0086$ nm |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2meV\left(1+\dfrac{eV}{2mc^2}\right)}}$ | de Broglie wavelength (relativistic) | For $V = 100$ kV: $\lambda \approx 0.0037$ nm |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{n \sin \alpha}$ | Rayleigh resolution limit (Abbe) | $n \sin \alpha$ = numerical aperture |
| 5 | $d_{min} \approx 0.61 \, \lambda$ | SEM resolution approximation (in air/vacuum, $\alpha \approx 1$) | $d$ in nm |
| 6 | $M = \dfrac{L_{CRT}}{L_{specimen}}$ | Magnification of SEM image | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_g = \dfrac{V_c}{I_c}$ | Gun brightness relation (Bethe's brightness) | Used for source comparison |
| 8 | $D = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \pi \beta}$ | Spherical aberration penalty (probe size) | $\beta$ = convergence angle |
| 9 | $I_p = B \cdot \alpha^2$ | Probe current relation | $B$ = brightness, $\alpha$ = convergence semi-angle |
| 10 | $R = 0.0276 \, \frac{A^{1/2} E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \rho}$ | Kanaya-Okayama electron range (µm) | $A$ = atomic weight, $Z$ = atomic number, $E$ in keV, $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| 11 | $E_{K\alpha} \approx \frac{3}{4}(E_{K} - E_{L})$ | Moseley's law (approx.) — for EDX identification | Energy in eV/keV |
| 12 | $f = \dfrac{K V}{(NI)^2}$ | Focal length of magnetic lens | $K$, $N$, $I$ = lens constants |

### Physical Constants (Remember These!)

| Symbol | Constant | Value |
|--------|----------|-------|
| $h$ | Planck's Constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| $m_e$ | Electron Rest Mass | $9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |
| $c$ | Speed of Light | $3.0 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| $e$ | Elementary Charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| $eV$ | Energy Conversion | 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of SEM

- **Semiconductor Industry:** Defect inspection, critical dimension (CD) measurement, failure analysis of ICs
- **Materials Science:** Fracture surface analysis, grain size measurement, phase identification
- **Biomedical:** Imaging of cells, tissues, biomaterials, drug particles
- **Nanotechnology:** Characterization of nanotubes, nanowires, quantum dots
- **Forensics:** Bullet casing analysis, fiber identification, fingerprint visualization
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** Surface roughness, coating thickness, weld inspection
- **Automotive & Aerospace:** Fatigue crack analysis, microstructural evolution of alloys

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** SEMs are increasingly used as **in-process metrology tools** in semiconductor fabs (e.g., CD-SEM for measuring transistor gate widths in 7 nm and 5 nm nodes). This is a potential interview/short-answer question.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Working Description of SEM Components

### 2.5.1 Electron Gun
- Generates the primary electron beam.
- Three types: Tungsten Filament (W), Lanthanum Hexaboride (LaB₆), Field Emission Gun (FEG).
- The gun is maintained at a high negative potential (cathode), and the anode is grounded.

### 2.5.2 Electromagnetic Lenses
- **Condenser Lens:** Controls the beam current and demagnifies the source.
- **Objective Lens:** Final demagnification and focusing to a sharp probe on the specimen.
- **Scanning Coils:** Deflect the beam to scan a rectangular area.
- **Stigmator:** Corrects astigmatism in the lens system.

### 2.5.3 Specimen Stage
- 5-axis or 6-axis stage (X, Y, Z, Tilt, Rotation).
- Range of motion: typically 50 mm × 50 mm × 25 mm, tilt 0°–90°, rotation 360°.

### 2.5.4 Detectors
- **Everhart-Thornley (E-T) Detector:** Most common for SE imaging. Comprises a scintillator (YAG crystal coated with aluminum), light pipe, and photomultiplier tube.
- **BSE Detector:** Solid-state (semiconductor) detector placed above the sample, sensitive to atomic number contrast.
- **EDS/EDX Detector:** Lithium-drifted silicon [Si(Li)] or Silicon Drift Detector (SDD) for X-ray analysis.

### 2.5.5 Vacuum System
- Required to prevent electron scattering by air molecules.
- Typically two-stage: rotary pump (10⁻³ Pa) + turbomolecular pump or ion pump (10⁻⁶ Pa or better).
- Environmental SEM (ESEM) operates at 100–1000 Pa using a differential pumping system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Electron Wavelength in SEM

### Why We Need This Derivation

The fundamental reason SEM can image features at the nanometer scale is the **short wavelength** of accelerated electrons. We need to derive the relationship between the accelerating voltage $V$ and the electron's de Broglie wavelength $\lambda$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Starting Point:** According to de Broglie's hypothesis, every particle with momentum $p$ has a wavelength $\lambda$ given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

**Step 1:** When an electron is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, it gains kinetic energy:

$$E_k = eV$$

**Step 2:** For non-relativistic velocities (typically $V < 30$ kV), kinetic energy relates to momentum by:

$$E_k = \frac{p^2}{2m_e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \sqrt{2 m_e E_k} = \sqrt{2 m_e eV}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting into the de Broglie equation:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

**Step 4:** Plug in the values of constants:
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$

where $V$ is in volts. This is the **standard non-relativistic formula**.

**Step 5:** For voltages above 30 kV, the electron velocity approaches a significant fraction of $c$, and the relativistic correction becomes important. The relativistic momentum is:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through 25 kV. Use both non-relativistic and relativistic formulas.

**Non-Relativistic Solution:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{25000}} = \frac{1.227}{158.11} = 0.00776 \text{ nm}$$

**Relativistic Solution:** First, compute the correction term:

$$\frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 25000}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2}$$

$$= \frac{4.005 \times 10^{-15}}{1.6398 \times 10^{-13}} = 0.02442$$

So:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{25000 \times 1.02442}} = \frac{1.227}{160.04} = 0.00767 \text{ nm}$$

The difference is small (~1.2%) at 25 kV, justifying the non-relativistic approximation for typical SEM voltages.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SEM Resolution

### Why We Need This Derivation

The **resolving power** determines the smallest distance between two features that can be distinguished in the image. SEM resolution is limited by the **probe size** (size of the focused electron spot) and the **interaction volume** in the sample.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The minimum probe diameter is limited by the diffraction of the electron beam (similar to optical microscopy), given by Rayleigh's criterion:

$$d_{diff} = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the convergence semi-angle of the lens.

**Step 2:** Additionally, the probe is broadened by **spherical aberration** of the magnetic lens:

$$d_{sph} = \frac{1}{2} C_s \alpha^3$$

where $C_s$ is the spherical aberration coefficient of the objective lens.

**Step 3:** The total probe diameter is the sum (in quadrature) of these contributions:

$$d_{probe} = \sqrt{d_{diff}^2 + d_{sph}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{0.61 \lambda}{\alpha}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{2} C_s \alpha^3\right)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiating with respect to $\alpha$ and setting to zero gives the optimum convergence angle $\alpha_{opt}$:

$$\alpha_{opt} = \left(0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{C_s}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$d_{min} \approx 0.43 \, (C_s \, \lambda^3)^{1/4}$$

### Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum probe size — and hence the resolution — improves as the wavelength decreases (higher $V$) and as $C_s$ decreases (better lens design). This is why **FEG-SEMs** with ultra-low $C_s$ objective lenses and high accelerating voltages achieve resolutions of **0.5 nm** or better.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Magnification

### Derivation

The magnification of an SEM image is purely a **geometric ratio** (unlike TEM, which uses real lenses for image formation):

$$M = \frac{\text{Dimension on display (CRT/PC screen)}}{\text{Dimension of scanned area on specimen}}$$

For example, if the beam scans a $5 \, \mu m$ line on the specimen and the corresponding line on the display is 50 mm long:

$$M = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}} = 10{,}000 \times$$

### Key Insight

Because the operator can change the scan area independently of the display size, **magnification in SEM is not a fixed property of the instrument** — it can be varied continuously from 10× to 2,000,000× simply by changing the scan-coil currents.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SEM with Optical Microscopy (Very Important for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the most frequently asked 7-mark questions. Memorize this matrix thoroughly.

| Parameter | Optical Microscopy (OM) | Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Source of Illumination | Visible light (photons) | Accelerated electrons |
| Wavelength | 400 – 700 nm | 0.001 – 0.01 nm (depending on V) |
| Resolution | ~200 nm (best case) | 0.5 – 10 nm |
| Maximum Useful Magnification | ~1000× – 2000× | 2,000,000× |
| Depth of Field | Low (~0.5 µm at 1000×) | Very high (~30 µm at 1000×) |
| Image Formation | Lenses (refraction) | Scan + Signal Detection |
| Specimen Preparation | Minimal | Conductive coating (Au, Pt, C) often required |
| Environment | Air | High vacuum |
| Information Obtained | Surface morphology (visible) | Topography, composition, crystalline structure |
| Cost | Low | Very high |
| Specimen Size | Flexible | Limited to vacuum chamber size |
| 3D Imaging | Limited | Stereo pair SEM |
| Live Observation | Yes | Yes (with caution for samples) |
| Signal Type | Photons (transmitted/reflected) | Secondary electrons, BSE, X-rays, etc. |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Different Types of Microscopy (Nano-Scale)

| Feature | SEM | TEM | AFM | STM |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Probe | Electron beam | Electron beam | Mechanical tip | Conductive tip |
| Signal | SE / BSE / X-ray | Transmitted electrons | Cantilever deflection | Tunneling current |
| Sample Type | Conductive / Coated | Ultra-thin (<100 nm) | Any solid | Conductive surface |
| Environment | Vacuum | High vacuum | Air/Liquid | Vacuum/Ultra-high vacuum |
| Resolution | ~0.5 nm | ~0.05 nm | Atomic | Atomic |
| 3D Surface | Yes (topographic) | No (2D projection) | Yes (3D) | Yes (3D) |
| Cost | High | Very High | Medium | High |

---

## 3.6 Advantages and Limitations of SEM

### Advantages
1. **Very high resolution** (down to sub-nanometer)
2. **Wide magnification range** (10× to 2,000,000×)
3. **Large depth of field** — gives a 3D appearance
4. **Multiple signal types** — topography + composition + crystallography
5. **Non-destructive** for many samples
6. **Digital imaging** — easy to store, process, share
7. **Combined with EDX** — gives elemental mapping

### Limitations
1. Requires **high vacuum** — limits liquid and wet samples (solved by ESEM)
2. Sample must be **electrically conductive** — non-conductive samples need gold/platinum coating
3. **Expensive** equipment and maintenance
4. **Sectioned or coated** sample preparation needed
5. Limited to **surface** information (with SE/BSE)
6. Beam damage possible for **delicate samples** (biological, polymers)

---

## 3.7 Specimen Preparation for SEM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steps to prepare a typical SEM sample:**
> 1. **Fixation** (for biological samples using glutaraldehyde)
> 2. **Dehydration** (through graded ethanol series)
> 3. **Drying** (critical point drying for biological specimens)
> 4. **Mounting** on a stub using conductive carbon tape or silver paste
> 5. **Sputter coating** with Au, Au-Pd, or Pt (5 – 20 nm thick) for non-conductive samples
> 6. **Loading** into the SEM vacuum chamber

> [!NOTE]
> For **conductive samples** (metals), sputter coating can be skipped.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SEM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Source
        EG[Electron Gun]
        HV[High Voltage Supply 0.5 to 30 kV]
    end

    subgraph Column["Vacuum Column"]
        CL1[Condenser Lens 1]
        CL2[Condenser Lens 2]
        OL[Objective Lens]
        AP[Final Aperture]
        SC[Scanning Coils]
        STG[Stigmator]
    end

    subgraph Specimen
        SS[Specimen Stage 5 axis]
        SP[Sample]
    end

    subgraph Detection
        ETD[Everhart Thornley SE Detector]
        BSE[BSE Detector]
        EDX[EDX Detector]
    end

    subgraph Output
        DSP[Display Monitor]
        SYNC[Synchronization Clock]
        VAC[Vacuum System]
    end

    EG --> CL1 --> CL2 --> OL --> AP
    AP --> SP
    SC --> OL
    STG --> OL
    HV --> EG
    SP -->|SE emitted| ETD
    SP -->|BSE emitted| BSE
    SP -->|X-ray emitted| EDX
    ETD --> DSP
    BSE --> DSP
    EDX --> DSP
    SYNC --> SC
    SYNC --> DSP
    VAC --> Column
    SS --> SP
```

**Description of the Flow:**
1. The **Electron Gun** emits electrons, accelerated by the **High Voltage Supply**.
2. The beam passes through **Condenser Lenses** (C1, C2) that control the current and demagnify the source.
3. The **Objective Lens** focuses the beam to a tiny probe (1 – 10 nm).
4. **Scanning Coils** deflect the beam to scan a rectangular area on the specimen.
5. When the beam strikes the **Sample**, multiple signals (SE, BSE, X-rays) are emitted.
6. The detectors collect these signals, which are synchronized with the scan to form an image on the **Display**.
7. The entire column is maintained at high **Vacuum** to prevent electron scattering.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Electron-Specimen Interaction Volume and Signal Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PE[Primary Electron Beam 1 to 30 keV] -->|enters| IV["Interaction Volume Pear Shaped 1 to 5 um deep"]
    IV --> SE1["SE1 Low Energy 50 eV escape depth 1 to 10 nm"]
    IV --> SE2["SE2 High Energy up to 50 eV deeper"]
    IV --> BSE1["BSE High Energy backscattered"]
    IV --> XR["Characteristic X rays"]
    IV --> AE["Auger Electrons"]
    IV --> CL["Cathodoluminescence"]
    IV --> AB["Absorbed Electrons Sample Current"]
    SE1 --> ETD2[Everhart Thornley Detector]
    SE2 --> ETD2
    BSE1 --> BSD[Solid State BSE Detector]
    XR --> EDX2[EDS Detector]
    AE --> AUG[Auger Spectrometer]
    CL --> CLD[CL Detector]
    AB --> SC[Specimen Current Amplifier]
```

**Description:**
The pear-shaped interaction volume is the region inside the specimen where primary electrons undergo multiple scattering events. Different signals escape from different depths:
- **SE1 (from top 1–10 nm):** Surface topography
- **BSE (from 0.1–1 µm):** Atomic number contrast
- **X-rays (from 0.5–5 µm):** Elemental analysis

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Image Formation in SEM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electron Gun] --> B[Accelerating Anode]
    B --> C[Condenser Lens]
    C --> D[Scan Coils]
    D --> E[Objective Lens]
    E --> F[Focused Probe on Specimen]
    F --> G[Signal Generation SE BSE X ray]
    G --> H[Detector]
    H --> I[Amplifier]
    I --> J[Display Pixel Brightness]
    K[Scan Generator] --> D
    K --> J
```

**Description:**
The scan generator sends synchronized signals to both the scan coils and the display. As the beam rasters across the specimen line by line, the corresponding pixel on the display lights up proportionally to the detected signal. A complete image is built up in a few seconds.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparison of SEM and Optical Microscope Imaging Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OM["Optical Microscope"]
        direction TB
        OML[Light Source Lamp] --> OMLL[Glass Objective Lens]
        OMLL --> OMS[Specimen]
        OMS --> OMEL[Eyepiece Lens]
        OMEL --> OME[Observer Eye or Camera]
    end

    subgraph SEM["SEM"]
        direction TB
        SEMEG[Electron Gun] --> SEMAC[Accelerating Voltage]
        SEMAC --> SEMCL[Electromagnetic Lenses]
        SEMCL --> SEMSC[Scan Coils]
        SEMSC --> SEMS[Specimen in Vacuum]
        SEMS --> SEMDT[Detector ETD or BSE]
        SEMDT --> SEMDSP[Computer Display]
    end
```

**Description:**
- **Optical Microscope:** Uses light, glass lenses, and direct viewing through an eyepiece.
- **SEM:** Uses electrons, electromagnetic lenses, and indirect viewing via a detector-and-display system.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: SEM Applications Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SEM[Scanning Electron Microscopy] --> A1[Semiconductor Failure Analysis]
    SEM --> A2[Materials Science Grain Size Phase]
    SEM --> A3[Biomedical Tissue Cells]
    SEM --> A4[Forensic Science]
    SEM --> A5[Nanotechnology Nanotubes Nanowires]
    SEM --> A6[Quality Control Surface Finish]
    SEM --> A7[EDX Elemental Mapping]
    SEM --> A8[EBSD Crystallographic Orientation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Define Micro and Nano Metrology. List any two techniques used for measurement at the micro/nano scale.**

**Model Answer:**

**Micro and Nano Metrology** is the branch of measurement science that deals with the precise quantification of dimensions, surface features, and geometric properties of features in the range of **0.1 nm to 1000 µm**. It encompasses both inspection (qualitative) and metrology (quantitative) of manufactured components and natural structures at the micro and nano scales.

The field is critical because:
- Modern devices (MEMS, ICs, biotech) have features below 1 µm.
- Tolerances for nano-components are typically 1–10% of feature size (i.e., nanometer-level).
- Properties like electrical conductivity, optical behavior, and mechanical strength depend critically on nanoscale geometry.

**Two techniques used for micro/nano metrology:**

1. **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM):** Uses a focused electron beam to image surfaces with resolution down to ~0.5 nm. Provides topographic, compositional, and morphological information.

2. **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM):** Uses a sharp tip (radius ~5–10 nm) mounted on a flexible cantilever that scans the surface. The interatomic forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, frictional) cause cantilever deflection, which is measured by a laser-photodiode system. Resolution: sub-nanometer, true 3D surface profiling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Other acceptable answers:** TEM, STM, Confocal Microscopy, Optical Profilometry, Stylus Profilometry, White Light Interferometry, X-ray Reflectometry, Focused Ion Beam (FIB).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: What is Scanning Electron Microscopy? State its working principle in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
**Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** is a high-resolution imaging technique that uses a focused beam of accelerated electrons, scanned in a raster pattern across the specimen surface, to produce magnified images based on the detection of secondary electrons, backscattered electrons, and other signals generated by electron-sample interactions.

**Working Principle (in brief):**

The working of SEM is based on the following sequence:

1. **Electrons are generated** by an electron gun (thermionic or field emission) and accelerated through a high potential difference (1 – 30 kV).

2. **Electromagnetic lenses** (condenser and objective) focus the electron beam into a very fine probe of diameter 1 – 10 nm on the specimen surface.

3. **Scanning coils** deflect the beam to scan a rectangular area on the specimen in a raster pattern (line by line).

4. When the beam strikes the specimen, the **electron-sample interaction** produces various signals — chiefly **secondary electrons (SE)** and **backscattered electrons (BSE)** — which are collected by a detector.

5. The **detector signal is amplified** and used to modulate the brightness of a corresponding pixel on a CRT or computer display, synchronized with the beam position.

6. The final image is built up **pixel by pixel**, providing magnified topographic and compositional information about the specimen surface with resolution down to 0.5 nm.

> [!NOTE]
> The magnification of an SEM is given by $M = L_{display} / L_{specimen}$ and can be varied continuously from 10× to 2,000,000×.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Standard pattern is **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Provide the choice as Question A and Question B; the student answers any ONE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**Q: Explain the principle, construction, and working of a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) with a neat labeled diagram. Also discuss the various signals generated when the primary electron beam interacts with the specimen.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**Part (a) — Principle and Construction (7 Marks)**

**Principle:**
SEM works on the principle of **electron-sample interaction**. When a focused beam of high-energy electrons is scanned across a specimen in a vacuum, the interaction produces several signals (SE, BSE, X-rays, etc.). These signals are collected and used to construct a magnified image.

The image formation is based on the fact that:
- The **wavelength of accelerated electrons** (e.g., 0.0086 nm at 20 kV) is far smaller than visible light, allowing nanometer-scale resolution.
- The **secondary electron yield** depends on the angle of incidence of the beam on the surface, giving topographic contrast.

**Construction (with reference to the diagram in Section 4.1):**

The major components of an SEM are:

1. **Electron Gun** — Source of electrons (W, LaB₆, or FEG filament).
2. **High-Voltage Supply** — Accelerates electrons (0.5 – 30 kV).
3. **Electromagnetic Condenser Lenses (C1, C2)** — Demagnify the electron source and control beam current.
4. **Objective Lens** — Final demagnification to produce a fine probe (1 – 10 nm) on the specimen.
5. **Final Aperture** — Stops the beam to a defined size.
6. **Stigmator** — Corrects astigmatism in the beam.
7. **Scanning Coils** — Deflect the beam to scan a rectangular region (e.g., 1 mm × 1 mm maximum).
8. **Specimen Stage** — 5/6-axis stage for X, Y, Z, Tilt, Rotation movements.
9. **Detectors:**
   - **Everhart-Thornley (E-T) Detector** for SE
   - **Solid-State Detector** for BSE
   - **EDS Detector** for X-ray analysis
10. **Vacuum System** — Rotary + turbomolecular/ion pumps (~10⁻⁶ Pa).
11. **Display & Computer System** — For image display, storage, and analysis.

**Part (b) — Working and Signal Generation (7 Marks)**

**Working:**

1. The electron gun emits electrons by thermionic/field emission.
2. Electrons are accelerated toward the anode at high voltage $V$.
3. The accelerated beam passes through a series of condenser and objective lenses, which demagnify it to a fine probe.
4. Scanning coils deflect the beam to scan the specimen surface in a raster pattern.
5. The primary electrons interact with the specimen, generating **secondary electrons, backscattered electrons, characteristic X-rays, Auger electrons, and cathodoluminescence**.
6. The detectors collect these signals and convert them into voltage pulses.
7. The voltage signal modulates the brightness of a corresponding pixel on the display, synchronized with the beam scan.
8. A complete image is built up pixel-by-pixel in a few seconds.

**Various Signals Generated:**

| Signal | Energy | Escape Depth | Detector | Information |
|--------|--------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| SE | <50 eV | 1 – 10 nm | E-T detector | Topography |
| BSE | >50 eV | 0.1 – 1 µm | Solid-state | Composition (Z-contrast) |
| Characteristic X-rays | 0.1 – 20 keV | 0.5 – 5 µm | EDS/EDX | Elemental analysis |
| Auger Electrons | 50 – 2000 eV | 0.5 – 5 nm | Auger spectrometer | Surface composition |
| Cathodoluminescence | 1 – 5 eV | 0.1 – 1 µm | CL detector | Optical/electronic |

> [!NOTE]
> **SE gives the most common topographic images** because of its shallow escape depth and high sensitivity to local surface inclination. **BSE gives compositional contrast** because backscattering coefficient increases with atomic number Z.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q: With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Scanning Electron Microscope. Compare SEM with Optical Microscope (OM) in terms of resolution, magnification, depth of field, and applications.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of SEM (7 Marks)**

**Construction:**
(Refer to the detailed block diagram in Section 4.1 and the construction list in Question A above.)

**Working — Stepwise:**

1. The specimen is mounted on the specimen stage and inserted into the vacuum chamber.
2. The vacuum pumps reduce the chamber pressure to ~10⁻⁶ Pa.
3. The electron gun (W or LaB₆) is heated to emission temperature; for FEG, a strong electric field extracts electrons.
4. Emitted electrons are accelerated through 1 – 30 kV potential.
5. The condenser and objective lenses demagnify the beam to a 1 – 10 nm probe.
6. Scan coils raster the beam across the specimen (e.g., 1024 × 768 lines).
7. Primary electrons enter the specimen and create an interaction volume of pear shape.
8. SE and BSE are emitted, collected by detectors, and converted to electrical signals.
9. The display shows the image pixel-by-pixel, synchronized with the scan.

**Part (b) — Comparison of SEM with Optical Microscope (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Optical Microscope (OM) | SEM |
|-----------|-------------------------|-----|
| **Source** | Visible light (λ = 400 – 700 nm) | Electron beam (λ = 0.001 – 0.01 nm) |
| **Resolution** | ~200 nm (Abbe limit) | 0.5 – 10 nm |
| **Magnification** | Up to ~2000× | Up to 2,000,000× |
| **Depth of Field** | ~0.5 µm at 1000× | ~30 µm at 1000× (100× better) |
| **Sample Preparation** | Minimal | Conductive coating often needed |
| **Environment** | Air | High vacuum |
| **Image Type** | Direct, color | Indirect, monochrome (digital) |
| **Signal Type** | Photons | Electrons (SE, BSE, X-rays) |
| **Information** | Surface morphology | Topography, composition, crystal structure |
| **Cost** | Low | Very high |
| **Sample Size** | Unlimited | Limited to chamber size |
| **3D Effect** | Limited | Excellent (stereo-pair SEM) |
| **Application Examples** | Biology, mineralogy, metallurgy | Nanotechnology, semiconductor, forensics |

> [!NOTE]
> The key conceptual reason SEM outperforms OM is the **de Broglie wavelength of electrons** (much shorter than visible light), which overcomes the diffraction limit of Abbe's equation: $d = 0.61\lambda/(n \sin \alpha)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in SEM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SEM with TEM:** SEM forms images from **scanned surface signals** (SE, BSE); TEM forms images from **transmitted electrons** through ultra-thin samples. Don't mix up their working principles.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention vacuum:** Most students forget that SEM requires a high vacuum environment. Always mention it.
>
> 3. **No labeled diagram = 2–3 marks deducted:** A well-labeled block diagram of SEM construction is worth at least 2–3 marks in any 7- or 14-mark question. Always draw one, even if not explicitly asked.
>
> 4. **Confusing SE and BSE:** SE = low energy, topographical, from <10 nm depth. BSE = high energy, compositional, from deeper regions. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
>
> 5. **Wrong formula for magnification:** In SEM, $M$ is a ratio of display area to specimen scan area, NOT a lens-based magnification. Writing $M = 1 + D/f$ (the OM formula) will lose marks.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the role of scanning coils:** These are essential — they raster the beam and synchronize with the display. Forgetting them is a common oversight.
>
> 7. **Sample preparation not discussed:** For 14-mark questions, always add a section on **specimen preparation** (mounting, coating, etc.) to score full marks.
>
> 8. **Not distinguishing between FEG and thermionic guns:** Examiners often ask "compare different electron sources." Students who don't know the difference between W, LaB₆, and FEG lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scanning Electron Microscopy**
>
> - **Definition:** SEM uses a focused, scanned electron beam to image surfaces via electron-sample interaction signals.
> - **Why SEM?** Electrons have ~100,000× shorter wavelength than visible light → resolution to 0.5 nm.
> - **Electron Source:** W (tungsten), LaB₆, or FEG (field emission gun) — FEG gives best resolution.
> - **Lenses:** Electromagnetic (condenser + objective); NOT glass lenses.
> - **Scanning Coils:** Deflect the beam in a raster pattern; synchronized with display.
> - **Specimen Stage:** 5- or 6-axis; X, Y, Z, Tilt, Rotation.
> - **Vacuum:** 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa; ESEM works at higher pressure.
> - **Detectors:** E-T (SE), solid-state (BSE), Si(Li) or SDD (EDX).
> - **SE (Secondary Electrons):** E < 50 eV, escape depth 1–10 nm, give topography.
> - **BSE (Backscattered Electrons):** E > 50 eV, give Z-contrast (compositional).
> - **X-rays:** Used for elemental analysis (EDX/EDS).
> - **Resolution:** $d_{min} \approx 0.61 \, \lambda$ (limited by lens aberrations in practice).
> - **Magnification:** $M = L_{display}/L_{specimen}$; can go up to 2,000,000×.
> - **Depth of Field:** 100× greater than optical microscopy.
> - **De Broglie Wavelength:** $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2meV}$ (non-rel.); 1.227/√V nm.
> - **Specimen Preparation:** Mount on stub → sputter coat with Au/Pt (5–20 nm) for non-conductors.
> - **Compare with OM:** SEM has better resolution, magnification, depth of field, but needs vacuum + conductive sample + expensive equipment.
> - **Compare with TEM:** SEM scans surface; TEM transmits through ultra-thin sample. SEM is non-destructive for most samples; TEM requires destructive sample prep.
> - **Compare with AFM/STM:** SEM is vacuum-based, uses electrons; AFM/STM work in air/vacuum, use physical probes.
> - **Key Applications:** Semiconductor inspection, materials science, failure analysis, forensic science, biomedical imaging, nanotechnology.
> - **Formula to Memorize:**
>   - $E_k = eV$
>   - $\lambda = 1.227/\sqrt{V}$ nm
>   - $d = 0.61 \lambda / (n \sin \alpha)$
>   - $M = L_{display}/L_{specimen}$
> - **Common 14-Mark Question Format:** "Explain SEM principle + construction + signals + comparison with OM/TEM."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning White Light Interferometry - Principle and Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scanning White Light Interferometry - Principle and Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Scanning White Light Interferometry (SWLI)** is a non-contact, full-field optical profiling technique used to measure the three-dimensional surface topography of micro- and nano-scale features with sub-nanometer vertical resolution. It is based on the principle of low-coherence (white light) interference in a Mirau, Michelson, or Linnik type interferometer configuration, where a broadband light source is employed so that interference fringes are localized only over a vertical span equal to the source's coherence length.

When a piezoelectric transducer (PZT) translates either the objective lens or the sample vertically along the optical axis, each pixel on the detector records a temporal interference signal known as the **correlogram**. The position of the peak of the fringe envelope corresponds to the local surface height, enabling a pixel-wise height map of the entire field of view to be reconstructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Optical Metrology):**
> SWLI is classified under *non-contact surface profiling* techniques and is officially listed as a high-weight topic for characterizing MEMS, micro-fluidic, and bio-medical micro-components. The two recurring short-answer areas are (i) *principle of low-coherence interference* and (ii) *mechanism of envelope-peak detection for height retrieval*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (typical industrial values):**
> - Vertical resolution: **< 0.1 nm**
> - Lateral resolution: **0.35 µm to 5 µm** (limited by objective NA)
> - Vertical scan range: **up to 100 mm** (with extended scan stages)
> - Coherence length of white LED source: **L_c ≈ 1 to 10 µm**
> - Measurement repeatability: **< 0.5 nm**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and standing on a rough, hilly ground covered with snow. You are given a long, flexible stick and told to drag it across the ground. Every time the stick touches the snow (i.e., reaches a "matched" height), you hear a faint crinkle; everywhere else you hear nothing. By slowly lowering the stick from a high position to a low position, you note the exact altitude at which the crinkle was loudest for each location — that altitude is the local ground height.

- The **stick** is the *optical path difference (OPD)* being scanned.
- The **snow contact** represents the *zero-OPD condition* (constructive interference only when OPD ≈ 0).
- The **crinkle sound** is the *interference fringe*.
- The **hilly ground** is your *sample surface topography*.
- The **broadband white light** ensures that the "crinkle" is sharp and localized — like a short, crisp sound rather than a long, confusing hum.

Because ordinary (laser) light would produce crinkles everywhere along the stick's path (long coherence length), you could never tell *where* the snow actually is. The short coherence of white light is what gives SWLI its **unambiguous vertical localization**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle – Structured Logic Flow

The complete SWLI measurement chain proceeds through the following causal stages:

1. **Broadband Illumination Stage**
