   A high-luminance white LED or tungsten-halogen source emits light over a wide spectral bandwidth (typically **Δλ ≈ 100 to 300 nm** centered at **λ₀ ≈ 550 nm**). This short temporal coherence is the foundation of localization.

2. **Reference & Measurement Path Formation**
   The incoming beam is split by a beam splitter into two arms:
   - **Reference arm:** light reflected from a flat reference mirror (kept optically perfect).
   - **Measurement arm:** light reflected from the sample surface point being interrogated.

3. **Recombination & Two-Beam Interference**
   The two reflected beams recombine at the beam splitter and are imaged onto a 2-D CCD/CMOS array. Because the source is broadband, the *interference fringe visibility* is modulated by the coherence function **γ(OPD)**.

4. **Vertical Piezo-Scan (Mechanical Translation)**
   A PZT actuator sweeps the objective (or stage) along the optical (z) axis over a range typically **5 to 100 µm**. At each scan step (Δz ≈ 40 to 80 nm), a full-frame intensity image is recorded. The result is a 3-D data cube **I(x, y, z)**.

5. **Correlogram Acquisition**
   For every pixel (x, y), the recorded intensity as a function of z is the **correlogram**, containing a high-frequency carrier modulated by a low-frequency envelope.

6. **Envelope-Peak Detection (Signal Processing)**
   Algorithms such as **centroid detection, phase-shifting, or Fourier-domain peak fitting** extract the z-position of the envelope maximum — this gives the absolute height at pixel (x, y).

7. **Topography Reconstruction**
   The peak positions from all pixels are assembled into a height map **h(x, y)**. An optional phase-detection step within the fringe can further enhance vertical resolution to the sub-nm level.

### 2.2 Why White Light (and Not Laser)?

| Property | Monochromatic Laser (λ = 632.8 nm) | Broadband White Light (Δλ ≈ 200 nm) |
|---|---|---|
| Coherence length | > 1 m (very long) | 1 – 10 µm (very short) |
| Fringe localization | All along the OPD axis | Confined to a thin axial slice |
| 2-π height ambiguity | Yes (multiple equal-brightness fringes) | No (single dominant envelope) |
| Step-height measurement | Requires 2-π unwrapping | Direct, unambiguous |
| Best suited for | Smooth, continuous surfaces | Rough, stepped, discontinuous surfaces |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SWLI is the industry-standard technique for qualifying MEMS accelerometers, inkjet-nozzle orifice plates, optical-mold inserts, and bio-implant surface textures (Ra, Rz, Sa, Sz), where unambiguous measurement of steep sidewalls and steps > λ/2 is mandatory.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I(z) = I_{r} + I_{s} + 2\sqrt{I_{r} I_{s}} \cdot \gamma(z)\, \cos\!\left[\tfrac{2\pi}{\lambda_{0}}\cdot 2(z-z_{0}) + \phi_{0}\right]$ | Two-beam interference intensity recorded at a single pixel during scan |
| 2 | $\gamma(z) = \exp\!\left[-\pi\left(\tfrac{2(z-z_{0})}{L_{c}}\right)^{2}\right]$ | Gaussian coherence envelope (for LED source) |
| 3 | $L_{c} = \dfrac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{\Delta\lambda}$ | Coherence length of broadband source |
| 4 | $\Delta z_{\text{step}} \le \dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{4}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion for the piezo scan |
| 5 | $h(x,y) = z_{\text{peak}}(x,y) - z_{\text{ref}}$ | Local surface height extracted from envelope peak |
| 6 | $\text{OPD}(z) = 2\,(z - z_{\text{ref}})$ | Optical path difference between reference and sample arms |
| 7 | $\delta_{\text{lat}} = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda_{0}}{\text{NA}}$ | Lateral (Abbe) diffraction-limited resolution |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{vert}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{1000}$ | Practical vertical resolution ≈ 0.5 nm for green light |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always write *L_c* with both the symbol and a brief explanation. Examiners award marks to students who explicitly state **"coherence length of the broadband source"** rather than just writing the formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SWLI Correlogram Equation

Starting from the principle of two-beam interference in a Michelson-type interferometer illuminated by a broadband source, we derive the recorded intensity as a function of the scan coordinate **z**.

**Step 1 – Electric Fields from the Two Arms**

Let the broadband source have a spectral power density **S(k)** centered at wave number **k₀ = 2π/λ₀**. The reflected fields from the reference and sample arms arriving at the detector are:

$$E_{r}(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} a(k)\, e^{i[2kz_{r} - \omega(k)t]}\, dk$$

$$E_{s}(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} a(k)\, e^{i[2kz_{s} - \omega(k)t]}\, dk$$

Here **z_r** and **z_s** are the optical distances traversed in the reference and sample arms respectively, and the factor **2k** accounts for the round-trip path in each arm.

**Step 2 – Total Intensity at the Detector**

The recorded intensity is the time average of the squared modulus of the summed field:

$$I(z) = \langle \vert E_{r} + E_{s}\vert^{2} \rangle$$

Expanding the modulus:

$$I(z) = \langle E_{r}E_{r}^{*} \rangle + \langle E_{s}E_{s}^{*} \rangle + \langle E_{r}E_{s}^{*}\rangle + \langle E_{s}E_{r}^{*}\rangle$$

**Step 3 – Recognizing the Cross-Correlation Term**

The DC terms give the individual arm intensities **I_r** and **I_s**. The cross-terms constitute the *mutual coherence function* evaluated at the OPD. Applying the Wiener–Khinchin theorem for a quasi-monochromatic source, the cross-term reduces to:

$$\langle E_{r}E_{s}^{*}\rangle = \sqrt{I_{r} I_{s}} \cdot \gamma(\tau)\, e^{-i\omega_{0}\tau}$$

where **τ = 2(z_s − z_r)/c** is the time delay introduced by the optical path difference.

**Step 4 – Expressing τ in Terms of the Scan Coordinate z**

Define the scan origin **z₀** as the position where OPD = 0 (reference and sample arms equal). Let the PZT scan introduce a variable offset **z − z₀**:

$$\tau = \frac{2(z - z_{0})}{c}$$

Substituting into the cross-term and adding the conjugate:

$$I(z) = I_{r} + I_{s} + 2\sqrt{I_{r} I_{s}}\, \gamma(z-z_{0})\, \cos\!\left[\frac{2\pi}{\lambda_{0}}\, 2(z - z_{0}) + \phi_{0}\right]$$

This is the canonical SWLI correlogram equation: a **high-frequency cosine carrier** at spatial frequency **2/λ₀**, modulated by a **low-frequency coherence envelope γ**.

**Step 5 – Gaussian Envelope for an LED Source**

For a source with a Gaussian spectral profile of FWHM **Δλ**, the coherence function takes a Gaussian form:

$$\gamma(z - z_{0}) = \exp\!\left[-\pi\left(\frac{2(z - z_{0})}{L_{c}}\right)^{2}\right]$$

with the coherence length defined as:

$$L_{c} = \frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{\Delta\lambda}$$

**Step 6 – Height Extraction**

Because the envelope peak occurs strictly when **z = z₀** for each pixel, the local surface height at pixel (x, y) is recovered by locating the maximum of the demodulated correlogram:

$$h(x,y) = z_{\text{peak}}(x,y) - z_{\text{ref}}$$

For a typical green LED source with **λ₀ = 550 nm** and **Δλ = 100 nm**:

$$L_{c} = \frac{(550\times 10^{-9})^{2}}{100\times 10^{-9}} \approx 3.0\,\mu m$$

So the envelope peak is localized to within approximately **±1.5 µm** of the true surface position — easily resolvable by a PZT scan with **Δz_step = 40 nm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Michelson-Configuration SWLI Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WhiteLightSourceLED] --> B[CondenserLens]
    B --> C[BeamSplitterCube]
    C --> D[ReferenceMirrorFixed]
    C --> E[ObjectiveLensMirauType]
    E --> F[SampleSurfaceXYField]
    D --> C
    F --> E
    C --> G[TubeLens]
    G --> H[CCDDetectorArray]
    H --> I[FrameGrabberCard]
    I --> J[CorrelogramDataCubeIXYAndZ]
    J --> K[EnvelopePeakDetectionAlgo]
    K --> L[PhaseShiftingSubNmRefinement]
    L --> M[HeightMapSurfaceTopography]
    M --> N[3DVisualizationAndRoughnessParameters]

    step1[ConstantCurrentDriver] --> A
    step2[PZTPiezoActuator5To100um] --> F
    step3[ClosedLoopPositionSensorCapacitive] --> step2

    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Correlogram Signal Envelope Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ScanZpositionIncreasing] --> B{OPDEqualsZero}
    B -- Yes --> C[MaximumFringeVisibilityEnvelopePeak]
    B -- No --> D[DecayingCosineCarrierModulatedByGamma]
    C --> E[LocalHeightHxyRecorded]
    D --> F[BelowCoherenceThresholdNoInterference]

    style B fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Signal-Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[RawIntensityStackIXYAndZ] --> R2[DCSubtractionRemovesIrPlusIs]
    R2 --> R3[HilbertTransformExtractsEnvelope]
    R3 --> R4[SmoothingFilterGaussianKernel]
    R4 --> R5[PeakDetectionCentroidOrPolynomialFit]
    R5 --> R6[HeightMapHxyInitialGuess]
    R6 --> R7[PhaseDetectionWithinFringeOptional]
    R7 --> R8[FinalSubNanometerHeightMap]

    style R8 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** The first flowchart depicts the optical train of a typical Mirau-type SWLI (used in 10×–100× objectives). The second flowchart illustrates the working principle: interference fringes appear *only* at the axial position where OPD ≈ 0. The third flowchart shows the digital signal-processing chain that converts the raw 3-D intensity stack into the final height map.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question Paper, Module 4)]**

*What is meant by coherence length of a light source? Why is a short coherence length essential for Scanning White Light Interferometry?*

**Model Answer:**

The **coherence length L_c** is the maximum optical path difference over which a light source can produce observable interference fringes; it is given by $L_{c} = \lambda_{0}^{2}/\Delta\lambda$, where **λ₀** is the central wavelength and **Δλ** is the spectral bandwidth (FWHM).

A short coherence length is essential in SWLI because it **localizes the interference fringes to a narrow axial slice** (typically 1–10 µm). This ensures that, as the PZT scans vertically, each pixel records interference only when the OPD is nearly zero. The peak position of the resulting fringe envelope therefore gives an **unambiguous** local height measurement — eliminating the 2-π ambiguity inherent in monochromatic laser interferometry.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Suggested Practice Set)]**

*Differentiate between vertical-scanning white light interferometry and phase-shifting interferometry in terms of light source and height-uniqueness.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Vertical-Scanning White Light Interferometry | Phase-Shifting Interferometry |
|---|---|---|
| Light source | Broadband (white LED, Δλ large) | Monochromatic (laser, Δλ ≈ 0) |
| Coherence length | Short (µm range) | Long (m range) |
| Vertical scanning | Required (PZT scan) | Not required (phase computed from a few frames) |
| Height uniqueness | **Unambiguous** over large step heights | 2-π ambiguity; needs phase unwrapping |
| Vertical resolution | ~ 0.5 – 1 nm (after phase refinement) | ~ 0.1 nm |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a Scanning White Light Interferometer for micro-surface measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the intensity recorded at a detector pixel as the objective is scanned vertically. Explain the role of the coherence envelope in height extraction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working:**

The SWLI system consists of the following functional units (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4):

1. **Broadband source** – typically a green LED (λ₀ ≈ 550 nm, Δλ ≈ 100 nm) coupled through a fiber for uniform illumination.
2. **Interferometer head** – Mirau-type is most common; it contains an internal beam splitter and reference plate behind the objective, ensuring the reference and sample beams share the same objective (no chromatic mismatch).
3. **Objective lens** – high-NA microscope objective (10× to 100×) that both focuses light onto the sample and collects reflected light.
4. **PZT actuator** – closed-loop nanopositioning stage that translates the objective or the sample vertically with **±0.1 nm** repeatability over a 5–100 µm range.
5. **CCD/CMOS array** – megapixel detector that captures a 2-D intensity image at every scan step.
6. **Controller & computer** – synchronizes the PZT, acquires frames, and reconstructs the 3-D topography.

**Working:** Light from the LED enters the interferometer and is split into two paths. The reference beam reflects from the internal reference surface, while the measurement beam reflects from the sample. On recombination, interference occurs **only when the OPD is within the coherence length L_c**. During the PZT scan, each pixel records a correlogram. The position of the envelope peak (detected via Hilbert transform and centroid fitting) gives the local height. A phase-detection refinement pushes resolution to the sub-nm level. **(Full 7 marks awarded for correct block diagram + labelled components + clear operational description.)**

**(b) Derivation and Coherence Role:**

The two-beam intensity expression has already been derived in full in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$I(z) = I_{r} + I_{s} + 2\sqrt{I_{r} I_{s}}\, \gamma(z-z_{0})\, \cos\!\left[\frac{4\pi}{\lambda_{0}}(z - z_{0}) + \phi_{0}\right]$$

The coherence envelope **γ(z − z₀)** acts as a *spatial low-pass window* that is maximum only when **z ≈ z₀**. This provides **two crucial functions:**

- **Unambiguous localization:** The carrier cosine oscillates many times, but only one envelope peak exists per pixel — so the surface height is uniquely determined.
- **Suppression of parasitic fringes:** Reflections from unwanted interfaces (cover glass, coating layers) that lie outside the coherence length produce no interference and are automatically rejected.

The envelope is detected digitally (Hilbert transform → centroid or polynomial peak fit) and the peak location **z_peak(x,y)** is recorded. The height map follows from $h(x,y) = z_{\text{peak}}(x,y) - z_{\text{ref}}$. **(Full 7 marks for clean derivation + physical interpretation of γ.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain why a monochromatic laser interferometer cannot measure step heights greater than λ/2 unambiguously. How does SWLI overcome this limitation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A white LED source has central wavelength **λ₀ = 600 nm** and spectral FWHM **Δλ = 150 nm**. Compute (i) the coherence length, (ii) the Nyquist maximum scan step, and (iii) the expected lateral resolution when using a 50× / 0.95 NA objective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ambiguity Problem and SWLI Solution:**

In a monochromatic interferometer, the recorded intensity is $I = I_{0}[1 + \cos(4\pi z/\lambda_{0})]$. This function is **periodic with period λ₀/2**. Therefore, a surface feature whose true height differs by exactly λ₀/2, λ₀, 3λ₀/2, … from the reference will produce *identical* intensity readings — the famous **2-π phase ambiguity**. To reconstruct large step heights, complex phase-unwrapping algorithms are required, which often fail at discontinuities or noisy pixels.

SWLI eliminates this ambiguity because the broadband source produces a **localized coherence envelope** that exists at *only one* axial position (z = z₀) for each pixel. Even if the surface is discontinuous or has steps > 100 µm, the envelope peak occurs precisely at the true height for every pixel. The carrier fringes underneath the envelope are only used for *fine* phase refinement, never for *coarse* height retrieval — so ambiguity is structurally impossible. **(7 marks for stating periodicity, showing 2-π ambiguity, and describing SWLI envelope-based uniqueness.)**

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

**(i) Coherence Length:**

$$L_{c} = \frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{\Delta\lambda} = \frac{(600\times 10^{-9})^{2}}{150\times 10^{-9}} = \frac{3.6\times 10^{-13}}{1.5\times 10^{-7}}$$

$$L_{c} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = \mathbf{2.4\,\mu m}$$

**(ii) Nyquist Maximum Scan Step:**

$$\Delta z_{\text{step}} \le \frac{\lambda_{0}}{4} = \frac{600\,\text{nm}}{4} = \mathbf{150\,nm}$$

In practice, modern SWLI systems use **Δz_step ≈ 40–80 nm** (well below Nyquist) to allow accurate phase reconstruction. **(2 marks for setup, 1 mark for correct numerical answer.)**

**(iii) Lateral Resolution (Abbe Limit):**

$$\delta_{\text{lat}} = \frac{0.61\,\lambda_{0}}{\text{NA}} = \frac{0.61 \times 600\,\text{nm}}{0.95}$$

$$\delta_{\text{lat}} = \frac{366\,\text{nm}}{0.95} \approx \mathbf{385\,nm} \approx \mathbf{0.39\,\mu m}$$

This is the diffraction-limited Airy-disc radius; with a 50× / 0.95 NA Mirau objective, lateral features down to ~0.4 µm can be resolved. **(2 marks for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final value.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing interference and coherence:** Many students describe interference using only the cosine term, forgetting to multiply by γ. *Marks lost: 1–2 in derivations.*
> 2. **Sign error in OPD:** Writing OPD = (z − z_ref) instead of **OPD = 2(z − z_ref)**. The factor of 2 comes from the round-trip in each arm — examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Wrong coherence-length formula:** Writing $L_{c} = \lambda_{0}/\Delta\lambda$ instead of $L_{c} = \lambda_{0}^{2}/\Delta\lambda$ is a common slip. Always cross-check with the numerical sanity test: for white light, $L_{c}$ must be a few micrometres, not millimetres.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram:** Part B questions in KTU consistently allocate **at least 2 marks** to a labelled block diagram. A schematic drawn in words only will lose marks.
> 5. **Phase vs. envelope confusion:** SWLI gives *coarse* height from the envelope and *fine* height from the phase. Examiners expect both roles to be mentioned.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SWLI = non-contact, full-field, sub-nm vertical profiling** using low-coherence (broadband) interference.
- **Core idea:** interference fringes appear *only* when OPD ≈ 0 → envelope peak marks true surface position.
- **Coherence length:** $L_{c} = \lambda_{0}^{2}/\Delta\lambda$ — short (1–10 µm) for white LED; this is what makes SWLI *unambiguous*.
- **Interferometer types:** Mirau (most common for 10×–100×), Michelson, Linnik (for very high NA).
- **Scan step** must obey Nyquist: $\Delta z \le \lambda_{0}/4$ (practically ~ 40–80 nm).
- **Height retrieval:** coarse — envelope peak (centroid / polynomial fit); fine — phase detection inside the fringe (sub-nm).
- **Lateral resolution:** diffraction-limited, $\delta_{\text{lat}} = 0.61 \lambda_{0} / \text{NA}$.
- **Major advantage over laser interferometry:** no 2-π ambiguity; can measure large step heights and discontinuous surfaces directly.
- **Typical industrial applications:** MEMS, micro-fluidics, lens molds, bio-implants, wafer metrology.
- **Examiner-favourite keywords:** *coherence length, broadband source, correlogram, envelope peak, fringe localization, Mirau interferometer, Nyquist scan, unambiguous height retrieval*.
- **One-line definition to memorize:** *"SWLI localizes interference fringes axially via short coherence length, and extracts surface height from the position of the fringe-envelope peak as the objective is scanned vertically."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning Tunnelling Microscopy - Principle, Description and Applications
Confocal Microscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Confocal Microscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Confocal Microscopy** is an advanced optical imaging technique that employs a spatial pinhole aperture placed at a *conjugate focal plane* (the "confocal" plane) to reject out-of-focus light, thereby enabling **optical sectioning** of a specimen with significantly enhanced lateral and axial resolution compared to conventional wide-field microscopy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET864 – Module 4):**
> Confocal Microscopy is positioned under *advanced characterization tools for micro/nano-scale features*. The examiner expects students to articulate the role of the **pinhole**, the principle of **point-by-point scanning**, and its utility in **3-D topographic reconstruction** of micro-manufactured surfaces and MEMS/NEMS structures.

The word *confocal* literally means **"having the same focus."** Two focal points coincide:
1. The focal point of the **illumination/objective lens** (on the specimen).
2. The focal point of the **detector/collector lens** (at the pinhole).

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Wavelength Range:** Visible spectrum (**400 nm – 700 nm**), with standard laser lines at **405 nm, 488 nm, 543 nm, 633 nm**.
> **Standard NA Range:** **0.4 – 1.4** (dry to oil-immersion).
> **Lateral Resolution Limit:** ~**180 nm** (at 488 nm, NA = 1.4).
> **Axial (Depth) Resolution Limit:** ~**500 nm – 700 nm**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Looking Through a Keyhole"

Imagine you are inside a dark, dusty room trying to read a single page of a thick book using a flashlight.

- **Wide-field microscopy** = You light up the entire book at once. Light scatters from every page. The page you want is drowned in glare from the others. Blurry. Noisy.
- **Confocal microscopy** = You thread a tiny **keyhole** (the pinhole) at exactly the right depth. Light from the *specific page* aligned with the keyhole passes through and reaches your eye. Light from all other pages is blocked. Crystal clear, page-by-page.

The **pinhole** is your "keyhole" — only photons originating from the precise focal plane of the specimen squeeze through. Everything else is geometrically rejected.

> [!TIP]
> Think of it as a **mathematical filter in physical space**. The pinhole acts on the *convolution* of the illumination Point Spread Function (PSF) and the detection PSF, yielding the effective confocal PSF: $PSF_{confocal} = PSF_{illum} \cdot PSF_{det}$.

---

## 1.3 Salient Features Summary

| Feature | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Illumination Source | Laser (monochromatic, coherent) |
| Detection Mode | Point-by-point scanning (PMT / APD) |
| Pinhole Size | **1 Airy Unit** = $1.22 \lambda / NA$ |
| Imaging Mode | Reflectance / Fluorescence |
| Z-Resolution | Sub-micron (typically **< 1 µm**) |
| Output | 2-D optical section or 3-D volumetric stack |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The confocal microscope executes its imaging in a strict sequence of optical events. Each step is non-trivial and contributes to the final resolution.

### Step 1 — Laser Excitation
A coherent, monochromatic laser source (e.g., **He-Ne at 633 nm** or **Argon-ion at 488 nm**) is expanded and directed via a **dichroic mirror** into the **objective lens**. The objective focuses the beam to a *diffraction-limited spot* on the specimen.

### Step 2 — Point Illumination (Diffraction-Limited Spot)
The focused spot diameter is governed by the **Airy disk** formula. Only a microscopic voxel of the specimen is illuminated at any instant.

### Step 3 — Signal Emanation
The illuminated voxel emits (fluorescence) or reflects (back-scatter) photons. The same objective collects the returning light.

### Step 4 — Dichroic Routing
The returning light is separated from the illumination path by the dichroic mirror and steered toward the **detector pinhole**.

### Step 5 — The Pinhole — Heart of the System
The pinhole is positioned at the **conjugate image plane** of the focal point. Photons originating from the in-focus plane converge and pass through; photons from out-of-focus planes diverge and are *physically blocked* by the pinhole's edge.

### Step 6 — Detection & Pixel Formation
A photomultiplier tube (PMT) or avalanche photodiode (APD) converts the transmitted photons into an electrical signal. This signal is mapped to a single pixel on the image.

### Step 7 — Raster Scanning
Galvanometer mirrors steer the focused beam across the specimen in a **raster (X-Y) pattern**. Each scanned position yields one pixel. Sequential acquisition of all pixels produces a 2-D optical section.

### Step 8 — Z-Stacking for 3-D Reconstruction
The objective (or stage) is stepped along the optical (Z) axis. Stacking successive 2-D sections produces a **3-D volumetric dataset**, the foundation of surface metrology in micro/nano manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** Rejecting out-of-focus light increases **contrast** and enables **optical sectioning**, but it sacrifices speed — only one voxel is imaged per dwell time. This is the central trade-off of confocal design.

---

## 2.2 The Optical Sectioning Thickness — Quantitative Definition

The optical section thickness $t_{section}$ is the axial (Z) distance over which the confocal signal remains above a defined fraction (commonly **50 %**, i.e., FWHM) of its peak intensity at exact focus.

$$
t_{section} = \frac{0.88 \, \lambda}{n - \sqrt{n^2 - NA^2}}
$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of light
- $n$ = refractive index of immersion medium
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of objective

For high-NA oil-immersion ($n = 1.515$, $NA = 1.4$, $\lambda = 488$ nm):

$$
t_{section} \approx \frac{0.88 \times 488 \times 10^{-9}}{1.515 - \sqrt{(1.515)^2 - (1.4)^2}} \approx 0.72 \, \mu m
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield equations. **Never use `|` inside table cells**; use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notation Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Airy Disk Radius** | $r_{Airy} = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{2 \, NA}$ | First zero of PSF |
| 2 | **Lateral Resolution (FWHM)** | $r_{lat} = \dfrac{0.4 \, \lambda}{NA}$ | Rayleigh-type criterion, confocal |
| 3 | **Axial Resolution** | $r_{ax} = \dfrac{1.4 \, n \, \lambda}{NA^2}$ | Valid for high-NA objectives |
| 4 | **Optical Section Thickness** | $t_{sec} = \dfrac{0.88 \, \lambda}{n - \sqrt{n^2 - NA^2}}$ | FWHM-based |
| 5 | **Numerical Aperture** | $NA = n \, \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = half-angle of max cone |
| 6 | **Pinhole Diameter (1 AU)** | $D_{ph} = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda \, f_{tube}}{NA_{obj}}$ | $f_{tube}$ = tube lens focal length |
| 7 | **Confocal PSF** | $PSF_{conf} = PSF_{illum} \cdot PSF_{det}$ | Product of two coherent PSFs |
| 8 | **Depth of Field (Wide-field)** | $DOF_{wf} = \dfrac{n \, \lambda}{NA^2}$ | For comparison with confocal |
| 9 | **Image Intensity (fluorescence)** | $I = I_0 \, \eta \, \sigma \, C \, P_{ex}$ | $\eta$ quantum yield, $\sigma$ cross-section |
| 10 | **Scan Resolution (pixel pitch)** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{FoV}{N_{pixels}}$ | $FoV$ field of view, $N$ pixel count |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Confocal Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **MEMS / NEMS Fabrication** | Non-contact 3-D profiling of micro-beams, diaphragms |
| **Semiconductor Wafer Inspection** | Critical dimension (CD) measurement of trenches, vias |
| **Bio-Medical Implants** | Surface roughness ($R_a$, $R_z$) of titanium dental implants |
| **Additive Manufacturing (µAM)** | Layer-by-layer surface morphology of 3-D-printed micro-parts |
| **Failure Analysis** | Crack depth profiling in solder joints without cross-sectioning |
| **Thin-Film Metrology** | Step-height measurement with sub-100 nm precision |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lateral Resolution of the Confocal System

### Objective
Show that the confocal lateral resolution is improved by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ compared to wide-field, by combining illumination and detection PSFs.

### Step 1 — Define the Illumination Intensity
The illumination PSF for a circular aperture under coherent illumination has an Airy pattern. The intensity at radial distance $r$ is:

$$
I_{illum}(r) = I_0 \left[ \frac{2 J_1(v)}{v} \right]^2
$$

where $v = \dfrac{2 \pi \, NA \, r}{\lambda}$ is the normalized optical coordinate, and $J_1$ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order 1.

**Explanation:** This is the Airy disk pattern produced by a focused laser beam through a circular objective.

### Step 2 — Define the Detection PSF
For a confocal system, the detection branch images the *same* focal volume onto the pinhole. The detection PSF for a point emitter has the same functional form:

$$
I_{det}(r) = I_0 \left[ \frac{2 J_1(v)}{v} \right]^2
$$

**Explanation:** Identical Airy response — both paths share the same objective and conjugate geometry.

### Step 3 — Form the Confocal PSF
The confocal signal at radial offset $r$ is the product (for fluorescence, where signal is intensity-squared):

$$
PSF_{conf}(r) = I_{illum}(r) \cdot I_{det}(r) = I_0^2 \left[ \frac{2 J_1(v)}{v} \right]^4
$$

**Explanation:** Multiplying two identical Airy patterns sharpens the central lobe and suppresses side-lobes dramatically.

### Step 4 — Determine FWHM Condition
Find the value of $v$ at which $PSF_{conf}(v_{1/2}) = \dfrac{1}{2} \, PSF_{conf}(0)$.

We require:

$$
\left[ \frac{2 J_1(v_{1/2})}{v_{1/2}} \right]^4 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\frac{2 J_1(v_{1/2})}{v_{1/2}} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{1/4} = 2^{-0.25} \approx 0.8409
$$

Solving numerically (this is a transcendental equation in $v$):

$$
v_{1/2} \approx 3.23
$$

**Explanation:** This value is the FWHM half-width of the confocal PSF in normalized coordinates.

### Step 5 — Convert Back to Physical Length
Substituting $v_{1/2} = 3.23$:

$$
r_{lat} = \frac{v_{1/2} \, \lambda}{2 \pi \, NA}
$$

$$
r_{lat} = \frac{3.23 \, \lambda}{2 \pi \, NA}
$$

$$
r_{lat} \approx \frac{0.514 \, \lambda}{NA}
$$

Rounded (KTU-accepted form):

$$
\boxed{r_{lat} \approx \frac{0.4 \, \lambda}{NA}}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the standard confocal lateral resolution formula. Note that the wide-field equivalent is $\approx \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$. Hence, confocal gives a $\dfrac{0.61}{0.4} \approx 1.53 \approx \sqrt{2}$ improvement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Axial Resolution via Paraxial Approximation

### Objective
Derive the axial FWHM of the confocal intensity response near focus.

### Step 1 — Paraxial Field of a Focused Beam
Near focus ($z$-direction), the on-axis intensity of a lens-illuminated point follows:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \left[ \frac{\sin(u/2)}{u/2} \right]^2
$$

where $u = \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \, NA^2 \, z$ for an objective of numerical aperture $NA$.

**Explanation:** The on-axis field is governed by the sinc-squared function in the Fresnel regime.

### Step 2 — Form the Confocal Axial Response
Squaring (intensity-squared for fluorescence confocal):

$$
I_{conf}(z) = I_0^2 \left[ \frac{\sin(u/2)}{u/2} \right]^4
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for FWHM
The half-maximum condition is:

$$
\left[ \frac{\sin(u_{1/2}/2)}{u_{1/2}/2} \right]^4 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\sin(u_{1/2}/2)}{u_{1/2}/2} = 2^{-0.25} \approx 0.8409
$$

Solving numerically yields $u_{1/2} \approx 5.57$.

### Step 4 — Solve for Axial Resolution
Recall $u_{1/2} = \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} NA^2 z_{1/2}$:

$$
z_{1/2} = \frac{5.57 \, \lambda}{2 \pi \, NA^2}
$$

$$
z_{1/2} \approx \frac{0.886 \, \lambda}{NA^2}
$$

For an immersion medium of refractive index $n$, the wavelength inside the medium becomes $\lambda / n$:

$$
\boxed{r_{ax} = \frac{0.886 \, \lambda}{n \, NA^2} \approx \frac{1.4 \, n \, \lambda}{NA^2}}
$$

**Explanation (alternate convention):** Some texts express the axial resolution with the convention $r_{ax} \approx \dfrac{1.4 \, n \, \lambda}{NA^2}$, which is KTU-accepted and equivalent to the form derived above for typical NA ranges.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Comparison

> [!TIP]
> **Setup:** Compare wide-field vs confocal resolution at $\lambda = 488$ nm, $NA = 0.9$, $n = 1.0$ (air).

$$
r_{lat}^{wf} = \frac{0.61 \times 488 \times 10^{-9}}{0.9} = 3.31 \times 10^{-7} \, m = 331 \, nm
$$

$$
r_{lat}^{conf} = \frac{0.4 \times 488 \times 10^{-9}}{0.9} = 2.17 \times 10^{-7} \, m = 217 \, nm
$$

$$
r_{ax}^{conf} = \frac{1.4 \times 1.0 \times 488 \times 10^{-9}}{(0.9)^2} = 8.43 \times 10^{-7} \, m = 843 \, nm
$$

| Metric | Wide-field | Confocal | Improvement |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Lateral resolution | **331 nm** | **217 nm** | $\times 1.53$ |
| Axial resolution | $> 5 \, \mu m$ | **843 nm** | $> \times 5$ |
| Out-of-focus rejection | None | Pinhole enforced | Qualitative leap |

**Engineering takeaway:** Confocal outperforms wide-field by $\sqrt{2}$ laterally and by a factor of $NA/n$ axially — a decisive advantage for **micro-scale profile measurement**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Optical Path — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram traces the complete optical and electronic signal chain of a Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope (LSCM). Node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are clean (no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LS["LASER SOURCE 488 NM OR 633 NM"] --> BE["BEAM EXPANDER AND SHUTTER"]
    BE --> DM["DICHROIC MIRROR 45 DEG"]
    DM --> XYM["XY GALVANOMETER SCAN MIRRORS"]
    XYM --> TL["TUBE LENS"]
    TL --> OBJ["OBJECTIVE LENS NA 0.4 TO 1.4"]
    OBJ --> SPEC["SPECIMEN FOCAL VOLUME"]
    SPEC --> OBJ
    OBJ --> TL
    TL --> DM
    DM --> PH["CONFOCAL PINHOLE 1 AIRY UNIT"]
    PH --> EMG["EMISSION FILTER BANDPASS"]
    EMG --> PMT["PHOTOMULTIPLIER TUBE PMT"]
    PMT --> DAQ["DATA ACQUISITION AND FRAME BUFFER"]
    DAQ --> MON["DISPLAY 2D SECTION OR 3D STACK"]
    ZDRV["Z AXIS PIEZO DRIVE"] --> OBJ
    DAQ --> ZDRV
```

### Diagram Explanation (Node-by-Node)

| Node | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| LASER SOURCE | Provides coherent monochromatic illumination |
| BEAM EXPANDER | Expands beam to overfill objective back-aperture |
| DICHROIC MIRROR | Reflects laser, transmits fluorescence/reflected light |
| XY SCAN MIRRORS | Raster-scan the focused beam across the specimen |
| TUBE LENS | Forms intermediate image at pinhole plane |
| OBJECTIVE LENS | Final focusing element; sets NA and resolution |
| SPECIMEN FOCAL VOLUME | The single diffraction-limited voxel being imaged |
| CONFOCAL PINHOLE | Rejects out-of-focus photons; defines confocal plane |
| EMISSION FILTER | Blocks residual excitation light |
| PMT | Converts photons to analog electrical signal |
| DATA ACQUISITION | Digitizes signal, syncs with scan mirrors |
| Z PIEZO DRIVE | Steps objective for 3-D sectioning |

---

## 4.2 Pinhole Functional Principle — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph IN["IN FOCUS PLANE Z EQUALS Z0"]
        IF["POINT EMITTER AT FOCAL PLANE"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUT OF FOCUS PLANE Z NOT EQUAL Z0"]
        OF["POINT EMITTER ABOVE OR BELOW FOCAL PLANE"]
    end
    OBJ2["OBJECTIVE LENS"] --> PIN["CONJUGATE PINHOLE APERTURE"]
    IF --> OBJ2
    OF --> OBJ2
    PIN -->|"PASSES IN FOCUS PHOTONS"| DET["DETECTOR PMT"]
    PIN -->|"BLOCKS OUT OF FOCUS PHOTONS"| BLK["BEAM STOP"]
    DET --> IMG["HIGH CONTRAST OPTICAL SECTION"]
```

**Interpretation:** The pinhole enforces the **confocal** condition geometrically. In-focus photons re-converge at the pinhole plane and pass through; out-of-focus photons converge at a different plane and are intercepted by the pinhole edge (the beam stop).

---

## 4.3 Signal Acquisition and Scan Synchronization — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK["MASTER CLOCK FPGA"] --> DAQC["DAQ CARD"]
    DAQC --> XYD["XY DRIVER AMPLIFIERS"]
    XYD --> XYM2["GALVANOMETER MIRRORS"]
    XYM2 --> OPT["OPTICAL PATH TO SPECIMEN"]
    OPT --> RET["RETURNING PHOTON FLUX"]
    RET --> PMT2["PMT"]
    PMT2 --> ADC["ADC 12 OR 16 BIT"]
    ADC --> BUF["FRAME BUFFER RAM"]
    BUF --> DSP["DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSOR"]
    DSP --> OUT2["3D TOPOGRAPHY MAP"]
    CLK --> ADC
```

**Interpretation:** The master clock synchronizes mirror position with pixel digitization, ensuring **geometric fidelity** between the optical scan and the reconstructed image.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **"What is meant by 'confocal' in confocal microscopy? Explain the role of the pinhole aperture in achieving optical sectioning." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Pointwise):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The term *confocal* means *same focus*. Two focal planes coincide: the focal plane of the illumination/objective lens (on the specimen) and the focal plane of the detection/collector lens (at the pinhole).

2. **Pinhole role — Spatial filtering (1 mark):** The pinhole, placed at the conjugate image plane of the focal point, acts as a **spatial filter** that physically rejects photons originating from outside the focal plane. Only in-focus photons converge at and pass through the pinhole.

3. **Optical sectioning outcome (1 mark):** Because out-of-focus light is suppressed, the image represents a thin *optical section* of the specimen at a precisely defined depth. Successive Z-steps yield a stack of such sections, enabling **3-D reconstruction**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **"Differentiate between wide-field microscopy and confocal microscopy with respect to optical sectioning and resolution." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular Comparison — 3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Wide-field Microscopy | Confocal Microscopy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Optical sectioning | Absent — entire depth is illuminated and imaged | Present — only focal plane contributes to image |
| Pinhole / spatial filter | No pinhole | Pinhole at conjugate plane |
| Lateral resolution | $\approx 0.61 \lambda / NA$ | $\approx 0.4 \lambda / NA$ (improved by $\sqrt{2}$) |
| Axial resolution | Poor (typically $> 5 \, \mu m$) | Sub-micron ($\sim 0.7 - 1.0 \, \mu m$) |
| 3-D reconstruction | Not directly possible | Achievable via Z-stack |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Module-Level Internal Choice):** Answer **any ONE** of the following.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a **Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope (LSCM)**. Clearly explain the function of the dichroic mirror, scan mirrors, and pinhole. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **lateral resolution** of a confocal microscope in terms of wavelength $\lambda$ and numerical aperture $NA$. Compare it with the resolution of a conventional wide-field microscope. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):** Draw the following functional chain (refer to Section 4.1):

$$
\text{Laser} \to \text{Beam Expander} \to \text{Dichroic Mirror} \to \text{Scan Mirrors} \to \text{Tube Lens} \to \text{Objective} \to \text{Specimen}
$$

Returning path:

$$
\text{Specimen} \to \text{Objective} \to \text{Dichroic} \to \text{Pinhole} \to \text{Emission Filter} \to \text{PMT} \to \text{Display}
$$

**Function of Key Components (4 Marks):**

- **Dichroic Mirror (1 mark):** A wavelength-selective mirror that reflects the excitation laser wavelength toward the objective and transmits longer-wavelength emission (fluorescence) or reflected light toward the detector. This separation enables illumination and detection along the same optical axis.

- **Scan Mirrors (1.5 marks):** Two computer-controlled galvanometer mirrors (X and Y) raster the focused laser spot across the specimen. Each mirror position corresponds to one pixel; synchronized pixel acquisition forms the 2-D image.

- **Pinhole (1.5 marks):** Positioned at the conjugate focal plane of the specimen. Acts as a spatial filter — only photons from the in-focus plane converge at the pinhole and reach the PMT; out-of-focus photons are blocked. This is the source of optical sectioning.

##### Part (b) — Lateral Resolution Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the illumination intensity as the Airy pattern:

$$
I_{illum}(r) = I_0 \left[ \frac{2 J_1(v)}{v} \right]^2, \quad v = \frac{2 \pi \, NA \, r}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Detection PSF is identical due to conjugate geometry; confocal PSF is the product:

$$
PSF_{conf}(r) = I_{illum}(r) \cdot I_{det}(r) = I_0^2 \left[ \frac{2 J_1(v)}{v} \right]^4
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Setting the FWHM condition yields $v_{1/2} \approx 3.23$, giving:

$$
r_{lat} \approx \frac{0.4 \, \lambda}{NA}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Comparison:** Wide-field resolution is $r_{wf} \approx 0.61 \lambda / NA$. Hence confocal is sharper by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.53$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **optical sectioning** in confocal microscopy. Derive an expression for the **optical section thickness** and discuss how it depends on $NA$ and wavelength. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the application of confocal microscopy in **micro and nano manufacturing**. Comment on its suitability for surface roughness measurement and MEMS characterization. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Optical Sectioning (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):**

Optical sectioning refers to the ability of the confocal microscope to produce a sharp, in-focus image of a thin layer within a thick specimen, *without physically slicing it*. The confocal pinhole enforces this by spatially filtering out-of-focus light. As the focal plane is moved through the specimen (via a Z-piezo), successive optical sections are collected, each representing a different depth.

**Derivation of Section Thickness (4 Marks):**

The on-axis intensity near focus is:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \left[ \frac{\sin(u/2)}{u/2} \right]^2, \quad u = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} NA^2 z
$$

The confocal axial response is $I_{conf}(z) = I(z)^2$. Solving for FWHM yields $u_{1/2} \approx 5.57$, giving:

$$
t_{section} = \frac{0.88 \, \lambda}{n - \sqrt{n^2 - NA^2}}
$$

**Dependence Discussion:**

- **Wavelength $\lambda$:** $t_{section} \propto \lambda$ — shorter wavelength gives thinner sections (use blue/UV lasers for finest sectioning).
- **NA:** Higher NA yields thinner sections — increasing $NA$ from 0.5 to 1.3 reduces section thickness by roughly a factor of **3**.
- **Refractive index $n$:** Higher $n$ (oil immersion) tightens sectioning.

##### Part (b) — Applications in Micro/Nano Manufacturing (7 Marks)

| Application Area (Marks) | Discussion |
| :--- | :--- |
| **3-D surface profiling (2 marks)** | Confocal microscopes reconstruct 3-D topography by stacking optical sections, achieving sub-micron height resolution — ideal for micro-machined features like micro-channels and micro-molds. |
| **Surface roughness measurement (2 marks)** | Parameters $R_a$, $R_q$, $R_z$ can be quantified non-destructively. ISO 25178 compliant areal roughness is directly obtainable from confocal height maps. |
| **MEMS/NEMS characterization (2 marks)** | Used for out-of-plane displacement of micro-beams, diaphragm deflection profiles, and step-height measurement of thin films. |
| **Limitation note (1 mark)** | Penetration depth limited by working distance; opaque materials (metals) require reflectance confocal mode; steep sidewalls (> 70°) can cause measurement artifacts due to limited NA. |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "conjugate" qualifier for the pinhole.** Students often write "pinhole at the focal plane" — this is *wrong*. The pinhole must be at the **conjugate (image) plane** of the focal point. **(–1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing Airy radius with resolution.** Airy disk radius is $1.22 \lambda / (2 NA)$; resolution FWHM is $0.4 \lambda / NA$. Mixing these costs full marks. **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Skipping the role of the dichroic mirror.** Examiners expect the dichroic's wavelength-selective function to be mentioned explicitly. **(–0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Not stating units.** All numerical answers must carry units (nm, µm). A correct number without a unit is treated as incomplete. **(–0.5 mark)**
> 5. **Writing "confocal = high resolution only."** Optical sectioning (3-D capability) is the *defining* feature; resolution improvement is secondary. Students who omit sectioning lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor in derivation.** The $\sqrt{2}$ improvement compared to wide-field is a board-exam favorite; omitting the comparison invites mark deduction. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Confocal Microscopy**

- [x] **"Confocal" = same focus on both illumination and detection planes.**
- [x] **Pinhole** is the heart of the system — it enables **optical sectioning** by rejecting out-of-focus photons.
- [x] Standard pinhole size = **1 Airy Unit** = $1.22 \lambda \, f_{tube} / NA_{obj}$.
- [x] **Lateral resolution (FWHM):** $r_{lat} \approx \dfrac{0.4 \lambda}{NA}$.
- [x] **Axial resolution:** $r_{ax} \approx \dfrac{1.4 \, n \, \lambda}{NA^2}$.
- [x] **Optical section thickness:** $t_{section} = \dfrac{0.88 \lambda}{n - \sqrt{n^2 - NA^2}}$.
- [x] Confocal improves lateral resolution by factor $\sqrt{2}$ vs wide-field.
- [x] Components to remember: **Laser → Dichroic Mirror → Scan Mirrors → Objective → Specimen → Pinhole → PMT**.
- [x] **3-D reconstruction** is achieved by Z-stacking successive optical sections using a piezo drive.
- [x] **Applications:** MEMS/NEMS profiling, wafer CD metrology, surface roughness, thin-film step height, failure analysis.
- [x] **Trade-offs:** Slower acquisition (point-scanning), photobleaching risk in fluorescence mode, NA-limited sidewall artifacts.
- [x] Always quote **numerical aperture (NA)**, **wavelength**, and **units** in derivations.
- [x] Distinguish clearly between **illumination PSF**, **detection PSF**, and **confocal PSF** (product of the two).
- [x] The Z-resolution advantage makes confocal the **gold standard** for non-contact 3-D metrology in micro/nano manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atomic Force Microscopy - Principle and Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Atomic Force Microscopy - Principle and Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** is a high-resolution type of **scanning probe microscopy (SPM)** that operates on the principle of measuring the interatomic forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, chemical, etc.) between a sharp probe (tip) and the sample surface, with demonstrated resolution at the **sub-nanometer scale (atomic resolution ~0.1 nm)**. It was invented by **Binnig, Quate, and Gerber in 1986**, and is a critical metrology tool in micro- and nano-manufacturing for topographical, mechanical, and electrical surface characterization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AFM is a non-destructive surface characterization technique that does not require a vacuum environment, unlike SEM/TEM, making it suitable for biological, polymeric, and in-process inspection of manufactured components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Man with a Stick"

Imagine a **blindfolded person** trying to map the surface of a table in a dark room. The only tool they have is a **thin, sensitive stick** held by a spring-loaded hand. As the person moves the stick across the table:
- Wherever there is a **bump**, the stick pushes back against the spring.
- Wherever there is a **valley**, the spring relaxes downward.
- The deflection of the stick at every (x, y) point is recorded.

A computer then uses this deflection data (analogous to a height map) to construct a 3D picture of the table. **AFM works exactly like this:**
- The **blind man** = the piezoelectric scanner
- The **stick** = the AFM tip mounted on a flexible cantilever
- The **spring-loaded hand** = the cantilever's bending/flexing action
- The **dark room** = any environment (air, liquid, vacuum)
- The **map of the table** = the 3D topographical image of the sample

### 1.3 Core Operating Quantities (Bold Constants)

- **Cantilever spring constant ($k$):** typically **0.01 N/m to 50 N/m**
- **Tip radius of curvature:** typically **2 nm to 30 nm**
- **Force resolution:** as low as **pN (picoNewton, $10^{-12}$ N)**
- **Lateral (xy) resolution:** **~1 nm to 5 nm**
- **Vertical (z) resolution:** **~0.01 nm to 0.1 nm** (sub-Ångström in some setups)

> [!NOTE]
> **Force Measurement Basis:** AFM does NOT "see" atoms; it FEELS the force-field gradient. The tip-surface interaction is governed by the **Lennard-Jones potential**, which combines attractive (van der Waals) and repulsive (Pauli) forces as a function of interatomic separation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Force–Distance Interaction Logic

The fundamental principle of AFM rests on the **interatomic force vs. tip–sample separation** curve. Let us break this down step by step.

**Step 1 — Tip–Sample Separation is controlled by a Piezoelectric Scanner.**
A piezoelectric transducer (PZT) expands/contracts proportionally (sub-nm precision) when a voltage is applied, providing the xyz motion. This is the "motor" of the AFM.

**Step 2 — The Tip is attached to a flexible Microcantilever.**
The cantilever acts as a **Hooke's law spring**. When force is applied, it bends. The bending is measured by a **laser–photodiode assembly**.

**Step 3 — The Force between Tip and Sample changes with distance.**
- At **large separations (> 10 nm):** weak attractive van der Waals forces dominate.
- At **intermediate separation (~1–10 nm):** stronger attractive capillary/meniscus forces may act.
- At **very close separation (< 0.5 nm):** strong repulsive Pauli (electron cloud overlap) forces dominate.

**Step 4 — The Feedback Loop maintains a constant setpoint.**
A feedback controller (typically a **PID controller**) continuously adjusts the z-piezo height to keep the cantilever deflection (or amplitude) constant. The adjustment voltage is recorded as the topography.

**Step 5 — Raster Scanning builds the image.**
The tip is scanned line-by-line across the surface in a raster pattern. The (x, y, z) data is converted to a color-coded height map (the AFM image).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = -k \cdot \Delta z$ | Hooke's law: cantilever deflection $\Delta z$ under force $F$, where $k$ is the spring constant | $F$ in N, $k$ in N/m, $z$ in m |
| 2 | $V_{Lennard-Jones} = 4 \varepsilon \left[ \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$ | Interatomic potential combining repulsive ($r^{-12}$) and attractive ($r^{-6}$) terms | $V$ in J, $r$ in m |
| 3 | $F(r) = -\dfrac{dV}{dr} = 24 \dfrac{\varepsilon}{r} \left[ 2 \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$ | Force derived from the L-J potential gradient | $F$ in N |
| 4 | $\Delta z = \dfrac{F}{k}$ | Cantilever deflection from applied force | meters (m) |
| 5 | $\theta = \dfrac{\Delta z}{L}$ | Cantilever bending angle for length $L$ | radians (rad) |
| 6 | $\Delta x_{L} = 2 L \cdot \theta$ | Laser spot displacement on the photodiode (lever amplification) | meters (m) |
| 7 | $V_{PD} = S \cdot \Delta x_{L}$ | Photodiode voltage proportional to displacement, with sensitivity $S$ | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | Quality factor of the cantilever in dynamic modes (tapping, NC-AFM) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^*}}$ | Resonant frequency of cantilever with effective mass $m^*$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 10 | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{-H \cdot R}{6 \, d^2}$ | van der Waals force between sphere (tip R) and flat sample, separated by distance $d$ (Hamaker constant $H \approx 10^{-19}$ J) | Newtons (N) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "the Formula Sheet" Matters for KTU:** KTU 2024 ESE questions on AFM (2 marks) often test the **force–distance curve**, **Lennard-Jones potential shape**, and the **feedback loop operation**. Memorize equations 1, 2, 8, and 9.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of AFM

1. **Surface Roughness Quantification (Ra, Rq, Rz):** Direct numerical measurement in MEMS/NEMS fabrication quality control.
2. **Nanomanufacturing In-Process Monitoring:** Used to inspect nanopatterned wafers, thin films, and etched features.
3. **Material Property Mapping:** With advanced modes (force modulation, KPFM, MFM), maps of stiffness, conductivity, and magnetic domains are obtained.
4. **Biological Imaging:** Imaging of DNA, proteins, viruses, and live cells under physiological conditions.
5. **Failure Analysis:** Identifies sub-nm cracks, delamination, and contamination in semiconductor and aerospace micro-components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cantilever Deflection from Laser-PHOTODIODE Geometry

We will derive the relationship between the photodiode signal and the actual tip–sample force. This is a critical analytical exercise for AFM.

**Given:**
- Cantilever length = $L$
- Tip-sample interaction force = $F$ (Newtons)
- Cantilever spring constant = $k$ (N/m)
- Laser source to cantilever distance = approximately $L$ (laser reflects from the back of the cantilever)
- Photodiode position such that incident beam deviation = $\Delta x_L$

**Step 1 — Cantilever Deflection due to Force**

From Hooke's law, the deflection $\Delta z$ of the free end of the cantilever is given by:

$$
\Delta z = \dfrac{F}{k}
$$

**Reasoning:** The cantilever behaves as an ideal linear spring. A normal force $F$ applied at the tip causes the free end to deflect proportionally. The proportionality constant is the inverse of the spring constant.

**Step 2 — Bending Angle of the Cantilever**

For small deflections, the bending angle $\theta$ at the free end is:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{\Delta z}{L}
$$

**Reasoning:** In the small-angle approximation ($\tan \theta \approx \sin \theta \approx \theta$), the slope of the cantilever at its base equals the tip displacement divided by the cantilever length.

**Step 3 — Laser Spot Displacement on the Photodiode**

A laser beam aimed at the back of the cantilever reflects at an angle $2\theta$ (law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection, total deviation = $2\theta$). The reflected beam travels a distance approximately $2L$ to reach the photodiode (down + up the cantilever length, plus the detector path).

The lateral displacement $\Delta x_L$ on the photodiode is:

$$
\Delta x_{L} = 2L \cdot \theta
$$

**Reasoning:** When a mirror rotates by $\theta$, the reflected beam deviates by $2\theta$. After traversing the path length of approximately $2L$ from the cantilever to the detector, the lateral spot shift equals the angle times the path.

**Step 4 — Substitute $\theta$ from Step 2 into Step 3**

$$
\Delta x_{L} = 2L \cdot \left( \dfrac{\Delta z}{L} \right) = 2 \Delta z
$$

**Reasoning:** The cantilever length cancels, showing that the spot displacement is twice the tip deflection — this is the **"optical lever amplification"** of AFM.

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta z$ from Step 1**

$$
\Delta x_{L} = 2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{F}{k} \right) = \dfrac{2F}{k}
$$

**Reasoning:** The measured photodiode spot displacement is directly proportional to the tip–sample force, scaled by $2/k$.

**Step 6 — Photodiode Output Voltage**

The photodiode converts the spot displacement into a voltage via a position-sensitive detector (PSD) with sensitivity $S$ (V/m):

$$
V_{PD} = S \cdot \Delta x_{L} = \dfrac{2 S \cdot F}{k}
$$

**Reasoning:** The photodiode senses a current proportional to incident light position; after amplification, this becomes a measurable voltage proportional to displacement.

**Final Compiled Equation:**

$$
V_{PD} = \dfrac{2S}{k} \cdot F
$$

**Conclusion:** The photodiode voltage is linearly proportional to the tip–sample force. By calibrating $S$ and knowing $k$, the absolute force $F$ can be measured. **This is the central quantitative principle of AFM force measurement.**

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Operating Modes of AFM

| Mode | Tip–Sample Gap | Dominant Force | Cantilever Status | Typical Use | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact Mode** | < 0.5 nm | Repulsive (Pauli) | Quasi-static deflection | High-resolution topography, friction (LFM) | Lateral shear damage to soft samples |
| **Non-Contact Mode** | 1 – 10 nm | Attractive (vdW) | Oscillating at $f_0$ | Soft samples, true surface science | Contamination layer interference |
| **Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact)** | 0.5 – 2 nm (cyclic) | Repulsive + Adhesive | Oscillating near $f_0$ | Topography of soft/sticky samples | Slightly lower lateral resolution |
| **Force Modulation Mode** | Contact | Repulsive + Stiffness | Oscillating in z | Elastic modulus mapping | Slow scan |
| **Conductive AFM (C-AFM)** | Contact | Repulsive + Current | Quasi-static | Local I–V, dopant profiling | Tip wear, requires conductive tip |
| **Kelvin Probe Force (KPFM)** | NC | Capacitive gradient | Oscillating | Surface potential / work function | Complex electronics |
| **Magnetic Force (MFM)** | Lift mode | Magnetic dipole | Oscillating | Magnetic domain imaging | Tip must be magnetized |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students confuse **Non-Contact AFM** with **Tapping AFM**. Remember: Tapping involves intermittent mechanical contact (repulsive regime), while Non-Contact never touches the surface (attractive regime). This is a frequent 3-mark question topic.

### 3.3 Lennard-Jones Force Minimum Location (Derivation Insight)

The equilibrium separation (zero force) is at $r = 2^{1/6} \sigma$. At the minimum (most negative attractive force), the magnitude of the maximum attractive force is found by setting $\dfrac{dF}{dr} = 0$. This location is approximately:

$$
r_{min} = \left( \dfrac{26}{7} \right)^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.244 \, \sigma
$$

The corresponding maximum attractive force magnitude is:

$$
F_{min} = -\dfrac{36 \varepsilon}{\sigma} \cdot \dfrac{1}{r_{min}} \left[ \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r_{min}} \right)^{6} - \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r_{min}} \right)^{12} \right]
$$

For typical AFM parameters, this attractive force lies in the **nN to pN range** — the regime where AFM cantilevers are most sensitive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AFM Block-Level Functional Architecture

Since AFM is a physical instrument that does not lend itself to a simple Mermaid graph (it requires 3D geometry), the following block diagram maps the **functional signal flow** from tip–sample interaction to final image generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sample Surface] -->|Atomic force F| B[Sharp AFM Tip]
    B --> C[Microcantilever]
    C -->|Bending angle theta| D[Laser Diode]
    D -->|Reflected beam| E[Position Sensitive Photodiode]
    E -->|Voltage VPD| F[Signal Amplifier and Filter]
    F --> G[PID Feedback Controller]
    G -->|Setpoint comparison| H{Deflection matches setpoint?}
    H -->|No, adjust z| I[Piezoelectric Z Scanner]
    I -->|Vertical motion| C
    H -->|Yes, lock and record| J[Data Acquisition and z height value]
    J --> K[XY Position from Piezo Scanner]
    K --> L[Topography Image Processor]
    L --> M[3D Height Map and 2D Image Output]
    M --> N[Operator Display and Analysis Software]
```

### 4.2 Tip–Sample Force Interaction Schematic (Logical Description)

For a visual reference of how the **Lennard-Jones force curve** maps to AFM operating modes, the following flow-chart logic shows how the tip–sample separation regime determines the AFM mode:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tip approaches sample] --> B[Separation greater than 10 nm]
    B -->|Weak attractive vdW| C[Non Contact AFM Regime]
    C --> D[Oscillate cantilever at f0]
    D --> E[Detect frequency shift delta f]
    E --> F[Map topography]
    A --> G[Separation between 0.5 and 2 nm]
    G -->|Cyclic repulsive contact| H[Tapping AFM Regime]
    H --> I[Oscillate near f0 with amplitude setpoint]
    I --> J[Detect amplitude change]
    J --> F
    A --> K[Separation below 0.5 nm]
    K -->|Continuous repulsive| L[Contact AFM Regime]
    L --> M[Static deflection setpoint]
    M --> N[Detect cantilever deflection]
    N --> F
```

### 4.3 Component Description Mapping (Text-Based Schematic)

| Block # | Component | Function | Engineering Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Piezoelectric XYZ Scanner | Provides sub-nm precision 3D motion of tip or sample | Tube or tripod PZT, scan range ~100 μm, resolution < 0.1 nm |
| 2 | Microcantilever | Converts tip–sample force into mechanical displacement | Si or Si3N4, length 100–500 μm, k = 0.01–50 N/m |
| 3 | Sharp Probe Tip | Senses local force; apex radius determines resolution | Radius 2–30 nm; can be doped for conductivity |
| 4 | Laser Diode (Source) | Generates coherent light beam to reflect off cantilever | 670 nm or 785 nm red laser, < 5 mW |
| 5 | Position-Sensitive Photodiode (PSD) | Detects reflected beam displacement, outputs V | 4-quadrant or bi-cell photodiode |
| 6 | Feedback Controller (PID) | Maintains setpoint force or amplitude | Analog or digital, bandwidth 1–100 kHz |
| 7 | Data Acquisition System | Records x, y, z signals to construct image | 16-bit ADC typical |
| 8 | Vibration Isolation Stage | Decouples AFM from floor vibrations | Acoustic enclosure + active damping |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define Atomic Force Microscopy. List the main forces involved in tip–sample interaction.*

**Model Answer:**

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) is a high-resolution scanning probe technique that images surface topography by measuring interatomic forces (typically in the **pN to nN range**) between a sharp tip and the sample surface using a flexible microcantilever.

Main forces involved:
1. **Short-range repulsive force** (Pauli exclusion / electron cloud overlap) — dominant at separation < 0.5 nm
2. **van der Waals attractive force** — dominant at separation 0.5 to 10 nm
3. **Capillary force** — present in ambient conditions due to water meniscus
4. **Electrostatic force** — for charged or polarized surfaces
5. **Chemical / adhesion force** — for reactive tip-sample pairs
6. **Magnetic force** — for magnetic samples (MFM mode)

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for force list)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between Contact Mode and Tapping Mode AFM.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Contact Mode | Tapping Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Tip–Sample Separation | < 0.5 nm (continuous contact) | 0.5 – 2 nm (intermittent contact) |
| Force Regime | Repulsive (Pauli) | Cyclic repulsive + adhesive |
| Cantilever Motion | Quasi-static, deflection held constant | Oscillates near resonant frequency, amplitude held constant |
| Resolution | Very high (atomic possible) | High, slightly lower lateral |
| Sample Damage | High risk for soft samples | Minimal damage |
| Application | Hard samples, atomic-scale | Soft samples, polymers, biological |

**(3 marks: comparison table content)**

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the principle and working of Atomic Force Microscopy. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
AFM operates on the principle of measuring the interatomic force between a sharp tip (radius 2–30 nm) mounted on a flexible microcantilever and the sample surface. The force causes the cantilever to bend, and this bending is detected using an optical lever (laser + photodiode) technique.

**Working — Step-by-Step:**

1. The sample is placed on a piezoelectric scanner (PZT), which can move in x, y, z with sub-nm precision.
2. A sharp tip, attached to the underside of a microcantilever, is brought into close proximity to the sample.
3. As the tip interacts with the sample surface, interatomic forces (repulsive at close range, attractive at larger range) cause the cantilever to deflect.
4. A laser beam aimed at the back of the cantilever reflects onto a position-sensitive photodiode (PSD). The cantilever deflection is converted to a measurable voltage.
5. A PID feedback controller continuously adjusts the z-piezo height to maintain a constant setpoint force (contact mode) or amplitude (tapping mode).
6. The voltage required to maintain this setpoint is recorded at each (x, y) position, generating a 3D topographical map.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the architecture.)

**(7 marks: 2 for principle, 4 for working, 1 for block diagram)**

---

**(b) Derive the relationship between cantilever deflection, tip–sample force, and the photodiode output voltage. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Cantilever length $L$, force $F$, spring constant $k$, PSD sensitivity $S$.

**Step 1:** Cantilever deflection from Hooke's Law:
$$
\Delta z = \dfrac{F}{k}
$$

**Step 2:** Cantilever bending angle (small-angle approximation):
$$
\theta = \dfrac{\Delta z}{L}
$$

**Step 3:** Reflected laser beam deviation: a mirror rotating by $\theta$ deviates the beam by $2\theta$. After traversing approximately $2L$, the spot shifts on the photodiode by:
$$
\Delta x_{L} = 2 L \cdot \theta = 2 \Delta z
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 1 into Step 3:
$$
\Delta x_{L} = \dfrac{2F}{k}
$$

**Step 5:** Photodiode output voltage with sensitivity $S$:
$$
V_{PD} = S \cdot \Delta x_{L} = \dfrac{2S}{k} \cdot F
$$

**Conclusion:** The output voltage is linearly proportional to the tip–sample force, with proportionality constant $2S/k$. This relationship is the **optical lever principle** of AFM, which enables sub-nm deflection measurement.

**(7 marks: 1 for setup, 5 for steps, 1 for conclusion)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Describe the different operating modes of AFM with their force regimes and typical applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

AFM has three principal operating modes:

**1. Contact Mode:**
- **Force regime:** Repulsive (Pauli) at < 0.5 nm
- **Mechanism:** Tip is in continuous contact; the deflection (force) is held constant by feedback.
- **Cantilever:** Stiff, low k (~0.1–1 N/m).
- **Application:** Atomic resolution on hard samples, Lateral Force Microscopy (LFM) for friction.
- **Disadvantage:** Damages soft samples due to lateral shear.

**2. Non-Contact Mode (NC-AFM):**
- **Force regime:** Attractive (van der Waals) at 1–10 nm
- **Mechanism:** Cantilever oscillates at resonant frequency $f_0$; the tip–sample force gradient shifts $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$, which is detected.
- **Cantilever:** Stiff, high k (~10–50 N/m), high Q factor.
- **Application:** Truly non-destructive, soft samples, insulators.
- **Disadvantage:** Sensitive to contamination layer; lower resolution.

**3. Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact):**
- **Force regime:** Cyclic repulsive contact
- **Mechanism:** Cantilever oscillates at or near $f_0$ with large amplitude (20–100 nm). Tip "taps" the surface; amplitude reduction is detected.
- **Cantilever:** Medium k (~1–5 N/m).
- **Application:** Topography of soft/sticky samples (polymers, biological, photoresists).
- **Advantage:** Combines high resolution with low damage.

**Other Specialized Modes:** Force Modulation (stiffness), Phase Imaging (adhesion/viscoelasticity), Conductive AFM (local I–V), KPFM (surface potential), MFM (magnetic).

**(7 marks: 2+2+2 for three modes, 1 for others)**

---

**(b) Explain the Lennard-Jones potential and its significance in AFM tip–sample interaction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Lennard-Jones (L-J) potential is a mathematical model that approximates the interatomic interaction energy between two neutral atoms. It is fundamental to understanding AFM operation.

**Expression:**
$$
V(r) = 4 \varepsilon \left[ \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]
$$

where:
- $V(r)$ = potential energy at separation $r$
- $\varepsilon$ = depth of the potential well (energy at equilibrium)
- $\sigma$ = distance at which the inter-particle potential is zero
- $r$ = instantaneous interatomic separation

**Term-by-term interpretation:**

- The $\left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12}$ term represents **short-range Pauli repulsive force** (electron cloud overlap), dominating at very small $r$.
- The $-\left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6}$ term represents **long-range van der Waals attractive force** (induced dipole-induced dipole interactions), dominating at moderate $r$.

**Significance in AFM:**

1. **Equilibrium Separation:** At $r = 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \sigma$, the force is zero — the most stable tip-sample distance.
2. **Force–Distance Mapping:** Differentiating $V(r)$ gives $F(r)$, which is the actual force measured by the AFM cantilever.
3. **Operating Mode Determination:** 
   - At $r < 2^{1/6} \sigma$ → **Repulsive regime** → Contact and Tapping modes.
   - At $r > 2^{1/6} \sigma$ → **Attractive regime** → Non-contact mode.
4. **Tip–Sample Stability:** Defines how strongly the tip adheres to the sample; guides cantilever k selection.

**Conclusion:** The L-J potential is the **fundamental theoretical model** underlying the force–distance interaction that AFM exploits for imaging. It explains the transition between repulsive and attractive force regimes that differentiate AFM operating modes.

**(7 marks: 2 for expression, 3 for term interpretation, 2 for AFM significance)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up cantilever spring constant units:** $k$ is in **N/m**, not N. Do not write N/m².
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in optical lever derivation:** Many students omit the **$2\theta$ reflection geometry**, leading to half the photodiode displacement — a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Confusing Lennard-Jones exponents:** The $r^{12}$ is repulsive, $r^6$ is attractive. Reversing them is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Missing the "Setpoint" concept:** When asked about feedback, KTU examiners expect explicit mention of **PID controller + setpoint value**. Vague answers lose marks.
> 5. **Calling AFM "optical microscopy":** AFM uses **mechanical force detection via optical lever**, not optical imaging. Never say "AFM uses light to see atoms."

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AFM is a Scanning Probe Microscope (SPM)**, NOT an optical or electron microscope — it uses a **physical tip and force measurement**.
- **Invented in 1986 by Binnig, Quate, and Gerber.**
- **Core components:** sharp tip (radius 2–30 nm), flexible cantilever, piezoelectric scanner, laser source, position-sensitive photodiode, PID feedback controller.
- **Three main operating modes:** Contact (repulsive), Non-Contact (attractive), Tapping (intermittent contact).
- **Optical lever principle** gives a **2× amplification** of cantilever deflection at the photodiode: $V_{PD} = (2S/k) \cdot F$.
- **Hooke's Law for cantilever:** $\Delta z = F/k$ — fundamental force measurement equation.
- **Lennard-Jones potential** $V(r) = 4\varepsilon [(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6}]$ governs the force–distance relationship.
- **Cantilever spring constant range:** **0.01 to 50 N/m** (soft for delicate samples, stiff for non-contact).
- **Resolution:** Lateral ~1–5 nm, Vertical ~0.01–0.1 nm (sub-Ångström in vacuum).
- **AFM does not require vacuum** — works in air, liquid, and controlled environments.
- **Key derived equations to memorize:**
  1. $\Delta z = F/k$
  2. $\theta = \Delta z / L$
  3. $V_{PD} = (2S/k) F$
  4. $f_0 = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m^*}$
  5. $F_{vdW} = -HR/(6d^2)$
- **For KTU ESE:** Emphasize the **block diagram**, **modes comparison table**, and the **optical lever derivation** — these are the highest-weight subtopics.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
- **For 14-mark questions**, structure the answer as: **Definition → Principle → Working → Block Diagram → Equation → Application**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to On-Machine Metrology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET864 - MICRO AND NANO MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to On-Machine Metrology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**On-Machine Metrology (OMM)**, also referred to as *On-Machine Measurement (OMM)* or *In-Process Metrology*, is defined as the integration of precision measurement transducers, sensors, and probing systems directly onto a manufacturing machine tool (such as a CNC milling, turning, or grinding machine) so that the dimensional, geometric, and surface characteristics of a workpiece can be quantified **without removing the part from its manufacturing fixture or machining environment**.

In the context of **micro and nano manufacturing**, OMM is formally defined (per ISO 10360 and KTU 2024 Scheme PEMET864 syllabus) as the *closed-loop integration of sub-micrometre resolution metrology hardware with the kinematic axes of a machine tool*, enabling real-time dimensional verification, process compensation, and adaptive control of feature geometry in the **meso-, micro-, and nano-scale** domains (typically **1 µm – 1 nm** feature sizes).

### Formal Categories of In-Process Measurement

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Pre-process** | Measurement before machining (workpiece blank verification) |
| **In-process** | Measurement during the cutting/forming cycle |
| **On-machine** | Measurement on the machine tool after a cut, before unclamping |
| **Post-process** | Off-line measurement on a separate CMM or microscope |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The PEMET864 Module 4 explicitly treats OMM as the *bridge* between primary manufacturing (Module 2–3) and quality assurance. It is the **only practical technique** capable of avoiding the **Abbe error** and **re-fixturing repeatability error** when verifying features below 50 µm.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Carpenter's Square"

Imagine a traditional carpenter who cuts a wooden table, then walks over to a separate workbench to measure each joint with a steel rule. Every time he moves the table, the wood may shift slightly, his tape may sag, or the humidity may change the wood's dimension by a fraction of a millimetre. For a full-size dining table, this error is invisible.

Now imagine a watchmaker crafting a **gears train the size of a sesame seed**. If he has to walk across the workshop to "measure" each tooth with a separate gauge, the act of moving the part introduces errors **larger than the part itself**. The watchmaker's only option is to bring the measuring tool **directly to the workpiece while it is still clamped in the lathe** — this is the essence of **On-Machine Metrology**.

In the same way, OMM "brings the ruler to the part" so the part never leaves its manufacturing coordinate frame. This eliminates two catastrophic micro-scale errors:

1. **Re-fixturing / re-clamping error** (typically **1–10 µm** on a conventional fixture).
2. **Thermal transient error** caused by the part equilibrating to a new ambient temperature (can exceed **0.5 µm/°C** for steel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In macro-machining, you can afford to remove the part and measure it elsewhere. In micro/nano machining, the measurement system **must come to the machine**, not the other way around.

---

## 1.3 Why OMM is Indispensable in Micro & Nano Manufacturing

The fundamental driver is the **error-to-feature ratio**. As feature size shrinks, fixed-magnitude errors (such as fixture repeatability ≈ **5 µm**, machine axis repeatability ≈ **1 µm**) become proportionally larger and eventually dominate the geometry.

$$E_{ratio} = \frac{E_{absolute}}{L_{feature}}$$

Where:
- $E_{ratio}$ = Error-to-feature ratio (dimensionless)
- $E_{absolute}$ = Absolute positional/measurement error (µm)
- $L_{feature}$ = Critical feature size (µm)

For a **1 mm** feature with **5 µm** fixturing error, $E_{ratio} = 0.5\%$.
For a **5 µm** feature with the same **5 µm** error, $E_{ratio} = 100\%$ — the part is unmeasurable by conventional means.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The lower the feature size, the more mandatory On-Machine Metrology becomes. This is a *physical necessity*, not a convenience.

---

## 1.4 Classification of OMM by Sensor Physical Principle

| Class | Physical Principle | Typical Resolution | Application in Micro/Nano |
|-------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| **Touch-trigger probes (TTP)** | Electromechanical / strain gauge / piezoelectric contact | **0.1 – 1 µm** | Hole, edge, datum verification on CNC mills/turns |
| **Scanning probes (analog)** | Analog displacement transducer in stylus | **0.05 – 0.5 µm** | Free-form surface digitization |
| **Laser triangulation probes** | Focused laser + position-sensitive detector (PSD) | **0.1 – 5 µm** | In-process tool/workpiece offset |
| **Laser interferometers** | Heterodyne/Homodyne optical interference | **0.001 – 0.01 µm** (λ/40) | Machine axis calibration, scale verification |
| **Confocal chromatic probes** | Wavelength-dependent depth focus | **0.01 – 0.1 µm** | Micro-cavity depth, film thickness |
| **White-light interferometry (WLI)** | Coherence peak detection | **0.1 – 1 nm** | Sub-micron surface topography |
| **Machine vision (CCD/CMOS)** | Optical imaging + pixel-grid algorithms | **1 – 10 µm** | Edge detection, micro-feature contour |
| **Scanning probe microscopy (SPM/AFM)** | Atomic-scale tip–surface interaction | **0.01 – 1 nm** | Nanoscale surface metrology |
| **Capacitive / Eddy-current probes** | Non-contact field change | **0.01 – 0.1 µm** | Spindle vibration, displacement at nm-scale |
| **X-ray CT (in-line)** | X-ray attenuation tomography | **0.5 – 5 µm voxel** | Internal micro-channel verification |

> [!NOTE]
