> **For KTU examinations, students are expected to know the operating principle, resolution, and typical application of at least 4–5 of the above sensor classes.**

---

## 1.5 The Three Functional Modes of OMM

1. **Verification Mode** – Part is machined, measured on-machine, accepted or rejected without unclamping. Used for high-value micro-components.
2. **Compensation / Correction Mode** – Measured deviation is fed back to the CNC controller, and a corrected tool path is generated for the next machining pass.
3. **Adaptive / Closed-Loop Control Mode** – Real-time measurement actively modulates cutting parameters (feed, speed, depth) during the cut itself.

The third mode is the **ultimate goal** in nano-manufacturing, and represents the merger of metrology with intelligent process control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OMM Operational Stack — A Five-Layer Architecture

On-machine metrology is not a single sensor; it is a **hierarchical system** of hardware, software, kinematics, and data-flow. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to diagram and explain this stack.

### Layer 1 — Physical Sensor Layer
The probe tip, laser head, camera, or interferometer that interacts physically with the workpiece. The dominant error sources here are:
- **Abbe offset error** (lateral offset between probe axis and measurement axis).
- **Probe form error** (stylus tip sphericity, typically **1–5 µm** for ruby ball, **0.05 µm** for diamond).
- **Stylus bending** under contact force.

### Layer 2 — Signal Conditioning Layer
Converts raw analog signals (voltage from a strain gauge, current from a photodiode, fringe count from an interferometer) into digital coordinates. Includes:
- **Amplifiers**, **A/D converters** (typically **16-bit / 24-bit**).
- **Anti-aliasing filters** (Nyquist criterion: $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$).
- **Thermal compensation circuits**.

### Layer 3 — Kinematic Reference Layer
Aligns the sensor's local coordinates to the **machine tool's reference frame** (typically the spindle nose or machine zero point). This is achieved through:
- **Datum calibration** routines.
- **Probe qualification** (a sphere or ring artefact of known geometry).
- **Thermal expansion compensation**:
$$\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
Where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion (for steel ≈ **11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**).

### Layer 4 — Computational Layer
Performs:
- **Least-squares fitting** of measured points to nominal geometry (circle, plane, cylinder, sphere, free-form NURBS).
- **Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T)** evaluation.
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charting.

### Layer 5 — Control / Decision Layer
The CNC controller or supervisory system that:
- Compares measured values to specification.
- Issues **offset corrections** to axes.
- Triggers **tool wear compensation** or **re-grinding cycles**.
- Logs data for **digital twin** maintenance.

---

## 2.2 The Abbe Principle in OMM Context

The **Abbe principle** (Ernst Abbe, 1890) states that the **maximum measurement accuracy is achieved when the measuring scale is collinear with the measured displacement axis**. Any lateral offset $\delta$ between the scale and the measuring line introduces a sine error:

$$E_{Abbe} = \delta \cdot \tan(\theta) \approx \delta \cdot \theta \quad \text{(for small angles)}$$

Where:
- $E_{Abbe}$ = Abbe offset error (µm)
- $\delta$ = Lateral offset between scale and measurement axis (mm)
- $\theta$ = Angular deviation (radians)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In OMM, the Abbe offset is typically much smaller than in a separate CMM** because the machine's own linear encoders (glass or steel scales, resolution **0.01–0.1 µm**) are aligned with the axes of motion. This is one of OMM's central technical advantages.

---

## 2.3 Resolution, Repeatability, and Accuracy — The Three R's

KTU examinations frequently test the distinction between these three metrological terms:

| Term | Definition | Typical Order (micro OMM) |
|------|------------|----------------------------|
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable increment of the measuring system | **0.001 – 0.1 µm** |
| **Repeatability** | Spread of measurements when the same operator measures the same feature under the same conditions | **0.05 – 1 µm** (2σ) |
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of a measurement to the true (traceable) value | **0.1 – 5 µm** (depends on calibration) |

The relationship in well-designed systems is:

$$R_{resolution} \leq R_{repeatability} \leq R_{accuracy}$$

Or in strict numerical form: resolution is usually **3–10× smaller** than repeatability, and accuracy is typically **2–5× larger** than repeatability.

---

## 2.4 The Probe Pre-Trigger Error (Touch Probes)

When a touch-trigger probe contacts a surface, it deflects by a small amount $\Delta x$ before triggering. This is the **pre-travel** or **overtravel**. The measured coordinate is therefore **delayed** by this deflection:

$$x_{measured} = x_{contact} - \Delta x_{pre-travel}$$

The triggering force $F_t$ relates to deflection through the probe's lateral stiffness $k$:

$$F_t = k \cdot \Delta x_{pre-travel}$$

Typical values for a Renishaw OMP60 probe:
- **Trigger force $F_t$** ≈ 0.06 – 0.2 N (low force variant)
- **Stiffness $k$** ≈ 1 – 5 N/mm
- **Pre-travel $\Delta x$** ≈ 4 – 12 µm (depends on approach speed)

> [!NOTE]
> **In micro-machining, low-touch-force probes are mandatory.** Standard CMM probes (force ≈ 1 N) can deflect or even damage features smaller than 50 µm.

---

## 2.5 The 3-2-1 Principle of Workpiece Location in OMM

Just as in conventional metrology, OMM relies on the **3-2-1 locating principle** to establish a deterministic kinematic constraint on the workpiece:

- **3 primary datums** (a plane) — 3 contact points → constrain 3 degrees of freedom (DoF).
- **2 secondary datums** (a line on the plane) — 2 contact points → constrain 2 additional DoF.
- **1 tertiary datum** (a point) — 1 contact point → constrains the final rotational DoF.

Total: **6 DoF constrained**, fully defining the part in the machine's coordinate system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **on-machine** setups, the same fixture used for cutting is also used for measurement, so the datum reference frame (DRF) is **identical** for manufacturing and inspection — a major source of error elimination.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $E_{ratio} = E_{absolute} / L_{feature}$ | Error-to-feature ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion | µm |
| 3 | $E_{Abbe} = \delta \cdot \theta$ | Abbe offset error | µm |
| 4 | $F_t = k \cdot \Delta x$ | Trigger force vs. probe stiffness | N |
| 5 | $\sigma_{combined} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 + \dots}$ | RSS of independent error sources | µm |
| 6 | $U_{95} = k_{coverage} \cdot \sigma_{combined}$ | Expanded measurement uncertainty (k≈2) | µm |
| 7 | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 8 | $R_a = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | Arithmetic mean roughness (use $\vert$ not $\mid$) | µm |
| 9 | $R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{L} \int_0^L z(x)^2 \, dx}$ | RMS roughness | µm |
| 10 | $MPE_E = A + L/K$ | CMM/OMM permissible linear error (ISO 10360-2) | µm |
| 11 | $\Delta T = T_{part} - T_{machine}$ | Thermal gradient (must be < 1°C for sub-µm) | °C |
| 12 | $\lambda = 632.8$ nm (HeNe laser) | Reference wavelength for laser interferometry | nm |
| 13 | $R = \lambda / (2 \cdot n)$ | Optical resolution limit (Abbe diffraction) | nm |
| 14 | $\varepsilon_{machine} = \pm (3 + L/250)$ µm | Typical machine tool volumetric accuracy (semi-premium) | µm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Root-Sum-Square (RSS) combination** of errors is the standard method to combine independent uncertainty contributions in OMM. The **ISO GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement)** specifies that the **expanded uncertainty $U_{95}$** at 95% confidence uses a coverage factor $k = 2$ for a normal distribution.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are not academic — they govern the production of:

- **Microfluidic chips** (channel width ≈ 50–500 µm) used in lab-on-a-chip diagnostics.
- **MEMS accelerometers** (proof mass geometry ≈ 1–10 µm).
- **Watch balance wheels and escapements** (pivot features ≈ 50–200 µm).
- **Aero-engine fuel injector nozzles** (hole diameter ≈ 80–300 µm, ±5 µm tolerance).
- **Optical moulds for smartphone lenses** (free-form surface, sub-100 nm form error).
- **Aerospace turbine blade airfoil profiles** (often measured on-machine between roughing and finishing to avoid scrap of expensive forgings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all of these cases, OMM prevents **scrap of high-value parts** (a turbine blade blank can cost **$5,000–$20,000**), and reduces **measurement cycle time** by **40–70%** by eliminating transfer to a separate CMM room.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Volumetric Uncertainty of an OMM System

The most analytically important result in OMM is the **uncertainty budget** for a 3-axis dimensional measurement. The KTU 2024 examiner frequently asks students to derive the combined uncertainty for an on-machine probe measurement of a length $L$.

### Statement of the Problem

A touch-trigger probe is used on a CNC milling machine to measure the length of a micro-feature. Identify the independent error sources and derive the **expanded uncertainty** at 95% confidence.

### Independent Error Sources

1. **Probe repeatability** $\sigma_p$ (Type A, statistical).
2. **Machine axis linear error** $\sigma_{axis}$ (Type B, from calibration certificate).
3. **Thermal expansion error** $\sigma_T$.
4. **Probe qualification (datum sphere) error** $\sigma_q$.
5. **Abbe offset error** $\sigma_A$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the measured length as a sum of true length and independent errors:**

$$L_{measured} = L_{true} + \delta_p + \delta_{axis} + \delta_T + \delta_q + \delta_A$$

**Step 2 — The expected (mean) value:**

$$\mathbb{E}[L_{measured}] = L_{true}$$

(Assumption: all errors are zero-mean, unbiased, and independent.)

**Step 3 — The variance of the measured length (linearity of expectation and independence):**

$$\sigma_{L}^2 = \sigma_p^2 + \sigma_{axis}^2 + \sigma_T^2 + \sigma_q^2 + \sigma_A^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Root-Sum-Square (RSS) combination (ISO GUM):**

$$\sigma_{combined} = \sqrt{\sigma_p^2 + \sigma_{axis}^2 + \sigma_T^2 + \sigma_q^2 + \sigma_A^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence:**

$$U_{95} = k \cdot \sigma_{combined}, \quad k = 2$$

$$\boxed{U_{95} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_p^2 + \sigma_{axis}^2 + \sigma_T^2 + \sigma_q^2 + \sigma_A^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for a micro-feature of nominal 500 µm:**

| Error Source | Magnitude $\sigma_i$ (µm) | $\sigma_i^2$ (µm²) |
|--------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Probe repeatability | 0.20 | 0.0400 |
| Axis linear error | 0.50 | 0.2500 |
| Thermal error | 0.30 | 0.0900 |
| Qualification error | 0.15 | 0.0225 |
| Abbe error | 0.10 | 0.0100 |
| **Sum of squares** | | **0.4125** |

$$\sigma_{combined} = \sqrt{0.4125} = 0.642 \, \mu m$$

$$U_{95} = 2 \times 0.642 = 1.284 \, \mu m$$

**Conclusion:** The measurement can be quoted as $L = 500.00 \pm 1.28 \, \mu m$ at 95% confidence. This is a **typical uncertainty budget** for an industrial OMM station measuring a 500 µm micro-feature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Drift Compensation in OMM

### Problem
A steel workpiece of nominal length $L_0 = 25.000$ mm is being measured on-machine. The part temperature is $T_p = 22.5$ °C, while the machine's reference scale is at $T_m = 20.0$ °C. Compute the apparent length error and the corrected reading.

### Step 1 — Identify the thermal expansion coefficient of steel.**

For standard AISI 4140 steel:

$$\alpha_{steel} = 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \, /°C$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_p - T_m = 22.5 - 20.0 = 2.5 \, °C$$

**Step 3 — Apply the linear thermal expansion formula:**

$$\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\Delta L = 25{,}000 \, \mu m \times (11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 2.5$$

$$\Delta L = 25{,}000 \times 29 \times 10^{-6} = 0.725 \, \mu m$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

The workpiece is **larger than the machine thinks it is** by 0.725 µm. To correct the reading, subtract this expansion from the indicated value.

**Step 5 — Corrected length:**

$$L_{corrected} = L_{measured} - \Delta L = L_{measured} - 0.725 \, \mu m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For sub-micrometre OMM, thermal gradients of < 0.1 °C must be enforced.** This is why precision OMM cells are often built inside **class 10,000 cleanrooms with ±0.1 °C temperature stability**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Abbe Error for a Tilted Probe Approach

### Problem
A touch probe approaches a micro-feature with its stylus offset $\delta = 5.0$ mm from the machine's vertical Z-axis. The probe contacts the surface at an approach angle that introduces a yaw rotation $\theta = 0.005$ rad. Compute the Abbe error.

### Step 1 — Identify the Abbe equation:**

$$E_{Abbe} = \delta \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the tangent for small angles:**

$$\tan(0.005) \approx 0.005 + \frac{0.005^3}{3} \approx 0.0050000417$$

(For such small angles, $\tan(\theta) \approx \theta$ is sufficient.)

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$E_{Abbe} = 5.0 \, mm \times 0.005 = 0.025 \, mm = 25 \, \mu m$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A 25 µm Abbe error is **catastrophic** for a 50 µm feature. This is why **OMM probe styli are designed with extremely short and stiff geometries** to minimize $\delta$ in the direction of measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Mitigation:** Use probe configurations that satisfy the **Abbe principle** — align the probe measurement axis with the machine's linear encoder. The shorter the stylus extension, the smaller the Abbe offset $\delta$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: OMM Sensor Selection by Application

| Application | Required Resolution | Required Accuracy | Best OMM Sensor | Reason |
|-------------|---------------------|-------------------|------------------|--------|
| **Micro-mould cavity depth (50 µm)** | 0.5 µm | ±2 µm | Confocal chromatic probe | Non-contact, sub-µm depth |
| **Fuel injector hole diameter (100 µm)** | 1 µm | ±3 µm | Machine vision + telecentric lens | Rapid 2D edge detection |
| **Turbine blade airfoil (mm-scale)** | 1 µm | ±5 µm | Scanning analog probe | Free-form 3D digitization |
| **Nano-imprint template (10 nm step)** | 1 nm | ±10 nm | White-light interferometer | Vertical scan, nm-scale |
| **Bearing race groove radius** | 0.1 µm | ±1 µm | Touch-trigger + calibration ring | Sub-µm diameter |
| **PCB micro-via diameter (50 µm)** | 2 µm | ±5 µm | Laser triangulation | Fast, in-process hole check |
| **Optical free-form surface (mirror)** | 5 nm | ±20 nm | Scanning probe microscope (AFM/LPM) | Atomic-scale texture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The general rule: **probe resolution should be 1/5 to 1/10 of the tightest tolerance** on the feature being measured. Selecting a probe with too coarse a resolution invalidates the measurement; selecting one with too fine a resolution wastes cost and slows the cycle.

---

## 3.5 The Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Bound for OMM

For any OMM sensor, the theoretical lower limit of detectable displacement is set by the **signal-to-noise ratio**:

$$SNR = \frac{V_{signal}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{shot}^2 + \sigma_{thermal}^2 + \sigma_{flicker}^2}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{shot}$ = shot noise of the photodetector (Poisson): $\sigma_{shot} = \sqrt{2 e I_{ph} \Delta f}$.
- $\sigma_{thermal}$ = Johnson-Nyquist noise of the transimpedance amplifier.
- $\sigma_{flicker}$ = 1/f noise dominating below 1 kHz.

**For a typical laser triangulation probe:**
- Optical power $P = 1$ mW, $I_{ph} \approx 0.3$ A/W × 1 mW = 0.3 mA.
- Bandwidth $\Delta f = 10$ kHz.
- Shot noise: $\sigma_{shot} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^4} = 9.8 \times 10^{-10}$ A.

This corresponds to a theoretical displacement noise floor of **< 0.1 nm**, in line with manufacturer specifications for high-end triangulation probes.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The OMM System Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the five-layer architecture of a typical on-machine metrology cell. It maps the flow of information from the physical sensor (stylus / laser / camera) to the final compensation command issued back to the CNC controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL SENSOR"]
        A1["Touch Trigger Probe"]
        A2["Laser Triangulation Probe"]
        A3["Confocal Chromatic Probe"]
        A4["Machine Vision Camera"]
        A5["White Light Interferometer"]
    end

    subgraph S2["LAYER 2: SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
        B1["Strain Gauge Amplifier"]
        B2["Photodiode Transimpedance"]
        B3["Spectrometer CCD Array"]
        B4["Frame Grabber"]
        B5["Piezo Scanner Driver"]
    end

    subgraph S3["LAYER 3: KINEMATIC REFERENCE"]
        C1["Datum Calibration Routine"]
        C2["Probe Qualification Sphere"]
        C3["Thermal Compensation Model"]
        C4["Axis Error Mapping"]
    end

    subgraph S4["LAYER 4: COMPUTATION"]
        D1["Least Squares Geometry Fit"]
        D2["GD&T Evaluator"]
        D3["Uncertainty Calculator RSS"]
        D4["SPC Chart Logger"]
    end

    subgraph S5["LAYER 5: CONTROL DECISION"]
        E1["CNC Offset Correction"]
        E2["Adaptive Feed Rate"]
        E3["Tool Wear Compensation"]
        E4["Reject or Accept Flag"]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1 --> E1
    A2 --> B2 --> C1 --> D1 --> E1
    A3 --> B3 --> C2 --> D2 --> E2
    A4 --> B4 --> C3 --> D3 --> E3
    A5 --> B5 --> C4 --> D4 --> E4

    E1 --> F["MACHINE TOOL AXES X Y Z"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The five sensor modalities (Layer 1) each feed a different signal conditioner (Layer 2), which is referenced to the machine's kinematic frame (Layer 3), mathematically processed (Layer 4), and converted into a CNC action (Layer 5). The final block `F` is the **machine tool's own servo axes**, completing the closed loop.

---

## 4.2 OMM Operational Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Workpiece Loaded into Fixture"] --> P1["Probe Calibration on Datum Sphere"]
    P1 --> P2["Set Tool Offset and Workpiece Zero"]
    P2 --> P3["Rough Machining Pass"]
    P3 --> P4{"On-Machine Measurement"}
    P4 --> P5["Deviation From Nominal"]
    P5 --> P6{"In Tolerance?"}
    P6 -->|"Yes"| P7["Finish Machining Pass"]
    P6 -->|"No"| P8["Compute Compensation Offset"]
    P8 --> P9["Apply Offset to CNC Tool Path"]
    P9 --> P3
    P7 --> P10["Final On-Machine Verification"]
    P10 --> P11{"In Tolerance?"}
    P11 -->|"Yes"| P12["Accept and Unload"]
    P11 -->|"No"| P8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diamond decision nodes `{"In Tolerance?"}` represent the **closed-loop branching** that distinguishes basic OMM (verification only) from **adaptive OMM** (compensation feedback). KTU 2024 questions often test whether students can identify which feedback paths are active in a given workflow.

---

## 4.3 Sensor-to-Scale Alignment Diagram (Abbe Compliance)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X["X-AXIS ENCODER SCALE"]
        X1["Glass Scale"]
        X2["Optical Read Head"]
    end
    subgraph Y["Y-AXIS ENCODER SCALE"]
        Y1["Glass Scale"]
        Y2["Optical Read Head"]
    end
    subgraph Z["Z-AXIS ENCODER SCALE"]
        Z1["Glass Scale"]
        Z2["Optical Read Head"]
    end
    subgraph P["PROBE MEASUREMENT AXIS"]
        P1["Stylus Tip"]
        P2["Probe Body"]
    end
    WP["Workpiece Surface to be Measured"]
    P1 -->|"Direct Contact"| WP
    P2 -->|"Lateral Offset delta"| X2
    X2 --> X1
    Y2 --> Y1
    Z2 --> Z1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram visually shows the lateral offset $\delta$ between the probe measurement line and the machine's encoder scale. **Minimizing $\delta$** is the central design rule for Abbe-compliant OMM probe mounting. Premium systems place the probe on the **spindle axis itself** so that the stylus axis **coincides** with the Z-encoder line, making $\delta \approx 0$ in the Z direction.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Standalone CMM vs. On-Machine Metrology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAND["STANDALONE CMM"]
        S1A["Part Machined on CNC"]
        S1B["Part Unclamped and Transferred to CMM"]
        S1C["Re-fixtured on CMM Table"]
        S1D["Long Settling Time for Thermal Equilibrium"]
        S1E["Measurement Performed"]
        S1F["Re-fixturing Error Plus Thermal Drift Included"]
        S1A --> S1B --> S1C --> S1D --> S1E --> S1F
    end

    subgraph OMMM["ON-MACHINE METROLOGY"]
        O1A["Part Machined on CNC"]
        O1B["Probe Mounted on Spindle Moves to Part"]
        O1C["Same Fixture as Machining"]
        O1D["No Re-fixing, No Re-thermalization"]
        O1E["Measurement Performed in Same Coordinate Frame"]
        O1F["Lower Uncertainty, Faster Cycle"]
        O1A --> O1B --> O1C --> O1D --> O1E --> O1F
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The side-by-side architecture highlights why OMM is **not just faster** but **fundamentally more accurate** for sub-100 µm features: it eliminates *entire categories* of systematic error (re-fixturing, thermal transient, datum frame mismatch) that are unavoidable in standalone CMM workflows.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, **Part A questions are compulsory, carry 3 marks each, and must be answered in 50–70 words**. They typically test direct definitions, basic principles, or classification recall.

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**Define On-Machine Metrology. How does it differ from conventional off-line metrology in the context of micro-manufacturing?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**On-Machine Metrology (OMM)** is the integration of precision measurement probes, sensors, and inspection routines directly onto a machine tool, so that the workpiece is measured **without removing it from its manufacturing fixture**.

In conventional off-line metrology, the part is unclamped and transferred to a separate Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM). In the micro-scale regime, this transfer introduces **re-fixturing errors (1–10 µm)** and **thermal transients (0.5 µm/°C)** that can exceed the entire tolerance of the feature. OMM eliminates these errors by keeping the part in the **same kinematic reference frame** throughout manufacturing and inspection.

**(Marking scheme: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for off-line CMM description, 1 mark for error comparison.)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**List any four sensor technologies used in On-Machine Metrology and state one application of each in micro-manufacturing.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| # | Sensor Technology | Application in Micro-Manufacturing |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Touch-trigger probe** | Measuring hole diameter and datum positions on a micro-machined part |
| 2 | **Laser triangulation probe** | In-process tool offset verification during CNC milling |
| 3 | **Confocal chromatic probe** | Depth measurement of micro-cavities and film thickness |
| 4 | **White-light interferometer** | Nanometre-scale surface topography of optical moulds |
| 5 | **Machine vision (CCD/CMOS)** | Edge detection of micro-fluidic channels |

**(Marking scheme: 0.5 mark per correct sensor, 0.25 mark per matching application. Total 3 marks.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks**, is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, and offers an **internal choice** (i.e., students answer either Question A or Question B, not both).

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**a) Explain the Abbe principle and discuss its importance in the design of an on-machine measurement system for a micro-feature of nominal size 50 µm. (7 marks)**

**b) With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the five-layer architecture of an On-Machine Metrology system. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — Abbe Principle and Its Importance (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Statement of the Abbe Principle (2 marks):**

The **Abbe principle**, formulated by Ernst Abbe in 1890, states that the *highest measurement accuracy is obtained when the measuring scale is collinear with the line of measurement*. When the probe's measurement axis is laterally offset from the machine's reference scale by a distance $\delta$, any angular deviation $\theta$ of the moving member produces a sine error:

$$E_{Abbe} = \delta \cdot \tan(\theta) \approx \delta \cdot \theta \quad (\text{for small angles})$$

**Step 2 — Numerical demonstration (2 marks):**

For a 50 µm feature with a probe offset $\delta = 5$ mm and a yaw rotation $\theta = 0.001$ rad:

$$E_{Abbe} = 5{,}000 \, \mu m \times 0.001 = 5 \, \mu m$$

This single error source equals **10% of the feature size** — completely unacceptable.

**Step 3 — Importance in OMM (3 marks):**

(i) OMM designs mount probes **on the spindle axis**, making the Z-encoder collinear with the measurement axis, so $\delta \to 0$.

(ii) For lateral X-Y measurements, the probe is moved along the **encoder axis**, with the **stylus extension kept as short and stiff as possible** (typical length 10–30 mm).

(iii) The principle is critical at the micro-scale because errors that are negligible at macro-scale become dominant at micro-scale.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Five-Layer Architecture (7 marks)

**The five layers are:**

1. **Physical Sensor Layer (1 mark):** Touch probes, laser probes, vision cameras, interferometers, AFM tips. These contact or image the workpiece surface.

2. **Signal Conditioning Layer (1.5 marks):** Strain gauge amplifiers, photodiode transimpedance amplifiers, frame grabbers, A/D converters, anti-aliasing filters.

3. **Kinematic Reference Layer (1.5 marks):** Datum calibration routines, probe qualification on reference spheres/rings, axis error mapping, thermal expansion compensation.

4. **Computational Layer (1.5 marks):** Least-squares fitting of measured point clouds to nominal geometry (circle, plane, free-form surface), GD&T evaluation, RSS uncertainty calculation, SPC charting.

5. **Control / Decision Layer (1.5 marks):** The CNC controller or supervisory system that issues offset corrections, adaptive feed-rate commands, tool-wear compensation, and accept/reject decisions.

**Conclusion:** The five layers form a closed loop, with measured deviations feeding back as corrections to the machine tool axes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 — Internal Choice]

**a) What are the sources of error in on-machine metrology? For a micro-feature of length 200 µm, an OMM system reports the following independent standard uncertainties: probe repeatability 0.15 µm, axis linear error 0.40 µm, thermal error 0.25 µm, qualification error 0.10 µm, and Abbe error 0.08 µm. Compute the combined standard uncertainty and the expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence. (7 marks)**

**b) Describe any three non-contact OMM sensor technologies with their operating principles, typical resolutions, and micro-manufacturing applications. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Combined Uncertainty (7 marks)

**Step 1 — List the independent standard uncertainties (1 mark):**

- $\sigma_p = 0.15$ µm (probe repeatability)
- $\sigma_{axis} = 0.40$ µm (axis linear error)
- $\sigma_T = 0.25$ µm (thermal)
- $\sigma_q = 0.10$ µm (qualification)
- $\sigma_A = 0.08$ µm (Abbe)

**Step 2 — Apply the RSS formula (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{(0.15)^2 + (0.40)^2 + (0.25)^2 + (0.10)^2 + (0.08)^2}$$

Compute each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.15)^2 &= 0.0225 \\
(0.40)^2 &= 0.1600 \\
(0.25)^2 &= 0.0625 \\
(0.10)^2 &= 0.0100 \\
(0.08)^2 &= 0.0064 \\
\text{Sum} &= 0.2614
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{0.2614} = 0.5113 \, \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Compute expanded uncertainty (2 marks):**

$$U_{95} = k \cdot \sigma_c = 2 \times 0.5113 = 1.0226 \, \mu m$$

**Step 4 — Statement of result (1 mark):**

The OMM measurement of the 200 µm micro-feature can be quoted as:

$$L = 200.00 \pm 1.02 \, \mu m \quad (\text{at } 95\% \text{ confidence})$$

The dominant contributor is the **machine axis linear error (0.40 µm)**, accounting for ≈ 61% of the total variance. Reducing this term (by tighter axis calibration) would yield the largest improvement.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Three Non-Contact OMM Sensors (7 marks)

**Sensor 1: Laser Triangulation Probe (2.5 marks)**

A focused laser beam strikes the workpiece surface. The reflected spot is imaged onto a **position-sensitive detector (PSD)** or linear CCD. The position of the spot shifts with surface height, and the shift is geometrically converted to a distance via the triangulation angle.

- **Resolution:** 0.1 – 5 µm
- **Application:** In-process tool offset, micro-cavity depth, surface profile of fuel injector nozzles.

**Sensor 2: Confocal Chromatic Probe (2.5 marks)**

White light passes through a **chromatic lens** that focuses each wavelength at a slightly different depth. Only the wavelength that is exactly in focus on the surface is reflected back through a pinhole and detected. The detected wavelength uniquely identifies the surface height.

- **Resolution:** 0.01 – 0.1 µm
- **Application:** Depth of micro-channels, film thickness, transparent layer measurement in microfluidics.

**Sensor 3: White-Light Interferometer (WLI) (2 marks)**

A **Michelson interferometer** uses a broadband light source. Interference fringes are formed only over a narrow axial range where the optical path difference is within the source's coherence length. By scanning the reference mirror vertically, the peak of the coherence envelope identifies the surface height at each pixel.

- **Resolution:** 0.1 – 1 nm
- **Application:** Nano-scale surface topography, finish quality of optical moulds and aspheric lens masters.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks on OMM Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing "resolution" with "accuracy":** These are *not* the same. Resolution is the smallest detectable increment; accuracy is closeness to the true value. Many students write one when the question asks for the other. **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Abbe offset:** When asked why OMM is more accurate than off-line CMM, students often mention "no re-fixturing" but forget the Abbe principle. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the coverage factor:** In uncertainty calculations, the **coverage factor $k=2$** for 95% confidence is mandatory. Students who write $U = \sigma_c$ directly lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing OMM with SPC:** Statistical Process Control is a *post-measurement analysis* tool; OMM is the *physical hardware* that produces the data. They are not synonyms.
> 5. **Writing "laser" without specifying the principle:** Saying "laser" alone is not enough; examiners expect "laser triangulation," "laser interferometry," or "confocal chromatic."
> 6. **Missing units:** Always quote µm, nm, °C with the numerical answer. Missing units cost 0.5 mark each occurrence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this section 24 hours before the exam.**

- [x] **OMM = measurement on the machine tool, without unclamping** the part. Eliminate re-fixturing and thermal-transient errors.
- [x] The **Abbe principle** demands alignment of the measurement axis with the linear encoder; offset $\delta$ × angle $\theta$ = sine error.
- [x] **Five-layer architecture:** Sensor → Signal Conditioning → Kinematic Reference → Computation → Control/Decision.
- [x] **3-2-1 locating principle** constrains all 6 DoF of the workpiece.
- [x] **Combined uncertainty (RSS):** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$.
- [x] **Expanded uncertainty:** $U_{95} = k \sigma_c$ with $k=2$.
- [x] **Thermal expansion:** $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$. For steel, $\alpha \approx 11.6 \times 10^{-6} /°C$.
- [x] **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ for sampling.
- [x] **Sensor resolution** should be 1/5 to 1/10 of the tightest tolerance.
- [x] **Touch probes** have a pre-travel (4–12 µm) and a low trigger force (0.06–0.2 N for micro).
- [x] **Laser triangulation:** Resolution 0.1–5 µm, used for in-process tool offset.
- [x] **Confocal chromatic:** Resolution 0.01–0.1 µm, used for micro-cavity depth.
- [x] **White-light interferometry:** Resolution 0.1–1 nm, used for optical mould topography.
- [x] **Machine vision:** Resolution 1–10 µm, used for 2D edge detection.
- [x] **ISO 10360** governs CMM/OMM accuracy specifications; **ISO GUM** governs uncertainty expression.
- [x] **Macro → Micro → Nano:** OMM becomes *mandatory* (not optional) as feature size drops below 50 µm.
- [x] OMM provides **three benefits:** (i) higher accuracy, (ii) faster cycle, (iii) reduced scrap of high-value parts.
- [x] **Closed-loop OMM** = measure → compare → compensate → re-machine, all on the same fixture.
- [x] Probe **stylus length** must be minimized to reduce Abbe offset and stylus bending.
- [x] For **sub-µm OMM**, thermal gradients < 0.1 °C and class 10,000 cleanroom environment are required.
- [x] The **dominant error contributor** in any well-designed OMM is usually the **machine axis error** (volumetric accuracy), not the probe itself.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** Before entering the exam hall, recite this mantra:
> *"OMM keeps the part in the same frame; Abbe says keep the line short; RSS adds the errors; coverage factor 2 gives U95."*
